subtitle: 平和記念 · 宮島 · ベジ／グルテンフリー

places.name: お好み焼 みっちゃん総本店 八丁堀本店 (Okonomiyaki Mitchan Sohonten Hatchobori Hont)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for gluten-free diners. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the signature okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based. The teppan is shared across all dishes, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The okonomiyaki contains egg and pork, and the signature dishes feature Hiroshima oysters and offal. Dishes are cooked with bonito/fish-based dashi and wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon, Wed-Fri): 11:30-14:30, 17:30-21:00. Saturdays, Sundays & holidays: 11:00-14:30, 17:00-21:00. Last orders 30 minutes before closing. Closed Tuesdays (or Wednesday if Tuesday is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, walking distance fro
places.notes: Founded in 1950 (Showa 25) as a flavor born and nurtured alongside Hiroshima's post-war reconstruction, this is the original house of Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. Founder Mitsuo Ijira is credited as the creator of the Hiroshima style, and the shop carries the title of o

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1950 (Showa 25) as a flavor born and nurtured alongside Hiroshima's post-war reconstruction, this is the original house of Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. Founder Mitsuo Ise is credited by the house as 広島流お好み焼の生みの親 (the parent/originator of the Hiroshima style), and the shop carries the title 元祖 (originator) of the layered, savory pancake the city is now known for.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to heartfelt, hand-made preparation even amid the rush, inviting every guest to taste the genuine flavor of Hiroshima.
chef_bio.specialty: The original Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki: a thin wheat crepe layered with cabbage, pork, egg and noodles, finished with the house's signature thick sauce. The evening teppan menu also showcases grilled local Hiroshima oysters (牡蠣焼き) and offal (ホルモン・コウネ).

anecdotes.text: The flagship is said to grill up to 800 okonomiyaki in a single day, a testament to its standing as a city institution.
The house developed the thick, fruit-and-vegetable-based okonomiyaki sauce — an original blend Ise commissioned from Otafuku — that became a defining element of the Hiroshima style.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: みっちゃん総本店公式サイト states 昭和二十五年創業 (1950) and credits 「広島流お好み焼の生みの親 井畝満夫」 delivering 「元祖 広島流お好み焼」 born 「広島の復興とともに」; オタフクソース column and Wikipedia give the reading いせみつお, the soba-layered style and the ドロっとした濃厚なお好みソース Ise had Otafuku custom-make; the 八丁堀本店 page confirms up to 800 okonomiyaki/day, 牡蠣焼き and ホルモン・コウネ.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle where wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-based sauce are prepared together, making wheat cross-contact unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature okonomiyaki sauce is a wheat-based sauce; standard Japanese okonomiyaki/soy seasonings contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes contain egg, pork, fish-based dashi, oysters and offal, all cooked on the same shared teppan; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a famous, busy okonomiyaki institution where you sit at a teppan counter; staff are used to tourists and a few words of Japanese plus pointing at the menu will get you through comfortably, but expect lines and a fast-paced atmosphere rather than leisurely English service.

places.name: お好み焼き 越田 本店 (Okonomiyaki Koshida Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter (here made by kneading flour with chicken-bone stock) plus wheat noodles and a wheat/soy-based sauce. No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The okonomiyaki is layered with pork, egg, squid tempura bits and other animal ingredients, finished with a soy/Worcestershire-style sauce. No vegan dishes or accommodations are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 18:00-03:00; Sun closed. Also closed irregularly during Obon and New Year holidays.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1958, Koshida is a long-running family okonomiyaki shop in the lively Nagarekawa quarter of Naka-ku, a short walk east of the Peace Memorial Park. It has been run across generations and still cooks on the same griddle it has used since the founding days.

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1958 (Showa 33), Koshida is a long-running family okonomiyaki shop in the lively Nagarekawa quarter of Naka-ku, a short walk east of the Peace Memorial Park. It has been run across three generations (親子3代) and still cooks on the same griddle it has used since the founding days.
chef_bio.philosophy: Care begins with the batter: chicken bones are pounded and rested a day, then the stock is worked into the flour and kneaded thoroughly so the crepe holds together without tearing. Each okonomiyaki is finished one at a time by hand.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki finished soft and fluffy: the cabbage is slowly steamed rather than pressed down with the spatula after the flip, and the thick noodles are gently loosened by hand, giving a moist, airy (ふわっともちっと) result rather than a crisp one.

anecdotes.text: The shop opens only in the evening and keeps cooking until 3 a.m., making it a favorite late-night stop, and it often fills soon after opening.
Run across three generations since its 1958 founding, the shop is now past its 60-year mark and still cooks on its original founding-era griddle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and local listings give 昭和33年(1958)創業, 親子3代 across three generations, and 18:00–翌3:00 hours; るるぶ&more. describes the 鶏のガラを叩き1日寝かせて粉と練る batter, the gently-steamed cabbage and hand-loosened thick noodles for a ふわっともちっと finish, and the founding-era griddle still in use.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a single shared griddle (teppan) used continuously since founding, on which wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are cooked together, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a soy/Worcestershire-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are layered with pork, egg, squid tempura bits and noodles on a single shared griddle, so any item would have animal-ingredient cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling late-night counter shop where the cooking happens on the griddle in front of you; basic Japanese greetings and pointing at what others are eating will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: お好み焼き 八昌 (Okonomiyaki Hassho)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki specialist. The crepe-style base is made from wheat-flour batter and the dishes are built with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and finished with okonomiyaki sauce, all of which contain wheat/gluten. There is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation, so it is not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The signature and essentially only dishes are pork-and-egg okonomiyaki (そば肉玉 etc.) cooked on a shared griddle with egg, pork, bonito/fish-based dashi flavorings and okonomiyaki sauce. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Thursday–Sunday 10:00–17:00; closed Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday. Note: a daytime-only shop that may close earlier on a given day once ingredients run out.
places.neighborhood: Nobori-cho / Ginzan-cho, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This Nobori-cho okonomiyaki shop opened in May 2014, founded by Hiroki Ogawa, who had previously run the renowned Yagenbori Hassho before passing that flagship on to a successor and striking out to open his own place. It carries the lineage of the celebrated Hassho fami

chef_bio.background: This Nobori-cho (Ginzancho) okonomiyaki shop opened in May 2014, founded by Ogawa Hiroki (小川弘喜), who had previously run the renowned Yagenbori Hassho before handing that flagship to a disciple, opening an Itsukaichi branch, and finally striking out with his own daytime-only shop. It carries the 'Ao-Hassho' line of the celebrated Hassho family of Hiroshima okonomiyaki.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hewing to the exacting Hassho method and ingredient standards Ogawa learned in the original shop, prioritizing quality and consistency over volume in a small daytime-only operation that runs until the day's ingredients are gone.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, especially the soba-niku-tama (layered crepe, cabbage, pork, fried noodles and egg), prepared in the signature Hassho manner with a generous double egg yolk.

anecdotes.text: Ogawa earned his reputation cooking in the Hassho lineage and running Yagenbori Hassho before passing it on and opening this shop, making it a direct continuation of that pedigree rather than an offshoot run by a disciple.
The shop is so well regarded that a salaryman who ate here (later a chef himself) wrote that it struck him profoundly and stands among the very peaks of Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The tohoho-web genealogy of Hassho shops states proprietor Ogawa Hiroki (小川弘喜) opened this 幟町/銀山町 shop in May 2014 after handing his 薬研堀 八昌 to a disciple and running an Itsukaichi store; it places the shop in the 青八昌系 line. Tabelog confirms a May 8, 2014 opening, the double-yolk soba okonomiyaki, and Tabelog-100 selection. Hitosara carries chef Yamaguchi Kazuya's testimony that the shop is among Hiroshima's finest.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a wheat-containing okonomiyaki sauce, and the dishes use wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, so gluten is pervasive throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are cooked on a shared griddle with pork, egg and fish-based seasonings; there are no separate vegan preparations documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling counter-and-table okonomiyaki shop with no English menu noted and frequent queues, but ordering is simple (point to そば肉玉) and a few words of Japanese plus patience in line go a long way.

places.name: 麗ちゃん (Rei-chan)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki crepe batter is made from wheat flour, the dish includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or preparation. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is layered with pork, egg, and often seafood (squid, oysters), and the sauce/dashi typically contain fish. Eggs and animal products are core to the dish. No documented vegan options.
places.neighborhood: 広島駅・松原町（ekie 1F, Minami-ku）
places.notes: Founded in 1957 inside Hiroshima Station, Rei-chan is one of the city's longtime okonomiyaki institutions, with well over six decades of history serving Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki to locals and travelers passing through the station. It now sits on the first floor of ek

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1957 inside Hiroshima Station, Rei-chan is one of the city's longtime okonomiyaki institutions, with well over six decades of history serving Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki to locals and travelers passing through the station. Since 2018 it sits on the first floor of ekie, the shopping complex connected to Hiroshima Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop says it has kept an unchanged Hiroshima flavor by sticking to four fundamentals: the batter, the freshness of the vegetables, the quality of the noodles, and careful control of the griddle temperature. The batter is thoroughly mixed and rested before cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with cabbage, noodles, pork and egg cooked on the teppan; fresh Nagasaki squid is a signature ingredient.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1957 and now in its seventh decade, the shop has become one of Hiroshima's representative okonomiyaki destinations, frequently drawing queues.
Welcomes solo diners, an easy, unintimidating stop for travelers passing through the station.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (o-reichan.jp) confirms 昭和32年 (1957) founding, the relocation to ekie 1F on 2018-03-29, the four fundamentals (batter/vegetables/noodles/griddle temperature) and Nagasaki squid as a signature. The mj202108 article confirms the 1957 founding, the ekie location and that the shop is comfortable for solo diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles cooked on a shared communal teppan, so gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce used on Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki contains wheat; standard for this dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared teppan alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so vegan-safe preparation is not available.

cultural_comfort.note: As a busy station-side okonomiyaki landmark used to tourists, a foreign student can comfortably get by with simple Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu, though detailed allergy conversations in English may be limited.

places.name: 自然食カフェ シマカラ食堂 (Shizenshoku Cafe Shimakara Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant explicitly labels certain menu items gluten-free, including a vegan potato croquette set and a soy-meat "fried chicken" dish. It is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, and other menu items (e.g., dishes using regular soy sauce) contain wheat. There is no documented dedicated fryer or cross-contamination protocol, so celiacs should call ahead (082-236-9303). GF options exist and are marked, but verify per dish.
places.vegan_detail: A natural-food eatery with several clearly plant-based options: a vegan potato croquette set, soy-meat "fried chicken," tofu sets, and dishes built around tofu/soy meat with island-grown organic vegetables, citrus and wild plants. However, the menu is NOT fully vegan — it also serves fish (seared bonito/katsuo tataki), chicken (free-range heritage-breed fried chicken, tofu-chicken tsukune) and other animal products. Vegans can eat well here but must select the plant-based items specifically.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Sun: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30, drinks L.O. 14:59); Wed-Sat: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30), 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00); public holidays: 11:00-15:00. Closed Mondays (Tuesday if Monday is a public holiday). Note: the official site lists slightly different lunch/cafe/dinner time slots.
places.neighborhood: Nakajima-cho (central Hiroshima, just south of Peace Memorial Park, within walking distance)
places.notes: A small natural-food eatery and cafe that opened in July 2022 in Nakajima-cho, just south of Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park. It is run by an operator who also manages daycare facilities and barrier-free housing; noticing how widespread food allergies and dietary concer

chef_bio.background: A small natural-food eatery and cafe that opened in July 2022 in Nakajima-cho, just south of Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park. It is run by an operator that also manages daycare facilities and barrier-free housing; noticing how many people struggle with allergies and dietary concerns, they created the cafe to offer meals people can eat with confidence. An adjoining natural-food shop, 'yotte-ne,' sells traditional and natural products.
chef_bio.philosophy: Food that is 'kind to both heart and body' (心と体にやさしいごはん), built on two ideas: being able to eat with confidence and knowing who made your food. Seasonings are additive-free, the rice is pesticide-free hinohikari, and the oil is non-GMO cold-pressed rapeseed.
chef_bio.specialty: Natural-food set meals built around tofu and soy meat, plus seared bonito (katsuo tataki) and free-range chicken; vegan and gluten-free options including a vegan croquette set.

anecdotes.text: Much of the produce is grown by the cafe's own staff on Osaki-Kamijima island — fresh vegetables and even foraged wild edible plants — and the kitchen also uses soy sauce that has long been made on the island.
The cafe grew out of the operator's work in daycare and barrier-free housing; seeing how many people struggle with allergies, they opened a place serving natural food anyone can enjoy.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Pecomag confirms the July 2022 opening, the operator's daycare/barrier-free-housing background and allergy motivation, staff-grown Osaki-Kamijima produce, foraged wild plants and island soy sauce, plus the tofu/soy-meat/seasonal-fish sets. The official yotte-ne-shimakara.com states '心と体にやさしいごはん', pesticide-free hinohikari rice (from Shimane), non-GMO cold-pressed rapeseed oil, pasture-raised antibiotic-free chicken, additive-free seasonings, and explicitly lists vegan (ヴィーガンコロッケ定食) and gluten-free items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen uses traditional island-made soy sauce in many dishes; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so non-GF-labeled dishes should be assumed to contain wheat.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific cross-contamination protocols or allergen warnings are published; the cafe was founded partly to serve people with allergy concerns, and customers with allergies are advised to contact the restaurant directly at 082-236-9303.

positives.text: Specific menu items are explicitly labeled gluten-free, including a vegan potato croquette set and a soy-meat fried 'chicken' dish.
All seasonings are additive-free, rice is pesticide-free, oil is non-GMO cold-pressed rapeseed, and chicken is free-range and antibiotic-free.
Clearly identified plant-based options (vegan croquette, soy meat, tofu sets) make it straightforward for vegans to order safely.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, welcoming natural-food cafe used to a mix of visitors near the Peace Park; no English menu or English service is documented, so a foreign student may want a translation app or a few Japanese phrases to ask about vegan/gluten-free items.

places.name: ふじたや (Fujitaya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The single dish, anagomeshi, is conger eel glazed with a generations-old tare sauce that is soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat, served over rice. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The menu is essentially a single dish, anagomeshi (conger eel over rice), with a fish/dashi-based and wheat-soy tare. There are no documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (last order 16:00). Often sells out earlier during the tourist season. Closures are irregular (one secondary source mentioned Wednesday, but the official site states an irregular schedule).
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), within walking distance of Itsukushi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A century-plus family-run institution tucked into a quiet residential lane within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in the early 1900s as a kappo/kaiseki restaurant, but the reputation of its conger eel grew so strong that around 40 years ago it pared ev (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A century-plus family-run institution tucked into a quiet lane within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in 1910 (Meiji 43) as a kappo restaurant, but the reputation of its conger eel grew so strong that around 40 years ago it pared everything back to become an anago specialist.
chef_bio.philosophy: Restraint and respect for the ingredient. The menu is famously almost a single dish. The kitchen insists on wild-caught anago rather than farmed, favoring smaller 100-120g eels said to have the best flavor, and grills each portion over charcoal, applying the sauce only after the order is placed to keep it aromatic over the hot rice.
chef_bio.specialty: Anagomeshi: charcoal-grilled wild conger eel laid over a bed of Hiroshima-grown Koshihikari rice, finished with a fragrant house tare passed down through the generations.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen handles only wild conger eel, prizing smaller 100-120 gram fish said to taste best, grilled over charcoal and sauced only after the order comes in.
Started as a kappo house and narrowed to anago-only about 40 years ago once the eel became its calling card; the rice is prefectural Koshihikari dressed in a generations-old house sauce.
No reservations are taken and it often sells out in the tourist season, so queues form for what regulars consider Miyajima's benchmark bowl of anagomeshi.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Crea (Bunshun) states Fujitaya was founded in Meiji 43 (1910), began as a 割烹 (kappo) and became an anago specialty about 40 years ago; it confirms 天然穴子 (wild anago), 県産コシヒカリ, and an inherited special tare, and notes a Michelin star with essentially a single dish. Hatsukaichi-Concierge confirms wild-only 1匹100〜120g eels, charcoal grilling with sauce applied after the order, 予約不可, and tourist-season sell-outs. The official site confirms hours and 予約不可.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dish is glazed with a house tare/sauce that is soy-sauce based, which conventionally contains wheat; no wheat-free version is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around conger eel and a fish/dashi-and-soy sauce; the rice itself is dressed with this sauce, so it is not suitable for vegans and there is no documented plant-based dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous but small family-run counter where no reservations are taken and you queue with everyone else, so a friendly greeting and patience go a long way; the menu is essentially one dish, which keeps ordering simple even with limited Japanese.

places.name: いな忠 (Inachu)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free restaurant. The signature anago-meshi is grilled eel over rice glazed with a sweet tare (basting sauce), which is made from soy sauce and almost always contains wheat; tempura/fried items use wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or protocol is documented. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly and should not assume the tare is safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around conger eel (anago) and Miyajima oysters; the house rice is even cooked in a dashi made from eel bones, head and kombu, and dishes are finished with wheat-based soy sauce tare. There are no documented plant-based mains.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Fri: 10:30–15:00. Sat, Sun, holidays: 10:30–15:30. Closed Thursdays (open daily in November).
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (Itsukushima Shrine area
places.notes: [Miyajima] いな忠 sits midway along Miyajima's bustling Omotesando shopping street, within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine, and has been serving the island for over a century. It is remembered locally as the shop that first sold anago-meshi (eel rice) as a takeaway bento on Mi

roles: Independent long-established anago specialist on Miyajima
chef_bio.background: いな忠 sits midway along Miyajima's bustling Omotesando shopping street, a few minutes' walk from the ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine, and has been serving the island for over a century. It is remembered locally as the shop that first sold anago-meshi as a takeaway bento on Miyajima, helping turn the dish into one of the island's signature foods.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on the quality of the eel and the grilling technique, letting the fish's natural umami carry the dish rather than masking it with a heavy sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-meshi — grilled conger eel over rice cooked in a broth made from eel heads, bones and kombu, giving the rice a deeper savory flavor than a plain eel donburi. The eel is grilled crisp and fragrant outside while staying soft inside.

anecdotes.text: Said locally to be the first shop on Miyajima to sell anago-meshi as a takeaway bento, an idea that spread to become an island specialty.
Passers-by can watch the eel being grilled right at the storefront before it is served over broth-cooked rice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Yahoo News expert article confirms いな忠 has over 100 years of history, is mid-Omotesando about 5 minutes from the pier, grills anago at the storefront, and serves anago-meshi (¥2,300) over rice cooked in a dashi of eel heads, bones and kombu — crisp outside, tender inside. Miyajima local press (miyajima-villa, miyajima-komachi), surfaced via web search, describe いな忠 as the first shop on Miyajima to sell anago-meshi as a takeaway bento; the claim is kept hedged as the pages are JS-rendered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-meshi is finished with a sweet soy-based tare, and Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat, so the dish should be treated as containing gluten unless the kitchen confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house rice is cooked in a dashi made from eel head, bones and kombu, so even the rice is not vegetarian.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in tourist-friendly eatery on Miyajima's main shopping street; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the eel-rice dish will get you through, but bring cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: 芝居茶寮 水羽 (Shibai Charyo Mizuha)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes that typically use wheat: conger eel and oyster preparations glazed or seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce, fried oysters (kaki fry) in wheat-flour batter, zaru soba (buckwheat noodles usually cut with wheat), and matcha desserts. Travelers requiring strict gluten avoidance should not assume safe options here.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused restaurant; signature dishes are conger eel (anago) and Miyajima oysters, and broths and seasonings rely on fish and wheat-based soy sauce. No documented vegan or vegetarian set. Not suitable for vegans; vegetarians would find essentially nothing animal-free confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (some sources list 11:00-17:00); irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Around Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Mizuha occupies a restored Edo-period Miyajima townhouse on the lane near Itsukushima Shrine. The site once held a traditional kabuki playhouse, and that theatrical heritage is the source of the name 'Shibai Charyo' (Theater Tea Pavilion). The renovation kept the buildi

chef_bio.background: Mizuha occupies a restored Edo-period Miyajima townhouse on the lane near Itsukushima Shrine. The site once held a traditional kabuki playhouse, and that theatrical heritage is the source of the name 'Shibai Charyo' (Theater Tea Pavilion). The renovation kept the building's original character: exposed wooden beams and posts, an open earthen-floor doma with tables at the front, tatami rooms tucked at the rear and a Japanese-style room upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes authentic local flavors and homemade cooking, sourcing Miyajima-grown oysters landed that same morning and serving them the same day, while aiming to make guests feel as relaxed as at home within the historic space.
chef_bio.specialty: Miyajima oysters and conger eel (anago) — including anago rice bowls, oyster donburi, the popular fried-oyster set served with two house sauces, and the seasonal 'Sanpo-don' three-treasure bowl offered roughly November to May.

anecdotes.text: The earthen-floor doma stays cool in summer and is warmed by braziers (hibachi) in winter, a traditional touch carried over from the old townhouse.
Oysters are bought from that day's Miyajima harvest and put on the menu the same day.
It is part of a small local family of businesses alongside the anago-don shop Ruju and the inn Tabiiro no Oyado Mizuhaso.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabiiro confirms Mizuha is a renovated Edo-period townhouse with exposed beams and a brazier-warmed doma, that oysters are landed and served the same day, and that sister businesses are the anago-don shop Ruju and the inn Tabiiro no Oyado Mizuhaso. The note.com visit report confirms the former-kabuki-theater site, the doma/tatami/upstairs layout ('夏は涼しく、冬は火鉢'), the fried-oyster set (牡蠣フライ御膳) with two sauces, and the Nov-May 三宝丼. The official mizuhaso.co.jp page was unreachable (TLS cert mismatch).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature anago and oyster dishes are seasoned/glazed in the Japanese style, which uses wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tourist-friendly Miyajima spot one minute from the shrine exit that regularly welcomes foreign visitors and offers floor chairs (zaisu) for those uncomfortable with tatami seating, so a foreign student can dine here comfortably.

places.name: 新天地 みっちゃん (Shintenchi Micchan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe layer and steamed wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing sauce is wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free option or dedicated preparation here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki uses egg, pork, and typically squid/shrimp/fish flakes (katsuobushi), with the standard wheat-and-fish-derived savory sauce. No documented vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: Tue & Thu 17:00-21:00 / Wed & Fri 11:00-14:00, 17:00-21:00 / Sat 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00 / Sun & holidays 11:00-15:00, 17:00-20:30 / closed Mon (next day off if a public holiday). Hours vary slightly by source; confirm by phone.
places.neighborhood: Ebisu-cho / Shintenchi (near Hatchobori Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 新天地 みっちゃん carries the original 'Micchan' noren, the curtain that has hung over Hiroshima okonomiyaki since the very beginning. The story starts around 1950, when founder Isoe Isao set up a one-sen yoshoku street stall in central Hiroshima as the city rebuilt from the wa

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima City, Chuo Ward (central Hiroshima, in the Shintenchi entertainment district)
chef_bio.background: 新天地 みっちゃん carries the original 'Micchan' noren, the curtain associated with Hiroshima okonomiyaki from the very beginning. The story starts around 1950, when founder Isoe Isao (井畝井三男) set up a street stall called Mikasaya (美笠屋) on Hiroshima's Chuo-dori as the city rebuilt from the war. His son Isoe Mitsuo (井畝満夫) renamed it 'Micchan' in 1953, after his own childhood nickname. As the name spread to roughly two thousand shops across the prefecture, this Shintenchi shop kept the founder's legitimate line: the original noren was passed directly within the family to the second daughter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the postwar Showa-era recipe and griddle technique that began with the founder's street stall, including the long-standing 'Okonomiyaki Special.'
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered on a thin wheat crepe with cabbage, pork, egg and noodles, finished on the iron griddle with a house sauce refined over decades.

anecdotes.text: Regarded as a birthplace of Micchan okonomiyaki, the shop traces its line from founder Isoe Isao's 1950 street stall through postwar reconstruction, with the original noren handed down directly within the founding family.
Inside there are several four-person tables plus a semi-private table in the back; table seats get dishes plated, while the counter is best for eating straight off the griddle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese Wikipedia states founder 井畝井三男 started a yatai named 美笠屋 on 中央通り around 1950 (Showa 25); his eldest son 井畝満夫 renamed it みっちゃん in 1953 (Showa 28) from his childhood nickname; the prefecture has roughly 2,000 Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shops, and the original noren was passed directly (直接譲渡) to the second daughter, whose line and the いせや line operate independently. Gnavi confirms 新天地みっちゃん as the shop carrying the founder's legitimate lineage (創業者からの正当な流れ) with ~25 seats in Shintenchi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish is assembled on a shared iron griddle from a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles; flour and gluten are pervasive throughout the cooking surface, so cross-contamination is unavoidable for a gluten-sensitive diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki is finished with a standard wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce (not gluten-free).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg, squid/shrimp and bonito-style toppings are standard on the shared griddle, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, no-frills neighborhood okonomiyaki shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through; English is limited, so a few Japanese words help.

places.name: 廣島つけ麺本舗 ばくだん屋 本店 (Hiroshima Tsukemen Honpo Bakudanya Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is tsukemen built on wheat noodles, and the signature 和風ダレ (Japanese-style dipping sauce) is soy-sauce based (wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Although the dish is vegetable-forward (cabbage, cucumber, green onion), the wheat noodles and the soy-sauce/dashi-based spicy sauce are not plant-only, and no vegan option is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 17:00-21:30 (Wed closed). Official site lists lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30), closed Wednesdays — hours vary slightly by source.
places.neighborhood: 新天地・八丁堀 (Shintenchi / Hatchobori), central Hiroshima
places.notes: Bakudanya was founded in 2000 and helped popularize Hiroshima-style tsukemen, a local cold-noodle dish that traces back to a 1950s Hiroshima shop. The chain grew from its first location to several branches across the city, with the Honten anchoring its presence in centr

chef_bio.background: Bakudanya was founded in 2000 and helped popularize Hiroshima-style tsukemen, a local cold-noodle dish that traces back to a 1950s Hiroshima shop. The chain grew from its first location to roughly eight branches across the city, with the Honten anchoring its presence in central Hiroshima's Shintenchi entertainment district.
chef_bio.philosophy: The signature spicy sauce is adjustable from no heat up to extreme levels (0 to 10 and beyond), letting each diner tune the dish while the underlying umami and sweetness remain. The generous vegetables are part of its appeal as a lighter, vegetable-heavy bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima tsukemen: chilled noodles piled with boiled cabbage, cucumber and plenty of green onion, eaten by dipping into a spicy Japanese-style sesame sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop has built a following of repeat customers who describe the spiciness as addictive, and it drew wider visibility after being featured on a popular Japanese YouTube channel.
Hiroshima tsukemen as a style dates back to the 1950s in the city; Bakudanya opened in 2000 and became one of the names most associated with spreading the dish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms the Shintenchi Honten among ~8 Hiroshima branches, the cold noodles with boiled cabbage/cucumber/green onion and spicy sesame dipping sauce, the 0-to-extreme spice scale, addictive-spice repeat customers, and a feature on the YouTube channel エガちゃんねる. 1goten states Bakudanya was founded in 2000 and that Hiroshima tsukemen originated around 1954 (first called reimen), with a 0-to-10+ spice scale.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is wheat-noodle tsukemen prepared in a shared noodle kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature spicy dipping sauce is a Japanese-style (wadafu) sauce, which conventionally uses wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free version is mentioned.

positives.text: The bowl is notably vegetable-forward (cabbage, cucumber, green onion) and the spice level can be set to zero, so the heat itself is fully adjustable.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, cash-only noodle shop where you order at the counter and can request your spice level from 0 up; basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu are enough, though staff may have limited English.

noodles.notes: Cold, smooth, glossy straight noodles with a clean throat-feel, served chilled and dunked into the cold spicy dipping sauce in the classic Hiroshima tsukemen manner.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled straight noodles plus boiled vegetables served with a cold, fiery shoyu-and-seafood dipping sauce; choose your heat from level 0 up to 20 (level 2 is the house standard).
ramen.chashu: Thin slices of tender, pale-pink braised pork laid over the cold noodles and vegetables.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a wheat-based shoyu dipping sauce make this unsafe for celiacs; no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on small-fish/seafood dashi and is finished with pork chashu; no plant-only broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is the shop credited with popularizing Hiroshima-style tsukemen: a cold, blazing red dipping sauce where small-fish and seafood dashi carries soy umami, rice vinegar tang, sesame and an adjustable wall of chili heat. You taste the city's lineage in that cool, vinegared, capsaicin-forward sauce — a refreshing-yet-addictive counterpoint to the hot, fatty tsukemen of the rest of Japan.

places.highlight: This is the shop credited with popularizing Hiroshima-style tsukemen: a cold, blazing red dipping sauce where small-fish and seafood dashi carries soy umami, rice vinegar tang, sesame and an adjustable wall of chili heat. You taste the city's lineage in that cool, vinegared, capsaicin-forward sauce — a refreshing-yet-addictive counterpoint to the hot, fatty tsukemen of the rest of Japan.

places.name: 穴子と牡蠣 まめたぬき (Anago to Kaki Mametanuki)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The core menu is steamed anago rice and fried Miyajima oysters; the fry batter is wheat-based, and the anago and rice are seasoned with wheat-soy-sauce-based tare. Dishes are dressed with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented gluten-free protocol. A celiac traveler should treat this as unsafe unless they confirm specific dishes directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians as a destination. The entire concept centers on conger eel (anago) and oysters; even side dishes and the rice are cooked with fish-derived dashi and tare. No documented vegan or vegetarian set is offered. A vegan would have essentially nothing safe to order here.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00–20:30 (L.O. 20:00). No regular weekly closed day; sporadic maintenance/holiday closures announced on the official site (e.g., Jun 22–26, Jul 14–15, Oct 6–7, Dec 14–18, with extended 11:00–19:00 hours Jun 27–Jul 5).
places.neighborhood: 宮島 表参道商店街 (Miyajima Omotesando, near Itsukushima Shrine)
places.cuisine: Anago-meshi (conger eel rice) & Miyajima oyster dishes
places.notes: [Miyajima] Mametanuki sits in the middle of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the ferry pier and Itsukushima Shrine. It is directly operated by Kinsuikan, a Miyajima inn founded in 1902, and grew out of that century-plus hospitality tradition into a casual r (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

roles: Restaurant directly operated by the Kinsuikan inn (錦水館直営)
chef_bio.origin: 宮島 (Miyajima), Hiroshima — operated by the Kinsuikan inn established in 1902
chef_bio.background: Mametanuki sits in the middle of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the ferry pier and Itsukushima Shrine. It is directly operated by Kinsuikan, a Miyajima inn founded in 1902, and grew out of that century-plus hospitality tradition into a casual restaurant where day-trippers can taste the island's signature dishes.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to express the seasonal shifts of the Seto Inland Sea through local ingredients, leaning on cooking techniques carried over from the parent inn's chefs and sourcing Hiroshima-grown produce, with local sake offered for pairing.
chef_bio.specialty: Steamed-and-baked anago rice served in a ceramic box: two whole simmered conger eels are layered over rice, steamed at high heat so the box stays piping hot through the meal, then finished with a torch sear for aroma. The most popular order pairs this anago rice with crisp fried Miyajima oysters.

anecdotes.text: The anago rice is steamed inside a ceramic box rather than grilled, a method meant to keep the eel meltingly soft and the dish hot from first bite to last.
Reservations are deliberately not taken; instead guests join a waiting list and explore the shopping street until their turn, reflecting its role as a steady-stream stop for shrine-bound visitors.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms Mametanuki is operated by Kinsuikan (established 1902), on Omotesando near Itsukushima Shrine, with signature ceramic-box anago rice using two whole simmered eels steamed at high heat and torch-seared, the anago + fried-oyster set as most popular, Seto-Inland-Sea sourcing and local Hiroshima sake. Tabelog confirms it is inside the Kinsuikan ryokan, uses contract-farmer Kuwata rice, takes no reservations (20 counter seats) and operates a waitlist system.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters are a core menu item, implying a shared fryer; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago rice and other dishes are seasoned with wheat-soy-sauce-based tare and standard Japanese soy sauce, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes are built around conger eel and oysters with fish-derived dashi/tare; no vegan or vegetarian preparation line is described.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy information is noted as available, but no specific gluten-free or vegan handling protocol is published; guests would need to ask staff directly.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, tourist-friendly restaurant on Miyajima's main shopping street with an English menu and walk-in ticket-machine seating, so a foreign student can dine here comfortably without Japanese.

places.name: ONE EARTH & lovefamcook（ワンアース&ラブファムクック） (ONE EARTH & lovefamcook)
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, but the rice-based dishes (curry over 3-grain brown rice, burrito with sautéed brown rice) are naturally suited to gluten-free diners, and at least one reviewer reported staff making a deliberate effort to prepare a gluten-free meal on request. The sandwich uses grain bread (contains wheat). No published information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination control, so a strict celiac should explain their needs clearly and ask before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A vegan-friendly organic cafe (not fully vegan — some animal-protein dishes are also served). There is a clearly defined plant-based lineup: an organic Moringa vegan curry plate, a vegetable burrito/sandwich made with multi-grain organic bread, power/protein plates, organic acai bowls, and organic smoothies (the Green and Purple smoothies and the core vegan dishes are confirmed vegan). The owner himself eats a vegan diet, and reviewers consistently confirm genuine, knowledgeable vegan options. Tell staff you want ヴィーガン (vegan) and they will steer you to the plant-based items.
places.hours_raw: Wed 09:00-17:00; Thu-Sun 11:30-17:00; Closed Mon & Tue (per Tabelog). Other listings show hours running later in the day (to ~19:00); confirm same-day via Instagram @oneearth_jp.
places.neighborhood: Mikawa-cho, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (near Hatchobori / Hiroshima PARCO)
places.notes: ONE EARTH grew out of founder Masayuki Kaminomae's years studying dance in California, where eating organic food was simply part of daily life. After returning to Japan he found his health suffering from a diet heavy in additives, and that experience led him to open an

chef_bio.origin: California (where the founder lived) and Hiroshima, Japan
chef_bio.background: ONE EARTH grew out of founder Masayuki Kaminomae's years studying dance in California, where eating organic food was simply part of daily life. After returning to Japan he found his health suffering from a diet heavy in additives, and that experience led him to open an organic cafe in central Hiroshima centered on additive-free, pesticide-free, organically grown ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding theme is '口から食べるもので未来を考える' — thinking about the future through what we put in our mouths — pairing deliciousness with health.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic plant-forward cafe food — the organic vegan curry plate, vegetable burritos, colorful all-vegan smoothies (the acai-based 'Purple' is most popular), and an acai bowl are signatures.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is tied to its own organic farm in nearby Yuki-cho, where the staff themselves take part in growing the pesticide-free vegetables served in the dishes.
The owner eats a vegan diet himself and is repeatedly described by visitors as genuinely passionate and 'super friendly,' often chatting with guests over the food.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese-heart confirms founder Masayuki Kaminomae, his California dance study and the organic-food habit there, his post-return health decline, the theme '口から食べるもので未来を考える', the vegan curry and burritos, five all-vegan smoothies (acai 'Purple' most popular), and the Yuki-cho (湯来町) organic farm where staff do farm shifts. HappyCow reviews confirm the owner is vegan, passionate and 'super friendly,' with clearly marked vegan items, acai bowls, smoothies and daily vegan soups.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff made a great effort to prepare a gluten-free meal for a diner's friend on request.
Listed as offering vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, macrobiotic and oriental-vegetarian menus, with an organic and allergy-aware focus.

positives.text: Owner and staff are repeatedly praised as super friendly and accommodating, and the owner is himself vegan, so plant-based requests are understood and taken seriously.
Strong organic, additive-free and pesticide-free sourcing, with vegetables from the cafe's own farm.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner and staff are warmly welcoming to foreigners, the owner speaks English (staff also use translation apps when needed), and an English menu is available, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about ingredients.

places.name: 中華そば くにまつ（國松） (Chuka Soba Kunimatsu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is wheat ramen noodles served with a soy-sauce and miso based dandan seasoning, both of which contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Dandan noodles here are built on minced pork and a meat/soy-sauce-miso base; egg toppings (onsen tamago) are offered. No vegan option is documented.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:00, closed Sat & Sun
places.neighborhood: 中区八丁堀（本通・平和記念公園至近 / central Hiroshima, Hatchobori-Tatemachi）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kunimatsu opened in central Hiroshima in December 2009 as a specialist in brothless dandan noodles (shiru-nashi tantanmen), a Hiroshima signature inspired by the dandan noodles of China's Sichuan province. The owners reworked the Sichuan original for local palates, buil

chef_bio.background: Kunimatsu opened in central Hiroshima in December 2009 as a specialist in brothless dandan noodles (shiru-nashi tantanmen), a Hiroshima signature inspired by the dandan noodles of China's Sichuan province. The owners reworked the Sichuan original for local palates, building the sauce around Japanese soy sauce and miso, and arrived at the recipe through repeated trial and error. From a tiny counter shop the name grew into one of the city's best-known brothless-dandan addresses.
chef_bio.philosophy: Their stated approach is hayai, yasui, narubeku umai — fast, affordable, and as delicious as possible: noodles boil in about 70 seconds and a bowl can reach the counter roughly two minutes after ordering.
chef_bio.specialty: Brothless dandan noodles (汁なし担担麺) with house-made thin noodles, chili oil, and the numbing tingle of Sichuan pepper, served with a selectable spice level from 0 to 4

anecdotes.text: The recipe was reached through repeated experimentation, adapting Sichuan dandan noodles to Japanese tastes with soy sauce and miso.
Mix the noodles thoroughly with the sauce, chili oil and Sichuan pepper, eat, then finish by adding a half-portion of rice to the remaining sauce in the bowl.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site states Kunimatsu opened in December 2009 as a 汁なし担担麺 specialist inspired by Sichuan, reworked for Japanese palates with soy and miso through 試行錯誤, with the motto '早い。安い。なるべく美味い。', ~70-second noodles / ~2-minute bowls, and spice levels 0-4. The menu page confirms mixing the noodles with tare, chili oil and Sichuan pepper and finishing with a half-portion of rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dandan sauce is based on soy sauce and miso, which are wheat-containing; the noodles are wheat ramen noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dish is built on minced pork with egg toppings available, so vegan diners cannot be safely accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast, casual, cash-only noodle counter where you order a single signature dish and pick a spice level 0-4, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will carry you through even with little Japanese.

noodles.notes: House-made medium-to-slightly-thin noodle developed specifically for shiru-nashi (broth-less) style; springy, chewy mochi-mochi bite that boils in about 70 seconds. Built to grip the rayu-and-sesame sauce when tossed.

ramen.signature_bowl: 汁なし担担麺 (shiru-nashi tantanmen) — the broth-less dandan noodles. Choose a spice level (mild 0 up to 4-kara, or the extra-hot KUNIMAX). Toss vigorously until the sauce coats every strand, then eat; many finish by mixing the spicy dregs into a half-portion of rice.
notable_toppings: seasoned ground pork (jajan, sweetened with tianmianjiang)

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a soy-sauce-based tare; both contain gluten. No rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The sauce carries warm chicken broth and the bowl is topped with seasoned ground pork; no documented plant-based version.

ramen.pantheon: This is the bowl that helped turn Hiroshima's brothless dandan noodles into a citywide craze: Sichuan-born shiru-nashi tantanmen retuned for the Japanese palate, where a soy-and-vinegar tare, mellow ground sesame and a five-spice house rayu coat springy noodles, finished with a bracing dusting of Sichuan pepper. You taste the lineage not in a soup but in the tossed sauce — the signature Hiroshima dance of chili heat (辛) and tongue-tingling numbness (痺れ).

places.highlight: This is the bowl that helped turn Hiroshima's brothless dandan noodles into a citywide craze: Sichuan-born shiru-nashi tantanmen retuned for the Japanese palate, where a soy-and-vinegar tare, mellow ground sesame and a five-spice house rayu coat springy noodles, finished with a bracing dusting of Sichuan pepper. You taste the lineage not in a soup but in the tossed sauce — the signature Hiroshima dance of chili heat (辛) and tongue-tingling numbness (痺れ).

places.name: 喫茶さえき (Kissa Saeki (Organic cafe & kitchen))
places.gf_detail: Gluten-free options are clearly marked on the menu, and some sets can be made gluten-free (e.g. by omitting a sauce). The Enzyme Brown Rice Ball Set is labeled both vegan and gluten-free, and gluten-free sweets are made in-house. However, the cafe is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen — wheat-based items (e.g. a wheat-gluten cutlet sandwich, scones, muffins) are made in the same space, and there is no documented dedicated fryer or formal cross-contamination protocol. Celiac travelers should confirm directly. Note that ordinary soy sauce contains wheat; the source material does not confirm whether a GF tamari is used in GF dishes, so ask.
places.vegan_detail: Strongly vegan-friendly: nearly all dishes are vegan, with only a small handful using chicken (a karaage / fried-chicken set made from open-air-raised Yamaguchi poultry). Signature vegan dishes include the lentil hamburger and millet croquette sets served with enzyme-fermented brown rice, plus vegan sweets (raw hemp chocolate tart, vegan muffins and scones). Vegetables are organic and locally grown in Hiroshima; no chemical seasonings or GMO foods are used. Because the kitchen also prepares the chicken item, strict vegans may wish to confirm cross-contact, though the menu is built around plant-based dishes.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (per Tabelog). Some other listings note 10:00-18:00 with last order 17:30. Closed 2nd & 4th Sundays and the 4th Monday.
places.neighborhood: 中区紙屋町（紙屋町東）／ Kamiyacho, central Hiroshima, near the A-Bomb D
places.notes: Kissa Saeki sits on a corner of central Hiroshima just minutes from the Peace Memorial Park. The site has deep family roots: the founders' grandfather once ran a public bathhouse here, and in 1977 their father opened a coffee shop in its place. In 2013 brothers Masashi

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, Japan — a multi-generational family business in the Kamiyacho district.
chef_bio.background: Kissa Saeki sits on a corner of central Hiroshima just minutes from the Peace Memorial Park. The site has deep family roots: the founders' grandfather once ran a public bathhouse here, and in 1977 their father opened a coffee shop in its place. In 2013 brothers Masashi and Naotoshi took over and reimagined the cafe around organic, plant-based food while keeping the warm, retro kissaten atmosphere their father created.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners describe their work as cooking that considers food and people's lives, prizing food that is genuinely good for you and not merely tasty ('口にするものはおいしいだけではいけない、本当の意味で健康になれるものでありたい') — sourcing organic Hiroshima vegetables grown without agrochemicals, avoiding chemical seasonings and GMOs, and extending that care even to eco-friendly cleaning products.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based home cooking built around enzyme-fermented brown rice (brown rice cooked with adzuki beans and salt, then rested several days for a chewy texture). The signature lentil hamburger and millet croquette sets are favorites, alongside organic fair-trade coffee and house-made vegan sweets.

anecdotes.text: The cafe stands where the family's grandfather once ran a bathhouse; the father converted it into a coffee shop in 1977, and the two sons turned it organic in 2013.
Vegetables are 100% Hiroshima-grown and the kitchen uses non-GMO-fed Yamaguchi chicken and fair-trade coffee, reflecting a farm-to-table, environmentally conscious ethos.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Orulab confirms Kissa Saeki is run by brothers Masashi and Naotoshi Saeki, that the site was the grandfather's bathhouse, the father opened a coffee shop in 1977, and the brothers made it organic in 2013; it confirms the lentil hamburger, enzyme brown rice (酵素玄米), free-range non-GMO Yamaguchi chicken karaage, 100%-Hiroshima vegetables, fair-trade coffee and the health-first philosophy. GetHiroshima confirms the three-generation history, enzyme brown rice, lentil hamburg (¥1,150), millet croquettes (¥990), avoidance of chemical seasonings, and vegan sweets (raw hemp chocolate tart, muffins, scones).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gluten-free items are marked on the menu and some dishes can be made gluten-free (e.g. by removing a sauce), but the kitchen also prepares wheat-based items so it is not a dedicated gluten-free environment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Almost all dishes are vegan-friendly except a small number that use chicken; vegan sweets and sets are clearly labeled.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe explicitly welcomes diners with food allergies, vegetarians/vegans, and those with religious dietary restrictions, describing its approach as 'food barrier-free' dining.

positives.text: Menus are in English and Japanese with clear vegan and gluten-free icons, and items like the Enzyme Brown Rice Ball Set are labeled both vegan and gluten-free.
Organic Hiroshima-grown vegetables, no chemical seasonings, and no genetically modified foods are used.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available and dishes are clearly marked for vegan and gluten-free diets, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about ingredients.

places.name: 焼がきのはやし (Yakigaki no Hayashi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional oyster specialty restaurant; oysters are typically grilled and served with soy-sauce-based seasonings and ponzu, and the menu includes deep-fried oysters (kaki fry) which use wheat batter. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or labeling is mentioned on any source. Travelers requiring strict gluten-free should not assume any dish is safe here.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster specialty restaurant; nearly every dish centers on oysters (grilled oysters, raw oysters, fried oysters, oyster rice). Seasonings used (soy sauce, ponzu, broths) are not vegan and oysters are animal products. No vegan menu or options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-17:00 (Last order 16:30). Closed Wednesday and Thursday. Hours may change seasonally.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street, near Itsukushima Shrine
places.cuisine: Grilled and raw oysters / oyster cuisine
places.notes: [Miyajima] Founded in 1948 on Miyajima, Yakigaki no Hayashi is widely regarded as the birthplace of grilled oysters in Japan, said to be the first shop to serve cooked grilled oysters to customers. The founder's grandfather wanted to do something no one else was doing and began of

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1948 on Miyajima, Yakigaki no Hayashi is widely regarded as the birthplace of grilled oysters in Japan, said to be the first shop to serve cooked grilled oysters to customers. The founder's grandfather, who ran what was then Hayashi Shokudo (Hayashi Food Hall), wanted to do something no one else was doing and began offering grilled oysters year-round, and the shop was also an early pioneer in serving raw oysters at a time when raw oysters were not popular in Japan. It sits in roughly the middle of the Omotesando shopping street, about an eight-minute walk from Miyajima port.
chef_bio.philosophy: The family aims to preserve the original taste of the birthplace shop, grilling oysters fresh after each order using techniques handed down since founding, and serving only premium oysters.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal/steam-grilled oysters cooked fresh to order, plus year-round raw oysters and oyster rice (kaki-meshi)

anecdotes.text: The founder's grandfather, wanting to do something no one else was doing, began offering grilled oysters year-round and the shop became known as the birthplace of grilled oysters.

They serve specially ordered Jigozen-brand three-year-old oysters, prized for their large, rich meat, cultivated in the stable, high-salinity waters near Daikoku Island; raw oysters are offered year-round.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site and tabiiro confirm 1948 founding, 'first to serve grilled oysters,' the grandfather's Hayashi Shokudo origin, year-round grilled and raw oysters, and specially ordered Jigozen-brand three-year-old oysters from waters near Daikoku Island.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oyster dishes are seasoned in the traditional Japanese style (soy sauce, ponzu), which normally contains wheat; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is an oyster specialty kitchen; effectively all dishes are oyster/seafood based, so there is no separation suitable for vegan diners.

positives.text: The shop has reportedly conducted norovirus testing on its oysters for about ten years with zero detections, indicating attention to oyster food safety (note: this concerns shellfish safety, not allergen handling).

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, tourist-friendly oyster shop on Miyajima's main shopping street with picture menus and counter/table seating, where a foreign student can comfortably order with basic phrases and pointing; no reservation is taken so just walk up.

places.name: 牡蠣屋 (Kakiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on oysters served with Japanese seasonings; oyster rice, fried oysters (breaded), and miso soup typically involve wheat-based soy sauce, dashi, and flour, so the food should not be assumed gluten-free. No dedicated fryer or allergy handling is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster-only specialty restaurant; essentially every dish is built around oysters (shellfish), and accompaniments use fish dashi and soy sauce. There are no documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily; hours vary by season and the shop closes once oysters sell out. (Some listings show 11:00-17:00 on weekdays/weekends; raw oysters served roughly November-March only.)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping arcade, Itsukushima, near Itsuk
places.notes: [Miyajima] Kakiya is Miyajima's oyster-specialist restaurant, started by a craftsman with some forty years of experience grilling oysters on the island. It sits in the middle of the lively Omotesando arcade between the ferry pier and Itsukushima Shrine, with a white lantern and an

chef_bio.background: Kakiya is Miyajima's oyster-specialist restaurant, started by a craftsman with some forty years of experience grilling oysters on the island. It sits in the middle of the lively Omotesando arcade between the ferry pier and Itsukushima Shrine, with a white lantern and an open grill of sizzling oysters at the storefront as its landmark.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house motto is to compete 'with oysters alone' - serving nothing but oysters and relentlessly pursuing what makes a single oyster truly delicious.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-style grilled oysters seared over an intentionally fierce flame to lock in the umami, plus a full oyster lineup: fried oysters, oyster rice, oil-marinated oysters, and oyster miso soup, often served as the Kakiya set meal.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen sources oysters directly from long-trusted growers, handling only top-quality shellfish for richness and freshness that rarely reaches the open market.
The signature grilled oysters are cooked over an unusually strong fire to sear them fast, leaving the surface aromatic and the inside surprisingly juicy.
Beyond oysters, the house pairs them with local touches like Hiroshima lemon cocktails, wine, and whisky.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site and tabiiro confirm Miyajima's only oyster-specialty shop, a craftsman with ~40 years of oyster-grilling experience, the white-lantern open-grill storefront, sear-over-strong-flame method, the oyster-only menu and Kakiya set, and direct sourcing from trusted growers.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oyster dishes are dressed with standard Japanese seasonings and the menu includes fried (breaded) oysters and oyster rice, which typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce, flour, and dashi; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish is built around oysters (shellfish) cooked on a shared grill and fryer, so the kitchen is not suitable for vegan diners and there are no plant-only options.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, tourist-friendly spot on Miyajima's main arcade where you order at a counter and can point at the grilled-oyster display, so basic greetings and gestures are enough even with limited Japanese.

places.name: お好み焼 長田屋 (Okonomiyaki Nagata-ya)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered noodles (soba/udon), so the core dish is not gluten-free, and the house okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol here. A gluten-sensitive diner cannot eat the standard or vegan okonomiyaki safely; only ask if you have a mild preference rather than medical celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-egg okonomiyaki house that has built genuine, dedicated vegan and vegetarian okonomiyaki onto its menu, available on request. The plant-based versions replace the usual pork and egg with vegetables and use a special animal-free sauce. Documented vegan builds include one with mochi, corn, garlic chips and green onion over soba (around 1,350 yen), a shiso-and-green-onion-over-soba version (around 990 yen), and a plain soba version (around 750 yen). English menus are available. Note the noodles and batter are still wheat-based, and the griddle is shared with meat/seafood okonomiyaki, so this is a good vegan option but not a fully vegan kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (Tabelog); listed elsewhere up to 20:30. Closed Tuesdays; irregular additional closures. Golden Week open with substitute closure.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (in front of Peace Memorial Park / Atomic Bomb Dome)
places.notes: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki house standing just seconds from the Peace Memorial Park, where the griddles run all day in a room styled after 1930s Showa-era Japan. Though younger than some of the city's decades-old institutions, it has grown into one of the area's busi

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki house standing a minute's walk from the Peace Memorial Park, where the griddles run all day in a room built around the look of a mid-Showa (1950s-60s) building. Though younger than some of the city's decades-old institutions, it has grown into one of the area's busiest okonomiyaki destinations, with lines forming well before lunch.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames its okonomiyaki as soul food for people of all ages and nationalities, and has deliberately developed full vegan and vegetarian versions, replacing pork and egg with vegetables and plant-based alternatives and using a special sauce free of animal-derived products, so that travelers with dietary restrictions can still share in Hiroshima's signature dish rather than being turned away.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built slowly on the teppan for a fragrant, layered finish, using a custom original sauce and specially made fresh okonomiyaki noodles. The signature Nagataya-yaki, generous with green onion and egg and filled with shrimp and squid, is the house favorite.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen slow-grills each okonomiyaki to build up its aroma and flavor, using fresh noodles made specifically for okonomiyaki and a custom original sauce.
A visiting blogger faced a 40-minute wait on a weekday morning and noted projected weekend waits of 1.5 to 2 hours, yet found the food genuinely satisfying.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mabo-blog confirms the Peace-Park-adjacent location (1-min walk), the 昭和30年代 (mid-1950s-60s) building concept, the Nagataya-yaki (green onion, egg, shrimp, squid), fresh okonomiyaki noodles, custom sauce, slow-grilling, and the 40-min weekday / 1.5-2 hr weekend waits. fooddiversity confirms the plant-based okonomiyaki and animal-free sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is assembled on a shared communal teppan and built on wheat batter plus wheat noodles, so there is no gluten-free preparation path and high cross-contact for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat; the menu does not break out sauce ingredients, so wheat should be assumed in the regular sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan and vegetarian okonomiyaki are cooked on the same teppan used for pork, egg, and oyster dishes, so strict vegans should be aware of shared-surface cross-contact even though the recipes themselves are animal-free.

staff_allergy_handling.text: English menus and English-speaking staff are available, and dedicated plant-based menus are provided on request, which helps with communicating dietary needs, though no formal allergen protocol is documented.

positives.text: Offers genuine dedicated vegan and vegetarian okonomiyaki using an animal-free special sauce, with vegetables replacing the usual pork and egg.
Plant-based diners can eat the city's signature dish here with English-language support, a rare accommodation among Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tourist-friendly spot right by the Peace Park with English menus and English-speaking staff, so a foreign student can order vegan or standard okonomiyaki comfortably, though long waits (often an hour or more on weekends) are normal.

places.name: 冷めん家 大手町店 (Reimenya Otemachi)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima tsukemen (cold dipping-noodle) specialist. The noodles are wheat-based and the spicy dipping sauce is built on soy sauce, so the core and only dish here is not gluten-free. There are no gluten-free options.
places.vegan_detail: The signature Hiroshima tsukemen is served with sliced pork/chashu and the dipping sauce is a soy-sauce and chili tare; the broth/tare is not vegan. No vegan version is offered, and the menu is essentially this one dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat: 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:50), 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:50). Closed Sundays and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (near Hondori and Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is a counter-style specialist devoted to Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled wheat noodles served with a fiery, addictive dipping sauce, piled with fresh vegetables and pork. The house traces its lineage directly to Shin Kaen, the shop credited as the birthplace of Hirosh

chef_bio.background: This is a counter-style specialist devoted to Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled wheat noodles served with a fiery, addictive dipping sauce, piled with fresh vegetables and pork. The house traces its lineage directly to Shin Kaen, the shop credited as the birthplace of Hiroshima cold noodles. The flagship Tokaichi shop opened in 1985, and at a time when the dish was treated as a seasonal item served only from spring through autumn, this house became the first specialist to offer it year-round. The Otemachi branch, a short walk from Hondori and the Peace Memorial Park, opened in October 2017 and is now the main place to sit down and eat in.
chef_bio.philosophy: A flavor that gets more addictive every visit — the kitchen keeps to one thing and does it the original, nostalgic way rather than chasing newer styles.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima tsukemen (cold dipping noodles) with a spicy soy-and-chili tare, fine noodles, and a generous mound of fresh vegetables

anecdotes.text: The shop descends directly from Shin Kaen, the original Hiroshima cold-noodle house, and was the first to serve Hiroshima cold noodles as a year-round specialty rather than a seasonal dish.
Founded in 1985, it is regarded as the second-oldest Hiroshima tsukemen shop, and its version is praised for a nostalgic flavor distinct from newer imitators.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site and syokuki confirm 1985 founding at Tokaichi, direct lineage from Shin Kaen, first to offer Hiroshima cold noodles year-round, self-described second-oldest such shop, and the Otemachi branch opening in October 2017.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu centers on wheat noodles boiled and chilled in a shared kitchen, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature spicy dipping sauce (tare) is soy-sauce based, which in Japan is normally brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dish is served with pork and a soy-based tare in a kitchen built around this one meat-and-wheat dish, so vegan diners cannot be safely accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only local noodle counter where a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the one signature dish will carry you through; little to no English is expected.

noodles.notes: Custom-made medium-thin Chinese-style noodles from a designated maker. Boiled then shocked cold for a smooth, chewy bite and clean throat-feel — served chilled in the Hiroshima reimen style.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima tsukemen (chilled dipping noodles) with the spicy fish-and-soy chili dipping sauce — pick your heat level (start at medium); add extra chili oil on the side.
ramen.chashu: House-made lean pork chashu with the fat trimmed away — kept clean and light to suit the cold, tangy dip rather than rich and fatty.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-and-kansui Chinese noodles and the dipping sauce is built on soy sauce (wheat) plus vinegar and fish broth — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The cold dipping sauce is fish-based (gyokai dashi) and the bowl includes pork chashu — no documented plant-only option.

ramen.pantheon: A defining bowl of Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled noodles plunged into a cold, vinegar-bright soy-and-fish dipping sauce shot through with fiery chili oil, eaten with cucumber and cabbage like a noodle salad with a kick. One of the city's elder statesmen of the genre, it keeps the broth additive-, salt-, and sugar-free so the tang of the seafood dashi and the clean burn of the rayu carry the whole soul of the dish.

places.highlight: A defining bowl of Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled noodles plunged into a cold, vinegar-bright soy-and-fish dipping sauce shot through with fiery chili oil, eaten with cucumber and cabbage like a noodle salad with a kick. One of the city's elder statesmen of the genre, it keeps the broth additive-, salt-, and sugar-free so the tang of the seafood dashi and the clean burn of the rayu carry the whole soul of the dish.

places.name: Organic cafe & kitchen 喫茶さえき (Organic cafe & kitchen Kissa Saeki)
places.gf_detail: All desserts/sweets are gluten-free (soy-milk based scones, cakes, muffins, raw hemp chocolate tart). The cafe stocks a gluten-free menu and aims to accommodate celiac and allergy diners. Note that several savory dishes use wheat: the fu (wheat gluten/seitan) cutlet and standard soy sauce contain wheat, so confirm individual savory items. Sweets are the most reliably GF offering.
places.vegan_detail: One of the largest vegan selections in central Hiroshima. The menu marks items clearly as "100% vegan, gluten-free, alcohol-free." Vegan mains include chickpea/millet croquette sets, lentil hamburg steak, vegan curry, and a wheat-gluten (fu/seitan) cutlet sandwich; sets come with miso soup, fermented germinated brown rice, salad and house pickles. A small number of dishes use chicken (free-range karaage), so it is not a fully vegan kitchen — but plant-based diners have abundant, clearly-labeled choices.
places.hours_raw: Official site: 10:00-21:00 (after 18:00 by reservation only; last order 17:30). Some listings show daytime hours of 10:00-17:00. Closed 2nd and 4th Sundays (and, per one listing, the 4th Monday).
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho / Kamiyacho-higashi, Naka Ward (near Peace Memoria
places.notes: A neighborhood coffee house that has stood in central Hiroshima's Kamiyacho since the 1970s. Originally opened around 1977 by the current owner's father, it was carefully renovated to keep the warm, old-fashioned kissaten atmosphere while reinventing the menu around org

roles: owner (second-generation of the founding Saeki family)
chef_bio.origin: Kamiyacho, Naka Ward, Hiroshima, near Peace Memorial Park
chef_bio.background: A neighborhood coffee house that has stood in central Hiroshima's Kamiyacho since 1977, when the current owner's father opened it. The founder's son, the second-generation proprietor, later reworked it — reopening in 2013 as an organic, plant-forward cafe while keeping the warm, old-fashioned kissaten atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners describe a place that 'considers food and people's lives,' aiming for food that is 'delicious not only to the mouth but also good for the body.' They want a barrier-free table where anyone — people with allergies, vegetarians, vegans, and those with religious dietary needs — can eat with peace of mind, and where staff can confidently explain every ingredient.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic Japanese set meals built around plant-based mains — chickpea/millet croquettes, lentil hamburg steak, and seitan cutlets — served with miso soup, fermented germinated brown rice, salad and house-made pickles, plus gluten-free, soy-milk sweets.

anecdotes.text: Vegetables come from partner farms around Hiroshima grown without chemical pesticides or fertilizers, the bread is baked at Oheso Cafe in rural Sera, and the rice is organic germinated whole grain — the care extends past food to eco-friendly detergents and bulk refill sales of oil, sugar, coffee and umeboshi.
Many guests come to dine alone, and the counter and wood-tone tables are arranged to make solo dining easy and unhurried.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gethiroshima, kaiten-portal and orulab confirm the 1977 founding by the current owner's father, the second-generation reopening as an organic cafe in 2013, chemical-free partner-farm vegetables, Oheso Cafe bread from Sera, germinated whole-grain rice, eco detergents and bulk refills, and the barrier-free allergy/vegan/vegetarian/religious-diet philosophy. No source names an owner 'Koji Okamura'; the family name is Saeki.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Some savory dishes use wheat-based items — a fu (wheat gluten/seitan) cutlet is on the menu, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — so gluten-avoiding diners should confirm individual savory dishes; the dessert case is the reliably gluten-free option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen also cooks a few non-vegan dishes (free-range chicken karaage), so it is a mixed kitchen rather than fully vegan; plant-based diners rely on items clearly labeled '100% vegan.'

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe states its goal is barrier-free dining where food safety is assured for those with allergies and that staff can confidently explain all ingredients with care.

positives.text: Menu items are clearly marked as '100% vegan food, gluten-free, alcohol-free,' making safe choices easy to identify, and an English menu is available.
All desserts are gluten-free and made with soy milk, including scones and cakes.
Ingredients are organic and local, with no chemical seasonings or GMO foods used in the dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: An English/Japanese menu is available and dishes are clearly labeled vegan and gluten-free, so a foreign student can order comfortably and dine alone with peace of mind.

places.name: 勝谷菓子パン舗 (Katsutani Kashi Pan-po)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-bread bakery. Every item is built on a koppe-pan (wheat flour) roll, and savory options such as Hiroshima okonomiyaki bread are doubly wheat-based. There is no gluten-free bread or any documented gluten-free option, and no allergy-handling information is published. Not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The bread itself is not documented as egg/dairy-free, and most fillings are clearly non-vegan: the signature buns add margarine, others use cream cheese, custard cream, whipped cream, egg salad, croquette, Miyajima oyster fry, or chicken nanban. A plain anko (red bean) or jam bun could in theory be vegetarian, but margarine is the house signature and there is no documented vegan offering or ingredient disclosure, so vegans cannot rely on it.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 daily; one regular closing day per week (sources conflict between Wednesday and Thursday)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Omotesando shopping street ne
