places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop carries forward a confectionery that the owner's grandfather ran on Miyajima from the Taisho era, making momiji-manju (maple-leaf cakes) so successful that the family even did demonstration sales overseas. When the grandfather's health failed, the shop closed f

chef_bio.background: The shop carries forward a confectionery that the owner's grandfather ran on Miyajima from the Taisho era, making momiji-manju (maple-leaf cakes) so successful that the family once did demonstration sales overseas — bringing their machines to Honolulu's Ala Moana Shopping Center for a Hiroshima products fair. When the grandfather's health failed, the shop closed for about three decades, but the family's prized red bean paste recipe was kept alive. The grandson, Hideki Hayakawa, had been working as a teacher in the Kanto region and returned to Hiroshima after the 2011 earthquake, eventually reopening the family location in April 2018 as a koppe-pan specialty shop on the same Omotesando street.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as a meeting of 'history and the present' - marrying the traditional anko inherited from the old confectionery with fluffy modern koppe-pan, and emphasizing carefully chosen, mostly local ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Koppe-pan rolls filled with the family's inherited smooth red bean paste, most famously paired with margarine; a rotating lineup of more than 27 sweet and savory fillings including Setouchi lemon-an with cream cheese, Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, and Miyajima oyster fry.

anecdotes.text: The grandfather's confectionery was well known for its momiji-manju and even carried its machines to Honolulu's Ala Moana Shopping Center for demonstration sales at a Hiroshima products fair before health problems forced it to close for about 30 years.

Owner Hideki Hayakawa had been a teacher in the Kanto region and returned home to Hiroshima after the 2011 earthquake, reviving the family shop in April 2018 using his grandfather's secret red bean paste recipe.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site and hatsumeshi confirm the Taisho-era grandfather's Katsutani confectionery, its momiji-manju and Hawaii (Ala Moana) demonstration sales, the ~30-year closure preserving the secret anko, owner Hideki Hayakawa's Kanto teaching career and return after the 2011 quake, the April 2018 reopening as a koppe-pan shop, and 27+ fillings including Setouchi lemon-an with cream cheese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entirely a wheat-flour koppe-pan bakery; there is no gluten-free product and no published allergen handling, so heavy cross-contact with wheat is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most buns contain dairy (margarine, cream cheese, custard, whipped cream), egg, or seafood/meat fillings, and the bread is not documented as vegan; ingredient and allergen disclosure is not provided.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed grab-and-go bakery counter where pointing at the buns you want and a friendly konnichiwa are all you need; English is not guaranteed but no complex ordering is involved.

places.name: 若貴 サンモール前店 (Wakataka Sun Mall-mae)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki uses egg and is layered with pork and typically topped with bonito/fish-based sauce; signature items feature oysters and pork offal (senjigara hormone). No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-23:00 daily (may close early when sold out); shortened hours over New Year (12/31, 1/1-1/3)
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Kamiyacho, Naka-ku, Hiroshima
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An old-established Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop founded in 1963 (Showa 38) and run continuously as an independent family business. In November 2011, marking its 50th year, the shop relocated and renovated into the Hondori shopping arcade, just steps from Peace Memorial Pa

chef_bio.background: An old-established Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop founded in 1961 (Showa 36) and run continuously as an independent, now second-generation family business. On 11 November 2011, marking its 50th year, the shop relocated and renovated into the Hondori shopping arcade (Kamiyacho, near Peace Memorial Park), fitting out every seat with far-infrared teppan griddles so guests can eat their okonomiyaki piping hot straight off the iron.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the traditional flavors of Hiroshima okonomiyaki.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, including an oyster okonomiyaki (kaki-oko) made with local Hiroshima oysters and the spicy noodle 'Aka-kara Carp-yaki'. The shop is also known for its own trademarked house-made senji-gara (deep-fried pork offal).

anecdotes.text: A longtime favorite near Peace Park, the shop reopened inside the Hondori arcade on 11 November 2011 to mark 50 years in business, equipping all seats with far-infrared teppan tables.
Its house-made senji-gara (pork offal), a registered trademark of the shop, has been praised by celebrities.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: umahiro and the shop's own ccv site confirm the reopening in the Hondori arcade on 11 Nov 2011 marking 50 years (hence a 1961/Showa 36 founding), far-infrared teppan at all seats, kaki-oko oyster okonomiyaki, Aka-kara Carp-yaki, and the trademarked, celebrity-praised senji-gara.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared communal teppan griddle alongside wheat batter, wheat noodles, and other dishes, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is seasoned with okonomiyaki/soy-based sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking is done on a shared teppan used for pork, oysters, egg, and fish-topped dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy local okonomiyaki counter where you watch your pancake cooked on the teppan in front of you; staff are used to visitors, but a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will smooth things along.

places.name: オーティス！ (OTIS!)
places.gf_detail: The owner's official site states "vegan & gluten free options" are available alongside the Tex-Mex menu. No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or fryer is documented, and many Tex-Mex staples (tortillas, flour-based items) are typically wheat-based, so a celiac traveler should confirm specific dishes and cross-contamination directly with the staff. Treat as "GF options on request," not a dedicated gluten-free establishment.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is Tex-Mex/omnivore (chili con carne, pork/chicken tacos) but the official site advertises "veggie food" and explicit "vegan options," and reviewers note popular healthy/organic lunch sets including a vegetable daily lunch. Bean tacos and vegetable-based plates are available. Confirm dairy and animal-fat specifics per dish, as Tex-Mex commonly uses cheese, sour cream, and lard.
places.hours_raw: Per the owner's official site: Mon-Fri 15:00-21:00, Sat 14:30-21:00; closed Sundays and Wednesdays (except when live shows are scheduled). Note: a separate aggregator listing shows broader/lunch hours (e.g., Mon-Sat 12:00-22:00, Sun/holidays 14:00-21:00), and some reviewers mention a weekday lunch service, so daytime opening can vary — confirm before a midday visit.
places.neighborhood: Kako-machi, Naka-ku — a short walk from Peace Memorial Park
places.notes: OTIS! opened on August 20, 1987 in Hiroshima's Kako-machi district as a hybrid of live-music room, Tex-Mex kitchen, and cafe. It arrived when the city had almost no venues devoted to blues, hippie, and world music, quickly becoming a pioneering live house. Just days aft

chef_bio.background: OTIS! opened on August 20, 1987 in Hiroshima's Kako-machi district as a hybrid of live-music room, Tex-Mex kitchen, and cafe. It arrived when the city had almost no venues devoted to blues, hippie, and world music, quickly becoming a pioneering live house. Just days after it opened, the International Animation Festival began at a hall across the street, filling the area with crowds and giving the young venue an early sense of purpose.
chef_bio.philosophy: An offbeat, smoke-free, intimate room built around music and good food; the owner's own words on the history page are simply that many musicians have played here and 'we'll keep going steadily.'
chef_bio.specialty: Tex-Mex plates such as chili con carne, tacos, and quesadillas, served alongside live music spanning blues, folk, rock, reggae, African, and world genres

anecdotes.text: Sitting close to the hypocenter of the first atomic bomb, OTIS! has for many years hosted an August 6th 'epicenter live' (爆心地ライブ) on the anniversary of the bombing, a memorial concert long associated with musician Michiro Endo.

The venue's wall is signed by visiting artists, with flamenco dancer Antonio Gades said to be the first to leave his name; the owner has recounted jamming on 'Sweet Home Chicago' with touring musicians from the American show 'A Chorus Line.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The venue's site and live-listing pages confirm the 20 Aug 1987 opening at Kako-machi 1-20, its position near the A-bomb hypocenter, and the annual Aug 6 爆心地ライブ that Michiro Endo ran for many years. The artist-signature and Chorus Line anecdotes come from OTIS!'s own about page.

positives.text: The owner's official site explicitly lists vegan and gluten-free options available alongside the Tex-Mex menu, and describes the room as a small, smoke-free space.

cultural_comfort.note: A laid-back, foreigner-friendly music bar and Tex-Mex spot whose owner keeps an English page online, so a little Japanese plus pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome.

places.name: 中村屋 (Nakamuraya)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house, not a gluten-free destination. The signature items are butter toast and morning sets built around bread, which contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free menu items, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the food offerings are wheat-based.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee (self-roasted blend, nel-drip) is suitable for vegans. Beyond drinks, the food is not vegan: the morning set and toast use butter and egg, and there is no documented dairy-free milk or vegan menu. A vegan can realistically have only black coffee here; treat it as a coffee stop rather than a meal.
places.hours_raw: Current (reduced) operation per recent visitor reports: 11:00–16:00, closed Sunday/Monday/Tuesday. An older listing showed Mon–Sat 07:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:30), holidays 09:00–18:00, closed Sunday — from the shop's earlier full-schedule years.
places.neighborhood: Sakai-machi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (right by Dobashi tram stop; within walking distance west of Peace Memorial Park / Atomic Bomb Dome)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 中村屋 opened in 1946, the year after the atomic bomb, when the founder — a war veteran who survived the blast — first built a barrack shop in the ruined city and later moved to its current location in Saikamachi. He recreated the Western, church-like architecture from his

chef_bio.background: 中村屋 opened in 1946, the year after the atomic bomb, when the founder — a war veteran who survived the blast — first built a barrack shop in the ruined city and later moved to its current location in Sakaimachi. He recreated the Western, church-like architecture from his memories of buildings he had seen during his military service. For decades it was a fixture of Hiroshima's Showa coffee-house culture and a pioneering 'music cafe,' once hosting live jazz; jazz records still play in the background today.
chef_bio.philosophy: A faithfully preserved Showa-era refuge — arched ceilings, a chapel-like room, chandelier lighting and antique coffee mills displayed like icons — kept running quietly out of devotion to the place and its regulars rather than expansion.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee served nel-drip style, plus simple kissaten classics like butter toast (grilled on both sides) and a long-running morning set.

anecdotes.text: The shop survived a fire that destroyed half the building; during that period the owners kept only the bean-roasting going, and regular customers helped the cafe come back.
The founder modeled the cafe's distinctive Western, church-like design on architecture he remembered from his wartime military service.

It is known as an early pioneer of 'music cafes,' featuring live jazz during the heyday of Showa coffee houses; the proprietress now pours nel-drip coffee amid antique registers and roasting equipment that draw photographers from across Japan.

Today Yoko Nakamura, wife of the second-generation owner Akihiro, runs the shop almost entirely on her own after he fell seriously ill; she simplified the menu and the large outdoor roasting machine stopped running after his illness.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabiyomi (Yomiuri) and note.com confirm the 1946 founding by a surviving veteran, the barrack-then-Sakaimachi move, the church-like Western design drawn from his wartime memory, the live-jazz 'music cafe' past, self-roasted nel-drip coffee and both-sides butter toast, and Yoko Nakamura now running it near-solo after her husband Akihiro's illness, with the large roaster idle.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food offerings (toast, morning set) use butter and egg; no dairy-free or vegan options are documented, so only black coffee is reliably plant-based.

positives.text: Coffee is self-roasted and brewed nel-drip; plain black coffee is naturally gluten-free and vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet, old-school neighborhood kissaten run almost single-handedly by the elderly proprietress, so basic Japanese and a calm, respectful manner will go a long way; English is unlikely.

places.name: インド料理 チュリヤカナック (Indo Ryori Churiya Kanak)
places.gf_detail: There is no dedicated gluten-free menu or certification. Staple breads such as naan are made of wheat (the "Kanak" in Churiya Kanak means "wheat" in Hindi). On the other hand, the curries served with rice have room for accommodation, and since the kitchen promotes low-oil, vegetable-focused cooking, the surest approach for those avoiding wheat/gluten is to phone ahead and discuss individually before visiting. Whether dedicated utensils or cross-contact precautions exist could not be confirmed from public information. Safety assessment: not safe for celiacs as-is; no gluten-free program or cross-contamination controls are confirmed, and core breads contain wheat. Only proceed with direct advance consultation.
places.vegan_detail: This is a halal-certified restaurant, and the establishment states explicitly that "in addition to halal-compliant meals, we also have meals suited to vegetarians and vegans." Drawing on India's vegetarian food culture, it serves vegetable-focused curries and follows a policy of using oil sparingly in cooking. Because vegan accommodation is handled on an individual-request basis, confirm the handling of dairy (ghee, butter, yogurt) and naan dough at the time of ordering. No alcohol is used in the dishes and no alcoholic beverages are served. Safety assessment: vegan-friendly with advance request; dairy is a real risk in Indian cooking, so confirm ingredients per dish when ordering.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (near Peace Memorial Park / Atomic Bomb Dome and the Hondori arcade)
places.notes: The restaurant's name "Kanak" means "gold" or "wheat" in Hindi. Wheat, the raw material for naan, is a staple in India and holds a cultural standing comparable to rice in Japan. The name was chosen with the wish to "shine like wheat at harvest time, yet remain modest."

chef_bio.background: 店名の「カナック（Kanak）」はヒンディー語で「金（gold）」または「小麦（wheat）」を意味します。インドで主食的存在のナンの原料である小麦は、日本の米に並ぶ文化的な存在。「収穫期の小麦のように、輝きながらも控えめでありたい」という願いを込めて名付けられました。
chef_bio.philosophy: 油を控えめにし、重たくなりすぎない健康志向の調理で、本場インドの味を提供することを大切にしています。インドの豊かな菜食文化を背景に野菜中心の料理を用意し、ムスリム・ベジタリアン・ビーガンなど多様な食の人が一緒に食卓を囲めることを目指しています。
chef_bio.specialty: 北インド系のカレーとナン。平日のダブルカレーランチ（2種のカレーを選べ、ナンまたはライス・サラダ付き）が手頃で人気。

anecdotes.text: ムスリムの方が安心して広島を観光できるよう、店内に礼拝スペースを無償で提供しています（営業時間内、12:00〜13:00を除く。事前に電話確認を推奨）。
店名「カナック」はヒンディー語で金・小麦を意味し、収穫期の小麦のように控えめに輝きたいという思いが込められています。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式aboutページとhalalgourmetが、店名カナック（金・小麦）の由来と控えめな輝きの思い、油控えめの健康志向、ハラール・ベジタリアン・ビーガン対応、そして営業時間内（12:00〜13:00を除く）に無償提供される礼拝スペースを確認。

staff_allergy_handling.text: ハラール・ベジタリアン・ビーガンに対応した食事を用意しており、客の食の要望に応じて調理を調整すると明記。来店前の電話相談が可能。

positives.text: ハラール認証に対応し、豚肉不使用、料理・店内ともにアルコール不使用。
ベジタリアン・ビーガン対応メニューを用意し、油控えめ・野菜中心の調理を掲げている。
ムスリム向けに無料の礼拝スペースを提供している。

cultural_comfort.note: 英語の公式サイトがあり、ハラール・ベジタリアン・ビーガン対応や無料の礼拝スペース提供を掲げているため、外国人留学生やムスリムの方でも安心して入りやすい店です。

places.name: あなごめし 和田 (Anagomeshi Wada)
places.gf_detail: The sole dish is anago (conger eel) glazed with a sweet soy-based sauce served over rice. Japanese soy sauce and the tare glaze contain wheat, so this dish is not gluten-free, and there is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented.
places.vegan_detail: The shop serves only one item, a grilled conger eel rice bowl with fish-based dashi miso soup; there are no vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (closes when sold out); name ledger reception begins around 10:00 on the day. Closed Tuesdays and Thursdays (plus occasional irregular closures).
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, around Toyokuni Shrine (Senjokaku); near Itsukushima Shrine
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small family-run shop set on the ground floor of a traditional Miyajima townhouse near Toyokuni Shrine, a short walk up from the ferry pier. It is the newest of the island's celebrated anago-meshi houses yet quickly earned a devoted following, with many locals conside (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small family-run shop set on the ground floor of a traditional Miyajima townhouse near Toyokuni Shrine, a short walk up from the ferry pier. It is one of the newer of the island's celebrated anago-meshi houses yet quickly earned a devoted following, and was listed in the Michelin Guide Hiroshima & Ehime 2018.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop keeps to one dish and prioritizes freshly caught local Seto Inland Sea conger, which means limited daily quantities and frequent early sellouts; service is deliberately quick so the line keeps moving.
chef_bio.specialty: A single dish: anago-meshi, a bowl of plump, charcoal-grilled Seto Inland Sea conger eel laid over white rice and finished with a refined, lightly sweet soy glaze, served with miso soup and pickles.

anecdotes.text: The walls are lined with photographs of celebrity visitors, including actress Yasuko Sawaguchi and comedian Takashi Okamura of Ninety-Nine, a quiet nod to its hidden-gem reputation.
The conger is prized for its thickness and firm texture and a charcoal-grilled fragrance with a refined sweetness rather than a smoky, rustic flavor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: genjitsutouhi confirms the townhouse near Toyokuni Shrine ~9 min from the pier, the single ¥2,500 anago-meshi of thick charcoal-grilled Seto-Inland-Sea conger with sweet soy glaze, the sign-in/first-come system with ~1-hr peak waits and early sellouts, the celebrity photos (Yasuko Sawaguchi, Takashi Okamura), and the Michelin Hiroshima & Ehime 2018 listing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago is finished with a sweet soy-based glaze (tare); standard Japanese soy sauce and such glazes contain wheat, making the dish unsuitable for a strict gluten-free diet.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The only dish served is grilled conger eel over rice with miso soup, so there are no vegan options and the kitchen is built entirely around fish.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional single-dish shop where service is fast and Japanese-only; come early, write your name on the queue ledger, bring cash, and be ready to order the one dish (anago meshi) with minimal English support.

places.name: 八昌 流川店 (Hassho Nagarekawa)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish, Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba or udon), and the okonomi sauce is wheat-based. Side dishes and teppanyaki items also use wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free menu, accommodations, or dedicated preparation are documented, and the single shared griddle means heavy cross-contact with wheat. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. The menu centers on meat (pork, a three-meat stewed specialty, beef tendon), egg, and seafood including Hiroshima oysters, plus wheat noodles and fish/meat-based sauces. Even the base okonomiyaki contains egg and is cooked on a shared griddle with meat and seafood. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri & holidays: 16:00–02:00; Sat & day before a holiday: 16:00–03:00; Sun: 16:00–00:00. Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima entertainment distric
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, family-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, operating for more than thirty years. Diners sit at the counter and watch their okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on the iron griddle in front of the

chef_bio.background: A long-running, family-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, operating for more than thirty years. Diners sit at the counter and watch their okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on the iron griddle in front of them, in the classic Hiroshima style.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on generous portions, satisfying flavors, and quality local Hiroshima ingredients such as Seto Inland Sea oysters.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, including a hearty 'Special' piled with meat and egg and a 'Special Soba with green onions' loaded with freshly boiled noodles and negi; locally caught Seto Inland Sea oysters; and a signature slow-simmered three-meat stew that has become a favorite among regulars.

anecdotes.text: Beyond okonomiyaki the shop is known for a slow-cooked stew of three blended meats simmered until tender and rich with collagen, kept hot in foil through the meal, which keeps regulars coming back.
It has been a fixture of the Nagarekawa district for over 30 years, with counter seating where you can watch your okonomiyaki being grilled.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabiiro confirms the 'over 30 years' (創業30余年) Nagarekawa shop, counter/teppan/table seating, the collagen-rich stew of three blended meats kept hot in foil, and Seto-Inland-Sea oysters; freelife confirms the foil-kept simmered dish and the Special Soba with green onions.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a single shared iron griddle alongside wheat noodles, wheat-flour batter, and wheat-based okonomi sauce, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cooking method.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a wheat-based okonomi/soy sauce; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is continuously used for pork, beef, egg, and seafood including oysters, so any item would share surface with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly late-night neighborhood griddle spot where pointing at the counter cooking and a little basic Japanese go a long way, though English support is limited.

places.name: 手打出雲そば いいづか (Teuchi Izumo Soba Iizuka)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Although the soba is buckwheat, there is no gluten-free certification or dedicated handling: the dipping/pouring tsuyu is made with wheat-based soy sauce, tempura uses wheat batter, and noodles are boiled in shared water alongside wheat-containing items. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-focused. The tsuyu (broth/sauce) for Izumo soba is built on fish-based dashi and wheat soy sauce, and signature sets include egg, tempura, and other animal-derived toppings. No documented plant-based dashi or vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 17:30-19:30 (lunch and dinner; dinner service noted as limited). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday.
places.neighborhood: Tenma-cho, Nishi-ku (Kannon-machi / Tenma-cho, west of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run hand-made soba shop carrying on the Izumo soba style passed down from the current proprietor's grandfather, now served in central Hiroshima just west of the Peace Memorial Park, a one-minute walk from Kannonmachi streetcar stop.

chef_bio.origin: The soba tradition originates in the Izumo region of Shimane Prefecture; the family brought that recipe and technique to Hiroshima.
chef_bio.background: A family-run hand-made soba shop dating back over a century (founded 1919), carrying on the Izumo soba style passed down from the current proprietor's grandfather, now served in central Hiroshima just west of the Peace Memorial Park, near the Kannon-machi streetcar stop on the Hiroden main line.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hand-made soba, made fresh and visible to guests at an open soba-making station at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Izumo-style soba made with whole buckwheat grains including the dark husk, giving an aromatic, robust noodle. Served in the classic Izumo styles - warigo soba (stacked round lacquer tiers over which you pour the sauce tier by tier) and kamaage soba (hot soba served in its cooking water) - alongside three-variety (san-mi) soba sets served with egg, grated yam, and grated daikon.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor is a dog lover, and several decorative dog figures greet customers at the storefront.
Diners can watch the soba being hand-cut at a making station from the counter seats, and the traditional Izumo serving finishes by pouring the leftover sauce into the soba cooking water to drink.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトは『祖父の代から受け継いだ出雲そば』と女将のブログを掲げる。しにせ探訪ブログは1919年（大正8年）創業・観音町電停角の立地・店内右手の手打ち場・三味そば（卵・とろろ・大根おろし）・店頭に並ぶ犬の置物を伝える。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles are boiled in shared water with wheat-containing items and the sauce uses wheat soy sauce; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Izumo soba tsuyu is based on standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tamari is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping/pouring sauce is built on fish-based dashi, so even plain soba is prepared with animal-derived broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional family-run soba counter with little to no English; bring a guide or translation app, point to the warigo or kamaage soba on the menu, and note it is cash or PayPay only.

places.name: パルコ デッラ パーチェ (PARCO della PACE)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. This is a wheat-flour pizzeria; the standard pizza crusts are wheat-based and no GF crust or GF menu was found. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Family-run pizzeria with a clearly labeled vegan section: four vegan pizzas (Marinara ¥990, Marinara Champignon ¥1,320, Marinara Special ¥1,375, Quattro Funghi ¥1,265) plus customizable pizzas and vegan desserts (rice-flour cocoa pound cake ¥550, seasonal fruit ¥330). Vegan/animal-free items are marked on the menu with hand-drawn icons and signage, and a separate English vegetarian/vegan menu is available. Note this is an omnivore restaurant that also serves meat and cheese pizzas, so it is not a fully vegan kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun 12:00-14:00 / 18:00-21:00 (lunch & dinner). Closed Mondays and the 2nd Tuesday of the month.
places.neighborhood: 中区土橋 (Dobashi), central Hiroshima
places.notes: Parco della Pace is a small, family-run wood-fired pizzeria tucked into the Dobashi neighborhood of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park. It has been run for roughly a decade by a husband-and-wife team known for their warmth toward visitors from

chef_bio.background: Parco della Pace is a small, family-run wood-fired pizzeria tucked into the Dobashi neighborhood of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park. It has been run for roughly a decade by a husband-and-wife team known for their warmth toward visitors from around the world.
chef_bio.philosophy: A welcoming, peace-themed table open to everyone: the kitchen makes a point of clearly labeling plant-based dishes so that vegan and vegetarian guests can order with confidence alongside everyone else.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic wood-fired Neapolitan-style pizza, baked in a striking blue mosaic-tiled oven, with a crisp, well-charred crust that many diners compare favorably to pizza in Italy.

anecdotes.text: The couple keep a map in the restaurant on which guests mark their home countries.

When a guest asked whether the rice-flour cocoa pound cake was truly vegan, the owner came back to the table with a piece of paper listing every ingredient written in English to confirm it contained no animal products.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour wood-fired pizzeria with no gluten-free crust or menu documented; flour is used openly in an open kitchen, so airborne and surface gluten cross-contact should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A reviewer observed staff using the same pizza cutter for meat and vegetarian pizzas, and not washing hands between handling meat and making the vegetarian pizza. Strict vegans should request fresh utensils and prep.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner provided a handwritten English ingredient list to confirm a dessert was free of animal products, indicating willingness to verify ingredients on request.

positives.text: Vegan and vegetarian pizzas are clearly marked on the menu with hand-drawn icons and signage, and a separate English vegetarian/vegan menu is available.
Four dedicated vegan pizzas plus vegan dessert options, with the ability to customize a pizza from listed toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A foreign student will feel at ease here: the owners are warm and welcoming, keep a world map of visitors' home countries, and provide an English menu with clearly marked vegetarian and vegan options.

places.name: お好み焼・鉄板焼 八誠 (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Hassei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering could be confirmed. The core dishes are Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat-based noodles (soba/udon) and a wheat-containing okonomi sauce, so the signature menu is not gluten-free. Teppanyaki side items are seasoned with standard soy sauce (contains wheat). No source mentions a dedicated gluten-free batter, separate griddle area, or celiac accommodation. A traveler with celiac disease should not assume any safe option here without a detailed direct conversation with the kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is described in several listings as having an English menu and a notably well-stocked range of vegetable teppanyaki dishes (e.g. lotus-root cheese, grilled yam, mushroom butter), with one aggregator noting a "vegetarian selection." However, classic Hiroshima okonomiyaki uses egg, pork, and a fish/kelp-powder base, and the standard okonomi sauce plus dashi mean most dishes are not vegan or even strictly vegetarian by default. No source documents a fully vegan dish, vegan sauce, or separate vegan preparation. A vegetarian could likely assemble a meal from vegetable teppanyaki and a customized okonomiyaki, but this should be confirmed in person; a strict vegan should ask carefully about egg, dashi, butter, and sauce.
places.neighborhood: 中区・富士見町（地蔵通り）/ Chuden-mae, Jizo-dori okonomiyaki street, cen
places.notes: A long-standing okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot tucked into Hiroshima's Jizo-dori, a small street lined with okonomiyaki shops in the Fujimicho area of central Naka-ku, within walking reach of the Peace Memorial Park. It is known locally as a generous, reasonably priced

chef_bio.background: A long-standing okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot tucked into Hiroshima's Jizo-dori, a small street lined with okonomiyaki shops in the Fujimicho area of central Naka-ku, within walking reach of the Peace Memorial Park. It is known locally as a generous, reasonably priced neighborhood favorite.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with plenty of cabbage steamed as it cooks, built on a savory base deepened with kombu (kelp) powder and fish powder, served alongside its signature soba-niku-tama and house Hassei-yaki, plus teppanyaki plates such as grilled squid legs (geso-yaki), oyster butter, and a spread of vegetable dishes.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers consistently praise it as tasty, cheap, and big in portion - a high-value pick among the cluster of okonomiyaki shops on Jizo-dori.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食記ドットコム等が富士見町・地蔵通りの人気店として紹介。名物はそば肉玉と八誠焼き、鉄板焼きのゲソ焼き等。キャベツは蒸し焼きにし、昆布粉と魚粉を隠し味に旨味を深めると説明。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle that continuously handles wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard okonomi sauce and soy sauce, both typically wheat-containing; no wheat-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable teppanyaki items are cooked on the same griddle used for pork, egg, oysters and butter, so vegan/vegetarian dishes are not prepared in isolation.

positives.text: An English menu with detailed explanations is reported, which makes it easier for foreign visitors to ask about ingredients and choose among the well-stocked vegetable dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: Multiple listings note an English menu with detailed explanations, so a foreign student can order comfortably here even with little Japanese.

places.name: 八昌 (Hassho (Okonomimura))
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The dish is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba or udon) and a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core menu is not gluten-free. There is no indication of gluten-free batter, dedicated equipment, or cross-contamination control. Not suitable for those requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made with egg and typically pork (and toppings such as squid, fresh shrimp, and oysters), cooked on a shared griddle, and finished with okonomiyaki sauce. Cabbage and noodles feature heavily but the base preparation and shared griddle make it unsuitable for vegans. No documented vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun); closes earlier once sold out. Some listings show a split of 11:00-15:00 and 17:00-20:00. Closed Thursdays (following day if a public holiday falls on Thursday).
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi / Hatchobori (Okonomimura, Naka-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hassho is one of the oldest shops in Okonomimura, the famous building in central Hiroshima where dozens of okonomiyaki counters are stacked over several floors. Its red noren (curtain) shop traces back to 1965, opened by Furuta Takanori when Okonomimura was still on the

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima; the 八昌 name in Okonomimura dates to 1965
chef_bio.background: The red-noren '八昌' traces back to 1965, when Furuta Takanori (古田隆則) - the eldest son of Furuta Shozaburo (古田正三郎), the first head of Okonomimura - opened it on the ground floor of the then prefab-era Okonomimura building. Furuta's original shop later moved out to Takeyacho in Naka-ku and was renamed 元祖八昌 (now run by his third daughter, Furuta Yuka, who took over in 2016), while the shop that stayed behind in Okonomimura kept the 八昌 name under a separate proprietor. This Okonomimura 八昌 - the red-noren line, distinct from the blue-noren 青八昌 system - stands at the 2nd-floor entrance of the complex and is one of its most popular stands.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop works from the idea that 'food is the source of life' (食は生命の源), aiming to provide safety and health through carefully chosen ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with cabbage, noodles and egg, with popular add-ons including fresh shrimp, squid tempura, cheese, mochi, double noodles, and oysters.

anecdotes.text: Often called one of the most popular stands inside Okonomimura, with a 16-seat counter where each okonomiyaki is cooked to order on the griddle in front of you.

The Okonomimura 八昌 was founded in 1965 as Furuta Takanori's red-noren shop; when the family's original shop relocated to Takeyacho and became 元祖八昌, the Okonomimura shop carried on under the same name under a different proprietor - a separate lineage from the blue-noren 青八昌.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: とほほのWWW入門とまいおこ（広島お好み焼き検索）によれば、お好み村の八昌（赤のれん系）は1965年に初代村長・古田正三郎の長男、古田隆則がプレハブ時代のお好み村1階で開業。のちに本家は竹屋町へ移転して『元祖八昌』となり（2016年より三女・古田由香が2代目を継承）、お好み村の店は別の経営者が同じ屋号で続けている。ぐるっぷは『食は生命の源。安心と健康』の理念と16席を掲載。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared flat griddle alongside wheat batter, wheat noodles and wheat-based sauce, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; standard Japanese soy sauce also contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Okonomiyaki is made with egg and pork and cooked on a shared griddle used for meat, seafood and eggs, so there is no separation from animal products.

positives.text: The shop states it selects ingredients with care for safety and freshness, including cabbage grown without chemical contamination, domestic meat and fresh eggs.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu and free Wi-Fi are available, and you sit at a counter watching your okonomiyaki cooked in front of you, so a foreign student can order comfortably even with little Japanese.

places.name: 元祖へんくつや 本店 (Ganso Henkutsuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with yakisoba/udon noodles (also wheat), then brushed with okonomiyaki sauce, which is typically wheat-based and made with wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, a dedicated cooking surface, or wheat-free options. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional okonomiyaki house where dishes are cooked on a shared teppan with egg, pork belly, squid/shrimp, and bonito-based seasonings; the okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat-soy sauce. No documented vegan or vegetarian menu items. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Main store: Thursday-Sunday 11:00-22:00; closed Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday. (The adjacent Shintenchi branch at Shintenchi 2-12 keeps much later hours, roughly 11:00 until early morning.)
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Nagarekawa / Shintenchi; ~3 min walk from Hatchobori Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] One of the original Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki houses, with roots going back to a street stall that began operating along the Nagare River in 1947, in the years just after the war. In 1954 a woman named Kawara Shizuko took over the stall, and the business grew from a p

roles: post-war origin shop of Hiroshima okonomiyaki
chef_bio.background: One of the original Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki houses, with roots going back to a street stall that began operating along the Nagare River in 1947, in the years just after the war. In 1954 a woman named Kawara Shizuko took over the stall, and the business grew from a post-war yatai into the long-standing teppan house it is today. It has remained family-run and is often cited as one of the genuine 'origin' shops of Hiroshima okonomiyaki.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as something created in Hiroshima just after the war and prides itself on staying unchanged over time, delivering the same value to its customers for more than half a century.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on a large L-shaped griddle, with layered wheat batter, cabbage, noodles and the shop's own sweet-and-spicy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop traces back to a 1947 street stall on the Nagare River; in 1954 Kawara Shizuko took it over from the previous owner, a friend of the founder of Mitchan Sohonten, which is why both shops can each claim early-pioneer 'origin' status from the same era.
The interior has a vintage character, with a large L-shaped teppan counter where guests watch the cooking and eat directly off the hot plate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat noodles, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is typically made with wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a communal griddle with egg, pork and seafood, so vegan dishes cannot be reliably separated.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, old-school counter spot where you can sit at the teppan and eat straight off the griddle; a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect cash only and limited English.

places.name: ふみちゃん 流川店 (Fumi-chan Nagarekawa-ten)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter (crepe layer) and yakisoba/udon noodles, and is finished with a wheat-containing okonomiyaki/soy-based sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore okonomiyaki specialist; standard okonomiyaki contains egg and pork, and toppings such as bonito and aonori are fish-based. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-23:30 (Sun & holidays 11:30-22:00). Closed Tuesdays and the 3rd Monday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa (Horikawa-cho, Naka-ku, Hiroshima; heart of the entertainment district)
places.notes: Fumi-chan opened in 1995 in the middle of Nagarekawa, Hiroshima's main entertainment and nightlife district, and has been a neighborhood fixture for around three decades, drawing a mix of local regulars unwinding after work and curious travelers.

chef_bio.background: Fumi-chan opened in 1995 in the middle of Nagarekawa, Hiroshima's main entertainment and nightlife district, and has been a neighborhood fixture for around three decades, drawing a mix of local regulars unwinding after work and curious travelers.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on technique over speed, aiming for an okonomiyaki that is satisfying yet not greasy, the kind of plate that sits well even late at night after a drink.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with cabbage, bean sprouts, pork, yakisoba noodles and egg. The kitchen leans light and clean rather than heavy: cabbage is steamed slowly to coax out its natural sweetness, the noodles are grilled thoroughly for fragrance, and it is finished with a modestly sweet Mitsuwa sauce for a refreshing aftertaste. A popular side is the slow-simmered beef-tendon stew dressed with a house ponzu of blended soy sauce and vinegar.

anecdotes.text: Beloved for about 30 years as a long-standing shop in the heart of the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, where local regulars and tourists mingle in a lively room.
Known for a light, classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki: slowly steamed cabbage and well-grilled noodles finished with a low-sweetness Mitsuwa sauce for a clean aftertaste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All okonomiyaki are cooked together on a shared teppan (griddle) where wheat batter and wheat noodles are constantly handled, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki/Mitsuwa sauce and a soy-sauce-and-vinegar ponzu, both of which are conventionally wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The same griddle is used for pork, egg and noodles, so any vegetable item would share surfaces with animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, foreigner-friendly okonomiyaki shop in the heart of the nightlife district where regulars and tourists mingle; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the counter griddle go a long way.

places.name: 紅葉堂 弐番屋 (Momijido Nibanya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature 揚げもみじ (fried momiji) is a castella-based momiji manju (wheat flour and egg) coated in a wheat-flour tempura batter and deep-fried; the plain steamed momiji manju is likewise made from wheat-flour castella batter. There are no gluten-free items and no documented allergy accommodation. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should avoid this shop entirely.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The momiji manju and 揚げもみじ are made with a castella batter that contains egg, and the cream and cheese fillings contain dairy. The anko (red bean) filling itself is typically plant-based, but the surrounding cake/batter is not, and the shop publishes no vegan or allergen information, so no item can be confirmed vegan. Strict vegans should treat this as off-limits; at most, ask staff about ingredients.
places.hours_raw: Around 9:30-17:30 (varies by season; until around 17:00 in the off-season)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Omotesando shopping street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Momijido was founded in 1912 by the first-generation owners Takeuchi Yohei and his wife Tora, who began by making thin-skinned manju and mochi-based confections for visitors to Miyajima. Over a century later it remains a family confectionery shop on the sacred island, w

roles: founders Takeuchi Yohei and Tora (first generation)
chef_bio.background: Momijido was founded in 1912 by the first-generation owners Takeuchi Yohei and his wife Tora, who began by making thin-skinned manju and mochi-based confections for visitors to Miyajima. Over a century later it remains a family confectionery shop on the sacred island, with a main shop and this second branch, Nibanya, which opened in 2006 in a renovated Edo-period townhouse on the Omotesando shopping street.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its guiding idea as honoring more than a century of craft while proposing new ways to enjoy momiji manju as a Miyajima souvenir - preserving tradition while continuing to innovate.
chef_bio.specialty: 揚げもみじ (fried momiji) - a fluffy castella-based momiji manju dipped in a specially developed batter and deep-fried, served fresh; offered in anko (red bean), cream, and cheese, alongside the classic steamed momiji manju and a fried-momiji soft-serve.

anecdotes.text: The signature 揚げもみじ was launched on New Year's Day 2002, created through trial and error with craftspeople who developed a special tempura-style batter for the castella manju; it has since become a Miyajima icon.
Nibanya occupies a renovated Edo-period building on the Omotesando shopping street, giving its cafe seating a nostalgic atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop deep-fries wheat-batter momiji manju; there is no gluten-free menu and no published statement about a dedicated fryer, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cream and cheese fillings (dairy) are prepared in the same kitchen as the anko items, and the castella batter contains egg; no separation for vegan diners is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, tourist-friendly walk-up sweets shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where you can point at the fried momiji you want and eat at the seating area; a few words of Japanese help but are not essential.

places.name: 割烹旅館・お食事 山一別館 (Kappo Ryokan Oshokuji Yamaichi Bekkan)
places.gf_detail: According to the official vegetarian/vegan guidance, on the premise of a phone consultation in advance, "all dishes can be changed to gluten-free specifications as far as possible," and allergy accommodation is also accepted. However, it is stated explicitly that there is no dedicated gluten-free kitchen and that there may be cases they cannot accommodate. If you require fully gluten-free, be sure to consult by phone when making a reservation. The standard menu (conger eel, oysters, tempura, soy-sauce-based seasonings, etc.) contains wheat. Safety assessment: not reliably safe for celiacs due to no dedicated kitchen and possible inability to accommodate; gluten-free is possible only with advance phone consultation and is not guaranteed.
places.vegan_detail: A fully plant-based vegan kaiseki is available. Dishes present vegetables in the guise of meat or fish, with even the dashi and seasonings made plant-based. Accommodation for excluding the five pungent roots (gogun: garlic, onion, scallion, etc.) is also available. Vegan/vegetarian dishes are prepared with dedicated plant oil not used for meat or fish and with dedicated utensils kept separate from meat and fish. Offerings include vegetable sushi, vegetable tempura, various thin rolls, vegetable tendon, an agedashi tofu set (vegan), and vegetable courses. Individual accommodation for lacto/ovo/pesco and similar requests is also available. Advance reservation required. The main fare in regular service is non-vegan dishes such as conger eel and oysters. Safety assessment: strongly vegan-friendly with dedicated oil and separate utensils, provided you reserve in advance; the default menu is not vegan.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima; Miyajima-cho, Hatsukaichi City, in front of the Miyajima pier / ferry terminal, near the Omotesando shopping street and Itsukushima Shrine area)
places.notes: [Miyajima] 宮島の桟橋・フェリーターミナル前に建つ、家族経営の割烹旅館。穴子の蒲焼や牡蠣料理など宮島の郷土の味を出す老舗である一方、もう一つの顔として、野菜を肉や魚に見立てた完全菜食の会席を用意し、ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン・ムスリムなど多様な食の背景を持つ旅行者を受け入れている。 (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: 宮島の桟橋・フェリーターミナル前に建つ、家族経営の割烹旅館。穴子の蒲焼や牡蠣料理など宮島の郷土の味を出す老舗である一方、もう一つの顔として、野菜を肉や魚に見立てた完全菜食の会席を用意し、ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン・ムスリムなど多様な食の背景を持つ旅行者を受け入れている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 宗教やライフスタイルによる食の制限があっても宮島の食事を楽しんでもらえるよう、出汁・調味料まで植物性に切り替え、五葷不使用や肉魚と分けた専用調理器具での調理など、菜食・ムスリム対応を一品ずつ個別にあつらえる姿勢。
chef_bio.specialty: 穴子料理・牡蠣料理などの宮島郷土割烹と、植物性のヴィーガン・ベジタリアン会席の二本立て

anecdotes.text: 宮島の名物である穴子や牡蠣を出す割烹旅館でありながら、肉や魚に見立てた野菜料理による完全菜食コースという「もう一つの顔」を持つ点が紹介されている。出汁・醤油まで植物性でまかなうと説明されている。
ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン料理は、肉魚に使っていない専用の植物油と、肉魚と分けた専用の調理器具で調理し、五葷（にんにく・玉ねぎ・ねぎ等）も使わないと公式に説明している。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 宿探しサイトは、桟橋前の家族経営割烹旅館で穴子丼・牡蠣など宮島名物を出す一方、出汁・醤油まで植物性の完全菜食会席（肉魚に見立てた野菜料理）を用意する数少ない店と紹介。公式英語ページは、専用の植物油・専用調理器具・五葷不使用を明記する。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 専用のグルテンフリー厨房はなく、グルテンフリーは「可能な限り」の対応。対応できない場合もあると公式に明記しているため、厳格なセリアック対応には予約時の電話相談が必須。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 通常の割烹料理は穴子の蒲焼など醤油ベースの味付けが中心で、標準の醤油は小麦を含む。グルテンフリー希望時は事前相談で仕様変更が必要。

vegan_cross_contact.text: ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン料理は、肉や魚に使っていない専用の植物油で調理し、肉魚用と分けたヴィーガン・ベジタリアン専用の調理器具のみを使用すると明記。
菜食料理には五葷（にんにく・玉ねぎ・ねぎなど）を使用しないと明記。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応も受け付けており、事前の電話相談を前提に可能な限り対応。対応できない場合はその旨理解を求めると公式に案内。
ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン・ペスコ・ラクト・オボ等、食のスタイルに合わせて個別に調整すると案内。

positives.text: 野菜を肉や魚に見立てた完全菜食の会席を用意し、出汁・調味料まで植物性に対応。ベジ寿司・野菜天ぷら・揚げ出し豆腐セット（ヴィーガン）など品数も豊富。
英語の公式案内ページがあり、ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン・ムスリム対応の経験が豊富で、食材や調理方法を事前に相談しやすい。

cultural_comfort.note: 英語の公式ページがあり、ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン・ムスリム対応に慣れているため、外国人留学生でも英語で食事内容や食材を相談しやすい。

places.name: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 大手町本店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Senmon Kingken Otemachi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is built on wheat ramen-style noodles (sourced from a Hatsukaichi noodle maker) plus a custom soy sauce and sesame-paste tare, all of which contain wheat. No gluten-free options are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature shirunashi tantanmen is topped with pork mince (豚ミンチ) and uses a soy-sauce/sesame-based seasoning; no plant-based version is documented.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog listing: 11:00-20:00 (Mon-Sat & holidays); closed Sunday. Note: other listings show split hours of 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:00 on weekdays and 11:00-15:00 on Sat/holidays — hours may vary, confirm on the day.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Chuden-mae Stn; south of the Atomic Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park, within walking distance)
places.notes: The Otemachi flagship opened in November 2011 in central Hiroshima, just south of the Peace Memorial Park, and grew into one of the shops that carried Hiroshima's soupless dandan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen) to national fame, later expanding to more than a dozen branc

chef_bio.background: The Otemachi flagship opened in November 2011 in central Hiroshima, just south of the Peace Memorial Park, and grew into one of the shops that carried Hiroshima's soupless dandan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen) to national fame, later expanding to more than a dozen branches across Tokyo, Osaka, Fukuoka and beyond.
chef_bio.philosophy: A near-obsession with aroma and freshness: the founder fell for the citrus-like fragrance of fresh Sichuan peppercorns on a trip to Chengdu, and the shop grinds its own blend of red and green sansho fresh every single morning at each location, replacing it daily because they believe the fragrance fades over time. Noodles are made by an established Hatsukaichi noodle maker and the soy sauce was developed together with a local brewer.
chef_bio.specialty: Shirunashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles): medium-thin crimped wheat noodles dressed in a sesame-paste and custom soy-sauce tare with chili oil, pork mince and green onion, finished with their signature Sichuan pepper.

anecdotes.text: The founder discovered fresh Sichuan peppercorns at a market in Chengdu around the time of opening and was struck by their citrus-like, fruity aroma, which became the heart of the dish.
Part of the ritual is stirring the noodles vigorously about 30 times before eating to coat them evenly, a step the shop turned into a playful signature of the experience.

The shop is credited as one of the first to make Hiroshima's soupless dandan noodles known nationwide, pairing noodles from an established Hatsukaichi maker with a soy sauce custom-developed with a local brewing company.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 中国新聞は、創業者が成都の市場で生の花椒の柑橘系の香りに惚れ込んだこと、赤・緑の山椒を各店で毎朝挽くこと、麺は廿日市の製麺所（口一製麺）、醤油は安佐南区の川中醤油と共同開発したこと、食べる前に約30回混ぜる作法を伝える。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish uses wheat noodles plus a wheat-containing soy-sauce and sesame tare; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop uses a soy sauce custom-developed with a local brewing company; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature noodles are topped with pork mince and seasoned with a sesame/soy tare, so the dish is not plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, famous specialty noodle counter where you order a single signature dish and mix it vigorously yourself; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu are enough, but it is fast-paced and not set up for detailed allergy questions in English.

places.name: 汁なし担担麺 味源 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Ajigen)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodle shop. The noodles are wheat-based and the sauce relies on soy sauce and sesame, so the core dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Dan dan noodles here are made with minced pork and a soy/sesame-based seasoning, so they are not vegan. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00, 17:30-20:00; Sat-Sun 11:00-15:00 (weekends lunch only). Sources differ slightly on the evening close time.
places.neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, near Hatchobori Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small brothless dan dan noodle shop that opened in November 2022 in Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, run by the owner with a small staff. Behind a modest white storefront with red lanterns and glass doors, the space opens into a tidy back room lined with counter seats

chef_bio.background: A small brothless dan dan noodle shop that opened in November 2022 in Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, run by the owner with a small staff. Behind a modest white storefront with red lanterns and glass doors, the space opens into a tidy back room lined with counter seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: A direct, unembellished approach that prizes clean, well-executed classic technique over flashy originality, with affordable bowls around 650 yen.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen) built on a sesame-oil base, with crushed nuts adding texture and a restrained, balanced level of spice and Sichuan-pepper numbness. Diners can dial up the heat themselves using a flower-Sichuan-pepper dispenser at the counter.

anecdotes.text: Local food writers single it out as one of Hiroshima's best simple, refined takes on brothless dan dan noodles, with effective crushed-nut texture and a sesame-oil foundation.
On Tuesdays the shop offers complimentary half-rice to pair with the noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is a brothless dan dan noodle made with wheat noodles and a soy/sesame seasoning, which conventionally contains wheat-based soy sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine at the entrance, so little Japanese is needed, but the machine and menu are in Japanese and no English support is documented, so a phrasebook or translation app helps.

places.name: オイスターバー MABUI 袋町 (Oyster Bar MABUI Fukuromachi)
places.gf_detail: This is an oyster bar serving raw oysters and oyster dishes such as garlic butter saute, ahijo, and oyster gratin, alongside Italian and seafood plates. Cooked dishes commonly use wheat-based ingredients and sauces, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodation is documented. Raw oysters themselves are naturally gluten-free, but cross-contact in a shared kitchen has not been addressed, so a celiac diner would need to ask in person and exercise caution.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept centers on oysters and seafood, with dishes built around shellfish, butter, cheese, sea urchin, and other animal products. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and the kitchen is not suitable for a strict plant-based diner.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–24:00 (L.O. food 23:00 / drinks 23:30); Sat/Sun/Holidays: 11:30–24:00. The official site states the kitchen runs through to midnight with lunch from 11:30 and dinner from 17:00; effectively open daily year-round.
places.neighborhood: Fukuromachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (3-5 min walk from Hondori / Tatemachi stns, around Hondori)
places.notes: An oyster bar tucked into the Fukuromachi quarter of central Hiroshima, a few minutes' walk from the Hondori shopping arcade and within easy reach of the city's central sights. The kitchen keeps fresh raw oysters on hand all year, drawing on Hiroshima's celebrated local

chef_bio.background: An oyster bar tucked into the Fukuromachi quarter of central Hiroshima, a few minutes' walk from the Hondori shopping arcade and within easy reach of the city's central sights. The kitchen keeps fresh raw oysters on hand all year, drawing on Hiroshima's celebrated local beds as well as oysters from elsewhere in Japan and abroad.
chef_bio.philosophy: A year-round celebration of Hiroshima oysters: shucked to order so guests can taste the differences between regions and growers side by side.
chef_bio.specialty: Raw oysters served on the half shell with seven to eight or more varieties available at any given time, alongside a 'tasting flight' comparison set, and signature cooked plates such as garlic-butter sauteed oysters, oyster-and-mushroom ahijo, and the house Oyster Rockefeller (oysters baked with spinach).

anecdotes.text: The bar markets itself as a place where you can eat fresh raw oysters every day of the year, keeping 7-8 or more varieties on the counter at once and offering a tasting set so guests can compare them.

A local Hiroshima media feature highlighted it as a spot where you can enjoy raw oysters year-round, including at lunch, with the side-by-side tasting itself being a valued experience for overseas visitors.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ぐるっぷは常時7〜8種類以上の生牡蠣と食べ比べセット、ガーリックバターソテーやアヒージョ、特製オイスターロックフェラーを掲載。ホームテレビの特集は、ランチを含め通年で生牡蠣が味わえ、食べ比べ自体が外国人に貴重な体験になると紹介。

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, casual oyster bar where a foreign student can comfortably point at menu items and order a tasting set; a few words of Japanese help, but English support is not documented so come ready to gesture and smile.

places.name: ロペズ (Lopez)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is a dish that requires a wheat-flour batter (a thin crepe-like layer) and Chinese-style noodles, so it inherently contains gluten. Toppings and the teppanyaki sauce also use wheat-derived soy sauce and sauce, and no information on gluten-free accommodation could be confirmed. Not suitable for people with celiac disease. Safety assessment: unsafe for celiacs; the dish is inherently wheat-based with no gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: Because the signature "nikutama soba" and other dishes use egg, pork, Chinese noodles, bonito dashi, and okonomi sauce (seafood- and wheat-derived), no information on vegan accommodation could be confirmed. There are Central/South American-style side dishes such as the signature jalapeno topping and tortilla chips, but the restaurant does not advertise vegan accommodation. Confirm before visiting. Safety assessment: not vegan-friendly; core dishes contain egg, pork, and fish, and no vegan option is confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Wed/Thu: 16:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30, dinner only) / Tue/Fri: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 14:00, lunch only) / Sat/Sun/holidays: closed. Closed for New Year (12/30-1/3).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born and raised in Guatemala during its civil war, he left the country after graduating high school. Under an uncle in New Orleans, USA, he trained at Italian and French restaurants, acquiring the fundamentals of cooking such as knife work and handling meat. During temporary work at a luxury hotel in Honolulu he met and married Makiko, a native of Hiroshima, and moved to Hiroshima in 1995. He then trained at the long-established Hiroshima okonomiyaki restaurant "Hassho" before going independent, opening his own place near JR Yokogawa Station in 2000.

chef_bio.chef_name: フェルナンド・ロペス（Fernando Lopez）
chef_bio.origin: グアテマラ（グアテマラシティ、1963年生まれ）
chef_bio.background: 内戦下のグアテマラに生まれ育ち、高校卒業後に出国。米ニューオーリンズの叔父のもとでイタリア料理店・フランス料理店で修業し、包丁さばきや肉の扱いといった料理の基礎を身につけた。ホノルルの高級ホテルでの仕事で広島出身の真喜子さんと出会って結婚し、1995年に広島へ移住。その後、広島の名店お好み焼き『八昌』（薬研堀）で修業して独立し、2000年にJR横川駅近くで自身の店を開いた。
chef_bio.philosophy: 店内に世界地図を掲げ、自国グアテマラを指し示すことで旅行者と対話する。『戦争になってからでは遅い。広島に来た人が、自分の国が正しい方向に進んでいるのかを裁ける人になってほしい』と語り、被爆地ヒロシマの記憶と、戦争の根本原因への理解を伝えることを大切にしている。
chef_bio.specialty: 広島風お好み焼き。看板は『肉玉そば』。他店にはない中南米由来の青唐辛子ハラペーニョのトッピングが名物で、ファヒータやトルティーヤチップスといった中南米系のサイドメニューも揃える。

anecdotes.text: 内戦下のグアテマラで育った店主は『毎週のように校門にばらばらの遺体が捨てられていた』と少年時代を振り返り、25歳まで生きられないと思い出国を決意した。広島出身の妻と結婚し、被爆者である義両親や妻のキリスト教コミュニティを通じてヒロシマの被爆の記憶に触れていった。
薬研堀の名店『八昌』で修業して独立し、横川駅近くで20年以上にわたり店を営む。ミシュランガイド広島のビブグルマンにも選ばれた評判の店。
他店では見られないハラペーニョのトッピングが業界に新風を吹き込み、グアテマラ出身店主ならではの個性として注目されている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ヒロシマ平和メディアセンターは、グアテマラシティ生まれ（1963年）・内戦体験・ニューオーリンズでの修業・ホノルルで広島出身の真喜子さんと結婚・1995年広島移住・2000年横川で開業・世界地図と平和のメッセージを詳報。4travelは薬研堀『八昌』での修業とミシュラン広島ビブグルマン選出を伝える。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 広島風お好み焼きは小麦粉の生地と中華そばが基本で、同一の鉄板で複数の品を調理するため、グルテンの交差汚染は避けられない。グルテンフリー対応の記載は確認できなかった。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: お好み焼きには小麦・魚介由来のお好みソースが使われるのが一般的で、本品も例外ではないと考えられる。小麦不使用ソースの提供情報は確認できなかった。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 看板メニューに豚肉・卵・中華そば・かつお出汁が使われ、同じ鉄板で肉や卵を焼くため、植物性のみの調理を分離している情報は確認できなかった。

positives.text: 名物のハラペーニョトッピングやトルティーヤチップス等の中南米系サイドメニューがあり、ベジタリアン/ヴィーガンが食べられる品があるか店主に直接相談しやすい（店主は英語・スペイン語も通じやすい）。

cultural_comfort.note: 店主はグアテマラ出身で英語・スペイン語も通じやすく、店内に世界地図を掲げて旅行者と交流するため、外国人留学生でも基本的な日本語の挨拶があれば気軽に立ち寄れます。

places.name: クロワッサンマルシェ (Croissant Marche)
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, but they sell a gluten-free bread (グルテンフリー食パン) made with rice flour (米粉) instead of wheat. The macrobiotic lunch sets are rice-based (fermented brown rice / 酵素玄米, switching to plain brown rice when sold out) with vegetable sides, so the core lunch is naturally largely rice-based — but individual side dishes may use wheat-containing seasonings (e.g. soy sauce / miso). No published dedicated-fryer or cross-contamination information; ask staff directly about specific dishes.
places.vegan_detail: The daily lunches are completely vegan (動物性不使用) — two macrobiotic options served on weekdays (around 1,000 yen + tax): a macrobiotic plate (one main plus four vegetable sides with fermented brown rice and gomashio) and a macrobiotic curry plate (red-lentil and seasonal vegetable curry, mild, with a lentil-quinoa salad). Important caveat: the shop also sells packaged natural-food/bento items, and some bento occasionally contain animal products following macrobiotic yin-yang philosophy, so check labels on retail items. The sit-down lunches themselves are vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (lunch 11:30-14:00, last order 14:00). Closed Sundays and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: 広島市中区堺町 (Sakaicho, Naka-ku) — a few minutes west of the Peac
places.notes: Croissant Marche is run by a married couple, Katsutoshi and Yuka Shinano. Their journey began in 1982 when they opened a general goods store called 'Croissant no Mise'; conversations with customers inspired them to create a place built around health-conscious food. They

chef_bio.chef_name: 信濃 克爾 (Shinano Katsutoshi) and 信濃 由佳 (Shinano Yuka)
chef_bio.background: Croissant Marche is run by a married couple, Katsutoshi and Yuka Shinano. Their journey began in 1982 when they opened a general goods store called 'Croissant no Mise'; conversations with customers inspired them to create a place built around health-conscious food. They opened the original Croissant Marche in 2006 in Nishi Ward, then relocated in 2014 to the current spot near Peace Memorial Park for better visibility, becoming one of Hiroshima's pioneering plant-based eateries.
chef_bio.philosophy: Their guiding idea is 'a handmade shop without animal products.' Yuka, who could not comfortably eat animal foods, found macrobiotics transformative after her husband took an interest in brown rice — they describe realizing they didn't need to force themselves to eat animal products. They eat fermented brown rice daily and credit their energy to this diet, sourcing vegetables from local farms grown with minimal pesticides in a local-to-table spirit.
chef_bio.specialty: Weekly-changing macrobiotic vegan lunch plates built on fermented brown rice (酵素玄米) with house-made gomashio, plus a lentil seasonal-vegetable curry; the shop also sells house-made breads including a rice-flour gluten-free loaf.

anecdotes.text: The husband was reported to still be working actively at the counter at around 80 years old, the couple attributing their vitality to their plant-based, brown-rice-centered diet.
Their clientele spans ages from teens to 80s, predominantly women, with many regulars and a growing number of health-conscious younger customers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: japanese-heart.com gives the owners' names, the 1982/2006/2014 timeline, the macrobiotic brown-rice philosophy, and the husband still working at ~80. senmacrobiotic.com confirms the weekly macrobiotic lunch, hand-made gomashio and fermented-brown-rice (酵素玄米) bento. family-dr.jp and Vegewel list the rice-flour gluten-free bread.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sit-down lunches are completely vegan, but the retail bento and packaged goods sold in the shop occasionally contain animal products following macrobiotic yin-yang principles, so check labels before buying take-out items.

positives.text: Daily lunches are entirely free of animal products and built on local organic, minimal-pesticide vegetables and fermented brown rice.
They sell a gluten-free bread made with rice flour in place of wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, warmly-run family shop where some basic Japanese (or a translation app) helps, but staff have welcomed international visitors before, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the day's lunch will get you far.

places.name: みやじま食堂 (Miyajima Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature anago (conger eel) rice and oyster dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce and sweet tare, fried oyster sets are breaded in wheat flour, and curry roux also contains wheat — so the core menu is not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood specialist built around conger eel and oysters; menus center on fish, shellfish, and pork (kone-yaki), with wheat-soy sauces throughout. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); hours vary by season. Some sources list 11:00-21:00.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (next to the post office
places.notes: [Miyajima] A cafe-style local diner sitting right in the middle of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, next to the post office and an easy walk from Itsukushima Shrine. The two-floor space has a warm wooden interior with tatami seating downstairs and tables upstairs, and it has (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A cafe-style local diner sitting right in the middle of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, next to the post office and an easy walk from Itsukushima Shrine. The two-floor space has a warm wooden interior with tatami seating downstairs and tables upstairs, and it has earned a following among women, families, and solo travelers for being unfussy and welcoming.
chef_bio.philosophy: A quick, comfortable stop for sightseers who want to taste the island's specialties without a long wait — dishes are known for arriving fast.
chef_bio.specialty: Unusually for the island, it serves both of Miyajima's signature ingredients under one roof: grilled conger eel (anago) over rice and Hiroshima oysters prepared as fried sets, rice bowls, and even oyster-cutlet curry. The anago-meshi set and the Miyajima set are the dishes people come for.

anecdotes.text: On winter firework nights, when many island restaurants have already closed, this place stays open late and draws long queues of visitors looking for a warm meal.
It is one of the rare spots on Miyajima that puts both anago and oyster dishes on the same menu, where the two specialties are usually sold separately.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: oyakodetanoshimou.com confirms the Omotesando/post-office location, the two-floor tatami-and-table layout, touchscreen ordering, quick service, the anago-meshi set (¥2,640) and Miyajima set (¥2,970) and oyster-cutlet curry (¥1,430), the rarity of serving both anago and oysters, and the winter-firework late-night queues.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago rice and oyster bowls are seasoned with sweet soy-based tare/soy sauce, which in standard Japanese cooking is wheat-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-friendly tourist-area diner where pointing at the photo menu and a few polite words of Japanese will get you a warm welcome, though staff may not speak much English.

places.name: 宮島いちわ (Miyajima Ichiwa)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature anago-meshi and oyster dishes are seasoned with Japanese soy sauce / tare, which contain wheat, and fried items (oyster cutlet) use wheat batter/breading. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. The restaurant is built around conger eel (fish) and oysters (shellfish); even the rice sets use fish-based tare/soy seasoning. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 11:30-16:00 (L.O. 15:30). Thu-Sat may also open for dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Mondays. The official site also lists hours as 11:30-16:30.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Machiya-dori (near Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Miyajima Ichiwa sits on Machiya-dori, the old townhouse street a few minutes inland from the Miyajima ferry pier and a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine, away from the busier main shopping lanes. It is a Japanese restaurant devoted to the island's two best-known specia (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Miyajima Ichiwa sits on Machiya-dori, the old townhouse street a few minutes inland from the Miyajima ferry pier and a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine, away from the busier main shopping lanes. It is a Japanese restaurant devoted to the island's two best-known specialties: anago-meshi (grilled conger eel over rice) and oyster dishes.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Miyajima's local cuisine using regional ingredients, with a spacious second-floor tatami room for groups and the island's catered bento delivery service.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-meshi made with purely domestic, wild-caught conger eel, and dishes featuring Miyajima oysters; signature plates include the anago-meshi set, the asari (clam) and anago rice set, and the 'Ichiwa set' pairing anago rice with a fried oyster cutlet.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen highlights 'purely domestic natural conger eel,' which it describes as synonymous with Miyajima cuisine, alongside 'pure Miyajima oysters.'
An original dish on the menu is the 'asari anago-meshi' set, combining clams with the conger-eel rice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official miyajima-ichiwa.com site confirms the Machiya-dori location ~6 min from the pier, 'purely domestic natural conger eel' and 'pure Miyajima oysters,' the asari-clam anago-meshi (¥1,890) and standard anago sets, fried-oyster-cutlet dishes, the second-floor tatami group room, and its billing as the only catered bento delivery on Miyajima.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi and the rice sets are seasoned with Japanese soy-sauce-based tare, which standardly contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on conger eel and oysters with fish-based seasonings; no vegan or vegetarian items are listed.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly local lunch spot on a quieter Miyajima street where the menu and an English website exist, so a foreign student with a few words of Japanese (and pointing at the anago-meshi set) will get by comfortably.

places.name: わたしの食卓 紙屋町店 (Watashi no Shokutaku Kamiyacho-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a home-style teishoku diner where dishes are seasoned with regular soy sauce and miso (both contain wheat), and fried items like chicken nanban and karaage use wheat-based batter and shared fryers. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on meat and fish set meals (chicken nanban, karaage, mackerel simmered in miso, pork kimchi) and uses fish-based dashi and soy sauce throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho / Hondori, Naka-ku (east of the Atomic Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park, within walking distance)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Watashi no Shokutaku is a casual, self-service set-meal diner in central Hiroshima, opened in December 2010, just steps from the Hondori shopping arcade and within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. It is one of a small handful of location

chef_bio.background: Watashi no Shokutaku is a casual, self-service set-meal diner in central Hiroshima, opened in December 2010, just steps from the Hondori shopping arcade and within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. It is one of a small handful of locations operated by the same family company within the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach is body-friendly, home-style cooking made entirely in-house, with the simple aim of serving warm, satisfying rice-based meals at a fair price.
chef_bio.specialty: Affordable, all-made-from-scratch Japanese set meals (teishoku) for under about 1,000 yen, with rice available as either white or mixed grain. The signature is the chicken nanban set, alongside karaage, miso-simmered mackerel, and pork kimchi sets.

anecdotes.text: Diners buy a ticket at the door and collect their tray when it is called, a streamlined self-service style praised for quick turnover that makes it easy to drop in even on a busy lunch break.
Local diners note the chicken breast set is prepared to stay tender with a lightly spicy seasoning, and that the spot is comfortable for solo office workers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog gives the 6 Dec 2010 opening, the ticket-machine self-service style, prices under ¥999 (chicken nanban ¥820), the chicken-nanban set as a top seller, and multiple (4+) company branches. goguynet confirms the ticket-then-collect flow, quick turnover, white/mixed-grain rice choice, and the tender lightly-spicy chicken-breast set.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals such as miso-simmered mackerel and pork kimchi rely on regular soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: You buy a meal ticket from a Japanese-only machine at the entrance and then pick up your tray at the counter, so a translation app or a quick look at the photo menu posted outside helps; staff are friendly but communication is mostly in Japanese.

places.name: 正弁丹吾 (Shoben Tango)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The cuisine relies heavily on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and ponzu, plus tempura/fried items, with no documented gluten-free menu or accommodations. A celiac diner could not eat here safely without extensive negotiation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around Seto Inland Sea seafood — fugu (pufferfish), oysters, anago (conger eel), sashimi and fish-based broths — and uses fish/dashi and wheat-soy seasonings throughout. No documented vegan or vegetarian set menu.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:15); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Open year-round except part of the year-end/New Year period (closed 12/31 through midday 1/4; reopens evening of 1/4).
places.neighborhood: Mikawa-cho, Naka-ku (central Hondori / Hatchobori shopping district)
places.notes: A long-established Japanese kaiseki house in the heart of Hiroshima, tucked into a side street off the bustling central shopping arcade between Hondori and Hacchobori, a short walk from Peace Memorial Park. The restaurant has been welcoming guests for well over half a c

chef_bio.background: A long-established Japanese kaiseki house in the heart of Hiroshima, tucked into a side street off the bustling central shopping arcade between Hondori and Hacchobori, a short walk from Peace Memorial Park. Founded in 1963, it has been welcoming guests for well over half a century, and is set across several floors with counter seating, sunken-hearth tatami rooms, and private rooms sized from intimate pairs to large parties.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than avant-garde experimentation, the kitchen honors traditional preparation to draw the maximum flavor from premium ingredients, pairing dishes with house-made condiments and serving each at its optimal moment. The house emphasizes omotenashi — attentive, refined hospitality in a calm adult atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Seto Inland Sea seafood — fugu (pufferfish) courses with house-made plum paste and ponzu, year-round oyster courses popular with out-of-prefecture visitors, anago (conger eel), and sashimi — alongside mountain ingredients from the Chugoku region. The kitchen makes its own soy sauce and ponzu.
