anecdotes.text: Year-round oyster courses are a particular draw for visitors traveling to Hiroshima from outside the prefecture, served alongside the restaurant's signature Seto Inland Sea fugu.
Fugu and anago are served with the house's own handmade umeboshi paste and ponzu, reflecting a philosophy of enhancing rather than overshadowing the ingredient.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog dates the founding to 1963 (60+ years), lists fugu, Seto Inland Sea seafood, oysters and matsutake, ~80 seats across counter/table/sunken-hearth tatami rooms, and states the house handcrafts its own soy sauce and ponzu. tabiiro confirms the secret handmade plum paste and ponzu served with fugu and thin-sliced anago.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on traditional Japanese seasonings including house-made soy sauce and ponzu, which are wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fish, fugu, oysters and dashi-based preparations, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive throughout the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a long-established, somewhat formal kaiseki restaurant where some Japanese language ability and familiarity with traditional dining etiquette will help; calling ahead to reserve a private room is wise for a comfortable experience.

places.name: 花山椒 八丁堀店 (Hanasansho Hatchobori-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a brothless dan dan noodle (汁なし担々麺) shop. Wheat noodles are the core of every dish, and the dishes are dressed in sesame or seafood-based sauces that conventionally contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The food centers on dan dan noodles with pork mince, a fish/seafood-based sauce option (元祖魚介タレ), and side bowls like roasted-pork-and-egg rice (焼豚玉子飯) and mapo tofu rice. No documented vegan or plant-based options. Not suitable for vegan diners.
places.neighborhood: Teppo-cho / Hatchobori, Naka-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent brothless dan dan noodle (汁なし担々麺) shop that opened in March 2022 on a main street in Hiroshima's Naka Ward, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop. The storefront is modest and famously carries no exterior sign, giving it a hidden-gem feel even

chef_bio.background: A small, independent brothless dan dan noodle (汁なし担々麺) shop that opened in March 2022 on a main street in Hiroshima's Naka Ward, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop. The storefront is modest and famously carries no exterior sign, giving it a hidden-gem feel even though it sits right on a busy street; the menu is posted outside so diners can choose before stepping in.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodles, where you choose between an original seafood-based sauce (元祖魚介タレ) and a rich sesame sauce (ごまだれ), with adjustable spice and Sichuan-pepper (山椒) numbing levels. The kitchen also turns out generous, budget-friendly set meals pairing the noodles with rice bowls such as roasted-pork-and-egg rice (焼豚玉子飯) for around 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop has no sign out front and even posts a lighthearted apology for it, leaning into its quiet, easy-to-miss charm despite a prime street-facing spot.

A local reviewer ordered the 950-yen set pairing gapao rice with sesame-sauce dan dan noodles at numbing-level 3, finding the creamy sauce mellowed the heat and that spooning the gapao rice in at the end gave a surprising, almost cheese-like richness.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: modokimaru.com and Tabelog confirm the 7 Mar 2022 opening at 鉄砲町8-24 (~3 min from Hatchobori), the deliberate no-sign 'hidden gem' with an apology note, the choice of original seafood tare vs sesame sauce with adjustable numbing levels, and the ¥950 A-set (dan dan + 焼豚玉子丼); the gapao-rice level-3 review is from modokimaru.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop specializes in wheat-noodle brothless dan dan noodles dressed in sesame or seafood-based sauces; such Japanese sauces conventionally contain wheat-based soy sauce, though no allergen statement is published.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 13-seat counter-and-table noodle shop with a Japanese-only menu and cash-only payment, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the posted menu and have cash on hand.

noodles.notes: Medium-gauge straight wheat noodles in the Hiroshima shiru-nashi style, chosen to grab and carry the thick chili-and-sesame tare from the bottom of the bowl. They are meant to be vigorously tossed before eating so every strand is coated.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (brothless dandan noodles) ordered with the original gyokai (seafood) tare and the "sansho lover" heat level; toss thoroughly before eating. Pair it with the grilled-pork egg rice (yakibuta tamago meshi) set.
ramen.chashu: No chashu in the noodle bowl itself — the protein is spiced ground pork (niku-miso) hidden beneath the noodles. Sliced braised pork appears only as a topping on the side grilled-pork egg rice, dressed with mayo, fried egg and a sweet-savory sauce.

gf.note: Brothless dandan noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the soy-based tare contains wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The dish is built on ground pork and a seafood (gyokai) or sesame tare with chili oil; no documented plant-only broth/tare bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima's homegrown shiru-nashi tantanmen — there is no soup at all, just a soy-and-chili tare you toss into the noodles so the soul of the bowl lives in the seasoning rather than a broth. The hallmark here is the wave of citrusy, mouth-numbing sansho that builds over the ground-pork heat, with a choice of a clean seafood-forward tare or a richer sesame one.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima's homegrown shiru-nashi tantanmen — there is no soup at all, just a soy-and-chili tare you toss into the noodles so the soul of the bowl lives in the seasoning rather than a broth. The hallmark here is the wave of citrusy, mouth-numbing sansho that builds over the ground-pork heat, with a choice of a clean seafood-forward tare or a richer sesame one.

places.name: ルーエ ぶらじる (Ruhe Brasil)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop whose signature offerings are built around wheat: homemade bread, toast, sandwiches, napolitan (wheat pasta), and gratin. No gluten-free items, menu, or accommodation are documented. Not suitable for those requiring gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: A retro kissaten centered on egg-based morning sets, dairy (gratin, coffee/tea service), and meat-containing napolitan and curry. No vegan options, labeling, or accommodation are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 07:00-19:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 07:00-18:00. Morning service served 07:00-10:30.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi / Takanobashi shopping street, Naka-ku (south of Peace Memorial Park, within walking distance)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1946 in the immediate aftermath of the war, this Otemachi coffee house sits along the Takanosashi shopping arcade, a short walk south of the Peace Memorial Park. It is widely credited as the birthplace of Japan's 'morning service' tradition, having begun offer

chef_bio.background: Opened in 1946 in the immediate aftermath of the war, this Otemachi coffee house sits along the Takanosashi shopping arcade, a short walk south of the Peace Memorial Park. It is widely credited as the birthplace of Japan's 'morning service' tradition, having begun offering a morning set of bread, egg, salad and coffee in the post-war years.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighborhood haven of Showa-era calm — soft lighting, jazz, a green inner courtyard and shelves of reading material — where regulars and visitors alike slow down over morning coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: Rotating morning sets and homemade bread baked on-site, alongside enduring kissaten classics like napolitan and gratin, served with coffee.

anecdotes.text: Customers describe businesspeople and locals settling in with the morning paper before work, choosing from several morning sets while freshly baked bread fills a counter near the entrance.
Regarded as the place where Japan's morning-service custom began, founded in 1946 and serving morning sets in the years that followed.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog describes Ruhe Brasil as a 1946-founded old-guard kissaten credited as the birthplace of Japan's morning-service, with daily-rotating morning sets (~¥750), on-site homemade bread, and classics like napolitan and gratin at 大手町5-6-11 on the Takanosashi arcade. tabetainjya adds the retro atmosphere and morning-crowd detail.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread is baked on-site and toast, sandwiches, napolitan and gratin are core menu items, so wheat is pervasive in the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on eggs, dairy and meat (egg morning sets, gratin, napolitan, curry); no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, old-fashioned local coffee shop where you order at your table; a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at the picture menu and the simple morning sets makes it easy and welcoming for a foreign student.

places.name: だいこん屋 (Daikonya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature momiji manju is a wheat-flour batter cake, and the set meals (anago rice, oyster dishes) rely on wheat-based soy sauce and breaded/fried items. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes are anago (conger eel), oysters, sashimi and seafood set meals; momiji manju typically contains egg and dairy (cream/ice-cream varieties). No vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima / Omotesando shopping arcade
places.cuisine: Momiji manju confectionery and Seto Inland S
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] だいこん屋 traces its roots to the early Edo period, when it operated as the Ōne-ya (大根屋), a boat-inn lodging house on Miyajima. In 1989 it was reborn as a souvenir and rest shop specializing in momiji manju, and in 1993 the surviving Edo-to-Taisho-era building was fully ren (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: だいこん屋 traces its roots to the early Edo period, when it operated as the Ōne-ya (大根屋), a boat-inn lodging house on Miyajima. In 1989 it was reborn as a souvenir and rest shop specializing in momiji manju, and in 1993 the surviving Edo-to-Taisho-era building was fully renovated into the shop that stands today on the island's main approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames its sweets as a way to share 'memories of taste' and hometown flavors with the people you care about, while preserving its historic building as part of Miyajima's streetscape.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked momiji manju in classic and original flavors (red bean, cream, chocolate, an original matcha cream, plus pie- and ice-cream-wrapped versions), alongside Seto Inland Sea set meals built around anago rice and Hiroshima oysters.

anecdotes.text: Inside the shop you can watch the momiji manju being made, with the smell of the cakes baking fresh filling the room, and relax over tea in a small interior Japanese garden with a koi pond.
An original matcha-cream momiji manju is one of the shop's own creations.
The business began in the early Edo period as the Ōne-ya boat-inn and was reinvented as a momiji manju shop and rest stop in 1989, with the historic building fully restored in 1993.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official daikonya.jp history page confirms the early-Edo boat-inn 大根屋 (Ōne-ya/Daikonya), the 1989 rebirth as a souvenir/rest shop, and the 1993 full renovation of the Edo-to-Taisho building. tabiiro confirms watching momiji manju being made, the interior koi-pond garden, and the momiji-manju flavor range including matcha cream, pie and ice-cream versions.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago rice and oyster set meals are seasoned in the regional style, which relies on wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on conger eel, oysters and other seafood, so vegan diners should not assume any shared equipment or stock is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, tourist-frequented Miyajima shop where you can watch momiji manju being baked and grab sweets or tea easily with a smile and a few words; the upstairs set-meal dining is more formal and works best with a phone reservation, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps there.

places.name: 竜胆 (Rindo)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is wheat ramen-style noodles in a Sichuan dandan sauce; noodles are wheat and the sauce is not gluten-free, so this is not suitable for gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Dandan noodles here use minced meat and a savory Sichuan sauce; no documented vegan option, so not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 12:30-15:00 / 18:30-22:00 (per Tabelog). Note: some secondary listings cite lunch starting 11:30; Tabelog shows 12:30 open.
places.neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, Naka-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run counter shop in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district devoted to broth-less dandan noodles in the Sichuan style. The shop is named after the rindo (gentian) flower, noted for its solitary bloom and bitter root, a quiet nod to the single-minded focus

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run counter shop in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district devoted to broth-less dandan noodles in the Sichuan style. The shop is named after the rindo (gentian) flower, noted for its solitary bloom and bitter root, a quiet nod to the single-minded focus of the place.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focused, no-frills shop: a short menu, short hours, and a few counter seats, letting the noodles and sauce do the talking.
chef_bio.specialty: Broth-less dandan noodles (shiru-nashi tantanmen) with a Sichuan-style sauce, served with adjustable heat. Chili powder and chili oil sit on the counter so guests can dial in their own spice, and toppings such as a soft-boiled egg are available.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so consistently busy that one reviewer recounts trying four times before finally getting a seat, succeeding only around 1pm.
The heat is built around Sichuan pepper rather than raw chili burn; a reviewer found the mid-level (3) spice gentle enough that the sauce's underlying sweetness stood out.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: oomin77.com confirms the gentian-flower (竜胆) namesake, the Hatchobori broth-less Sichuan tantanmen (¥500), counter seating with tableside chili powder/oil for self-adjusting heat levels (1–3 up to 7), the Sichuan-pepper-led rather than chili-burn profile with notable sauce sweetness at level 3, and the four-attempts-before-a-1pm-seat anecdote.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dandan sauce is a savory Sichuan-style seasoning typically built on soy/sesame-based components; no gluten-free preparation is documented, and the wheat noodles share the same kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard dandan noodles include minced meat, so vegan diners cannot rely on the regular dish; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only local shop with no English noted and a regular queue; comfortable if you can order simply in Japanese, point at the dandan noodles, and accept cash-only with quick turnover.

places.name: 鉄板焼 お好み焼 修竜 薬研堀本店 (Teppanyaki Okonomiyaki Shuryu Yagenbori Hont)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which is built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and grilled dishes are typically finished with wheat-based sauces and soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on okonomiyaki with egg, pork and seafood, plus offal (hormone), wagyu steak, oysters and sea-urchin dishes. Sauces and soy sauce are not vegan. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 18:00-02:00 (next day) (L.O. 01:30); closed Sundays; closed New Year period (Dec 31-Jan 3)
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku
places.notes: A family-run, locally rooted teppanyaki and okonomiyaki shop in the Yagenbori nightlife district of central Hiroshima, open late into the early hours for the area's after-work and after-night-out crowd.

chef_bio.background: A family-run, locally rooted teppanyaki and okonomiyaki shop in the Yagenbori nightlife district of central Hiroshima, open late into the early hours for the area's after-work and after-night-out crowd.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on craftsmanship and carefully chosen ingredients, from the twin-yolk eggs to the hand-picked noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki made with distinctive double-yolk eggs and noodles personally selected by the owner, alongside teppan dishes such as wagyu steak, year-round Hiroshima oysters, and sea-urchin with spinach.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for okonomiyaki using rare double-yolk eggs and noodles the owner selects personally.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own gorp.jp page confirms the Yagenbori teppanyaki/okonomiyaki, opening late (Mon–Sat 18:00–02:00), the double-yolk 'kodawari' eggs and owner-selected noodles, year-round Hiroshima oysters, wagyu steak, and uni-with-spinach, plus ~45 seats and group banquet plans.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, making cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki here are finished with standard sauces and soy sauce, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is shared with pork, seafood, offal and egg, so any item would have animal-product cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively late-night local teppan spot where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a long way, though English support is limited.

places.name: 広島つけ麺 ひこ 流川店 (Hiroshima Tsukemen Hiko Nagarekawa-ten)
places.gf_detail: The tsukemen noodles are wheat-based and the dipping sauce is soy-sauce based, so the core dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are listed and there is no indication of allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: The dipping broth is built on seafood (魚介) stock and a soy-based sauce, and toppings center on pork, egg and chashu. A no-noodle vegetable order (リャンサイ) exists but the broth itself still uses fish stock, so nothing on the menu is reliably vegan.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (near Ebisu-cho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tsukemen shop in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, known as a go-to spot for a satisfying late-night finish (shime) after drinking, staying open into the early morning hours.

chef_bio.background: A tsukemen shop in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, known as a go-to spot for a satisfying late-night finish (shime) after drinking, staying open into the early morning hours.
chef_bio.philosophy: Customization and a clean, not-too-heavy finish—offering many toppings and even a no-noodle vegetable bowl for a lighter option.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chewy wheat noodles served with a refreshing, lighter dipping sauce built on vegetables and seafood stock, with adjustable spice levels reaching extreme intensities for chili lovers.

anecdotes.text: Diners can dial the spice up dramatically (up to roughly 100x), with the chili blend adjusted per heat tier.
Positioned as an ideal late-night shime meal, the shop stays open until the early morning in the Nagarekawa nightlife area.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: super-men.jp confirms the Nagarekawa Hiroshima-style tsukemen with chewy noodles and a vegetable-and-seafood-stock dipping sauce, spice up to ~100x with the chili blend changed per tier, 18 toppings, a no-noodle 'yasai' vegetable bowl, and 11:00–03:00 hours suited to a post-drinking shime.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce is described as soy-based, and standard soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is built on seafood (魚介) stock alongside vegetables, so it is not suitable for vegans/vegetarians.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual late-night tsukemen counter in the Nagarekawa nightlife district; ordering is simple by size and spice level, but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese words or pointing at the menu helps.

noodles.notes: Firm, springy medium straight noodles served chilled in the Hiroshima tsukemen style, with a crisp, glossy bite meant to carry the spicy soy-vinegar dip.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (Hiroshima-style cold dipping noodles) — order it with your chosen chili level and the popular tororo (grated yam) + nori topping.
ramen.chashu: Char-grilled style pork (the kitchen also turns it into an aburi pork rice bowl); served as a topping alongside the noodles rather than as a braised slab.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based dipping sauce make this unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The dip is a vegetable-and-seafood soy base (fish dashi) and is paired with pork, so it is not vegan as served; a noodle-free vegetable-only order exists but the seafood-based sauce remains, so confirm directly.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook bowl of Hiroshima tsukemen: cold, firm straight noodles dunked into a clear, refreshing soy dip built on vegetables and seafood, brightened with vinegar and a snowfall of white sesame, then dialed up the chili ladder to whatever heat you can handle. The soul here is that bracing sweet-tart-spicy contrast against chilled noodles — a regional lineage born in postwar Hiroshima rather than the hot, thick pork-gyokai dips of Tokyo.

places.highlight: A textbook bowl of Hiroshima tsukemen: cold, firm straight noodles dunked into a clear, refreshing soy dip built on vegetables and seafood, brightened with vinegar and a snowfall of white sesame, then dialed up the chili ladder to whatever heat you can handle. The soul here is that bracing sweet-tart-spicy contrast against chilled noodles — a regional lineage born in postwar Hiroshima rather than the hot, thick pork-gyokai dips of Tokyo.

places.name: ミヤジマベース (Miyajima Base)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→high. Open, legit, allergen-aware GF karaage/curry cafe (rice-flour karaage, GF soy sauce); celiac blogger ate safely, 'very clued up on celiac, separate oil.' But 'dedicated' fails: same source explicitly says 'NOT dedicated gluten free'; own page hedges 'MOST dishes' wheat/egg/dairy-free; 'separate' GF oil implies a non-GF fryer = shared-kitchen CC. — Prior note: A dedicated gluten-free cafe: every dish on the menu is made without wheat, egg, or dairy. The signature non-fried rice-flour karaage uses rice flour and gluten-free (wheat-free) soy sauce, and they fry it in separate, dedicated oil to avoid cross-contact. The owner is a former dietician who developed the recipes and pays close attention to seasonings. Staff are knowledgeable about celiac disease and adjusted their practices (switching to gluten-free soy sauce) after feedback from a celiac customer. Widely regarded by celiac travelers as the safest place to eat on Miyajima.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The two signature mains are animal-based: rice-flour karaage (chicken) and a spiced beef/beef-tongue curry. Dishes are free of egg and dairy, so some sides, the lemon squash, and donuts may suit vegans, but the headline dishes contain meat. Vegans should ask about specific items before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Cafe 10:00-17:30 (L.O. 17:00; cafe-only use L.O. around 16:00); coworking 9:30-17:30. Hours vary by season.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (behind Itsukushima Shrine toward Momijidani Park; ~5 min walk from the shrine)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Miyajima Base is a small cafe set in a roughly century-old renovated farmhouse on the path toward Momiji-dani (Maple Valley) Park, about a five-minute walk behind Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in 2021. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: The owner, Genta Yoshizawa, trained as a dietician and spent around ten years in food service — including hospital and school cafeteria cooking and sports-nutrition guidance. Through that work, and moved by people with food allergies who couldn't share the same dishes as those around them, he set out to create a place where people with allergies could enjoy a memorable meal without worry.
chef_bio.background: Miyajima Base is a small cafe set in a roughly century-old renovated farmhouse on the path toward Momiji-dani (Maple Valley) Park, about a five-minute walk behind Itsukushima Shrine. Yoshizawa first opened the spot as a cafe bar in 2021 and rebranded it as Miyajima Base in 2022.
chef_bio.philosophy: Every dish is made completely without wheat, egg, and dairy. The owner says the smiles of people finally able to eat something delicious without anxiety are what make the work worthwhile, and the cafe is built as an inclusive space where families and groups can share an unhurried meal together.
chef_bio.specialty: Crispy, juicy non-fried rice-flour karaage and a spiced beef-tongue curry are the signature dishes, alongside a fresh Hiroshima lemon squash; rice-flour petit donuts are also on offer.

anecdotes.text: The cafe originally used regular soy sauce in its karaage, but after a customer with celiac disease explained why that wasn't safe, the owner switched to gluten-free soy sauce, adopted separate frying oil, and put 'celiac' on the menu — a change the guest credited as genuine celiac awareness.
The tatami-seating lounge has become especially popular as a relaxed spot where families and groups gather for unhurried meals.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The hiroshima-starters.com owner interview names Genta Yoshizawa (吉沢 元太), a dietician-trained founder with ~10 years in hospital/school food service and sports nutrition, and dates the 2021 cafe-bar opening and 2022 rebrand to Miyajima Base. miyajima-base.com (about-us/food) confirms the ~100-year-old renovated farmhouse 5 min from Itsukushima Shrine, all-dishes wheat/egg/dairy-free, the rice-flour non-fried karaage, spiced beef-tongue curry, lemon squash and rice-flour donuts. endlessdistances.com documents the celiac soy-sauce switch, separate oil and 'celiac' menu note.

gf_cross_contamination.text: They fry the karaage in separate, dedicated oil so there is no cross-contact.
The entire menu is free of wheat, egg, and dairy, so there are no gluten-containing dishes prepared in the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: They use gluten-free (wheat-free) soy sauce; seasonings are also wheat-free.
The owner, a former dietician, developed the recipes and is careful about seasonings being wheat-free.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are very knowledgeable about celiac disease and changed their practices (adopting gluten-free soy sauce) after learning from a celiac customer.
The owner worked about ten years as a dietician across schools, hospitals, and corporate cafeterias before opening the cafe.

positives.text: Described as probably the best place for celiacs to eat anywhere on Miyajima Island, with carefully prepared, excellent karaage.
All meals are wheat-, egg-, and dairy-free and recommended for those seeking a gluten-free meal.

cultural_comfort.note: A foreign student will feel very welcome: the celiac/allergy-aware owner caters specifically to people who normally can't eat out, and the cafe is well-documented in English gluten-free travel guides, so explaining your dietary needs is easy here.

gf_review.why: Open, legit, allergen-aware GF karaage/curry cafe (rice-flour karaage, GF soy sauce); celiac blogger ate safely, 'very clued up on celiac, separate oil.' But 'dedicated' fails: same source explicitly says 'NOT dedicated gluten free'; own page hedges 'MOST dishes' wheat/egg/dairy-free; 'separate' GF oil implies a non-GF fryer = shared-kitchen CC.
red_flags: Most authoritative celiac source explicitly says NOT dedicated GF
Own page hedges 'most dishes' not all
History: previously used regular WHEAT soy sauce while advertising GF, corrected only after a celiac flagged it
'Separate oil' implies a non-GF fryer on premises
gf_review.plain_rice: Good. Rice-centric menu; curry confirmed wheat-free and staff celiac-aware.

places.name: みやじま華屋敷 (Miyajima Hanayashiki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature grilled oysters are finished with soy-butter, miso-butter and lemon-soy-butter sauces, all of which use wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and miso, and other menu items include fried (battered) oysters and croquettes. No gluten-free preparation or labeling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on oysters (raw, grilled, fried), conger eel (anago) and other seafood, with butter, cheese and bacon among the toppings. No plant-based options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (along the approach to Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small family-run shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, on the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. It began in 1970 as a souvenir store and grew into one of the street's go-to spots for grilled oysters, with charcoal grills running in front of the storefront. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small family-run shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, on the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. It began in 1970 as a souvenir store (formerly Sakai Kanko Bussan-ten) and grew into one of the street's go-to spots for grilled oysters, with charcoal grills running in front of the storefront.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled local oysters served in a range of styles -- classic soy-butter and miso-butter, plus playful variations like lemon-soy-butter, cheese-bacon, and garlic butter -- alongside raw oysters, fried oysters, and conger eel (anago) rice bowls.

anecdotes.text: Oysters are grilled over charcoal right at the storefront so you can watch them sizzle, and the shop is known for queues of both international visitors and locals stopping mid-stroll along the arcade.
The shop opened in 1970 as a souvenir store and still sells gifts alongside its grilled-oyster counter, fitting the eat-while-you-walk rhythm of the Omotesando shopping street.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The grilled oysters are finished with soy-butter, miso-butter and lemon-soy-butter sauces, which use standard wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and miso.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up oyster stall used to many foreign visitors, so pointing at the charcoal-grilled oysters and a few Japanese words will get you served comfortably.

places.name: 牡蠣ひよっこ商店 広島本店 (Kaki Hiyokko Shoten Hiroshima Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu is built around fried oysters, oyster tempura, gratin, peperoncino and pots, and Japanese izakaya seasoning relies on regular soy sauce and ponzu (which contain wheat). No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or GF accommodations are documented; a strictly gluten-free diner should avoid this venue.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Essentially every dish is oyster- or seafood-based (oysters, sashimi, smoked oyster, seafood pots), and sauces use fish/soy-based seasonings. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: daily 17:00–01:00 (L.O. 00:00). Official site lists 18:00–07:00 (L.O. 06:30); hours may vary, so confirm when booking.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori / Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima nightlife
places.notes: An oyster-specialty izakaya in central Hiroshima's Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, built on the idea of serving plump Hiroshima oysters year-round at reasonable prices rather than only in winter. The proprietor has worked in the trade for over a decade and holds

chef_bio.background: An oyster-specialty izakaya in central Hiroshima's Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, built on the idea of serving plump Hiroshima oysters year-round at reasonable prices rather than only in winter. The proprietor has worked in the trade for over a decade and holds a wholesale license at the Hiroshima Kusatsu wholesale market, personally visiting oyster facilities and the market to source.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let guests enjoy quality Hiroshima oysters all year at a fishmonger's price by buying directly at wholesale level.
chef_bio.specialty: A wide variety of oyster preparations offered all-you-can-eat -- from steamed and fried oysters to smoked oysters and oyster gratin, dressed with sauces ranging from ponzu to garlic butter -- alongside other fresh seafood dishes.

anecdotes.text: The owner buys at wholesale level with a Kusatsu market license (stall 1508) and visits oyster work-sheds and the market personally to compete on both quality and price.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site states the owner has 10+ years in the trade, holds a Hiroshima Kusatsu market wholesale license (stall 1508), and visits oyster sheds and the market personally; Tabelog lists an oyster all-you-can-eat course and many oyster dishes (steamed, fried, gratin, smoked).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried oysters, oyster-and-root-vegetable tempura and oyster gratin; fried items share oil/breading typical of izakaya and no GF handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as oyster ponzu and pots rely on standard Japanese soy sauce/ponzu, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Virtually all dishes are oyster- or seafood-based, so vegan diners cannot be reliably accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local late-night oyster izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the all-you-can-eat oyster course will get you far, though staff may have limited English.

places.name: 唐々亭 新天地店 (Karakaratei Shintenchi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is tsukemen, made with wheat flour noodles, and the spicy dipping sauce is built on a soy-sauce base (which contains wheat). No gluten-free menu, noodle substitution, or allergen accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The spicy dipping broth is a typical Hiroshima tsukemen sauce (soy sauce, chili, sesame) and the menu centers on meat and dashi-based toppings — chashu (roast pork) is the recommended order, with special and natto bowls also built around animal/fish components. No vegan menu or documented plant-based option.
places.neighborhood: 新天地 (Shintenchi), Naka-ku — Ebisucho area, walking distance
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, counter-only tsukemen shop tucked into the Shintenchi entertainment quarter of central Hiroshima, open since 2001 and serving until the early hours of the morning.

chef_bio.background: A small, counter-only tsukemen shop tucked into the Shintenchi entertainment quarter of central Hiroshima, open since 2001 and serving until the early hours of the morning.
chef_bio.specialty: Spicy Hiroshima-style tsukemen -- wheat noodles served with a chili dipping sauce whose heat is fully adjustable, from a gentle 1x up to a fierce 20x, so both spice novices and chili obsessives can dial it in. The chashu (roast pork) bowl is the house recommendation for first-timers.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the spicy tsukemen here as habit-forming, and the cramped late-night counter has made it a favorite stop for a post-drinking meal in the Shintenchi/Nagarekawa nightlife district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish is wheat-noodle tsukemen prepared in a shared noodle kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The spicy dipping sauce is a standard soy-sauce-based tsukemen broth, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around pork chashu and dashi/animal-based broths; no plant-based or vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, late-night counter ramen-style spot where ordering in basic Japanese (and pointing at the spice level you want) goes a long way — no English menu is documented, so it suits a student comfortable with a little Japanese.

noodles.notes: Moderately thick, chewy straight noodles served chilled, built to grip the fiery red dipping sauce. Portion is adjustable from a half-ball up to three balls and beyond.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-mori tsukemen (チャーシュー盛り) — chilled straight noodles with a soy-and-chili dipping sauce, ordered at your chosen heat from 1x up to 20x (the house even pushes a 30x for the brave).
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu laid over the noodles; the menu also offers leaner steamed chicken-breast (sasami) and shabu-shabu pork versions as alternatives.
notable_toppings: cabbage and shredded vegetables hidden beneath the noodles

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles paired with a soy-based dipping sauce; nothing gluten-free is offered.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on seafood dashi and the standard bowls carry pork or chicken; no documented plant-only broth option.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled straight noodles plunged into a blazing scarlet dipping sauce where layered chili heat (dialed from a gentle 1x to a fearsome 20x and beyond) rides atop a soy tare deepened with seafood dashi and aromatic vegetables. What sets it apart is balance: the burn never buries the umami, so the savory-spicy "umakara" lineage of the city sings through every dip.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima tsukemen — chilled straight noodles plunged into a blazing scarlet dipping sauce where layered chili heat (dialed from a gentle 1x to a fearsome 20x and beyond) rides atop a soy tare deepened with seafood dashi and aromatic vegetables. What sets it apart is balance: the burn never buries the umami, so the savory-spicy "umakara" lineage of the city sings through every dip.

places.name: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 地蔵通り本店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Musashibo (Jizo-dori Ho)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is built on low-hydration wheat noodles, and the soy-sauce version uses regular soy sauce (typically wheat-based). No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Every bowl comes loaded with ground pork as a core ingredient, alongside wheat noodles, sesame or soy-based sauce, and green onions. No plant-based or meat-free version is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sun: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-22:00; Fri, Sat & day-before-holiday: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-00:00; Tue: closed
places.neighborhood: Fujimicho, Naka-ku — central Hiroshima south of Hondori, nea
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Musashibo is one of central Hiroshima's standard-bearers for the city's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen — the broth-less, vigorously-mixed dry version of dan-dan noodles. Opened in 2013 on Jizo-dori in the Fujimicho quarter, the shop has grown into a small Hiroshima insti

chef_bio.background: Musashibo is one of central Hiroshima's standard-bearers for the city's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen -- the broth-less, vigorously-mixed dry version of dan-dan noodles. Opened in 2013 on Jizo-dori in the Fujimicho quarter, the shop has grown into a small Hiroshima institution, expanding to a handful of branches and even a mail-order line while keeping this counter as its flagship.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking centers on the ritual of the bowl: low-hydration noodles meant to be mixed thoroughly the moment they arrive, then tuned by the diner using house-made Musashibo sansho (four kinds of Japanese pepper) and kasho vinegar set out on the table, so each person dials in their own heat and aroma.
chef_bio.specialty: Soupless tantanmen served in two house styles -- a rich sesame (濃厚胡麻) bowl and an aromatic soy-sauce (芳醇醤油) bowl -- each topped with ground pork and a mound of green onion.

anecdotes.text: Every bowl is designed to be self-contained, arriving with all five elements together -- noodles, sauce, ground meat, green onion, and the shop's own sansho.
Diners customize their bowl tableside with four varieties of sansho and kasho vinegar to adjust heat and fragrance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat noodles; soy-sauce version uses standard soy sauce. A shared kitchen handling wheat throughout means high gluten cross-contact risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The aromatic soy-sauce (芳醇醤油) bowl is built on soy sauce, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls contain ground pork as a standard topping; no meat-free preparation is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, fast-paced 20-seat noodle counter where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the two main bowls will carry you through; cash only, so bring yen.

places.name: 岩むら (Iwamura)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The anago-meshi is glazed and served with conger eel cooked in a wheat-based soy sauce tare; udon (wheat noodles) is also on the menu. No indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not rely on this shop.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire menu centers on conger eel (anago), a fish, plus rice bowls and udon typically served with fish/dashi-based and wheat-soy tare. No documented plant-based dishes.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:30 (closed Thursdays)
places.neighborhood: 宮島町 (Miyajima island, Omotesando shopping street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent conger-eel shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, about a ten-minute walk from the ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine. The shop operated as a maker of traditional simmered-conger anago-meshi until early 2021, when the kitchen was renovated  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small independent conger-eel shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, about a ten-minute walk from the ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine. The shop operated as a maker of traditional simmered-conger anago-meshi until early 2021, when the kitchen was renovated and reopened on February 10, 2021. The family is documented as connected to the long-standing Iwamura/Iwamura-momiji-ya lineage on the island.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on a single dish done one way: premium Tsushima conger, charcoal-grilled to order, served as a rice bowl in an unfussy island setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled conger-eel rice (anago-meshi). The shop is described as Miyajima's only place serving anago-meshi made with charcoal-grilled conger rather than the more common simmered style, grilled over high-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal. The conger comes from the Tsushima archipelago off Nagasaki, an origin prized by top sushi houses, and is offered in a standard grade and a premium grade (特上) available only when in stock.

anecdotes.text: Until January 2021 the shop served simmered conger; after a kitchen renovation it reopened on February 10, 2021 as the island's sole charcoal-grilled anago-meshi specialist, grilling over high-grade Tosa binchotan.
The conger is sourced from the Tsushima archipelago off Nagasaki, the same premium origin sought by leading sushi restaurants, and is offered in standard and premium (特上) grades.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi is served with a glaze/tare conventionally made from wheat-containing soy sauce, and the menu also includes udon (wheat noodles); no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built entirely around conger eel and fish/dashi-based rice bowls and udon; no plant-only preparation is documented and cross-contact with fish is effectively unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, ticket-machine island eatery where a basic greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus and ordering are simple but staff may have limited English, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing will get you through.

places.name: お食事処 大和屋 (Oshokuji-dokoro Yamatoya)
places.gf_detail: The core dishes — anago-meshi, oyster cutlet, meat udon — rely on a soy-based glaze (wheat) and breaded/wheat components, so there is no reliable gluten-free option here.
places.vegan_detail: This is a conger-eel and oyster specialist; the signature dishes are built on fish, shellfish, meat udon, and a fish/soy-based sauce, so there are no vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree: ~10:30–16:00 daily (closes when ingredients run out) per one listing; 10:30–15:30 with closures mainly on Tuesdays per a visit report; another lists weekend/holiday-only 11:30–16:00. Treat hours as irregular and confirm by phone.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Hatsukaichi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual, family-run eatery on Miyajima island, set in a building with the feel of a traditional old farmhouse (kominka), with a spacious tatami area for groups. It sits a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the island aquarium, slightly off the busiest stretch of th

chef_bio.background: A casual, family-run eatery on Miyajima island, set in a building with the feel of a traditional old farmhouse (kominka), with a spacious tatami area for groups. It sits a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the island aquarium, slightly off the busiest stretch of the approach, which makes it a quieter alternative when the famous anago-meshi shops near the pier have long lines or require advance booking.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is food that is unforgettable once tasted, with the eel baked until soft and fluffy. The house sauce is made with local Miyajima honey, giving it a distinctive sweet note.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-meshi -- a bowl of rice topped with densely packed, locally favored conger eel that regulars describe as light and delicate compared with freshwater eel, finished with an aromatic sauce. Oyster cutlet and meat udon round out the menu, and takeout anago bento are available.

anecdotes.text: Customers report walls covered with autographed photos of celebrities who have stopped in, and staff who proactively warn that each order takes about 15-20 minutes because the eel is prepared fresh.

One visiting couple found it as a calm hidden gem after being turned away from busier anago shops that required reservations, enjoying their meal with only a couple of other groups in the room before noon.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-meshi and other dishes are finished with a soy-based sauce, which in Japan is normally brewed with wheat and is not gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run island spot with no reservations and cash only; a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, and expect a wait since each anago-meshi is cooked to order.

places.name: みこと屋 (Mikotoya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature dishes use wheat-based soy sauce and tare on the grilled anago, and the dashimaki egg omelette typically contains dashi (fish-based); no dedicated GF preparation is mentioned.
places.vegan_detail: This is an anago (conger eel) specialist; the core dishes are eel, fish dashi, and egg, with no documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat 12:00-14:30 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:30); Thu & Sun 12:00-14:30 (L.O. 13:30) lunch only; bento sold from 11:00 until sold out. Closed Mondays and 1st & 3rd Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Hatsukaichi — Omotesando shopping st
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-style shop tucked into Miyajima's Omotesando area near Itsukushima Shrine, Mikotoya is the kind of place island residents themselves return to rather than a tour-bus stop. The dining room seats only around a dozen guests across a short counter and a few  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small, family-style shop tucked into Miyajima's Omotesando area near Itsukushima Shrine, Mikotoya is the kind of place island residents themselves return to rather than a tour-bus stop. The dining room seats only around a dozen guests across a short counter and a few tables, with cheerful rice-bowl illustrations on the signage.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the Golden Conger Rice Bowl (黄金穴子重), about 3,600 yen: grilled anago laid densely over rice and crowned with a fluffy dashimaki (dashi egg omelette). The eel is finished to a golden color and comes out tender with a gentle, not-too-rich fat. Other offerings include a roast-beef-and-anago version using Hiroshima beef.

anecdotes.text: Mikotoya is a local favorite where island residents return as regulars, valued for its personable, home-style hospitality and open kitchen away from the busy shrine approach.
The grilled anago is finished to a golden color and paired with a thick dashi egg omelette, producing eel that is tender with a mild, not-heavy fat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Blog and Tabelog matome describe the signature 黄金穴子重 (~3,600 yen) with grilled anago and a dashi egg omelette, tender with mild fat; Tabelog lists a 広島牛ローストビーフ穴子重 and 14 seats (6 counter, 8 table), noting Mikotoya is unusual in taking reservations.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The grilled anago is glazed with a house tare/sauce that, like standard Japanese eel sauces, is based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free version is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on conger eel, egg, and fish-based dashi, so animal products are present throughout the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, mostly Japanese-language local shop where island residents eat; pointing at the photo menu works, but little English is documented, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.

places.name: 博多屋 (Hakataya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core product is momiji-manju, a castella sponge cake made from wheat-flour batter, and the fried version (揚げもみじ) coats that same wheat cake in additional batter before frying. The fry bar also sells gansu (a battered fish cake) and other wheat-containing items. There is no gluten-free option here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The castella batter used for momiji-manju is a standard sponge that contains egg (and the shop states no dietary/allergen accommodation). While the red-bean (koshian) filling is itself typically plant-based, the cake around it is not, and the adjacent fry bar fries fish-cake gansu and oyster-based items, so shared frying and egg-containing batter make this unsuitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-17:00, open daily year-round (including holidays)
places.neighborhood: 宮島町 (Miyajima island) — Omotesando shopping street, near Its
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hakataya is a family-run sweets shop on Miyajima that traces its roots to the early Meiji period and has been in business for around 140 years. It began not as a confectioner but as a dealer in Miyajima's traditional craft of shakushi (the carved wooden rice scoops the  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: 宮島町 (Miyajima island), Hiroshima -- on the Omotesando shopping street that leads toward Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.background: Hakataya is a family-run sweets shop on Miyajima that traces its roots to the early Meiji period and has been in business for around 140 years. It began not as a confectioner but as a dealer in Miyajima's traditional craft of shakushi (the carved wooden rice scoops the island is famous for), and over the generations it grew alongside the island's history into a maker of momiji-manju, the maple-leaf-shaped cakes that are Miyajima's signature treat.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its aim as taking the natural character of this place -- its history, culture and scenery -- and passing it on to the future in new forms, while making sweets by hand. They prepare their own castella batter and a homemade smooth koshian bean paste, soaking the beans repeatedly to draw out impurities and bitterness.
chef_bio.specialty: Momiji-manju made fresh on site every day -- both the classic baked version and the fried 'agemomiji,' coated and fried to order so the outside is crisp and the inside fluffy and warm. They keep several flavors, from the traditional smooth red-bean to varieties like chocolate and matcha, and run a 'Fry Bar' where the momiji and local Hiroshima specialties such as gansu are fried after you order.

anecdotes.text: The business started as a wholesaler of Miyajima's wooden rice scoops (宮島杓子) before turning to confectionery, and it has grown together with the island's history over roughly 140 years.
Hakataya makes momiji-manju by hand and sells it the same day, with a 'Fry Bar' that fries momiji and local specialties like gansu fresh to order for the best texture and temperature.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site states Hakataya dates to early Meiji (~140 years), began as a shakushi (wooden rice-scoop) wholesaler before becoming a confectioner, makes its own castella batter and soaked-bean koshian, and runs a 'Fry Bar' serving agemomiji and gansu fried to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The fried items (agemomiji and battered fish-cake gansu) are all wheat-battered and appear to be fried in the same fry bar, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively guaranteed; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The fry bar fries fish-cake gansu and oyster-based items alongside the sweets, so fried sweets share equipment with animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site provides no allergen or dietary-restriction information for its products.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, casual walk-up sweets shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where you can point at the freshly fried momiji and pay with a smile and a few words of Japanese; English signage is limited but ordering is simple.

places.name: かき小屋袋町 海平商店 (Kakigoya Fukuromachi Umihei Shoten)
places.gf_detail: This is an oyster and seafood izakaya with no documented gluten-free program. Core dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and there is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate fryers, or staff allergen handling. Travelers with celiac disease should treat it as not safe.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood, served grilled, steamed, and as sashimi, typically with wheat-based soy sauce. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), open Mon-Sat; closed Sundays (when Monday is a public holiday, open Sunday and closed Monday instead); also closed around New Year (approx. 12/31-1/2) plus occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: 中区袋町 (Fukuromachi), central Hiroshima — a short walk from Ho
places.notes: An oyster shack (kaki-goya) in the Fukuromachi quarter of central Hiroshima, serving Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood year-round rather than only in winter. It presents itself as a direct affiliate of the well-known Ujina kaki-goya lineage, bringing that gr

chef_bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Fukuromachi, Naka-ku); positioned as part of the original Ujina oyster-shack family of shops.
chef_bio.background: An oyster shack (kaki-goya) in the Fukuromachi quarter of central Hiroshima, serving Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood year-round rather than only in winter. It presents itself as a direct affiliate of the well-known Ujina kaki-goya lineage, bringing that grill-shack style into the heart of the city within easy reach of Hondori and the Peace Memorial Park.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its priorities as delicious food, good sake, and customers' smiles, with an emphasis on freshness and sourcing.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal grilling and steaming of in-shell Hiroshima oysters at a long counter where guests watch their food cooked in front of them; signature gangan-yaki (steamed oysters) and an oyster-focused course paired with sake.

anecdotes.text: The house describes itself as a direct-line shop of the original Ujina oyster shacks, bringing the kaki-goya grilling style into central Hiroshima and serving Hiroshima oysters all year rather than only in the winter season.
A signature offering is the steamed gangan-yaki, meant to show off the pure umami of the oysters, alongside an oyster-focused course built around Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea fish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on oysters and seafood served grilled, steamed, and as sashimi, conventionally accompanied by Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared on shared oyster grills and seafood stations, so any non-seafood item would carry a high risk of contact with shellfish and fish.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local oyster izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the counter or menu will go a long way; English support is not documented, so come with basic phrases and a relaxed attitude.

places.name: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き そり家 (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Soriya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe base plus wheat noodles (soba), and the finishing sauce is a wheat/soy-based okonomiyaki sauce. Teppanyaki and izakaya sides also commonly use wheat-soy seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes use egg, pork, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, cooked on a shared griddle, and the okonomiyaki sauce is animal/fish-influenced. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) / 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:00); closed Sundays (may open by arrangement)
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku (~2-3 min walk from Hatchobori Stn, toward Noboricho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Sori Osamu was born in Hiroshima in 1974 and grew up eating the city's signature Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. He spent years running a yakiniku and teppanyaki restaurant before stepping away into another industry, and once his children had grown more independent h

chef_bio.background: Owner Sori Osamu was born in Hiroshima in 1974 and grew up eating the city's signature Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. He spent years running a yakiniku and teppanyaki restaurant before stepping away into another industry, and once his children had grown more independent his desire to cook for people returned. He opened Soriya in December 2018, bringing roughly 18 years of professional griddle experience.
chef_bio.philosophy: He named the place 'そり家' using the character 家 (home) rather than 屋 (shop) so guests would feel they were coming to someone's home. The house creed is hospitality rooted in gratitude and humility: gratitude toward customers, staff, suppliers, and the ingredients themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Standard Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (niku-tama-soba) made with hand-cut cabbage griddled with no added water so it cooks down sweet and fluffy, layered with fresh noodles delivered daily.

anecdotes.text: The cabbage is hand-shredded and cooked using only its own moisture, with no added water, so each okonomiyaki turns out plump and full-volume.
He says 'I can keep going because I have my family,' and the shop is run as a tight two-person team of the owner and one staff member.
He chose 家 ('home') over 屋 ('shop') in the name to make the restaurant feel welcoming, and describes himself as caring most about putting heart into the food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is layered on a wheat-flour crepe with wheat noodles and finished with wheat/soy-based sauce, all cooked on one shared teppan griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki/teppanyaki seasoning relies on wheat-containing okonomiyaki and soy sauces typical of Hiroshima-style cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg, pork, and fish-based ingredients are cooked on the same shared griddle as all dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, family-run neighborhood okonomiyaki counter where a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way; menus and staff are Japanese-oriented, so a foreign student should come ready to point and smile.

places.name: お食事処 梅山 (Oshokujidokoro Umeyama)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional seafood eatery built around anago (conger eel) rice bowls, oyster dishes, and udon. The anago is glazed with a house sweet-savory sauce that is soy-sauce based (contains wheat), udon is wheat noodles, and fried oysters use a wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on conger eel, oysters, and other seafood, with soy-sauce-based and dashi-based seasonings throughout. There are no documented vegan dishes. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00, closed Thursdays
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Omotesando shopping street and
places.notes: [Miyajima] A long-standing family seafood dining hall on Miyajima that has been welcoming visitors for more than a hundred years and is now run by the third generation. It sits a short walk from the ferry terminal and only a few minutes from Itsukushima Shrine, making it a classic (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A long-standing family seafood dining hall on Miyajima that has been welcoming visitors for more than a hundred years and is now run by the third generation. It sits a short walk from the ferry terminal and only a few minutes from Itsukushima Shrine, making it a classic stop for travelers exploring the island.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing fresh local Seto Inland Sea ingredients, with oysters bought in daily, served in a relaxed setting suited to a leisurely break during sightseeing.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-don (conger eel rice bowl) made with Seto Inland Sea conger eel that is grilled without steaming for a thick, tender texture and finished with a house-made sweet-savory glaze; also oyster rice bowls with mountain vegetables, fried and grilled oysters, and trumpet-shell tsuboyaki.

anecdotes.text: Said to have over a century of history and to be carried on by the third generation of the family.
The trumpet-shell tsuboyaki is a favorite that brings repeat customers back, alongside the signature anago and oyster dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-don is finished with a house sweet-savory sauce that is soy-sauce based, which in Japan standardly contains wheat; oyster udon uses wheat noodles and fried oysters use wheat batter.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is entirely seafood-based (conger eel, oysters, shellfish) with dashi and soy seasonings; no plant-only preparations are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, tourist-friendly local seafood hall on Miyajima where pointing at the picture menu and a few polite Japanese words will get you through comfortably, though detailed dietary questions in English may be hard.

places.name: 中華そば 陽気 大手町店 (Chuka Soba Youki Otemachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The sole dish is wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone/chicken/vegetable broth finished with a soy-sauce-based tare (soy sauce contains wheat). There is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The only menu item is a pork-bone and chicken-bone (tonkotsu/torigara) broth ramen with soy-sauce tare; the broth is animal-based, so there is no vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (closed on the 6th of each month and Sundays)
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (right by Chuden-mae tram stop; within walking distance east of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 陽気 traces its roots to 1958 (昭和33年), when Hiroyuki Hara took over the nameplate of a same-named ramen yatai (street cart) from a fellow believer and, with his wife Koto, grew it into one of Hiroshima's defining ramen names. The Otemachi branch is one of several family-r

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima City — born from a postwar ramen yatai in 1958.
chef_bio.background: 陽気 traces its roots to 1958 (昭和33年), when Hiroyuki Hara took over the nameplate of a same-named ramen yatai (street cart) from a fellow believer and, with his wife Koto, grew it into one of Hiroshima's defining ramen names. The Otemachi branch is one of several family-run shops (alongside the Eba main store and others) carrying on that single-bowl tradition for over six decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: Founding matriarch Koto's teaching guides the kitchen: an ordinary day for the cook is a once-in-a-lifetime bowl for the customer, so each serving is made with sincere dedication. The shop deliberately offers only one dish to keep that taste constant.
chef_bio.specialty: 中華そば: a single, unchanging bowl of Hiroshima-style ramen — wheat noodles in a pork-bone, chicken-bone and vegetable broth finished with a house-made soy tare.

anecdotes.text: The business began when Hiroyuki Hara, who had come to Hiroshima for work tied to his church, acquired the 陽気 yatai nameplate from a fellow believer in 1958 and built it with his wife Koto.

Current owner Hara Yumi carried the shop and the family's faith forward after her husband Tsutomu, who had inherited it, passed away; the shop still closes on certain monthly observances, reflecting how the family's faith and the kitchen remain intertwined.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The single dish is wheat ramen noodles cooked in shared boiling water; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-sauce-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is simmered from pork bones, chicken bones and vegetables, so it is animal-based and not separable for vegan diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills, cash-only local ramen counter with a single dish (中華そば) and no English menu, so a few words of Japanese and pointing will make ordering smooth for a foreign student.

noodles.notes: Specially-made medium-thin straight noodles from a local noodle maker, served slightly firm; they soften and drink up the broth toward the end of the bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the shop's single bowl: soy-tare pork-bone broth softened with chicken and vegetables, thin straight noodles, chashu, fine bean sprouts and scallion.
ramen.chashu: Traditional braised pork in two cuts — leaner loin and richer belly — chewy and old-school rather than melt-soft or torched.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui Chinese noodles and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken and vegetables with a soy tare, and bowls come with pork chashu; no plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A living piece of Hiroshima's postwar ramen lineage, born from a 1950s street stall: a soy-tare pork-bone broth deliberately lightened with chicken and a generous load of vegetables, so it drinks gentle and faintly sweet rather than heavy. You taste that local soul — tonkotsu warmth wrapped in soy and a whisper of garlic — over thin straight noodles, the comforting Hiroshima counterpart to its better-known Onomichi cousin.

places.highlight: A living piece of Hiroshima's postwar ramen lineage, born from a 1950s street stall: a soy-tare pork-bone broth deliberately lightened with chicken and a generous load of vegetables, so it drinks gentle and faintly sweet rather than heavy. You taste that local soul — tonkotsu warmth wrapped in soy and a whisper of garlic — over thin straight noodles, the comforting Hiroshima counterpart to its better-known Onomichi cousin.

places.name: 汁なし担々麺 麻沙羅 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Masara)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat noodles (the signature Sichuan version uses thick hand-pulled noodles from aged domestic wheat), and tantan sauces typically include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Soupless tantan noodles are built on minced pork and a sesame/chili sauce typically using soy sauce and animal-based stock; no vegan menu or option is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-15:00, 17:30-21:00; Sun closed. Occasional irregular closures announced via SNS. (Shop relocated to its current Tatemachi 3-20 address on Nov 11, 2025.)
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku — central Hiroshima, walkable from Hondor
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Masara opened in 2013 in central Hiroshima's Naka-ku, dedicated to the city's beloved soupless tantan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen). It has since become a well-known local specialist for the style, and in November 2025 moved to its current spot on Tatemachi.

chef_bio.background: Masara opened in 2013 in central Hiroshima's Naka-ku (Otemachi), dedicated to the city's beloved soupless tantan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen). It has since become a well-known local specialist for the style, and in November 2025 moved to its current spot on Tatemachi.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Masara' comes from the Indian word for a mix of spices (masala): the kitchen blends ten spices, including clove, star anise and cinnamon, tuning the combination to each dish, and lets diners dial in chili heat and Sichuan-pepper numbness on a 0-4 scale.
chef_bio.specialty: Soupless tantan noodles. The signature 'Sichuan' bowl began as a third-anniversary special before joining the regular menu, built on thick house-made noodles made from aged domestic wheat that grip the sauce, finished with Hiroshima-grown scallions and almonds.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Masara' is taken from the Indian term for a blend of spices, reflecting the shop's use of about ten spices such as clove, star anise and cinnamon matched to each menu item.

The flagship Sichuan-style soupless tantan bowl started life as a limited third-anniversary item and proved popular enough to stay on permanently; many regulars add rice to mix into the leftover sauce as a finishing course.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: るるぶ／公式サイトによると、2013年開業の汁なし担々麺専門店（中区大手町）。店名『麻沙羅』はインドの混合香辛料マサラに由来し、丁子・八角・シナモンなど約10種の香辛料を使用。辛さと痺れを0〜4で調整。四川汁なし担々麺は3周年限定から定番化。2025年11月に立町へ移転。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan-style soupless tantan noodles such as those served here are made with wheat noodles and seasoned with chili/sesame sauces that conventionally include wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dish is built on minced pork and animal-derived seasoning typical of tantan noodles; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy counter noodle shop where ordering is simple (point to a menu item and pick a spice level 0-4), but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese words or a translation app help.

noodles.notes: Aged, high-hydration noodles made from domestic wheat flour with a chewy bite. The flagship Sichuan bowl uses specially-ordered thicker, flatter noodles that grip the sauce; standard bowls run a bit thinner. Flat, chewy and built to cling to the chili-sesame dressing.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shisen (Sichuan) shiru-nashi tantanmen — soupless noodles dressed in house chili oil, sesame paste and a soy tare, dusted with sansho, with spice level and numbness each adjustable 0-4. Mix it up gradually from the bottom, then add vinegar/rice to chase the dregs.

gf.note: The noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the tare is soy-sauce based, so the dish is not gluten-free. No documented rice or konjac noodle option.

vegan.note: The dressing is built on chili oil, sesame paste and soy tare with no fish-stock broth (it is soupless), but the standard bowl carries spiced minced pork. No documented fully plant-based version; ask whether a meat-free build is possible.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima's homegrown shiru-nashi tantanmen — the broth-less, Sichuan-inspired noodle that the city made its own. Here the soul lives not in a soup but in a layered dressing of house chili oil, sesame paste and a soy tare bloomed with a ten-spice blend (clove, star anise, cinnamon, cumin) and lip-numbing sansho, scooped up by chewy domestic-wheat noodles.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima's homegrown shiru-nashi tantanmen — the broth-less, Sichuan-inspired noodle that the city made its own. Here the soul lives not in a soup but in a layered dressing of house chili oil, sesame paste and a soy tare bloomed with a ten-spice blend (clove, star anise, cinnamon, cumin) and lip-numbing sansho, scooped up by chewy domestic-wheat noodles.

places.name: 牡蠣祝 (Kakiwai)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The cafe's signature sweet is a Hiroshima lemon rare cheesecake and other cakes, which are wheat-based; coffee, tea and wine are served. No allergen or gluten-free information is published, so a celiac visitor cannot be assured anything is safe here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house specialty is an oyster oil-preserve made by the affiliated oyster restaurant, and the cake menu (cheesecake) uses dairy and likely eggs. No plant-based or vegan items are documented.
places.hours_raw: Listings vary: weekdays 11:00-17:00, Sat/Sun & holidays 11:00-17:30 (Tabelog); an article lists 12:00-16:30. Hours appear to shift; confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), on a hill near the five-story pagoda
places.notes: [Miyajima] Tucked into a renovated traditional wooden house on a quiet hill above Miyajima's busy main street, this hideaway cafe is the second home of a beloved island oyster specialist. Reached by climbing narrow back alleys near the five-story pagoda, it trades the crowds for a (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Tucked into a renovated traditional wooden house on a quiet hill above Miyajima's busy main street, this hideaway cafe is the second venue of a beloved island oyster specialist (Kakiya). Reached by climbing narrow back alleys near the five-story pagoda, it trades the crowds for a slow, gentle pace where you can hear the wind and birdsong and look out over the ferries and oyster rafts of the Seto Inland Sea.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe is built around restraint: no flashy presentation, no background music, valuing emptiness and quiet so that small sounds and the view become the experience. The aim is to let visitors rediscover Miyajima's original calm rather than its tourist bustle.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is an oyster oil-preserve, made by hand in the traditional way from exceptionally high-grade oysters that rarely reach the open market. It is served at the cafe and also sold bottled to take home. Alongside it the kitchen offers a Hiroshima-lemon rare cheesecake made with Seto Inland Sea lemons, plus coffee, Japanese tea, and wine for pairing.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is run by the same people behind the famous Miyajima oyster restaurant Kakiya, and centers its identity on their handcrafted oyster oil-preserve made from rare, top-quality oysters; a cafe serving costs around 800 yen and a take-home bottle around 1,300 yen.
Set deliberately away from the crowds, the space keeps silence instead of music so that 'sound itself becomes valuable,' with an open terrace looking over ferries and oyster rafts.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: co-trip・公式によると、宮島の牡蠣専門店『牡蠣屋』が営む2号店のカフェ。看板は希少な高級牡蠣で作る牡蠣のオイル漬け（店内約800円／瓶詰約1300円）。BGMを流さず『音がないこと自体が価値』とする静けさを重視し、テラスからフェリーや牡蠣筏を望む。瀬戸内レモンのレアチーズケーキも。

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house specialty is an oyster oil-preserve and the cafe also serves dairy-based cheesecake, so the kitchen routinely handles seafood and dairy; no separate vegan preparation is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A tranquil tourist-area cafe used to visitors; basic Japanese greetings help, but note it is cash-only, takes no reservations, and does not admit children under 10.

places.name: 宮島珈琲 (Miyajima Coffee)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free options. The menu centers on coffee, handmade cakes, and light meals such as Hiroshima oyster Japanese-style pasta and rice bowls, all of which involve wheat. No gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is published, so coeliac travelers should not rely on this café.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan options. This is a coffee-focused café whose food menu (handmade cakes, oyster pasta, rice bowls, soft-serve, sundaes) uses dairy, eggs and seafood. Black coffee and plain teas are available, but there is no published plant-based food menu or non-dairy milk information.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri & Sun 9:00-18:00; Sat 9:00-19:00; open daily (no regular closing days)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima / Itsukushima (Omotesando shopping street)
places.notes: [Miyajima] A coffee house tucked into the Omotesando shopping street on the sacred island of Miyajima, a short walk from the ferry pier on the way toward Itsukushima Shrine. Set in an old folk-house-style building with a calm ground floor and a warmer, wood-toned upstairs that was

chef_bio.origin: 宮島・厳島（表参道商店街）
chef_bio.background: A coffee house tucked into the Omotesando shopping street on the sacred island of Miyajima, a short walk from the ferry pier on the way toward Itsukushima Shrine. Set in an old folk-house-style building with a calm ground floor and a warmer, wood-toned upstairs that was renewed in October 2024 to give weary island visitors more room to rest.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is built around serving fresh, high-quality coffee to the international travelers and pilgrims who pass through Miyajima, roasting beans in-house and tailoring its brews to a wide range of tastes.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted coffee, served alongside handmade cakes and light cafe fare such as sweets and cold drinks.

anecdotes.text: The upstairs seating was renovated in October 2024 into a spacious, wood-toned space meant as a welcoming rest stop for island visitors.
Freshly roasted beans from the shop's own roasting are sold to take home so the in-store cup can be recreated at home.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式・食べログによると、宮島表参道商店街の自家焙煎珈琲店。古民家風の建物で2024年10月に2階を改装。焙煎豆の販売や手作りケーキなども提供。（牡蠣パスタの提供は確認できず削除。）

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed tourist-area café where a smile, a point at the menu and a few words of Japanese will get you a coffee and cake with ease.

places.name: 一麺天に通ず (Ichimen Ten ni Tsuzu)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. Both the ramen and tsukemen/abura-soba noodles are wheat-based, and the soy-sauce-driven broths use standard soy sauce (which contains wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The signature broths are built on fish and shellfish stock (nodoguro/blackthroat seabream, bonito, and shrimp) plus shoyu, and toppings such as chashu and ajitama (seasoned egg) are common. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.neighborhood: Ebisu-cho, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, near Kanayamacho tram
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is run by a Hiroshima native who went to Osaka to train in ramen, first at the well-known Jinrui Mina Menrui and then at its offshoot Sekai ga Men de Michiru Toki, before returning home to open this counter shop in July 2019.
