chef_bio.background: The shop is run by a Hiroshima native who went to Osaka to train in ramen, first at the well-known Jinrui Mina Menrui and then at its offshoot Sekai ga Men de Michiru Toki, before returning home to open this counter shop in 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: A precise, quality-focused approach to seafood-forward shoyu ramen carried over from the Osaka lineage where the owner trained.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen built on a refined seafood broth (bonito, mackerel and round-herring powders with a house aromatic oil), with a standout nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) soy ramen offered as a rotating limited item; the menu also runs several other shoyu ramen, tsukemen, and abura-soba (brothless) bowls, with pairings like oi-meshi (rice to finish in the soup).

anecdotes.text: After training at Jinrui Mina Menrui and then Sekai ga Men de Michiru Toki in Osaka, the Hiroshima-born owner brought that seafood-broth shoyu style back to his hometown.
The shop has since grown into a small local group with additional branches around Hiroshima.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ラーメンwalker・もっとスーパー麺等によると、大阪『人類みな麺類』系列『世界が麺で満ちる時』出身の広島出身店主が2019年（令和元年）に開業。カツオ・サバ・ウルメ等の魚介粉と自家製香味油の醤油出汁。のどぐろ醤油は限定麺。2〜3号店へ拡大。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths are shoyu (soy-sauce) based, and standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from fish and shellfish (nodoguro, bonito, shrimp), so no bowl can be made vegan or vegetarian on request.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only ramen bar where ordering is straightforward but signage and conversation are in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help; expect to be seated quickly and eat at the counter.

noodles.notes: Chewy medium-thick noodles made with whole-grain (zenryufun) flour for a springy, mochi-mochi bite that stands up to the deep seafood shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Katsuo (bonito) shoyu ramen — a clear seafood-shoyu bowl finished with house aromatic oil; the Ebi (shrimp) bowl and the signature "Orange and Shellfish" (orange-oil + clam) ramen are the other standouts. Finish with the oi-meshi rice topped with fu (wheat gluten) and the leftover broth.
ramen.chashu: Rare-cooked pork shoulder loin (buta kata-rosu no rea chashu) — tender, ham-like slices served low-and-slow rather than braised soft.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented, and the oi-meshi rice course is served with wheat gluten (fu).

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a fish dashi (bonito, mackerel, round-herring) under a shoyu tare, topped with pork chashu; no plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Hiroshima shop that builds its soul on a clear, gentle-sweet seafood dashi — bonito, mackerel and round herring layered under a soy tare and lifted by a house aromatic oil — rather than any classic regional template. It is best known for inventive bowls like its citrus-oil "orange and shellfish" ramen, a creative riff you taste nowhere else.

places.highlight: A modern-original Hiroshima shop that builds its soul on a clear, gentle-sweet seafood dashi — bonito, mackerel and round herring layered under a soy tare and lifted by a house aromatic oil — rather than any classic regional template. It is best known for inventive bowls like its citrus-oil "orange and shellfish" ramen, a creative riff you taste nowhere else.

places.name: 中華そば 寿々女 (Chuka Soba Suzume)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style chuka soba (ramen) shop. The noodles are wheat and the broth is a soy-sauce pork-bone base; Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented, so it is not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-bone-and-soy-sauce ramen shop whose core dishes (chuka soba, braised-pork rice, tantanmen) center on pork and animal-based broth. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-15:00 (until the soup sells out). Thursday only also open evenings 19:30-22:00; Saturday only also open evenings 19:00-22:00. Closed Sundays and the 14th of each month (official site). Tabelog lists lunch 11:30-15:00, closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Funairi-saiwaicho, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (by Funairi-saiwaicho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Suzume continues the legacy of a beloved Hiroshima ramen shop of the same name that operated for some sixty years before closing in 2015, to the regret of many regulars. It reopened in February 2016 at this Funairi location, carrying forward the original 'traditional fl

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (continuing a local lineage dating to the mid-20th century)
chef_bio.background: Suzume continues the legacy of a beloved Hiroshima ramen shop of the same name that operated for some sixty years before closing in 2015, to the regret of many regulars. It reopened in February 2016 at this Funairi location, carrying forward the original 'traditional flavor' and the so-called 'small bird' (suzume) lineage of Hiroshima ramen, whose postwar identity grew out of the city's street-vendor noodle culture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the nostalgic traditional taste of the original shop while gradually evolving the menu, embodying a 'tradition and innovation in tandem' approach.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional Hiroshima-style chuka soba in a soy-sauce pork-bone broth, served with a mild house-made chili oil; also offers a mini braised-pork rice bowl and newer Sichuan-influenced dishes such as beni-kara soba and brothless tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: The current proprietor came from a corporate career drawing air-conditioning blueprints, and put that technical background to use by building custom partitions and fabricating the ceiling ductwork himself at the new shop.
Long associated only with a single 'chuka soba' dish, the revived shop surprised longtime customers by adding Sichuan-influenced items like spicy red noodles and brothless tantanmen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログマガジンによると、約60年続き2015年に閉店した名店を継ぎ2016年2月に舟入で再開。店主は元は空調図面を描く会社員で、仕切りや天井ダクトを自作。醤油豚骨の中華そばに加え紅辛そば・汁なし担々麺など四川系も追加。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as a soy-sauce pork-bone (醤油豚骨) base characteristic of Hiroshima ramen; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are pork-based ramen and braised-pork rice in animal broth; no vegetarian or vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional cash-only lunch counter with a ticket-machine ordering system and little to no English, so it helps to arrive knowing what you want to order and to have cash ready.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight wheat noodles with a slippery, springy bite that grips the soy-pork broth from first slurp to last.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (the classic Hiroshima soy-pork bowl)
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, sliced with a firm chew and a deep, well-seasoned savor with a touch of fatty sweetness.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork bones and soy, finished with pork chashu and pork-fat richness; no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A torchbearer of old-school Hiroshima chuka soba, this bowl carries the lineage of a beloved 60-year shop: a pork-bone-and-soy broth that looks dark and weighty yet drinks gentle and clean, its umami creeping in slowly beneath a fragrant, lightly tangy soy nose. You taste post-war Hiroshima in the soup — the city's signature marriage of mellow tonkotsu depth and soft-spoken shoyu, slurped with thin straight noodles and crisp bean sprouts.

places.highlight: A torchbearer of old-school Hiroshima chuka soba, this bowl carries the lineage of a beloved 60-year shop: a pork-bone-and-soy broth that looks dark and weighty yet drinks gentle and clean, its umami creeping in slowly beneath a fragrant, lightly tangy soy nose. You taste post-war Hiroshima in the soup — the city's signature marriage of mellow tonkotsu depth and soft-spoken shoyu, slurped with thin straight noodles and crisp bean sprouts.

places.name: 汁なし担担麺 きさく (Shirunashi Tantanmen Kisaku)
places.gf_detail: The single signature dish is soup-less tantanmen built on wheat ramen noodles, so it is not gluten-free. The savory sauce blends chicken and dried-sardine broth with Sichuan peppercorn and almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce or seasonings. There is no gluten-free menu or noodle option, and the kitchen is a tiny single-dish noodle counter not set up for gluten avoidance. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The house tantanmen is topped with ground meat and dressed in a sauce made from chicken and dried-sardine (niboshi) broth, so the core dish is not vegan or vegetarian. The optional soft-boiled egg topping and the wheat noodles add further animal/non-vegan elements. There is no documented plant-based version. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:00 / Sun 11:00-15:00 / holidays 11:00-14:00 / closed Wednesdays, plus one irregular day off per month
places.neighborhood: Funairi-kawaguchicho (Naka-ku; right by Funairi-saiwaicho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kisaku opened in central Hiroshima in 2001 as an ordinary ramen shop, but the owner struggled to draw customers. Determined to turn things around, he traveled to Sichuan Province in China to study tantanmen at the source, then spent months experimenting back home to ada

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (founded here in central Funairi, Naka Ward)
chef_bio.background: Kisaku opened in central Hiroshima in 2001 as an ordinary ramen shop, but the owner struggled to draw customers. Determined to turn things around, he traveled to Sichuan Province in China to study tantanmen at the source, then spent time experimenting back home to adapt what he had learned. The result was a soup-less tantanmen that went on to become a Hiroshima staple, with Kisaku widely regarded as the original shop that started the city's now-famous shirunashi tantanmen.
chef_bio.philosophy: A generous, no-frills counter ethos: a single dish done well, adjustable spice levels (including half and quarter heat), and famously cheap white rice (about 50 yen) offered all-you-can-eat to pair with the noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Soup-less tantanmen kept deliberately minimal: just noodles, green onion and ground meat, dressed in a secret sauce that marries chicken and dried-sardine broth with the tingling numbness of Sichuan peppercorn (sansho/huajiao). Diners are told to lift and toss the noodles from the bottom at least ten times before eating so the sauce coats everything.

anecdotes.text: The founder went all the way to Sichuan to apprentice in tantanmen and reworked the recipe at home until he landed on the soup-less version that locals now treat as the origin of the genre in Hiroshima.
House guidance: mix the noodles by lifting from the bottom more than ten times before the first bite so the sauce and peppercorn distribute evenly.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 舟入おでかけガイド・home-tv等によると、2001年開業（諸説あり）。店主が四川省で担担麺を学び汁なし版を考案、広島の汁なし担担麺の元祖とされる。ネギと挽肉、鶏と煮干の秘伝ダレに山椒。ご飯約50円食べ放題。食前に10回以上混ぜる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a single-dish wheat-noodle counter; noodles are boiled in shared water and the sauce contains gluten-bearing seasonings, so gluten contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature sauce is a chicken-and-dried-sardine broth blend; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated and standard wheat-based seasoning should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core dish uses ground meat and a fish/chicken broth sauce, and is prepared on a single shared counter line, so animal ingredients are integral to the only menu item.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, cash-only 15-seat counter run by locals where Japanese is the working language and ordering is quick and simple; point at the one signature dish and you'll be fine, but don't expect English menus or staff.

places.name: 五エ門 胡町本店 (Goemon Ebisucho Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles (described as 100% domestic wheat), and the kitchen is a shared teppan, so there is no safe gluten-free path here for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki and teppanyaki rely on pork, egg, seafood (oysters, anago/conger eel), and fish-derived sauces; no documented vegan offering.
places.neighborhood: Ebisu-cho, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (right by Hiroden Ebisu-cho / Hatchobori tram stops)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A locally founded, independent okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of Hiroshima's downtown, a short walk from the Ebisucho/Hatchobori streetcar stops. Built-in grills at every table let guests enjoy everything piping hot, and the room is known for an energetic

chef_bio.background: A locally founded, independent okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of Hiroshima's downtown, a short walk from the Ebisucho/Hatchobori streetcar stops. Built-in grills at every table let guests enjoy everything piping hot, and the room is known for an energetic, casual atmosphere where staff calls and customer laughter fill the space.
chef_bio.philosophy: Self-described as an okonomiyaki shop that 'competes with flavor and energy' (味と元気で勝負するお好み焼き屋) — firmly seasoned, crisp-textured food served in a warm, high-spirited room.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki made with noodles of 100% domestic wheat that are freshly boiled then grilled crisp, plus over 60 teppanyaki dishes. The house signature is the 'Goemon-maki' (五ェ門巻き), born from staff meals and refined over time, combining yakisoba, pork, egg, and green onion with a spicy kimchi accent. Seasonal Hiroshima specialties like winter oyster butter-grill and anago also feature, paired with local sake and house nigori.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'Goemon-maki' roll started as a staff meal and was perfected through repeated trial and error before joining the menu.
The shop carries the cheerful Hiroshima-dialect catchphrase 'まあいっぺん食うてみんさい、うまいけぇ！' (just give it a try, it's delicious!).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 旅色・公式によると、胡町の独立系お好み焼・鉄板焼店。国産小麦100%の麺、鉄板料理60種超。看板の五ェ門巻きはまかないから誕生。牡蠣バター焼や穴子など季節物も。『味と元気で勝負するお好み焼き屋』を掲げる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan grill alongside wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, so gluten contact is unavoidable across the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce typically contains wheat; no gluten-free sauce is documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are grilled on a communal teppan shared with pork, egg, and seafood (oysters, anago), making vegan separation impractical.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, welcoming local okonomiyaki house where solo diners and newcomers are at ease, but staff speak mostly Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help you order comfortably.

places.name: 間借麺業 あらや識 (Magari Mengyo Araya Shiki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dishes are ramen and tsukemen built on wheat noodles (hand-kneaded medium-thick noodles), and the soy-sauce-based broth contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is animal-based, made from Shiga-raised black chicken (Omi kurodori) combined with several dried fish ingredients including Ibuki Island niboshi (dried anchovy). Toppings include marinated egg, duck, and pork. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (lunch only); closed Sunday
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka-ku (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area)
places.cuisine: Niboshi (dried sardine) shoyu ramen & kombu-water tsukemen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Araya Shiki is a 'magari mengyo' (borrowed-space noodle business): a lunch-only ramen operation that sets up inside the premises of a charcoal-grill izakaya, serving from late morning until early afternoon before the space returns to its evening life. It is a small, cou

chef_bio.background: Araya Shiki is a 'magari mengyo' (borrowed-space noodle business): a lunch-only ramen operation that sets up inside the premises of a charcoal-grill izakaya, serving from late morning until early afternoon before the space returns to its evening life. It is a small, counter-only place tucked into central Hiroshima's lively Nagarekawa nightlife district.
chef_bio.specialty: A delicate light soy-sauce ramen (tanrei shoyu ra-men) and a kombu-water tsukemen that is still uncommon in Hiroshima. The clear broth is built from Shiga-raised black chicken (Omi kurodori) blended with about four kinds of dried seafood, including niboshi (dried anchovy) from Ibuki Island, and paired with smooth, medium-thick hand-kneaded noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a lunch-only 'borrowed-space' venture, cooking inside a charcoal-grill izakaya during the day and serving a refined chicken-and-niboshi shoyu bowl rather than a heavy tonkotsu style.
Offering a kombu-water tsukemen as a standing menu item is noted as a rarity for Hiroshima.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・sun-maltによると、炭火焼西尾の店内を昼だけ間借りする昼営業限定のラーメン。流川／銀山町。淡麗醤油らぁ麺と広島では珍しい昆布水つけ麺。滋賀・近江黒鶏に伊吹島の煮干など約4種の乾物を合わせた清湯、手もみ中太麺。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles and wheat-containing soy-sauce broth are the core of every dish, so gluten is present throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in standard Japanese ramen contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from chicken and multiple dried fish (niboshi), and the shop is a daytime tenant inside a charcoal-grill (meat/fish) izakaya, so animal ingredients are pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only lunch spot run by one cook; ordering is in Japanese and it is cash only, so a foreign student should be ready with basic Japanese and come prepared with yen.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick hand-kneaded (te-momi) noodles with a clean wheat aroma and a smooth, satisfying slurp. For the tsukemen they are served bathed in glossy kombu-water (kombu-sui), which clings to the strands and adds a silky body and gentle umami before they are dipped.

ramen.signature_bowl: 煮干し醤油 昆布水つけ麺 (niboshi-shoyu kombu-water tsukemen) — handmade noodles draped in kombu water, dipped into a sharp, clean niboshi-shoyu broth, finished with a squeeze of sudachi. The 淡麗醤油らぁ麺 (light shoyu ramen, optionally with ajitama or extra duck/meat) is the other must-order.
ramen.chashu: Not clearly documented; the menu offers an "extra meat" upgrade that includes duck (using the Omi black chicken / kamo lineage of the broth), but the precise cut and preparation are not confirmed.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the tare is soy-sauce based (wheat); no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken plus iriko/niboshi and other dried seafood; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg, no katsuo or animal stock) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Hiroshima bowl whose soul is a delicate W-soup: Shiga's prized Omi black chicken married to Ibuki-island iriko and a quartet of dried goods, yielding a clear, refined niboshi-shoyu that is bracingly savory yet free of bitterness. The kombu-water tsukemen — a style still rare in Hiroshima — lets you taste the noodle and the dashi separately before they meet, a contemporary craft-ramen statement rather than a regional gotochi tradition.

places.highlight: A modern-original Hiroshima bowl whose soul is a delicate W-soup: Shiga's prized Omi black chicken married to Ibuki-island iriko and a quartet of dried goods, yielding a clear, refined niboshi-shoyu that is bracingly savory yet free of bitterness. The kombu-water tsukemen — a style still rare in Hiroshima — lets you taste the noodle and the dashi separately before they meet, a contemporary craft-ramen statement rather than a regional gotochi tradition.

places.name: おこのみ魂　悟空 (Okonomi-damashii Goku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with wheat-flour batter and wheat soba/ramen noodles, finished with a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce. Teppanyaki and izakaya dishes also rely on wheat-containing sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature dishes center on pork, egg, and noodles, with Hiroshima oysters and beef offal among the teppanyaki options; the okonomiyaki sauce and batter are not plant-based, and no vegan items are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 daily (last order food 21:00, drinks 21:30). Counter seating only 13:00-17:30. Closed year-end/New Year.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori (Naka-ku, central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, long-running okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter tucked into Hiroshima's Hatchobori business district, just south of the Hiroshima YMCA. It has become a go-to for local office workers seeking an unfussy, satisfying plate.

chef_bio.background: A small, long-running okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter tucked into Hiroshima's Hatchobori business district, on the south side of the Hiroshima YMCA professional school. It has become a go-to for local office workers seeking an unfussy, satisfying plate.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen presses each okonomiyaki by hand so the ingredients meld together, staying focused on doing one classic dish well.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style 肉玉そば (pork-and-egg okonomiyaki layered with noodles), built since opening around ultra-thin fresh noodles. A house variation, the spicy '悟空焼き' (Goku-yaki) loaded with chili, is named for the shop itself; aburi-cheese okonomiyaki is another recommendation.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature spicy 'Goku-yaki' uses extremely thin noodles infused with habanero and topped with a heap of chili pepper, named after the restaurant.
Sits on the south side of the Hiroshima YMCA professional school with a clean, black-toned interior, and is a regular haunt of local office workers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式・街画コム等によると、八丁堀・YMCA専門学校南側の黒基調のお好み焼店。手押し焼きの極細生麺の肉玉そばが看板。激辛の看板『悟空焼き』はハバネロ練り込み麺に唐辛子を大量に。炙りチーズも。地元サラリーマンに人気。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is finished with a standard okonomiyaki/soy-based sauce, which contains wheat; the batter and noodles are also wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood okonomiyaki counter popular with local office workers; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English is unlikely.

places.name: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 じぞう通り本店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Musashibo (Jizo-dori Ma)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is wheat-based ramen-style noodles (low-hydration wheat noodles), and the savory sauce and toppings typically include soy-sauce-based seasoning. No gluten-free menu or noodle alternative is documented. Celiac/gluten-sensitive travelers should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The core dish is built around ground pork (挽き肉) mixed into wheat noodles, plus chili/Sichuan-pepper sauce that is animal- and soy-based. No vegan or vegetarian version is documented on the official site or listings.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sun 11:00-15:00, 18:00-22:00 / Fri, Sat & days before holidays 11:00-15:00, 18:00-24:00 / closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 中区富士見町・立町 (Fujimi-cho / Tatemachi, central Hiroshima, near H
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Musashibo is a specialist in Hiroshima's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles), the brothless, mix-it-yourself style of spicy Sichuan-influenced noodles that became a citywide craze. The shop is part of the wave of dedicated tantanmen houses that grew

chef_bio.background: Musashibo is a specialist in Hiroshima's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles), the brothless, mix-it-yourself style of spicy Sichuan-influenced noodles that became a citywide craze. The shop is part of the wave of dedicated tantanmen houses that grew out of Hiroshima's noodle revival, and it has since become one of the city's go-to names for the dish.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the specialty-shop flavor straight to the bowl (and to the home kitchen via their mail-order sets), emphasizing the interplay of fragrant Sichuan pepper, chili, and freshly mixed noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Shiru-nashi tantanmen: low-hydration wheat noodles tossed with a spicy savory sauce, ground pork, scallions and the shop's own blend of Sichuan pepper. Diners are encouraged to first taste it as-is, then dial it in with four kinds of Sichuan pepper, a splash of vinegar, and house sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop is closely tied to Hiroshima's tantanmen revival led by the pioneering shop Kunimatsu, and the two have even joined forces in a collaboration counter at Hiroshima Station's ekie complex.
Customers are guided to eat it in stages, first plain, then adding four varieties of Sichuan pepper, vinegar, and the house sauce to adjust the heat and aroma to taste.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式・ekieによると、広島の汁なし担担麺専門店。低加水麺にタレ・挽肉・ネギ・武蔵坊山椒。まず素のまま、次に四種の山椒・（高麗人参）酢・自家製タレで調整。通販セットも。ブームを牽引した『くにまつ』と広島駅ekieでコラボ店を展開。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is built on wheat noodles, so the entire menu centers on gluten; there is no documented gluten-free preparation or separate handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The savory sauce for shiru-nashi tantanmen is typically soy-sauce based, which in Japan generally contains wheat; no gluten-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ground pork is mixed directly into the noodles as a core component, so all bowls are prepared with meat and there is no documented vegan separation.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, fast-paced local noodle shop where ordering is simple (pick a spice level) and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will get you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: Specially ordered low-hydration, unsalted noodles with a firm, springy bite. Cut in half before boiling so they mix easily with the sauce, then boiled only about 33 seconds so they grip the chili-sesame tare. The 'batsubatsu' (crinkled, dense) low-hydration character is central to the bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen, "rich sesame" (nokō goma) version — the broth-less dan dan you mix vigorously at the table, then dial in heat by choosing a spice level (1-4) and dosing your own sansho from the four table jars.
notable_toppings: self-serve four sansho/Sichuan pepper varieties (red, green, young, Musashibo flower pepper)
Korean ginseng vinegar and house tare on the table

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare option mean the bowl is not celiac-safe; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The dish centers on spiced ground pork over the noodles, with a chili-sesame/soy tare; no plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A flagship of Hiroshima's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen — the city's soupless, mix-it-yourself dan dan tradition where the soul lives not in a broth but in a chili oil and the prickle of sansho. Here a medicinal-spiced rauyu (chili oil with cinnamon, star anise and dried tangerine peel) coats low-hydration noodles, and four self-serve Sichuan peppers let each diner sculpt the tongue-numbing mala finish to taste.

places.highlight: A flagship of Hiroshima's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen — the city's soupless, mix-it-yourself dan dan tradition where the soul lives not in a broth but in a chili oil and the prickle of sansho. Here a medicinal-spiced rauyu (chili oil with cinnamon, star anise and dried tangerine peel) coats low-hydration noodles, and four self-serve Sichuan peppers let each diner sculpt the tongue-numbing mala finish to taste.

places.name: 鉄板 お好み焼 くらはち (Teppan Okonomiyaki Kurahachi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and teppan dishes are typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians as a destination. The signature okonomiyaki includes egg, pork, and seafood, and the menu centers on Hiroshima oysters, teppan meat/seafood dishes, and izakaya fare cooked on a shared griddle. Sauces use fish-derived and wheat-based ingredients. No vegan options are documented; the pesticide-free vegetable side dishes may be the only plant-forward items, but preparation is not confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: Official site: 18:00-03:00 (L.O. 02:30), closed Tuesday and Wednesday. Aggregator listings vary: HotPepper shows 18:00-02:00 daily with no regular closed day; Tabelog shows 18:00-01:00. Dinner-only.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (near Kanayamacho tram stop, in the Nagarekawa back alleys)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into a back alley off the main Nagarekawa strip, a short walk from the Ginzan-cho streetcar stop, Kurahachi is a small, relaxed teppan-and-okonomiyaki izakaya in the heart of central Hiroshima's nightlife district. It is the kind of unpretentious neighborhood spo

chef_bio.background: Tucked into a back alley off the main Nagarekawa strip, a short walk from the Ginzan-cho streetcar stop, Kurahachi is a small, relaxed teppan-and-okonomiyaki izakaya in the heart of central Hiroshima's nightlife district. It is the kind of unpretentious neighborhood spot where you can drop in for a quiet solo drink at the counter or take over the floor with a group.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners aim for a warm, easygoing atmosphere where anyone, from solo drinkers to large parties, can relax without standing on ceremony.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'Kurahachi-yaki,' built on crisp griddled noodles made by a dedicated noodle maker. The kitchen deliberately tops the okonomiyaki with a raw egg so guests can break and fold it in to cook it to their own taste, and offers generous toppings like Hiroshima oysters, kimchi, and avocado for customizing. Alongside the okonomiyaki are teppan dishes and izakaya plates.

anecdotes.text: The crisp noodles in the signature Kurahachi-yaki come from a dedicated noodle maker, and the egg is served raw on top so guests cook and mix it to their own preference.
The izakaya welcomes everyone from solo drinkers at the counter to large groups, and lets diners customize the okonomiyaki with generous toppings such as Hiroshima oysters, kimchi and avocado.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式・ホットペッパーによると、流川・銀山町の鉄板お好み焼き居酒屋。看板『くらはち焼き』は製麺所特注のパリパリ麺で、生玉子を乗せ客が好みに焼く。牡蠣・キムチ・アボカド等トッピング豊富。（崎島の無農薬野菜の記載は確認できず削除。）

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, friendly local teppan izakaya that welcomes solo diners and groups alike, but it is a small Japanese-language neighborhood spot, so basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 岩村もみじ屋 (Iwamura Momijiya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Momiji manju are baked from a wheat-flour batter (a castella-style sponge), so the cakes contain gluten. There is no gluten-free option here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Momiji manju batter is conventionally made with eggs (and often honey) in addition to wheat flour and sugar; the filling is sweet azuki bean paste (Hokkaido azuki + white sugar), which is plant-based, but the cake itself is not vegan. No vegan version is documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (Tabelog). Official/secondary source notes 9:00-17:00, closes when sold out. Irregular days off; some sources note Wednesday closures (subject to variation). May close early once sold out.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, near Itsukushima Shrine (on the route toward Daish
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Iwamura Momijiya is one of the oldest momiji-manju makers on Miyajima, founded at the end of the Meiji era (early 1900s) and run by the Iwamura family for generations. It sits a little off the main shopping arcade, along the route between Itsukushima Shrine and Daisho-i (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Iwamura Momijiya is one of the oldest momiji-manju makers on Miyajima, founded at the end of the Meiji era (early 1900s) and run by the Iwamura family for generations. It sits a little off the main shopping arcade, along the route between Itsukushima Shrine and Daisho-in temple, and is known to locals as a quiet, understated gem.
chef_bio.philosophy: A dedication to handcrafted bean paste: using carefully selected azuki and slow-cooking it for hours, and baking the cakes to order so guests can eat them hot.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked momiji manju — both the classic smooth koshian and the shop's signature tsubu-an version ('tsubu-momiji') — made with custom house-cooked bean paste from carefully selected Hokkaido azuki and white sugar, the paste simmered for six to eight hours. Many visitors single out the aroma of the bean paste and the pleasure of eating the cakes warm, fresh off the iron.

anecdotes.text: The shop is credited with originating the tsubu-an (whole-bean) momiji manju: when Prince Takamatsu visited Itsukushima on May 10, 1934, he asked the then-owner Iwamura Eikichi, 'Don't you have one with tsubu-an?' — and that question led to the creation of 'tsubu-momiji'.
Its tsubu-momiji was honored with the Chairman's Award at the 21st National Confectionery Exposition in 1988.

A visitor recounts arriving to find the shop had sold out for the day, yet a young staff member kindly offered them a sample cake that hadn't met the selling weight — and notes the wonderful aroma of the bean paste outside the shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Wikipedia等によると、明治末期創業の宮島の老舗もみじ饅頭店で岩村家が代々経営。1934年5月10日、高松宮が厳島来訪時に主人・岩村栄吉へ粒あんを所望したのを機に『つぶもみじ』を考案。1988年第21回全国菓子大博覧会で会長賞。北海道産小豆を6〜8時間炊き、焼きたてを提供。

positives.text: The bean paste filling is made simply from carefully selected Hokkaido azuki beans and white granulated sugar, with no documented additives.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional family sweet shop where a smile, a point, and a few words of Japanese go a long way; English is unlikely, but ordering momiji manju is simple and friendly.

places.name: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 上八丁堀店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Musashibo Kamihatchobor)
places.gf_detail: The single signature dish is shirunashi tantanmen built on wheat noodles, dressed with a soy-sauce-based or sesame-based tare and topped with ground pork. Wheat noodles and soy sauce mean it is not gluten-free, and there is no documented gluten-free option or dedicated preparation.
places.vegan_detail: The dish is centered on ground pork (挽き肉) over wheat noodles with a soy-sauce/sesame tare, so it is not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based version is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00, 17:30-21:00 / Sat 11:00-15:00 / closed Sundays (hours may vary)
places.neighborhood: Kamihatchobori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Hatchobori; in front of Shukkeien Garden)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A specialist devoted almost entirely to one dish: Hiroshima-style shirunashi tantanmen, the city's beloved brothless dan dan noodle. The shop grew into multiple locations around Hiroshima (with a branch reaching as far as Yokohama) on the strength of that single focus,

chef_bio.origin: 広島市中区上八丁堀（八丁堀・縮景園前エリア）
chef_bio.background: A specialist devoted almost entirely to one dish: Hiroshima-style shirunashi tantanmen, the city's beloved brothless dan dan noodle. From its Fujimicho main shop the business grew to several locations — a Yokogawa branch in Hiroshima's Nishi ward and a branch reaching as far as Yokohama (Okurayama, Kanagawa) — on the strength of that single focus. It is reported to share a parent operation, the eatery 'Himawari,' from which its popular Singapore eggplant curry side dish is inherited.
chef_bio.philosophy: まずはそのまま麺と具をよく混ぜて味わい、その後に四種の山椒や高麗人参酢で自分好みに変化させて楽しむという食べ方を勧めている。
chef_bio.specialty: 汁なし担々麺。低加水のバツバツ麺に挽き肉・ネギ・自家製タレを絡め、四種の山椒・高麗人参酢・自家製タレで味変しながら食べるスタイル。芳醇醤油と濃厚ゴマの2系統がある。

anecdotes.text: 麺はバツバツの低加水で、すべての具材を絡めて食べるのが身上。卓上の四種の山椒・高麗人参酢・自家製タレで途中から味を変えていくのが店の薦める食べ方。
看板の汁なし担々麺は『芳醇醤油』と『濃厚ゴマ』の2系統で、香り高さが評判。親店『ひまわり』から受け継いだシンガポールナスカレーなどのサイドもある。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトとsyokukiのレビューで、芳醇醤油・濃厚ゴマの2系統、低加水麺、四種の山椒・高麗人参酢での味変、横浜を含む複数店舗、親店『ひまわり』由来のシンガポールナスカレーを確認。焼き鳥・つけ麺との系列関係は裏付けが取れず削除。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish uses low-hydration wheat noodles, so wheat is central to the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: One of the two signature styles is a soy-sauce (醤油) based tantanmen; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dish is topped with ground pork (挽き肉) as a standard component, mixed directly into the noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local noodle counter where ordering is quick and menu Japanese-only — knowing the dish name and a few words of Japanese helps, and bring cash since cards are not accepted.

noodles.notes: Bouncy, low-hydration fresh noodles with a firm bite that grip the sesame-chili sauce and clinging ground pork. Tossed thoroughly before eating, soupless style.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shirunashi Tantanmen - Rich Sesame (濃厚胡麻); add a soft-boiled egg (温玉) and rice to finish the leftover sauce as tantan-rice. The Aromatic Shoyu (芳醇醤油) version is the leaner alternative.
notable_toppings: self-serve sansho/Sichuan peppercorn (4 varieties: young, red, green sansho and Musashibo kasho)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu/sesame sauce make this unsuitable for celiacs; no gluten-free option is offered.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on seasoned ground pork worked into the sauce; no plant-based version is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A flagship of Hiroshima's homegrown shiru-nashi tantanmen movement: no soup at all, just bouncy low-hydration noodles tossed with a chili-and-sesame sauce, savory ground pork and scallion. Musashibo's soul is its mellower, sweet-and-deep sesame base, with the citrusy numb of sansho left entirely in the diner's hands via a tableside spread of four peppercorns.

places.highlight: A flagship of Hiroshima's homegrown shiru-nashi tantanmen movement: no soup at all, just bouncy low-hydration noodles tossed with a chili-and-sesame sauce, savory ground pork and scallion. Musashibo's soul is its mellower, sweet-and-deep sesame base, with the citrusy numb of sansho left entirely in the diner's hands via a tableside spread of four peppercorns.

places.name: お好み焼き・鉄板焼 福ちゃん (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Fukuchan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the savory sauce is wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Dishes center on egg, pork, seafood and a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce; the iron plate is oiled with lard (ラード), and the house sauce is made with Hiroshima oysters. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 12:00–22:00; Tue 14:30–22:00 (per Tabelog). Note: another local listing reported 11:00–20:00 with Monday closed; hours appear to vary, confirm directly.
places.neighborhood: Tokaichimachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (right by Teramachi tram stop, west of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in March 2020 by a young chef surnamed Kato, who honed his okonomiyaki craft at Hiroshima station-area shops and ran a location in Tokyo's Harajuku before returning to open his own place in Hiroshima. By a fitting coincidence, a long-running 福ちゃん had operated at

chef_bio.background: Opened in March 2020 by a young chef surnamed Kato, who honed his okonomiyaki craft at Hiroshima station-area shops and ran a location in Tokyo's Harajuku before returning to open his own place in Hiroshima. By a fitting coincidence, a long-running 福ちゃん had operated at this very spot for years until its elderly proprietor retired; the current shop took over the name and address but is an entirely fresh start with a new owner, new menu, and new flavors. It is run as a small owner-operated shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kato's approach emphasizes meticulous technique and a gentle touch — careful cabbage preparation for ideal texture, and a distinctive house sauce (広島わしらの結晶ソース) made with Hiroshima oysters and lemon for a bright, appetite-whetting acidity. The plate is finished with lard for added savoriness.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with signature creations like a scallion-loaded 山王焼き and a seared-cheese-with-perilla okonomiyaki, plus teppanyaki sides such as shiitake butter.

anecdotes.text: The shop carries the name and exact location of a beloved older 福ちゃん whose elderly owner retired; the current chef revived the spot in 2020 as a completely reimagined okonomiyaki shop while keeping the cherished name.
Before opening here, the chef worked at okonomiyaki spots near Hiroshima's stations and managed a branch in Tokyo's Harajuku.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tokaichi.comで加藤氏の経歴（広島駅前の店や原宿店を経て2020年3月開業）、旧・福ちゃんの跡地・屋号を継いだ新装開店、牡蠣とレモンの『広島わしらの結晶ソース』、山王焼き・炙りチーズ大葉・椎茸バター等を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is assembled on a shared griddle from a wheat-flour batter and includes wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact on the cook surface is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki/teppanyaki style relies on a wheat-based savory sauce (the house sauce is described as oyster-and-lemon based); assume the sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is oiled with pork lard and the house sauce uses Hiroshima oysters, so vegan diners face animal-product contact across the cooking surface and seasonings.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly counter-style neighborhood okonomiyaki shop where a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way; English menus are not documented, so a foreign student should be comfortable pointing and ordering simple dishes.

places.name: 十日市茶房 (Tokaichi Sabou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature food items are buttered toast and sandwiches, both made from wheat bread, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu or handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The core food is the "Tokaichi Service" set of coffee with buttered toast and a soft-boiled egg (both egg and butter are animal products), plus sandwiches. Coffee itself is plain, but there is no documented plant-milk or vegan food option.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat 08:00-14:00; closed Monday and Sunday
places.neighborhood: Tokaichi-machi, Naka-ku (west of Peace Memorial Park, walkin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tokaichi Sabou opened in October 1946, the year after the atomic bombing, started by the current proprietor's father after he was repatriated from China. It has served the Tokaichi neighborhood for well over seven decades from its corner spot directly in front of the To

chef_bio.background: Tokaichi Sabou opened in October 1946, the year after the atomic bombing, started by the current proprietor's father after he was repatriated from China. It has served the Tokaichi neighborhood for well over seven decades from its corner spot directly in front of the Tokaichi-machi streetcar stop, keeping the warm, retro feel of an authentic old-school pure kissaten.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighborhood constant: a place where regulars of all ages come for their daily cup, and where the staff notice and worry when a familiar face stops appearing.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee and an all-day breakfast set. The house blend 'Tokaichi Mix' is bright and clean and easy to drink, while the darker, heavier 'Moon Blend' is favored by enthusiasts. The all-day 'Tokaichi Service' pairs a cup of coffee with buttered toast and a soft-boiled egg.

anecdotes.text: The 'Moon Blend' was inherited from a now-closed Hiroshima Station kissaten called 'Moon,' preserving that shop's beloved dark-roast flavor for its former customers.
Regulars range in age from about 3 to 94, and many have come for years; the staff become concerned when a long-time face disappears, wondering if they have fallen ill.
Founded in October 1946 by the proprietor's father after he returned from China, it is one of Hiroshima's long-surviving postwar coffee houses.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tokaichi.com及び食べログのレビュー・RCC記事で1946年10月創業（先代が中国からの引揚げ後に開業）、十日市町電停前、自家焙煎、ムーンブレンド（旧・広島駅『ムーン』由来）、十日市サービス、常連の年齢層（3〜94歳）を確認。

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood coffee house with a loyal older local crowd and smoking allowed indoors; little to no English, so basic Japanese and a relaxed, respectful manner go a long way.

places.name: 自然食cafe&bar ゆらり（夢楽輪） (Shizenshoku cafe&bar Yurari (Yumerakuwa))
places.gf_detail: Gluten-free menu items are explicitly available and the owners state that people avoiding gluten and those with allergies can dine comfortably; accommodations are best arranged with advance notice. It is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, so confirm specific dishes when ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe explicitly offers vegan and vegetarian menu items alongside its omnivore dishes (lunch options include a hamburger, fried chicken, curry, fish and gapao rice), so it is not fully vegan but accommodates vegans, ideally with advance notice. Organic, additive-free ingredients are used throughout.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu & Sun: 11:00-16:00 (food L.O. 14:30, drinks L.O. 15:00); Fri & Sat: 11:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). The official site lists Sun-Thu 11:00-16:00, Fri & Sat 11:00-21:00. No regular closing day (closed 3-5 days for Obon and New Year). Operating schedule revised as of June 1, 2026.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Takanobashi shopping street / Takanobashi Stn)
places.cuisine: Natural food & organic cafe and bar
places.notes: A natural-food cafe and bar tucked into the Takanobashi shopping street in central Hiroshima, run as an independent organic kitchen that sources its vegetables from the affiliated "Organic Kitchen" (オーガニックの台所).

chef_bio.origin: The owner started the venture out of a wish to protect future generations (未来の若者を守りたい), responding to concerns about rising health problems linked to pesticides, chemical fertilizers and food additives.
chef_bio.background: A natural-food cafe and bar tucked into the Takanobashi shopping street in central Hiroshima, run as an independent organic kitchen that sources its vegetables from the affiliated "Organic Kitchen" (オーガニックの台所).
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is that the secret to deliciousness is subtraction (おいしさの秘訣は引き算): zero chemical pesticides, zero synthetic fertilizers, zero antibiotics and zero additives, letting ingredients keep their natural character. Their motto is "あなたの食べられるがここにある" — "what you can eat is here" — so that people with allergies, gluten-free or vegan needs can share a table comfortably.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic, additive-free lunches with several rotating daily choices plus standards like a hamburger, fried chicken, curry, fish set, gapao rice and rice-ball sets; handmade organic sweets; and an evening menu of additive-free salads, small plates and rare organic craft beer, sake, shochu, gin and whiskey. House-made shio-koji (salt koji) and soy koji are made with naturally cultivated rice and sun-dried salt and also sold.

anecdotes.text: Prices are kept deliberately affordable to draw in younger customers and encourage a return to traditional, healthy Japanese eating habits.
The cafe hosts a bimonthly market called "ゆらり円日 (ゆらりえんにち)" on the second Sunday of even-numbered months, selling organic produce and additive-free foods, along with cooking classes and events.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: organic-japanのインタビューと公式サイトで、店主・宮原雄太、『おいしさの秘訣は引き算』の理念、アレルギー・グルテンフリー・ヴィーガン対応の方針、隔月第2日曜の『ゆらり円日』、自然栽培米と天日塩の自家製塩麹・醤油麹、若者向けの手頃な価格を確認。

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owners state that people with allergies and those who are gluten-free can dine without worry (アレルギーの方も、グルテンフリーの方も気兼ねなく), and dietary restrictions are accommodated, ideally with advance notice.

positives.text: Explicit vegan, vegetarian and gluten-free menu items are offered.

All ingredients and seasonings are organic and additive-free, with no chemical pesticides, synthetic fertilizers, antibiotics or additives; seasonings like shio-koji are house-made from naturally cultivated rice and sun-dried salt.
The kitchen explicitly aims to let people with allergies, gluten-free and vegan needs all eat together at the same table.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, casual organic cafe that explicitly aims to accommodate allergies, gluten-free and vegan diets, so a foreign student should feel comfortable asking about ingredients, though it helps to have your dietary needs written in Japanese or to call ahead.

places.name: 握手カフェ ORGANIC (Akushu Cafe ORGANIC)
places.gf_detail: The signature "白米の絹ソフト" (silk rice soft serve), made from organic Yamada Nishiki rice with rice milk, soy milk and coconut milk, is documented as egg-, dairy- and wheat-free, and the mochi/rice crackers served with it are also labeled egg/dairy/wheat-free. An allergy-focused review tags the soft serve as gluten-free. The cafe posts clear allergen labeling, but it is not a fully dedicated gluten-free kitchen, so confirm individual items at the counter.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based cafe: no animal-derived ingredients. The rice soft serve uses rice/soy/coconut milk with organic cane sugar and agave (no dairy, egg, or refined white sugar). Menu also includes organic fruit sodas with house-made spiced syrups, organic coffee and spiced hot drinks. Concept is "wholesome seasonal flavors for everyone."
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Orizuru Tower (in front of the Atomic Bomb Dome, 1 min walk)
places.notes: Opened March 23, 2024 as a renewal of the former Akushu Cafe inside Oriduru Tower, the observation tower standing right beside Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. The 1F counter was relaunched as an organic, plant-based cafe.

roles: operator: 株式会社握手カフェ
chef_bio.background: Opened March 23, 2024 as a renewal of the former Akushu Cafe inside Oriduru Tower, the observation tower standing right beside Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. The 1F counter was relaunched as an organic, plant-based cafe.
chef_bio.philosophy: 'からだにやさしい旬をすべての人へ' — wholesome seasonal flavors for everyone. The cafe sources fruit directly from Hiroshima farmers, deliberately using weather-blemished produce to celebrate regional character and reduce waste, and avoids animal products, additives and refined sugar.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based 'silk' rice soft serve made from organic, pesticide-free Yamada Nishiki rice blended with rice, soy and coconut milk; organic fruit sodas with house-made spiced syrups.

anecdotes.text: The shop deliberately uses Hiroshima fruit with minor weather blemishes, framing the surface marks as proof of hardy, honest growth rather than a defect.

The rice soft serve and even the puffed-rice crackers served alongside it are labeled by the shop as free of egg, dairy and wheat, and an allergy-focused visitor with a soy aversion found the rice-based flavor pleasant with no beany taste.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトとアレルギー系ブログ(dessa)で、2024年3月23日におりづるタワー1Fで握手カフェをリニューアル、山田錦の『白米の絹ソフト』（米・豆・ココナッツミルク）、天候による傷のある広島産果物の活用、卵・乳・小麦不使用のソフトとポン菓子（イラスト付アレルギー表示）を確認。

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe displays clear, illustrated allergen and vegan labeling, and the full menu with ingredient information is published online so guests can verify before visiting.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based menu using organic, pesticide- and additive-free ingredients with no animal products, eggs or refined white sugar.
The silk rice soft serve and its accompanying rice/mochi crackers are documented as egg-, dairy- and wheat-free, and described as gluten-free in an allergy-focused review.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual counter in a tourist tower right by the A-Bomb Dome with clear illustrated allergen/vegan labeling and an online menu, so a foreign student can order easily with minimal Japanese.

places.name: ぎゃらりぃ宮郷 (Gallery Miyazato)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature foods are wheat-based: natural-yeast bread, pizza toast and cheese toast, plus chiffon cake (wheat flour). The savory option is a curry served with bread/rice. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation, and no dedicated preparation surfaces. A celiac traveler should not rely on this café for safe food; at most a plain coffee or tea would be advisable.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented café. The main savory dish (Pakistani curry) contains chicken; pizza/cheese toast use dairy cheese; chiffon cake and other Western sweets typically contain eggs and dairy; ozenzai (sweet red-bean soup) is plant-based in ingredients but may include mochi. No labeled vegan items are documented, so a vegan would need to ask in person. Realistically only black coffee/tea or possibly the red-bean ozenzai (confirm) would suit a strict vegan.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: 10:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30), closed Wednesdays. Miyajima Tourism Association: 10:00-18:30 (winter 10:00-18:00), closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: 宮島・町家通り (Miyajima, Machiya-dori), a short walk inland from t
places.cuisine: Gallery café and sweets shop in a renovated
places.notes: [Miyajima] Gallery Miyazato occupies a wooden machiya more than 200 years old that once operated as a shakushi (rice-scoop/ladle) wholesaler — fitting, since Miyajima is the birthplace of the carved wooden shakushi. The family carefully restored the house and reopened it on April  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Hatsukaichi, Hiroshima — on the quiet Machiya-dori lane behind the main shrine approach.
chef_bio.background: Gallery Miyazato occupies a wooden machiya more than 200 years old that once operated as a shakushi (rice-scoop/ladle) wholesaler — fitting, since Miyajima is the birthplace of the carved wooden shakushi. The family carefully restored the house and reopened it in April 2003 as a gallery tea room, keeping the building's history visible throughout.
chef_bio.philosophy: A cultural space as much as a café: the rebuilt interior shows off hand-drawn glass windows, reclaimed beams, carved transom (ranma) work, and a stair handrail and counter densely decorated with ladles, doubling as a rental gallery for local art.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made natural-yeast bread (sourced from La Pain Juju in Hatsukaichi) turned into pizza toast and cheese toast, a slow-simmered Pakistani-style chicken-and-vegetable curry, hand-dripped original-blend coffee, and homemade chiffon-cake sets and ozenzai enjoyed while looking onto the garden.

anecdotes.text: The staircase handrail and counter are lined with many wooden ladles, and the renovation preserved antique pottery and ornamental kumiko/osa transom panels from the building's life as a shakushi wholesaler.
The Pakistani curry is chicken and vegetables slow-cooked with spices, described as spicy but mild in heat; the pizza toast uses generous Hokkaido Yotsuba cheese.
The tourism association describes it as a gallery tea room in a 200-plus-year-old house where you can rest over a handmade chiffon-cake set or ozenzai while gazing at the garden.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: seesaaブログ・宮島観光協会・広島県『たてものがたり』で、杓子問屋だった築200年余りの町家を改装し2003年4月にギャラリー喫茶として開業、杓子で飾られた手すり・カウンター、天然酵母パン（La Pain Juju）、パキスタンカレー、四葉チーズのピザトースト、シフォンケーキセット・おぜんざいを確認。開業日は『4月』までが裏付け可能。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat bread (pizza toast, cheese toast) and wheat-flour chiffon cake are core menu items prepared in a small café kitchen, so flour cross-contact is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy cheese (pizza/cheese toast) and chicken (Pakistani curry) are prepared in the same kitchen; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, welcoming gallery café where pointing at the menu and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; English support is not guaranteed, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 喫茶しま (Kissa Shima)
places.gf_detail: This is a bakery and café built around handmade bread and baked goods made with Hokkaido wheat flour, plus toast sets, sandwiches, pizza toast and curry over rice. The signature offerings are wheat-based and there is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on butter, milk, Hiroshima eggs and honey in its bread and baked goods, and savory items include ham, cheese, teriyaki chicken, BLT and beef curry. No vegan items or plant-based options are documented, so it is not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 8:30-17:00, closed Wednesdays. Morning toast sets served from opening until 11:00.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Kitanocho-hama (Miyajima Omotesando shopping street area, near Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1966 by Funatsuki Masaru, a former railway worker who fell in love with carefully brewing coffee, the shop has stood on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street for roughly half a century. After a renovation it reopened run by his granddaughter Yamauchi Akiko, s

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1966 by Funatsuki Masaru, a former railway worker who fell in love with carefully brewing coffee, the shop has stood on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street for roughly half a century. After a renovation it reopened run by his granddaughter Yamauchi Akiko, supported by her mother Matsuo Michiko, keeping the family line and the original shop name out of respect for longtime local regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: Aiming every day for bread and sweets that comfort rather than tire the palate, while preserving the previous generation's approach to coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-baked breads (about ten kinds) and baked sweets (around fifteen kinds), the signature 'Miyajima Weekend Citron' lemon cake made with Miyajima honey and Seto Inland Sea lemon, and the house 'Grandfather's Deep-Roast Coffee' carried over from the founder. Morning toast sets, sandwiches, omelette rice, beef curry and pizza toast round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The house coffee is named 'Grandfather's Deep-Roast Coffee' in honor of the founder, a former railway worker who started the shop in 1966 out of a passion for brewing coffee.
Owner Yamauchi Akiko spent about a decade working at a Western pastry shop before returning to bake the bread and sweets now sold at the counter.
The signature 'Miyajima Weekend Citron' cake is handmade with honey harvested on Miyajima and lemon from the Seto Inland Sea.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 広島経済新聞と公式サイトで、1966年に元鉄道員・船附丞氏が創業、四代目で孫の山内晶子さん（洋菓子店に約10年勤務）が母・松尾通子さんと共に営業、『じいちゃんの深煎り珈琲』、宮島産はちみつと瀬戸内レモンの宮島ウイークエンドシトロン、パン約10種・焼菓子約15種を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a working bakery producing about ten kinds of bread and fifteen kinds of baked goods on site with Hokkaido wheat flour, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination very likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods use butter, milk, eggs and honey, and savory dishes use meat and cheese; no separation from animal products is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed family-run café in a major tourist area used to international visitors; a friendly greeting and pointing at the counter pastries will get you far even with little Japanese.

places.name: お好み焼き・鉄板焼 福ちゃん エキエ (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Fukuchan Ekie)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and yakisoba/udon noodles, and the okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat, so the signature dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki uses egg and is typically topped with pork and bonito/fish-based dashi and sauce, and the teppanyaki menu centers on Hiroshima oysters, wagyu, and other seafood. No vegan dishes or accommodation are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00 - 22:00（L.O. 21:30）, open daily, no regular closed days
places.neighborhood: 広島駅 ekie（南区松原町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter tucked inside the ekie food area at JR Hiroshima Station, making it a convenient first or last taste of the city's signature dish for travelers passing through.

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter tucked inside the ekie food area at JR Hiroshima Station, making it a convenient first or last taste of the city's signature dish for travelers passing through.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking emphasizes the natural sweetness of well-steamed cabbage and locally sourced Hiroshima ingredients, especially the region's prized oysters.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen leans on slowly steam-grilled cabbage layered with its own noodles to draw out the vegetables' natural sweetness, in classic Hiroshima style. Beyond the namesake Fukuchan-yaki, the menu showcases local seafood on the griddle, especially Hiroshima oysters — as Hiroshima oyster okonomiyaki and oysters grilled in butter-soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature plate is the Fukuchan-yaki, and it is known for the oyster dishes that make the most of Hiroshima's local catch, such as Hiroshima oyster okonomiyaki and oysters grilled in butter-soy sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 店舗情報サイト(gorp)でJR広島駅エキエ内の立地、看板の福ちゃん焼き、広島産牡蠣のお好み焼き・牡蠣のバター醤油を確認。ウニほうれん草・和牛など未確認のメニューは一般化して削除。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter, noodles, and the finishing sauce all contain wheat, and the griddle is shared across wheat-based dishes, so heavy cross-contamination should be assumed for anyone avoiding gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce and the butter-soy-sauce oyster dish use standard Japanese soy/okonomi sauces, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle cooks pork, oysters, wagyu and other seafood alongside any vegetable items, and okonomiyaki uses egg and fish-based dashi, so animal-product cross-contact is unavoidable.

positives.text: English and simplified-Chinese menus are available, which makes it easier to ask questions about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: Inside the ekie shopping complex right at JR Hiroshima Station with English and simplified-Chinese menus, this is an easy, welcoming stop for a foreign student.

places.name: 沖野水産 (Okino Suisan)
places.gf_detail: Freshly grilled oysters served plain are naturally free of wheat, and the shop recommends eating them with no condiments, so a simple order of yaki-gaki can work. However, fried oysters (kaki-fry) use a wheat batter and vinegar/ponzu-dressed oysters may use soy-sauce-based seasonings that contain wheat. This is a tiny producer-direct stall with no documented gluten-free protocol, so a celiac traveler should point to the grilled oysters and ask for them with nothing added.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around oysters (grilled, fried, and in ponzu/vinegar preparations), which are animal products. There are no vegan options here.
places.hours_raw: 09:00 - 18:00 (open daily)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Okino Suisan is the only shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street run directly by a local oyster producer, sitting near the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. The family farms Miyajima oysters in the mineral-rich waters around the island and sells them straight from t (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Okino Suisan is the only shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street run directly by a local oyster producer, sitting near the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. The family farms Miyajima oysters in the mineral-rich waters around the island and sells them straight from the source, which keeps prices comparatively modest for the area.
chef_bio.philosophy: As producers, they grill the oysters with careful heat control to bring out a plump, fresh texture and encourage eating them plain so the creamy, gently briny flavor of the oyster speaks for itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Grill-fired oysters cooked fresh at the storefront, served hot and plump, alongside fried oysters and ponzu/vinegar-dressed oysters; the shop also sells and ships whole oysters.

anecdotes.text: As the only producer-direct oyster shop on the Omotesando shopping street, the oysters are sold relatively cheaply and grilled fresh right at the storefront, hot and plump off the grill.
The shop suggests eating a whole grilled oyster on a toothpick with no condiments, letting the creamy taste and moderate saltiness spread across the palate.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 宮島博多屋ブログとnohgakuで、表参道で唯一の宮島牡蠣生産者直営店であること、生産者直営ゆえ比較的安価、店頭で焼きたて（ふっくら）、爪楊枝で調味料なしで丸ごと食べる推奨、クリーミーで程よい塩味を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The same small stall prepares wheat-battered fried oysters alongside the grilled oysters, so cross-contact is plausible; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ponzu/vinegar-dressed oyster items may use soy-sauce-based seasonings, which typically contain wheat; ingredients are not documented.

positives.text: Grilled oysters are recommended to be eaten plain with no condiments, so a simple grilled-oyster order avoids added sauces.

cultural_comfort.note: A quick, friendly oyster stall where you can point at the grill and order with a few words of Japanese; English is not guaranteed, so know that "yaki-gaki" means grilled oyster and have cash ready.

places.name: お好み焼き ももちゃん（百々商店） (Okonomiyaki Momochan (Momo Shoten))
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop and the dishes are built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and wheat noodles (soba), plus a sweet okonomiyaki sauce that typically contains wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation. Not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki uses egg, and standard fillings are pork, oyster (kaki), and anago/chikuwa (fish), with a wheat-soy sauce. Bonito/fish-based elements are typical of Hiroshima okonomiyaki. No vegan preparation is advertised, so this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (irregular; generally open daily)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, around Itsukushima Shrine / Omotesando
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Momochan is a long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked along the Omotesando shopping street on Miyajima, roughly a five-to-eight-minute walk from the ferry pier and a short stroll from Itsukushima Shrine. It has the feel of a neighborhood institution rather  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Momochan is a long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked along the Omotesando shopping street on Miyajima, roughly a five-to-eight-minute walk from the ferry pier and a short stroll from Itsukushima Shrine. It has the feel of a neighborhood institution rather than a polished tourist restaurant, with simple tables and pipe chairs and an unpretentious, lived-in atmosphere that regulars and visitors alike appreciate.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking okonomiyaki the traditional Hiroshima way, layering ingredients and griddling them rather than mixing, so each component stays distinct even when eaten together.
chef_bio.specialty: Layered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with plenty of cabbage and noodles, finished with egg. The island shows in the large, prized oysters (kaki) folded in, and the sweet sauce paired with the noodles is a noted highlight.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe it as a charmingly old-fashioned spot whose cluttered, casual setting is part of the appeal, with the oysters singled out for their size and quality.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabipressoで、フェリーから徒歩約8分の昔ながらのお好み焼き店、パイプ椅子の素朴で雑然とした雰囲気、広島流の重ね焼き、大ぶりで質の良い牡蠣、甘めのソースを確認。アナゴ竹輪は未確認のため削除。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All okonomiyaki here are built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles cooked on a shared teppan griddle, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a sweet okonomiyaki sauce that typically contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared griddle alongside pork, egg, oysters, and fish (anago/chikuwa), so vegan-safe preparation is not possible here.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming local okonomiyaki shop on Miyajima's tourist street where pointing at the picture menu and a few friendly words of Japanese will get you served comfortably.

places.name: 木島商店 (Kijima Shoten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes are grilled and fried oysters; fried oysters are coated in wheat batter, and seasoning relies on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and miso. No gluten-free menu, no documented allergen handling, and no dedicated fryer.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is an oyster and seafood specialist; nearly every dish centers on oysters or other shellfish/fish, and sauces use wheat soy sauce and likely fish-based dashi. No documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 09:00 - 19:00 (Mon-Sun); hours may vary, confirm before visiting
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando Shopping Street
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An oyster and seafood shop set along Miyajima's Omotesando approach, about a seven-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry pier and close to Hokoku Shrine. A white paper lantern marks the entrance. Oysters are grilled at street level on the ground floor, while a calmer, caf (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A casual oyster and seafood counter along Miyajima's Omotesando approach. Oysters — and the shop's grilled anago-chikuwa (conger-eel fish cake) — are cooked fresh to order right at the storefront, in the budget-friendly, street-stand style typical of the island's oyster stalls.
chef_bio.specialty: Grilled oysters and freshly grilled anago-chikuwa cooked to order at the storefront, with beer and highball to drink alongside; an inexpensive, casual stop rather than a sit-down restaurant.

anecdotes.text: Oysters and anago-chikuwa (conger-eel fish cake) are grilled to order right at the storefront in casual street-stand style.
The counter serves grilled oysters alongside beer and highball, an inexpensive stop on the Omotesando approach.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ(34017538)で、店頭で焼く焼きガキとアナゴ竹輪、ビール・ハイボール提供、予算〜999円・9〜19時の気軽な店であることを確認。2階カフェ・テラス、白提灯、豊国神社至近、ランチ定食＋日本酒等の記述は出典(blog.sanbikaki)に木島商店の記載がなく、他店と混同の可能性が高いため削除。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oyster dishes and grilled-oyster seasoning rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around oysters and seafood, so plant-based diners face unavoidable shellfish/fish content and likely fish dashi in shared cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed walk-up oyster stand on the main tourist street where a friendly greeting and pointing at the oysters is enough; English is not guaranteed but the casual order-and-eat format makes it easy for a foreign student.

places.name: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 本通店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Senmon King-ken Hondori)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is shiru-nashi tantanmen built on wheat noodles (custom medium-thin Chinese-style noodles), and the tare is made with a wheat-based soy sauce specially produced by Kawanaka Shoyu (川中醤油). There is no documented gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth-less dandan noodles use a minced-pork (meat) topping and a sesame/chili-oil sauce over wheat noodles, with a wheat-soy-sauce tare; no documented vegan or vegetarian version.
places.neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Hondori shopping street; within walking distance of the Atomic Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: King-ken opened in Hiroshima in November 2011 and quickly became a destination for the city's distinctive broth-less dandan noodles, drawing daily lines; it later expanded to Tokyo in 2015 and to other cities, and its style was even turned into a commercial instant nood

chef_bio.background: King-ken opened in Hiroshima in November 2011 and quickly became a destination for the city's distinctive broth-less dandan noodles, drawing daily lines; it later expanded to Tokyo in 2015 and to other cities, and its style was even turned into a commercial instant noodle. The Hondori branch sits in the heart of the covered Hondori shopping arcade, within walking distance of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
chef_bio.philosophy: Obsessive sourcing of every component: a house five-spice chili oil aged at least a month and a half to round out the heat; a soy tare hand-made with a 'King-ken' soy sauce specially commissioned from Kawanaka Shoyu (founded 1906 / Meiji 39); and a proprietary Sichuan-pepper blend ground fresh in-shop each morning in the exact daily quantity.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style shiru-nashi tantanmen (broth-less dandan noodles): firm, custom medium-thin noodles boiled briefly, tossed with a sesame-and-chili-oil sauce, minced pork, and green onion, with adjustable spice and a numbing Sichuan-pepper kick. The classic finish is to mix everything thoroughly and then add rice to soak up the remaining sauce.

anecdotes.text: Custom medium-thin noodles were developed with the long-established Kuchii noodle factory in Hatsukaichi, near the World Heritage island of Miyajima, and the green onions are fragrant 'Takarajima' negi grown in Kurahashi.
The shop grinds its Sichuan pepper (huājiāo) in-house each morning, using only the amount it will serve that day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ajibana、キング軒公式(kodawari)、広島市公式ブランドページで、2011年11月開業・2015年東京進出、明治39年(1906)創業の川中醤油に特注した『キング軒用醤油』、廿日市『くちい製麺』の特注中細麺、倉橋産『宝島ねぎ』、1ヶ月半以上熟成の五香辣油、当日挽きの花椒を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy tare, so any dish risks gluten contact; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy tare is hand-made using a 'King-ken' soy sauce specially commissioned from Kawanaka Shoyu (founded 1906), a conventional wheat-fermented Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dish includes a minced-pork topping and is prepared in a kitchen dedicated to meat-based dandan noodles, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast, casual counter-only noodle bar where you can point at the simple menu and eat quickly; basic Japanese greetings help but it is very approachable for a foreign student.

places.name: お好み焼き 鉄板焼き こひなた (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Kohinata)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. Okonomiyaki here is built on a wheat-flour batter and crispy wheat noodles, and dishes are finished with Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce (which contains wheat). There is no indication of gluten-free menu items, separate prep, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood okonomiyaki/teppanyaki izakaya. The signature こひなた焼き contains pork, egg, and squid tempura, and dishes broadly use egg, fish-based dashi/sauces, and seafood. No documented vegan or vegetarian options. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 18:00-23:00 (L.O. weekdays/Sat 22:30; Sun & holidays 22:00). Closed the 1st Monday of the month.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi (near Chuden-mae Stn, Peace Memorial Park area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, homey okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop in central Hiroshima's Otemachi district, opened in 2009. By day it is an okonomiyaki spot; in the evening it turns into a relaxed teppanyaki izakaya where regulars and visitors sit at the counter and watch the dishes come

chef_bio.origin: The owner trained at a Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop called Hinata, and named this place Kohinata in tribute to that mentorship.
chef_bio.background: A small, homey okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop in central Hiroshima's Otemachi district, opened in 2009. By day it is an okonomiyaki spot; in the evening it turns into a relaxed teppanyaki izakaya where regulars and visitors sit at the counter and watch the dishes come together on the griddle.
chef_bio.philosophy: An at-home, welcoming atmosphere built around the counter, where the cooking is part of the experience and conversation flows between guests.
chef_bio.specialty: The house dish, Kohinata-yaki, layers cabbage, pork, squid tempura and green onion, finished with crisp griddle-fried noodles and a runny egg under sweet-savory okonomiyaki sauce. A grilled potato salad mashed tableside on the teppan with mayo, cheese and spices, topped with a soft egg, is another favorite.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from Hinata, the Hiroshima (Naka-ku) okonomiyaki restaurant where the owner trained before opening their own place in 2009.

For the signature okonomiyaki, the noodles are firmly seasoned and cooked slowly, with oil worked in so they stay crisp without drying out even when cooked gently, a small technique the shop is known for.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: るるぶ記事が店名の由来（中区のお好み焼店「ひなた」で修業）、2009年創業、看板のこひなた焼き（麺に油を含ませ下味をつけて焼く技法）、鉄板でマッシュするポテトサラダを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made on a shared teppan griddle using wheat batter and wheat noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent across the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with sweet okonomiyaki sauce (Otafuku-style), which is made with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on one shared griddle alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so vegan separation is not feasible.

cultural_comfort.note: A homey, locals-favored counter shop where a little Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way; English is not advertised, so a translation app and pointing at the menu will help.

places.name: フクヤ食堂 (Fukuya Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on dishes that rely on wheat: anago-meshi and oyster bowls seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, plus breaded/fried items such as oyster fry, shrimp fry and tonkatsu. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or awareness.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused diner; its identity dishes are oysters and conger eel (anago), and Western set meals use meat and fish. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented, and standard seasonings here use fish/dashi and wheat soy sauce.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Hamanocho, seafront near the pier on the approach
places.notes: [Miyajima] A roomy, Showa-era retro diner sitting right on the Miyajima seafront, a short walk from the ferry pier as you head toward the great torii and Itsukushima Shrine. Tables near the entrance look out over the water, while inner seats face a quiet garden that leads back tow (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A roomy, Showa-era retro diner sitting right on the Miyajima seafront, a short walk from the ferry pier as you head toward the great torii and Itsukushima Shrine. Tables near the entrance look out over the water, while inner seats face a quiet garden that leads back toward the main building, giving the place an old-fashioned, unhurried feel.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-meshi (conger eel over rice) and local oyster dishes — raw with lemon, vinegared, grilled, and as oyster fry — alongside homey Japanese and Western set meals and cafe plates.
