anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Miyajima's two signature ingredients, oysters and conger eel, and rounds the menu out with comforting Western-style sets like tonkatsu, hamburg and fried shrimp, plus simple cafe fare.
Its draw is as much the setting as the food: sea-view seats by the door and a tranquil courtyard view inside make it a relaxed stop on the walk from the pier toward the shrine.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 旅色が宮島桟橋近くの立地、海が見える入口席と中庭が見える奥席、看板のあなごめし、カキ料理、とんかつ・ハンバーグ等の洋食セットを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Breaded/fried items (oyster fry, shrimp fry, tonkatsu) and wheat-based soy seasoning are central to the menu, so gluten exposure is pervasive; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi and oyster bowls are seasoned in the standard Japanese style with wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around oysters, conger eel and meat dishes, with fish/dashi-based seasoning throughout; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in family diner where a smile and a little Japanese go a long way; picture-style set menus make ordering manageable for a foreign student.

places.name: 岸本食堂 (Kishimoto Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free offering documented. Lunch is a spice curry set; curry roux and spice blends commonly contain wheat flour, and the evening izakaya menu relies on Japanese sake-bar small plates that typically use wheat-based soy sauce. A celiac diner would need to ask the proprietor directly about each dish. No gluten-free preparations are confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The lunch spice curry can include ingredients such as smoked mackerel (e.g., a smoked-saba curry), and the evening izakaya centers on sashimi, Seto Inland Sea seafood and sake-bar plates, most using fish or dashi. The brown-rice spice curry might be adaptable, but no vegan options are documented; a vegan diner would need to ask in advance.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-13:30 (sources also list 11:30-14:00); Dinner 18:00-22:00; Sunday 12:00-18:00. Closed Mondays and holidays.
places.neighborhood: Around Hiroshima Station, Osugacho (~4 min walk from Hiroshima Stn north exit)
places.cuisine: Lunch: original spice curry set meal
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small retro neighborhood shokudo within a few minutes' walk of Hiroshima Station, operating since around 2012. It wears two faces: by day a counter serving an original brown-rice spice curry set, and by evening an intimate izakaya built around hard-to-find Hiroshima l

chef_bio.background: A small retro neighborhood shokudo within a few minutes' walk of Hiroshima Station, opened in 2012 and run single-handedly by the owner. It wears two faces: by day a counter serving an original brown-rice curry set, and by evening an intimate izakaya built around hard-to-find Hiroshima local sake paired with creative small plates.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on prefecture-grown and local ingredients and a personal, hands-on approach: the owner picks sake to match each guest's taste and explains it in a way that's friendly even to first-timers.
chef_bio.specialty: Original curry over brown rice at lunch (including offbeat versions such as a smoked-mackerel curry); in the evening, rare Hiroshima regional sake with creative otsumami and fresh Seto Inland Sea seafood, including a Seto sashimi platter.

anecdotes.text: The owner selects sake to match each customer's preference and explains it clearly, making it approachable even for sake beginners.
The counter looks out on shelves of Hiroshima sake, and the kitchen leans on prefecture-sourced ingredients with a Seto Inland Sea sashimi platter and dishes like cheese tofu.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログが2012年開業、店主一人営業、瀬戸内の刺身盛り合わせ、広島の地酒重視、鯖カレーやチーズ関連のキーワードを確認。Rettyが昼は玄米のスープカレー、店主が客に合わせ日本酒を選び初心者にも解説する点を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Evening service centers on Japanese sake-bar small plates and sashimi, which conventionally use wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Lunch curry can contain fish (e.g., a smoked-mackerel version) and the evening menu features sashimi and seafood, so shared preparation with animal products is likely.

positives.text: Small owner-run counter where the proprietor personally explains the menu and sake to guests, so dietary questions can be discussed directly.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter shokudo run by the owner; a little basic Japanese helps for ordering and chatting about the sake, though a curious foreign student would be welcomed at the counter.

places.name: KIMURA（菓子処きむら） (KIMURA (Kashidokoro Kimura))
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment, but it offers a notable rice-flour (米粉) Basque cheesecake described as made without wheat — a genuine wheat-free dessert option. However, the shop's heritage products (momiji manju, the みやじまオムレット sponge cake, breads such as かきパン) are wheat-based, and the savory oyster chowder (かきチャウダー) is not confirmed gluten-free. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or celiac-level cross-contamination controls are documented. A gluten-sensitive visitor should confirm the rice-flour cheesecake's full ingredients and ask directly about shared baking surfaces.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. As a Western confectionery, core items (cream-filled みやじまオムレット, cheesecakes, momiji manju) use dairy and eggs, and the savory menu features Hiroshima oysters and seafood. Plant-based diners would need to ask whether any item (e.g., a fruit-based shaved ice or jam drink) can be served without animal products; nothing is confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 daily; additionally 19:00-22:00 on Fri-Sat (evening service). Closed Thursdays. (Sources differ slightly on evening hours; daytime 10:00-17:00 is consistent.)
places.neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（清盛通り近く、厳島神社参道）/ Miyajima Omotesando shopping stree
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] KIMURA grew out of 菓子処きむら (Kashidokoro Kimura), a long-loved Miyajima confectionery best known for making momiji manju from a chiffon-sponge batter rather than the usual castella-style cake — a distinctive twist that built a devoted local and visitor following over the  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: KIMURA grew out of 菓子処きむら (Kashidokoro Kimura), a long-loved Miyajima confectionery best known for making momiji manju from a chiffon-sponge batter rather than the usual castella-style cake — a distinctive twist that built a devoted local and visitor following over the decades. The shop sits on the bustling Omotesando shopping street that leads pilgrims and travelers toward Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The makers describe working with carefully selected ingredients and blending Western pastry technique with modern equipment to make sweets that bring joy to customers, while preserving the flavors longtime fans grew up with.
chef_bio.specialty: The みやじまオムレット (Miyajima Omelette) — a folded fluffy sponge cake with lightly sweetened cream that has been a long-seller since its 1970 debut, its chiffon texture unchanged from the original recipe — alongside the shop's chiffon-based momiji manju.

anecdotes.text: In May 2025 the heritage confectionery was reborn as a stylish cafe pairing Western sweets with Japanese (wakōcha) tea, while deliberately keeping the elements old fans loved — the chiffon momiji manju and the みやじまオムレット remain on the menu.
The みやじまオムレット has been a long-seller since its 1970 debut; its airy oven-baked chiffon sponge is kept as it was at launch, now filled with a lighter, less-sweet cream that appeals across generations.

The cafe leans into its location, offering savory items made with Hiroshima-grown oysters — an oyster chowder (かきチャウダー) and an oyster bread (かきパン) of oil-marinated oysters in soft French bread — plus a MASU tiramisu whose matcha swirl evokes the Seto Inland Sea.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 旅色がみやじまオムレット（1970年発売のロングセラー、シフォン生地、甘さ控えめのクリームに改良）、かきチャウダー、かきパン、MASUティラミスを確認。百鬼丸もどきブログが2025年5月21日のカフェ化と和紅茶、シフォンのもみじ饅頭継続を確認。

positives.text: Offers a rice-flour (米粉) Basque cheesecake described as made without wheat, giving a genuine wheat-free dessert choice in a heritage sweets shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly local sweets cafe right on Miyajima's busy shrine-approach shopping street, used to tourists — a few words of Japanese and pointing at the display case will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English menu support is not guaranteed.

places.name: お食事処 よさこい (Oshokujidokoro Yosakoi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Core dishes are not gluten-free: okonomiyaki is made with a wheat-flour batter, anago and oyster dishes are typically prepared with wheat-based soy sauce and tare, and udon is wheat noodles. No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on fish (anago/conger eel), shellfish (oysters), pork (black pork), chicken (jidori), and egg-and-seafood okonomiyaki. Dishes rely on wheat-soy-based seasonings and dashi. No documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:15); Dinner 17:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:15), evening service Thursday–Saturday only. Closed Wednesdays. Hours vary seasonally and by reservation.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Hatsukaichi — near the Omotes
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual sit-down eatery on the second floor of a building along Miyajima's approach to Itsukushima Shrine, a short walk from the ferry terminal and the Omotesando shopping street. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A casual sit-down eatery on the second floor of a building along Miyajima's approach to Itsukushima Shrine, a short walk from the ferry terminal and the Omotesando shopping street.
chef_bio.specialty: Conger eel (anago) rice bowls and an anago shabu-shabu hot pot, alongside local Hiroshima oysters and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. The kitchen also turns out set meals, rice bowls, and udon, so a group can mix island specialties with simpler fare.

anecdotes.text: The dining room looks out over the great torii gate and the Seto Inland Sea, so diners can take in the island's signature view while they eat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式ページが2階の立地、宮島の大鳥居・瀬戸内海の眺望、名物の穴子・牡蠣料理、穴子しゃぶしゃぶ、お好み焼き、丼・うどん・定食を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago and oyster dishes and okonomiyaki are standard Hiroshima preparations that use wheat-based soy sauce/tare and wheat-flour batter; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers identify ingredients before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the place is used to international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably without Japanese.

places.name: 広島名物 牡蠣一番 (Hiroshima Meibutsu Kaki Ichiban)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Most dishes rely on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce, and signature items such as fried oysters (カキフライ), okonomiyaki-style grilled oysters, and oyster gratin all use wheat flour/breading. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. A celiac traveler would have very few safe options here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire concept centers on oysters (raw, grilled, fried, in rice, in hot pot), and the kitchen leans heavily on seafood, fish-based dashi, and wheat-soy sauce. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Hatchobori / Nagarekawa area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact, modern oyster izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's Shintenchi block, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop, opened in January 2023. The kitchen revolves entirely around Hiroshima's signature oyster, offering around fifteen styles between raw and grilled.

chef_bio.background: A compact, modern oyster izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's Shintenchi block, about a five-minute walk from the Hatchobori tram stop, opened on January 20, 2023. The kitchen revolves entirely around Hiroshima's signature oyster, offering around fifteen styles — six raw and nine grilled.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner sources oysters directly, personally visiting producers and serving only ones they have approved, so guests can taste a broad spread of preparations in one sitting, paired with a deep list of local sake, shochu and wine.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served raw or grilled in a wide range of preparations — plain, with lemon, with green onion and grated radish ponzu, a Yamagata-dashi version, topped with salmon roe, a spicy mala style, and an okonomiyaki-inspired style — alongside fried oysters, oyster rice, oyster dote-nabe hot pot and oyster gratin.

anecdotes.text: All fifteen oyster varieties (six raw, nine grilled) are chosen by the owner, who visits the producers directly and serves only oysters they have vetted; lunch leans oyster-forward with sets such as fried oysters and oyster rice at a modest price.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式ページが生牡蠣6種・焼牡蠣9種（計15種）、店主自ら生産者を訪ね納得した牡蠣のみ提供、レモン/ねぎ紅葉おろしポン酢/山形ダシ/いくらのせ/麻辣/お好み焼き風などを確認。食べログ・ホットペッパーが2023年1月20日開業、新天地・八丁堀の立地、牡蠣飯・グラタン・土手鍋を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters, oyster gratin and okonomiyaki-style oysters use wheat flour/breading, and the fryer is also used for wheat-coated items, so cross-contact with gluten is likely throughout the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya style with wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Effectively every dish involves oysters, other seafood or fish-based dashi, so a fully plant-based meal is not realistic here.

positives.text: An English-language menu is available, which makes it easier for travelers to identify ingredients before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the central, casual setting makes it easy for a foreign student to walk in and order without speaking much Japanese.

places.name: 野菜to発芽酵素玄米弁当 YOU&I (Yasai to Hatsuga Koso Genmai Bento YOU&I (TA)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→options): The '100%グルテンフリー' claim carries a (※) asterisk hedge and rests on recipe swaps (米粉 for パン粉), not contamination control; produced by 酔心 (Suishin), a wheat-using Hiroshima-cuisine restaurant, with no stated separate kitchen or dedicated fryer. Diet-marketing trap, not celiac-grade. — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→high. Strong own claim (全商品100%グルテンフリー, rice flour not panko, press says no wheat even in soy-sauce seasoning), takeout-only whole-shop-GF concept, multiple JP outlets + GF blog corroborate. Downgraded: no first-hand celiac confirmation; FMGF community flags 'not a dedicated facility / may not be safe for celiac'; one FMGF review saw only 1 GF option (sold out), inconsistent with all-GF. — Prior note: Fully dedicated gluten-free shop. Every menu item is gluten-free: no wheat flour or gluten-containing ingredients are used, and wheat is avoided even in seasonings such as soy sauce. Rice flour replaces breadcrumbs in fried dishes (e.g. the salt-koji karaage). All rice is sprouted enzyme brown rice (発芽酵素玄米). This is a genuine GF destination, not a place with incidental options.
places.vegan_detail: A fully plant-based vegan bento (1,260 yen, tax incl.) is offered, built on traditional Japanese Buddhist (shojin) cooking with no animal-derived ingredients. It is limited to 10 per day and sells out, so come early. The rest of the menu includes meat and fish bento (karaage, sea-bream-dashi curry, daily meat/fish bento), so the shop is not fully vegan — but the dedicated vegan bento is a real, well-documented offering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (until sold out). Closed Wednesdays, Sundays, and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku (inside Nakanotana shopping street / Hondori; near Kamiyacho, within the Peace Park area)
places.notes: YOU&I was created by Suishin, a long-established Hiroshima Japanese-cuisine company, as a new takeout-only format opened in October 2021 in the Nakanotana arcade, right beside the Suishin main building in central Tatemachi. The idea grew from the company's own female st

roles: Operated by Suishin (long-established Hiroshima Japanese-cuisine company)
chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (Tatemachi, Naka-ku), launched by the Hiroshima Japanese-restaurant company Suishin.
chef_bio.background: YOU&I was created by Suishin, a long-established Hiroshima Japanese-cuisine company, as a new takeout-only format opened in October 2021 in the Nakanotana arcade, right beside the Suishin main building in central Tatemachi. The idea grew from the company's own female staff, who wanted to eat something healthy yet genuinely delicious even on their busiest days.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated concept is to support women's beauty and wellbeing through food (女性の美を食から支える), sharing the joy that delicious food and good health can go together. Because the parent company already had deep experience cooking shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) cuisine, extending into gluten-free and vegan cooking was a natural step; as executive director Tamura Megumi put it, the Japanese technique of drawing out ingredients' natural flavors matched what health-minded diners were looking for.
chef_bio.specialty: Gluten-free bento built on sprouted enzyme brown rice (発芽酵素玄米) — fermented brown rice aged with adzuki beans and salt for a nutty, chewy texture — including a daily-limited fully plant-based vegan bento rooted in shojin (Buddhist) cooking.

anecdotes.text: The shop was born from a wish voiced by the company's own female employees: that even on busy days they wanted at least one meal that was both healthy and truly tasty.

The shop states that everything is gluten-free down to the seasonings — wheat is avoided even in the soy sauce — and rice flour stands in for breadcrumbs in the fried dishes such as the salt-koji fried chicken.

The signature sprouted enzyme brown rice is made by fermenting brown rice, mixing it with adzuki beans and salt, then keeping it warm and aging it for a chewier, more nutritious result than plain brown rice.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 広島経済新聞が酔心運営、2021年10月開業、中の棚での立地、女性社員の声から誕生、調味料（醤油）まで小麦不使用、パン粉の代わりに米粉、精進由来のヴィーガン弁当、田村めぐみ取締役のコメントを確認。中の棚モールが発芽酵素玄米の製法を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop states that wheat is completely avoided even in seasonings such as soy sauce, and no wheat flour or gluten-containing ingredients are used in any item.

positives.text: Entirely dedicated gluten-free kitchen: all menu items are gluten-free, with rice flour replacing breadcrumbs in fried dishes and wheat excluded even from seasonings.
A genuinely vegan bento with no animal-derived ingredients, based on traditional Japanese shojin (Buddhist) cuisine, is offered daily (limited to 10).

cultural_comfort.note: This is a Japanese-language takeout counter where you point, pay, and go, so a foreign student can manage with a few Japanese words or a translation app — but for the limited vegan bento it helps to arrive early or call ahead, and don't expect English service.

gf_review.why: The '100%グルテンフリー' claim carries a (※) asterisk hedge and rests on recipe swaps (米粉 for パン粉), not contamination control; produced by 酔心 (Suishin), a wheat-using Hiroshima-cuisine restaurant, with no stated separate kitchen or dedicated fryer. Diet-marketing trap, not celiac-grade.

places.name: 御茶処 沖みつ (Ochadokoro Okimitsu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. Core dishes are not gluten-free: anago-meshi is glazed/seasoned with wheat-based soy-sauce tare, cha-soba is buckwheat noodles typically blended with wheat flour, and warabi-mochi is served with kinako (roasted soybean powder) and kuromitsu. No allergen or gluten information is published.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The signature anago-meshi and the anago cha-soba variant use fish, and savory dishes rely on wheat-soy-sauce-based seasoning, so they are not vegan. Some sweets (e.g. warabi-mochi with kuromitsu and kinako, shiratama zenzai) may be plant-based, but ingredients such as dairy in the assorted sweets platter and broth bases are not confirmed, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–15:30 (last order 15:15); may close earlier if sold out. Closed Tuesdays (may stay open through peak seasons). Sources vary: official site lists 10:00–15:00, guide lists 10:00–15:30.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Takishoji alley near Fujidana Park,
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small family-run teahouse set inside a renovated traditional wooden house more than a century old, tucked along the quiet Takishoji slope near Fujidana Park, a short walk uphill from Itsukushima Shrine. The building is recognized as a registered tangible cultural prop (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small family-run teahouse set inside a renovated traditional wooden house about a century old, tucked along the quiet Takishoji slope near Fujidana Park, a short walk uphill from Itsukushima Shrine. The building is designated a traditional building within Miyajima's historic townscape, and its old timber beams and fixtures give the room a hushed, time-slipped calm that contrasts with the bustle of Miyajima's main streets.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the original character of the historic house while keeping cooking handmade and unhurried, so a visit feels like a gentle pause after visiting the shrine.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-meshi (conger eel over rice) and handmade warabi-mochi served with kuromitsu black syrup and kinako (roasted soybean powder).

anecdotes.text: The house's handmade warabi-mochi has a delicate, melt-in-the-mouth texture and is served with black syrup and roasted soybean powder that lift its aroma.

Set on the quiet Takishoji slope near Fujidana Park, the renovated old house keeps original beams and fittings and blends into Miyajima's historic landscape, giving it a withdrawn, unhurried atmosphere.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトが築約100年の古民家で伝統的建造物に選定、あなごめしと手作り甘味を確認。japan-strollが滝小路・藤棚公園近くの立地、古材の梁を生かした店内、黒蜜ときなこの手作りわらび餅を確認。あなごの調理法・茶そば・半量セットは裏付けが取れず削除。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago-meshi is seasoned with a savory soy-sauce-based glaze; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no gluten-free alternative is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, family-run traditional teahouse off the main tourist strip where English is unlikely, so a foreign student should be ready to order by pointing at the menu, pay in cash, and not expect allergen guidance.

places.name: 汐まち寿司 つるみ (Shiomachi Sushi Tsurumi)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. As a traditional sushi and anago-meshi restaurant, dishes rely on soy sauce and sweet anago tare (eel glaze), which are wheat-based, and items like California rolls and tamago contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not rely on this restaurant; contact them directly and in detail before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The menu centers on raw fish nigiri, oysters, and live-killed anago (conger eel), and even the rice is cooked in a two-day kombu-and-bonito dashi, with bonito flakes used in dishes. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Wednesday (per Tabelog). The restaurant's own site lists lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), dinner 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:00), closed Tuesday and Wednesday — confirm current hours by phone.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small sushi counter on the ground floor of Shiomachi-an, a townhouse with roughly a century of history standing just a short walk from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine toward the Miyajima aquarium. The dining room was created so guests of the historic lodging upstairs,  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small sushi counter on the ground floor of Shiomachi-an, a historic converted townhouse standing about a minute's walk from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine toward the Miyajima aquarium. The dining room was created so guests of the historic lodging upstairs, and walk-in visitors, could enjoy proper Edomae-style sushi and a local specialty on the island itself.
chef_bio.philosophy: Careful attention to fundamentals: the sushi rice is served at gentle body temperature because the chef believes that warmth changes how the flavor reads, and the rice itself is cooked in a kombu dashi drawn from water over two days. The kitchen prizes ingredient quality and temperature control above flash, using live-killed (活〆) fresh fish.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri and the house anago dish made with conger eel, alongside Hiroshima oysters and local sake.

anecdotes.text: The anago dish layers simmered conger eel, kamaage whitebait, local Hiroshima-na mustard greens and bonito flakes, so a single bite carries several textures of the island's flavors.
The shop occupies the first floor of Shiomachi-an, a converted townhouse near Itsukushima Shrine, and grew out of wanting to give the overnight lodging's guests a place to eat well.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: はつめしが人肌のシャリ、2日かけて水から出す昆布だしで炊く米、穴子・釜揚げしらす・広島菜・鰹節の重ね寿司、宿泊客向けに始めた経緯を確認。公式ページが古民家の寿司屋、活〆天然鮮魚の寿司、地酒、厳島神社出口徒歩1分を確認。築年数の具体値は裏付けなく緩和。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi service and the sweet anago tare glaze rely on standard soy-sauce-based seasonings, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even the rice is cooked in a kombu-and-bonito dashi and bonito flakes are used within dishes, so fish products are pervasive throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 13-seat sushi counter run by the chef in a century-old townhouse; some Japanese or a translation app will help, and a phone reservation is wise since seating is very limited.

places.name: お好み焼がんちゃん (Okonomiyaki Ganchan)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The batter, the layered noodles (yakisoba), and the okonomiyaki sauce are all wheat-based, and the signature 肉玉そば (pork, egg, and noodle okonomiyaki) is not gluten-free. There is no indication of gluten-free flour, dedicated equipment, or any allergen accommodation. Travelers who must avoid gluten should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: A standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki griddle shop. The dishes are built around egg, pork, and noodles, and the sauce contains animal-derived ingredients; okonomiyaki here is also typically finished with bonito (fish) flakes. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Nagarekawa area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, recognizable by its yellow sign near Yagenbori-dori. With just a ten-seat counter and doors that open only late in the evening, it has the feel of a ne

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, recognizable by its yellow sign near Yagenbori-dori. With just a ten-seat counter and doors that open from 21:00 into the small hours, it has the feel of a neighborhood late-night spot where regulars and night owls drift in for a hot plate off the griddle.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the 肉玉そば (pork, egg, and soba-noodle okonomiyaki) as the house standard, optionally topped with green onion.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the shop by its yellow sign near Yagenbori-dori and praise the nicely judged cooking of the cabbage and noodles on the griddle, flavorful enough to need little extra sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログが流川（歓楽街）の立地、薬研堀通り近くの黄色い看板、カウンター10席のみ、21:00-03:00の深夜営業、肉玉そば（ネギトッピング）、キャベツと麺の程よい焼き加減を確認。

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny 10-seat late-night counter shop in the nightlife district where the cook works the griddle right in front of you, so a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude help; pointing at the popular 肉玉そば works fine, but bring cash and don't expect English.

places.name: だいち (Daichi)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and monja are built on wheat batter and wheat noodles, and the finishing sauce (Otafuku-style) is wheat-based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork-belly okonomiyaki, hormone (offal) grills and egg, with bonito/fish-based sauces and toppings, so dishes are not vegan. No vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Ebisu-cho / Kanayamacho, Naka-ku (inside Teppan Yokocho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent okonomiyaki and monja stand tucked into Teppan Yokocho, a basement teppan food alley in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa-Ginzancho nightlife district. It opens in the evening and runs deep into the early morning, the kind of late-night griddle spot that fills

chef_bio.background: A small independent okonomiyaki and monja stand tucked into Teppan Yokocho, a basement teppan food alley in Ebisucho, in central Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter. It opens in the evening and runs into the early morning (roughly 18:00 to 05:00), the kind of late-night griddle spot that fills up with people winding down after a night out.
chef_bio.specialty: Layered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built up in stages, with favorites like pork-and-egg soba okonomiyaki, grilled hormone (offal), and potato-cheese okonomiyaki finished with a rich sweet-savory Otafuku sauce.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the okonomiyaki as built up in many layers and praise the rich, sweet-savory Otafuku-sauce finish, alongside hormone (offal) grills and a potato-cheese variation.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログが胡町2-12 広栄ビルB1F「てっぱん横丁」の立地、18:00-05:00の深夜営業、肉玉そば・ホルモン・ジャガチーズ焼き・おたふくソースを確認。

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny late-night counter spot inside a teppan food alley with mostly Japanese-speaking regulars, so a few words of Japanese and a friendly, relaxed attitude will help a foreign student feel welcome.

places.name: 武田風林亭 (Takeda Furintei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core dishes — anagomeshi and rice bowls glazed with wheat-based soy/tare sauce, udon noodles (wheat), tempura, and breaded fried oysters — all contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, ingredients, or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The menu is built around conger eel (anago), oysters, and fish-based dishes, with sauces and broths that typically use dashi and wheat-containing soy sauce. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-16:00 (closed Tuesdays; open if Tuesday is a public holiday)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (near Itsukushima Shrine
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-run eatery tucked into Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, the lane that leads visitors toward Itsukushima Shrine. It serves the island's signature anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) alongside udon and Hiroshima's famous oysters, with a streetside coun (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small, family-run eatery tucked into Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, the lane that leads visitors toward Itsukushima Shrine. It serves the island's signature anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) alongside udon and Hiroshima's famous oysters, with a streetside counter where grilled oysters are cooked and handed over fresh to passers-by.
chef_bio.specialty: Anagomeshi — fluffy conger eel laid over rice with a light, restrained seasoning — plus a broad udon menu (including conger-eel tempura udon), fried oyster sets, and freshly grilled oysters.

anecdotes.text: Visitor reviews mention the eel dishes go over well with children, making it an easy stop for families who want to share a Miyajima eel meal together.
Set on the main approach to Itsukushima Shrine, it is valued as an affordable, quick lunch spot among the souvenir shops, with a grilled-oyster counter at the storefront for eating on the move.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 旅色がホクホクのあなごがふんだんに乗ったあっさり味のあなごめし、店頭の焼きガキカウンター、あなご天ぷらうどん、参道中心部の手頃な立地（700-2,100円）を確認。じゃらん口コミが子ども連れに好評な点を確認。

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual local eatery on the busy Omotesando shopping street where ordering is straightforward by pointing at the picture menu, though staff likely speak limited English, so a foreign student should be ready to use a phrase or two of Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: かきふくまる (Kakifukumaru)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated. Fried oysters and the grilled-and-fried sets use wheat flour and breadcrumbs, and flavored grilled oysters include miso and other sauces that typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. The kitchen is small and shared, and no allergen handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster specialist; the entire menu is built around shellfish (grilled and fried oysters), with sides like beef croquette and oyster combination sets. There are no vegan dishes, and seasonings such as miso, butter, mayonnaise, and cheese appear across the menu. Drinks (beer, soft drinks) are the only animal-free items.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (varies by season)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street
places.notes: [Miyajima] Kakifukumaru is the directly operated shop of Ogoshi Suisan, an oyster-farming company based in Miyajima. Sitting just inside the Omotesando shopping street a short walk from the pier, it is marked by blue noren curtains and banners and grew out of the farm's own harves (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: Operated by Ogoshi Suisan, an oyster aquaculture business in Miyajima, Hiroshima.
chef_bio.background: Kakifukumaru is the directly operated shop of Ogoshi Suisan, an oyster-farming company based in Miyajima. Sitting just inside the Omotesando shopping street a short walk from the pier, it is marked by blue noren curtains and banners and grew out of the farm's own harvest in the surrounding waters.
chef_bio.philosophy: As a producer-direct shop, it uses only oysters harvested that same day and cooks everything to order rather than in advance, so each oyster is served freshly grilled or fried at its peak.
chef_bio.specialty: Robatayaki-style grilled oysters offered in around six flavors (plain/lemon, garlic butter, cheese mayonnaise, gratin-style, miso and an ajillo/oil-based version), alongside freshly fried oysters and grilled-and-fried combination sets.

anecdotes.text: Because the farm runs the shop itself, the oysters are landed that day and prepared the moment you order, letting visitors eat farm-fresh oysters at a reasonable price right on the shopping street.
The grilled oysters come in roughly six flavor variations, with garlic butter being the most popular among visitors.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Ogoshi Suisan's official site (大越水産) confirms Kakifukumaru is its 生産者直営店 on Miyajima Omotesando, using only same-day oysters with nothing pre-made (つくりおきはしていません) and six 400-yen grilled flavors; tabiiro corroborates six flavors and garlic butter as the bestseller.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up oyster counter on Miyajima's main shopping street where you order by pointing at the picture menu, so a foreign student can get by with a smile and a few words of Japanese.

places.name: お食事処 快心友 (Oshokujidokoro Kaishin'yu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation documented. The signature dishes rely on a house-made sweet sauce for the anago rice bowl (anago/eel-style tare is typically made with wheat-based soy sauce and mirin), and the popular fried items (oyster fry, nigiri-ten fish-cake tempura) are battered/breaded with wheat flour. No dedicated fryer or GF menu is indicated on any source. Travelers with celiac disease should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm ingredients directly.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around conger eel (anago) and oysters, both animal products, and the rice dishes use fish-derived sweet tare. Even the side items (nigiri-ten) are made from fish cake (kamaboko). No documented plant-based or vegetable-only options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily; open year-round
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (表参道商店街), near Itsukushi
places.notes: [Miyajima] A small, family-style eatery tucked into the bustling Omotesando shopping arcade on Miyajima, just a short walk from the great torii gate and Itsukushima Shrine. It serves Miyajima's two signature local foods, charcoal-grilled conger eel and freshly harvested oysters, a

chef_bio.background: A small, family-style eatery tucked into the bustling Omotesando shopping arcade on Miyajima, just a short walk from the great torii gate and Itsukushima Shrine. It serves Miyajima's two signature local foods, charcoal-grilled conger eel and freshly harvested oysters, at prices friendly to everyday travelers rather than the splurge level of the island's famous specialist houses.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Miyajima's local ingredients, especially its prized conger eel and oysters, in an approachable, good-value form, with many items offered as takeout for the ferry or as a souvenir.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-don: fluffy charcoal-grilled conger eel over rice with a house-made sweet tare. The kitchen also offers a Kaishin'yu set pairing the eel bowl with locally caught fried oysters, plus nigiri-ten (fish-cake tempura on a skewer, in flavors like scallion-octopus and cheese-bacon) for eating as you stroll.

anecdotes.text: The shop was featured on the nationally broadcast TV program Kaere Monday in June 2025, where musician Koji Kikkawa highlighted its anago dish, drawing fresh attention to this modest island spot.

Its location right on the main Omotesando approach to Itsukushima Shrine, about six minutes on foot from the ferry pier, makes it a convenient stop for sightseers, and its takeout anago bento is popular to carry onto the boat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: activitv's episode recap confirms 快心友 appeared on 帰れマンデー (June 2025) with 吉川晃司 featuring its あなご重, and a 快心友セット combining the anago bowl with fried oysters; tabiiro places it on Omotesando ~6 min from the pier with nigiri-ten and takeout bento.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-don uses a house-made sweet tare (sauce); eel-style tare is conventionally based on wheat-containing soy sauce and mirin. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on conger eel and oysters with fish-cake (kamaboko) sides; no plant-based options are documented, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, tourist-friendly shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where pointing at the picture menu and a few polite words in Japanese will get you a budget anago rice bowl with no fuss; takeout is also available if you'd rather eat on the ferry.

places.name: 菜食健美 広島店 (Saishoku Kenbi Hiroshima)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen's signature meat substitutes are wheat gluten (seitan/麸) and soy meat, and dishes include ramen, pasta, katsu curry and breaded cutlets — all wheat-based. Okara-konjac is also used but the core protein swaps rely on wheat, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: A fully meat- and seafood-free vegetarian kitchen with many vegan dishes, but not 100% vegan: the restaurant states it primarily uses plant-based ingredients while some menu items contain egg or dairy. Standout plant-based items include a vegan mapo rice bowl made without the five pungent vegetables (no garlic or onion), an Oriental vegetarian katsu curry, and lunch sets where you choose a rice bowl, pasta, or ramen. Vegan diners should confirm egg/dairy on a given dish when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); lunch service 11:00-14:30. Closed Tuesdays, with occasional temporary closures.
places.neighborhood: Koiue (toward Nishi-Hiroshima Stn; on the hills of western Hiroshima)
places.cuisine: Shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) cuisine; vegan/vegetarian creative dishes
places.notes: Saishoku Kenbi is the restaurant arm of a small group that operates plant-based eateries in a handful of Japanese cities, with the Hiroshima location set in the quiet western hills of the city. The kitchen builds satisfying, meat-style dishes entirely from plants, leani

chef_bio.origin: Part of a multi-city plant-based group (locations also in Tokyo, Nagoya, and Nakatsugawa).
chef_bio.background: Saishoku Kenbi is the restaurant arm of a small group that operates plant-based eateries in a handful of Japanese cities, with the Hiroshima location set in the western Koi hills of the city. The kitchen builds satisfying, meat-style dishes entirely from plants, leaning on wheat gluten, soy protein, and okara-konjac to recreate hearty textures.
chef_bio.philosophy: The group's guiding idea is that the foundation of all of life is what we eat — '生活すべての基本は食生活から' — and that the most delicious food is also 'kind and joyful to both body and mind' (からだにも心にもやさしく、うれしい). They frame their cooking as 健美食 (health-and-beauty food): vegetarian cuisine meant to nourish wellbeing, using no meat or seafood, in the spirit of shojin cuisine that avoids the five pungent vegetables (no garlic or onion) in signature dishes like the mapo rice bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based comfort dishes — vegan mapo rice bowl, Oriental vegetarian katsu curry, creamy mushroom pasta, and lunch sets with a choice of rice bowl, pasta, or ramen — built from wheat gluten, soy meat, and okara-konjac, with organic vegetables.

anecdotes.text: Signature dishes follow the shojin-style avoidance of the five pungent vegetables (no garlic or onion), as seen in the vegan mapo rice bowl.
The kitchen recreates hearty, meaty textures from plants using okara-konjac, wheat gluten, and soy protein, with organic vegetables sourced from Hokkaido.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: daisho-kikaku.com states the group's philosophy 生活すべての基本は食生活から and its 健美食 concept (からだにも心にもやさしく、うれしい), lists four branches (Tokyo, Nagoya, Hiroshima, Nakatsugawa), and confirms okara-konjac, wheat gluten, soy protein and organic Hokkaido vegetables; the Hiroshima page gives the Koi (己斐上) address and mapo/katsu-curry lunch sets.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core protein substitutes are wheat gluten and soy meat, and the menu spans ramen, pasta, breaded cutlets and curry — wheat is central throughout the kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is entirely free of meat and seafood, but states that some menu items use egg or dairy, so vegan diners should confirm egg/dairy on each specific dish.

positives.text: No meat or seafood is used anywhere in the kitchen, and signature dishes omit the five pungent vegetables (garlic/onion).

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, lunch-focused plant-based spot where ordering is straightforward via set menus, but staff communication is likely mostly in Japanese, so a translation app or a few key phrases will help a foreign student.

places.name: 広島牡蠣処 大衆酒場バケツ 立町店 (Hiroshima Kakidokoro Taishu Sakaba Baketsu T)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Signature items such as fried oysters and oyster gratin use wheat, and seafood dishes use standard (wheat-based) soy sauce; there is no indication of gluten-free options or handling.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated oyster and seafood izakaya; the menu centers on oysters, anago (conger eel), small sardines (小鰯) and other seafood, with sauces and dressings that typically rely on fish and wheat-based soy sauce. There are no documented vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun, holidays & days before holidays 11:30-14:00, 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); Thursday 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Lunch may occasionally be closed; lunch is cash only.
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku
places.notes: A back-alley oyster izakaya near the Tatemachi tram stop in central Hiroshima, with the dining room on the second floor. The house specializes in Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood, built around its signature 'bucket platter' of steamed and raw oysters served

chef_bio.background: A back-alley oyster izakaya near the Tatemachi tram stop in central Hiroshima, with the dining room on the second floor. The house specializes in Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood, built around its signature 'bucket platter' of steamed and raw oysters served in a metal bucket.
chef_bio.philosophy: Each dish is prepared to order after it is requested (一品一品オーダーを受けてから), emphasizing freshly cooked seafood.
chef_bio.specialty: Oysters in roughly 50 preparations, anchored by the bucket platter of steamed and raw oysters, with fried oysters, butter-grilled oysters, oyster gratin, and oyster ajillo; also anago and small sardines.

anecdotes.text: The oysters are sourced from Miyamoto Kaisan in Etajima, and the menu also features Hiroshima specialties such as kone (whale skin/コーネ).
The signature raw-oyster bucket platter (生食用カキ 鬼 バケツ盛り, 6 pieces) is a centerpiece of the menu, with a wide range of oyster dishes made one by one to order.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hotpepper/tabelog listings confirm the shop offers 約50種類 of oyster dishes, sources its oysters directly from 江田島の宮本海産, features the 鬼 バケツ盛り (6-piece raw / 8- and 16-piece steamed buckets) and Hiroshima コーネ (whale), sits on the 2nd floor a 3-min walk from the Tatemachi tram, and cooks each dish to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters and oyster gratin are on the menu, indicating wheat (breading/flour) is used in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese seafood izakaya dishes here rely on conventional wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is almost entirely oysters and other seafood, so vegan diners face pervasive seafood contact with no documented plant-based options.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local oyster izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and atmosphere; a foreign student should be comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app, and dinner accepts cards while lunch is cash only.

places.name: お好み焼き じゅにあ (Okonomiyaki Junia)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are okonomiyaki and Fuchu-yaki built on a wheat-flour batter, wheat noodles, and a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The specialty Fuchu-yaki uses beef-fat mince, and okonomiyaki here typically includes egg, pork, and fish-based dashi/bonito plus a wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun: 12:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:45), 18:15-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Mondays. Hours subject to change.
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (right by Tatemachi tram stop; Hondori / Hatchobori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing, individually run okonomiyaki shop in central Hiroshima, tucked near the Tatemachi tram stop behind the Nomura Securities building. The owner-chef cooks every order himself on the first-floor teppan counter, with sofa private rooms upstairs.

chef_bio.background: A long-standing, individually run okonomiyaki shop in central Hiroshima, tucked near the Tatemachi tram stop behind the Nomura Securities building. The owner-chef cooks every order himself on the first-floor teppan counter, with sofa private rooms upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its approach as keeping to old-fashioned cooking methods and ways of eating, and takes particular pride in its premium hand-pulled noodles, which it says even the noodle maker has full confidence in.
chef_bio.specialty: Fuchu-yaki, a regional style that uses beef-fat mince rather than the usual pork; the result is crisp on the outside and fluffy inside, with the sweetness of the cabbage playing against the sauce. The shop also serves classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, teppan dishes, and even hamburgers.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the owner as extremely shy and quiet, soft-spoken rather than gruff, and ultimately a very kind gentleman behind the griddle — as the shop itself notes, '店主は人見知りが激しく愛想も無く無口ですが怒っているわけではございません。とても優男です。'
The shop is known for being crowded with lines forming regularly, but locals say the wait is worth it.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site places Junia at 中区立町3-21 (1F-2F), one minute from the Tatemachi tram behind Nomura Securities, with the owner-chef cooking each order on the 1F teppan and sofa private rooms upstairs; it highlights Fuchu-yaki and premium hand-pulled noodles and carries the verbatim note that the proprietor is very shy but a kind man (とても優男).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki and Fuchu-yaki are finished with a standard sweet okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-and-soy based; the dishes are also built on wheat batter and wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature Fuchu-yaki is made with beef-fat mince, and a shared teppan is used for all dishes including meat, egg, and noodles, so vegan separation is not feasible.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional counter-style okonomiyaki shop where the chef cooks in front of you; little to no English is documented, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at the menu works fine for a relaxed casual meal.

places.name: ミカヅキ (Mikazuki)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (そば) or udon, then finished with a wheat-soy-based okonomi sauce. The core dishes here are inherently not gluten-free, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: The signature 肉玉そば and 肉玉うどん contain pork, egg, and a fish/bonito-derived savory base plus wheat-soy okonomi sauce, so the menu is not suitable for vegans. No plant-based version is documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun 11:30-14:00, 17:30-22:00 (closed Mondays)
places.neighborhood: Kako-machi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (near Funairicho tram stop; within walking distance west of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run okonomiyaki shop tucked into a quiet residential pocket of central Hiroshima, a short walk west of the Peace Memorial Park area. It keeps a deliberately narrow menu, focusing on doing the classic meat-and-egg okonomiyaki well rather than offering a lo

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run okonomiyaki shop in a quiet residential pocket of Naka Ward near Funairicho, southwest of the Peace Memorial Park, open since 2011 with just about a dozen counter-and-table seats. It keeps a deliberately narrow menu, focusing on doing the classic meat-and-egg okonomiyaki well rather than offering a long list of variations, and has been named to Tabelog's Top 100 Okonomiyaki list.
chef_bio.philosophy: A traditional Hiroshima technique of pressing the okonomiyaki down with a weight on the griddle, using fine boiled noodles griddled to a crisp, fragrant finish on a meticulously maintained teppan.
chef_bio.specialty: The house standard is 肉玉そば (meat, egg, and noodles), with an udon version also available; cheese and double-egg are among the few simple add-ons.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen presses the okonomiyaki with a weight while it cooks and uses fine-textured boiled noodles griddled until crisp, giving a noticeable toasted-wheat aroma that regulars single out.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists ミカヅキ in Naka Ward near Funairicho/Kakomachi (加古町) as owner-run, open since Sept 2011, 12 seats, a Tabelog Top 100 Okonomiyaki 2023 selection, with 肉玉そば (¥880), udon substitution and cheese/double-egg add-ons, weight-pressed on a well-kept griddle with crisp noodles (麺はパリ).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is finished with a standard okonomi/Worcester-type sauce that is wheat- and soy-based.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny family-run okonomiyaki counter where ordering is simple (point to 肉玉そば or 肉玉うどん) but little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 太閤うどん 中町店 (Taiko Udon Nakamachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are handmade wheat udon (a blend of three domestic wheat flours), so the core dish is not gluten-free. Tempura batter is also wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or wheat-free dashi/soy-sauce alternative, so this is not a suitable destination for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The Nakamachi branch has a dedicated vegan menu that uses no animal ingredients at all — notably soy-milk tantan udon and a no-soup (dry) tantan udon, available only at this location. The vegan dashi is made 100% from plant materials (kombu, vegetables, fruit) after extensive in-house research, and plant-based tempura is fried in a separate pot and oil kept apart from other orders. Note the standard (non-vegan) dashi uses bonito/mackerel/anchovy and wheat-containing soy sauce, so you must order from the vegan menu specifically. Foreign-language menus are available, which helps when specifying vegan needs.
places.neighborhood: Nakamachi, Naka-ku (Fukuromachi / Hondori area; within walking distance of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: Taiko Udon opened this central Hiroshima location in 2016 as its second shop, set in the city's downtown business and tourism district within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and Hondori shopping street. By day it is a handmade-udon restaurant; in the ev

chef_bio.background: Taiko Udon opened this central Hiroshima location in 2016 as its second shop, set in the city's downtown business and tourism district within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and Hondori shopping street. By day it is a handmade-udon restaurant; in the evening it shifts into an izakaya-style spot known as 'Udon-mae Taiko' (饂飩前太閤), serving small plates built around local seasonal ingredients alongside the noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen uses no chemical seasonings (mukacho) and emphasizes carefully selected, safe, local seasonal ingredients. The shop went to unusual lengths for plant-based diners, developing a 100% plant dashi from kombu, vegetables and fruit and keeping a separate fryer and oil for plant-based tempura.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade udon made from a blend of three domestic wheat flours and mineral-rich salt water, given a two-stage rest and made over a full day so it can be served freshly cut and freshly boiled. Signature dishes include the house-favorite ojiya udon and a creative carbonara udon.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers a free 'baby udon' made only with white dashi for families dining with infants ('白だしだけで作った赤ちゃんうどんは無料でお出ししています').
On the last day of each month the shop runs a daytime-only ramen service ('Taiko Ramen').

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Nakamachi page states the shop opened in 2016 as the 2号店 in central Hiroshima, becomes 饂飩前太閤 (izakaya style) at night, offers a free white-dashi 赤ちゃんうどん, and names ojiya udon (当店一番人気) and carbonara udon as signatures; vegewel confirms 無化調, a 100% plant vegan dashi from kombu/veg/fruit, a separate fryer and oil, three domestic wheat flours, and month-end daytime 太閤ラーメン.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The udon noodles are made from a blend of three domestic wheat flours, and tempura uses wheat batter; no gluten-free option is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard dashi uses Marushima soy sauce from Shodoshima (a conventional wheat-based shoyu) along with bonito, mackerel and anchovy, so the regular broth is neither vegan nor wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: For plant-based tempura and fried items, the shop prepares a dedicated oil and pot used only for plant-based items, kept separate from other customers' orders.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Foreign-language menus and a clearly marked vegan menu are available, and the vegan dishes are explicitly stated to contain no animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: The vegan dashi is made 100% from plant materials (kombu, vegetables and fruit) after extensive in-house research.
Dedicated separate oil and pot are used for plant-based tempura to avoid cross-contact with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: Foreign-language menus, free Wi-Fi, and counter power outlets make this an easy, welcoming stop for a foreign student, and staff can point vegan diners to the dedicated plant-based menu.

places.name: 伊都岐珈琲 宮島店 (Itsuki Coffee Miyajima)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labeling is documented. Drip coffee, espresso and the specialty-coffee soft serve are not gluten-containing by nature, but food items like the morning hot sandwich and cream pan use wheat bread. No allergy or gluten-free information is published, so a GF traveler must ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: Primarily a coffee shop. Black drip coffee and espresso are plant-based, but the signature soft serve uses fresh dairy cream, the latte/mocha use milk, and the morning set uses ham, cheese and bread. No vegan menu or plant-milk option is documented, so a vegan should ask about dairy-free milk before ordering.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, near Itsukushima Shrine (Omotesando shopping street area)
places.cuisine: Specialty coffee cafe with house roasting
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founder Keisuke Sasaki grew up in Hiroshima City. As a university student he and two friends launched a venture pulling rickshaws on Miyajima, which led him to study the island's history and culture to answer tourists' questions. While doing this he also worked part-tim (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Founder Keisuke Sasaki is from Asaminami Ward in Hiroshima City. As a university student he and three friends launched a venture pulling rickshaws on Miyajima, which led him to study the island's history and culture to answer tourists' questions, before turning toward the world of coffee. In 2006 he opened his first self-roasted-coffee cafe at age 23, keeping it open until midnight in a back-alley spot with few customers. By 2009 he had opened a second cafe, sarasvati, as Japan's appreciation for specialty coffee grew.
chef_bio.philosophy: He aims for an 'unnoticed specialty' (意識されないスペシャルティ): he is happiest when people enjoy his coffee without consciously thinking about how special it is, letting it blend naturally into a quiet moment of their day.
chef_bio.specialty: Specialty coffee that he roasts himself on the island, using only the roughly 5% of beans in the market that qualify as 'specialty', alongside a popular specialty-coffee soft serve.

anecdotes.text: He describes the early years bluntly: 'barely surviving, starting in an alley where almost no one came, so we did not get many customers, staying open until midnight' ('やっぱり生きていくのに精一杯。路地裏の、誰も来ないような場所で始めたので、お客さんもそんなに来なくて、夜12時まで開けていました').
The shop is the only place self-roasting specialty coffee on Miyajima, a UNESCO World Heritage island, set in a renovated old-house-style (古民家風) building near the shrine approach.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The note.com interview gives Sasaki's Asaminami-ku origin, the student rickshaw venture with three friends, the 2006 self-roast cafe at 23, the verbatim alley/midnight struggle quote, and the 意識されないスペシャルティ ideal; the official site confirms Itsuki as the only self-roasting specialty shop on Miyajima, ~5% specialty-grade beans, soft serve, a renovated old-house building, and sarasvati (2009).

positives.text: An English menu is available, which helps travelers communicate basic preferences when ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and ordering is casual cafe-style, so a foreign student can visit comfortably; note that credit cards are not accepted (electronic money and QR payments only).

places.name: 広島つけ麺 弁慶 (Hiroshima Tsukemen Benkei)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat-based: tsukemen uses wheat noodles dipped in a soy-sauce-and-chili sauce (Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat), and the tantanmen is also a wheat-noodle dish. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature tsukemen is topped with char siu (pork) and served with a soy-chili dipping sauce, and the tantanmen is meat-and-sesame based. No vegan option is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 / 19:00-07:00 (open daily, year-round)
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima nightlife district);
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A veteran Hiroshima tsukemen shop that has been a fixture of the Nagarekawa nightlife street for more than ten years, marked by a red noren curtain near the Billy the Kid bar.

chef_bio.background: A veteran Hiroshima tsukemen shop and longtime fixture of the Nagarekawa nightlife street, run under the Billy the Kid group and marked by its red noren curtain by the Billy the Kid bar.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make-it-your-own bowls: diners choose their noodle portion and toppings and dial the spice level up or down to taste, with a squeeze of lemon to brighten the dip.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chewy noodles served cold with crisp shredded cabbage, julienned daikon, and char siu, dipped in a bright, refreshingly tangy and spicy sauce; also a Hiroshima specialty soup-less tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: Open from late night into the early morning in the heart of the drinking district, it is a favorite spot to round off a night out with a bowl of noodles.
Marked by a red noren on Hiroshima's Nagarekawa street by the Billy the Kid bar, it is a long-loved tsukemen shop.
The tsukemen has a refreshing sour edge, comes piled with cabbage, shredded daikon and char siu, and lets you adjust spice and portion; adding lemon makes it even more refreshing.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog places 弁慶 in Nagarekawa (open into the early morning, 11:30-14:30 and 19:00-07:00), operated under Billy the Kid Inc. (est. 1994), serving cold Hiroshima tsukemen with cabbage/daikon/char siu, an adjustable tangy-spicy dip finished with lemon, plus a soupless tantanmen; retty corroborates the toppings and customizable spice/portion.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat-based (wheat tsukemen/ramen noodles and a soy-sauce-based dip), so a shared kitchen makes gluten avoidance impractical.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima tsukemen is dipped in a soy-sauce-and-chili sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on pork char siu and meat/sesame-based tantanmen, so animal products are central and shared.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills local noodle counter in a nightlife alley with no reservations and a mostly Japanese-speaking, late-night crowd; pointing at the menu and choosing your noodle size and spice level works fine, but expect little to no English.

noodles.notes: Thin straight noodles with firm koshi, served chilled in the Hiroshima style and dipped into a cold spicy seafood sauce.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chilled thin straight noodles with a mound of boiled cabbage, dipped into a cold, additive-free seafood dipping sauce balanced with house chili heat (choose your spice from mild to extreme), brightened with a squeeze of lemon.
ramen.chashu: Firm-textured chashu, sliced and served cold atop the noodles in the Hiroshima tsukemen manner rather than melt-soft braised belly.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy-based dipping sauce; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented, so it is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on katsuo (bonito) and kombu seafood dashi and the bowl includes pork chashu; no plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima tsukemen, where the soul lives in a cold dipping sauce: a clean kombu-and-bonito seafood dashi laced with house chili oil, nerigoma, and a tightening prickle of sansho, scaled from gentle to ferociously hot. You taste the city's signature lineage in the chilled noodles, the bracing acidity of lemon, and the sweet crunch of boiled cabbage that defines this regional bowl.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima tsukemen, where the soul lives in a cold dipping sauce: a clean kombu-and-bonito seafood dashi laced with house chili oil, nerigoma, and a tightening prickle of sansho, scaled from gentle to ferociously hot. You taste the city's signature lineage in the chilled noodles, the bracing acidity of lemon, and the sweet crunch of boiled cabbage that defines this regional bowl.

places.name: とりい (Torii)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes — fried oysters (wheat-flour batter and breadcrumbs), tempura-style items, udon/soba/ramen noodles, and grilled/simmered seafood glazed with standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) — all rely on gluten. There is no documented dedicated fryer or gluten-free menu, so cross-contamination from the shared fry oil and soy-based sauces is likely. The parent company notes it can discuss food allergies in general, but no celiac-safe protocol is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around oysters, anago (conger eel), and other Setouchi seafood; set meals include chawanmushi (egg custard) and dashi-based soups, and dishes are seasoned with fish stock and soy sauce. No documented plant-based or vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–16:00 (per official site). Note: Tabelog lists 11:00–16:00.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine and Senjokak
places.notes: [Miyajima] A seafood dining hall on the island of Miyajima, set among the shops on the approach near Itsukushima Shrine and Senjokaku Hall, where oysters are grilled out front to draw in passers-by. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A seafood dining hall on the island of Miyajima, set among the shops on the approach near Itsukushima Shrine and Senjokaku Hall, where oysters are grilled out front to draw in passers-by.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house aims to be a place to enjoy the flavors of the Seto Inland Sea and to serve a taste that stays in your memory, choosing ingredients and cooking with care.
chef_bio.specialty: Fried-oyster and Miyajima set meals (anago rice paired with oyster fry), anago (conger eel) dishes, and front-of-shop charcoal-grilled oysters, alongside Setouchi seafood, donburi, and noodles.

anecdotes.text: Oysters are grilled at the storefront, a classic Miyajima sight, with the seasonal Setouchi lemon soft serve and strawberry shaved ice (spring-autumn) offered alongside the seafood.
Seating spans two floors, including window-side counter seats and tatami zashiki rooms upstairs, making it comfortable for families and groups visiting the shrine.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: toriiya.co.jp describes とりい as a Setouchi seafood dining hall on Miyajima with storefront charcoal-grilled oysters (6 for ¥1,650), a Miyajima Set (¥2,500) of anago rice + oyster fry, donburi/udon/soba/ramen/curry, seasonal fresh-strawberry shaved ice (Apr-Oct), and two floors with counter and tatami zashiki seating (~115 seats).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature fried oysters use wheat-flour batter and breadcrumbs, and the kitchen also serves udon, soba, and ramen, so the shared fry oil and prep surfaces present a high gluten cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Grilled and simmered seafood and donburi are glazed/seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is seafood-centered (oysters, anago) with egg-based chawanmushi and dashi/soy seasoning throughout, so there is no separation from animal ingredients.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The affiliated company's site states food-allergy inquiries can be accommodated, but no celiac- or vegan-specific protocol is documented for the dining hall.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, tourist-friendly seafood dining hall right by the shrine where pointing at picture menus works fine, but staff may have limited English and there is no vegan or gluten-free provision, so a guide or translation help is useful for anyone with strict dietary needs.

places.name: 貴家。 地蔵通り本店 (Takaya Jizodori Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The okonomiyaki itself is built on a wheat-flour batter (crepe layer), is layered with udon or soba noodles, and is finished with okonomiyaki sauce — all of which contain wheat/gluten. There is no indication of any gluten-free menu or accommodation, so it is not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: The core dish is a "meat and egg" (肉玉) okonomiyaki containing pork, egg, and noodles, and the standard sauce/preparation are not plant-based. No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Tuesdays; also closed 1st and 3rd Mondays (holiday hours may vary).
places.neighborhood: Fujimi-cho, Naka-ku (Jizo-dori), near Chuden-mae
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run okonomiyaki counter tucked along Hiroshima's historic Jizo-dori in Fujimi-cho. The proprietor has spent roughly three decades devoted to okonomiyaki, grilling each one personally and one at a time right in front of guests at the 12-seat counter.

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run okonomiyaki counter tucked along Hiroshima's historic Jizo-dori in Fujimi-cho. The proprietor has spent roughly three decades devoted to okonomiyaki, grilling each one personally and one at a time right in front of guests at the 12-seat counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is proud of being a 'seitoha' (orthodox) Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — authentic, traditional preparation done meticulously. The owner changes the cabbage variety with the seasons and pays close attention to how the vegetables are cut and grilled so their natural sweetness comes through, finished with a house sauce made with plenty of vegetables that is clean and not overly sweet.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with a meat-and-egg base, customized with your choice of udon or soba; the noodle layer is grilled until distinctively crisp, and the seasonal cabbage and house vegetable-rich sauce are the signatures.

anecdotes.text: You eat straight off the teppan griddle using only a metal spatula — no chopsticks — which feels intimidating to newcomers but becomes part of the charm once you get the hang of it.
The cabbage is swapped by season and the vegetables are cut and grilled with care to draw out their sweetness, paired with a clean, not-sweet house sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ttcbn describes 貴家。地蔵通り本店 as a 12-seat counter on Jizo-dori in Fujimi-cho where the owner-chef of roughly three decades grills each okonomiyaki to order, serves orthodox (正統派) meat-egg okonomiyaki with udon/soba and a crisp noodle layer, and has diners eat off the teppan with a spatula (no chopsticks); tabiiro corroborates the seasonal cabbage and clean, not-sweet vegetable-rich sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle where wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-containing sauce are all prepared, making cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard okonomiyaki contains pork, egg, and noodles cooked on a shared griddle alongside other meat dishes; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only local spot where the owner grills in front of you and you eat straight off the hot griddle with a metal spatula (no chopsticks) — charming but with its own etiquette, so some basic Japanese or a willingness to follow along helps.

places.name: 上下左右 (JoGeSaYu)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free venue. Although the menu is allergy-conscious, no gluten-free certification, dedicated GF preparation, or GF menu is documented. Several signature items are inherently wheat-based: okonomiyaki batter, ramen noodles, tempura/karaage batter, katsu breading; and standard soy sauce typically contains wheat. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask directly about specific dishes and cross-contamination before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan. 100% plant-based and alcohol-free across all dishes and desserts, with no animal-derived ingredients used anywhere in the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). 15:00-17:00 is prep time. Irregular days off (closures announced on Instagram).
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (near Peace Memorial Park / Atomic Bomb Dome; ~2-5 min walk from Hiroden Fukuromachi tram stop)
places.notes: JoGeSaYu opened in May 2025 in central Hiroshima as a fully plant-based restaurant. Its food is overseen by Katsumi Kusumoto, the chef behind Saido, which the international vegan platform HappyCow named the world's best vegan restaurant in 2019, while the desserts are g

chef_bio.origin: Otemachi, central Hiroshima, near Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome
chef_bio.background: JoGeSaYu opened in May 2025 in central Hiroshima (Otemachi) as a fully plant-based, alcohol-free restaurant. Its food is overseen by Katsumi Kusumoto, the chef behind Saido, which the international vegan platform HappyCow named the world's best vegan restaurant in 2019, while the desserts are guided by award-winning pastry chef Hironobu Tsujiguchi. The kitchen turns Japanese comfort food such as sushi, okonomiyaki, tempura bowls and karaage into entirely plant-based versions.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name (up, down, left, right) expresses acceptance of every direction, value, and form of diversity. The team set out to create a single table where people of any background — religion, allergies, dietary practice — can share a meal together, and to prove that plant-based food need not lack flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based renditions of Japanese classics: vegan sushi, okonomiyaki, tempura bowls and karaage, alongside refined vegan desserts such as parfaits and lemon cake.

anecdotes.text: The name was chosen to symbolize openness to all directions and forms of diversity, reflecting its goal of a table where everyone can eat together regardless of dietary restriction.
Culinary direction comes from Katsumi Kusumoto of Saido, named the world's best vegan restaurant by HappyCow in 2019, with desserts by pastry chef Hironobu Tsujiguchi.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site states outright that culinary director Katsumi Kusumoto is the chef of Saido (HappyCow world's best vegan restaurant 2019), sweets are by patissier Hironobu Tsujiguchi, and 上下左右 symbolizes acceptance of all directions and diversity; Balcom (料理監修は…世界1位に輝いた) and fooddiversity corroborate the leadership, the May 2025 Otemachi opening, 100% plant-based/alcohol-free food, and dishes including vegan sushi, okonomiyaki, tempura bowls, karaage, lemon cake and parfaits.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information published on whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are gluten-free or wheat-free; standard soy sauce typically contains wheat. Confirm directly if avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is 100% plant-based with no animal products or alcohol used anywhere, so there is no animal-ingredient cross-contact risk.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu is described as built to accommodate diverse backgrounds including religious dietary rules and allergies, so diners with and without restrictions can eat together.
The restaurant states it welcomes all guests regardless of religious, health, or lifestyle restrictions.

positives.text: 100% plant-based and alcohol-free across the whole menu, making it a fully safe choice for vegans and vegetarians.
Vegan versions of Japanese favorites such as sushi, okonomiyaki, ramen, curry, tempura and karaage, plus vegan parfaits and lemon cake.
Official website available in English, Japanese, and Chinese, easing communication for international diners.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant's own website is offered in English, Japanese, and Chinese with an English contact option, so a foreign student should feel comfortable communicating here.

places.name: かんらん車 (Kanransha)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made with egg, pork, and a fish/bonito-derived sauce on a shared griddle, and there is no documented vegan or vegetarian option. The shop's signature is cabbage-forward ('倍ヤサイ' double-vegetable is offered) but the dishes themselves are not vegan.
places.hours_raw: Tue, Wed, Thu, Sat, Sun: 11:30-13:00 / 18:00-19:30 (L.O. 13:00 and 19:30). Closed Mondays and Fridays.
places.neighborhood: Nakaku (Nekoyacho), near Tsuchihashi / Tokaichimachi tram st
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, tucked-away okonomiyaki shop in a back alley near the Tsuchihashi/Tokaichimachi trams, only about an eight-minute walk from the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. Despite being off the main tourist routes, it has been a neighborhood favorite for many years, r

chef_bio.background: A small, tucked-away okonomiyaki shop in a back alley near the Tsuchihashi/Tokaichimachi trams, only about an eight-minute walk from the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. Despite being off the main tourist routes, it has been a neighborhood favorite for many years, run intimately by the cook and his wife.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the vegetable lead and finish each okonomiyaki with care. The cook works the griddle by reading its different temperature zones to cook precisely, a hands-on craft at a counter that seats only a handful of people.
chef_bio.specialty: Cabbage-forward Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. The name plays on 甘藍 (kanran, classical Japanese for cabbage), signaling that cabbage is the true star; the shop even offers a double-vegetable ('倍ヤサイ') option.

anecdotes.text: The cook is described as a true craftsman, deftly using the different heat zones across the iron griddle to finish each okonomiyaki carefully.
Though traditionally eaten straight off the hot griddle, the shop will serve okonomiyaki on a plate for visitors who prefer it, a small kindness toward travelers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: onetabi.com documents the husband-and-wife operation, the griddle heat-zone technique, the 甘藍 (cabbage) name origin, the 倍ヤサイ double-vegetable option, roughly 7 counter seats plus a couple of tables, and plate service for visitors; it locates the shop about 8 minutes from the Peace Park near the Tsuchihashi/Tokaichimachi trams.
