gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared iron griddle (teppan) used for wheat batter, wheat noodles, pork, and egg, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-and-soy based; standard for Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on one griddle shared with pork and egg, so no dish can be considered free of animal-product cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny owner-run counter shop where ordering and chatting happen mostly in Japanese, though the cook is used to visitors and will plate your okonomiyaki rather than serve it straight off the griddle if you prefer.

places.name: ビーガン フルーツカフェ タマル 紙屋町店 (Vegan Fruits Cafe Tamaru Kamiyacho)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is documented on any available source. The menu centers on fruit-forward vegan items (parfaits, oatmeal, smoothies) that may suit gluten-avoidant diners, but sandwiches and toast use bread, and there is no stated GF preparation or cross-contamination control. A traveler with celiac disease should ask in person.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan since an April 2022 renovation made the entire menu plant-based. No animal products (eggs, dairy) and no white sugar are used. Signature items include fruit parfaits with whole bunches of seasonal fruit (e.g. Shine Muscat), vegan sandwiches, and fruit oatmeal served with almond milk.
places.neighborhood: Kamiyachō / Hondori, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (near A-Bomb
places.cuisine: All-vegan fruit cafe: fruit parfaits, vegan
places.notes: Vegan Fruits Cafe Tamaru is part of TAMARU, a Hiroshima fruit business that grew out of a small fruit vendor near Hiroshima Station with roughly 70 years of history. The downtown Kamiyacho location reinvented itself in April 2022, turning its entire menu plant-based to

chef_bio.background: Vegan Fruits Cafe Tamaru is part of TAMARU, a Hiroshima fruit business that grew out of a small fruit vendor near Hiroshima Station with roughly 70 years of history. In 2022 the downtown Kamiyacho location reinvented itself, turning its entire menu plant-based to introduce locals and visitors to vegan food.
chef_bio.philosophy: To show that vegan dessert can be genuinely delicious by building everything around high-quality fruit and grains rather than animal products or refined white sugar.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit-forward vegan parfaits and sweets made without eggs, dairy, or white sugar; signature parfaits pile on whole bunches of seasonal fruit such as Shine Muscat, alongside vegan sandwiches and fruit oatmeal with almond milk.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is an offshoot of a long-running family fruit vendor near Hiroshima Station that has been selling fruit for about 70 years, so the parfaits are built on a deep fruit-sourcing pedigree.
The Kamiyacho branch renovated and switched its entire menu to vegan, eliminating eggs, dairy, and white sugar.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: restaurant-guide.jp confirms the Kamiyacho shop's all-vegan menu (no eggs, dairy, or white sugar), the fruit oatmeal with almond milk, parfaits and vegan sandwiches, and its ties to the TAMARU fruit business; japantravel documents the ~70-year fruit-vendor origin near Hiroshima Station, and the Shine Muscat parfait is documented across guides. The exact renovation month (April 2022) could not be traced, so it is stated only as 2022.

positives.text: The entire menu is plant-based, made without eggs, dairy, or white sugar, so vegan diners do not need to scrutinize individual dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming fruit cafe in a busy downtown shopping area; menus are mostly in Japanese with lots of photos, so a few basic phrases and pointing will get you by comfortably.

places.name: 牡蠣と海鮮酒場 うみいち 流川店 (Kaki to Kaisen Sakaba Umiichi Nagarekawa-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. As an izakaya, fried/tempura oysters use wheat batter and most dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce; no dedicated GF preparation indicated.
places.vegan_detail: An oyster and seafood izakaya — the menu centers on oysters, sashimi and fish, with wheat-soy-sauce seasonings. No vegan offerings indicated.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:00-01:00 (next day), Fri 17:00-02:00 (next day), Sat 16:00-02:00 (next day), Sun 17:00-01:00 (next day) (official site). Note: reservation aggregators list earlier midday openings on some days; official site hours used here.
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Ebisu-chō / Nagarekawa entertainment district, ~1 m
places.notes: An oyster-and-seafood izakaya in the heart of Nagarekawa, central Hiroshima's largest nightlife quarter, serving oysters and seafood bought from local Hiroshima suppliers.

chef_bio.background: An oyster-and-seafood izakaya in the heart of Nagarekawa, central Hiroshima's largest nightlife quarter, serving oysters and seafood bought from local Hiroshima suppliers.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served many ways — creamy raw oysters by the piece, fragrant grilled oysters, oyster tempura, fried oysters and sake-steamed oysters with butter — alongside sashimi platters and a deep lineup of Hiroshima local sake such as Kamotsuru, Ugo no Tsuki and Kirei.

anecdotes.text: Set in a modern, black-toned interior with atmospheric lighting and fully private rooms with sunken-floor seating, it pairs market-fresh oysters with regional sake.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms oysters bought from local Hiroshima suppliers and served raw by the piece, grilled, as tempura, fried and sake-steamed with butter, plus a Hiroshima local-sake list (Kamotsuru, Ugo-no-Tsuki, Kirei) and private sunken-floor rooms in the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local izakaya in the Nagarekawa nightlife district where some basic Japanese or a translation app will help, as menus and staff are oriented to Japanese guests.

places.name: 薬研堀 八昌 (Yagenbori Hassho)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe layered with cabbage, pork, wheat noodles (soba/udon), and egg, then finished with a wheat-containing okonomiyaki sauce. Effectively every dish here contains gluten, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki centers on pork, egg, and a single shared griddle, with bonito/fish-based seasonings and oysters added in winter. No vegan version is documented and the cooking is fundamentally animal-based.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat 16:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:30); Sun & holidays 16:00-21:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Mondays, and the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori, walkable to Hondori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yagenbori Hassho is the original, owner-run shop tucked into the Yagenbori district of central Hiroshima, around the corner from the Hatchobori entertainment area and within walking distance of Hondori. It has been grilling Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki for roughly four d

chef_bio.background: Yagenbori Hassho is a celebrated, owner-run shop tucked into the Yagenbori district of central Hiroshima, around the corner from the Hatchobori entertainment area and within walking distance of Hondori. It has been grilling Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki for roughly four decades and is regarded as one of the city's defining okonomiyaki houses.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen takes its time, cooking each okonomiyaki slowly over about 20 to 30 minutes so the cabbage steams down sweet and the layers meld, prizing careful technique and good ingredients over speed.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the soba-niku-tama (noodle, pork and egg) okonomiyaki as the signature; oysters appear on the griddle in winter.

anecdotes.text: Each okonomiyaki is grilled patiently for about 20 to 30 minutes, a deliberately slow approach that is part of the shop's reputation.
The house favorite is the soba-niku-tama okonomiyaki, layering wheat noodles, cabbage, pork and egg.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official gorp site states 創業40年 (established 40 years), names soba-niku-tama as the top seller made with 'twin eggs', and notes the ~20-30 minute cook time; Tabelog confirms seasonal winter oysters and consistent Tabelog-100 okonomiyaki selection. Whether this is the very first Hassho shop is not documented, so that claim was removed.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is a wheat-containing sauce, and the noodle versions use wheat soba/udon.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on one griddle used for pork, egg and fish-based seasonings, with no separate vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the counter is welcoming, but it is a small, busy, cash-only neighborhood shop, so a friendly greeting and patience during the long cook time go a long way.

places.name: さらしな (Sarashina)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; there is no documented gluten-free option here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The traditional batter and broth lean on bonito (katsuo) and tororo-kombu dashi flavor, the signature dish includes pork and egg, and dishes are cooked on a shared teppan with meat and seafood. No vegan version is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:30 (Tue-Sun); closed Mondays (open the following day if Monday is a public holiday)
places.neighborhood: 新天地・お好み村 (Shintenchi / Okonomimura), Naka-ku, near Hatchobor
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sarashina has been making Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki since 1951, having started in the post-war years and grown into one of the long-running stalls that became part of Okonomimura, the multi-floor okonomiyaki complex in the Shintenchi district just east of the Peace Me

chef_bio.background: Sarashina has been making Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki since 1951, having started in the post-war years and grown into one of the long-running stalls that became part of Okonomimura, the multi-floor okonomiyaki complex in the Shintenchi district just east of the Peace Memorial Park.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, careful approach: the cook grills each okonomiyaki slowly and asks each guest about preferences such as whether to add red pickled ginger, tailoring the dish to the person in front of the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional griddle-cooked okonomiyaki layered with cabbage, pork, egg and noodles; the kitchen is known for generously folding in bonito flakes and tororo-kombu (shaved kelp) for a deep, gentle umami that defines its old-school flavor. The most-ordered plate is the classic nikutama-soba (pork, egg and noodles).

anecdotes.text: The shop dates to 1951 and is part of Okonomimura, the okonomiyaki food theme park near Hatchobori; it has kept the same tororo-kombu-and-bonito tradition for decades.
Staff take the time to ask guests about toppings like benishoga (red pickled ginger) so each okonomiyaki matches the customer's taste.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: r.gnavi confirms Sarashina's 1951 founding, its spot on the 2nd floor of Okonomimura (Shintenchi 5-13, Naka-ku, near Hatchobori), its generous use of bonito and tororo-kombu, the nikutama-soba as its popular plate, and staff asking guests about benishoga.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared flat-top griddle where wheat batter and wheat noodles are prepared together, so gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cooking surface.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is finished with traditional okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are griddled alongside pork, egg and seafood-derived dashi/bonito on a shared teppan, so animal-product cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional teppan counter run mostly by one cook with no English menu, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the popular nikutama-soba will carry you through; come with cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: ちいちゃん (Chiichan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing sauce is a wheat-based okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house specialties center on egg (signature soft half-cooked egg negiyaki), pork, seafood and winter Hiroshima oysters, cooked on a shared griddle, with bonito/fish-based and egg-containing toppings and wheat-soy sauce. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri 11:30-21:00; Sat-Sun 11:00-21:00; closed Tuesdays. (One source lists opening from 11:00 with variable close.)
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi / Hatchobori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chiichan is one of the oldest shops in Hiroshima's Okonomi-mura, the famous building stacked with okonomiyaki stalls. It was opened by Shozaburo Furuta, who became the very first 'village head' of Okonomi-mura when the collective of food stalls was organized in 1965, an

chef_bio.chef_name: Furuta family (founded by Shozaburo Furuta, the first head of Okonomi-mura)
chef_bio.background: Chiichan is one of the oldest shops in Hiroshima's Okonomi-mura, the famous building stacked with okonomiyaki stalls. It was opened by Shozaburo Furuta, who became the very first 'village head' of Okonomi-mura when the collective of food stalls was organized in December 1965 — and whose own nickname, 'Chiichan', gave the shop its name. It has been passed down within the family and is now carried on by the second generation, keeping a single griddle counter of about 18 seats going for over half a century.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking centered on local Hiroshima ingredients while keeping salt and oil to a minimum, prizing the soft-runny egg and the fragrant crispness of the griddled noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style negiyaki piled with sweet locally grown green onion (negi) and a trademark soft, runny half-cooked egg; seafood-and-oyster negiyaki in winter is a draw, alongside classic Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki.

anecdotes.text: Chiichan's founder, Shozaburo Furuta, was the first 'village head' (shochou) of Okonomi-mura when the stall collective was formed in December 1965, and the shop — named after his nickname — has operated for over 50 years, carried on by the next generation.
The shop is known for negiyaki using sweet Hiroshima-grown green onion and a signature runny half-cooked egg, with winter Hiroshima oysters among its seasonal highlights.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese Wikipedia states Okonomi-mura was established in December 1965 and its first village head was 古田正三郎 (Shozaburo Furuta), nicknamed 'ちいちゃん' — the source of the shop's name. Guides confirm the shop is now run by the second generation, seats about 18, and is known for Hiroshima green-onion negiyaki with a creamy half-cooked egg.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki and negiyaki are cooked on a single shared teppan griddle and built from wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with a wheat-based okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle is used for pork, egg, seafood and oysters; specialties prominently feature egg, so vegan separation is not feasible.

positives.text: Small two-person family operation cooking at the counter, so you can watch your dish made and speak directly with the cook about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, family-run counter griddle shop inside the tourist-familiar Okonomi-mura; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way, and staff are used to visitors.

places.name: 進 (Susumu)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce, plus typically yakisoba/udon noodles. This is not a gluten-free-friendly dish or venue and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki here uses egg, pork belly, and a bonito/dashi-influenced wheat sauce, and noodles are added on the griddle shared with meat and seafood. There is no documented vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-23:00; closed Tuesdays (one directory listing notes Wednesday instead)
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (Okonomimura), central Hiroshima
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Susumu is one of the small, individually run okonomiyaki stalls packed inside Okonomimura, the iconic multi-floor okonomiyaki building in central Hiroshima's Shintenchi district. Like its neighbors, it serves classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on

roles: okonomiyaki stall in Okonomi Kyowakoku Hiroshima-mura
chef_bio.origin: Part of お好み共和国 ひろしま村 (Okonomi Kyowakoku Hiroshima-mura), a Hiroshima okonomiyaki theme park in the Shintenchi district near Hatchobori that gathers several independent okonomiyaki shops across two floors of the Shintenchi New-ji building.
chef_bio.background: Susumu is one of the individually run okonomiyaki shops inside お好み共和国 ひろしま村, an okonomiyaki theme park that opened in 2001 in central Hiroshima's Shintenchi district near Hatchobori — a separate complex from the older, similarly named お好み村. Like its neighbors, it serves classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on a hot teppan right in front of guests seated at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (cabbage, batter crepe, noodles, pork, egg, and okonomiyaki sauce griddled to order); the most-ordered plate is the nikutama-soba (pork, egg and noodles).

anecdotes.text: Susumu sits on the 2nd floor of お好み共和国 ひろしま村 with roughly 26 seats, and its most-ordered dish in reviews is the nikutama-soba.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and okonomiyaki.or.jp confirm 進 is a stall on the 2nd floor of お好み共和国 ひろしま村 (a Shintenchi okonomiyaki theme park that opened in 2001, distinct from お好み村), seating about 26, with the nikutama-soba (¥1,000) as its signature plate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter, wheat-based sauce, and noodles; high gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cuisine.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared griddle used for pork, egg, and seafood; not suitable for strict vegans.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny family-run teppan stall inside the bustling Okonomimura food building; staff are used to tourists but English is limited, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way.

places.name: 文ちゃん (Bunchan)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop. The okonomiyaki crepe base, the savory sauce, and the noodles all contain wheat, and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built around egg, pork, and a layer of fried noodles, finished with a savory sauce and bonito; the menu centers on these. There is no documented vegan option, so this is not a suitable destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:30 (Tabelog). Some listings show 11:00–23:00.
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi / Okonomimura (Naka-ku, central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run okonomiyaki counter tucked on the second floor of Okonomimura, the famous Hiroshima building that gathers dozens of okonomiyaki stalls under one roof. The shop traces its roots back to the postwar street-stall (yatai) era and has been cooking okonomiyaki he

chef_bio.background: A family-run okonomiyaki counter tucked on the second floor of Okonomimura, the famous Hiroshima building that gathers dozens of okonomiyaki stalls under one roof. The shop traces its roots back to the postwar street-stall (yatai) era and has been cooking okonomiyaki here for decades, now in the hands of a second-generation owner.
chef_bio.philosophy: A homey, no-frills counter where the okonomiyaki is kept light and easy to finish — a style locals reach for as a satisfying close to an evening out.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, layered on a thin crepe with cabbage and freshly boiled noodles cooked daily, griddled until the base is crisp and fragrant.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for a lighter, crispier okonomiyaki that diners describe as easy to finish even after drinking, making it a popular end-of-night spot.
It has been serving okonomiyaki inside Okonomimura for over sixty years, with a history reaching back to the era of postwar food stalls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms 文ちゃん sits on the 2nd floor of お好み村, traces to the postwar yatai era (1950s), is now run by the second generation (at its current spot since 1992), seats about 15, boils its noodles fresh daily, and grills a deliberately lighter, crispier okonomiyaki without pressing it with the spatula.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat-based noodles; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based, and standard soy sauce contains wheat; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single griddle is used for pork, egg, and bonito-finished dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only counter shop inside the bustling Okonomimura okonomiyaki building; a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way, and you can watch your okonomiyaki cooked on the griddle right in front of you.

places.name: 廣島辛つけ麺 くじら屋 (Hiroshima Kara Tsukemen Kujiraya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are tsukemen and abura soba built on wheat noodles, and the dipping broth uses standard (wheat-based) soy-style seasoning. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature spicy dipping broth is built on seafood (oyster and scallop adductor), and toppings include squid tempura and other animal-based items. No vegan option is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 18:00-03:00 (L.O. 02:30); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (south of Nagarekawa / Hatchobori, central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small late-night noodle counter in Hiroshima's Yagenbori district that opened in April 2022, sitting just south of the Nagarekawa and Hatchobori nightlife and drawing diners looking for a satisfying bowl to cap off an evening out.

chef_bio.background: A small late-night noodle counter in Hiroshima's Yagenbori district that opened in April 2022, sitting just south of the Nagarekawa and Hatchobori nightlife and drawing diners looking for a satisfying bowl to cap off an evening out.
chef_bio.philosophy: Standing out from the crowd: the shop leans into unusual, original ingredients rather than copying the standard local tsukemen template.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style spicy tsukemen (dipping noodles) with a seafood-forward broth made using oyster and scallop, served with curly dry noodles and distinctive toppings like lemon-scented squid tempura and mushroom duxelles; oil-based abura soba is also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: A local food writer remarked he had never seen Hiroshima tsukemen differentiated this far, calling out ingredients not found at other shops.
The shop is open only at night and is widely treated as a go-to spot for a finishing bowl after drinking in the nearby entertainment district.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The modokimaru blog confirms the April 2022 opening in Yagenbori, the night-only hours (18:00-03:00), the oyster-and-scallop seafood broth with curly dry noodles, the lemon-scented squid tempura and mushroom-duxelles toppings, the abura soba, roughly 14 seats, and the writer's remark that he had never seen Hiroshima tsukemen differentiated this far.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the base of both the tsukemen and abura soba, so the kitchen handles wheat throughout; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth uses a soy-style seasoning typical of Hiroshima tsukemen, which is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth itself is seafood-based (oyster and scallop), so it is not separable from animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A small late-night local counter where ordering is in Japanese and the vibe is a post-drinking noodle stop; a foreign student comfortable pointing at the menu or with basic Japanese will do fine.

noodles.notes: Curly, dry-textured noodles custom-made by a long-established noodle maker in Asakusa, Tokyo — an unusual choice for Hiroshima tsukemen, which typically uses straighter strands. The chewy, ridged surface grabs the spicy dipping liquid.

ramen.signature_bowl: 廣島辛つけ麺 (Hiroshima spicy tsukemen) — cold curly noodles served with a chilled, scallop- and oyster-driven seafood dashi dipping liquid spiked with house-made chili oil; heat is dialed in on a 0–10 scale, with a "Red Kujira / Godly Spicy" tier above that.
ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-cooked chicken breast chashu — lean, tender and pink rather than the braised pork belly more common elsewhere; served on the abura-soba and available as a topping.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui curly noodles plus a shoyu-based, chili-oil dipping liquid; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on oyster and scallop seafood dashi, and the bowls carry chicken chashu, egg and squid. No plant-only broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern voice in Hiroshima's signature cold-noodle kara-tsukemen tradition — chilled curly noodles dunked in a chili-laced dipping liquid where the heat is yours to set. What sets this bowl apart is its soul: instead of the usual chicken-and-seafood base, the dip is anchored in a clean, umami-deep dashi of scallop and oyster, lifted by house-made chili oil and a fifteen-spice blend, with quirky flourishes like lemon-scented squid tempura.

places.highlight: A modern voice in Hiroshima's signature cold-noodle kara-tsukemen tradition — chilled curly noodles dunked in a chili-laced dipping liquid where the heat is yours to set. What sets this bowl apart is its soul: instead of the usual chicken-and-seafood base, the dip is anchored in a clean, umami-deep dashi of scallop and oyster, lifted by house-made chili oil and a fifteen-spice blend, with quirky flourishes like lemon-scented squid tempura.

places.name: 昇龍軒 (Shoryuken)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen shop. The noodles are wheat-based and the broth and tare contain wheat-derived soy sauce, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are documented and there is no indication of allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: The signature ramen is built on a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with chashu pork, egg, and back-fat. The broth itself is animal-based, so the menu is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians and no plant-based bowl is documented.
places.hours_raw: 19:00-07:00 daily (Tabelog). Other listings give 20:00-05:00 (next day). Late-night ramen shop; hours can vary, confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Nagarekawa area, central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny late-night ramen counter tucked into Hiroshima's Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife quarter in Naka Ward, Shoryuken caters to the after-drinking crowd, staying open through the small hours when most of the city has closed. It operates as a sister shop of the Hakata r

chef_bio.background: A tiny late-night ramen counter tucked into Hiroshima's Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife quarter in Naka Ward, Shoryuken caters to the after-drinking crowd, staying open through the small hours when most of the city has closed. It operates as a sister shop of the Hakata ramen house Nagahama No. 1 (長浜ナンバーワン), run by the elder brother while the younger runs Nagahama No. 1.
chef_bio.specialty: Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu ramen: a rich pork-bone broth served with thin, firm noodles you can order to your preferred firmness, soft chashu, plenty of green onion, and a half boiled egg, finished with a touch of back-fat. Free-to-add toppings like takana (pickled mustard greens) and red pickled ginger are on the counter, and tsukemen and spicy versions are also offered.

anecdotes.text: Shoryuken is run as a sister shop of the Hakata ramen house Nagahama No. 1, reflected in its Nagahama-style thin-noodle tonkotsu bowls.
It is known locally as a go-to spot for a satisfying late-night bowl after a night out in the Nagarekawa district.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own Instagram (@syour_yuken) is titled '長浜ナンバーワン姉妹店 昇龍軒', confirming it is the sister shop of Hakata's 長浜ナンバーワン (elder brother runs Shoryuken, younger brother runs Nagahama No.1). Reviews confirm the Nagahama-style thin-noodle tonkotsu finished with back-fat and tabletop takana/benishoga/sesame; listings confirm the late-night 20:00-05:00 hours in Yagenbori (~13 seats).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a tonkotsu ramen shop, the broth tare uses standard wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and the noodles are wheat; gluten is unavoidable in the core menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu); there is no animal-free broth, so even noodles-only orders are cooked in and around animal-based products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small late-night counter ramen shop in a nightlife district where ordering is in Japanese and there is no English menu, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help; pointing at the menu works fine for a simple bowl.

noodles.notes: Extremely thin, low-hydration straight Hakata/Nagahama-style noodles; firmness is chosen to order (kata/hard is the recommended default). Because thin noodles soften fast, the first portion is modest and topping up with kaedama (extra noodles) is the standard move.

ramen.signature_bowl: The standard tonkotsu ramen, ordered noodles kata (firm) with a kaedama to follow — eaten the Hakata way by stirring in beni-shoga, karashi-takana and sesame partway through.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, served as a couple of soft, thin slices laid over the noodles.
notable_toppings: table condiments: beni-shoga (pickled red ginger), karashi-takana (spicy mustard greens), sesame

gf.note: Wheat-based thin Hakata noodles plus a pork-bone broth seasoned in the tonkotsu tradition; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.

vegan.note: The entire bowl is built on long-simmered pork-bone broth with pork chashu and animal fat; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A late-night Hakata transplant in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa drinking quarter, this bowl carries the Nagahama tonkotsu lineage of its parent house: a milky, gently sweet pork-bone broth simmered until smooth and clean rather than heavy. You taste that Fukuoka pedigree in the soup and in the gossamer-thin straight noodles built for the kaedama ritual — a true 〆 (after-drinks) bowl.

places.highlight: A late-night Hakata transplant in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa drinking quarter, this bowl carries the Nagahama tonkotsu lineage of its parent house: a milky, gently sweet pork-bone broth simmered until smooth and clean rather than heavy. You taste that Fukuoka pedigree in the soup and in the gossamer-thin straight noodles built for the kaedama ritual — a true 〆 (after-drinks) bowl.

places.name: 庭カフェ 御山 (Niwa Cafe Miyama)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on home-style set meals, sweets/coffee, and a wagyu beef steak set; sauces and seasonings typically contain wheat-based soy sauce, and no dedicated gluten-free preparation is advertised. A celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume safe options.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian establishment. The set lunch includes home-style side dishes (obanzai) and miso soup, and a signature dish is Hiroshima wagyu beef; miso soup and dashi-based dishes commonly contain fish stock. A few vegetable sides may be available, but there is no documented vegan menu, so ask in advance.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict: an earlier listing shows Lunch 11:00-14:00 / Cafe 14:00-17:00 / Dinner 17:00-20:30 with Tuesday closed; a more recent listing shows Mon/Fri/Sun 17:00-20:30, Sat 11:00-20:30, and Tue/Wed/Thu closed. Confirm directly before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Machiya-dori (a quiet lane just off the Omotesando
places.cuisine: Garden cafe in a traditional Miyajima townho
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Niwa Cafe Miyama opened in April 2020 on Machiya-dori, a quiet lane that runs parallel to Miyajima's busy Omotesando shopping arcade, away from the crowds that fill the island's main street. It occupies a traditional townhouse, and visitors reach it down a narrow alley

chef_bio.background: Niwa Cafe Miyama opened in April 2020 on Machiya-dori, a quiet lane that runs parallel to Miyajima's busy Omotesando shopping arcade, away from the crowds that fill the island's main street. It occupies a traditional townhouse, and visitors reach it down a narrow alley and through a garden courtyard before stepping inside.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe is built around its rare garden setting, offering a calm, unhurried place to eat at your own pace rather than the rushed, crowded experience common at tourist spots on the island.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style daily set lunch with seasonal vegetable sides, miso soup and pickles, plus a Hiroshima wagyu beef steak set; coffee and sweets in the afternoon.

anecdotes.text: Having a cafe with its own garden is unusual on Miyajima, and the spot is known as a hidden retreat reached through a quiet courtyard off the main lane, with views of the garden from inside.
It was announced as a newly opened dining spot on Miyajima's machiya street in April 2020.
anecdotes.source: https://hg-nishichugoku.co.jp/グランドオープン　庭カフェ御山/

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hg-nishichugoku announced the grand opening on 13 April 2020 on Miyajima's Machiya-dori; co-trip confirms the rare garden setting reached via a back alley, the family-style daily set lunch with seasonal vegetables, miso soup and pickles, and an A5-grade Hiroshima-wagyu steak set, with coffee and sweets.
sources: https://hg-nishichugoku.co.jp/グランドオープン　庭カフェ御山/

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally run Japanese cafe off the tourist path; some basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help, though the relaxed garden setting is welcoming to visitors.

places.name: 颯爽-別邸- (Sassou Bettei)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat-noodle ramen (汁なし担々麺, 台湾まぜそば, 油そば) and the sauce/tare is soy-sauce based, so wheat is present in both the noodles and the seasoning. No gluten-free menu, noodle, or preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Documented ingredients include pork mince (豚ミンチ) in most dan dan noodles, raw egg (生卵) on the Taiwan mazesoba, a chicken-bone broth, and bonito/tuna katsuobushi (マグロ削り節) used in the broth and tare. Peanuts appear in one oil-noodle dish. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (until sold out). Some sources list 11:00-20:30. Closed Wednesdays (official/Tabelog); some articles list Thursday instead.
places.neighborhood: Mikawa-cho, Naka-ku (Hondori / Hatchobori area, behind Hiroshima PARCO)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is the second shop ('Bettei', meaning annex) of Menya Sassou, which owner Masanobu Miyafuji opened in Saijō, Higashi-Hiroshima in 2016 and built into a local favorite for brothless dan dan noodles. He opened this central Hiroshima location on September 2, 2022, in

chef_bio.origin: Higashi-Hiroshima (Saijō); opened this central Hiroshima branch behind Hiroshima PARCO
chef_bio.background: This is the second shop ('Bettei', meaning annex) of Menya Sassou, which owner Masanobu Miyafuji opened in Saijō, Higashi-Hiroshima in 2016 and built into a local favorite for brothless dan dan noodles. He opened this central Hiroshima location on September 2, 2022, in a clean, stylish, wood-grain café-like space with a counter and open kitchen, deliberately designed to be easy to enter for women dining alone and welcoming to international visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: Miyafuji emphasizes balance over heat — prioritizing aroma rather than spiciness. He makes his own chili oil tuned for fragrance, lets diners adjust heat themselves with a five-spice chili oil, fruit vinegar, and sansho pepper on the table, and recommends level 1 heat for newcomers. The tare is a soy-sauce base with sesame paste (芝麻醬) and vinegar; the pork mince is salt-stir-fried then simmered in pure-rice sake to render out oil and water; the chicken broth is built up with plenty of aromatic vegetables and sweetened with tuna katsuobushi; the noodles come from a maker in Higashi-Hiroshima.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodles (汁なし担々麺), alongside Taiwan mazesoba and abura soba

anecdotes.text: After opening during the COVID period the central Hiroshima shop was initially quiet, but word of mouth grew from about three months in and it now has a loyal regular following.
Miyafuji describes his approach as balance-focused, prioritizing fragrance over heat, with a house-made chili oil and a tare built on soy sauce, sesame paste and vinegar.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms the central Hiroshima branch opened on 2 September 2022; Chugoku Shimbun confirms owner 宮藤正信, the 2016 Saijō original (麺屋颯爽), the aroma-over-heat philosophy, house-made chili oil, the soy/sesame-paste/vinegar tare, the sake-simmered pork mince, the chicken-and-katsuobushi broth and the Higashi-Hiroshima noodles; e-tomato describes the clean, wood-grain café-style space welcoming to solo women and the post-opening word-of-mouth growth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All main dishes are wheat noodles prepared in a shared noodle kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare (sauce) is described as soy-sauce based with sesame paste and vinegar; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes use pork mince, raw egg, chicken broth and tuna/bonito katsuobushi; animal products are central to the menu with no vegan items documented.
The chicken-bone broth is sweetened with tuna katsuobushi and the pork mince is simmered in sake, so fish and meat are integral even to the base components.

positives.text: The official menu lists specific allergen notes per dish (pork mince in most dan dan noodles, raw egg in Taiwan mazesoba, peanuts in the junk oil noodles), which helps diners identify what to avoid.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, stylish counter noodle shop with a PayPay-enabled ticket machine that is comfortable for solo diners and described as welcoming to international visitors, though ordering is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help.

places.name: ゆうき亭 (Yuki-tei)
places.gf_detail: The core dishes are tsukemen and dandan noodles made with wheat flour noodles, plus char siu pork and a chili-based dipping sauce that typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Dishes are built around wheat noodles topped with char siu pork, and the dipping/seasoning sauce is meat- and likely fish/soy-based. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: New Yagenbori location (reopened 2026-03-01) does not publish exact hours on its listing; mobile-order system. Prior operating pattern: Mon-Sat 18:00-03:00 (some listings 17:30-03:30), closed Sundays and holidays (also Obon and New Year).
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Nagarekawa entertainment district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent late-night noodle shop in central Hiroshima's nightlife district, specializing in Hiroshima-style tsukemen (cold dipping noodles) and chili-driven dandan noodles. After years near Kochi-cho/Ginzan-cho, the shop relocated a short distance to a counte

chef_bio.background: A small, independent late-night noodle shop in central Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, specializing in Hiroshima-style tsukemen (cold dipping noodles). After years near Kochi-cho/Ginzan-cho, the shop relocated a short distance to a counter-only (L-shaped counter) space in Yagenbori, reopening on March 1, 2026.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unfussy, noodle-first approach: well-textured noodles and a clean dipping sauce that lets the chili and the noodles speak rather than heavy seasoning.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: firm, springy wheat noodles served cold with cucumber, green onion, boiled cabbage and soft char siu, dipped into a chili-based sauce whose heat can be customized up to 20x.

anecdotes.text: A regular described the dipping sauce as pleasantly simple and not over-seasoned, with heat that starts mild and builds gradually as you eat; staff steady customers by suggesting a starting spice level (around 3x for chili fans).
The noodle prep is something of a spectacle: after boiling, the cook drains and flips the noodles in a large colander dozens of times (reportedly 40-50) to firm up their texture before plating.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists the current Yagenbori shop (胡町からの移転、2026年3月1日オープン、L字カウンターのみ); HotPepper confirms tsukemen with spice adjustable up to 20倍 and ~19 seats; an Ameblo review documents the 3x-recommended heat and the repeated 麺返し draining technique.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are boiled in shared water and the menu is entirely noodle-based, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chili dipping/seasoning sauce for tsukemen and dandan noodles typically relies on wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are topped with char siu pork and the sauces are meat/fish-based, so all menu items involve animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny late-night counter ramen shop with a mobile-order system and a Japanese-only menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps; the spice level is fully customizable, so it is easy to order something mild.

noodles.notes: Cold (chilled-and-tightened) medium-thick straight noodles with a smooth, springy bite and good throat-feel, served chilled separate from the dipping sauce in Hiroshima tsukemen fashion.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (tsukemen) — cold straight noodles with the shop's spicy shoyu dipping sauce, ordered with your chosen heat level (0x up to 20x) plus noodle, vegetable, and meat amounts.
ramen.chashu: Soft braised pork chashu, served sliced alongside the cold noodles rather than in the dip.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu dipping sauce make this unsuitable for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on bonito (katsuo) and kombu seafood dashi and the bowl is dressed with pork chashu; no plant-only broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful carrier of the Hiroshima tsukemen lineage born in 1950s Hatchobori: cold straight noodles plunged into a soy-based dip whose soul is a katsuo-and-kombu seafood broth sharpened with chili and sesame, refreshing and tart underneath the heat. What sets this bowl apart is its dial-it-yourself spice, climbing from a gentle zero all the way to a face-tingling 20x.

places.highlight: A faithful carrier of the Hiroshima tsukemen lineage born in 1950s Hatchobori: cold straight noodles plunged into a soy-based dip whose soul is a katsuo-and-kombu seafood broth sharpened with chili and sesame, refreshing and tart underneath the heat. What sets this bowl apart is its dial-it-yourself spice, climbing from a gentle zero all the way to a face-tingling 20x.

places.name: デリカセロリ (Delica Celery)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is a wheat-noodle soupless dan dan men, and the seasoning is built on a miso-and-soy-sauce base (soy sauce normally contains wheat). There is no indication of gluten-free menu items or preparation, so this is not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The dan dan noodles are topped with a miso-seasoned minced-meat base, and the evening menu is bento/izakaya fare; the broth/seasoning uses soy sauce and animal ingredients. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–13:30, 18:00–22:00; closed Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. (Lunch noodle service runs only the two midday hours.)
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (behind Chuden Hospital; near Kamiyacho / Peace Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Delica Celery started life as a bento and prepared-foods shop in Nakamachi, where the owner, Yamamoto Hiroyuki, set out roughly 50 large trays of dishes every day under a punishing by-the-portion sales grind. The relentless workload eventually wore down his health, prom

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (originally in the Nakamachi district before relocating to Otemachi)
chef_bio.background: Delica Celery started life as a bento and prepared-foods shop in Nakamachi, where the owner, Yamamoto Hiroyuki, set out roughly 50 large trays of dishes every day, sold by the portion/weight under a punishing sales grind. The relentless workload eventually wore down his health, prompting a move to the present Otemachi location behind Chuden Hospital and a complete reinvention of the business about a dozen years ago.
chef_bio.philosophy: After burning out on the old high-volume deli, Yamamoto rebuilt the shop around a focused, sustainable concept: a short, two-hour lunch service (11:30-13:30) centered on one dish done well. His seasoning is deliberately bold so it doubles as a sauce for rice.
chef_bio.specialty: Soupless dan dan noodles (汁なし担々麺, 650 yen) at lunch, with the shop turning into an izakaya in the evening

anecdotes.text: The miso seasoning is made strong on purpose so it pairs with rice - in the owner's words, 'みその強い味付けにしているのでご飯との相性がいいんです.' The base layers slow-simmered root vegetables, ginger, garlic and aromatics into a soy-sauce broth, then piles on miso minced meat, sesame, crunchy Korean chili and a mountain of green onion, leaving plenty of sauce to finish over self-serve rice (50 yen a bowl, refillable).

The rice habit borders on legend: on one midweek lunch the shop blew through 4.5 measures of cooked rice in two hours and had to cook more, thanks to neighborhood office workers ordering refill after refill to soak up the leftover sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Chugoku Shimbun feature confirms owner Yamamoto Hiroyuki's Nakamachi bento-shop past, the health-driven move to Otemachi (behind Chuden Hospital) and reinvention ~12 years ago, the 650-yen soupless dandan, the 'miso pairs with rice' quote, 50-yen refillable rice and the 4.5-measure rice rush. The ~50-trays-a-day detail is corroborated by shop-history writeups found via search.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat noodles seasoned with a miso and soy-sauce base, so gluten is integral and there is no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/seasoning is built on soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) layered with miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature noodles are topped with miso-seasoned minced meat; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, cash-only neighborhood spot run by the owner with ordering via ticket machine and little to no English, so basic Japanese and a relaxed attitude will help a foreign student feel at home.

noodles.notes: Thick, high-hydration (tasui) noodles with real chew, eaten 'washa-washa' style since there is virtually no broth to soften them. Tossed at the table with the meat-miso, sesame and chili at the bottom of the bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: 汁なし担々麺 (shiru-nashi tantanmen) — broth-less Hiroshima-style dandan noodles; order it at 0.5-kara or 1-kara and add a 50-yen self-serve bowl of rice to finish the seasoned dregs.
notable_toppings: a towering mound of chopped green onion (negi)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy-sauce reduction in the tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The dish is built on a pork niku-miso and a meat-forward seasoning; no plant-only version is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima's own broth-less dandan tradition: instead of soup, the soul lives in a low-and-slow soy-sauce reduction simmered with root vegetables and aromatics, then cut with miso, sesame, rayu and Korean chili. By day a humble bento-and-deli counter, it turns out a deliberately dry, savory tangle of meat-miso and mountains of scallion that is engineered to be shoveled over a refillable bowl of rice — Hiroshima soul food more than restaurant ramen.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima's own broth-less dandan tradition: instead of soup, the soul lives in a low-and-slow soy-sauce reduction simmered with root vegetables and aromatics, then cut with miso, sesame, rayu and Korean chili. By day a humble bento-and-deli counter, it turns out a deliberately dry, savory tangle of meat-miso and mountains of scallion that is engineered to be shoveled over a refillable bowl of rice — Hiroshima soul food more than restaurant ramen.

places.name: 津田清風堂 (Tsuda Seifudo)
places.gf_detail: Momiji manju are sponge cakes made from wheat flour batter, so the core product is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are indicated. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Momiji manju batter is a castella-style sponge typically made with eggs and dairy, and flavors include milk chocolate and others with dairy. The seasonal oyster gratin bun (kaki gra-man) contains shellfish and dairy. No vegan offerings are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:30 (irregular days off)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established maple-leaf bun (momiji manju) shop on Miyajima founded in 1908 (Meiji 41) and now run by the third generation of the same family. It sits in the middle of the Omotesando shopping arcade leading toward Itsukushima Shrine, in a nostalgic retro storefron

chef_bio.background: A long-established maple-leaf bun (momiji manju) shop on Miyajima founded in 1908 (Meiji 41) and now run by the third generation of the same family. It sits in the middle of the Omotesando shopping arcade leading toward Itsukushima Shrine, in a nostalgic retro storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing novelty, the shop values doing the ordinary things well and keeping the old-fashioned method consistent across generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional koshi-an (smooth red bean) momiji manju baked in the classic style, with varieties such as red bean, milk chocolate, blueberry, and black sesame; from October through March it also sells a distinctive oyster gratin steamed bun (kaki gra-man, made with Shimada Suisan oysters) and offers shaved ice and soft serve in warmer months.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been baking momiji manju in the classic style since 1908 and is now in its third family generation, and invites visitors to sit in its small rest area to enjoy a freshly baked manju with tea.
A beautiful calico cat acts as the shop's signboard mascot and is a popular photo subject for visitors, often appearing on the shop's Instagram.
Its in-shop pamphlet recounts the playful origin legend of momiji manju tied to a remark attributed to Ito Hirobumi about maple-leaf-shaped hands.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: shinise.tv records the 1908 (明治41) founding, third-generation family operation, koshi-an classic-style manju, the small tea rest area, and the Ito Hirobumi maple-leaf-hands legend from the in-shop pamphlet. oyakodetanoshimou.com confirms the calico-cat mascot, the milk-chocolate/blueberry/black-sesame varieties, the October-March oyster-gratin bun (かきグラまん, Shimada Suisan), and on-site shaved ice/soft serve.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's entire core product line is wheat-flour sponge cake baked on the premises, so the environment is flour-heavy with no gluten-free preparation noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Manju are baked with batter and offered alongside milk chocolate and dairy items, with the seasonal oyster gratin bun containing shellfish and cream sauce, indicating shared handling of animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Momiji manju are hand-made and baked in-shop, and can be eaten fresh in a small seating area.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed walk-up confectionery counter on the main Miyajima arcade; you can point at the maple-leaf cakes and pay easily, though staff likely speak little English so a few words of Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 和奏 ぺったらぽったら本舗 (Wasou Pettara Pottara Honpo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The mochi/rice base is gluten-free in itself, but each piece is glazed and finished with a sweet soy sauce, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation or separate handling is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The only items are grilled mochi-rice topped with oyster (牡蠣) or filled with conger eel (穴子), both finished with a soy-based glaze. There is no plant-based option on the menu.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:30 (roughly 10:00-17:00; varies with weather, etc.)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, waterfront (Itsukushima Shrine area, Kitanohama)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 和奏 ぺったらぽったら本舗 is a small independent stall on Miyajima, set along the seaside walking path that leads toward Itsukushima Shrine. Its signature is 'pettara pottara' — a Miyajima specialty of mochi-rice (a blend of glutinous and regular rice) molded into a small oval onig

chef_bio.background: 和奏 ぺったらぽったら本舗 is a small independent stall on Miyajima (Kitanohama, on the seaside walking path that leads toward Itsukushima Shrine, quieter than the main arcade). Its signature is 'pettara pottara' - a Miyajima specialty of mochi-rice (a blend of glutinous and regular rice) molded into a small oval onigiri-like cake, brushed with a sweet-savory soy glaze and grilled until the outside is crisp and caramelized while the inside stays chewy ('crispy outside, chewy inside'). It comes two ways: topped with a plump local oyster, or with conger eel (anago) tucked inside.
chef_bio.specialty: Grilled mochi-rice 'pettara pottara' with either oyster on top or conger eel inside, finished to order with a torch and a brush of sweet soy sauce

anecdotes.text: The name 'pettara pottara' is the shop's own registered trademark and evokes the rhythmic sounds of pressing and shaping the rice; a little in-shop jingle sings the words, and customers say it lodges in your head long after you leave.

Each piece is finished to order with an extra brush of soy and a quick torching, sending up a fragrant aroma of caramelized soy that visitors say you can smell from along the path; one customer warns the glaze drips easily, so mind your clothes when you tilt the wrapper.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 4travel.jp confirms the registered-trademark name and in-store jingle plus the two versions (oyster on top / anago inside) of the grilled mochi-rice cake with sweet-soy glaze. food.onarimon.jp confirms the to-order torching, the caramelized-soy aroma drifting along the path, and the Kitanohama seaside-path location on the way to Itsukushima Shrine.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Each piece is glazed and finished with a sweet soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so these items are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The only menu items contain oyster or conger eel plus a soy-based glaze, so there is no vegan-safe item to protect from cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny walk-up stall on Miyajima's seaside path; you simply point to the oyster or conger-eel version, pay cash, and eat standing, so very little Japanese is needed but no English menu or service should be expected.

places.name: ジェラテリア バッカーノ (gelateria BACCANO)
places.gf_detail: A dedicated gelato shop. The gelato itself (a frozen dairy custard) does not inherently contain wheat, and many fruit and milk-based flavors are naturally gluten-free, but this is not a gluten-free establishment and no gluten-free protocols are documented. Served in a cup avoids the wheat cone. Flavors such as genmaicha (brown rice) are fine, but verify nothing contains barley/wheat-derived additives, and note possible cross-contact from shared scoops/equipment. Ask staff directly about each flavor.
places.vegan_detail: The core product is dairy gelato made with local Chichiyasu 'Hiroshima delicious milk', so standard flavors are not vegan. Fruit-based sorbet-style options (e.g. strawberry, pineapple, and the signature Hiroshima/Setoda lemon) appear seasonally and are typically water-and-fruit based, which can be dairy-free — but the shop does not explicitly advertise vegan items, and recipes/equipment may share dairy. A vegan visitor should confirm with staff which sorbets are made without milk.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (2nd-floor seating until 16:30); the official site lists 10:00-18:00. Closes at 17:00 in winter. Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, between Omotesando shopping street and Machiya-dori (toward Itsukushima Shrine)
places.cuisine: Additive-free, locally sourced house-made gelato specialist
places.notes: [Miyajima] A mother-and-son shop that opened in August 2014 on Miyajima, tucked between the Omotesando shopping arcade and the quieter Machiya-dori lane on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. Etsuko Doi had long dreamed of running her own business; when her son, who had worked in Itali (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A mother-and-son shop that opened in August 2014 on Miyajima, tucked between the Omotesando shopping arcade and the quieter Machiya-dori lane on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. Etsuko Doi had long dreamed of running her own business; when her son, who had worked in Italian dining, proposed opening a gelateria, they made the dream real together.
chef_bio.philosophy: All gelato is made with no artificial coloring, no artificial flavoring, and no preservatives, so every vivid color in the case comes from the ingredient itself (the pink apple gelato, for instance, gets its hue from red apples cooked with their skins). The shop champions local sourcing, building the base from Chichiyasu's 'Hiroshima delicious milk' and featuring seasonal produce such as strawberries from contracted Miyajima-area farms.
chef_bio.specialty: 無添加の自家製ジェラート。看板はストレート果汁を使った広島・瀬戸田レモン。黒ごま・ピスタチオ・抹茶・ほうじ茶・玄米茶なども人気で、常時約18種類が並ぶ。

anecdotes.text: The name 'BACCANO' means 'revelry' or 'a lively din' in Italian, chosen to express the family's wish that the shop always be bustling with happy customers.
The pink color of the apple gelato is entirely natural, achieved by cooking red apples together with their skins rather than using any coloring.
Around 18 flavors are available daily, with seasonal fruit such as strawberry and pineapple rotating in during the early-summer rainy season.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: japan-stroll.com confirms mother Etsuko Doi and her Italian-dining son opened BACCANO in August 2014 between Omotesando and Machiya-dori, the additive-free (無添加) policy, the naturally pink red-apple-skin gelato, Chichiyasu Hiroshima milk, Setoda-lemon signature and the 'revelry' meaning of BACCANO. hatsumeshi.com confirms ~18 daily flavors with seasonal fruit rotation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol is documented; shared scoops and a common gelato case mean cross-contact between flavors and with any wheat cones is possible. Request a cup and ask staff per flavor.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The gelato base is dairy (Chichiyasu Hiroshima milk); fruit sorbet-style flavors may be dairy-free but are produced in the same kitchen, so cross-contact with milk is possible. Confirm specific flavors with staff.

positives.text: Gelato is made in-house with no artificial coloring, flavoring, or preservatives, and the milk base and many ingredients are locally sourced, which gives clear visibility into what is in each flavor.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly family-run gelato counter where pointing at the colorful case and a few words of Japanese go a long way; the upstairs tatami room is shoes-off and asks each person to order something.

places.name: 広島大衆居酒屋 十升 流川通り店 (Hiroshima Taishu Izakaya Tomasu Nagarekawa-d)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on breaded-and-fried items (gansu is white fish paste breaded and deep-fried; lever katsu is breaded fried liver) and izakaya dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, ponzu, and wasabi-soy. Fried foods share fryers with battered items. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diner.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and meat-forward izakaya. Signature dishes are Hiroshima Bay oysters, conger eel (anago) sashimi and grilled, sashimi platters, senjigara (fried pork offal), lever katsu (liver), and Takamiya brand chicken. Dishes rely on fish, shellfish, meat, and dashi/soy seasonings. No vegan menu or documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays: 11:00-09:00 (next day) (nearly 24 hours); closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka-ku (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori)
places.notes: A casual, around-the-clock neighborhood izakaya in central Hiroshima built around fresh Seto Inland Sea seafood and Hiroshima regional dishes, with locally sourced ingredients including Takamiya brand chicken.

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, in the lively Nagarekawa drinking quarter of Naka-ku
chef_bio.background: A casual, late-into-the-night neighborhood izakaya (十升, read 'Tomasu') in central Hiroshima built around fresh Seto Inland Sea seafood and Hiroshima regional dishes, with locally sourced ingredients including Takamiya brand chicken.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house aims to be a warm, approachable shop 'loved by the local oyaji (regulars),' the kind of place friends say 'let's meet at Tomasu again tonight.' The kitchen emphasizes heartfelt, homemade cooking and seasonal local ingredients at friendly prices.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima Bay oysters (raw, fried, butter-sauteed, sake-steamed, tempura), conger eel/anago prepared as seared sashimi with wasabi-soy or thin-sliced with ponzu, homemade gansu (fish-paste fritter), and homemade senjigara (fried pork offal).

anecdotes.text: The signature anago (conger eel) is offered as seared sashimi dressed with wasabi-soy, or thin-sliced with ponzu, prized for its fatty sweetness and texture.

Gansu, a Kure-area specialty, is made here from white fish paste mixed with vegetables and seasoning, breaded and deep-fried to order for a crisp outside and soft center; senjigara (fried dried pork offal) is also made in-house.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official concept page (thomas-hiroshima.com) shows the shop name 十升 is read トオマス (Tomasu) and describes its approachable neighborhood-izakaya ethos, the anago seared-sashimi (wasabi-soy) / thin-sliced-ponzu preparations, the Kure-style homemade gansu and in-house senjigara, and Seto Inland Sea seafood with Takamiya-brand chicken.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu features multiple breaded deep-fried items (homemade gansu fish-paste fritters, lever katsu breaded liver, fried oysters, anago tempura) that share fryer oil, presenting wheat cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and soy-based dressings (e.g., anago sashimi with wasabi-soy or ponzu), which are wheat-containing; no gluten-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A seafood- and meat-centric izakaya (oysters, conger eel, pork offal, liver, chicken, sashimi) using fish-based dashi and seasonings; no documented vegan dishes or separate preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a lively local izakaya in the Nagarekawa nightlife area where Japanese is the working language and no English menu is documented, so a foreign student should be comfortable ordering in basic Japanese and bring cash.

places.name: 肆一饗 (Hoshiimama Ikkyo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a Japanese izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, ponzu, tempura/fried items and marinades. Sashimi and grilled items could potentially be adapted, but there is no documented GF handling, so a celiac diner would need to communicate carefully and confirm each dish; cross-contamination is likely given the menu.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu is built around fish, oysters, anago, chicken, horse-meat sashimi and grilled beef, and standard Japanese seasonings use bonito dashi and wheat-soy-sauce. No documented vegan dishes; a vegan would find very little here beyond possibly a plain vegetable side, which would still need to be confirmed dashi-free.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat, holidays & days before holidays 17:00-00:00 (next day) (food L.O. 23:00, drinks L.O. 23:00); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: 薬研堀・流川 (Yagenbori / Nagarekawa), Naka-ku — central Hiroshima
places.notes: 肆一饗 (Hoshiimama Ikkyo) is a Japanese izakaya tucked into the Nagarekawa-Yagenbori nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, opened in December 2019. It is designed as an 'adult hideaway' — a calm, traditionally styled space of dug-in horigotatsu private rooms that can sea

chef_bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Naka-ku), Setouchi Inland Sea region
chef_bio.background: 肆一饗 (Hoshiimama Ikkyo) is a Japanese izakaya tucked into the Nagarekawa-Yagenbori nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, opened in December 2019. It is designed as an 'adult hideaway' - a calm, traditionally styled space of dug-in horigotatsu private rooms that can seat anywhere from a couple to a party of around fifty (about 64 seats in total).
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen works directly with producers to source seasonal regional ingredients, pairing local Hiroshima sake and premium nationwide labels with seafood-forward Japanese cooking in an intimate private-room setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Inland Sea seafood and local Hiroshima ingredients: sashimi platters of Setouchi fish, oyster dishes made with Etajima winter-bloom oysters, and local Takamiya-area chicken served as a low-temperature steamed chicken, alongside roughly 40 kinds of sake.

anecdotes.text: The house highlights Etajima winter-bloom oysters and locally raised Takamiya chicken prepared by low-temperature cooking, sourced through direct relationships with producers.
Signature plates include a tuna rare-cutlet, horse-meat sashimi, and grilled beef 'koune', a Hiroshima specialty.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site (ikkyou.owst.jp) and Tabelog confirm the December 2019 opening in the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori quarter, horigotatsu private rooms seating 2 to ~48-50 (about 64 total seats), ~40 kinds of sake, seasonal Setouchi seafood and Hiroshima ingredients, and the signature tuna rare-cutlet, horse-meat sashimi and grilled koune beef.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling is documented; the menu includes fried items (tuna rare-cutlet), and Japanese izakaya cooking relies on wheat-based soy sauce, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese izakaya, standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used across sashimi, simmered and grilled dishes; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on fish, oysters, anago, chicken, horse and beef, with bonito dashi typical of the cuisine; no vegan preparations are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an evening-only adult izakaya in Hiroshima's nightlife quarter with private tatami-style rooms; basic Japanese helps, reservations are smart for the private rooms, and note it is a smoking venue with no entry for under-18s.

places.name: キヨ コラージュ (Kiyo courage)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a course-based French kitchen that bakes its own bread and relies on flour in sauces and pastry, so gluten is present throughout the menu. There is no published information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination control. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner must call ahead (082-221-2077) to ask whether a wheat-free course can be arranged; do not assume any dish is safe.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around pesticide-free organic vegetables delivered twice weekly from a long-standing grower, and listings report it offers vegetarian and partial vegan options. There is no fixed vegan menu: a meat-free or fully plant-based course is made to order, and reservations are required so the kitchen can prepare it. A classic French kitchen uses butter, cream, eggs and stock, so confirm at booking exactly how strict (vegetarian vs. vegan, dairy/egg-free) your needs are.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-15:00 (L.O. 13:30) / Dinner 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 20:00); closed Wednesdays (also closed in summer and over New Year)
places.neighborhood: Minami-senda-nishimachi, Naka-ku (formerly in Noboricho before relocating)
places.notes: Chef Sumida Kiyokazu was born in Hiroshima in 1979 and entered the kitchen at nineteen. He spent roughly a decade training between Japan and France before opening his own restaurant at twenty-eight in 2008. After more than sixteen years in the city's center, he moved th

chef_bio.background: Chef Sumida Kiyokazu was born in Hiroshima in 1979 and entered the kitchen at nineteen. He spent roughly a decade training between Japan and France before opening his own restaurant at twenty-eight in 2008 in Nobori-cho. After more than sixteen years in the city's center, he moved the restaurant to a new home in Minamisenda-Nishimachi, southern Naka-ku, in May 2025 to pursue his ideal more fully.
chef_bio.philosophy: The aim is to draw out the full, honest character of regional ingredients rather than mask them with technique. Vegetables come from a grower he has worked with for over ten years, delivered twice a week, and dishes are kept deliberately simple so that the produce's natural minerality and freshness carry the plate.
chef_bio.specialty: Natural-style French cooking that lets local ingredients speak for themselves, anchored by pesticide-free organic vegetables and the seafood and mountain produce of the Seto Inland Sea region.

anecdotes.text: A signature spinach purée is described by the chef as quite literally just spinach turned into purée, yet it lands as vividly mineral and alive thanks to the quality of the vegetable.

The vegetables are grown without pesticides using organic fertilizer by a farmer in Yamaguchi Prefecture with whom the restaurant has kept a relationship for more than ten years, with deliveries twice a week.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gnavi's chef profile confirms Sumida Kiyokazu was born in Hiroshima in 1979, began cooking at 19, trained ~10 years across Japan and France, and opened Kiyo courage at 28 in 2008. The official site (kiyocourage.jp) confirms the May 2025 relocation to 広島市中区南千田西町 after 16+ years in Nobori-cho. syokuki.com documents the 'just spinach' purée and the 10-plus-year relationship with a pesticide-free Yamaguchi grower delivering twice weekly.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests are advised to inform staff of vegetarian or vegan preferences before ordering and to reserve in advance so the kitchen can accommodate; specific dairy, egg, and cross-contact details are not published and should be confirmed directly.

positives.text: Vegetarian and partial vegan options can be prepared, and an English menu plus English-speaking staff have been reported, making it possible to discuss dietary needs.
The cooking centers on pesticide-free, organically grown vegetables delivered twice weekly from a trusted long-term grower.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu and English-speaking staff have been reported, and the kitchen will prepare a vegetarian/vegan course if you tell them when you book, so a foreign student can dine comfortably as long as they reserve ahead and explain their needs.

places.name: 上海総本店 (Shanhai Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth; the soy tare is wheat-based. No gluten-free options are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a tonkotsu (pork-bone) soy base, dishes feature char siu pork, wontons, and meat, and the soy tare contains wheat. No plant-based options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku (right by Jogakuin-mae tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family ramen shop with roots going back more than 60 years. The lineage began with the founder's grandfather, who started a noodle-making business after returning from Manchuria; that led the founder's father to open the original shop, named simply 上海 (Shanhai), in th

chef_bio.background: A family ramen shop with roots going back more than 60 years. The lineage began with the grandfather, who started a noodle-making business after returning from Manchuria; that led the father to open the original shop, named simply 上海 (Shanhai), in the Nobori-cho area of central Hiroshima. The business later moved to its current Hatchobori location and became 上海総本店. It has been run by the Fujimoto family across generations, with the third generation now running operations and younger family members working in the shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving an unbroken flavor across generations. The soy-based tare has been continuously topped up since the shop opened, keeping the taste alive over the decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-style Hiroshima chuka-soba (ramen): a rich pork-bone and soy-sauce broth (simmered roughly 6 hours) with medium-thick straight noodles, served with generous toppings of bean sprouts, green onion and menma; the wonton ramen and char siu are longtime favorites.

anecdotes.text: Owner Toshiaki Fujimoto says the flavor hasn't changed since his father's time ('味は店をはじめた親父の頃から何も変えとらん'), and the soy tare has been continuously replenished for over 60 years since founding.

Fujimoto ran the shop alongside his twin brother Eiji (英二) as an inseparable pair for about 40 years, sharing shifts and deliveries, until Eiji's recent retirement - and the two still go fishing together.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: zetsumeshi-hiroshima.jp confirms owner Toshiaki Fujimoto (2nd generation, with the 3rd generation now running the shop), the lineage from a Manchuria-returnee grandfather noodle-maker to the father's original 上海 in Nobori-cho to the Hatchobori 上海総本店, the ~6-hour tonkotsu-shoyu broth, the 60-plus-year continuously-topped tare with the 'nothing has changed since my father' quote, and the twin brother Eiji (英二) who worked alongside him ~40 years until his recent retirement.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the core of nearly every dish, so a shared noodle-boiling pot and wheat-heavy kitchen make this unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that has been continuously replenished for decades; Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) and dishes include char siu pork and wontons, so animal products are integral and unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where ordering a bowl in basic Japanese (or by pointing) is enough; no English menu is documented, so a translation app helps but the experience is friendly and approachable.

noodles.notes: Springy straight noodles supplied by a local noodle maker (Harada Seimen); smooth and slippery, drinking up the dark soy broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the house tonkotsu-shoyu bowl; wonton-men (ワンタンメン) is the beloved upgrade.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork — modest slices but deeply savory and chewy; regulars say the chashu-men is the move.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option documented.

vegan.note: Built on a pork-bone broth with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Hiroshima institution since 1953, this is old-school tonkotsu-shoyu chuka soba whose soul is its wild, unmistakable pork-bone aroma — the kind that announces the shop from the sidewalk and that locals recognize on sight. The dark soy broth looks heavier than it tastes: a sharp, nostalgic shoyu edge riding a wave of raw porky funk that sets it apart from milder Hiroshima bowls.

places.highlight: A Hiroshima institution since 1953, this is old-school tonkotsu-shoyu chuka soba whose soul is its wild, unmistakable pork-bone aroma — the kind that announces the shop from the sidewalk and that locals recognize on sight. The dark soy broth looks heavier than it tastes: a sharp, nostalgic shoyu edge riding a wave of raw porky funk that sets it apart from milder Hiroshima bowls.

places.name: 鉄板焼･お好み焼 花子 本店 (Teppanyaki Okonomiyaki Hanako Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered noodles (typically wheat ramen-style noodles), and the okonomiyaki sauce and teppanyaki seasonings generally contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The cooking is centered on pork (the signature 国泰寺焼き uses pork jowl/tontoro), egg, bonito (katsuobushi) powder, and the griddle is shared with meat and seafood. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sundays & holidays
places.neighborhood: Kokutaijimachi, Naka-ku (near Chuden-mae tram stop; Otemachi
places.notes: Hanako is a long-loved Hiroshima okonomiyaki house in the Kokutaijimachi district near the Chuden-mae tram stop, within walking distance of Otemachi and Peace Memorial Park. It grew from its main shop into several branches around Hiroshima and even a Tokyo location, but

roles: family-run independent okonomiyaki and teppanyaki restaurant
chef_bio.background: Hanako is a long-loved Hiroshima okonomiyaki house in the Kokutaijimachi district near the Kokutaiji-mae tram stop, within walking distance of Otemachi and Peace Memorial Park. It grew from its main shop into around six branches across Hiroshima and Tokyo (the Tokyo location has been featured in the Michelin Guide), but the original honten remains the heart of the operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for a savory but not heavy okonomiyaki, leaning on crisped noodles and aromatic ginger and garlic rather than weight, which has earned a loyal local following.
