chef_bio.specialty: Its signature dish is the original 国泰寺焼き (Kokutaiji-yaki): instead of the usual pork belly it uses tontoro (pork jowl), cracks a whole egg into the center, dusts the batter with bonito powder, crisps the thin noodles by drizzling oil around the edges, and finishes with ginger and garlic for a flavor described as rich yet refreshingly light.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature 国泰寺焼き breaks from Hiroshima convention by using pork jowl and a whole egg cracked into the center, with the noodles deliberately crisped at the edges via an oil-drizzling technique and bonito powder sprinkled on the batter.
The crispy thin-noodle style of the 国泰寺焼き, fried with fragrant pork and finished with ginger and garlic, is the dish the restaurant is best known for.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabicoffret.com details the signature 国泰寺焼き (tontoro pork jowl instead of pork belly, whole egg cracked in the center, bonito powder, oil-drizzled crisped thin noodles, ginger and garlic) and the Kokutaijimachi honten near the Kokutaiji-mae tram. The official okonomi-hanako.com confirms the crispy thin-noodle Kokutaiji-yaki plus six branches including Michelin-featured Tokyo locations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-soy-sauce, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cooking surface.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce and teppanyaki seasonings used in Hiroshima-style cooking are generally based on wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is used continuously for pork, egg, and bonito-topped okonomiyaki, so any dish would share a surface with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, locally popular griddle house where pointing at the okonomiyaki menu and a few words of Japanese will get you by, though staff are not specifically set up for detailed English allergy discussions.

places.name: 天心閣 (Tenshinkaku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on coffee, tea, lemon soda, cheesecake and lemon cake; the cakes are conventional wheat-flour bakes, so a celiac would have essentially no safe food option here. Drinks (coffee, tea, Hiroshima lemon soda) are naturally gluten-free, but cross-contact is not addressed. Ask staff directly if you have a strict gluten need.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The food is cheesecake and lemon cake, which normally contain dairy and eggs, so they are not vegan. Black coffee and tea are vegan; plant milk availability is not stated. Best to ask whether any cake or milk alternative is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 13:00-17:00 (official site states open year-round; last order around 17:00). Hours and closed days may change seasonally — one owner interview noted Wednesday/Thursday closures.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), elevated terrace beside the Five-Sto
places.notes: [Miyajima] Tenshinkaku is the hilltop coffee salon of the Itsuki Coffee (伊都岐珈琲) group, founded by Hiroshima native Sasaki Keisuke, a Hiroshima Shudo University graduate who started out working in cafes before building his own specialty-coffee company. He opened Tenshinkaku to brin (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Hiroshima — on a historic hilltop once tied to the Itsuki Shrine and later used as the town mayor's residence.
chef_bio.background: Tenshinkaku is the hilltop coffee salon of the Itsuki Coffee (伊都岐珈琲) group, founded by Hiroshima native Sasaki Keisuke, a Hiroshima Shudo University graduate who started out working in cafes before building his own specialty-coffee company. He opened Tenshinkaku to bring a serious, authentic coffee experience to Miyajima.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sasaki stresses consistency and transparency — '絶対的に同じ品質のものを出し続けるのも大事' (continuing to serve the same quality matters) — sourcing traceable specialty beans. The shop name 天心閣 evokes a view so sublime it cleanses the spirit, and he chose to share this restored hilltop with visitors rather than keep it as a private home.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin specialty coffee, hand-dripped, with beans rotated seasonally (often Guatemala and Ethiopia); also homemade cheesecake and Hiroshima lemon cake, plus a Hiroshima lemon soda.

anecdotes.text: The hilltop site had decayed into what was described as a jungle before Sasaki rebuilt the stone walls and, in a 2022 renovation, extended the cypress-wood deck terrace so guests can view the Five-Story Pagoda and Senjokaku at eye level.

Reaching the cafe means climbing roughly 70 stone steps up a narrow alley off the Omotesando street, a flight locals jokingly call the 'stairway to heaven,' rewarded by a terrace overlooking the pagoda, Senjokaku and the Seto Inland Sea.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: woodone.jp confirms the Miyajima hilltop's history (once tied to Itsuki Shrine, later the town mayor's residence), its 'jungle' state before Sasaki Keisuke (a Shudo University graduate) rebuilt it, the 2022 cypress-deck renovation, the Five-Story Pagoda/Senjokaku eye-level views, and Itsuki Coffee operating ~six locations. japan-stroll.com confirms the ~70-step 'stairway to heaven,' the terrace views, and the house-roasted Guatemala/Ethiopia coffee with cheesecake and Hiroshima lemon cake.

positives.text: Drinks are simple and many are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, such as black drip coffee, tea, and the Hiroshima lemon soda.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed tourist-area cafe where you order simple drinks and cakes, so basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu are enough — just be ready to climb about 70 stone steps to reach it.

places.name: VEGAN CAFE -精進-（シャンティヨガ） (Vegan Cafe Shojin (Shanti Yoga))
places.gf_detail: No source confirms dedicated gluten-free preparation. The menu centers on 精進カレー (shojin curry) and a 精進料理 set; curry roux and any bread/bagel items typically contain wheat, so gluten-free cannot be assumed. A diner needing gluten-free should call ahead (082-247-8529) and ask directly. The "グルテンフリー対応" label in the brief is not corroborated by any fetched page.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. The cafe is run by the Shanti Yoga studio and explicitly excludes meat, fish, eggs, dairy and all animal-derived foods (「肉や魚、卵などの乳製品、動物性食品を使わない」), grounded in the yoga principle of ahimsa (non-violence). Documented dishes include a 精進料理 set with dessert and matcha (1,500 yen) and 精進カレー with side dish and drink (1,000 yen). Kitchen staff historically made items from scratch, including tofu-based mayonnaise.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-17:00; closed Sundays and Mondays. Reservations recommended.
places.neighborhood: Mikawa-cho, Naka-ku (Nagarekawa / Hondori area, within the entertainment district)
places.cuisine: Vegan shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) cuisine & shojin curry
places.notes: The cafe grew out of the Shanti Yoga studio in central Hiroshima's Mikawacho, which wanted to extend its wellness practice from the mat to the table. Studio leaders said they 'wanted to propose food as well' (「食事も提案していきたい」), and the kitchen was originally run by two you

chef_bio.background: The cafe grew out of the Shanti Yoga studio in central Hiroshima's Mikawacho, which wanted to extend its wellness practice from the mat to the table. Studio leaders said they 'wanted to propose food as well' (「食事も提案していきたい」), and the kitchen was originally run by two young pastry-school graduates who made dishes from scratch, including a tofu-based mayonnaise.
chef_bio.philosophy: Eating is treated as an extension of yoga practice and the principle of ahimsa (non-violence): no meat, fish, eggs, dairy or other animal products, offered in a relaxed, casual setting. As the studio put it, it is 'a specialized food style, but we want to propose cuisine with creative freedom' (「特殊なフードスタイルだが、自由な発想の中で料理を提案していきたい」).
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based 精進 (shojin) cooking — a set meal served with dessert and matcha, and a hearty 精進カレー (vegetable curry) with a side dish and drink — built around seasonal organic vegetables, much of it from Hiroshima.

anecdotes.text: The cafe began as an offshoot of the yoga studio so members and visitors could practice mindful, plant-based eating; over time it drew health-conscious diners, foreign visitors and parents of children with food allergies.
A later refresh reopened it as 'Vegan Cafe & Deli,' adding prepared deli dishes and takeout, with a showcase displaying about ten dishes organized by cooking method so guests could eat casually.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hiroshima.keizai.biz/723 records that the cafe grew from Shanti Yoga's wish to 'propose food too' (「食事も提案していきたい」), that two young pastry-school graduates ran the kitchen making everything (including tofu mayonnaise) from scratch, and that it drew health-conscious diners, foreign visitors and families with food allergies. hiroshima.keizai.biz/1539 documents the later 'Vegan Cafe & Deli' renewal with ~10 deli dishes arranged by cooking method, a vegetable-curry set and takeout. No gluten-free/allergen safety is asserted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is plant-based, excluding meat, fish, eggs, dairy and all animal products, so there is no animal-product cross-contact in the cafe.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe historically attracted parents of children with food allergies, suggesting staff are accustomed to allergy-aware requests, though no specific handling protocol is documented.

positives.text: Fully vegan menu of shojin cuisine and curry made from seasonal organic vegetables, prepared from scratch.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, welcoming yoga-studio cafe used to health-conscious and foreign visitors; a few words of Japanese and a phone call to reserve will smooth your visit since menus and signage are primarily Japanese.

places.name: YOGI 広島店 (YOGI Hiroshima)
places.gf_detail: The chain advertises a "gluten-free" menu at some of its other locations, and the strained yogurt itself (milk and cultures only) is naturally gluten-free, as are acai, fresh fruit, and honey toppings. However, the house granola is a signature topping and granola commonly contains oats/wheat. No gluten-free or cross-contamination details are documented for the Hiroshima shop specifically, so a gluten-sensitive guest should ask in person and request bowls without granola.
places.vegan_detail: This is fundamentally a Greek yogurt shop, and the yogurt is dairy (produced at a Hokkaido facility from milk and lactic-acid cultures), so the signature yogurt plates are NOT vegan. The acai is described as fully organic with no additives or artificial sweeteners, and the chain promotes a build-your-own, high-freedom lineup, so a dairy-free acai-and-fruit bowl may be possible if ordered without yogurt and without honey. There is no documented dedicated vegan menu, plant-based milk option, or vegan certification for the Hiroshima location, so vegans must ask staff to confirm each component.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30), open daily (per Tabelog listing)
places.neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku (Hondori shopping street)
places.cuisine: Additive-free Greek yogurt & acai bowl specialist
places.notes: YOGI is billed as Japan's first specialty shop dedicated to additive-free Greek yogurt and acai bowls, and the Hiroshima branch opened on April 1, 2026, in the Hondori covered shopping arcade in central Naka-ku. The Greek yogurt is produced at a dedicated Hokkaido facil

chef_bio.origin: Hokkaido-made yogurt; shop located in Hiroshima's Hondori arcade
chef_bio.background: YOGI is billed as Japan's first specialty shop dedicated to additive-free Greek yogurt and acai bowls, and the Hiroshima branch opened on April 1, 2026, in the Hondori covered shopping arcade in central Naka-ku. The Greek yogurt is produced at a dedicated Hokkaido facility from carefully selected lactic-acid cultures and slowly drained over three days (about 72 hours) with no sugar or additives, giving it a rich, low-acidity, cream-cheese-like texture.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand's stated mission is to deliver beauty and health to everyday life through treats that delight both heart and body, aiming to make eating that is 'healthy yet indulgent' an everyday norm.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick additive-free Greek yogurt plates and organic acai bowls, finished with fresh fruit, house-made granola, and comb honey; flavored yogurts include plain, blueberry, strawberry, Earl Grey, and cookie.

anecdotes.text: On opening, the shop was popular enough that visitors reported waiting around 30 minutes to be served.
The yogurt is strained over roughly 72 hours without sugar or additives, producing a thick, low-acid, cream-cheese-like texture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site describes YOGI as Japan's first Greek-yogurt & acai-bowl specialty shop, with yogurt made at a Hokkaido facility over 72 hours of draining, no sugar/additives, cream-cheese-like low-acid texture. jouhou.nagoya confirms the 72-hour process and lists five flavors (plain, blueberry, strawberry, Earl Grey, cookie). The ameblo review confirms the Hiroshima (Naka-ku Hondori) branch opened April 1, 2026 with a ~30-minute opening-day wait.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free or cross-contamination information is documented for the Hiroshima shop; the signature house granola topping may contain gluten and should be omitted by gluten-sensitive guests.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core product is dairy Greek yogurt, so vegan guests should ask staff to build an acai-and-fruit bowl with no yogurt and no honey; no plant-milk option or dedicated vegan handling is documented.

positives.text: Yogurt and acai are advertised as additive-free, with the acai described as fully organic with no artificial sweeteners.

cultural_comfort.note: A bright, trendy build-your-own yogurt and acai bowl counter where pointing at the display and a few words of Japanese will get you served easily, though there is no documented English menu.

places.name: かき傳 (Kakiden)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. The menu centers on dishes prepared with Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and items such as anago tempura that are wheat-battered. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would find very little that is safe and should not assume any dish is gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya built around oysters and conger eel; nearly every dish involves fish, shellfish, or dashi/soy-based seasonings. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, so vegans should not plan to eat here.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays. Monday lunch is closed except when Monday is a public holiday.
places.neighborhood: Hikarimachi, Higashi Ward (near JR Hiroshima Station / Shink
places.cuisine: Seafood izakaya specializing in Hiroshima oy
places.notes: Opened in 1980, this is a long-running seafood izakaya in the Hikarimachi district a short walk from Hiroshima Station. It serves regional Setouchi cuisine and local sake in a setting that includes private tatami rooms with sunken-kotatsu seating, making it a popular ch

chef_bio.background: Opened in 1980, this is a long-running seafood izakaya in the Hikarimachi district a short walk from Hiroshima Station. It serves regional Setouchi cuisine and local sake in a setting that includes private tatami rooms with sunken-kotatsu seating, making it a popular choice for both travelers stopping near the station and locals gathering for celebrations.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the seasonal seafood of Hiroshima and the Seto Inland Sea paired with regional sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served raw, grilled, or steamed, and anago (conger eel) prepared as white-grilled, sashimi, and tempura; the anago rice bowl is a signature dish offered at lunch.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen makes its own original items such as smoked mackerel and potato buns alongside the classic seafood lineup, and the long-standing anago rice dish is a favorite of both tourists and regulars.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site states かき傳 was established in 1980, sits in Hikarimachi (Higashi-ku), a 6-minute walk from Hiroshima Station, and centers on Hiroshima oysters and anago. The menu page confirms oysters raw/grilled/steamed, anago white-grilled, thin sashimi and tempura, the anago-meshi lunch signature, and house-made original items (smoked mackerel, potato 'jagaman' buns).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese style with soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Japanese seafood izakaya with private tatami rooms; a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English support is not advertised.

places.name: 四季祭 (Shikisai)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on sashimi, simmered and grilled seafood, and miso-based hot pots, which rely on Japanese soy sauce, miso, and fish dashi — all typically containing wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. This is a seafood-focused izakaya whose core offerings are oysters, sashimi, and fish/crab/pufferfish hot pots, all built on seafood and fish-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:00 - until sold out (Tabelog lists 17:00-22:00). Lunch by reservation only on weekdays/holidays (minimum 4 guests, 3 days advance notice). Sundays and public holidays operate irregularly — call ahead.
places.neighborhood: 幟町 (Noboricho), Naka Ward — about a 3-minute walk from the H
places.cuisine: Seafood izakaya specializing in Hiroshima oy
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shikisai is a small, refined seafood izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in Hiroshima's Noboricho district, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop. It works closely with local suppliers, sourcing fish and oysters directly from the Seto Inland Sea so tha

chef_bio.background: Shikisai is a small, refined seafood izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in Hiroshima's Noboricho district, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop. It works closely with local suppliers, sourcing fish and oysters directly from the Seto Inland Sea so that each plate arrives at the peak of freshness.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen prizes quality, freshness, and direct sourcing, preparing each dish with care so that every plate earns genuine appreciation.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood: sashimi, grilled and steamed preparations, and a rotating line of seasonal hot pots — oyster-miso nabe, snow/matsuba crab, pufferfish (torafugu), and monkfish — paired with Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: The house serves carefully prepared, refined Japanese cooking in the relaxed setting of an izakaya, in an intimate counter-and-table room seating only around 17 guests.
Seasonal hot pots rotate through the year — from oyster-miso nabe to crab, pufferfish, and monkfish — reflecting the shop's name, which evokes a 'celebration of the four seasons.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists 17 seats (10 table, 7 counter) on the 2nd floor in Noboricho near Hatchobori, with a focus on local Hiroshima ingredients and Japanese sake. The official siki-sai.com site confirms Seto Inland Sea seafood, direct-sourcing emphasis (品質・鮮度・産地直送), and the seasonal nabe lineup (matsuba/zuwai crab, torafugu-chiri, miso oyster nabe, monkfish). 'Kappo-quality' was not stated in sources and has been softened.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Core dishes (sashimi, simmered seafood, miso-based hot pots) rely on Japanese soy sauce and miso, which typically contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes are seafood- and fish-dashi-based (oysters, sashimi, fish/crab hot pots); no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, traditional owner-run Japanese seafood izakaya with no documented English support, so a foreign student would be most comfortable with some basic Japanese and an open mind toward an intimate local-dining setting.

places.name: かきと水晶焼き 空 (Kaki to Suishouyaki Ku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. As a Hiroshima izakaya the menu relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, ponzu, and grill sauces, plus breaded/fried and flambeed dishes, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and meat-centric izakaya built around Hiroshima oysters, conger eel, and crystal-grilled local beef and pork. No vegan dishes are advertised, and standard seasonings use fish and wheat-soy-sauce, so it is not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-02:00 (food L.O. around 01:30, drink later). Open daily including weekends and holidays.
places.neighborhood: 中区薬研堀 / Yagenbori, Naka Ward
places.notes: An izakaya in Hiroshima's Yagenbori nightlife quarter built around two pillars: 100% Hiroshima-grown oysters and a theatrical cooking style called suishou-yaki, or crystal grilling. The owners source oysters directly from local producers rather than through wholesale ma

chef_bio.background: An izakaya in Hiroshima's Yagenbori nightlife quarter built around two pillars: 100% Hiroshima-grown oysters and a theatrical cooking style called suishou-yaki, or crystal grilling. The owners source oysters directly from local producers rather than through wholesale markets, aiming for daily premium freshness, with raw oysters offered into spring and grilled oyster preparations served year-round.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on local Hiroshima ingredients sourced as freshly and directly as possible, presented through showy tableside cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Suishou-yaki crystal grilling, where meat and vegetables are cooked on a crystal-stone plate said to use far-infrared heat to keep the meat juicy, finished tableside with a dramatic flambe to seal in the umami. Signature ingredients include Sera Minori beef and local pork, alongside Hiroshima oysters and conger eel.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen sources 100% Hiroshima-grown oysters delivered daily, buying direct from producers rather than through markets to maximize freshness.
The crystal-grilling course is finished with a bold tableside flambe described as sealing in the meat's umami.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official gorp site states the shop provides '広島産牡蠣100％' sourced directly from producers (not via markets), with raw oysters through around March and grilled oysters year-round, and describes the signature suishou-yaki (crystal grilling) with tableside flambe, Sera Minori beef, local pork and conger eel. The shop notes bronze awards in the 'Oishii! Hiroshima' program (2023-2024).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Hiroshima izakaya the menu relies on standard Japanese seasonings, which are wheat-based soy sauce and ponzu; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking surfaces and grills are shared across oysters, conger eel, beef, and pork, so plant-only items cannot be guaranteed free of animal contact.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language menu and other foreign-language menus are available, and the venue takes online reservations, so a foreign student should be comfortable here; note it is a smoking-permitted izakaya open late into the night.

places.name: 広島かき酒場 MOMIJI (Hiroshima Kaki Sakaba MOMIJI)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free provision is documented. The menu centers on oysters and local Hiroshima izakaya fare (gansu and senjigara are wheat-flour-battered fried items), and Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is standard for seasoning and dipping. Travelers requiring strict gluten-free should not assume any dish is safe here.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster and seafood izakaya; the signature offerings are oysters, octopus sashimi, and fried fish-paste snacks (gansu, senjigara), with sake and beer to pair. Dishes rely on seafood and wheat-containing soy sauce, so there is no documented vegan or vegetarian option.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-13:30 (lunch), 17:00-22:30 (dinner); Sat 17:00-22:00; Sun closed
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (~2 min walk from Kamiyacho-nishi tram stop; very close to Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: An oyster-specialist izakaya that opened in central Hiroshima within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. The proprietor's idea is simple and generous: bring the island's best oysters into the heart of the city so visitors can enjoy fresh, h

chef_bio.background: An oyster-specialist izakaya that opened in central Hiroshima within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. The proprietor's idea is simple and generous: bring the island's best oysters into the heart of the city so visitors can enjoy fresh, high-grade shellfish without having to travel out to the producing islands.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing premium local oysters and Hiroshima flavors in an easygoing, welcoming setting close to the city's most visited sites.
chef_bio.specialty: Salt-field-aged oysters from Farm Suzuki, alongside Hiroshima local dishes such as gansu (fried fish-paste cake), senjigara, octopus sashimi, and a lineup of Hiroshima sake and ice-cold draft beer.

anecdotes.text: The owner is proud that you can eat fresh, top-quality Farm Suzuki oysters here without having to make the trip out to the island.
Small touches set the tone — boiled peanuts served as an opening nibble before the oysters, beer, and sake arrive.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Parkside Hotel gourmet blog places MOMIJI in central Hiroshima (Naka-ku, Otemachi 1-4-27) a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, praising salt-field-aged Farm Suzuki oysters that can be enjoyed downtown without traveling to the islands, plus Hiroshima specialties (gansu, senjigara, octopus sashimi), boiled peanuts as an opening nibble, and Hiroshima sake and draft beer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya manner with soy sauce, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around oysters and seafood, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable for vegan diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, unpretentious local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at dishes will get you a warm welcome, though an English menu is not guaranteed.

places.name: ひろしま丸かじり 中ちゃん (Hiroshima Marukajiri Naka-chan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, whose batter and the noodles layered inside (soba/udon) are wheat-based. Teppanyaki and izakaya dishes are seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce, and signature plates like uni-cresson use soy sauce and butter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on seafood and meat: seasonal oysters, sea urchin (uni-cresson), fish dishes, and okonomiyaki containing egg, pork and bonito/seafood. Dishes use butter and wheat soy sauce. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 17:00 until sold out (closed Sundays)
places.neighborhood: Yayoi-cho, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (about a 5-minute walk
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki institution founded in 1970 by Mamoru Nakasako. The shop's familiar name, 'Naka-chan,' comes from the founder's own affectionate nickname. Decades on, it remains a small, bustling counter-and-table spot beloved by locals and visito

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki institution founded in 1970 by Mamoru Nakasako. The shop's familiar name, 'Naka-chan,' comes from the founder's own affectionate nickname. Decades on, it remains a small, bustling counter-and-table spot beloved by locals and visitors alike, filling up the moment its doors open.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep serving the same beloved, generously prepared Hiroshima comfort food that has drawn regulars for half a century, cooking until the day's ingredients run out.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is 'uni-cresson' (sea urchin and watercress on the teppan with butter, soy sauce and lemon) — a dish the original owner invented after drawing inspiration from Western cooking, and one credited here as its birthplace. Alongside it are classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, a wide spread of teppanyaki and izakaya plates (dozens of menu items in all), and prized local oyster dishes in winter.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is credited as the birthplace of 'uni-cresson,' a dish the first-generation owner Mamoru Nakasako created by taking inspiration from Western cuisine; it has been a favorite from the day it was first served.
The shop name 'Naka-chan' is taken from the affectionate nickname of the first-generation owner, Mamoru Nakasako.
Founded in 1970, the counter packs with a steady stream of customers from the moment it opens each evening.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Rurubu &more article 15505 states the shop was founded in 1970 (Showa 45) in Naka-ku Yayoi-cho, that 'Naka-chan' comes from the nickname of the previous/first owner 中迫守 (Nakasako Mamoru), and that he invented Hiroshima's 'uni-cresson' after drawing inspiration from Western food. otonano-shumatsu confirms uni-cresson originated here (同店発祥) and that the counter fills from opening. The originally cited otonano article did not itself carry the founder's name or founding year, so rurubu was added as the supporting source.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A teppan (flat-grill) okonomiyaki and teppanyaki kitchen where wheat batter, wheat noodles and wheat-containing soy sauce are cooked on shared surfaces, making cross-contamination essentially unavoidable for gluten-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature and everyday dishes (e.g. uni-cresson and teppanyaki items) are seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is standardly wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on seafood and meat (oysters, sea urchin, fish, pork, egg) cooked on the same teppan with butter, so animal-product contact is pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a lively, locals-favorite teppanyaki/okonomiyaki spot where a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way; come early since they close once dishes sell out.

places.name: オイスター&スモークドバー サンゴ (Oyster & Smoked BAR SANGO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes that typically use wheat-based seasonings and breading: fried oysters (karaage), smoked items, pasta, and izakaya plates seasoned with soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or cross-contamination controls are mentioned on any page reviewed.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster and smoked-seafood bar; the entire concept is built around raw, grilled, and fried oysters plus house-smoked seafood, bacon, and cheese. Dishes are seasoned with fish- and wheat-soy-based sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and it is not a suitable destination for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sun, holidays & days before holidays 18:00-00:00 (food/drink L.O. 23:30); closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Ekinishi (west-of-Hiroshima-Station area), Osugacho
places.notes: A casual oyster-and-smoke bar in Hiroshima's buzzing Ekinishi district, a short walk west of the station. It pairs fresh oysters with a wine-bar sensibility and a lineup of house-smoked dishes.

chef_bio.background: A casual oyster-and-smoke bar in Hiroshima's buzzing Ekinishi district, a short walk west of the station. It pairs fresh oysters with a wine-bar sensibility and a lineup of house-smoked dishes.
chef_bio.philosophy: Oysters are bought directly from producers, with the varieties rotating by season and region; the kitchen highlights notably large, plump, sweet-and-savory oysters.
chef_bio.specialty: Direct-sourced seasonal oysters served raw, grilled, and fried, plus a signature raw oyster crowned with uni and ikura, oyster ajillo, and a wide range of house-smoked items such as oysters, bacon, chicken wings, eggs, cheese, salmon, and takuan pickles.

anecdotes.text: The first-floor open-kitchen counter lets guests watch oysters being shucked and smoked dishes prepared, while a quieter second floor offers table seating for groups.

A house signature is raw oysters topped with sea urchin and salmon roe, and the kitchen runs an in-house smoker turning out everything from smoked oysters and bacon to smoked cheese and pickled radish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hotpepper places SANGO 2 minutes from Hiroshima Station in the Ekinishi area, with oysters sourced directly and rotated seasonally, served raw/grilled/fried; signatures include a raw oyster with uni and ikura and oyster ajillo. It confirms an in-house smoker (smoked oysters, bacon, eggs, cheese) and the layout: first-floor open-kitchen counter, second-floor table seating; smoked items paired with wine and whiskey.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Izakaya-style oyster and smoked dishes here are typically seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen smokes and fries seafood, bacon, and cheese on shared equipment, so any vegetable item would share surfaces and oil with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a lively local izakaya-style oyster bar where the menu and conversation are mainly in Japanese, so a phrase or two of Japanese (or a translation app) will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: どんつき左 (Dontsuki Hidari)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. As a shellfish izakaya, dishes rely on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and typical izakaya seasonings; no dedicated gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The restaurant is a dedicated shellfish specialist and its own site states plainly that people who dislike shellfish will find nothing to eat here; the menu centers on oysters, shellfish and seafood.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:30–23:00 (Food L.O. 22:00, Drink L.O. 22:30). Closed Sundays; if a Monday is a public holiday the shop opens that Monday and closes the following day instead.
places.neighborhood: Tachimachi, Naka-ku (about 1 min from Tachimachi tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is an authorized offshoot (noren-wake) of the Hiroshima establishment 笹組 (Sasagumi), opened around 2014 in the Tachimachi quarter of central Hiroshima.

chef_bio.background: The shop is an authorized offshoot (noren-wake) of the Hiroshima establishment 笹組 (Sasagumi), opened in 2014 in the Tachimachi quarter of central Hiroshima.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on quality fats such as extra-virgin olive oil and plant-based safflower oil and avoids chemical seasonings and preservatives, and the house is unapologetically focused: it states outright that this is a shellfish specialty house and that those who do not enjoy shellfish will find little to eat.
chef_bio.specialty: A specialist in shellfish cuisine, best known for oysters served several ways, a comparison plate of three kinds of raw oysters, shell-roasted oysters, vinegar-dressed oysters, and assorted shellfish sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The owner's site warns guests directly that this is a shellfish specialty house and that people who dislike shellfish will find nothing to eat, signalling a single-minded devotion to the ingredient.
It carries on the lineage of 笹組 as an authorized noren-wake spinoff.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site names owner 石神英樹 (Hideki Ishigami), shows the '笹組から暖簾分け承認' (Sasagumi noren-wake) mark, places the shop in Tachimachi (3-23), and states it uses only EXV olive oil and plant-based safflower oil (trans fats <=0.3%) with no chemical seasonings or preservatives, warning that shellfish-averse guests will have nothing to eat. Tabelog confirms an opening date of October 24, 2014 and the shellfish/raw-oyster and grilled-shellfish focus.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese shellfish izakaya, dishes are expected to use wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and standard seasonings; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around shellfish and seafood; the house states that those who dislike shellfish will find nothing to eat, so no plant-only meal is offered.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner's site asks guests to review allergy and disliked-ingredient information before booking, indicating allergies can be discussed at reservation by phone or LINE.

positives.text: Cooking fats are limited to extra-virgin olive oil and plant-based safflower oil with low trans-fat content, and the kitchen avoids chemical seasonings and preservatives.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, locally-run shellfish specialist where reservations are taken by phone or LINE and the menu is built almost entirely around shellfish, so a foreign student should be ready to communicate dietary needs in Japanese and expect a dinner-only, course-style experience.

places.name: 中華そば 陽気 江波本店 (Chuka Soba Yoki (Eba Honten))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The sole offering is wheat ramen noodles (specially made by Harada noodle company) in a soy-sauce-and-pork-bone broth. Soy sauce is the defining seasoning of the soup and is virtually certain to contain wheat. There is no indication of GF accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The single signature dish is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and soy-sauce broth topped with char siu (roast pork), green onion, and bean sprouts. The broth itself is animal-based, so even a topping-free bowl would not be vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-13:30, 18:00-23:00. Closed days reported as the 1st, 12th, 13th and 26th of each month (official site); a listing also reports Tuesday/Wednesday closed — confirm locally before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Eba, Naka Ward (central Hiroshima, ~3.5km SW of the A-Bomb D
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1958 in the Eba neighborhood of central Hiroshima, this shop began as a humble stall and grew into one of the city's defining ramen institutions. The founder learned his craft in the lineage that traces back to the earliest Hiroshima ramen, and the original E

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1958 in the Eba neighborhood of central Hiroshima, this shop began as a humble stall and grew into one of the city's defining ramen institutions. Founder Hideaki Tsuruta learned his craft within the lineage that traces back to the earliest Hiroshima ramen, and the original Eba shop has served the same nostalgic bowl for generations.
chef_bio.philosophy: A nostalgic, everyday taste meant to be loved by everyone — simplicity and consistency over novelty, with house-spec noodles made to order by a local noodle maker.
chef_bio.specialty: A single, focused offering: chuka soba with thin straight noodles in a soy-sauce-and-pork-bone broth, topped with char siu, green onion and bean sprouts. Soy sauce is treated as the soul of the soup.

anecdotes.text: Founder Hideaki Tsuruta learned ramen-making within the lineage regarded as the origin of Hiroshima ramen (from the shop 'Shimai'), and the shop serves essentially one dish — chuka soba — to this day.
The Eba honten is the original location of what has since grown into several shops across Hiroshima and Fukuyama, and it still sells its broth and char siu for home cooking.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: youki.world confirms founding in 1958 (Showa 33) in Naka-ku Eba, the soy-forward tonkotsu chuka soba (char siu, scallion, bean sprouts) as essentially the sole dish, and frozen-ramen home sales plus Hiroshima/Fukuyama branches. ramenmode's Youki page (and Tenri-jiho) supply the founder's name, 津留田秀明 (Hideaki Tsuruta), and the lineage — he learned from Oki of 'Shimai,' regarded as the original Hiroshima ramen shop. The founder's name was not on the official site, so ramenmode was added as the supporting source.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares wheat ramen noodles as its only product, so any gluten-free request would face heavy cross-contact in a shared noodle-cooking environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is described as the key ingredient of the soup, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork-bone and soy-sauce base and the dish is topped with char siu pork, so animal products are central to every bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills local ramen counter where ordering is simple but staff and menu are Japanese-only, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and cash) will make the visit smooth.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium (chu-hosomen) straight noodles with good wheat fragrance, sourced from Harada Seimen; cooked to a normal/standard firmness and made to cling to the soy-pork broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba - the shop's one and only bowl: amber soy-pork broth with thin straight noodles, sliced chashu, crisp bean sprouts and scallion (a sprinkle of black pepper to taste).
ramen.chashu: Thin-cut braised pork slices, meaty and firm in texture with a slightly bold, well-seasoned flavor rather than fatty melt.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare and pork-bone broth make this bowl unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered pork-bone broth with soy tare and pork chashu; no plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A living monument of Hiroshima chuka soba, simmering its amber pork-bone-and-soy broth essentially unchanged since the late 1950s, when it grew from a postwar street stall into the city's defining ramen lineage. You taste that heritage in the soup: a gently sweet, vegetable-rounded shoyu-tonkotsu - lighter and more elegant than Hakata's chalky tonkotsu - draped over thin straight noodles in the minimalist Hiroshima style where the broth is unmistakably the star.

places.highlight: A living monument of Hiroshima chuka soba, simmering its amber pork-bone-and-soy broth essentially unchanged since the late 1950s, when it grew from a postwar street stall into the city's defining ramen lineage. You taste that heritage in the soup: a gently sweet, vegetable-rounded shoyu-tonkotsu - lighter and more elegant than Hakata's chalky tonkotsu - draped over thin straight noodles in the minimalist Hiroshima style where the broth is unmistakably the star.

places.name: 清盛茶屋 (Kiyomori Chaya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house anago donburi uses a soy-sauce-based "secret sauce" (Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the menu centers on udon and soba (wheat/buckwheat noodles) plus dishes like beef curry rice. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or GF accommodation is documented. Travelers requiring strict gluten avoidance should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes are anago (conger eel) and oysters, and the prized udon/soba broth is made abundantly with Seto Inland Sea dried sardines (niboshi), so even the noodle dishes contain fish. No plant-based menu or vegan accommodation is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (Tabelog); 10:30–17:00 (official site). Evening service possible by advance phone reservation.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), seaside pine grove beyond Its
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A seaside teahouse on Miyajima that has been run by the same family across three generations since 1955 (Showa 30). It sits in a quiet pine grove along the shore past Itsukushima Shrine, near the small Kiyomori Shrine and on the way toward the aquarium — a calm retreat  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A seaside teahouse on Miyajima that has been run by the same family across three generations since 1955 (Showa 30). It sits in a quiet pine grove along the shore past Itsukushima Shrine, near the small Kiyomori Shrine and on the way toward the aquarium — a calm retreat away from the crowded main shopping street.
chef_bio.philosophy: Their stated spirit is to let visitors pause and rest while listening to the sound of the tide — 潮の音を聴きながら、のどかな場所で一休み — offering a peaceful seaside breather from the bustle.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) donburi finished with a long-guarded house sauce, alongside udon and soba in a broth made generously from Seto Inland Sea dried sardines. The signature Kiyomori Set lets you enjoy a half-portion anago bowl together with your choice of noodles. Grilled small sardines (iwashi shioyaki) are a house exclusive.

anecdotes.text: The teahouse has welcomed visitors since 1955 and is still run by the founding family into a third generation, tucked into the seaside pine grove beyond Itsukushima Shrine near Kiyomori Shrine.
In winter a wood-burning stove warms the room, and the spot is welcoming to families with children, with high chairs, terrace seating, and ocean views; pets are also welcome.
Its prized noodle broth is made abundantly with dried sardines (niboshi) from the Seto Inland Sea, and the anago donburi is finished with a secret house sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official kiyomori-chaya.shop site states the teahouse has run since 1955 (Showa 30) across three generations, in a seaside pine grove past Itsukushima Shrine, with anago donburi in a secret sauce, niboshi (Seto Inland Sea dried-sardine) noodle broth, the half-anago-plus-noodles Kiyomori Set, and house-exclusive grilled iwashi. japan-stroll adds the location near Kiyomori Shrine toward Miyajima Aquarium, winter wood stove, terrace with ocean views, pet- and child-friendly (high chairs), and the 'sound of the tide' resting theme.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Udon and soba are prepared on-site and the kitchen handles wheat-based dishes throughout, so cross-contact with gluten is likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago donburi is finished with a soy-sauce-based house sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature udon/soba broth is made with Seto Inland Sea dried sardines (niboshi), so noodle dishes are not vegetarian or vegan; the menu centers on conger eel and oysters.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed family-run seaside teahouse with terrace seating where you can point at the photo menu and order simple set meals; no English service is documented, so a translation app and a little patience will help.

places.name: tuGiico（ツギイコ） (tuGiico)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. Signature creative dishes such as oyster gratin and oyster tomato sauce typically use wheat flour and dairy, and oyster dishes generally involve wheat-based seasonings, so this is not a gluten-free destination. Confirm directly with staff if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: An oyster-focused bar — every documented dish centers on Hiroshima oysters (shellfish), so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Beverages such as wine, beer, and Hiroshima lemon soda are available, but there are no documented vegan food options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00; closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Miyajima island (between Machiya-dori and Omotesando shoppin
places.cuisine: Oyster bar / Italian-style creative oyster d
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] tuGiico opened on October 1, 2021, in a renovated traditional wooden townhouse tucked along an alley between Miyajima's machiya street and the Omotesando shopping arcade, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine. It is a small, independent oyster bar run by a chef couple wh

chef_bio.background: tuGiico opened on October 1, 2021, in a renovated traditional wooden townhouse tucked along an alley between Miyajima's machiya street and the Omotesando shopping arcade, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine. It is a small, independent oyster bar serving Hiroshima oysters paired with wine, beer, and Hiroshima lemon soda.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name reflects its guiding idea: because Miyajima has so many wonderful spots and eateries, guests should enjoy a quick bite here and then move on to the next place. To make this possible, oysters are sold one at a time — an unusual practice in Miyajima — so even solo travelers can drop in casually.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served by the single piece — from simple grilled oysters that let the ingredient shine to Italian-influenced creative plates like oyster gratin and oyster tomato (basil) sauce.

anecdotes.text: Selling oysters by the single piece is described as a rare practice in Miyajima, deliberately designed so visitors can sample a little and keep exploring the island.
Set in a stylishly renovated old townhouse on a quiet alley between the machiya street and the main Omotesando arcade, the spot is small with just a six-seat counter and one table for four.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Rurubu &more 15202 confirms tuGiico opened Oct 1, 2021 in a renovated townhouse between Miyajima's machiya street and the Omotesando arcade, with a single-piece oyster concept tied to the owner's wish that guests explore the island's many shops; menu includes grilled oysters and creative gratin/tomato-sauce oysters with beer, wine and Hiroshima lemon soda. modokimaru confirms a six-seat counter plus one four-seat table and per-piece grilled oysters (350 yen), gratin and basil-sauce oysters. The 'chef couple' detail was not stated in sources and was removed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around oysters (shellfish); creative dishes such as gratin also use dairy, so there is no separation from animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, casual walk-in oyster counter where you can order a single oyster, point at the menu, and enjoy a quick bite — friendly for a foreign student, though a few basic Japanese words and cash on hand will smooth things along.

places.name: ぐりる彦星 (Grill Hikoboshi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature Japanese-style omelette rice, hamburger steak, curry, and Western sauces (roux/demi-glace style) are wheat-based, and no dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Although vegetables are the centerpiece of the cooking, dishes deliberately combine seasonal vegetables with seafood, meat, and dairy to highlight flavors (egg-based omelette rice, hamburger steak, etc.). No vegan menu is documented; a plant-based diner would need to ask the owner directly.
places.hours_raw: Tue, Thu–Sat: 12:00-14:00 lunch, 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30) dinner; Wed: dinner only 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30); Sun/Holidays: dinner only 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi (Hondori shopping arcade, central Naka-k
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Inspired to cook by a popular television cooking program in his student years, Fukuma trained in the banquet kitchen of the Hiroshima Grand Hotel, where he learned the fundamentals of cooking thoroughly. He went on to work at a well-known Western-cuisine restaurant, ros

chef_bio.background: Inspired to cook by a popular television cooking program in his student years, Fukuma trained in the banquet kitchen of the Hiroshima Grand Hotel, where he learned the fundamentals of cooking thoroughly. He went on to work at a well-known Western-cuisine restaurant, rose to become its branch manager, and eventually took the business over to open his own place, Grill Hikoboshi.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fukuma approaches cooking and service with sincerity every day, aiming to welcome guests comfortably and to tailor dishes thoughtfully to each customer's preferences rather than imposing a fixed menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward creative Western cooking; the house favorite is a gentle Japanese-style omelette rice, alongside hamburger steak and seasonal vegetable dishes that pair produce with seafood, meat, and dairy to bring out each ingredient's flavor.

anecdotes.text: Fukuma runs the intimate 16-seat room largely on his own, creating a warm, homey atmosphere where guests can dine quietly and at their own pace.
The Japanese-style omelette rice is the dish guests most associate with the place and is especially popular.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara's chef profile names owner-chef 福間武彦 (Takehiko Fukuma), born 1964, inspired by a TV cooking program as a student, trained in the Hiroshima Grand Hotel banquet kitchen, then worked at a well-known Western-cuisine restaurant where he became branch manager before taking it over to open Grill Hikoboshi. It confirms the 16-seat room, vegetable-forward Western cooking with Japanese-style omelette rice and hamburger steak, and a sincere, guest-tailored approach.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner-chef states he tailors dishes to customer preferences rather than imposing a set menu, so accommodation requests can be discussed directly; however, no specific allergy or gluten/vegan handling procedures are documented.

positives.text: Vegetables are the centerpiece of the cooking, with many seasonal vegetable dishes available.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run counter-and-table spot where service is in Japanese; a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese or a translation app will be welcomed warmly, but no English menu is documented.

places.name: イエスタディー (Yesterday)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core menu is bread-based — ham-and-egg sandwiches, hot dogs on wheat buns, and morning-set toast — so it is not a suitable choice for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan items are documented. The food menu centers on hot dogs, ham-and-egg sandwiches and similar items containing meat, egg and dairy; only plain drinks (coffee, tea, juice) would be incidentally plant-based, and no plant-milk option is noted.
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, self-service retro coffee house tucked into the arcade between Hatchobori and Tatemachi in central Hiroshima, marked by a Beatles sign next to the Kamakura Pasta shop. The cafe spreads over upper floors with a counter and table seating in a vintage, vagu

chef_bio.background: A long-running, self-service retro coffee house in central Hiroshima's Hondori/Kinsai arcade near Hattchobori, marked by a Beatles sign next to the Kamakura Pasta shop. The cafe spreads over upper floors with counter seating on one level and table seating above, in a vintage, vaguely British atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately simple, affordable menu meant to evoke an old-school neighborhood kissaten where regulars can linger from early morning until late at night.
chef_bio.specialty: Inexpensive cafe fare: hot dogs (including the 'country dog'), morning sets with ham-and-egg sandwiches, and simple, well-made iced coffee.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is named after the Beatles song, and the interior is packed with the band's music, photographs and record jackets, with a Beatles sign marking the entrance.
Despite high local ratings it is treated as a hidden gem; drinks run around 250 yen and food items roughly 200-300 yen, and it opens from 8:00 in the morning until midnight daily.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists the Hondori/Kinsai arcade location, three floors (2F counter 11 seats, 3F tables 36), Beatles decor, morning sets and hot dogs, 8:00-24:00. The inagena review confirms the Beatles theme, ~250 yen drinks, 200-300 yen food, and the Kamakura Pasta landmark.

cultural_comfort.note: A laid-back self-service retro café where you order at the counter and find a seat; little to no English, but pointing at the simple coffee-and-hot-dog menu works fine and prices are very gentle.

places.name: 深山 (Shinzan)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The cooking relies on wheat-containing seasonings such as ponzu and miso-based shabu-shabu broths, and the meal-closing courses include rice/risotto-style and noodle finishes; no gluten-free accommodations are advertised. A celiac traveler should not rely on this restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built almost entirely around wild boar and other game meats (boar shabu-shabu, botan miso hot pot, grilled boar and pheasant, boar sashimi). The only plant-leaning items are tofu and seasonal mountain-vegetable dishes, but they are part of a meat-centric kitchen using fish/meat-based broths and soy seasonings.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-14:00 and 17:00-22:00; public holidays 17:00-22:00; closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima shopping arcade, ~0.8 km
places.cuisine: Japanese wild game and hot pot
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tucked-away specialist in Hiroshima's Hondori arcade devoted to wild boar and mountain game, the kind of quietly serious place locals seek out rather than stumble into. It is described as having moved from an earlier location while keeping its hidden-gem character.

roles: wild game and hot pot specialist
chef_bio.background: A tucked-away specialist in Hiroshima's Hondori arcade devoted to wild boar and mountain game, the kind of quietly serious place locals seek out rather than stumble into. It is described as having moved from an earlier Hondori location while keeping its hidden-gem character.
chef_bio.philosophy: Letting seasonal, naturally raised game speak for itself, with the boar praised for tender meat and clean, sweet fat rather than gaminess.
chef_bio.specialty: Wild boar (inoshishi): boar shabu-shabu and botan miso hot pot, grilled boar and pheasant, and boar sashimi, rounded out with seasonal mountain vegetables and a rice-porridge (zosui) or ramen finish.

anecdotes.text: Diners single it out as a Hiroshima destination for wild boar, praising meat that is tender with fat 'so sweet it tasted ten times better than expected' and little of the odor people fear from game.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms a Hondori-arcade game specialist (HM Hondori Bldg 5F) built around wild boar shabu-shabu and hot pot with mochi and a ramen/zosui finish; reviews praise sweet fat and tender, low-gaminess meat, and note it relocated from an earlier location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shabu-shabu and hot pot are served with ponzu and miso-based sauces/broths, which are typically wheat-containing soy seasonings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around boar and game meat with meat/fish-based hot pot broths, so even tofu and vegetable dishes share a thoroughly non-vegan cooking environment.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional game-meat specialist with mostly Japanese-language menus and no advertised English support, so a translation app and some basic Japanese will help you order and explain any needs.

places.name: ガリバー (Gariber)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or claim. The curry is a slow-simmered beef roux blended with ~20 spices, which in this style typically includes wheat flour, and no GF accommodation or dedicated preparation is documented. Travelers requiring strict gluten-free should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The base curry is beef, simmered for three days until the beef dissolves into the roux, and signature dishes feature bacon, shrimp, octopus, squid, and cheese. Even the house lassi uses yogurt. No vegan or vegetarian curry option is documented.
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi, Naka-ku (near Hondori and Hatchobori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gariber is a long-established curry shop in Hiroshima's Hondori area, founded in 1984 (Showa 59). It is now run by the second-generation owner, who preserves the recipe inherited from the founder, in a small shop with just 10 counter seats. It has long been cherished as a landmark shop wafting the aroma of curry through Hiroshima's central shopping arcade.

chef_bio.origin: 広島市中区本通（本通アーケード周辺）
chef_bio.background: ガリバーは1984年（昭和59年）に創業した広島・本通エリアの老舗カレー店で、現在は二代目が初代から受け継いだレシピを守りながら営む、カウンター10席だけの小さな店。広島の中心商店街でカレーの香りを漂わせる名物店として長く親しまれている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 初代の味を忠実に守り、国産素材と時間を惜しまない仕込みで『変わらない味』を提供すること。
chef_bio.specialty: ビーフをベースに約20種のスパイスを調合し、一から出汁を取って弱火でじっくり牛肉が溶けるまで煮込み、寝かせて完成まで最低3日かける手間ひまかけたカレー。肉も野菜もすべて国産にこだわる。看板メニューにはエビ・タコ・イカ・ベーコン・きのこ・ナスが入る『ガリバースペシャルカレー』や、ベーコン・ナス・きのこがたっぷりの『山の幸スペシャル』など。

anecdotes.text: 辛さは0番（マイルド）から20番まで選べ、16番以上を食べ切った人には『辛党認定ステッカー』が贈られる。0〜3番は無料、4番以降は追加料金。
牛肉が溶けるまで弱火で煮込むため、ルーの完成には最低3日かかると紹介されている。
ヨーグルトを使った自家製ラッシーがスパイスの辛さを和らげる定番のお供。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト（gariber.org）とHOME広島の記事が、1984年創業・二代目・約20種スパイス・出汁から3日がかりの牛肉カレー・全食材国産・カウンター10席を裏付ける。食べログのメニューで『ガリバースペシャル』（エビ・タコ・イカ・ベーコン・きのこ・ナス）と『山の幸スペシャル』（ベーコン・ナス・きのこ）を確認。

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared kitchen builds all curries on the same beef-based roux simmered for three days, so any vegetable order would carry meat-based stock; no separate vegan preparation is described.

positives.text: All meat and vegetables are sourced domestically (国産) and roughly 20 spices are blended in-house, reflecting transparency about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter curry counter run by locals; ordering is simple (pick a curry and a spice level 0–20) but expect Japanese-only service, cash only, and possibly a queue, so a little Japanese or pointing helps.

places.name: 宝 (Takara)
places.gf_detail: This is a tempura shop, and tempura batter is made with wheat flour, so the signature fried dishes are not gluten-free. The dipping tsuyu (broth) is also typically soy-sauce based (contains wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on seafood and eel: anago (conger eel) tempura don, oyster tempura, shrimp tempura, and sushi, with a dashi/fish-based dipping broth and wheat-soy sauces. There is no documented vegan offering.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 / Dinner 17:30-22:30 (sources also list 10:30-15:00 / 17:00-21:00); open daily with irregular closures
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Kamiyacho (Hondori shopping arcade, near the A-Bom
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, long-running tempura counter tucked into Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from the A-Bomb Dome. With just a handful of counter and tatami seats, it is the kind of unassuming neighborhood spot regulars return to for an honest, generous fried lun

chef_bio.background: A small, long-running tempura counter tucked into Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from the A-Bomb Dome. With just a handful of counter and tatami seats, it is the kind of unassuming neighborhood spot regulars return to for an honest, generous fried lunch.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tempura is fried to order, which keeps service unhurried but means each plate arrives fresh and crisp.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) tempura over rice and seafood tempura bowls, served with the dipping broth on the side so each piece can be eaten to taste; oyster and shrimp tempura and sushi also feature.

anecdotes.text: The anago tempura don is famous for portions so large the eel spills over the rim of the bowl, served with rice, pickles, grated daikon and miso soup, and a separately served broth praised for its rich dashi.
The shop's big-portion lunch was featured on the local TV program Mantenma (満点ママ).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The syokuki review confirms an anago tempura donburi with portions overflowing the bowl, served with pickles, grated daikon, miso soup and a separate dashi broth, plus shrimp/oyster tempura and sushi sets, featured on the TV program 満点ママ, at Hondori 5-13.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a tempura specialist, the kitchen fries wheat-battered items throughout; a shared fryer and wheat-based tsuyu make cross-contamination very likely for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes and the dipping broth use a dashi/soy-sauce base, which standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on seafood, eel and fish-based dashi, so vegan diners face pervasive animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual local tempura counter in the Hondori arcade where ordering is in Japanese and seating is tight, so a few basic Japanese words and patience help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: 平和園 (Heiwaen)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional town-Chinese kitchen built around fried rice, ramen, dumplings and stir-fries; soy sauce, wheat noodles and wheat-flour dumpling wrappers are central, and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat, egg and seafood Chinese dishes (fried rice, dumplings, ramen) and uses standard soy sauce; some vegetable stir-fries exist but are typically cooked with meat-based broth, lard or oyster/soy seasonings, so a vegan would need to ask directly and options are likely very limited.
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi, Naka-ku (Hondori / Hatchobori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, family-style Chinese counter shop tucked into central Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, said to have been serving the neighborhood for more than 40 years. With just a row of counter seats and a single-house storefront, it keeps the nostalgic feel of a

chef_bio.background: A long-running, family-style Chinese counter shop tucked into central Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade near Tatemachi tram stop. With just a row of counter seats in a single-house storefront, it keeps the nostalgic feel of an old-school Showa-era 'town Chinese' diner.
chef_bio.specialty: Wok-fried rice (yakimeshi / chahan) is the signature, alongside everyday town-Chinese plates such as mapo-tofu rice bowls and gyoza, and a very affordable set lunch.

anecdotes.text: Regulars come for big, cheap, satisfying plates, with lunches landing under 1,000 yen and set lunches around 650-800 yen.
The shop keeps a nostalgic Showa-era town-Chinese atmosphere with counter-only seating (about 11 seats) in the heart of the Hondori arcade.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists a Hondori-arcade town-Chinese with 11 counter-only seats, yakimeshi/chahan as the specialty, mapo-tofu rice bowls and gyoza, and lunches under 1,000 yen (~650-800). Hitosara and Gnavi confirm the Hondori 4-1 location but give no founding date, so 'more than 40 years' could not be verified.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a small single-counter wok kitchen serving noodles, dumplings and fried rice, shared woks, soy-based seasonings and wheat ingredients make cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is used throughout the cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes are prepared in the same wok and seasonings as meat, egg and seafood dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, cash-only counter shop run in Japanese with no reservations or English menu, so a few basic Japanese phrases and patience will make the visit smoother for a foreign student.

noodles.notes: Slightly yellow, firm-textured noodles that carry little flavor on their own, letting the light soy broth lead.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka soba) — the old-fashioned clear soy ramen; locals also pair it as the 半チャンラーメン set with half-portion fried rice.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised pork, sliced soft with a gentle savory-sweet character; modest in size, in the classic town-Chinese style.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based soy tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented, so the bowl is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The clear broth is built on pork and chicken with a soy tare, and the bowl is dressed with braised pork chashu; no plant-based version is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A vanishing kind of bowl: the honest old-school chuka-soba of a long-running neighborhood Chinese kitchen, where a clear, gently oily soy soup carries pork-and-chicken savor without a hint of modern heaviness. It tastes of the nostalgic, light Setouchi soy lineage — kin to Onomichi-style clear ramen — quietly comforting rather than showy.

places.highlight: A vanishing kind of bowl: the honest old-school chuka-soba of a long-running neighborhood Chinese kitchen, where a clear, gently oily soy soup carries pork-and-chicken savor without a hint of modern heaviness. It tastes of the nostalgic, light Setouchi soy lineage — kin to Onomichi-style clear ramen — quietly comforting rather than showy.

places.name: ひまわり (Himawari)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat noodles (tsukemen and ramen), and the dipping broth/seasoning relies on standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The tsukemen broth is built on bonito (katsuo) fish dashi, dishes are topped with chashu pork and boiled egg, and the evening service is a beef yakiniku course. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-14:00, 17:00-21:00; Sun and holidays closed. Evening service (yakiniku course) is by reservation only.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner left a corporate career to move into the restaurant business and opened this tsukemen and ramen shop in May 2001, just as Hiroshima's local-style tsukemen was taking off, making it one of the early standard-bearers of the city's dipping-noodle culture.

chef_bio.background: A tsukemen and ramen shop opened in May 2001, just as Hiroshima's local-style tsukemen was taking off, making it one of the early standard-bearers of the city's dipping-noodle culture. It sits a few minutes north of the Hatchobori area in Naka-ku.
chef_bio.philosophy: A wear-two-hats neighborhood spot run with care, holding to never-frozen, carefully sourced beef for its evening course and keeping a warm, Showa-retro room full of regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chilled wheat noodles on a large plate with a colorful spread of toppings (boiled egg, chashu, cucumber, seaweed, lemon, radish sprouts and more) and a bonito-dashi dipping sauce. By night the same space becomes a reservation-only yakiniku course of high-grade (A4) never-frozen beef.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in May 2001, riding the wave of the Hiroshima tsukemen boom that had begun the year before.
The walls are lined with autographed photos of pro baseball players; the standout is Shohei Ohtani, believed to date from his Nippon-Ham Fighters days.
A loyalty card rewards ten visits with a free bowl of your favorite noodles.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The h-megourmet review states the shop opened May 2001 during the tsukemen boom that began in 2000, serves thin Hiroshima-style tsukemen with a bonito-dashi dip, turns into a reservation-only A4-wagyu (never-frozen) yakiniku course at night, displays a Shohei Ohtani (Fighters-era) autograph among pro-baseball photos, and runs a 10-visit loyalty card. No source mentions the owner leaving a corporate job.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat noodles for both tsukemen and ramen, so flour and wheat-based broth are present throughout; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce and seasonings use standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made with bonito (fish) dashi and dishes include chashu pork and egg, so animal products are integral to the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only neighborhood noodle shop with a retro Showa-era feel and many regulars; little to no English is likely, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, and lunchtime tsukemen is the easy, walk-in-friendly option.

noodles.notes: Very thin, smooth, slippery straight strands served chilled for the tsukemen, with standard firmness; they grip and carry the spicy seafood-soy dipping sauce well. The hot ramen uses similarly thin, firm noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (広島風つけ麺): chilled thin noodles with a spicy soy-and-seafood dipping sauce, dressed with a colorful spread of vegetables, served with a small rice ball at lunch. Chili heat is adjustable across several levels from mild to fiercely hot.
ramen.chashu: Thick, generous slices of pork from a shop that doubles as a premium butcher and yakiniku house; the meat is left largely unseasoned to showcase its quality, with a substantial, meaty bite (some cuts carry pleasant cartilage texture).

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy-sauce-based, seafood dipping sauce make this unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on bonito/seafood dashi and soy, and the bowl is dressed with pork chashu and egg; no plant-only broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl belongs to the Hiroshima tsukemen lineage: chilled, very thin noodles plunged into a soy-based dipping sauce sharpened with bonito-seafood umami and a tongue-tingling chili kick, where the diner sets the heat. What sets this kitchen apart is the meat — run by butchers who turn into a premium yakiniku house by night, so the thick, near-unseasoned pork tastes of the cut itself rather than of marinade.

places.highlight: This bowl belongs to the Hiroshima tsukemen lineage: chilled, very thin noodles plunged into a soy-based dipping sauce sharpened with bonito-seafood umami and a tongue-tingling chili kick, where the diner sets the heat. What sets this kitchen apart is the meat — run by butchers who turn into a premium yakiniku house by night, so the thick, near-unseasoned pork tastes of the cut itself rather than of marinade.

places.name: 生粉 手打そば 結喜庵 (Kiko Teuchi Soba Yukian)
places.gf_detail: The house specialty is kiko juwari soba — 100% buckwheat noodles made with no wheat binder (tsunagi), so the noodles themselves are naturally wheat-free. However, this is a traditional soba shop with no documented gluten-free program: the dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, and tempura is wheat-battered. There is also no information confirming a dedicated buckwheat-only kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. A celiac or highly sensitive diner would need to communicate carefully and could realistically only have the plain noodles without the wheat-based tsuyu, tempura, or soy sauce.
places.vegan_detail: Soba broth (tsuyu) is traditionally made with bonito/fish dashi, so standard cold and hot soba here should be assumed non-vegan. Some menu items such as the vegetable tempura (maitake mushroom, lotus root, paprika) are plant-based as ingredients, but they are fried in shared oil and the noodles are normally served with fish-based tsuyu. No vegan menu or vegan dashi is documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) daily; dinner 17:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00) Monday-Saturday. Sunday is lunch only. Sources vary — one listing shows lunch-only hours — so hours can change; confirm before a dinner visit.
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori / Kanayamacho tram stops
places.notes: This is a small, owner-run handmade soba shop tucked into the Noboricho district of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the downtown tram stops and about 1.5 km from the Peace Memorial Park. The family's connection to traditional food runs deep — the owner's roots trac

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run handmade soba shop tucked into the Noboricho district of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the downtown tram stops. The family's connection to traditional food runs deep — the owner's roots trace back to a Meiji-era dengaku (skewered tofu) teahouse run by his father. The owner came to soba through his brother, who was devoted to the craft, and the two worked side by side refining their own noodle.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop uses only carefully selected buckwheat and adds no binding flour, letting the grain itself carry the aroma, texture, and the satisfying slip down the throat that defines great soba.
chef_bio.specialty: Juwari soba — 100% buckwheat noodles cut with no wheat binder. The house is known for a signature 'shogen' (笙幻) soba: an unusually pale, almost translucent, glossy noodle prized for its smooth texture and clean finish, offered alongside other styles such as sarashina and inaka (rustic).

anecdotes.text: A popular pairing is the shogen soba served with a vegetable tempura of maitake mushroom, lotus root, and paprika; the bowl looks modest but eats with surprising substance, the noodles delicately aromatic with a clean, smooth finish.
The signature pale, translucent 'shogen' noodle was developed together with the owner's brother, building on the family's older roots in a traditional dengaku teahouse.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The gorp.jp official page confirms an owner-run 100% buckwheat (no wheat binder) soba shop in Noboricho whose roots trace to a Meiji-era tofu/dengaku teahouse run by the father, with a signature glossy translucent '笙幻 (shogen)' noodle developed with the owner's brother. The modokimaru review confirms the shogen soba with maitake/lotus-root/paprika tempura.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional soba shop with no documented gluten-free protocol; tempura is wheat-battered and the kitchen handles wheat, so cross-contact with the buckwheat noodles cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with traditional tsuyu based on Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard soba broth uses fish-based dashi; tempura is fried in shared oil. No vegan dashi or separate preparation is documented.

positives.text: The noodles are genuine kiko juwari (100% buckwheat) made with no wheat binder, so the soba itself contains no wheat in the noodle.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, traditional soba shop where ordering is simple by pointing at the soba you want, but it is cash-only with little English, so bring yen and a few Japanese phrases.

places.name: 蕎場 しらかわ (Soba Shirakawa)
places.gf_detail: Standard soba contains wheat flour, and the dipping/broth dashi is made with wheat-based soy sauce, so the menu is not gluten-free. A daily limited "十割" (100% buckwheat) soba is offered, but the broth/tsuyu still uses regular soy sauce containing wheat and there is no documented gluten-free or cross-contamination protocol, so this is not a safe choice for those needing strict gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The broth (tsuyu/dashi) is built on bonito and mackerel flakes plus kombu, so it is not vegan; menu highlights also include duck, kurobuta pork, and tempura. No vegan-specific dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 11:00-14:45 / 17:30-21:30; Wed 11:00-14:45; closed Sundays (lunch cash only; dinner accepts card and QR payment)
places.neighborhood: Fukuromachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (Hondori / Tatemachi area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small wa-modern soba shop tucked into the Fukuromachi area of central Hiroshima, near the Hondori shopping arcade. The shop is run by manager Shirakawa, known as approachable and warm, which has made the counter a comfortable spot even for solo diners.
