chef_bio.background: A small wa-modern soba shop tucked into the Fukuromachi area of central Hiroshima, near the Hondori shopping arcade. It is run by a manager known simply as Shirakawa-san, whose approachable, warm manner has made the counter a comfortable spot even for solo diners.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the quality of the buckwheat flour and a meticulously built broth speak for themselves, served in a calm, jazz-tinged modern-Japanese room.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade soba using flour from a Hokkaido farm (Uehara Farm) that earned an Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries Minister's award, paired with a dashi carefully drawn from selected bonito and mackerel flakes and premium kombu, finished with a specially sourced soy sauce meant to let the buckwheat's flavor stand out.

anecdotes.text: The room is a sophisticated wa-modern space with jazz playing, drawing a diverse crowd and welcoming solo female diners who enjoy chatting with the manager.
A rare 100% buckwheat soba is offered daily but limited to roughly 30 portions, alongside seasonal dishes like avocado soba and plum-radish soba.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The e-chic feature describes a Fukuromachi wa-modern soba shop with jazz, a warm female manager (referred to as Shirakawa-san), Hokkaido Uehara-Farm buckwheat that won a MAFF Minister's award, a bonito/mackerel/kombu dashi and select soy sauce, plus ~30 daily portions of 100% buckwheat and seasonal avocado and plum-radish soba. No source publishes a full personal name for the manager.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard soba is made with wheat flour and shares the same kitchen and broth as all dishes; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a specially sourced soy sauce, which in standard soba shops contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is made from bonito and mackerel flakes plus kombu, so broths and dipping sauces are fish-based and not vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, busy local soba shop where ordering is easiest with a little Japanese or by pointing at the menu; the friendly owner makes solo diners feel welcome.

places.name: 蕎麦きり 吟 (Sobakiri Gin)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. While the soba is made from stone-ground buckwheat, soba noodles typically include wheat flour as a binder and the dipping broth (tsuyu) is based on wheat-containing soy sauce. The tempura (including the signature anago/conger eel) uses a wheat-flour batter. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core soba broth is built on katsuo/kombu dashi (fish), the standout dishes feature anago (conger eel) and duck (kamo nanban), and sides such as dashimaki tamago (egg) and agedashi tofu use dashi. Soy sauce-based tsuyu is used throughout. No vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Sources differ: Tabelog lists 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 15:00); a detailed feature article lists 11:30-14:30 and 17:30-20:30, closing earlier when the day's soba runs out. Reservations available on weekdays; dinner courses require reservation.
places.neighborhood: Danbara, Minami Ward (east of Hiroshima Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run soba specialist tucked into the Danbara neighborhood just east of Hiroshima Station. It has built a quiet reputation among soba lovers as an 'adults' hideaway' (大人の隠れ家的お店) rather than a flashy destination.

roles: certified sake sommelier (唎き酒師) who curates the sake list
chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run soba specialist tucked into the Danbara neighborhood east of Hiroshima Station, honored with a Bib Gourmand in the 2018 Michelin Guide Hiroshima. It has built a quiet reputation among soba lovers as an 'adults' hideaway' (大人の隠れ家的お店) rather than a flashy destination.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to handmade soba crafted from flour the owner stone-grinds himself, beginning with carefully sourced whole buckwheat. Milling everything in-house is described as uncommon among Hiroshima soba shops.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade soba made from buckwheat the owner stone-grinds in-house. He starts from whole, unhulled buckwheat kernels (玄蕎麦), removes the shells and impurities, then mills the flour himself with a stone mill before cutting each batch by hand. Signature dishes are cold seiro soba and a prized anago (conger eel) tempura; duck nanban and side dishes like dashimaki tamago and agedashi tofu round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a certified sake sommelier (kikizakeshi) and pairs the soba with a curated selection of Japanese sake; the dining room has a refined Japanese feel with jazz playing in the background.
Grinding one's own buckwheat from whole kernels, with all shells and impurities removed before stone milling, is noted as rare among soba shops in Hiroshima.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Both reviews confirm a Danbara (Minami-ku) owner-run soba shop that stone-grinds whole buckwheat kernels in-house (rare in Hiroshima) and hand-cuts the noodles; the owner is a certified kikizakeshi with a curated sake list; signatures include seiro soba (¥950) and anago tempura, with duck nanban and dashimaki. Hiroshimagourmet-quest notes a 2018 Michelin Bib Gourmand.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles here are stone-ground buckwheat but, as is standard, are not certified gluten-free and the broth uses wheat-based soy sauce; tempura is fried in wheat batter in the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba dipping broth (tsuyu) is based on dashi and Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-based dashi (katsuo/kombu); menu centers on anago (eel), duck, and egg dishes, so plant-based diners cannot avoid animal ingredients in the standard offerings.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional owner-run soba shop with no indication of English support and cash-only payment, so basic Japanese phrases and exact-change cash will make a visit much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 板蕎麦 香り家 京橋店 (Itasoba Kaoriya Kyobashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a buckwheat soba house, but Japanese soba is normally blended with wheat flour and the dipping sauces (including the signature sesame dare and the soy-based tsuyu) are made with wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is documented. Treat as not gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: The dipping sauces are dashi/soy-based (typically fish-derived) and the set meals include egg omelet and tempura; no vegan menu is documented. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. dishes 21:15, drinks 21:30). Irregular days off; some sources list no fixed closing day except the New Year period.
places.neighborhood: Minami-ku, Hiroshima (Kyobashi / around Hiroshima Station)
places.notes: A soba house on the bank of the Kyobashi River, set in a striking black box-shaped building within the Kyobashi River open-cafe stretch where pastry shops and cafes also gather. Floor-to-ceiling glass frames the riverside scenery, and the shop has become a lunchtime lan

chef_bio.origin: Specializes in Yamagata-style country soba (inaka-soba) served on the rectangular wooden board that gives 'itasoba' its name; the presentation takes its cue from the wooden boxes traditionally used to store soba, using the board itself as the serving vessel.
chef_bio.background: A soba house on the bank of the Kyobashi River, set in a striking black box-shaped building within the Kyobashi River open-cafe stretch where cafes and shops also gather. Floor-to-ceiling glass frames the riverside scenery, and the shop has become a lunchtime landmark known for the queue that forms outside.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasizes hospitality and care in serving whole-grain country soba to guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Stone-milled whole-grain country soba offered in your choice of thin-cut (for a smooth throat-feel) or thick-cut (for chew and fuller buckwheat flavor), notably paired with a sesame dipping sauce (gomadare) uncommon among soba shops. The signature lunch set comes with two dipping sauces, a seasonal small dish, tamagoyaki, tempura, refillable rice, and soba-cha to finish.

anecdotes.text: Reporters describe a black box-shaped building beside the Kyobashi River with full-height windows overlooking the water, and a sesame dipping sauce that is rare for a soba shop.
Local coverage notes the soba is served on the board itself, in the spirit of hospitality, using stone-milled whole-grain country soba.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The mrs.living reporter confirms a black box-shaped riverside building with floor-to-ceiling glass, weekday queues, thin/thick-cut Yamagata-style soba on wooden boards, a rare gomadare dip, and a ¥1,680 lunch set (two dips, small dish, tamagoyaki, tempura, refillable seasoned rice, soba-cha). Syokuki confirms board service and stone-milled whole-grain country soba.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauces are soy-sauce and dashi based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular riverside soba spot that often has a queue and a Japanese-language menu, so a little basic Japanese (or a translation app) and patience in line will help a foreign student feel comfortable.

places.name: たち花 (Tachibana)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature dishes are breaded fried oysters and fried shrimp (wheat batter/breadcrumbs), curry, and noodles, and Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused diner; its core menu is built around oysters, fried shrimp, and other seafood. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sat, Sun 10:30-16:30; Closed Friday
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima Island), near Omotesando shopping stre
places.notes: [Miyajima] A casual island diner on Miyajima serving Hiroshima's celebrated oysters in homely set-meal form — most notably fried-oyster set meals — alongside fried shrimp, curry, and noodle dishes, just a short walk from the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A casual island diner on Miyajima serving Hiroshima's celebrated oysters in homely set-meal form — most notably fried-oyster set meals — alongside shrimp curry and noodle dishes, a short walk from the approach to Itsukushima Shrine, with roughly 70 seats across table and tatami.
chef_bio.specialty: Fried oyster set meal (kaki-fry teishoku) and other oyster and seafood dishes

anecdotes.text: A relaxed, English-menu island eatery about eight minutes on foot from the ferry terminals, popular for fried-oyster set meals and shrimp curry.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists a casual Miyajima teishoku/seafood diner near the Itsukushima Shrine approach (~8 min from the ferry), with fried-oyster set meals, shrimp curry and noodle dishes, 70 seats (table and tatami), and English menus.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual island diner where you can point at a set-meal photo and pay by card or cash; no English service is documented, so a phrasebook or translation app helps.

places.name: お食事処 大たに (Oshokujidokoro Otani)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature fried oysters use wheat batter/breadcrumbs, anago and donburi are served with wheat-based soy sauce and tare, and the menu leans heavily on udon, soba, curry and katsu — all wheat-containing. No gluten-free preparations or dedicated equipment are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around oysters, conger eel and other seafood, plus egg-and-meat donburi (katsu-don, oyako-don) and dashi/soy-based broths. No vegan dishes are listed and dashi and tare contain fish.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-17:00; irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine and Miyajima
places.notes: [Miyajima] A long-standing casual eatery on Miyajima island, set a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine in front of Kiyomori Shrine and just about a minute from the Miyajima Aquarium. It serves the island's classic local fare in a traditional, clean dining room with both tatami and  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A long-standing casual eatery on Miyajima island, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine in front of Kiyomori Shrine and about a minute from the Miyajima Aquarium. It serves the island's classic local fare in a traditional, clean dining room with both tatami and table seating.
chef_bio.specialty: Miyajima specialties — fried oysters, oyster rice bowls and oyster udon drawn from waters that have long been one of Hiroshima's great oyster-farming grounds, alongside conger eel (anago) served grilled or as an anago set meal with clam miso soup; tonkatsu, oyakodon, curry and noodle dishes round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on its location: the seas around Miyajima have long thrived as a Hiroshima oyster-farming region, reflected in the oyster-forward menu (fried oyster, oyster rice bowl, oyster udon).
The dining room keeps both tatami (zashiki) and table seating and welcomes group bookings.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own site confirms a Miyajima eatery near Itsukushima Shrine (~4 min toward the Aquarium) whose signature is an anago set meal with clam miso soup plus grilled anago, oyster dishes (fried oyster, oyster rice bowl, oyster udon) tied to Miyajima's oyster-farming waters, and other fare (tonkatsu, oyakodon, curry, noodles), with tatami and table seating and group bookings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago and rice-bowl dishes are served with Japanese soy sauce and tare, which are standard wheat-containing seasonings; no gluten-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on oysters, conger eel and other seafood plus meat and egg donburi, with fish-based dashi broths; there are no documented vegan items or separate vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-run Miyajima eatery with plastic food samples displayed out front so you can point to what you want; a little basic Japanese helps but the visual menu makes ordering easy for a foreign student.

places.name: てらにし珈琲本店 (Teranishi Coffee Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese coffee house whose food centers on house-baked thick-cut bread, sandwiches and toast — all wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and the morning set is built around bread, so it is not suitable for someone requiring gluten-free meals. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free if you simply order a drink.
places.vegan_detail: Black self-roasted nel-drip coffee and plain tea are vegan, and an oat-milk iced coffee is offered as a plant-milk option. Food, however, is built around bread with butter/jam, salad and sandwiches, and the signature cream coffees use dairy cream, so there is no documented fully vegan meal. A vegan could drink the black coffee or oat-milk coffee but should ask about any food item rather than assume.
places.hours_raw: 8:00〜18:30 (morning service 8:00-11:00); closed Sundays and holidays
places.neighborhood: Takaramachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (near Hijiyama-bashi; about
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Teranishi Coffee was opened in 1978 by Takayuki Teranishi after he spent about two years training at the renowned Nishimura Coffee in Kobe, where he learned to brew and absorbed a deep commitment to craft. When a director of the Kobe shop encouraged him to strike out on

chef_bio.background: Teranishi Coffee was opened in 1978 by Takayuki Teranishi after he spent about two years training at the renowned Nishimura Coffee in Kobe, where he learned to brew and absorbed a deep commitment to craft. When a director of the Kobe shop encouraged him to strike out on his own, he returned to Hiroshima and founded his own coffee house, which has now served the city for more than 45 years.
chef_bio.philosophy: Teranishi distinguishes between merely 'getting tasks done' (こなす) and truly 'hosting' a guest (もてなす), guided by the question of how many people one can make happy in a lifetime. He keeps no written service manual, preferring to pass hospitality on person to person; staff learn regulars' preferred seats, newspapers and drinks, and by a third visit a guest is treated as a regular.
chef_bio.specialty: Beans roasted in-house every day on a Probat roaster and brewed by hand through an original nel (cloth) filter, served alongside house-baked thick-cut morning bread; the simple morning set pairs that bread with salad, and the kitchen also pours dairy-cream coffees such as its iced au lait (アイスオーレ) in custom-made ceramic cups.

anecdotes.text: Bread for the morning set is made with salt rather than sugar, and the accompanying jams (strawberry, blueberry, apricot) were chosen only after extensive tasting.
The owner adjusts milk ratios by the dropper and had custom ceramic cups made to bring out the best in each coffee.
Staff are known for learning a customer's preferred seat, newspaper and order, treating guests as regulars by the third visit.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms founding in 1978, daily roasting on Probat machines, and extraction through an original nel filter. Cliiip covers the salt (not sugar) morning bread, tasted-then-chosen jams, dropper-adjusted milk and custom cups. Morning-hiroshima documents the third-visit-regular hospitality, the simple bread-and-salad morning set, and the cream-based iced au lait.

positives.text: An oat-milk iced coffee is offered, giving a plant-milk option alongside black self-roasted coffee.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood coffee house where service is in Japanese and the warm, regulars-focused hospitality is best enjoyed with a few polite Japanese phrases; pointing at the menu and a smile will carry you through.

places.name: ツバイＧ線 (Tsubai G-sen)
places.gf_detail: A traditional kissaten serving coffee, sandwiches, pasta (okonomiyaki-style spaghetti), curries and pilafs — wheat is central to the food menu (bread, pasta) and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for those needing strict gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu. Most food items contain egg, dairy, meat or fish (morning sets with eggs and sandwiches, grilled-meat lunches, salmon/tarako pasta). Black siphon coffee and possibly plain toast could be ordered, but nothing is documented as plant-based and staff allergy/vegan handling is unknown — a vegan would need to ask in advance.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-18:00 (food L.O. 17:00, drinks L.O. 17:30). Morning service from 7:00 a.m.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (City Hall / Kamiyacho area, very close to Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Showa-era kissaten in central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, tucked one street back from the streetcar avenue in Otemachi. The original shop opened in 1970, and this second location followed about a decade later; it is now run by at least the th

chef_bio.background: A Showa-era kissaten in central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, tucked one street back from the streetcar avenue in Otemachi. The original shop opened in 1970, and this second location followed about a decade later; it is now run by at least the third generation of the same family.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the calm, nostalgic world of the traditional pure coffee house — a classical-music interior with newspapers and magazines to linger over.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted coffee brewed cup-by-cup in a siphon (with free refills), morning sets, daily lunches, and a signature okonomiyaki-style spaghetti.

anecdotes.text: The name carries the family's musical heritage: 'Tsubai' is German for 'two' (this being the second shop), and 'G-sen' comes from 'Air on the G String,' a reference to the second-generation owner who played the violin.
The method for cooking the lunchtime spaghetti noodles is a closely guarded family recipe, developed by the previous generation to keep a freshly-cooked texture even during the busy lunch rush.
The shop opens at 7am and fills with regulars from the morning, with classical music playing while siphon coffee is brewed to order.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Home-tv's feature confirms the 1970 original shop, the second location roughly a decade later, third-generation family operation, and the name story (German 'zwei/two' for the second shop; 'G-sen' from 'Air on the G String' for the violin-playing second-generation owner), plus the company-confidential spaghetti method. Tabetainjya and gnavi confirm 7am opening, classical music, and to-order siphon coffee. No source publishes the current owner's personal name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around wheat (sandwiches/bread, pasta, lunch plates) with no documented gluten-free handling; high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food items center on egg, dairy, meat and fish (egg sandwiches, grilled-meat lunches, salmon/tarako pasta); no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic local kissaten with little to no English and an all-smoking interior, so a foreign student will be most comfortable visiting with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend who can help order.

places.name: かき船 かなわ (Kakibune Kanawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional oyster and seafood restaurant with no documented gluten-free program. Dishes rely heavily on Japanese soy sauce and ponzu (both contain wheat), fried oysters (kaki fry) are breaded in wheat flour, and many courses include tempura or wheat-based seasonings. There is no published information about gluten-free menus, dedicated fryers, or cross-contamination controls. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe without a careful direct conversation, and even then options would be very limited (e.g., plain grilled or raw oysters without sauce).
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is built entirely around oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood; the signature dishes are raw oysters, grilled oysters, fried oysters, and oyster rice. Sauces use fish-based dashi and soy sauce. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and the concept is fundamentally seafood-centric, so it is not a suitable destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00 (L.O. 14:00 on 2F Wakyu / 14:30 on 1F Seto); Dinner 17:00 (L.O. 20:00). Open daily.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi / south side of Peace Memorial Park & Atomic Bomb Dome (a moored boat on the Motoyasu River)
places.cuisine: Hiroshima specialty oyster dishes & Seto Inland Sea seafood
places.notes: Kanawa traces its roots to 1867 (Keio 3), when Hisataro Miho began oyster farming as a family enterprise in Hiroshima. By the postwar years the third generation opened fresh raw-oyster counters in Tokyo and Osaka department stores, introducing Hiroshima oysters to the c

chef_bio.background: Kanawa traces its roots to 1867 (Keio 3), when Hisataro Miho began oyster farming as a family enterprise in Hiroshima. By the postwar years the third generation opened fresh raw-oyster counters in Tokyo and Osaka department stores (from 1946), introducing Hiroshima oysters to the capital for the first time. In 1963 the family opened this floating restaurant, reviving the Edo-period tradition of the 'kakibune' (oyster boat) that had once been used to sell and serve oysters along the rivers.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house prioritizes safety and quality by sourcing oysters from the clean, high-salinity offshore waters of Dakokujima (Fukaura) rather than the inner bay. Moored opposite Peace Memorial Park, it also frames the act of sharing Hiroshima oysters as a small contribution to lasting peace.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served raw, grilled, fried, and as oyster rice, alongside Seto Inland Sea seafood, eaten aboard a traditional moored oyster boat on the Motoyasu River.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant grew out of an oyster-farming family that began in 1867; the 'kakibune' boat format was deliberately chosen as a way to popularize oyster cuisine, reviving an Edo-era sales tradition.
Oysters are farmed in the clean offshore waters of Dakokujima (Fukaura), chosen for high salinity and clarity so they meet the standards for oysters eaten raw.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kanawa's official history page confirms the 1867 (Keio 3) start under first-generation Hisataro Miho, the third generation's 1946 raw-oyster counters in Tokyo/Osaka department stores as the first Tokyo push for Hiroshima oysters, and the 1963 revival of the Edo-era kakibune boat. The official oyster page places farming in the clean high-salinity waters off Dakokujima/Fukaura (大黒神島深浦), meeting raw-eating standards. Sources name only the first and third generations, not six.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's oyster and seafood dishes use Japanese soy sauce, ponzu, and dashi-based seasonings, which standardly contain wheat; fried oysters are breaded in wheat flour. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is oyster- and seafood-centered with fish-based dashi; there is no documented vegan or vegetarian preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A well-known sightseeing-area restaurant with an English website and menu and experience hosting international visitors, so a foreign student can dine comfortably, though it is an upscale sit-down experience rather than a casual budget spot.

places.name: 貝や 廉 (Kaiya Ren)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a shellfish izakaya where most dishes are seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and items like the oyster kamameshi (rice) and various sauces are not gluten-free. No celiac-safe accommodation is documented; cross-contamination from a shared kitchen should be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is shellfish (oysters, sazae, scallops and other shellfish nationwide) plus seafood, with dishes such as shellfish sashimi, grilled oysters, shellfish-dashi chawanmushi (egg and fish/shellfish stock) and oyster kamameshi. There is no documented plant-based menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 18:00-01:30 (L.O. 01:00); Fri-Sat 18:00-02:30 (L.O. 02:00); some sources list 18:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:00). Closed Sundays and holidays (may open on some holidays).
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori (Naka-ku; entertainment district near Ebisu-cho / Hatchobori)
places.notes: A small specialist shellfish izakaya in Hiroshima's Yakkenbori nightlife quarter, devoted to kai (shellfish) from across Japan. The team travels directly to markets from Hokkaido in the north to Fukuoka in the south, building relationships with trusted suppliers to brin

chef_bio.background: A small specialist shellfish izakaya in Hiroshima's Yagenbori nightlife quarter, devoted to kai (shellfish) from across Japan. The team travels directly to markets from Hokkaido in the north to Kyushu in the south, building relationships with trusted suppliers to bring in carefully chosen shellfish daily.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcase seasonal shellfish at their peak with minimal preparation, offering each variety raw, grilled, or simmered so guests can taste its natural aroma and umami; staff suggest pairings such as oysters with white wine and raw shellfish with sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Shellfish in every form: shellfish sashimi (a signature), grilled oysters, oyster kamameshi (clay-pot rice), and a shellfish-dashi chawanmushi. A standout is the Oki Islands rock oyster branded 'Seikai' (清海), which the shop says it alone handles in Hiroshima.

anecdotes.text: The owners go directly to markets from Hokkaido down to Kyushu to source from trusted suppliers, bringing in selected shellfish fresh each day.
The shop highlights the Oki Islands rock oyster 'Seikai' as a dish it alone handles in Hiroshima, and recommends pairing oysters with white wine and raw shellfish with sake.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Kaiya Ren page describes direct market sourcing from Hokkaido to Kyushu with trusted suppliers and daily fresh shellfish; signature shellfish sashimi, grilled oysters, oyster kamameshi and shellfish-dashi chawanmushi; wine/sake pairing guidance; and an oyster 'handled exclusively by this shop.' Search corroborates that this exclusive item is the Oki Islands brand rock oyster 'Seikai' (清海). No owner name is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shellfish sashimi and grilled shellfish are served and seasoned in the Japanese style, which standardly uses wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely shellfish- and seafood-based (oysters, shellfish sashimi, fish/shellfish dashi), so any plant items would share equipment with seafood.

cultural_comfort.note: A late-night local shellfish izakaya in a busy nightlife district; little English is likely, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, and it is a hands-on spot best enjoyed if you eat oysters and other shellfish.

places.name: ひろしまテッパン produced by 餃子家 龍 (Hiroshima Teppan produced by Gyozaya Ryu)
places.gf_detail: There is one dedicated gluten-free dish: a rice-flour okonomiyaki (米粉使用グルテンフリーお好み焼き, around 1,200 yen). It is built on a rice-flour sheet instead of the usual wheat batter, uses thick chewy rice-flour noodles in place of wheat soba, and is finished with a wheat-free sauce so that people with a wheat allergy can eat it. This is unusual and genuinely useful for GF diners. However, the rest of the menu is heavily wheat-based (standard okonomiyaki, gyoza, many teppan items), and the GF okonomiyaki is cooked on the same shared teppan griddle as wheat dishes, so it is a GF-friendly option rather than a dedicated GF kitchen. Strict celiacs should confirm preparation when ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The menu lists a vegetable gyoza described as vegan (野菜餃子・vegan, around 500 yen). Beyond that, this is a meat-and-seafood izakaya: okonomiyaki contains egg and pork, and most teppan dishes use beef, pork, chicken, seafood, oysters and butter, so vegan choices are minimal and there is no dedicated vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sun, holidays & days before holidays: 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:30, drinks L.O. 21:30). No closing day (open daily year-round).
places.neighborhood: Fukuromachi (Hondori / Tatemachi area, next to Fukuromachi Park)
places.notes: This central-Hiroshima teppan izakaya is produced by Gyozaya Ryu, a Hiroshima gyoza specialist that runs as the direct outlet of a gyoza-wrapper maker (餃子皮メーカー直営). It opened on June 30, 2023 in a wood-grained, old-school izakaya-style space next to Fukuromachi Park, pai

chef_bio.background: This central-Hiroshima teppan izakaya is produced by Gyozaya Ryu, a Hiroshima gyoza specialist that runs as the direct outlet of a gyoza-wrapper maker (餃子皮メーカー直営). It opened on June 30, 2023 in a wood-grained, old-school izakaya-style space directly in front of Fukuromachi Park, pairing local teppan favorites and the house gyoza with Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Hiroshima ingredients and the city's okonomiyaki and gyoza culture, while offering a rice-flour, wheat-free-sauce version so guests avoiding wheat can enjoy the local specialty (diners with allergies should always confirm handling with the shop directly).
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and house gyoza alongside teppan dishes, including a rice-flour okonomiyaki the kitchen developed with a wheat-free sauce so people avoiding wheat can enjoy the local specialty.

anecdotes.text: Their wheat-free okonomiyaki is built on a rice-flour sheet rather than wheat batter and layered with thick, chewy white rice-flour noodles, then finished with a wheat-free sauce; cooked slowly without pressing, it comes out juicy with a natural sweetness from the rice flour.
The operator describes itself as a gyoza-wrapper manufacturer's own restaurant serving 'Hiroshima gyoza born and raised in Hiroshima.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: HTV's okonomiyaki program confirms the June 30, 2023 opening, the Fukuromachi 2F location, the Gyozaya Ryu production, and the rice-flour-sheet, thick white rice-flour-noodle, wheat-free-sauce okonomiyaki cooked slowly without pressing for a juicy, naturally sweet result. Gyozaya Ryu's shop page confirms the 'gyoza-wrapper-maker direct outlet' self-description and the Fukuromachi Park frontage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The gluten-free rice-flour okonomiyaki is cooked on the same shared teppan griddle used for wheat-based okonomiyaki, gyoza and other dishes; no dedicated griddle area or separate preparation is documented, so cross-contamination is likely for strict celiacs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen specifically uses a wheat-free sauce for the gluten-free okonomiyaki so that guests with a wheat allergy can eat it (ソースは小麦が入っていないものを使う).

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant created the rice-flour okonomiyaki and wheat-free sauce explicitly so that people with a wheat allergy can eat it safely (小麦アレルギーの人でも安心して食べられる), indicating awareness of wheat-allergy needs, though no formal cross-contact protocol is documented.

positives.text: Offers a genuine rice-flour, wheat-free-sauce gluten-free okonomiyaki using a rice-flour sheet and rice-flour noodles, letting wheat-sensitive diners try the Hiroshima specialty.
A vegetable gyoza is listed as vegan, giving plant-based diners at least one option.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, dinner-only izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's shopping district; staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the picture menu will go a long way, and you can mention 小麦アレルギー (wheat allergy) when ordering the rice-flour okonomiyaki.

places.name: サラスヴァティ (Sarasvati)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature dishes are pasta (wheat) and cakes/scones (wheat flour), and bread is served with the morning and lunch sets, so the core menu is wheat-based. Assume no gluten-free options unless you confirm directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The cafe is coffee- and dessert-focused; signature savory dishes (Miyajima oyster and beef pasta) contain seafood and meat, and cakes typically use dairy/eggs. Black coffee and the Hiroshima lemon-honey syrup drink may suit a relaxed plant-based diner, but ask about specific items.
places.hours_raw: Open roughly 8:30/10:00-18:00/19:00 daily (sources vary slightly); morning service available; open year-round.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), in the machiya backstreets near the
places.cuisine: Self-roast coffee house and cafe in a renova
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sarasvati occupies a warehouse surviving from the Taisho era, renovated into a calm two-story cafe tucked into the quiet machiya backstreets just inland from the five-story pagoda and Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in 2009 and is run as the cafe arm of Itsuki Coffee, a M (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Sarasvati occupies a warehouse surviving from the Taisho era — once a souvenir-wholesaler's storehouse — renovated into a calm two-story cafe tucked into the quiet machiya backstreets just inland from the five-story pagoda and Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in 2009 and is run as the cafe arm of Itsuki Coffee (伊都岐珈琲), a Miyajima specialty-coffee roaster.
chef_bio.philosophy: The space is conceived as a special place dedicated to enjoying coffee: beans are roasted in-house on a 3kg direct-fire roaster, with monthly rotating single-origin specialty coffees served in a deliberately serene, unhurried atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee, a morning service, and a signature lunch of Miyajima oyster and braised beef-shin pasta served with bread; sweets such as a rich cheesecake.

anecdotes.text: The cafe roasts its own beans on a 3kg direct-fire roaster on site and rotates the featured single-origin specialty coffee each month.
The building is a renovated warehouse remaining from the Taisho period, set in the quiet lanes behind the main approach near the pagoda and shrine.
The standout lunch is a hearty pasta of Miyajima oyster and braised beef shin served with bread.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Itsuki Coffee's official Sarasvati page confirms the on-site 3kg direct-fire roaster, cafe service from morning to lunch, and cheesecake. Search (umahiro, hiroshima-mag, pecomag) confirms the renovated Taisho-era warehouse (a former souvenir wholesaler's storehouse), the 2009 opening, the parent roaster Itsuki Coffee (伊都岐珈琲), and the signature Miyajima-oyster and braised beef-shin pasta.

positives.text: All seats are non-smoking.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed tourist-area cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; some English signage and a foreign-visitor-friendly setting, but staff English may be limited.

places.name: そぞ (Sozo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built on a thin wheat-flour batter and layered with house-made wheat (Chinese-style) noodles. The house "crystal" sauce and standard toppings also make this unsuitable for those avoiding gluten. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Core dishes use egg, pork, oysters, conger eel, beef offal, fish-cake (gansu), bonito/fish-based dashi and savory sauce. No vegan okonomiyaki or plant-based option is documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), open daily year-round
places.neighborhood: In front of Hiroshima Station (Matsubara-cho; Hiroshima Okonomi Monogatari Ekimae Hiroba)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked into a counter spot in the station-front food hall, built around an unusually all-in-house approach. The team runs its own dedicated noodle factory, the "Sozo Noodle Factory," pressing fresh okonomiyaki noodles from domestic whe

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked into a counter spot in the station-front food hall, built around an unusually all-in-house approach. The team runs its own dedicated okonomiyaki-noodle factory, pressing fresh noodles for a chewy, springy bite you do not get from bought-in noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooks describe themselves as iron-plate craftsmen who use no chemical seasonings at all, aiming to let the natural flavor of the ingredients carry the dish. To that end they make their own 'crystal' sauce, blending Seto Inland oysters and Hiroshima lemon to pair with okonomiyaki rather than relying on a generic sweet sauce; the lighter, less-sweet sauce is what makes the grated-daikon topping work so well.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature okonomiyaki is finished with a free mound of grated daikon on top, which lightens the whole thing and plays against the sauce. Beyond the standard pork-and-noodle build, the kitchen leans into Hiroshima and Seto Inland specialties: oyster butter-yaki, rare beef shoulder (koune), miso-cooked beef lung, white-grilled conger eel, fried fish-cake (gansu), and a slow offal stew of several cuts in an original broth.

anecdotes.text: The shop makes its own okonomiyaki-specific noodles in a dedicated noodle factory, prizing a chewy, mochi-like texture in the fresh noodle.
Their original 'crystal sauce' is built around Seto Inland oysters and Hiroshima lemon, made less sweet so a free grated-daikon topping can sit on top and keep the okonomiyaki refreshing.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Ekimae-hiroba confirms the shop's own noodle factory pressing custom okonomiyaki wheat noodles, the no-chemical-seasoning iron-plate craft, the oyster-and-lemon 'crystal sauce', the free grated-daikon topping, and the specialty lineup (oyster butter-yaki, koune, miso beef lung, white-grilled conger eel, gansu, offal stew). Umaihiroshima corroborates the ponzu-like less-sweet crystal sauce and daikon topping. Sources confirm 'wheat' noodles but not specifically 'domestic' wheat, nor a proper factory name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared griddle is used for pork, oysters, eel, beef offal and egg, so any item will share surfaces with animal products.

positives.text: The kitchen states it uses no chemical seasonings and makes its own sauce and noodles in-house, so ingredient sourcing is unusually transparent.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly counter-style okonomiyaki stall inside a tourist-oriented food hall by the station; pointing at the menu and a few words of Japanese will carry you, though full English support is not guaranteed.

places.name: 手作りのパン 河内ベーカリー (Tezukuri no Pan Kouchi Bakery)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour bakery; bread, sandwiches and pastries are all made from wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: A classic Japanese bakery whose signature items include egg sandwiches, ham-and-egg mix sandwiches and curry bread, which are not vegan. Plain breads may be vegan-friendly but milk, butter and egg are common in Japanese bakery bread, so a strict vegan would need to ask about each item; no vegan labeling is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 08:00–18:00; closed Sat, Sun, and holidays; also closed 2nd and 4th Wednesdays. (Some local listings note hours as roughly 07:00–19:00; the most detailed listing gives 08:00–18:00.)
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Atomic Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A handmade-bread bakery in the Nobori-cho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, within walking distance of the Hatchobori area and the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome. It has been a fixture of the neighborhood for more than half a century, with listings citing roughly 60

chef_bio.background: A handmade-bread bakery in the Nobori-cho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, within walking distance of the Hatchobori area and the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome. It has been a fixture of the neighborhood for decades — local listings variously cite it as roughly 40 to 50-plus years old — and it has kept making bread the old-fashioned way the whole time.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is defined by a commitment to additive-free (無添加) baking and to preserving traditional, old-fashioned flavors over the decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Its best-known item is cinnamon toast — a rusk-like bread heavily dusted with dark cinnamon that locals treat as a beloved Hiroshima casual treat — alongside a hearty, volume-packed mix sandwich, curry bread, egg sandwiches and a wide range of everyday loaves and pastries.

anecdotes.text: Local food writers describe it as a beloved, retro neighborhood bakery — the kind of long-standing local spot people walk past for years before finally stepping in and discovering just how many kinds of bread it stocks.
The cinnamon toast is singled out as a signature treat, while the volume-packed mix sandwich is recommended as an ideal lunch.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Panyasan-navi confirms the Nobori-cho handmade-bread bakery, additive-free old-fashioned baking, a rich variety of breads, and long-established (creation 50-plus years) status. 4travel confirms the signature rusk-like cinnamon toast heavily coated in dark cinnamon and the recommended volume mix sandwich, but describes the shop as roughly 40 years old — so the exact age is uncertain. No owner name is published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional wheat-flour bakery; flour is handled throughout, so the environment is unsuitable for anyone avoiding gluten and there is no gluten-free offering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items include egg and ham-and-egg sandwiches and curry bread; egg, dairy and meat are used in the kitchen, and plain breads may share equipment, so vegans should ask about specific items.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny old-school neighborhood bakery where you just point at what you want and pay in cash (PayPay also works), so almost no Japanese is needed, though an English menu is unlikely.

places.name: 宮島 レ・クロ (Miyajima Les Clos)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The course menus center on seasonal pasta (wheat) and Western dishes with sauces, and desserts (tiramisu, chocolate cake) contain wheat/dairy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Courses are built around Seto Inland Sea seafood (including the local conger eel/anago) and Japanese beef, with pasta and dairy-rich desserts. No plant-based course or substitution is mentioned.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Itsukushima Shrine / pier area (Machiya-dori, a 100+ year-old residence)
places.cuisine: Italian & Western dishes and cakes made with local ingredients
places.notes: [Miyajima] Les Clos occupies a mansion completed in 1912 (Meiji 45), a residence now more than a century old that has been carefully renovated into a restaurant and cafe. Original architectural details such as the latticed shoji screens and carved transom panels were preserved, an (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: 宮島（廿日市市宮島町）, on Miyajima island a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the pier
chef_bio.background: Les Clos occupies a mansion completed in 1912 (Meiji 45), a residence now more than a century old that has been carefully renovated into a restaurant and cafe. Original architectural details such as the latticed shoji screens and carved transom panels were preserved, and the dining rooms look out onto a Japanese garden that changes with the seasons.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let Japanese tradition and Western modern design coexist in one space, pairing the heritage of the old house with cuisine that showcases seasonal local ingredients of the Seto Inland Sea.
chef_bio.specialty: Western and Italian cooking built on Seto Inland Sea produce, including seafood such as the local conger eel, alongside Japanese-beef steaks and house-made cakes and desserts

anecdotes.text: The building dates to 1912 and retains period features like its shoji and transom woodwork, giving diners the feel of eating inside a preserved Meiji-era home with a seasonal garden view.
Set along Miyajima's machiya street, a roughly 7-15 minute walk from the Miyajima pier, it pairs the historic setting with seasonal lunch and dinner courses.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Les Clos page confirms chef-owner Isamu Kurokoshi (黒越勇), the 1912 (Meiji 45) mansion now over 100 years old, the preserved 吾妻障子 shoji and carved 欄間 transom woodwork, the seasonal Japanese garden, Western/Seto-Inland-Sea cuisine including conger eel and beef with house-made desserts, and the roughly 7-minute walk from the pier.

cultural_comfort.note: A refined sit-down restaurant in a restored historic mansion; menus and service are mainly in Japanese, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help a foreign student feel comfortable ordering courses.

places.name: 中国料理 煌蘭苑 (Chugoku Ryori Kohranen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a Cantonese-style Chinese kitchen, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, oyster sauce, and other wheat-containing seasonings, and there is no mention of gluten-free preparation or a dedicated fryer. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant offers a dedicated vegetarian Chinese course and à la carte items prepared without meat or fish, built around vegetables, mushrooms, black sticky rice, and tofu. However, the official course page describes it as lacto-ovo vegetarian: it includes dairy (e.g., a milk-based pudding) and eggs, so it is not fully vegan as listed. A strict vegan would need to call ahead (the vegetarian course requires reservation at least 3 days in advance) and explicitly request removal of dairy and eggs, plus confirm seasonings. Also confirm whether vegetable dishes use oyster sauce or chicken/fish stock common in Cantonese cooking.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Tuesdays (open on holidays).
places.neighborhood: Hiroshima Station area (central)
places.notes: 煌蘭苑 is the Chinese restaurant on the second floor of Hotel Granvia Hiroshima, directly connected to Hiroshima Station so guests can reach it without stepping outside even on rainy days. It serves Cantonese-style Chinese cuisine in a setting that ranges from open dining

chef_bio.background: 煌蘭苑 is the Chinese restaurant on the second floor of Hotel Granvia Hiroshima, directly connected to Hiroshima Station so guests can reach it without stepping outside even on rainy days. It serves Cantonese-style Chinese cuisine in a setting that ranges from open dining to private rooms of several sizes, geared toward family meals and formal entertaining alike.
chef_bio.philosophy: Head chef Daiji Yamamoto cooks around the ancient idea that food and medicine come from the same source (医食同源), building well-balanced dishes meant to bring diners health and joy suited to the occasion.
chef_bio.specialty: Cantonese cuisine guided by the principle of 医食同源 ("food and medicine share the same origin"), with carefully balanced menus that combine a wide range of ingredients. Hong Kong-style clay-pot (煲仔) dishes are a signature, alongside multi-course menus and à la carte selections.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen prepares a dedicated vegetarian Chinese course without meat or fish, centered on vegetables, mushrooms, black sticky rice, and tofu, so that guests with dietary preferences can still enjoy authentic Chinese cooking when they reserve ahead.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The hotel's Kohranen page confirms head chef 山本大治 (Yamamoto)'s 医食同源 philosophy, the Cantonese base, the Hong Kong-favored clay-pot (煲仔/ボウジャイ) signature, the 2F station-connected location, and private rooms of varying sizes. The plan page confirms the reserve-ahead lacto-ovo-vegetarian course of vegetables, mushrooms, black sticky rice and tofu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cantonese-style Chinese cooking at this restaurant relies on standard Chinese seasonings such as soy sauce and oyster sauce, which contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegetarian course is lacto-ovo: it explicitly contains no meat or fish but does include dairy (a milk-based pudding) and eggs, so it is not vegan without modification.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A dedicated meat-and-fish-free vegetarian course is offered with a required reservation at least three days in advance, indicating the kitchen accommodates dietary requests arranged ahead of time.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated vegetarian course and à la carte vegetarian items free of meat and fish, built on vegetables, mushrooms, tofu, and black sticky rice.

cultural_comfort.note: As the restaurant inside Hotel Granvia Hiroshima (directly connected to Hiroshima Station), staff are used to international hotel guests and can handle English and special dietary requests made in advance, so a foreign student should feel comfortable here.

places.name: ベーカリー Roti (Bakery Roti)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery, so essentially all products contain gluten. No gluten-free options or dedicated gluten-free handling are documented; not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific information is documented. This is a conventional Japanese bakery whose breads typically contain egg, milk, and butter, and signature items (cheeseburger bread, snow-crab cream, BLT dog, French toast, cinnamon rolls) clearly include dairy, egg, meat, and seafood. A vegan visitor would need to ask item-by-item and should not assume any product is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 7:00–19:00, closed Wednesdays (per local blog and news coverage). One platform listing alternatively shows 7:00–18:30 and Tue–Wed closed; confirm directly with the shop.
places.neighborhood: Sakai-machi, Naka-ku (west side of the Dobashi / Hondori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run neighborhood bakery in Hiroshima's Sakaimachi district, a short walk west of the downtown Hondori shopping street and the Peace Memorial Park. Open since 2009, it has become a quietly beloved local fixture, filling the shop each morning with the smell

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run neighborhood bakery in Hiroshima's Sakaimachi district, a short walk west of the downtown Hondori shopping street and the Peace Memorial Park. Open since 2009, it has become a quietly beloved local fixture, filling the shop each morning with the smell of freshly baked bread.
chef_bio.specialty: Known for an unusually wide and playful range of savory filled breads — a Japanese-style cheeseburger bun finished with grated-daikon (おろしだれ) sauce, a BLT hot dog, karaage (fried chicken) dog, and creative fillings like smoked cheese with potato salad and Sicilian-style caponata — alongside sweet options such as French toast and cinnamon rolls.

anecdotes.text: The owner recommends coming before noon, when the full lineup of breads is on display, reflecting a hands-on, made-fresh-daily approach.
The interior is described as a warm, calming space with bread arranged in the center and along the windows, plus a small eat-in corner so you can enjoy a roll on the spot.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Good-tomorrow confirms the Sakaimachi (堺町) owner-run bakery, 7:00-19:00 hours (closed Wednesdays), the eat-in corner, the before-noon full lineup, and the savory breads (cheeseburger, BLT, karaage dog, smoked-cheese potato salad, Sicilian caponata) plus French toast and cinnamon rolls. Search confirms the 2009 opening and that the cheeseburger is finished Japanese-style with a grated-daikon sauce (おろしだれ). No owner name is published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour bakery; flour is handled openly throughout, so airborne and surface gluten cross-contact is unavoidable. No gluten-free production documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and fillings include dairy, egg, meat, and seafood; no separation from animal ingredients is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed self-service neighborhood bakery where you pick up bread on a tray and pay at the counter, so very little Japanese is needed, though staff likely speak little English.

places.name: 鉄板焼×府中焼 あっちゃん (Teppanyaki x Fuchuyaki Acchan)
places.gf_detail: This is an okonomiyaki/Fuchu-yaki house. The batter and the layered noodles are wheat-based, and the savory sauce typically contains wheat, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The signature Fuchu-yaki is built on crispy minced beef, and other dishes feature pork, squid and egg, plus a sauce that is generally not vegan. No vegan menu or accommodation is documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–13:15, 17:00–21:15; closed Monday (per Tabelog). One secondary listing shows 11:00–14:30 / 17:00–22:00 with irregular closures, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 中区立町 (Tatemachi), central Hiroshima near Hondori/Hatchobori,
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small second-floor okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop that opened in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district in November 2023, a short walk from the Hondori shopping arcade.

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, specializing in Fuchu-yaki, the minced-meat style of okonomiyaki that originated in the city of Fuchu.
chef_bio.background: A small second-floor okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop that opened in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district on November 17, 2023, a short walk from the Hondori shopping arcade.
chef_bio.specialty: Fuchu-yaki: instead of sliced pork, the cook uses 100% beef mince griddled until crisp, layered with noodles fried until crackly-crunchy. A house favorite is the Acchan Special, combining pork, shiso (perilla) leaf, fresh squid and plenty of green onion for a mix of crispy noodles and snappy scallions.

anecdotes.text: The shop's draw is as much the cook as the food: he is known for a big, funny personality, and regulars say you fall for the manager before you even taste the okonomiyaki.
The Fuchu-yaki is made with 100% beef mince griddled crisp, giving the noodles a signature crackly texture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The mypl new-open article confirms the Tatemachi 2F location near Hondori, the Fuchu-yaki made with crisp-griddled minced meat and crackly noodles, the Acchan Special (pork, shiso, squid, green onion), and the manager's big, entertaining personality. Tabelog records the opening date as November 17, 2023. No owner name is published.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, lively counter shop where the cook chats with guests; a little conversational Japanese or a translation app will make ordering and the back-and-forth much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 鉄板屋 我んが (Teppanya Ganga)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is okonomiyaki (wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles), yakisoba, and teppanyaki served with standard Japanese sauces (okonomi/soy sauces typically contain wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on meat (1-pound steak, Hiroshima wagyu, hormone), seafood (local oysters, uni-and-spinach 'uni-horen'), and egg/pork-based okonomiyaki. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (some sources 11:30-13:30); Dinner Mon-Thu 17:30-23:00, Fri & days before holidays 17:30-24:00, Sat 17:30-24:00 (L.O. 23:00). Sources disagree on the closed day (one lists Wed, others Sun/holidays, others irregular).
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku (near Tatemachi tram stop / Hondori arcade)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independently run teppanyaki and okonomiyaki spot tucked in the Tachimachi/Nakanotana shopping street area of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Hondori arcade and the Peace Memorial Park. The counter sits right at the grill, so guests take in the taste,

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run teppanyaki and okonomiyaki spot on Tachimachi (立町) in central Hiroshima, beside the Nakanotana shopping street and a short walk from the Hondori arcade and the Peace Memorial Park. The counter sits right at the grill, so guests take in the taste, the sizzle, and the aroma of Hiroshima ingredients cooked in front of them.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Hiroshima regional ingredients on the teppan in a relaxed, neighborhood setting suited to everything from after-work drinks to small gatherings.
chef_bio.specialty: House '我んが焼 (Ganga-yaki)' okonomiyaki, the hearty value-priced 1-pound steak, fried local oysters (from Eba/江波), and a uni-and-spinach teppan dish (uni-horen / ウニホーレン); a brothless (no-soup) dandan noodle is offered for takeout. Local sake and wine are paired with the grill dishes.

anecdotes.text: The shop bills itself around tasting Hiroshima's local gourmet on the teppan, with the counter described as a place to enjoy taste, sound, and aroma all at once.
Signature items highlighted by the shop include the popular value-priced 1-pound steak, the house 'Ganga-yaki' okonomiyaki, fried Eba oysters, and a uni-and-spinach dish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Nakanotana mall page and Gnavi listing describe 我んが as a Tachimachi teppanyaki/okonomiyaki spot built on Hiroshima ingredients, with counter seats billed for enjoying taste, sound and aroma at once; signatures include the 1-pound steak, 我んが焼, ウニホーレン and カキフライ (Eba oysters), plus a takeout-only 汁なし担々麺. Address 中区立町3-23.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based okonomiyaki batter and wheat noodles are cooked on a shared teppan grill alongside other dishes; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard okonomiyaki/soy-style sauces, which typically contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on meat and seafood (steak, oysters, uni) cooked on a shared grill; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly small local teppanyaki-izakaya where counter seats put you right by the grill; little to no English is documented, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, but the casual atmosphere is welcoming to visitors.

places.name: 鉄人中華 きむら (Tetsujin Chuka Kimura)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu centers on Chinese dishes using soy sauce, XO sauce and other wheat-based seasonings, plus noodles (ramen) and fried rice; mapo tofu and stir-fries rely on soy/wheat-containing sauces. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Signature dishes are meat- and seafood-heavy (chicken, shrimp, squid; mapo tofu and mapo eggplant typically contain minced pork) and rely on animal-based and fermented sauces (XO sauce, soy sauce). No documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-23:00; closed Sundays and holidays
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka-ku (near Nagarekawa / Hatchobori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef, nicknamed the "Tetsujin" (Iron Man), trained at well-regarded Sichuan and Cantonese restaurants before opening his own counter-style place in central Hiroshima.

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef — whose shop name 鉄人 (Iron Man) nods to his command of the wok — trained at well-regarded Sichuan and Cantonese restaurants before opening his own counter-style place in Hiroshima's Kanayama-cho (銀山町).
chef_bio.philosophy: He makes his seasonings by hand from scratch — the Sichuan pepper and the sauces — and pursues ingredient quality, down to shrimp peeled one by one, serving authentic, handmade Chinese food in a casual, reasonably priced setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Sichuan-Cantonese Chinese cooking, with a mapo tofu (麻辣豆腐) served bubbling-hot and heavy on Sichuan pepper that has become a local favorite; other signatures include mapo eggplant (麻婆ナス), ebi chili (shrimp in chili sauce), and cuttlefish stir-fried in XO sauce.

anecdotes.text: The counter seats are the recommended spot because diners get a front-row "live" view of the chef working the wok.
When a local food blogger polled their Instagram followers for the best mapo tofu in town, this shop placed third.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog states the owner trained at renowned Sichuan/Cantonese restaurants and hand-makes his sansho and sauces and hand-peels shrimp; the modokimaru blog (Jan 2025) confirms the 麻辣豆腐, 麻婆ナス, ebi chili and XO cuttlefish, recommends the counter for watching the wok, and notes the shop placed 3rd in the blogger's Instagram mapo-tofu poll. Counter-focused, ~18 seats, 中区銀山町11-23.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use soy sauce, XO sauce and other Chinese seasonings that are typically wheat-containing, and the menu includes ramen and fried rice.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (chicken, shrimp, squid; pork-based mapo dishes), so heavy shared-equipment animal cross-contact is expected.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, lively counter-style Chinese izakaya run by one chef; basic Japanese helps for ordering and casual conversation, and there is no indication of an English menu.

noodles.notes: Specific noodle gauge and shape are not documented. The bowl reads as a classic Chinese-restaurant chuka-soba meant as a light finishing dish, suggesting a standard medium noodle that defers to the clear broth and scallions.

ramen.signature_bowl: 葱ラーメン (Negi Ramen) — a clear, light soy-based chuka-soba crowned with a generous heap of fresh, crisp scallions, served as the comforting finish to a Chinese meal.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Served at a Chinese kitchen whose broth and seasonings rely on animal stock; no documented plant-only broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: This isn't a ramen specialist's bowl but a beloved Chinese kitchen's negi ramen — the kind of clear, soy-kissed chuka-soba that regulars order to close a feast. Its soul is nostalgia: a light, crisp broth that simply lets a mountain of fresh scallions sing.

places.highlight: This isn't a ramen specialist's bowl but a beloved Chinese kitchen's negi ramen — the kind of clear, soy-kissed chuka-soba that regulars order to close a feast. Its soul is nostalgia: a light, crisp broth that simply lets a mountain of fresh scallions sing.

places.name: 焼鳥かけはし 銀山 (Yakitori Kakehashi Kanayama)
places.gf_detail: This is a charcoal yakitori and chicken-hotpot izakaya. The grilled skewers are basted in tare and seasonings built on regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and fried/marinated items and the hotpot broth also rely on standard soy-based seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer information, and no indication that wheat can be avoided across the menu. Gluten-free travelers should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm specifics directly with the kitchen in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: A specialist chicken restaurant with no documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 18:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00 / drinks L.O. 22:30, last entry 21:30). Closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka-ku (near Kanayamacho tram stop; Nagarekawa / Hatchobori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact charcoal-yakitori and chicken-hotpot spot tucked away in Hiroshima's Ginzan-cho nightlife district, a few minutes' walk from the Ginzan-cho tram stop. It opened in 2024 as a sister to a nearby Nagarekawa chicken restaurant under the same Kakehashi name.

chef_bio.background: A compact charcoal-yakitori and chicken-hotpot spot tucked into Hiroshima's Kanayama-cho (銀山町) nightlife district, about a three-minute walk from the Kanayama-cho tram stop. It opened on 30 May 2024 when the original 地鶏かけはし in Horikawa-cho split into two shops — a main shop and this Kanayama branch under the same Kakehashi name.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on Tosa binchotan charcoal to draw out the true character of each chicken breed, crisping the surface while keeping the inside juicy, and frames itself as an artisanal, sophisticated hideaway.
chef_bio.specialty: Skewers of carefully selected heritage-breed jidori chicken grilled over Tosa binchotan charcoal, plus a rich chicken water-hotpot (mizutaki) featuring Miyazaki jidori.

anecdotes.text: The menu showcases distinct regional breeds chosen for different roles, including Akita's Hinai-jidori, Mie's Kumano-jidori, and Ehime's Himekko-jidori on the grill, with Miyazaki jidori starring in the hotpot.
A local food blogger covered the shop as a newly opened yakitori spot (opened 30 May 2024) that split off from the Horikawa-cho 地鶏かけはし.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The gorp page confirms Tosa-binchotan grilling and the breeds (秋田比内地鶏, 三重熊野地鶏, 愛媛媛っこ地鶏 for skewers; みやざき地頭鶏 for the water-hotpot) at 中区銀山町11-23 西川ビル2F; the kingofchem ameblo dates the opening to 30 May 2024 as a split of the Horikawa-cho 地鶏かけはし into a main shop and this Kanayama branch (~18 seats).

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a yakitori and izakaya kitchen, grilled skewers, fried items, and the hotpot share seasonings and surfaces built on wheat-containing soy sauce and tare; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare and dipping/seasoning sauces for the grilled chicken are based on standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built entirely around chicken, with broths and seasonings of animal/soy origin, so plant-based dishes would share equipment and stocks with meat.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, intimate local yakitori counter where some basic Japanese will go a long way; a foreign student can enjoy it but should be ready to order in Japanese or with pointing, and ideally reserve ahead.

places.name: 瀬戸内料理 雑草庵瀬戸 (Setouchi Ryori Zassouan Seto)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. As a Setouchi izakaya the cooking relies on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based sauces, plus tempura (octopus tempura) and other battered/fried items, so cross-contact with wheat is pervasive. No documented gluten-free menu or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept centers on local Seto Inland Sea seafood — oysters (including year-round raw oysters), conger eel, sea bream, octopus, small sardines — plus regional pork. Dashi (fish stock) and wheat-soy-based seasonings run through the menu. No documented vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:30); some listings show extended Fri/Sat hours to ~02:00. Closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Gin'yamachi, Naka-ku (near Nagarekawa / Yagenbori nightlife,
places.notes: Zassouan is a Hiroshima dining group with roughly a quarter-century of history, built on a simple ambition: to make a restaurant that represents Hiroshima using only Hiroshima ingredients and Hiroshima sake. The Seto (銀山町) branch carries that idea into the Nagarekawa ni

chef_bio.background: Zassouan is a Hiroshima dining group that describes roughly a quarter-century of history built on a simple ambition: to make a restaurant that represents Hiroshima using only Hiroshima ingredients and Hiroshima sake. The Seto (銀山町) branch carries that idea into the Nagarekawa/Yakugenbori nightlife district, serving the bounty of the Seto Inland Sea in an intimate setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: Local production for local consumption (地産地消) — the kitchen set out to champion Hiroshima's own ingredients and rice wines before the farm-to-table idea became fashionable, so that transplants and out-of-town guests can taste and understand Hiroshima's food culture in one sitting.
chef_bio.specialty: Setouchi local cuisine: oysters, conger eel (anago — the conger eel rice, anago-meshi, is the signature #1 favorite), sea bream, octopus, small sardines, and regional pork, paired with Hiroshima local sake.

anecdotes.text: Through ongoing research and testing with oyster producers, the restaurant secured a supply of sterilized oysters that lets it serve raw oysters all year round — a distinction it shares with only one dedicated oyster specialist in the city.

The house aims to be a place where newcomers to the region can grasp Hiroshima's food culture, presenting marine fare alongside mountain produce such as Momiji pork, Akitsu potatoes, and pickled Hiroshima greens.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hiroshima-ouen.com states 穴子めし is the #1 dish and that, through testing with oyster producers, the shop serves 無菌 (sterile) raw oysters year-round — one of only two places in Hiroshima city (with one oyster specialist) to do so. koune and nagarekawa pages confirm the Seto-Inland-Sea/Hiroshima focus and local sake, Momiji pork, Akitsu potatoes and Hiroshima greens. The ~quarter-century, only-Hiroshima-ingredients concept is reported across listings (zassouan.net is now offline).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes tempura (e.g., octopus tempura) and other fried items, indicating shared frying and battering with wheat flour.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasonal Setouchi seafood dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya manner, relying on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fish, shellfish (oysters), and meat, with fish-based dashi throughout; no separation for plant-only diets is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Hiroshima izakaya where menus and service are primarily in Japanese; a little Japanese or a translation app will make ordering the local seafood dishes much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 山村茶屋 (Yamamura Chaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The core dishes rely on wheat: anagomeshi and the various rice bowls are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), omurice is made with wheat-containing seasonings, and udon noodles are wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on animal products: conger eel (anago), oysters, eggs (omurice, tamago-don, kaki-tama), and dishes seasoned with fish-based dashi and soy sauce. No documented plant-based or vegetarian set meals.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-14:00 (closed Wednesday and Thursday)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), near Momijidani Park / Itsuku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked inside a small park on Miyajima island, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the Momijidani Park ropeway area, Yamamura Chaya is the kind of unassuming neighborhood eatery that islanders themselves drop into. While most of Miyajima's dining caters to day-trip (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Tucked inside a small park (Fujinno-dana) on Miyajima island, a walk downhill from the Momijidani ropeway area and near Itsukushima Shrine, Yamamura Chaya is the kind of unassuming neighborhood eatery that islanders themselves drop into. While most of Miyajima's dining caters to day-trippers, this is a genuine old-fashioned shokudo (everyday diner) serving honest, affordable home-style lunches.
chef_bio.philosophy: An ordinary, comforting lunch over casual conversation rather than a tourist showpiece; the appeal is the warm, lived-in atmosphere of a real island teahouse.
chef_bio.specialty: Conger eel over rice (anagomeshi) alongside everyday comfort plates the locals order most: a fluffy omurice, oyster rice bowls, egg rice bowls, fried rice, and udon.

anecdotes.text: A diner who went looking for somewhere genuinely local rather than tourist-facing described it as the place 'where Miyajima residents come to eat in a cozy atmosphere,' valuing the comfort and the offhand conversation with the staff, and noted the omurice as the most popular item among islanders.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Tabelog review describes 山村茶屋 as a local Miyajima shokudo where residents eat in a relaxed atmosphere; omurice (¥700) is the islanders' most popular order, fried rice (¥600) is 'plainly delicious', and anago dishes are available. It sits in Fujinno-dana Park, downhill ~10 min from the ropeway.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anagomeshi and the rice bowls are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce/tare, which contains wheat; udon is also wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on conger eel, oysters, eggs, and fish-based dashi, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable across the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a relaxed local spot where residents chat casually with the staff, so a little basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help you feel welcome; English support is unlikely.

places.name: お好み焼 よっちゃん (Okonomiyaki Yocchan)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The dish is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and finished with okonomiyaki sauce, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or accommodations are documented, and everything is cooked on a single shared griddle. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every okonomiyaki here includes pork and egg as standard, and the signature dishes use them plus optional squid, shrimp, and other animal toppings. The sauce and griddle are shared with meat. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-14:30 / Sat & holidays 11:00-14:00 / closed Sundays (lunchtime only)
places.neighborhood: Motomachi / Kamiyacho-higashi, Naka-ku (basement beside the Hiroshima Prefectural Government offices; very close to Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter-style Hiroshima-okonomiyaki shop tucked into the basement of an office building right next to the Hiroshima Prefectural Government, founded by owner Hidenori Yoshiyama in 1990 and run as a beloved weekday-lunch spot for local office workers.

chef_bio.background: A small counter-style Hiroshima-okonomiyaki shop tucked into the basement of an office building in the Motomachi/Kamiyacho district next to the Hiroshima Prefectural Government, founded by owner Hidenori Yoshiyama in 1990 and run as a beloved weekday-lunch spot for local office workers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yoshiyama believes great okonomiyaki comes from reading the griddle: 'a person who understands the griddle's feelings can make delicious okonomiyaki.' He steams the cabbage gently to coax out its natural sweetness for a light, fluffy texture rather than a dense one, and holds to a simple standard of cheap prices, generous portions, and good taste.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the most popular order being the soba nikutama (noodles layered with pork and egg). The owner sources fresh noodles from a local maker (Isono Seimen) and finishes the dish with Mouri Brewing's Carp Sauce from Miyoshi, which uses vegetable-based natural sweetness rather than artificial sweeteners.

anecdotes.text: The owner carefully controls cooking time and the cabbage's position on the griddle to bring out its natural sweetness, aiming for a fluffy texture instead of a hard one.
He chooses Mouri Brewing's Carp Sauce because it has no artificial sweeteners, so when brushed onto the hot okonomiyaki it gives off an appetizing aroma.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Chugoku Shimbun's okonomiyaki feature profiles owner 吉山英典 (est. 1990): he gently steams cabbage to draw out sweetness for a fluffy texture, holds to 'cheap, generous, tasty', uses Isono Seimen (磯野製麺) fresh noodles and Mouri Brewing's Carp Sauce (Miyoshi/三次) with no artificial sweeteners. Tabelog lists the shop basement in Motomachi/Kamiyacho, 肉玉 the signature.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan griddle on which wheat-based okonomiyaki and noodles are cooked together; no separation documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Finished with okonomiyaki/Carp brand sauce (Mouri Brewing), which is wheat-containing and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All okonomiyaki are made with pork and egg as standard and cooked on a shared griddle with other meat and seafood toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, cash-only lunchtime counter shop in an office-building basement frequented by local workers, so come ready to order in Japanese (or by pointing) and don't expect English menus or card payment.

places.name: 体にやさしい創作おばんざい AGRI（アグリ） (Karada ni Yasashii Sosaku Obanzai AGRI)
places.gf_detail: According to several reservation-information sites, the menu using local ingredients can be customized to gluten-free on request. However, there is no mention of a dedicated cooking line, and as a Japanese restaurant the standard menu likely uses wheat-containing seasonings (soy sauce, miso, etc.). For gluten-free requests, advance consultation and confirmation by phone (082-246-7721) is necessary. Safety assessment: not reliably safe for celiacs; gluten-free is offered as an on-request customization with no dedicated line, and wheat-based seasonings are likely in standard dishes.
