places.vegan_detail: The restaurant's signature is "body-friendly" obanzai centered on vegetable dishes, and it states explicitly that a vegetarian menu is available. According to reservation-information sites, vegan customization is also possible on request. However, because the standard obanzai and the dashi often use animal-derived ingredients such as seafood and bonito dashi, those wanting fully vegan should consult at the time of advance reservation. Safety assessment: vegetarian-friendly and vegan-possible on request, but the default dashi is often animal-based, so advance consultation is needed for strict vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00) / Sun: 17:00-22:00 (some sources say closed Sun). Takeout/delivery 11:00-18:00. Irregular days off during private bookings or large catering jobs.
places.neighborhood: Nakamachi, Naka-ku (Fukuromachi, within walking distance of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: She obtained her cook's license in 1979 and graduated from the Faculty of Letters of Hiroshima Jogakuin University in 1980. After marrying she devoted herself to homemaking, but prompted by her husband's illness she opened the soup and obanzai shop "Kitchen Potluck" in 2000. In October 2010 she relocated to Fukuromachi/Nakamachi in Naka-ku and opened the natural-food restaurant "AGRI." She serves as a director of Slow Food Hiroshima and is involved in efforts to promote rice flour, as well as being active in many fields such as cooking segments on TV and radio and lectures at her alma mater, Hiroshima Jogakuin University.

chef_bio.origin: 広島市出身（1956年生まれ）。実家は魚屋兼仕出し業を営んでいた。
chef_bio.background: 1979年に調理師免許を取得、1980年に広島女学院大学文学部を卒業。結婚後は家事に専念していたが、夫の病気をきっかけに2000年にスープとおばんざいの店『キッチンポットラック』を開業。2010年に中区袋町へ移転して自然派の店『AGRI』をオープンした（現在は中町）。スローフード広島の理事を務め、広島市の米粉推進委員として米粉普及にも関わるほか、テレビ・ラジオの料理コーナーや2013年からの母校・広島女学院大学での講義など、多方面で活動している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 『野菜の力を創り出す』『食と人をつないで新しい価値を創り出す』をコンセプトに、体にやさしい料理で食卓を豊かにすることを目指す。2020年には減塩レシピ本『魔女の幸せ100年レシピ』を著すなど、減塩・健康志向の食を発信している。
chef_bio.specialty: 季節の野菜をたっぷり使い、20種類以上の食材を一皿に盛り込む『おばんざいランチ』。広島県産の米や玄米を用い、塩分2g以下に抑えた『減塩ランチ』も看板。

anecdotes.text: 実家が魚屋兼仕出し業という食の環境で育ち、夫の病気を機に2000年に自身の店を開いたことが料理研究家としての出発点となった。
広島の減塩サミット(2012年・2014年)に出展し、瀬戸内ブランド認定を受けた『まるごと広島レモンケーキ』など地域食材を生かした商品開発にも取り組んでいる。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese Wikipedia gives her bio: born 1956 in Hiroshima to a family running a fish shop/caterer, cook's license 1979, graduated 広島女学院大学文学部 1980, opened キッチンポットラック in 2000 after her husband's illness, moved to Fukuromachi and opened AGRI in 2010; Slow Food Hiroshima director, Hiroshima city rice-flour committee, university lecturer from 2013. The AGRI site/HotPepper confirm the 20+-ingredient おばんざいランチ with 玄米, the 塩分2g以下 減塩ランチ, the 減塩サミット (2012/2014) and the Setouchi-brand まるごと広島レモンケーキ.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 和食・おばんざいの店であり、醤油・味噌など小麦を含む調味料が通常使われているとみられる(出汁・味付けの基本)。グルテンフリー希望は事前相談が必要。

vegan_cross_contact.text: おばんざいや出汁には魚介・かつお出汁など動物性食材が使われることが多く、完全ヴィーガンには事前のカスタマイズ相談が前提。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 地元食材を使ったメニューはリクエストに応じてヴィーガン・グルテンフリーにカスタマイズ可能と案内されている。

positives.text: 野菜料理にこだわり、ベジタリアンメニューがあると明記。塩分2g以下の減塩ランチなど健康配慮メニューが充実。
料理研究家が営む個人店で、玄米(美人玄米)や20品目以上の食材を使った体にやさしいおばんざいが看板。

cultural_comfort.note: 料理研究家のオーナーが営む個人店で、英語メニューや英語対応の記載は確認できないため、基本的な日本語(または翻訳アプリ)があると安心して食事と食事制限の相談ができます。

places.name: 笑福 宮島店 (Shofuku Miyajima)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Core dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings (which typically contain wheat), and grilled/glazed anago and oyster rice use soy-based sauces. Onigiri and rice bowls are rice-based but the seasonings are not gluten-free, and no gluten-free preparation or labeling is documented. A celiac traveler should not rely on this spot.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built around oysters (raw and grilled), anago (conger eel), scallops and other seafood, with dashi and fish-based seasonings throughout. There are no documented plant-based main dishes.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-20:00 daily (primary listing). A separate listing shows 10:30-18:00 with Tuesdays closed, so closing time and a possible Tuesday rest day can vary seasonally.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima island, by the ferry terminal at the entrance to Om
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shofuku opened in 2017, with its main shop in Hiroshima city's Horikawa-cho district; the Miyajima shop is its second location, set right in front of the ferry terminal at the entrance to the Omotesando approach. It is a small independent operation rather than a chain. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima city (Horikawa-cho main shop), expanding onto Miyajima island.
chef_bio.background: Shofuku opened in 2017, with its main shop in Hiroshima city's Horikawa-cho district; the Miyajima shop is its second location, set right in front of the ferry terminal at the entrance to the Omotesando approach. It is a small independent operation rather than a chain.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on fresh, carefully handled local oysters and anago, served in both sit-down and grab-and-go formats so visitors can taste Miyajima's signature seafood while strolling.
chef_bio.specialty: Local oysters served raw and grilled, including large, plump oysters prized for sweetness and umami, plus oyster rice and fresh anago (conger eel) dishes. The shop leans on oysters that are described as non-frozen and not treated with chlorinated water, prized for clean, clear flavor.

anecdotes.text: Diners highlight the friendly, knowledgeable hospitality, with staff happy to share local tips alongside the seafood.
The shop is praised for fresh oysters that are not frozen and not disinfected with chlorinated water, giving them a notably clean, transparent taste.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms 笑福 opened 2017 with its main shop in Horikawa-cho and the Miyajima shop as the 2nd location in front of the ferry terminal at the Omotesando entrance; it serves local oysters raw and grilled, oyster rice and anago, and prides itself on non-frozen oysters not treated with chlorinated water for a clean, clear taste (24 seats, no reservations). Hitosara notes friendly, knowledgeable service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as glazed/grilled anago, oyster rice and onigiri rely on Japanese soy- and dashi-based seasonings, which conventionally contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is entirely seafood-based (oysters, anago, scallops) with dashi seasonings, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable across the whole operation.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up oyster and anago shop right by the ferry where you can point at what you want and eat on the go, so a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way even if no English menu is guaranteed.

places.name: 蔵VITTO (Kura Vitto)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Signature dishes are wheat-based: the Hiroshima oyster margherita pizza and the daily-changing pasta both contain wheat. Tapas and light fare are not described as gluten-free, and no gluten-free menu or preparation is mentioned anywhere. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should treat this venue as unsuitable unless they confirm specific options directly.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on oysters (oyster pizza), and the documented tapas include low-temperature salmon and roast beef; pizza and pasta typically use cheese and dairy. No vegan dishes or plant-based options are documented. The draw is local Hiroshima sake, shochu, wine, and craft beer paired with seafood and meat small plates.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00 / drinks L.O. 22:30); lunch from around 11:30
places.neighborhood: On Miyajima island (~1 min walk from Miyajima pier, near Omotesando)
places.cuisine: Bar/izakaya with oyster dishes, tapas & local sake
places.notes: [Miyajima] 蔵VITTO is the bar of Hotel Miyajima Besso, a modern property produced by Kinsuikan, a long-running Miyajima hot-spring inn founded in 1902. The name plays on 蔵 (kura, a sake brewery storehouse), a nod to the idea that you have arrived at the gateway of Miyajima with Hir (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: Miyajima island, Hiroshima — set just inside the main entrance of Hotel Miyajima Besso, a short walk from the ferry pier
chef_bio.background: 蔵VITTO is the bar of Hotel Miyajima Besso, a modern property produced by Kinsuikan, a long-running Miyajima hot-spring inn founded in 1902. The name plays on 蔵 (kura, a sake brewery storehouse), a nod to the idea that you have arrived at the gateway of Miyajima with Hiroshima's celebrated local brews waiting for you.
chef_bio.philosophy: Celebrating Hiroshima as one of Japan's great sake regions and pairing local craft drinks with cuisine built on fresh regional produce — an everyday oasis for grown-ups that welcomes both hotel guests and passing travelers.
chef_bio.specialty: A relaxed adult bal showcasing Hiroshima's drink culture alongside small plates such as a generous Hiroshima oyster pizza and rotating daily tapas from the hotel chef, served from morning (9:00) through late evening.

anecdotes.text: The bar pours Sakurao Gin distilled in nearby Sakurao (Hatsukaichi) and local Miyajima craft beer, with signature cocktails like a Miyajima Gin Tonic and Hiroshima Spumoni developed under bartender Shingo Noma.
You can enjoy fresh bread bought from the hotel's own bakery, Shimaumai PAN (島旨PAN), right at the bar counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Hotel Miyajima Besso 蔵VITTO bar page confirms the bar is produced by Kinsuikan (founded 1902), pours Sakurao Gin (Hatsukaichi), Miyoshi-winery wine and Miyajima/Hiroshima craft beer from 9:00, offers signature cocktails Miyajima Gin Tonic and Hiroshima Spumoni under Shingo Noma, an oyster pizza and tapas, and bread from its Shimaumai PAN (島旨PAN) bakery. The mrs.living reporter article covers the same bar.

cultural_comfort.note: As a stylish bar inside a hotel right by the Miyajima ferry pier, it's used to receiving travelers, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome even with limited Japanese.

places.name: お好み焼まとちゃん (Okonomiyaki Matochan)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki house. The okonomiyaki batter and crepe layer are made from wheat flour, the layered noodles (そば) are wheat, the signature あげおこ is deep-fried battered okonomiyaki, and dishes are finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki/Otafuku sauce and soy-sauce-based seasonings. The fried oysters (牡蠣フライ) use wheat breadcrumbs. No gluten-free menu, preparation, or accommodation is documented anywhere. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg-and-pork okonomiyaki, oysters (Miyajima brand 牡蠣), squid, shrimp, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, with sauces that are not vegan. The closest non-meat item documented is an oven-baked potato-salad-and-cheese dish (ポテサラチーズ焼, dairy, not vegan). No vegan or fully vegetarian options are documented, and no vegan accommodation is mentioned. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu (and holidays): 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00). Fri, Sat, Sun: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Tuesdays. May close early when sold out.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Miyajima-cho, Hatsukaichi City) / Itsukushima Shrine & Omotesando shopping street area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into a quiet alley just steps from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine on Miyajima, this Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop occupies a converted house more than twenty years old, giving it a calm, lived-in feel away from the bustle of the main shopping street. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Tucked into a quiet alley just steps from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine on Miyajima, this Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop occupies a converted house more than twenty years old, giving it a calm, lived-in feel away from the bustle of the main shopping street.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pairing honest, classic Hiroshima okonomiyaki with the best local Miyajima oysters, so the place works equally for a quick walking snack and for diners who come specifically for the oysters.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and Miyajima oysters. The standout is あげおこ, a deep-fried okonomiyaki made for eating on the move, offered in soba, squid, oyster, and cheese versions. The kitchen also leans hard into local oysters, using the premium Hiroshima 'Gokusen (極選)' brand for dishes like the oyster special (five large oysters piled on the okonomiyaki) and oyster ponzu with a house-blended ponzu.

anecdotes.text: The owner's own favorite build is an okonomiyaki with shrimp, squid, shiso (oba), and squid tempura bits, and he personally recommends the seseri (chicken neck) served with grated-radish and ponzu.
あげおこ (deep-fried okonomiyaki) has a crisp texture unlike ordinary okonomiyaki and is made for eating on the move — a popular grab-and-go for shrine visitors.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own site confirms まとちゃん occupies a converted 20+-year-old house near the Itsukushima Shrine exit; signatures are あげおこ (crispy deep-fried okonomiyaki for eating on the move, in soba/squid/oyster/cheese versions), premium Gokusen-brand oysters (oyster special = five large oysters; oyster ponzu with house-blended ponzu), the owner's shrimp-squid-shiso-tempura build, and seseri (chicken neck) with grated-radish ponzu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes (okonomiyaki batter, layered wheat noodles, deep-fried あげおこ, and breaded fried oysters) are all wheat-based and cooked on shared teppan/fryer surfaces; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki/Otafuku sauce and soy-sauce-based seasonings (e.g., ponzu), which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around egg, pork, oysters, squid, shrimp, and fish-based dashi/toppings cooked on a shared griddle; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual family-run okonomiyaki spot in a converted old house just off the Itsukushima Shrine path; English is not advertised, but pointing at the picture menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get a foreign student through comfortably.

places.name: 宮島鮨 天扇 (Miyajima Sushi Tensen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional sushi counter built around soy sauce (the kitchen pairs sushi with four soy-sauce styles including tamari and a tsuyu/dashi-soy blend, all conventional wheat-containing or dashi-based seasonings), plus dishes like seared Hiroshima beef and oyster soup. No gluten-free menu or wheat-free soy sauce is documented, and there is no dedicated fryer or GF handling on record.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu centers on raw and grilled Seto Inland Sea fish, Miyajima oysters, and Hiroshima beef, with fish-based dashi and soy sauces throughout. No vegan or vegetarian course is documented; meals are chef's-choice omakase.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (last entry 13:00, L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (last entry 20:30, L.O. 21:30). Closed Wednesdays, and the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, by the Omotesando shopping street (Itsukushima Shrine / Miyajima pier area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small hideaway-style sushi counter tucked into a quiet alley just off Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the Miyajima ferry pier. It opened in 2017 and seats guests at an intimate counter where the chef serves an omakase progression. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small hideaway-style sushi counter tucked into a quiet alley just off Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk (about five minutes) from the Miyajima ferry pier. It opened in 2017 and seats guests at an intimate counter where the chef serves an omakase progression.
chef_bio.philosophy: A devotion to local sourcing: the seasonal Seto Inland Sea fish, the wasabi, the rice, and the sake are all from Hiroshima Prefecture. The sushi rice is seasoned with a blend of rice vinegar and red vinegar, and rather than one all-purpose soy sauce the counter pairs each piece with one of four styles - sudachi soy, Tosa soy, tamari, and a tsuyu-style blend - chosen to match the ingredient.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fish of the Seto Inland Sea, served as nigiri and small plates, alongside signature local items such as seared Hiroshima beef and a soup made with Miyajima oysters.

anecdotes.text: Seasonal menus rotate with the catch; a winter lineup featured yellowtail, sea cucumber, oysters, and Japanese jack mackerel.
The kitchen deliberately switches between four different soy sauces - sudachi, Tosa, tamari, and a tsuyu blend - depending on which fish is being served.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hatsukaichi-concierge confirms 天扇 opened 2017, ~5 min from the ferry, a counter omakase using Seto-Inland-Sea fish, wasabi, rice and sake all from Hiroshima Prefecture; the shari blends rice and red vinegar; four soy sauces (sudachi, Tosa, tamari, tsuyu blend) are matched to each piece; specialties include seared Hiroshima beef and Miyajima oyster soup; a winter lineup featured yellowtail, sea cucumber, oysters and jack mackerel.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant pairs sushi with four soy-sauce styles (sudachi, Tosa, tamari, and a tsuyu/dashi-soy blend); these are conventional Japanese soy-based and dashi-based seasonings, none documented as gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely fish-, shellfish-, and meat-based (Seto Inland Sea fish, Miyajima oysters, Hiroshima beef) with fish dashi, so no separation from animal products is possible.

cultural_comfort.note: A reservation-only counter sushi-ya where the chef serves omakase course by course; a little Japanese and an understanding of sushi-counter etiquette will help, though staff are used to visitors to Miyajima.

places.name: 広島つけ麺 ぶちうま (Hiroshima Tsukemen Buchiuma)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is tsukemen — wheat-flour noodles dipped in a sauce built on soy sauce (which contains wheat). There are no documented gluten-free options.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The dipping broth is a three-blend of chicken-and-pork bone (鶏ガラ豚骨) with seafood (魚介) and tomato, and the dish is served with chashu (roast pork). The wheat noodles and soy-sauce-based dare make this unsuitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Hondori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Hiroshima-style tsukemen specialist tucked into the Wing Building on Tatemachi, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Hondori shopping districts and just steps from Tatemachi tram stop. It is reported to be the Hiroshima arrival of a well-known Tokyo tsukemen name, bri

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima-style tsukemen specialist tucked into the Wing Building on Tatemachi, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Hondori shopping districts and just steps from the Tatemachi tram stop. By night the same space becomes the restaurant Pul Dil Mare, so the tsukemen runs chiefly as a daytime operation.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style spicy tsukemen: chewy wheat noodles served with a dipping sauce blending pork-bone richness, seafood umami, and tomato, topped with juicy chashu, with diner-selectable chili heat.

anecdotes.text: The signature is a dipping sauce combining pork-bone richness with seafood umami and tomato, paired with juicy chashu, with spice levels customizable to the diner's preference.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog describes ぶちうま as an independent Hiroshima-style tsukemen shop (not a chain) in the Wing Building on Tatemachi (立町, ~116m from the tram stop) whose space becomes 'Pul Dil Mare' at night; the つけ麺 (¥1,100) has a 豚骨 (pork-bone) + seafood + tomato dipping sauce with customizable heat and chashu. Gnavi confirms the 中区立町6-1 ウイングビル1F address.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle tsukemen shop with soy-sauce-based dipping broth; gluten is central to the menu and there is no indication of gluten-free handling or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat); the broth is a chicken-pork-bone, seafood and tomato blend.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based (chicken-pork bone plus seafood) and the dish is topped with pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny lunch-only counter noodle shop where ordering is simple (point to the tsukemen and tell them your spice level); a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa will carry you through with no English expected.

noodles.notes: Cold, thin, firm straight noodles in the Hiroshima-tsukemen mold — closer to a chilled chuka-men, served chilled so they stay taut and snappy for dipping into the cool spicy sauce.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima-style cold tsukemen: chilled thin noodles, crisp cabbage and cucumber, and chashu, dipped into a cool deep-red chili-shoyu sauce. Choose your heat from 0 to 40 (around 5-10 is plenty for most), and finish with shime rice poured into the leftover sauce.
ramen.chashu: Low-temperature cooked pork, served pink and rare with a generous run of fat — tender and rich rather than braised or char-grilled, finished with a touch of pepper.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a shoyu (wheat) based dipping sauce; no gluten-free option documented.

vegan.note: The cool dipping sauce is built on animal and seafood dashi with a pork-forward bowl; no plant-broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Hiroshima tsukemen in its purest form — a regional cousin to mainstream hot dipping ramen where everything arrives cold and the soul lives in a chilled, fire-engine-red dipping sauce of shoyu, chili, rayu and white sesame over a light animal-and-seafood dashi. You taste Hiroshima's love of heat: a sauce that drinks clean up front, then ambushes you with a slow-building, sesame-rounded chili burn dialed anywhere from 0 to a punishing 40.

places.highlight: This is Hiroshima tsukemen in its purest form — a regional cousin to mainstream hot dipping ramen where everything arrives cold and the soul lives in a chilled, fire-engine-red dipping sauce of shoyu, chili, rayu and white sesame over a light animal-and-seafood dashi. You taste Hiroshima's love of heat: a sauce that drinks clean up front, then ambushes you with a slow-building, sesame-rounded chili burn dialed anywhere from 0 to a punishing 40.

places.name: 鳥居屋 (Toriiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature momiji croissant ("Momikuro") is made from wheat croissant dough, and momiji manju are wheat-and-egg sponge cakes. Local dishes such as anago-don (conger eel rice) and oyster dishes are served with standard wheat-based soy sauce and sauces. No dedicated gluten-free items or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Sweets contain dairy and egg (cream, cheese, chocolate croissant varieties; momiji manju use egg). Savory menu centers on conger eel (anago) and oysters, which are not vegan, and uses fish/wheat-soy-based seasonings. No documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 8:30-17:00 (shop). The dining area is roughly 10:30-16:00. Hours vary during busy seasons (April-June, September-November).
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (along the approach to Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Toriiya sits right on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, on the lively path that runs from the ferry pier toward the great torii and Itsukushima Shrine. It works as a tourism center: the ground floor is a souvenir shop where momiji manju (maple-leaf-shaped cakes) ar (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Toriiya sits right on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, on the lively path that runs from the ferry pier toward the great torii and Itsukushima Shrine. It works as a tourism center (観光センター): the ground floor is a souvenir shop where momiji manju (maple-leaf-shaped cakes) are sold, while the 2nd and 3rd floors host dining rooms for groups, with the in-house eatery serving Hiroshima specialties such as anago-don.
chef_bio.philosophy: The 'momiji croissant' is made as a hand-held food-walk (tabe-aruki) snack to enjoy while strolling the arcade, and its fillings are built on the shop's own house-made an (sweet bean paste) rather than bought-in paste.
chef_bio.specialty: The shop is best known for the 'momiji croissant' nicknamed 'Momikuro' (もみクロ), sold at its storefront for snacking as you stroll. A square of crisp croissant dough frames a soft maple-leaf-shaped center, offered in six standing varieties — red bean, cream/custard, apple, chocolate, cheese, and one rotating seasonal flavor — with the recommended tsubu-an using the shop's house-made anko.

anecdotes.text: The Momikuro is a media-featured 'new specialty' of the shop and is described as palm-sized (手のひらサイズ), so it satisfies without filling you up before other island treats.
It comes in six standard flavors, and the shop's own house-made anko is used for its recommended red-bean (tsubu-an) filling, setting it apart from ordinary momiji manju.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site and Tabetainjya food-walk blog: Toriiya is a Miyajima tourism center (souvenirs + upstairs group dining serving anago-don); its Momikuro momiji croissant comes in six flavors (red bean, cream, apple, chocolate, cheese, seasonal), palm-sized, with house-made anko in the recommended red-bean version.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory island dishes such as anago-don and oyster dishes are served with standard Japanese seasonings, which typically include wheat-based soy sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, tourist-friendly arcade shop where you can simply point at a croissant and pay at the counter, so a few words of Japanese and a smile are all you need to enjoy a food-walk snack.

places.name: 木村家本店 (Kimuraya Honten)
places.gf_detail: Momiji manju are made from a wheat-flour batter, so they are not gluten-free. No gluten-free products are documented at this shop.
places.vegan_detail: The classic red-bean (koshi-an / tsubu-an) momiji manju centers on sweet adzuki paste, but the manju batter and cake-style varieties typically contain egg, and the shop also makes cheese and cream fillings that are not vegan. No certified vegan items are documented; a strict vegan would need to ask whether any specific manju is made without egg, dairy, or honey.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-17:00 (9:00-21:00 during the busy spring and autumn seasons). Another source lists 9:30-18:30. Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, entrance of the Omotesando shopping street (approach to Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1933, Kimuraya Honten is a family-run momiji manju shop standing on a prominent corner right where the Omotesando shopping street begins on the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. For generations it has made its maple-leaf cakes by hand, and visitors can watch th (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1933, Kimuraya Honten is a family-run momiji manju shop standing on a prominent corner right where the Omotesando shopping street begins on the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. For generations it has made its maple-leaf cakes by hand, and visitors can watch the manju being baked right at the storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop deliberately keeps its original formulation and traditional dough, making its momiji manju without emulsifiers or additives — reportedly one of the only makers on Miyajima to do so, which appeals to families and additive-conscious visitors.
chef_bio.specialty: Momiji manju with a notably firm, chewy dough that sets it apart from the softer versions sold elsewhere on the island, filled with a sweet, sticky adzuki bean paste made to the original recipe. Beyond the classic red-bean version, the shop offers fillings such as soy-milk cream, cheese, raisin, pumpkin, and chestnut.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor describes keeping the recipe unchanged: making the manju with the traditional dough and without emulsifiers ('配合を変えず昔ながらの生地にこだわり、乳化剤等を使用せず製造').
The firm dough and unchanged-since-opening sweet bean paste give the cakes a distinctly old-fashioned Miyajima character, best enjoyed with green tea.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Oyakode-tanoshimou blog: Kimuraya Honten, est. 1933, corner shop at the start of Omotesando; firm chewy dough, emulsifier/additive-free (reportedly the only such maker on Miyajima), fillings include soy-milk cream, cheese, raisin, pumpkin, chestnut.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All momiji manju are baked from a wheat-flour batter, so the entire product line contains gluten; there is no gluten-free option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop makes dairy-based fillings (cheese, soy-milk cream) alongside the bean-paste varieties in the same small operation, so cross-contact for a strict vegan cannot be ruled out.

positives.text: Momiji manju here are made without emulsifiers or additives, keeping the original traditional recipe.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly traditional sweets shop on the Miyajima approach where you can point at what you want and buy individual momiji manju; a smile and basic Japanese greetings go a long way, and English is limited.

places.name: 山代屋 (Yamashiroya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The signature anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) is glazed/seasoned with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and udon is a wheat noodle, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No dedicated GF preparation or fryer documented.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on conger eel (anago), grilled oysters, and seafood; no vegan dishes documented. Soy-sauce-based seasonings and fish-based dishes make this unsuitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (daily); may close early once sold out
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Omotesando area, in front of
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yamashiroya sits right at the heart of Miyajima's approach, directly in front of the Karamon gate of Itsukushima Shrine, occupying a renovated old house with tatami-room seating that gives the place a calm, traditional feel amid the busy Omotesando shopping street. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Yamashiroya sits right at the heart of Miyajima's approach, directly in front of the Karamon gate of Itsukushima Shrine, occupying a renovated old house with tatami-room seating that gives the place a calm, traditional feel amid the busy Omotesando shopping street.
chef_bio.specialty: Anagomeshi — locally caught conger eel grilled and laid over rice — is the house dish, served alongside grilled Setouchi oysters and udon.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is housed in a renovated former residence and keeps a deliberately short menu focused on anagomeshi, generous portions, and grilled oysters; it was established in 2004 and is run by a small team of about four (owner, one kitchen hand, two servers).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog: Yamashiroya sits in front of Itsukushima Shrine's Karamon gate in a renovated old house with tatami seating; specializes in anagomeshi, grilled Setouchi oysters and udon; established 2004; run by about four people; open 11:00-16:00, no reservations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Anago is finished with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare and udon is a wheat noodle, so gluten is present throughout the core menu; no GF handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anagomeshi glaze/seasoning is soy-sauce based, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on conger eel and oysters; no vegan-safe preparation indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A small tourist-area eatery with a short, photo-friendly menu (anagomeshi, oysters); basic Japanese greetings help but pointing at the limited menu works fine, and walk-in only means no reservation hurdle.

places.name: 坂本菓子舗 (Sakamoto Kashiho)
places.gf_detail: Momiji-manju are made from a wheat-flour castella sponge, and the seasonal and salted-butter varieties are likewise wheat-based, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options or allergy accommodation are documented.
places.vegan_detail: The classic anko momiji uses house-made azuki bean paste, which can be plant-based, but the castella sponge of momiji-manju typically contains egg and the signature salted-butter momiji contains dairy butter. No ingredient list confirms egg-free batter, so a vegan visitor would need to ask directly. Treat as ask, not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Shop 09:00-17:00; tea space 10:00-16:00 (opens around 12:00 on days following a rest day)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima island, on a side street one block off the Omotesan
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sakamoto Kashiho is a family-run sweets shop on Miyajima that has been baking momiji-manju since 1955, more than seventy years of single-family craft. It sits a block off the bustling Omotesando arcade, a quieter spot where visitors can step away from the crowds about t (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Sakamoto Kashiho is a family-run sweets shop on Miyajima that has been baking momiji-manju since 1955, more than seventy years of single-family craft. It sits a block off the bustling Omotesando arcade, a quieter spot where visitors can step away from the crowds about ten minutes from Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than buying in pre-made fillings, the shop makes everything by hand: the father personally crafts both the bean paste and the castella sponge, carefully removing the skins of the azuki beans to produce a refined paste with a gentle sweetness and smooth texture.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-baked momiji-manju with house-made azuki bean paste, and a signature filling-free salted-butter momiji.

anecdotes.text: The signature salted-butter momiji breaks from tradition with no anko at all, instead kneading French sea salt and New Zealand butter into the fluffy castella sponge for an entirely different flavor; it is the shop's most popular (No.1) item.
Seasonal momiji rotate through the year: cherry-leaf-scented sakura anko in spring, Uji matcha with whole azuki in summer, chestnut-cream Mont Blanc in fall, and roasted sweet-potato paste in winter.
A small tea space (about 14 seats) lets guests eat the momiji fresh from the oven, warm and fluffy, with complimentary self-serve tea.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japan-stroll gourmet blog: Sakamoto Kashiho, est. 1955, off Omotesando (~10 min from the shrine); father hand-makes bean paste and castella, peeling azuki skins; No.1 salted-butter momiji (French salt, NZ butter, no filling); seasonal flavors; ~14-seat space with free self-serve tea.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run sweets shop tucked off the main arcade; a friendly greeting and pointing at the freshly baked momiji you want will carry you through, though detailed allergy questions may be hard without some Japanese.

places.name: つけ麺本舗 辛部 十日市店 (Tsukemen Honpo Karabu Tokaichi-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen shop; the dipping noodles are wheat, and the spicy dipping broth uses soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and other seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiac/strict GF diners.
places.vegan_detail: The tsukemen comes standard with chashu pork, and the dipping broth is built on a meat/fish base with wheat-based soy seasoning. Toppings are cabbage, cucumber, negi, nori, and dried bonito. No vegan option is documented; not suitable for vegans or strict vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:45), 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:45); evening service ends when sold out. Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku Tokaichimachi, central Hiroshima (near Tokaichimachi
places.notes: Karabu (辛部) is one of the standard-bearers of Hiroshima-style tsukemen, the city's spicy cold dipping-noodle dish. Though it arrived after the genre's pioneers, it won a following on the strength of its noodles and broth and grew into a small chain with several Hiroshim

roles: Original main store of the Karabu group
chef_bio.background: Karabu (辛部) is one of the standard-bearers of Hiroshima-style tsukemen, the city's spicy cold dipping-noodle dish. Though it arrived after the genre's pioneers, it won a following on the strength of its noodles and broth and grew into a small chain with several Hiroshima locations; the Tokaichi shop is the original main store (十日市本店).
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima tsukemen: firm, springy wheat noodles served cold alongside a clean, spicy dipping broth, dressed with cabbage, cucumber, negi, nori or bonito, and chashu, with a customizable heat scale.

anecdotes.text: The spice level is set on a wide scale from 0 up to around 30, with a signature stick-style heat gauge; locals note that even '10' lands only just short of medium-hot.
The noodles are described as firm with a crunchy bite and the broth as simple and clean enough to drink down, drawing many female customers and strong local support.
Regarded as one of the representative Hiroshima tsukemen names, comparable in recognition and scale to other major local specialists, having expanded to multiple branches across the city.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Modokimaru and Syokuki blogs: Karabu is a representative Hiroshima tsukemen shop, the Tokaichi location being the honten; spice runs 0-30 on a stick gauge; firm crunchy noodles and a clean drinkable broth; many female customers; expanded to roughly six branches across Hiroshima.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the core product, so flour and wheat are pervasive in the kitchen; no GF preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is seasoned with soy sauce, which in this style is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free soy alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on meat/fish broth and topped with pork chashu and bonito, so shared cookware and broth make vegan service impractical.

cultural_comfort.note: Ordering is by ticket machine and the menu is mostly in Japanese, so glance at the posted menu outside and learn the spice scale before stepping up to order.

noodles.notes: Custom-made noodles with a notably smooth, springy, chewy bite. Rinsed quickly and thoroughly in cold water to strip surface starch and keep the surface intact, so they stay slick and firm through the meal. Served chilled for dipping.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけ麺) with chili level chosen 0 to 30 — 15 to 20 is the house sweet spot balancing heat and umami; add the crumbled nori topping
ramen.chashu: Sliced pork chashu served alongside the cold noodles; standard braised-style pork slices (not aburi/char-grilled)

gf.note: Wheat noodles with kansui and a soy-sauce (wheat) seasoned dipping broth; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on fish/bonito (katsuo) seafood dashi and the bowl comes with pork chashu; no documented plant-only broth. The 0x spice level removes chili but not the animal-derived base.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of the Hiroshima tsukemen lineage: chilled springy noodles dipped into a cold, soy-and-seafood (bonito-forward) sauce charged with the city's signature chili heat, scaling from gentle to ferocious. Sweet raw cabbage and green onion are the native garnish, cooling the burn and keeping the bowl bright rather than heavy.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of the Hiroshima tsukemen lineage: chilled springy noodles dipped into a cold, soy-and-seafood (bonito-forward) sauce charged with the city's signature chili heat, scaling from gentle to ferocious. Sweet raw cabbage and green onion are the native garnish, cooling the burn and keeping the bowl bright rather than heavy.

places.name: わかば亭 (Wakabatei)
places.gf_detail: This is a tsukemen shop: the noodles are wheat and the spicy dipping broth uses soy sauce (wheat-based) and fish dashi, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The signature spicy dipping broth is built on bonito/seafood (fish) dashi, so the tsukemen is not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; vegetable toppings exist but the broth itself contains fish.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-14:30, 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat 11:30-14:30; Sun closed. Also closed during Obon and New Year holidays.
places.neighborhood: Takanobashi shopping street, Otemachi, Naka-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter-only tsukemen shop tucked into the Takanobashi shopping street in central Hiroshima, opened as Hiroshima-style spicy dipping noodles grew in popularity. It is run as a family operation, with the proprietress tending the broth and a partner helping with s

chef_bio.background: A small counter-only tsukemen shop tucked into the Takanobashi shopping street in central Hiroshima, opened as Hiroshima-style spicy dipping noodles grew in popularity. It is run as a family operation by a couple, with the proprietress tending the broth and a partner helping with service; the room is an L-shaped counter of about ten seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: A restrained, seafood-forward dipping broth with the spice dialed exactly to the diner's request, served in an intimate counter setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: thin wheat noodles served with a cold, seafood-and-bonito dipping broth that you can order at a customizable heat, from no spice up to eye-watering challenge levels.

anecdotes.text: The shop is famous for its escalating spice levels; diners who conquer the most extreme heat have their names posted in the shop, and a 'Hell Ramen' at the top spice level (with multiplier challenges reaching 150x and 200x) is offered as a challenge even regulars find brutal.
The broth leans on bonito dashi with restrained acidity, and the noodles are noticeably thinner than at rival Hiroshima tsukemen shops.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Oomin77 blog: Wakabatei on Takanobashi shopping street, L-shaped counter (~10 seats) run by a couple; famous for escalating spice with conquerors' names posted, a 'Hell Ramen' and 150x/200x multiplier challenges; bonito-forward broth with restrained acidity; noodles thinner than rivals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat noodles boiled in shared water and a soy-sauce-based dipping broth, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima tsukemen dipping broth is soy-sauce based, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is made with fish/bonito and seafood dashi, so all tsukemen contains animal-derived stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 12-seat counter shop run by the owners; ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English, so knowing a few Japanese words for ordering and your desired spice level helps.

noodles.notes: Medium, straight noodles served chilled, with a slightly soft, smooth bite; eaten by dipping into the cold spicy fish-soy sauce in classic Hiroshima tsukemen fashion.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima-style cold tsukemen (chilled noodles with the spicy dipping sauce) — pick your size from small to extra-large and dial in the chili level, from a gentle 1x up through the legendary 100x and beyond (150x/200x for the truly fearless).
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu served alongside the cold noodles and vegetables.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based (wheat) dipping sauce mean this is not celiac-safe; no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The cold dipping sauce is built on bonito/dried-fish dashi and the bowl is served with pork chashu, so it is not vegan; no documented plant-only broth.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima tsukemen, where the soul lives not in a hot pot but in a chilled, ruby-red dipping sauce of bonito-rich fish dashi, soy, and roasted chili — bracingly cold noodles meet building, tongue-numbing heat. The graduated spice scale, climbing all the way to a notorious 100x (and beyond), turns the city's signature style into a personal endurance ritual.

places.highlight: A standard-bearer of Hiroshima tsukemen, where the soul lives not in a hot pot but in a chilled, ruby-red dipping sauce of bonito-rich fish dashi, soy, and roasted chili — bracingly cold noodles meet building, tongue-numbing heat. The graduated spice scale, climbing all the way to a notorious 100x (and beyond), turns the city's signature style into a personal endurance ritual.

places.name: インド料理 ルーパリ 若草町店（本店） (Indo Ryori Roopali Wakakusacho-ten (Honten))
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. Naan and the breads are wheat-based, and the popular set lunches center on naan, so they are not gluten-free. Turmeric/saffron rice with a curry could be a starting point, but Indian curries here may use wheat-thickened or dairy bases and no celiac-safe handling is stated. A coeliac should phone ahead (082-264-1333) and confirm dish by dish; assume cross-contact in a busy shared kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: As a North Indian restaurant it carries genuine meat-free items: vegetable salad with beans, vegetable pakora (Indian-style vegetable tempura), and vegetable/chickpea-style curries. However these are not certified vegan — paneer (cheese) pakora is on the menu, and curries and naan commonly use ghee, butter, cream, yogurt or milk. Ask for a fully plant-based vegetable or dal curry without dairy, and pair with plain rice rather than naan (naan often contains milk/yogurt). Vegetarian is easy here; strict vegan requires asking the staff to omit dairy.
places.neighborhood: Wakakusacho, Higashi Ward, Hiroshima (Shinkansen exit / Wakakusacho side of Hiroshima Stn, ~5 min walk)
places.notes: A long-running Indian restaurant a short walk from Hiroshima Station, where much of the kitchen and floor staff are from India, giving the food and the room an authentic feel. It has become a neighborhood fixture, especially at lunch, when a queue often forms for its ge

chef_bio.background: A long-running Indian restaurant a short walk from Hiroshima Station (Wakakusacho, Higashi-ku), where much of the kitchen and floor staff are from India, giving the food and the room an authentic feel. It has become a neighborhood fixture, especially at lunch, when a queue often forms for its generous curry sets.
chef_bio.specialty: North Indian curries cooked with Indian spices, served with freshly baked naan and rice. The set lunches let you choose from several curries (including the Roopali Special Set with tandoori items) with free refills of naan and rice.

anecdotes.text: At lunchtime a line forms outside for the choose-your-curry sets, a sign of how the spot has been embraced locally.
Diners pick their favorites from several curries and the naan and rice can be refilled, which is part of its everyday appeal.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Syokuki and Tabelog: Roopali Wakakusacho honten near Hiroshima Station; lunchtime queues for choose-your-curry sets; Roopali Special Set pairs curry with naan, rice and tandoori items; North Indian curries with freshly baked naan; open 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-21:30.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan and the bread-based sets are wheat; in a shared Indian kitchen with flour and fried items, cross-contact with wheat is likely and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable pakora is fried alongside paneer (cheese) pakora, and curries/naan commonly use ghee, butter, cream or yogurt, so plant-based dishes may share oil and dairy unless you ask staff to omit them.

positives.text: Genuine meat-free items exist, including vegetable salad with beans, vegetable pakora, and vegetable/chickpea-style curries, so vegetarians have real choices.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, foreigner-friendly Indian restaurant where much of the staff is from India and English is often understood, so a foreign student can order easily; a few words of Japanese or simple English both work fine.

places.name: グラフィティ (Graffity)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Core dishes (burritos, soft tacos, enchiladas, chimichangas) are built on wheat-flour tortillas; fried items like fish tacos and chimichangas use battered/deep-fried components, and there is no documented dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol. No gluten-free menu or claims found on any source. Corn-tortilla or naturally GF options may be possible to ask about in person, but cross-contact cannot be assumed safe.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore, meat-forward diner (beef, chicken, catfish), but it carries several vegetarian dishes and staff have said they can make them vegan on request. Everything (salsa, guacamole) is made from scratch, which helps with customization. There is no dedicated vegan menu, so vegans should communicate clearly when ordering and confirm no animal products (cheese, sour cream, lard) are used.
places.hours_raw: Mon 17:00-00:00; Tue closed; Wed 17:00-00:00; Thu 17:00-22:00; Fri 17:00-23:00; Sat 11:30-14:00 (LO 13:15) & 17:00-23:00; Sun 11:30-14:00 (LO 13:15) & 18:00-23:00; holidays lunch 11:30-14:00. Lunch service is sometimes closed for food prep; dinner is the main service. (Sources vary slightly on exact closing times.)
places.neighborhood: 袋町・並木通り (中区), central Hiroshima near Fukuromachi Park
places.notes: Graffity is a San Diego-inspired Mexican and California-style diner in central Hiroshima, run by the Nakashima brothers and going strong for around 17 years. Chef Kai developed his Mexican and Tex-Mex cooking during five years working in the San Diego area before bringi

chef_bio.origin: California / San Diego-style Mexican and Tex-Mex, adapted in Hiroshima
chef_bio.background: Graffity is a San Diego-inspired Mexican and California-style diner in central Hiroshima's Fukuromachi, run by two brothers and going strong since it opened in October 2009 (around 17 years). Chef Kai developed his Mexican and Tex-Mex cooking during five years working in the San Diego area before bringing that style home to Hiroshima.
chef_bio.philosophy: A from-scratch kitchen: every dish and condiment is made in-house so the flavors are as full and fresh as they should be, exemplified by the super-fresh salsa and guacamole.
chef_bio.specialty: Burritos, enchiladas and soft tacos with grilled chicken or beef, fish tacos and Cajun-grilled catfish, alongside the kitchen's own fresh salsa and guacamole

anecdotes.text: Chef Kai picked up his ideas about Mexican and Tex-Mex food during five years working in the San Diego area, and makes every dish and condiment from scratch.
The restaurant is run by two friendly brothers and has been a popular, expat-loved spot, with a fun casual space, sofas by the windows, and counter seating.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: GetHiroshima and Hiroshima Keizai Shimbun: Graffity is a brothers-run Mexican/American (California-style) diner in Fukuromachi, opened Oct 2009; the chef learned his craft over five years in the San Diego area and makes everything from scratch (fresh salsa and guacamole).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dishes are built on wheat-flour tortillas (burritos, soft tacos, enchiladas) and several items are battered and deep-fried (fish tacos, chimichangas), so gluten exposure and shared-fryer cross-contact are likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian dishes can reportedly be made vegan on request, but Mexican dishes here commonly include cheese and sour cream and the kitchen is meat/fish-heavy, so confirm omission of dairy and animal fats when ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as very friendly and have indicated they can adapt vegetarian dishes to vegan, suggesting willingness to accommodate requests communicated at the table.

positives.text: Everything is made from scratch in-house, including the fresh salsa and guacamole, which makes plant-based customization easier to request.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, expat-friendly Mexican diner where the staff are known to be very welcoming and English is workable, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about vegetarian or vegan adaptations.

places.name: お好み焼き・鉄板串焼き やまだ (Okonomiyaki Teppan Kushiyaki Yamada)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and topped with fresh wheat noodles (肉玉そば) and okonomiyaki sauce, which is typically wheat/soy-based. No gluten-free batter, noodle, or sauce alternatives are documented, and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free preparation area.
places.vegan_detail: Largely not vegan. Core dishes use egg, pork, beef, chicken, and local oysters, and okonomiyaki includes egg plus likely fish/bonito-derived sauce. The menu page notes some vegetarian options exist, but nothing is documented as confirmed vegan (no specified plant-based broth or sauce), so a vegan diner would need to ask in person about specific items.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka Ward (~5 min walk from Kamiyacho-nishi / Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent teppan izakaya that opened in 2018, tucked into an alley behind the Edion main-store west building and about a minute on foot from the Kamiyachō-nishi tram stop, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park.

chef_bio.background: A small independent teppan izakaya that opened in December 2018 in Hiroshima's Otemachi district, near the Edion main store and a short walk from the Kamiyachō-nishi tram stop and the Peace Memorial Park. Owner-chef Junji Yamada trained for about seven years at a well-known okonomiyaki restaurant before striking out on his own.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes its own touches on the classics, using fresh noodles seasoned with an original paitan (chicken-bone) broth that are meant to stand up well to okonomiyaki sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki paired with a wide range of teppan-grilled skewers, letting guests enjoy two of the city's signature ways of cooking on the iron griddle in one sitting.

anecdotes.text: The house okonomiyaki, やまだ焼き, layers in squid tempura, shiso (perilla) leaf, and green onion, with deluxe and special versions adding cheese, garlic chips, kimchi, and scallion.

Skewers run from shiso-wrapped pork and chicken-cheese to beef harami and ponzu-dressed gizzard, and a casual 'choi-nomi' set pairs a drink with a small dish and three skewers for easy after-work grazing.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog: Owner-chef 山田淳司 (Junji Yamada) opened this okonomiyaki/teppan-kushiyaki shop on 3 Dec 2018 in Otemachi (Fabrica Bldg), ~3 min from the Kamiyachō-nishi tram stop; he trained about seven years at a well-known okonomiyaki shop; menu pairs Hiroshima okonomiyaki with teppan skewers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter and the topping noodles are wheat-based and cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside many other dishes, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is typically wheat/soy-based; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, beef, chicken, egg, and local oysters are cooked on the same shared teppan as any vegetable dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

positives.text: The menu page notes that some vegetarian options are available, so a flexitarian diner may find a few plant-leaning dishes by asking.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, locally run evening teppan izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will go a long way; note one order per person is required.

places.name: 勝ちゃん (Katsuchan)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. This is not a gluten-free-friendly venue and no gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki centers on egg, pork, and wheat noodles, and dishes use animal ingredients and wheat-based sauce. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Thu 17:00-04:00; Fri-Sat 17:00-06:00; Sun & holidays 17:00-01:00; Monday closed
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka Ward (Hatchobori / Nagarekawa area, heart of the entertainment district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop that opened in 2013 in the heart of Hiroshima's downtown nightlife district, run by an energetic owner with a small staff. It keeps long late-night hours, drawing locals after work and after a night out.

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop that opened in October 2013 in the heart of Hiroshima's downtown Yagenbori nightlife district, run by an owner with two staff. It keeps long late-night hours, drawing locals after work and after a night out.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles, prized for a pleasantly crisp finish; the soba-niku-tama (noodle, pork and egg) is a house favorite.

anecdotes.text: Known among regulars for a lively, welcoming atmosphere and a crisp-edged okonomiyaki, served late into the night in the Yagenbori entertainment quarter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog: Katsuchan opened Oct 2013 in Yagenbori (YG Bldg 1F); Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with a pleasantly crisp finish, soba-niku-tama popular; run by the owner plus two staff; late-night hours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared on a shared griddle with egg and pork, so cross-contact with animal products is expected.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a lively, late-night local okonomiyaki spot where ordering is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help a first-time foreign visitor.

places.name: YUBU（ユブ） (YUBU)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is built on a wheat-flour batter, topped with wheat noodles (fresh ramen from Iso Seimen), tenkasu (fried wheat batter bits), and finished with wheat-containing okonomiyaki/Carp-brand sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, and the single shared teppan means heavy cross-contact with wheat.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki is made with generous fish powder (魚粉), pork belly, and egg, and the noodles are seasoned with dashi on the griddle; the sauce is standard (non-vegan) okonomiyaki sauce. The menu centers on pork-and-egg-and-noodle okonomiyaki, with no documented vegan option.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog (current): Tue/Wed/Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); closed Mon, Thu, Sat, Sun, and holidays. (An older blog noted lunch 11:30-13:30 plus dinner 17:30-19:30 with noodle dishes from 13:00 — hours have varied.)
places.neighborhood: Teppocho, Naka Ward (near Hatchobori, ~3 min walk from Ebisucho tram stop on the Hiroden line)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing, family-run okonomiyaki and steak-house counter in central Hiroshima's Teppocho, just a short walk from the Hatchobori district. It is run as a small team built around the owner, with a daughter and a couple of staff working the griddle.

chef_bio.background: A long-standing, family-run okonomiyaki and steak-house counter in central Hiroshima's Teppocho, just a short walk from the Hatchobori district. It is run as a small team built around the owner.
chef_bio.philosophy: A minimal, ingredient-forward approach described as okonomiyaki 'made with the eater in mind' rather than relying on heavy technique.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked with a distinctive light touch: a slightly thick batter is laid down and topped with plenty of fish powder, cabbage, salt and pepper, tenkasu, rinsed bean sprouts, and two slices of pork belly. The cook uses no binding agents, spreads lard onto the griddle rather than over the food, and does not press the okonomiyaki down with the spatula, instead letting high heat steam it so it stays voluminous and never squeezed dry. The fresh noodles (from Isono Seimen) are seasoned right on the griddle with dashi, salt, and garlic powder rather than being pre-sauced.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known as a place former Prime Minister Fumio Kishida frequents when in Hiroshima, and his visits have been featured on national news.

Reviewers note the okonomiyaki is never pressed flat with the spatula, leaving it light and full-bodied rather than dense (high heat steams it), and the griddled Isono Seimen noodles are seasoned with dashi, salt, and garlic powder instead of sauce.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Isono Seimen shop directory confirms YUBU is a Kishida haunt aired on national news; Oomin77 blog details the light-touch method — no binding agents, lard on the griddle, no pressing (steamed by high heat), Isono Seimen noodles seasoned on the griddle with dashi, salt and garlic powder, layered with fish powder, cabbage, tenkasu, rinsed bean sprouts and two pork-belly slices.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan griddle used for wheat-batter okonomiyaki, wheat noodles, and tenkasu (fried wheat bits), creating heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Finished with standard okonomiyaki/Carp-brand sauce, which is wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Okonomiyaki uses fish powder, pork belly, and egg, and the noodles are seasoned with dashi on the same griddle, so no dish is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only, no-reservation local counter shop with limited open days and no English menu noted, so some basic Japanese and flexibility with the short hours will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.

places.name: お食事処 新京本店 (Oshokujidokoro Shinkyo Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature curry soup uses a wheat-flour curry roux, and side dishes use wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The curry soup contains beef and beef tendon and a fish-based dashi (kombu, iriko, katsuo); side dishes include meat, fish roe and egg. No plant-based options documented.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–06:00 next day (Mon–Sat); closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa-cho, Naka-ku (Nagarekawa entertainment district,
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop began in 1945 as a nameless stall a couple opened in the black market in front of Hiroshima Station right after the atomic bombing. After an eviction it moved to the Nagarekawa entertainment district, and after a fire it settled at its current location, where i

chef_bio.background: The shop began in 1945 as a nameless stall a couple opened in the black market in front of Hiroshima Station right after the atomic bombing. After an eviction it moved to the Nagarekawa entertainment district, and after a fire it settled at its current location, where it has now run for about 41 years. Third-generation owner Kono Noriaki (河野憲昭) worked as a banker until 55, then took early retirement to carry on the family business, standing at the counter twelve hours a day except Sundays.
chef_bio.philosophy: Old-fashioned home cooking with simple seasoning that never tires the palate, so regulars keep returning; about forty pre-made side dishes are served instantly, a style born from early customers who were rushed taxi drivers and nightlife workers.
chef_bio.specialty: Kare-jiru (curry soup): a light, soothing thinned curry simmered with potato, onion, carrot, beef and beef tendon, finished with a house dashi of kombu, sardine and bonito.

anecdotes.text: The curry soup was born about forty years ago when the owner's brother-in-law, a heavy drinker, asked for curry thinned out because curry rice was too heavy; the owner's sister Tomoko recalled a school-lunch curry soup and finished it by thinning with dashi. Turmeric is added generously as a hangover soother for the late-night drinking crowd.

The kitchen effectively runs around the clock: the owner's sister Tomoko preps from 6 a.m. to evening, then hands off to Kono for the 6 p.m.-6 a.m. service, with clientele shifting from salarymen to bar staff to small-business owners through the night.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hiroshima Home-TV feature: Shinkyo began 1945 as a nameless black-market stall by Hiroshima Station, moved to Nagarekawa after eviction, then its current site (~41 yrs); third-gen owner 河野憲昭 (ex-banker to 55) works 6pm-6am; kare-jiru created by sister Tomoko (school-lunch memory, dashi-thinned, half spicy/half mild roux, turmeric for hangovers), homemade kombu/sardine/bonito dashi; near-24h operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Home-style braised side dishes and dashi-based dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature curry soup contains beef and beef tendon and a fish-based dashi (kombu, iriko, katsuo), so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

cultural_comfort.note: A deep-night counter spot run by an elderly proprietor where regulars order in Japanese by pointing at rows of pre-made side dishes; manageable by pointing but a very local, no-English atmosphere.

places.name: みのる食堂 エキエ広島店 (Minoru Shokudo Ekie Hiroshima)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on teishoku with hamburg steak, fried items, tempura, and soy-sauce/miso-based seasonings, all of which typically contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer is indicated.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The concept and signature dishes are built around meat and fish — Hiroshima wagyu and pork hamburg, wagyu steak, and other animal-based mains — with soy-sauce and dashi-based seasoning. While some vegetable dishes (e.g., tomato-miso roll cabbage, vegetable tempura) exist, there is no documented vegan menu or vegan-safe preparation.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (some sources list 11:00-23:00). Irregular days off (follows Ekie's schedule)
places.neighborhood: 南区松原町（JR広島駅ビル ekie 1F）
places.cuisine: Set-meal diner using local ingredients from Hiroshima and the Chugoku-Shikoku region
places.notes: The restaurant is part of the みのりみのるプロジェクト (Minori Minoru Project), launched in 2009 by JA Zennoh, the national federation of Japan's agricultural cooperatives. The project was created so that producers and consumers could connect through food and farming, showcasing do

chef_bio.origin: Opened March 29, 2018 at the ekie commercial complex inside JR Hiroshima Station, on the first floor.
chef_bio.background: The restaurant is part of the みのりみのるプロジェクト (Minori Minoru Project), launched in 2010 by JA Zennoh, the national federation of Japan's agricultural cooperatives, with its first shops opening that September at Ginza Mitsukoshi in Tokyo. The Hiroshima ekie location is the seventh in the series. The project was created so that producers and consumers could connect through food and farming, showcasing domestic, seasonal regional produce.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on the idea of 'producers and consumers thinking of one another through food and agriculture,' emphasizing local-production-for-local-consumption (地産地消) and regional revitalization, here highlighting Hiroshima and the broader Chugoku and Shikoku regions.
chef_bio.specialty: Teishoku set meals built around local ingredients — the signature みのる定食 lets diners choose two mains from a rotating lineup, served with rice and miso soup. Standout dishes include a Hiroshima wagyu-and-pork hamburg developed with JA Zennoh Meat Foods and steak/rice bowls featuring Hiroshima wagyu branded 'Motonari'.

anecdotes.text: When the Hiroshima location opened in 2018, the concept was to start with Hiroshima Prefecture ingredients and progressively expand to feature products from across the Chugoku and Shikoku regions.
The flagship みのる定食 is a build-your-own set: pick two mains from a lineup that mixes everyday staples with seasonal specials, paired with rice and miso soup.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, easy-to-enter set-meal restaurant inside the Hiroshima Station ekie complex with picture-friendly teishoku menus, so a foreign student can manage with basic Japanese greetings and pointing, though staff English may be limited.

places.name: 米米食堂 (Komekome Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu centers on breaded chicken cutlet, karaage and tempura-style fried chicken (wheat batter and flour coating), and dishes are seasoned with regular wheat-based soy sauce and sauces. There is no dedicated fryer and no gluten-free menu, so this is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. The menu is built around chicken (karaage, cutlet, chicken nanban), meat-and-vegetable, and fish (hokke/mackerel) set meals, with egg used in some dishes and animal-based dashi and soy sauce throughout. No documented plant-based set meal.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat 11:00-22:00; Sun 11:00-21:30; Wed & Thu closed (per Tabelog). Note: Gurunavi lists 11:00-21:40 (L.O. 22:00) Mon-Sat and 11:00-21:10 (L.O. 21:30) Sun/holidays with no fixed closed day — hours and closed days vary by source.
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho / Tatemachi, Naka Ward (near Hondori / Parco East Building, within walking distance of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, home-style set-meal counter in the heart of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, built around carefully cooked locally grown rice served fresh each day with free refills alongside its set meals.

chef_bio.background: A small, home-style set-meal counter in the heart of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, built around carefully cooked locally grown rice served fresh each day with free refills alongside its set meals.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, welcoming everyday table where even solo diners, including women eating alone, feel comfortable, with rice treated as the centerpiece of the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Set meals of salt-marinated karaage (fried chicken), chicken cutlet, chicken nanban, and grilled fish, served with generous rice that can be refilled.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Hiroshima-grown rice cooked with care daily, with free rice refills on set meals, framing the spot as an honest neighborhood table rather than a destination restaurant.
Ordering runs through a touch-panel ticket machine before you sit, keeping the busy lunch rush moving quickly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried chicken, karaage and breaded cutlets are cooked in shared oil with wheat-coated items, so gluten cross-contamination in the fryer is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals are seasoned in the standard Japanese teishoku style with wheat-based soy sauce and sauces.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is dominated by chicken, meat and fish dishes with egg and animal dashi; no plant-based separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local set-meal counter where you order at a touch-panel ticket machine and pay cash, so a foreign student can manage with minimal Japanese, but staff communication is in Japanese and there is no English menu, so bringing a translation app or this guide helps.

places.name: お好み焼 きしべ (Okonomiyaki Kishibe)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and crepe, includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and is topped with Otafuku-style sweet sauce that contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature niku-tama-soba uses pork and egg, other versions add squid, shrimp and other seafood, and the dishes are finished with bonito/fish-based dashi and Otafuku sauce. No vegan version is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat-Sun 11:00-14:00; closed Thursday and Friday. (Some sources note possible evening hours on Fridays/holidays and weekend evening service; weekday lunch is generally not offered.)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Machiya-dori (about a 10-minute walk from the Miya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-feeling okonomiyaki shop tucked one block off Miyajima's busy Omotesando shopping street, on quieter Machiya-dori. It is run day to day by two women, described as the proprietress and her daughter, and keeps the relaxed, unhurried feel of a neighborhood  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

roles: proprietress (okami) and her daughter run the shop together
chef_bio.background: A small, family-feeling okonomiyaki shop tucked one block off Miyajima's busy Omotesando shopping street, on quieter Machiya-dori. It is run day to day by two women, described as the proprietress and her daughter, and keeps the relaxed, unhurried feel of a neighborhood place rather than a tourist counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: A homey spot where you can eat at a leisurely pace; the owners chat casually with guests and the place is built around regulars and an easygoing local atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki; the standard niku-tama-soba (pork, egg, cabbage, bean sprouts and noodles) is the house favorite, cooked slowly for a fluffy rather than crisp-edged result, and finished with sweet Otafuku sauce against crunchy vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run by two women, described as the okami and her daughter, who keep an at-home, leisurely atmosphere and chat casually with customers.
Because most shops on Miyajima close by early evening, Kishibe's weekday hours running later in the evening make it a handy option for a meal at the end of a day on the island.
The okonomiyaki is cooked slowly for a fluffy texture rather than pressed to crisp edges, with generous cabbage, bean sprouts, green onion and seafood toppings available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Everything is cooked on a single shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with sweet Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle and dishes routinely involve pork, egg and seafood, so vegan cross-contact cannot be avoided.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the two women who run the shop are used to foreign visitors, so a student with no Japanese can order comfortably here.

places.name: 島飯 縁 (Shimameshi Enishi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature braised anago and anago-tororo rice rely on a soy-sauce-based braising sauce (soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the fried oysters are breaded in wheat flour/panko. No gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on conger eel (anago) and oysters, with rice and sauces prepared using fish-based dashi and soy sauce. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 16:00-18:30 (listed daily; one review noted dinner to 21:00 — hours may vary)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), off Omotesando shopping street near
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, hidden-gem eatery tucked into a quiet side street off the bustling Omotesando approach to Itsukushima Shrine, near the island's post office. Set on the upper floor of a traditional folk-house-style building, it has an intimate, calm room with just a handful of

chef_bio.background: A small, hidden-gem eatery tucked into a quiet side street off the bustling Omotesando approach to Itsukushima Shrine, near the island's post office. Set on the upper floor of a traditional folk-house-style building, it has an intimate, calm room with just six counter seats and three tables and warm indirect lighting.
chef_bio.specialty: Braised conger eel (anago) over rice, including an anago-and-grated-yam (tororo) rice set, and fried local oysters. Unlike the grilled anago common elsewhere on the island, the kitchen here slow-braises the eel until it is soft and tender.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described it as a quiet hidden find off the crowded main approach, praising the soft braised anago over rice and noting the lunch sets run well under 2,000 yen, roughly half what comparable Miyajima specialty restaurants charge for similar dishes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters are breaded in wheat flour and deep-fried; no information indicates a separate gluten-free frying setup.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature anago dishes use a soy-sauce-based braising/seasoning sauce, which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around conger eel and oysters with fish-based stocks; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter-style local spot where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner go a long way; English support is uncertain, so pointing at the set menu works well.

places.name: カフェ レンテ (Cafe Lente)
places.gf_detail: A rice-based "red-rice and spinach soy-milk risotto" is introduced as gluten-free and noted by several outlets as popular with foreign tourists. However, no information on a dedicated cooking environment or cross-contamination measures could be confirmed, and as a coffee-focused cafe, wheat-using items (house-made cheesecake, hot sandwiches, etc.) also coexist. If you require GF, specify the applicable item such as the risotto and confirm directly with the shop to be safe. Safety assessment: a specific GF dish exists but no cross-contamination controls are confirmed in a kitchen that also handles wheat; celiacs should confirm directly and treat with caution.
places.vegan_detail: The "red-rice and spinach soy-milk risotto" uses miso, soy milk, and olive oil, and is introduced by several outlets as edible for vegetarians and vegans. On the other hand, the tomato curry contains chicken, and the cheesecake and cafe au lait use dairy, so the menu overall is not vegan. If you want vegan, confirm the accommodating items such as the risotto. Safety assessment: one confirmed vegan dish (the risotto), but the broader menu is not vegan; confirm the specific dish.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (per Tabelog). The official site says "from 11:00 / irregular days off excluding Sat, Sun & holidays," while another outlet lists "11:00-18:00 / irregular days off," so hours and closing days differ between sources. Phone 0829-44-1204. The 2nd floor is lodging (private inn lente).
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima Shrine area, Kita-onishi; about a 15-min walk from the Miyajima pier, near the exit of the Five-Storied Pagoda and Itsukushima Shrine)
places.cuisine: Cafe / light meals. Organic blend coffee, house-made cheesecake, tomato curry
