places.notes: [Miyajima] An ocean-view cafe making use of an old private house, standing in a quiet corner a little away from the bustling shopping street, right near Miyajima's Itsukushima Shrine. In an atmospheric space that retains old beams and pillars, it arranges antique furniture, sofas, and refined tables, and the grand torii gate and the Seto Inland Sea can be seen from every seat. The 1st floor is the cafe and the 2nd floor operates as a two-room lodging, "private inn lente."

chef_bio.background: 宮島・厳島神社のすぐ近く、賑わう商店街から少し離れた静かな一角に建つ古民家を生かしたオーシャンビューのカフェ。古い梁や柱を残した趣ある空間に、アンティーク家具やソファ、洗練されたテーブルを配し、全席から大鳥居や瀬戸内の海を望める。1階がカフェ、2階は2室の宿泊施設『private inn lente』として営まれている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 席間隔を広く取り、世界遺産の島の風景を眺めながらゆったり寛いでほしいという思いで運営されている。
chef_bio.specialty: オーガニックのオリジナルブレンドコーヒーと、パルメザン・クリームチーズを使い自家製ブルーベリーソースを添えた自家製チーズケーキ。軽食では鶏もも肉とマッシュルームのトマトカレーや、赤米・ほうれん草・豆乳・味噌・オリーブオイルを使ったリゾットが知られる。

anecdotes.text: 赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾットはグルテンフリーで、ベジタリアン・ヴィーガンの人でも食べられるとして外国人観光客に人気を集めていると紹介されている。
商店街の喧騒から離れた静かな場所にあり、全席から厳島神社の大鳥居を望めるのが魅力と紹介されている。

positives.text: 赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾットはグルテンフリーで、ベジタリアン・ヴィーガンの人でも食べられると複数媒体で紹介されている。
野菜をたっぷり使った健康志向のメニューで、外国人観光客にも人気と記載されている。

cultural_comfort.note: 外国人観光客に人気でヴィーガン・グルテンフリー対応メニューが知られており、簡単な日本語と指差し・メニュー名の提示があれば留学生でも安心して利用できる落ち着いたカフェ。

places.name: 大丸堂 (Daimarudo)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-soy-sauce based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made with egg, pork, squid, shrimp and bonito/dashi-based sauces; the menu centers on meat and seafood (Hiroshima Special, Deluxe with squid and shrimp) plus izakaya side dishes from the chef's Chinese-cuisine background. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–00:00 next day (Sun and holidays listed as 11:30-24:00). Open daily
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (Okonomimura 4F, ~4 min walk from Hatchobori Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A counter okonomiyaki shop on the 4th floor of Okonomimura, Hiroshima's famous building packed with okonomiyaki stalls. The owner came to okonomiyaki after a career as a Chinese-cuisine cook, and that background shows up in a fuller-than-usual lineup of side dishes alon

chef_bio.background: A counter okonomiyaki shop on the 4th floor of Okonomimura, Hiroshima's famous building packed with okonomiyaki stalls. The owner came to okonomiyaki after a career as a Chinese-cuisine cook, and that background shows up in a fuller-than-usual lineup of side dishes alongside the griddle work.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on the teppan in front of you and kept hot to the last bite; signature builds include the Hiroshima Special (noodles, meat, egg, squid tempura, green onion) and the Deluxe (noodles, meat, egg, fresh squid, shrimp), plus Chinese-influenced sides such as shrimp chili.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor moved into okonomiyaki after working as a Chinese-cuisine cook, and the shop is described as a noted spot that has won over many entertainers and celebrities.
Okonomiyaki is cooked on the iron griddle right in front of the guest at the counter and held warm on the teppan until the end of the meal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a single-griddle teppan okonomiyaki counter where wheat batter and wheat noodles are cooked on the same surface, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki/soy-based sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle cooks pork, squid, shrimp and egg, so any item would have animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, lively counter okonomiyaki stall inside the famous Okonomimura food building; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way, and English support is limited.

places.name: カフェポンテ イタリアーノ (Caff'e Ponte ITALIANO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on wood-fired pizza, fresh pasta, and bread, all wheat-based, so this is not a suitable destination for someone requiring gluten-free dining. No celiac-safe preparation or dedicated handling is mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant lists vegetarian options, but vegetarian here likely includes dairy and eggs (cheese pizza, gelato), and no fully vegan dish is documented. Much of the menu uses oysters, fish-based sauces, and cheese. A vegan diner would need to ask directly about plant-only preparations; a simple marinara pizza or vegetable pasta might be possible but is not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 10:00-22:00; weekends and holidays 08:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). In August, weekdays also open from 08:00. Open year-round, no regular closing day.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka Ward (along the Motoyasu River, right by Genbaku Dome-mae Stn, waterfront overlooking Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: An open-air riverside Italian cafe that opened on August 1, 2008, on the green riverbank at the eastern end of the Motoyasu Bridge, directly across the water from Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and within sight of the Atomic Bomb Dome. Its white parasols and terrace un

chef_bio.background: An open-air riverside Italian cafe that opened on August 1, 2008, on the green riverbank at the eastern end of the Motoyasu Bridge, directly across the water from Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and within sight of the Atomic Bomb Dome. Its white parasols and terrace under the trees, with fresh oranges stacked at the entrance, have made it a landmark stop for both local visitors and travelers from abroad.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name comes from the Italian word ponte, meaning bridge, reflecting the founders' wish to offer a riverside place of rest and connection, a calm and refreshing waterside spot for people to gather and exchange.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic Italian cooking built on carefully chosen seasonal ingredients: wood-fired pizzas, fresh pasta, escargot-style roasted Hiroshima oysters, and house gelato including a Hiroshima lemon sherbet, plus takeaway gelato, freshly squeezed orange juice, coffee, and beer.

anecdotes.text: The cafe takes its name from the Italian word for bridge, ponte, chosen because it sits at the foot of the Motoyasu Bridge and was conceived as a place of rest and exchange along the water.
Its white parasols and terrace seating beneath lush trees, with fresh oranges displayed at the entrance, overlook Peace Memorial Park and have made it popular with international visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen runs a wood-fired pizza oven and fresh pasta service, so wheat flour is central to the cooking and gluten cross-contact should be assumed; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes commonly use cheese, oysters, and fish-based sauces; no separate vegan preparation is described, so a vegan diner should confirm ingredients and shared cooking surfaces directly.

positives.text: The restaurant advertises vegetarian options and provides menus in English, Chinese, and Korean, which makes it easier to discuss dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly open-air cafe right beside Peace Memorial Park with multilingual menus, so a foreign student can comfortably walk in and order in English.

places.name: 胡桃屋 (Kurumiya)
places.gf_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made on a wheat-flour batter with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. There is no indication of gluten-free batter or a dedicated cooking surface.
places.vegan_detail: This is a Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppan shop built around pork, egg, squid, shrimp, noodles and a wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce, so there are no vegan or vegetarian options. The batter, sauce and shared griddle all rule it out for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat and holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-21:00; closed Sun (irregular closures on holidays)
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka Ward (near Tatemachi tram stop, Hondori / Hatchobori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kurumiya is a small, family-run okonomiyaki house tucked on the second floor of a building down a side street in Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, a short walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. With only about a dozen seats around a counter and a couple of tables, it has the

chef_bio.background: Kurumiya is a small, family-run okonomiyaki house tucked on the second floor of a building down a side street in Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, a short walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. With only about a dozen seats around a counter and a couple of tables, it has the feel of a neighborhood spot rather than a tourist destination.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop puts its pride in ingredients, using free-range (yusei-ran) eggs and its own green onions, and in a careful steaming-and-griddling technique that brings out the natural sweetness of the cabbage.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on the griddle, with a signature 'special' piling pork, egg, squid, shrimp and noodles together; regulars order the niku-tama soba (pork-and-egg with noodles) and pile on the generous green-onion topping.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen makes a point of using free-range eggs and homemade green onions, and is known for a steaming technique that draws out the sweetness of the cabbage.
The okonomiyaki 'special' brings together meat, egg, squid, shrimp and noodles in one layered Hiroshima-style pancake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and cooked alongside wheat noodles on a shared teppan griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with a Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single griddle is used for pork, egg and seafood, so any vegetable-only order would share a surface with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood okonomiyaki counter where a few friendly words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way; an English menu has been noted, so a foreign student can manage comfortably.

places.name: 広島牡蠣と鉄板焼き 一娯一笑 (Hiroshima Kaki to Teppanyaki Ichigo Ichie)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Core dishes rely on wheat: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and noodles, and the teppanyaki and oyster dishes are typically finished with soy sauce (e.g., butter-soy or ponzu on grilled oysters), which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is documented. A shared teppan griddle also makes cross-contact likely.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around oysters (raw and grilled), sea urchin, conger eel, and Hiroshima beef, and dishes use butter, soy sauce, and fish/seafood. Okonomiyaki typically contains egg, pork, and a seafood/meat-based sauce. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 18:00–23:55 (LO 23:00); Sat 17:00–23:55 (LO 23:00); Sun & Holidays 17:00–22:55 (LO 22:00). Sundays often closed; ¥480 seat charge. (Tabelog lists close at 23:00; some listings show close at midnight.)
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small teppanyaki counter-and-table spot tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, operating since 2009. It was conceived as a place where you can taste 'all the best of Hiroshima in one spot' — pairing the city's signature ingredients with hot

chef_bio.background: A small teppanyaki counter-and-table spot tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, operating since 2009. It was conceived as a place where you can taste 'all the best of Hiroshima in one spot' — pairing the city's signature ingredients with hot-griddle cooking in a relaxed setting suited to casual nights out, light entertaining, and travelers.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house keeps three words at its heart: 安心・丁寧・笑顔 — reassurance, care, and a smile. The name 一娯一笑 plays on 'one enjoyment, one smile.'
chef_bio.specialty: Premium Hiroshima oysters served both raw and grilled on the teppan — including prized 'Kanawa' brand raw oysters by the shell and grilled oysters finished with butter-soy or ponzu — alongside uni-and-spinach (uni houren) eaten with baguette, local conger eel, Hiroshima beef, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen highlights 'Kanawa' oysters, described as among the safest, highest-quality oysters in Hiroshima, sourced from the clear Seto Inland Sea and sold by the individual shell, with a summer seasonal oyster also offered.
A signature uni-and-spinach dish is bound in the house's own mellow sea-urchin sauce and eaten with baguette — presented as a teppanyaki-style Hiroshima specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (wheat batter plus noodles) is cooked on the same surface as other dishes, making gluten cross-contact likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Grilled oysters are finished with butter-soy sauce or ponzu, and teppanyaki dishes use soy-based seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on oysters, sea urchin, conger eel, and Hiroshima beef cooked on a shared griddle with butter and soy seasonings; no plant-based or vegan options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small late-night local teppanyaki and oyster spot where some Japanese will help a lot; reservations are recommended, the room allows smoking throughout, and minors are not admitted.

places.name: 花菱 (Hanabishi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional anago (conger eel) and oyster eatery. The signature anagomeshi (eel over rice) and oyster dishes are prepared with Japanese soy sauce and sauces that contain wheat, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen handling. Not suitable for someone who needs to avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around conger eel and oysters, with seafood and wheat-based soy sauce throughout. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–15:00 (open daily, lunch only)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando / around Itsukushima Shrine
places.cuisine: Anagomeshi (conger eel rice), oysters, and other Setouchi regional dishes
places.notes: [Miyajima] Hanabishi is a sit-down eatery just off the seafront promenade near the entrance to Miyajima's main approach to Itsukushima Shrine, about a five-minute walk from the ferry pier. It specializes in the island's two signature dishes: anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) and l

chef_bio.background: Hanabishi is a sit-down eatery just off the seafront promenade near the entrance to Miyajima's main approach to Itsukushima Shrine, about a five-minute walk from the ferry pier. It specializes in the island's two signature dishes: anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) and locally prized oysters.
chef_bio.specialty: Anagomeshi made with wild conger eel, and oyster dishes including oyster clay-pot rice (kaki kamameshi).

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the wild conger eel as having firm flesh that is fragrant, light, and clean-tasting.
The interior is often noted as refined and cafe-like, and the room tends to be calmer than the busier shops along the main approach street.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anagomeshi and oyster dishes at this kind of eatery are conventionally prepared with Japanese soy sauce / tare, which contains wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A reservation-only, cash-only sit-down restaurant where lunch runs about 3,000-4,000 yen and middle-school-age and younger children are not admitted, so a foreign student should arrange a booking in advance and come prepared with cash and basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: アールカフェ キッチネッテ (R Cafe Kitchenette)
places.gf_detail: Documented gluten-free option: all of the cafe's cakes are made without wheat flour (小麦粉不使用), offered for health-conscious customers. The savory menu, however, is not gluten-free — pasta, bagels, French toast, sandwiches and breaded cutlets all contain wheat, and there is no stated celiac-grade kitchen protocol. Coffee and tea are made with naturally alkaline spring water the owner sources himself. Confirm cross-contact directly if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: This is a health-leaning but omnivore cafe, not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The signature dishes center on egg (the fluffy 15-grain-rice omurice), dairy (grass-fed butter, Hiroshima Sunatani-dairy milk), and meat (demi-glace hamburger, grass-fed beef bagel). There is no advertised vegan menu. A vegetarian could likely find vegetable-forward plates or pasta, but most items use egg/dairy/meat, so a strict vegan should ask staff about specific dishes before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Fri-Sat 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun & holidays 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Lunch served 11:30-16:00 (L.O. 15:00, while supplies last).
places.cuisine: Portland-style bistro cafe. Fluffy omurice on fifteen-grain rice, demi-glace hamburg steak, gratin
places.notes: Kitchenette is the sister cafe to R[a:r] cafe, and its name means 'small kitchen.' It was conceived as an open, light-filled space inspired by the cafes of Portland, Oregon — a city admired as a place creators from around the world aspire to. The interior was designed b

chef_bio.background: Kitchenette is the sister cafe to R[a:r] cafe, and its name means 'small kitchen.' It was conceived as an open, light-filled space inspired by the cafes of Portland, Oregon — a city admired as a place creators from around the world aspire to. The interior was designed by Makoto Tanijiri of Suppose Design Office, the Hiroshima-based architecture studio.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe embraces a local-production, local-consumption (地産地消) ethos and aims to be a crossroads where people meet and a platform from which Hiroshima's culture can reach the wider world.
chef_bio.specialty: The fluffy-and-runny omurice built on nutritious 15-grain rice is the signature dish, alongside a hand-formed demi-glace hamburger plate and a grass-fed-beef bagel. Cooking leans health-forward: naturally leavened bread, locally produced Sunatani milk and seasonal Hiroshima vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The cafe brews its signature 'famous-water' drip coffee with naturally alkaline 'beauty-bath' spring water sourced from the Kanagi area of Hamada, Shimane Prefecture.
The coffee program centers on Q-grade specialty beans (origins such as Brazil, Ethiopia and Costa Rica) served as hand drip.

positives.text: All cakes are made without wheat flour, offered as a gluten-free sweet option for health-conscious guests.
Health-forward sourcing: grass-fed butter, naturally leavened bread, local Sunatani-dairy milk and seasonal Hiroshima vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, stylish cafe in central Hiroshima where a foreign student can order comfortably with a few Japanese words and gestures; a picture menu and well-known dishes like omurice make ordering easy, though staff may have limited English.

places.name: お好み焼 ひらの (Okonomiyaki Hirano)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe batter and includes yakisoba wheat noodles, plus wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce. There is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The okonomiyaki centers on pork, egg, and the shop's signature dishes use pork fat for cooking; sauce and toppings are not plant-based. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-13:30, 17:00-20:00 (sources vary on lunch close, 13:30 per Tabelog / 14:00 per other listings; open until sold out in evening). Closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: Minamimachi (Minami-ku), south-central Hiroshima — roughly a
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop in the Minamimachi district that opened in 1989 and has been run, for more than three decades, by its founder Hirano-san, who cooks alone behind the counter. Warm and talkative, she has become as much of a draw as the food, and the shop h

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop in the Minamimachi (皆実町) district that opened in 1989 and has been run, for more than three decades, by its founder Hirano-san, who cooks alone behind the counter. Warm and talkative, she has become as much of a draw as the food, and the shop has the easy, lived-in feel of a true local institution.
chef_bio.philosophy: Her stated outlook is simple: as long as customers enjoy eating it, that's enough for her. She cooks without added oil, relying on the rendered fat from quality Hiroshima (Sera) pork, and uses firm noodles chosen to keep the dish from turning soggy, aiming for something filling yet light.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is 'soba rice' (そばライス): a hearty okonomiyaki layered with plenty of cabbage, pork, egg and yakisoba noodles, with sauce-coated rice folded in. It was born in the early years when a hungry high-school boy told her the standard size 'won't fill me up,' prompting her to build a more filling version that became the shop's defining dish.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'soba rice' was created after a hungry local high-school student complained that the regular okonomiyaki wouldn't fill him up, so the owner devised a bigger, rice-loaded version.

With the football powerhouse Hiroshima Minami High School nearby, the shop draws many students, and over the years athletes connected through sport — including players from Sanfrecce Hiroshima, the Hiroshima Carp, and the Hiroshima Dragonflies basketball team — have become regulars, making it a quiet hangout for local sports figures.
The owner cooks using no added oil, drawing only on the fat rendered from quality pork raised in Sera, Hiroshima, and selects firm noodles so the dish stays light rather than greasy or soggy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle and built from wheat batter and wheat yakisoba noodles, so the cooking surface is heavily exposed to gluten; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce is a wheat-containing sauce and the noodles are wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked using rendered pork fat on a shared griddle and center on pork and egg, so all items have animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, warm neighborhood counter shop run single-handedly by a chatty local owner; little to no English is likely, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way for a foreign student.

places.name: 本家うずしお（うずしお） (Honke Uzushio (Uzushio))
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe and layered noodles, and the finishing sauce is a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki contains egg and pork and is cooked on a shared griddle, oysters feature prominently, and the sauce is fish/animal-derived; no vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-22:30 (some sources list 09:30-21:30 or 10:00-23:00; listings vary). Open daily
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka Ward (Hondori, ~3 min walk from Genbaku Dome / Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked on the upper floor near the west end of the Hon-dori shopping arcade, just a few minutes' walk from the Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. It has served locals and visitors for roughly a quarter-century.

chef_bio.background: A long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked on the upper floor near the west end of the Hon-dori shopping arcade, a three-minute walk from the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park. It has served locals and visitors for roughly a quarter-century.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles, with a popular oyster (kaki) version using plump Hiroshima oysters and butter-grilled oysters (kaki-bata) as a signature add-on.

anecdotes.text: The walls are covered with autographs and signed photos from many celebrities and Hiroshima Carp players who have eaten here over the years, giving the small dining room a cluttered, well-loved feel.

The shop has been picked up as a notable Hiroshima okonomiyaki spot in voice actress Nobuyo Oyama's national restaurant-tour feature '(全国)おいしい店めぐり', helping build its reputation among visitors to the Peace Park area.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists the shop at Otemachi 1-5-15 2F, roughly 25 years in business, 40 seats, with butter-grilled oysters and celebrity autographs, and notes it was featured in Nobuyo Oyama's 1996 guide 'おいしい店めぐり'. Mapple frames the same '全国おいしい店めぐり' feature as a Nobuyo Oyama program and lists カキバター from ¥200; a personal blog confirms the Carp-player signed photos and rapid griddle service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and noodles, so any non-wheat order would face heavy cross-contact; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, which contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg, oysters and the same griddle are used across dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, tourist-friendly counter okonomiyaki spot where a smile and a few Japanese words go a long way, but don't expect detailed English allergy support.

places.name: 本通り 然然 (Hondori Sasa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a robata grill built around soy-sauce-based seasonings (which contain wheat) and grilled seafood; the menu (straw-seared sashimi, charcoal-grilled dishes, izakaya plates) is not designed for gluten avoidance and no GF accommodation is mentioned on the official site or listings.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The restaurant centers on seafood (oysters, Seto Inland Sea fish), wagyu beef, and seafood/meat-based robata dishes, with soy-sauce and fish-based seasonings throughout. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are advertised.
places.neighborhood: 中区本通り (near the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: Sasa occupies a roughly 70-year-old building dating to Hiroshima's post-war reconstruction. Rather than tear it down, the owners renovated it, keeping the original brick-walled, nostalgic character so that diners step into a space that feels removed from the everyday wh

chef_bio.background: Sasa occupies a roughly 70-year-old building dating to Hiroshima's post-war reconstruction. Rather than tear it down, the owners renovated it, keeping the original brick-walled, nostalgic character so that diners step into a space that feels removed from the everyday while remaining warm and comfortable.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the fire and the season lead: source local, fresh ingredients from the Seto Inland Sea and adjust the offering with the seasons, cooking simply at the counter hearth so guests can watch, hear, and smell the food as it cooks.
chef_bio.specialty: Robata hearth cooking using four distinct fire techniques — straw-flame searing (wara-yaki), primitive open-fire grilling (genshi-yaki), wood-fired cooking (maki-yaki), and charcoal grilling (sumibi) — applied to fresh Seto Inland Sea fish, Hiroshima oysters, local vegetables, and charcoal-grilled wagyu. Straw-seared sashimi is a signature.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is built around an irori hearth and a stone/wood-fired kiln at the counter, where guests can watch the flames and catch the aroma of food cooking over straw and charcoal.
The restaurant opened on June 1, 2020 inside a vintage 1940s-50s reconstruction-era building reworked into a brick-walled, nostalgic space.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site and its 2020-06-01 opening announcement confirm 'ロ 本通り 然然' opened that date in a renovated ~70-year-old reconstruction-era building on Hondori (中区本通6-14), cooking with four fire methods (藁炙り/原始焼き/薪焼き/炭火焼き) around an irori hearth. Tabiiro corroborates the 1940s-50s building, brick-walled nostalgic interior, and Seto-Inland-Sea-centered Hiroshima produce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned with soy-sauce-based seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is centered on a shared hearth, kiln and charcoal grill used for fish, oysters and meat, so vegetable items would share fire and surfaces with animal proteins.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local robata izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the open hearth go a long way; online reservation is available if you'd rather avoid a phone call.

places.name: おばんざい処 やましたさん家 (Obanzai-dokoro Yamashita-san'chi)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment and no GF accommodation is documented. The obanzai lunch is built around many small side dishes that in traditional home cooking typically use soy sauce (wheat-containing) and dashi; a panko-breaded salt-koji pork cutlet (wheat) is a regular item. No dedicated fryer or GF protocol is mentioned anywhere. A celiac traveler would need to ask in detail in Japanese and should not assume any dish is safe.
places.vegan_detail: Strongly vegetable-centered: the daily lunch features 10+ small dishes built from fresh Shimane (Iinan-cho) vegetables with house-made condiments (mayonnaise, sesame dressing). However it is not a vegan restaurant. The set includes animal items (salt-koji pork cutlet, dashi-maki tamago egg roll, Onan-cho eggs), and traditional obanzai commonly uses fish-based dashi and soy sauce. There is no documented vegan set. Because the kitchen is small and ingredient-focused, a vegetarian who eats egg may fare reasonably by asking; a strict vegan would need to confirm dashi and individual dishes directly and may have limited options.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 14:30); closed Wednesdays. Lunch-only operation.
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Nekoya-cho (just west of Peace Memorial Park; ~2 mi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop began in 2021 as 「旬菜マルシェやました」, a direct-sales outlet for vegetables from the owner's home town of Iinan-cho in Shimane, and in September 2025 it was reborn as a sit-down obanzai lunch spot run by a husband-and-wife pair with one additional staff member. The coo

chef_bio.background: The shop began in June 2021 as 「旬菜マルシェやました」, a direct-sales outlet for vegetables from the owner's home town of Iinan-cho in Shimane, and in September 2025 it was reborn as a sit-down obanzai lunch spot run by a husband-and-wife pair with one additional woman on staff. The cook, Yumi, holds a food-education (shokuiku) instructor qualification and still drives back to Iinan-cho two or three times a week to hand-pick seasonal vegetables, rice and other produce from a circle of local farmers.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners want guests to feel as if they have come home to a relative's house in the countryside, and they cook with additive-free, health-minded seasonings (cane/unrefined sugar, natural sea salt, rice-bran oil) so people can eat with peace of mind.
chef_bio.specialty: A single daily 「ゆみちゃんのおばんざいランチ」 (¥1,800) — a tray of ten-plus small home-style dishes that change every day, built around Shimane vegetables, locally milled rice, and house-made seasonings like mayonnaise and sesame dressing.

anecdotes.text: The owner drives to her home town of Iinan-cho in Shimane two or three times a week to personally choose the day's vegetables from local farmers.
On the menu philosophy the cook explains she wants customers to be able to enjoy many different things, each in small portions, all at once.
The name was chosen to evoke the feeling of returning to a rural relative's home, and the kitchen deliberately uses additive-free seasonings so guests can dine safely.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own site (shunmaru831) and tabiiro confirm the June-2021 origin as vegetable direct-sales 旬菜マルシェやました and the Sept-2025 relaunch as obanzai lunch, owner/cook ゆみちゃん (shokuiku instructor) sourcing Iinan-cho (Shimane) produce 2-3 days a week, and a 10+-small-dish daily tray. A press search confirms the 3-person team (owner couple + one woman). Wink details ¥1,800 lunch, house-made mayo/sesame dressing, unrefined sugar/sea salt/rice-bran oil.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is traditional home-style obanzai with many small dishes, which conventionally rely on soy sauce and dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free preparation is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The daily set combines vegetable dishes alongside animal items such as a salt-koji pork cutlet and a dashi-maki egg roll on the same tray, so vegan and non-vegan dishes are prepared and plated together.

positives.text: The kitchen emphasizes additive-free, health-conscious seasonings (cane sugar, mineral-rich brown sugar, rice oil, house-made mayonnaise and sesame dressing) rather than packaged sauces.
Cooking is vegetable-forward, using fresh Shimane (Iinan-cho) produce hand-selected by the owner, with tofu and eggs also sourced from nearby Onan-cho.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, warm family-run lunch spot where English is unlikely, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the day's set and use a few words of Japanese, but the welcoming "like visiting a country relative" atmosphere makes it forgiving.

places.name: ロン (Ron)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The dish is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter, layered with soba (wheat) noodles, and finished with a sweet okonomiyaki sauce that is wheat-and-soy based. There is no indication of gluten-free batter, noodles, sauce, or any allergy accommodation. Not suitable for someone with celiac disease or a strict gluten-free need.
places.vegan_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki here is made with egg in the batter, pork (the signature 肉玉そば / niku-tama-soba is a pork-and-egg style), and bonito-derived sauce/seasonings; griddle is shared with meat. The other popular item is a grilled mochi-and-cheese dish (dairy). No vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation. Not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: 中区新天地 (Shintenchi) — Okonomimura building, near Hatchobori /
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ron was born and raised in Hiroshima and loved okonomiyaki from a young age. Opening this shop on the third floor of the famous Okonomimura building fulfilled a childhood dream.

chef_bio.background: Ron was born and raised in Hiroshima and loved okonomiyaki from a young age. Opening this shop on the third floor of the famous Okonomimura building fulfilled a childhood dream.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sticking to the old-fashioned, traditional Hiroshima flavor while keeping a warm, friendly welcome at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the niku-tama-soba (pork, egg and soba) as the house favorite, plus a popular griddle-seared mochi-and-cheese dish; a broad lineup of teppanyaki sides rounds out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The owner, Ron, speaks English and the shop draws a large number of overseas visitors, who feel at ease chatting across the griddle.
The house favorite is the niku-tama-soba: soba griddled until fragrant and crisp, then coated generously in the shop's special sauce — a natural match for a cold beer.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Okonomimura's official 3F floor page and corroborating listings confirm Ron on the 3rd floor of Okonomimura (中区新天地5-13), an English-speaking owner named Ron who is Hiroshima-born and opened the shop to fulfill a childhood love of okonomiyaki, with niku-tama-soba the recommended dish and cheese-mochi grilled okonomiyaki popular; the shop draws many overseas visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and soba noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a sweet okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce that is wheat- and soy-based; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared griddle cooks pork, egg and seafood-derived items, so any dish will have animal-product cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner, known as Ron, speaks English and is used to welcoming foreign visitors, so an exchange student can order and chat comfortably at the counter.

places.name: かえるっ亭 (Kaeruttei)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce typically contains wheat-derived ingredients. Squid tempura (ika-ten) topping is also wheat-battered. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Dishes are cooked on a shared griddle and the standard build includes egg and pork, with toppings such as Hiroshima oysters (with bacon), scallop, squid, shrimp, and tuna-mayo; okonomiyaki/teppanyaki sauces commonly contain fish/bonito and wheat-soy. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat & holidays: 11:30–24:00; Sun: 11:30–15:00. (If a Monday is a public holiday, the shop stays open Sunday and closes Monday instead.) Note: Tabelog lists Sunday as closed except for reservations, while HotPepper lists Sunday 11:30–15:00 — hours are somewhat irregular and worth confirming by phone.
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka Ward, Okonomimura 4F (Hatchobori / Peace Memorial Park area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kaeruttei has run its single griddle counter inside Okonomimura — the original multi-shop okonomiyaki hall in central Hiroshima — since 1991, operated by the owner with one other staff member.

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, in the Okonomimura building at Shintenchi.
chef_bio.background: Kaeruttei has run its single griddle counter inside Okonomimura — the original multi-shop okonomiyaki hall in central Hiroshima — since 1991, operated by the owner with one other (female) staff member across about 17 seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop leans toward lightly-seasoned cooking and good-quality, largely domestic and local ingredients (noodles included), with about half its customers being regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked in front of you on the iron plate, featuring the local touches of squid tempura and thin bean sprouts, plus seafood toppings like fresh Hiroshima oysters and scallop-butter.

anecdotes.text: The name comes from the owner's wish that customers will come back again — a play on the Japanese verb kaeru, 'to return home.'

A visitor described picking the shop on a whim inside Okonomimura and watching the okonomiyaki — with its squid tempura and thin bean sprouts, alongside oysters — cooked right in front of them on the big iron griddle, calling the result delicious.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: HotPepper explains the name (customers 'returning home', kaeru) and notes light seasoning, a commitment to main ingredients including noodles, and ~half regulars. Tabelog gives opening year 1991, 17 seats, a two-person owner+one-female-staff operation inside Okonomimura, and toppings of squid tempura, bean sprouts and scallop-butter. A note.com visitor confirms squid tempura, thin bean sprouts and oysters grilled at the counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made on a shared iron griddle with wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable in this style of cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are cooked together on one communal griddle alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so plant-only preparation cannot be assured.

positives.text: Ingredients are largely domestic/local and the cooking is described as lightly seasoned, and the food is prepared on the griddle in front of you so you can see exactly what goes in.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run griddle counter inside the Okonomimura okonomiyaki hall with about half regulars; a little Japanese helps, but pointing at menu items and watching the chef cook on the iron plate in front of you makes ordering manageable.

places.name: 姉妹 (Shimai)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and finished with okonomiyaki sauce (Otafuku-style) that contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is standardly made with egg, pork, and seafood, and uses bonito/fish-based elements and wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 16:00-23:00 / Sat-Sun 12:00-23:00 (per Tabelog). Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka Ward (Hatchobori / Peace Memorial Park area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 姉妹 is one of the small okonomiyaki stalls inside お好み共和国ひろしま村, a Shintenchi food complex devoted to Hiroshima's post-war okonomiyaki culture. The shop is known for a former sumo wrestler who works the teppan, grilling each Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki by hand in full view

chef_bio.background: 姉妹 is one of the small okonomiyaki stalls inside お好み共和国ひろしま村, a Shintenchi food complex devoted to Hiroshima's post-war okonomiyaki culture. The shop is known for a former sumo wrestler who works the teppan, grilling each Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki by hand in full view of the counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The building it sits in espouses okonomiyaki that evokes nostalgia without heavy seasoning, rooted in Hiroshima's postwar food culture.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — the layered, wheat-crepe-and-noodle griddle dish that the surrounding complex is built around.

anecdotes.text: The cook at the griddle is a former sumo wrestler, and customers come to watch him grill the okonomiyaki right in front of them at the counter.
The shop sits inside お好み共和国ひろしま村, a Shintenchi okonomiyaki complex focused on unfussy, nostalgic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The complex's official shoplist places 姉妹 inside お好み共和国ひろしま村 in Shintenchi (Naka-ku), a themed hall of postwar-style Hiroshima okonomiyaki. Tabelog/gnavi listings and search results confirm the stall is run by a former sumo wrestler (with a sumo-stable training connection), ~16 seats on the 2F, open daily. No published personal name for the cook was found, so chef_name is left null.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 7-seat counter okonomiyaki stall inside a multi-shop building, where you watch the cook work the griddle right in front of you — a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, and pointing at what others are eating works fine.

places.name: 汁なし担々麺専門店 楽 小網町店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Senmonten Raku Koamicho)
places.gf_detail: The signature dish is wheat-noodle dandan noodles, and the savory tare typically relies on soy sauce (which contains wheat) plus meat- and sesame-based seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free option, so this is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-forward noodle shop — the dandan noodles and the other recommended bowls (chicken soba) are built on chicken/pork umami and animal-based broths and toppings. No vegan option is documented.
places.neighborhood: Koamicho, Naka Ward (Dobashi / Hondori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small specialist shop devoted to shirunashi tantanmen — Hiroshima-style brothless dandan noodles — that opened on September 29, 2014. It sits a one- to two-minute walk from the Hiroden Tsuchihashi tram stop in the Koamicho district just west of the Peace Memorial Park

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, central Naka Ward near Tsuchihashi/Dobashi
chef_bio.background: A small specialist shop devoted to shirunashi tantanmen — Hiroshima-style brothless dandan noodles — that opened on September 29, 2014. It sits a one- to two-minute walk from the Hiroden Tsuchihashi tram stop in the Koamicho district (小網町7-1) just west of the Peace Memorial Park, and runs as a compact 12-seat counter operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Build the bowl on layered texture and umami: a chicken-bone-based tare, chewy noodles, generous pickled mustard greens (zaasai), and crunchy garlic chips.
chef_bio.specialty: Brothless dandan noodles (汁なし担々麺) served at 800 yen, with the eater choosing both a spice level (0, 1, 2, 3, or MAX) and a temperature (hot or cold); a chicken soba and a jiro-style bowl have since been added.

anecdotes.text: A distinctive feature of the bowl is the unusual sweetness of the pickled mustard greens (zaasai), which gives the dish more richness and depth than a typical brothless dandan noodle.
Regulars treat a small portion of rice as essential to soak up the seasoned sauce left at the bottom of the bowl, and table-side garlic-chip chili oil lets you shift the flavor partway through.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog gives the opening date 2014.9.29, 12 counter seats, and a tare built on chicken-bone broth (鶏ガラ) with red miso, sugar and dark soy. Modokimaru confirms the ¥800 price, spice levels 0/1/2/3/MAX, hot-or-cold choice, address 小網町7-1, crunchy zaasai, table garlic-chip chili oil, and rice to soak the sauce. Pokomichi highlights the signature sweetness of the zaasai and notes a later change of ownership that added chicken soba and jiro-style ramen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-counter shop centered entirely on wheat noodles, so gluten is present throughout the cooking line; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The savory tare for brothless dandan noodles characteristically uses soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes use chicken/pork umami and animal toppings; a shared single-counter kitchen means no separation from animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 12-seat counter shop run single-handedly with a ticket-machine ordering system, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) helps; just pick your spice level (0 to MAX) and hot or cold.

noodles.notes: Firm, springy (koshi) low-hydration noodles built to grip the oily sansho-and-chili sauce; tossed thoroughly until almost no liquid remains, in the Hiroshima shiru-nashi style.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shirunashi tantanmen (broth-free dandan noodles) ordered with extra negi and a spice level of your choice (0 to MAX), hot or cold, finished with a small bowl of rice tossed into the leftover sauce. Optional crispy-chicken-skin (kawa-kari) topping is a popular add-on.
ramen.chashu: No traditional sliced chashu — the protein is a seasoned pork mince (niku-miso) tossed through the noodles.
notable_toppings: zasai (pickled mustard greens) with a hint of sweetness

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The dish is built on seasoned pork mince and savory animal-based tare and oils; no documented plant-only version.

ramen.pantheon: This is Hiroshima's hometown obsession in its purest form: brothless noodles slicked with a numbing-hot lacquer of sansho pepper, chili oil and sesame that you toss yourself until the bowl runs nearly dry. What sets this kitchen apart is a quiet sweetness from chopped pickled mustard greens that rounds out the tongue-tingling heat, ending in the ritual scoop of rice through the spicy dregs.

places.highlight: This is Hiroshima's hometown obsession in its purest form: brothless noodles slicked with a numbing-hot lacquer of sansho pepper, chili oil and sesame that you toss yourself until the bowl runs nearly dry. What sets this kitchen apart is a quiet sweetness from chopped pickled mustard greens that rounds out the tongue-tingling heat, ending in the ritual scoop of rice through the spicy dregs.

places.name: 中国料理 川菜味 (Chugoku Ryori Kawanami)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The signature dishes are wheat-noodle tantanmen and brothless tantanmen, and Sichuan stir-fries here use soy sauce and likely wheat-based seasonings. No dedicated GF menu or preparation is documented, so this is not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu centers on meat-based Sichuan dishes (mapo tofu typically with pork, karaage, beef oyster-sauce stir-fry) and pork/sesame-based tantanmen. No documented vegan options or plant-based preparation. Vegetable dishes likely contain meat-derived stocks, soy sauce, or oyster sauce.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun 11:30-14:00, 17:30-21:00; closed Monday
places.neighborhood: 東区光町 (Hikarimachi, Hiroshima Station area)
places.notes: The owner-chef built his craft over a long apprenticeship in authentic Sichuan cooking before opening this small restaurant a short walk from Hiroshima Station, where he turns out classic Sichuan dishes alongside the city's beloved brothless tantanmen.

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef built his craft over a long apprenticeship in authentic Sichuan cooking before opening this small restaurant a short walk from Hiroshima Station, where he turns out classic Sichuan dishes alongside the city's beloved brothless tantanmen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing genuine Sichuan technique to an everyday neighborhood setting, and giving the local brothless tantanmen a personal twist with added vegetables.
chef_bio.specialty: Brothless tantanmen (shirunashi tantanmen) and Sichuan classics such as mapo tofu

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef is described as having trained for 21 years under celebrated Sichuan master Chen Kenichi at Shisen Hanten, grounding the kitchen in serious Sichuan technique.
His brothless tantanmen stands out by including bean sprouts, where most Hiroshima versions use only green onion as a topping, adding a fresh vegetable element to the dish.
It is cited as one of the closest places to Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit to get a proper bowl of brothless tantanmen, about a 5-7 minute walk away.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Umaihiroshima reports the owner-chef trained 21 years under Chen Kenichi (陳建一) at Shisen Hanten (四川飯店), that his brothless tantanmen uniquely adds bean sprouts (where most Hiroshima shops use only green onion), and that the shop is about a 5-minute walk from Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit — one of the closest spots for a proper bowl; the ¥800 set includes rice and almond tofu, and mapo tofu is on the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The brothless tantanmen is described as having a soy-sauce-based seasoning, and Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual neighborhood Chinese restaurant where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome and a great bowl of noodles.

noodles.notes: Thin, white, delicate-textured noodles tossed in the soy-and-sesame-chili tare; mixed at the table so the thin sauce clings and thickens around them.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dan dan noodles) — thin white noodles dressed in a soy-based, sesame-and-chili-oil tare, tossed at the table. A soup-style (tsukejiru) tantanmen is also offered.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy (wheat)-based tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented, so it is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The tare carries spiced ground pork and the kitchen is a Sichuan meat-and-seafood operation; no documented plant-only broth or topping swap.

ramen.pantheon: A Sichuan kitchen near Hiroshima Station turning out the city's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen: no broth at all, just thin white noodles cloaked in a soy-and-sesame chili tare that you mix to coat every strand. The chef's two decades of Sichuan training show in the chili-oil warmth, though the sansho numbing is kept gentle, and a heap of bean sprouts gives this version a homier, crunchier signature than the specialist soupless-tantan shops.

places.highlight: A Sichuan kitchen near Hiroshima Station turning out the city's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen: no broth at all, just thin white noodles cloaked in a soy-and-sesame chili tare that you mix to coat every strand. The chef's two decades of Sichuan training show in the chili-oil warmth, though the sansho numbing is kept gentle, and a heap of bean sprouts gives this version a homier, crunchier signature than the specialist soupless-tantan shops.

places.name: やまだ屋 宮島本店 (Yamadaya Miyajima Honten)
places.gf_detail: Their signature momiji manju and kiriyoka are wheat-flour confections (castella-style sponge), so the core products are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or dedicated allergen handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Momiji manju is a castella-batter cake that traditionally contains eggs (and commonly dairy/honey in modern varieties), so it is not vegan. No vegan products or labeling are documented for this shop.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–18:00; open daily (both the shop and cafe)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (near Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Yamadaya is a long-established Miyajima confectioner founded in 1932 as a maker of momiji manju, the maple-leaf-shaped cakes that are the island's signature sweet. Its main store sits along the busy approach to Itsukushima Shrine. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Yamadaya is a long-established Miyajima confectioner founded in 1932 (Showa 7) as a maker of momiji manju, the maple-leaf-shaped cakes that are the island's signature sweet. Its main store sits on Miyajima (宮島町835-1), along the approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes that its confections are all handmade by artisans, and at the main store visitors can watch the sweets being made and even hand-bake their own.
chef_bio.specialty: Momiji manju in a wide range of flavors (more than a dozen year-round, over twenty including seasonal varieties) and its own popular kiriyoka (桐葉菓) — a chewy mochi-flour cake filled with a blend of house-made smooth and chunky bean paste, the shop's number-one seller.

anecdotes.text: The Miyajima main store doubles as an experience destination: a corner offers hand-baking of momiji manju (by reservation, with a souvenir), and an attached cafe (RAKU山田屋) serves the brand's handmade sweets on site.
Yamadaya developed kiriyoka, a chewy chestnut-brown mochi cake of blended bean pastes, which has become the brand's number-one product alongside momiji manju.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Yamadaya's official site and company profile give a founding of 1932 (昭和7年10月20日), 13+ year-round momiji-manju flavors (20+ with seasonal ones), and kiriyoka (桐葉菓) — mochi-flour wrapped around blended smooth/chunky bean paste — as the top seller, all handmade by artisans. The store page confirms the Miyajima main shop (宮島町835-1), hand-baking corner/experience and the RAKU山田屋 handmade-sweets cafe.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature products (momiji manju, kiriyoka) are wheat-flour cakes made in a shared confectionery production environment; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Momiji manju is an egg-based castella cake; no vegan products or egg/dairy-free handling are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, tourist-friendly confectionery shop on Miyajima's main approach where you can buy momiji manju, watch them being made, try a hand-baking experience, or relax in the attached cafe; pointing works fine and no Japanese is needed to order.

places.name: 大衆食堂 遊のすけ (Taishu Shokudo Yunosuke)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen is built around wheat-based soy sauce, fried foods and dumpling-style items like shumai, so gluten is pervasive and there is no documented gluten-free option or allergy handling. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. This is a meat-and-fish-forward town-Chinese shokudo and izakaya (shumai, fried dishes, set meals with soup, pickles and salad). Dishes are built around pork and other meats and are seasoned with soy-sauce-based seasonings; no plant-based menu or vegan accommodation has been described.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sun 11:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Tue closed. (Some sources list weekday lunch service 11:30-15:00; hours appear to vary, so confirm before a daytime visit.)
places.neighborhood: Central Hiroshima, Hondori arcade area near the Tatsumachi a
places.cuisine: Town-Chinese comfort food and set meals
places.notes: Opened on April 25, 2023, this is a modern take on the old-school neighborhood Chinese diner, billed as a town-Chinese (machi-chuka) eatery crossed with a neo-izakaya. It is the kind of place built for everything from a midday set meal to an early, casual drink with fri

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, in the Hondori arcade district of central Naka-ku.
chef_bio.background: Opened on April 25, 2023, this is a modern take on the old-school neighborhood Chinese diner, billed as a town-Chinese (machi-chuka) eatery crossed with a neo-izakaya. It is the kind of place built for everything from a midday set meal to an early, casual drink with friends.
chef_bio.philosophy: An easygoing, anyone-welcome shokudo atmosphere where you can eat well and drink casually from daytime onward.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty daily set meals and town-Chinese standards that rotate regularly, with the meat shumai set a signature plate; portions are known for being generous.

anecdotes.text: The shop has a retro bathhouse-tile look outside with white square tiles and a white noren curtain, and inside, cream walls, a wooden counter and a big TV for catching baseball games.
The signature meat shumai set (about 900 yen) comes with rice, soup, pickles and salad; the shumai has a dense, slightly chewy texture and arrives fast and piping hot.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Modokimaru and listing sites confirm 遊のすけ opened 2023-04-25 as a 町中華×ネオ居酒屋 at 中区本通1-22 (33 seats), serving from lunch with alcohol available from daytime; the exterior uses white square bathhouse-style tiles and a white noren, and the signature 肉しゅうまい set is ¥900 with rice, soup, pickles and salad. A specific '~21' set-meal count could not be verified, so it is described as a regularly rotating lineup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on fried town-Chinese dishes, dumplings/shumai and soy-sauce-seasoned plates, so wheat and shared frying are expected throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Town-Chinese set meals and izakaya dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese/Chinese soy-sauce-based seasonings, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are meat- and fish-forward (pork shumai, set meals with soup); no plant-based or vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming local eatery where a foreign student will be comfortable, though menus and staff are mostly in Japanese, so a translation app or pointing at dishes helps.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight, square-cut noodles a touch thinner than a bean sprout; smooth and slick on the throat, served on the firmer side as a closing bowl after drinks.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima chuka soba (Hiroshima-style Chinese noodle soup)
ramen.chashu: Two slices of braised pork chashu; cut and style not documented in detail.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles in a soy-based tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented, so it is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bones with a soy tare and pork chashu; no plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A gentle, golden pork-bone soy soup with a glint of fat on the surface and a slightly forward saltiness, paired with thin slick straight noodles. It speaks the everyday language of Hiroshima town-Chinese chuka soba, a light tonkotsu-shoyu lineage meant to be sipped to the last drop, often as a satisfying close to a meal or a few drinks.

places.highlight: A gentle, golden pork-bone soy soup with a glint of fat on the surface and a slightly forward saltiness, paired with thin slick straight noodles. It speaks the everyday language of Hiroshima town-Chinese chuka soba, a light tonkotsu-shoyu lineage meant to be sipped to the last drop, often as a satisfying close to a meal or a few drinks.

places.name: 中華そば つばめ (Chuka Soba Tsubame)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Chinese-style wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce broth; the soy sauce contains wheat. The oden is simmered in the same soy-based ramen stock and includes wheat-containing items (fish cakes, etc.). There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy-sauce base, topped with house chashu (pork) and the oden is built on the same animal stock. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:40-21:50); closed Sun. Closed for New Year (Dec 31–Jan 4).
places.neighborhood: 西区東観音町／天満町 (Higashi-Kannonmachi / Temmachō), central-west Hi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run Hiroshima ramen shop that has been serving the same pork-bone, soy-sauce-based chuka soba for around 40 years. The business has carried on across three generations, with the previous-generation owner still involved in preparing the broth, keeping the recipe

roles: broth still prepared by previous-generation owner
chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, started as a street-stall (yatai) ramen operation
chef_bio.background: A family-run Hiroshima ramen shop that has been serving the same pork-bone, soy-sauce-based chuka soba for around 40 years. The business has carried on across three generations, with the previous-generation owner still involved in preparing the broth, keeping the recipe and methods unchanged from the shop's street-stall beginnings.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to keeping the time-honored taste consistent rather than chasing trends - just the same broth done carefully, with a welcoming neighborhood atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style chuka soba: a pork-bone and soy-sauce broth with house-made chashu, green onion, and bean sprouts. Alongside the ramen, the shop simmers oden in a stock based on the same ramen broth, offering about eight varieties; the daikon radish is a standout, made by sourcing large radishes and using only the center portions, simmered for a precise time.

anecdotes.text: The pork-bone soy-sauce broth has been kept unchanged for about 40 years, passed down across three generations, with the former owner still helping make the soup today.
The oden is built on the ramen's own soup stock; the daikon is prepared from large radishes using only the center sections, simmered for a precise duration for the best flavor.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own gorp page states the pork-bone soy-sauce broth has stayed unchanged ~40 years across three generations with the previous owner still making it, traces the business to a yatai street stall, and lists chashu/green onion/bean sprouts on the chuka soba. It also describes oden built on the ramen stock (8 kinds, ~¥150 each), with the prized daikon cut only from the centers of large radishes and simmered a precise time. Seating is ~7 tables + 3 counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-containing soy-sauce broth are central to the menu, and oden shares the same soy-based stock, so gluten is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with pork chashu, and oden uses the same animal stock and fish-based items, so no dishes are free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a no-frills, cash-only local ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese; a foreign student comfortable pointing at the menu and knowing a few basic phrases will do fine, but little to no English support should be expected.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles developed by trial and error to match the soup; smooth, easy-slurping texture. No kaedama or large portions offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the single, unchanged tonkotsu-shoyu bowl with house chashu, scallion and bean sprouts (~750 yen).
ramen.chashu: House-made chashu cut from a lean portion (described as fat-trimmed / 脂身なし), braised-style and tender rather than charred.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this bowl unsuitable for gluten-free or celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered pork-bone broth seasoned with shoyu and finished with pork-fat richness, with pork chashu on top — no plant-based broth or topping option exists.

ramen.pantheon: A living example of old-school Hiroshima ramen: a tea-brown pork-bone-and-soy broth, gentle and nostalgic yet deepening with every sip, that traces its lineage to the city's postwar yatai (street-stall) tradition. Carried on across three generations with a recipe essentially unchanged for forty years, it is the antithesis of Tokyo's clear chicken-shoyu — you taste Hiroshima's softer, pork-led soul in the soup.

places.highlight: A living example of old-school Hiroshima ramen: a tea-brown pork-bone-and-soy broth, gentle and nostalgic yet deepening with every sip, that traces its lineage to the city's postwar yatai (street-stall) tradition. Carried on across three generations with a recipe essentially unchanged for forty years, it is the antithesis of Tokyo's clear chicken-shoyu — you taste Hiroshima's softer, pork-led soul in the soup.

places.name: ブルーマウンテン (Blue Mountain)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a traditional kissaten whose menu centers on omelette rice (omurice), hamburger steak, cakes and morning toast sets — wheat-based dishes and bread dominate, and there is no evidence of any gluten-free preparation or awareness. Celiac travelers should treat it as unsuitable.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Signature dishes (omurice made with egg, hamburger steak with demi-glace, cake sets, morning sets with toast and egg) rely on egg, dairy and meat. There is no evidence of plant-based menu items, so vegans should treat it as unsuitable.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 8:00-11:00 (morning), 11:00-17:00 (lunch); closed Tue
places.neighborhood: 中区本通 (Hondori / Tatemachi, central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running retro coffee shop tucked on the second floor of a building along the bustling Hondori shopping arcade in central Hiroshima, said to date back several decades. It is the kind of nostalgic neighborhood kissaten where regulars gather for conversation away fr

chef_bio.background: A long-running retro coffee shop tucked on the second floor of a building along the bustling Hondori shopping arcade in central Hiroshima. It is the kind of nostalgic neighborhood kissaten where regulars gather for conversation away from the crowds of the street below.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-fashioned coffee-shop fare, with omelette rice (omurice) — a fluffy egg over shrimp-and-pepper fried rice, topped with a small hamburger steak and sauce (¥1,200) — as a signature plate, alongside a morning toast set and cake/curry options.

anecdotes.text: The interior leans into Showa-era nostalgia: cream-toned walls and ceilings hung with artwork, red velvet chairs and chandelier-style lighting, with fresh flowers set on the tables.
Tables are finished with thin wooden strips about 5mm thick that give a subtle texture, and the upstairs room stays quiet and relaxed despite sitting right above the busy arcade.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog describes ブルーマウンテン as a 2F retro kissaten on Hondori with cream ceilings, wall artwork, wood flooring, red velvet chairs and chandelier lighting (26 seats), serving morning toast sets, omurice, curry and mini-cakes, with an omurice-with-hamburger plate at ¥1,200. Oomin's blog confirms the 2F-above-the-arcade quiet, table surfaces of ~5mm wooden strips, fresh flowers, and the omurice topped with a small hamburger and sauce. No source states a specific founding year.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, regulars-oriented retro coffee shop with no indication of English support, so a foreign student is best off going with a Japanese-speaking guide or being prepared to point at the menu and use simple Japanese.

places.name: 瀬戸内グリル じぱんぐ (Setouchi Grill Zipang)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The kitchen centers on anago (conger eel) prepared with sweet soy-based tare and tempura items (oysters, small sardines) coated in wheat batter and deep-fried in shared oil. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There is no documentation of GF menu items, GF soy sauce, or any allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around seafood (Seto Inland Sea conger eel, Miyajima oysters), fish cake (gansu), pork bowls, and karaage. Sauces are soy/fish-based. No documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00; irregular days off (calling ahead recommended)
places.neighborhood: 宮島 中江町（厳島神社裏手の「滝小路」、岩村もみじ屋の向かい / Nakae-cho, on the quiet "Ta
places.cuisine: Conger eel and oyster dishes / Setouchi grill and dining
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked away on the quiet "Taki Koji" lane behind Itsukushima Shrine, diagonally across from the well-known Iwamura Momiji-ya manju shop, this small grill is a welcome change of pace from the bustling main approach. Across two cozy floors with only about ten seats, it of

chef_bio.origin: Miyajima (Nakae-cho), behind Itsukushima Shrine, about a 15-16 minute walk from the ferry pier.
chef_bio.background: Tucked away on the quiet "Taki Koji" lane behind Itsukushima Shrine, diagonally across from the well-known Iwamura Momiji-ya manju shop, this small grill is a welcome change of pace from the bustling main approach. Across two cozy floors with only about ten seats (a counter downstairs and a few tables upstairs), it offers Hiroshima's signature flavors at prices that are notably gentle for Miyajima.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on local Seto Inland Sea ingredients and value: the anago is bought and grilled fresh daily, and the rice is the owners' own self-grown Koshihikari from Higashi-Hiroshima, served in an unpretentious, at-home atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Conger eel (anago) sourced fresh each morning from the Seto Inland Sea and grilled to order, served as anago-meshi rice bowls; plus tempura of locally grown Miyajima oysters (boiled, then fried). The kitchen also turns out small-sardine karaage, gansu (white fish cake), a popular signature karaage set, and a Hiroshima lemon pork bowl.

anecdotes.text: The conger eel is brought in fresh from the Seto Inland Sea every morning and grilled to order for the anago-meshi.
The rice served is the owners' own self-grown Koshihikari from Higashi-Hiroshima.
Prices are notably affordable for Miyajima, with dishes like a Hiroshima lemon pork bowl around 700-800 yen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mrs.living.jp confirms the address behind Itsukushima Shrine on Taki Koji lane opposite Iwamura Momiji-ya, ~10 seats over two floors, daily Seto-sourced anago (毎朝仕入れる瀬戸内産の穴子), self-grown Higashi-Hiroshima Koshihikari (東広島産の自家製コシヒカリ), and boiled-then-fried Miyajima oysters. freelife.ne.jp confirms the Hiroshima lemon pork bowl at ~700-800 yen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura items (oysters, small sardines) are coated in wheat batter and deep-fried, with no indication of a separate fryer or gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi and grilled dishes use a sweet soy-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around conger eel, oysters, fish cake, pork, and karaage; sauces are soy/fish-based, so vegan diners would face pervasive animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, friendly ~10-seat family spot off the main approach; a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way, and pointing at the photo menu makes ordering easy for a foreign student.

places.name: 台湾薬膳料理 青葉 (Taiwan Yakuzen Ryori Aoba)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. This is Taiwanese/Chinese cooking, where soy sauce and many sauces are wheat-based, and items like the dried-radish omelet, spring rolls and fried dishes typically involve wheat. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free menu, so this is not a safe choice for someone with celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward: the signature herbal hot pot broth contains duck, black-bone chicken and pork, and the menu leans heavily on shrimp, scallops, chicken and beef. A few plant-leaning dishes appear (stir-fried greens 炒青菜, homestyle tofu 家常豆腐, fried tofu, mapo eggplant, vegetable soup), but these may be cooked with meat-based broth or sauces, and there is no dedicated vegan/vegetarian menu. A flexible vegetarian could likely cobble together a vegetable-and-tofu meal by asking, but vegans should not assume any dish is animal-free without checking.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Lunch served weekdays only.
places.neighborhood: Hirosecho, Naka-ku (near Tokaichimachi tram stop, just west
places.cuisine: Taiwanese herbal-medicine cuisine and hot po
places.notes: A long-running Taiwanese restaurant in central Hiroshima's Hirosecho, just west of the Peace Memorial Park, open since 1993. It is known among locals as an everyday-quality spot for authentic Taiwanese home cooking rather than a special-occasion-only destination.

chef_bio.background: A long-running Taiwanese restaurant in central Hiroshima's Hirosecho, just west of the Peace Memorial Park, open since 1993. It is known among locals as an everyday-quality spot for authentic Taiwanese home cooking rather than a special-occasion-only destination.
chef_bio.philosophy: Wellness-minded dining that treats food as nourishment, blending traditional Taiwanese cooking with medicinal herbs.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiwanese herbal-medicine (薬膳) hot pot - the signature 沙茶火鍋 (shacha hot pot) is built on a deeply flavored broth made with around 25 medicinal herbs, with a base of ginger, mugwort (艾葉) and dong quai (当帰), and the herb mix shifts with the season. With advance notice the kitchen will tailor the medicinal ingredients to a diner's season, age, gender and physical condition.

anecdotes.text: Regulars say the food is delicious well beyond the famous medicinal hot pot, singling out the dried-radish omelet (切り干し大根のオムレツ) as a standout and praising the hearty meat-filled spring rolls and home-style dishes like stir-fried greens and tofu.
The herbal hot pot can be customized in advance to the diner's body and the time of year, with recommended ingredients chosen for each guest.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabelog confirms the Hirosecho address (2 min from Tokaichimachi), a 1993 opening, and the 沙茶火鍋 made with ginger/mugwort/dong quai plus ~25 seasonal herbs and 12 ingredients, tailored to each guest's 体調・年齢・性別・健康状態. syokuki.com praises the dried-radish omelet, meaty spring rolls, stir-fried greens and tofu as standouts beyond the hot pot.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Taiwanese/Chinese cooking here relies on soy-sauce-based seasonings and sauces that are normally wheat-based; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature herbal hot pot broth is animal-based (duck, black-bone chicken, pork meatballs), so vegetable dishes cooked in shared broth or sauces may carry animal content.

positives.text: With advance notice the kitchen will adjust the herbal hot pot's ingredients to suit a diner's needs, suggesting willingness to customize when asked ahead.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood Taiwanese spot where ordering happens in Japanese; no confirmation that English is spoken, so a foreign student may want a translation app or a guide for the herbal hot pot, which can be tailored to your body and the season if you ask in advance.

places.name: 権兵衛 広島 (Gonbei Hiroshima)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional oden house. Both broth styles rely on fish dashi and the signature miso oden uses white Fuchu miso (miso is typically made with wheat/barley koji and the dashi/seasonings contain wheat-based soy sauce), so the food is not gluten-free and there is no indication of GF accommodation. Not suitable for celiac travelers.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on fish-dashi oden and seafood/meat items (oysters, anago/conger eel, beef, chicken skin, handmade shumai, beef lung), and even vegetable items simmer in animal-based dashi. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–23:00 (closed Sun and holidays). Reported that the 2nd-floor seating stays open late into the night Tue–Sat.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area, near Kanayamacho tram stop)
places.notes: Founded in 1924 (Taisho 13), Gonbei is regarded as Hiroshima's oldest oden house. It began as a kaiseki restaurant where oden was first served almost as an appetizer while diners waited for their main courses, gradually becoming the dish the place is now known for. Orig

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1924 (Taisho 13), Gonbei is regarded as Hiroshima's oldest oden house. It began as a kaiseki restaurant where oden was first served almost as an appetizer while diners waited for their main courses, gradually becoming the dish the place is now known for. Originally in the western entertainment quarter before the war, it moved after the atomic bombing and settled in the Nagarekawa/Yakenbori nightlife district in 1953. The shop is now run by Kodama, the fourth-generation proprietor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house links its miso oden to the older tradition of dengaku (田楽), historically served with miso: the founder chose Fuchu white miso because it most closely resembled the miso he had used during his apprenticeship in Kyoto, blending it with the house dashi to create the restaurant's distinctive sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Two styles of oden: a Seto Inland Sea dashi oden and a signature white-miso oden made with Fuchu miso, including local oysters and anago (conger eel). A range of handmade items and unusual ingredients also appear, and meals are often finished with chameshi (tea rice), optionally with miso.

anecdotes.text: The unusual name is said to come from the old phrase 'nanashi no Gonbei' (nameless Gonbei): the shop operated without an official name, so people simply called it Gonbei - though the source notes this is an unconfirmed 'so they say' story.
The founder selected Fuchu white miso for the signature miso oden because it reminded him of the miso he had used during his apprenticeship in Kyoto.
The restaurant has kept a partnership of more than 50 years with Sapporo Lager beer.
