chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: akaboshi-tanteidan (a Sapporo Lager interview) confirms the ~100-year history, both dashi and miso oden, the Fuchu white miso chosen because the founder trained in Kyoto (初代が京都で修行... 一番近いのが府中市のみそ), the 50-year Sapporo tie, and identifies the current owner as fourth-generation Kodama-san (大将の児玉さん / 自分で4代目) - no first name given. shinise.tv gives the 1924 founding, 1953 Yakenbori move, and the hedged 'nanashi no Gonbei' name-origin tale.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Both the dashi oden and the signature white-miso (Fuchu miso) oden are built on traditional Japanese seasonings; miso and oden broths conventionally contain wheat (miso koji and wheat-based soy sauce), so the food should be treated as containing gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden simmers all ingredients together in shared fish dashi alongside oysters, anago, beef and chicken items, so vegetable items are not free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A historic, well-loved local oden house where Japanese is the working language and payment is cash only, so come with some basic phrases or a translation app and be ready to point at what looks good on the counter.

places.name: おでん 末吉 (Oden Suekichi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Oden is simmered in a dashi seasoned with regular Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat, and many oden items (fishcakes such as chikuwa, hanpen and satsuma-age) contain wheat starch/flour. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication that wheat-free preparation is available.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans — the broth is fish/bonito based and dishes include fishcakes, egg, beef neck meat, scallop and sea cucumber; even vegetables are simmered in the fish dashi.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00–22:00; closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: 中区銀山町 (near the Kanayamacho/銀山町 tram stop, central Hiroshima
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Suekichi is a small, owner-run oden specialist tucked into central Hiroshima. For years it occupied a cramped little space near the Kanayamacho tram stop before moving in May 2025 to a cleaner, calmer room of its own — a quiet refuge from the surrounding nightlife distr

chef_bio.background: Suekichi is a small, owner-run oden specialist tucked into central Hiroshima. For years it occupied a cramped little space near the Kanayamacho (Ginzan-cho) tram stop before moving in May 2025 to a cleaner, calmer room of its own, carrying over its old fixtures to keep the same feel - a quiet refuge from the surrounding nightlife district.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, careful approach: a hushed, relaxed atmosphere and patiently simmered ingredients, prizing refinement over bustle.
chef_bio.specialty: A clear, gentle, Kyoto-leaning oden dashi rather than a heavy dark broth. Beyond the classics like slow-simmered daikon and soft-boiled egg, the pot features Hiroshima's local 'koune' (beef neck meat) and unusual seasonal pieces such as scallop, sea cucumber and fresh wakame.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the oden as 'upper class yet rich in flavor' and 'gentle,' set in a calm, quiet room that feels subdued rather than rowdy despite sitting in the middle of the entertainment district.
In May 2025 the shop relocated from its former cramped venue to a cleaner, more accessible space, bringing over fixtures to recreate the previous ambiance.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabelog reviews confirm the May 8, 2025 relocation near Ginzan-cho (銀山町, romanized Kanayama-cho) station, koune/wakame/scallop/sea-cucumber oden, and a refined dashi ('上品だけどコクのある美味しいおでん'). The shop's Instagram account (@odensuekichi96) carries the owner's name 黒川 正法 (Kurokawa Masanori).

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is documented; the oden broth is seasoned with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and the pot contains wheat-bearing fishcakes, so cross-contamination of any item simmered in the shared pot is effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden in Japan is conventionally simmered in a dashi seasoned with standard soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented at this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items share a single fish/bonito-based oden pot, so vegetables are cooked in animal-derived broth; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, intimate counter-style oden shop where ordering is easiest with some basic Japanese; phone reservations help, and pointing at the day's oden pot works if you don't speak the language.

places.name: おかげさん (Okagesan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a Japanese seafood, sushi, and izakaya kitchen, dishes rely on regular soy sauce (which contains wheat) and other wheat-based seasonings, so it should not be considered safe for celiac/gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya and sushi spot built around fresh Seto Inland Sea fish; dishes use fish, dashi, and wheat-based soy sauce. No vegan menu or vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat: 11:30-14:00 (lunch on irregular days, ends when sold out), 17:30-22:00. Closed Sun and holidays. No sushi served in the evening.
places.neighborhood: 中区袋町（本通・八丁堀から徒歩圏、並木通り周辺）／ Fukuromachi, Naka-ku — central Hir
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A husband-and-wife team have run this small, warm seafood and sushi spot in central Hiroshima for around 17 years. The room keeps a nostalgic Showa-era feel, with a sushi-bar-style counter and a handful of seats tucked into the back.

chef_bio.background: A husband-and-wife team have run this small, warm seafood and sushi spot in central Hiroshima (Fukuro-cho, near Tatemachi) for around 17 years. The room keeps a nostalgic Showa-era feel, with a sushi-bar-style counter and a handful of seats tucked into the back.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh fish from the Seto Inland Sea, served as sushi and seasonal seafood dishes, with creative small plates (sushi features at lunch; evening service is seafood and izakaya plates). The reasonably priced lunch is a local favorite and runs until the day's catch sells out.

anecdotes.text: The couple have kept the restaurant in its current form for about 17 years, building a loyal following for their fresh, inventive cooking and homey atmosphere.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabelog confirms a husband-and-wife operation of about 17 years in Fukuro-cho (~269m from Tatemachi station), a retained Showa atmosphere (昭和の雰囲気が残っていました), Seto Inland Sea fish, counter seating, and lunch that ends when ingredients sell out; it notes sushi is not served in the evening.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese seafood/sushi/izakaya kitchen, dishes rely on standard soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on fish and seafood with fish-based dashi; shared prep makes this unsuitable for strict vegan or vegetarian diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm, husband-and-wife counter spot with a Showa-era feel; little to no English is documented, so a few basic Japanese phrases and a relaxed attitude will help a foreign student feel welcome.

places.name: 94BENIO (Kushibenio)
places.gf_detail: This is a yakitori izakaya. Grilled skewers are typically glazed with tare made from wheat-based soy sauce, and small plates such as the chicken-skin gyoza use a wheat wrapper, so the menu is not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet. No gluten-free accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: A chicken-focused yakitori izakaya built around grilled chicken skewers and chicken-skin gyoza, paired with sake. The food centers on poultry and other animal products, with no documented plant-based options, so it is not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori entertainment district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, intimate counter-only yakitori izakaya tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, open late into the early hours and popular with regulars.

chef_bio.background: A small, intimate counter-only yakitori izakaya (about eight seats) tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, open late into the early hours and popular with regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: Grilled chicken skewers and a much-loved chicken-skin gyoza, paired with reasonably priced sake.

anecdotes.text: Guests describe the owner as soft-spoken and courteous, presiding over a cozy counter room frequented by regulars; the chicken-skin gyoza is singled out as a favorite.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: tabelog places 94BENIO in Nagarekawa, a counter-only ~8-seat spot open Mon-Sat 19:00-03:00, with a mild-mannered owner and regulars praising the chicken-skin gyoza (鶏皮ぎょうざが1番すき).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, late-night locals' counter bar in the heart of Hiroshima's nightlife district; basic spoken Japanese and comfort with a small, regulars-heavy room will make the visit smoother, and note that indoor smoking is permitted.

places.name: 海鮮酒場 牡蠣いかだ屋 (Kaisen Sakaba Kaki Ikadaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes dressed or simmered with standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-based) and includes battered items such as oyster tempura, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster and seafood izakaya; the core menu is oysters, sashimi, fish, and wagyu, with soups and sauces built on fish/dashi and soy sauce. No vegan dishes or accommodations are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun & holidays 17:00-02:00 (food L.O. 01:00, drink L.O. 01:30). Closed Monday (if Monday is a holiday, closed Tuesday instead).
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Fukuromachi (near Namiki Rotary; short walk from Ho
places.notes: A casual seafood izakaya in central Hiroshima built around the region's most famous ingredient: the local oyster. The kitchen sources Hiroshima-brand oysters (described as 'Kaki Komachi') prized for their plump, thick bodies and deep umami.

chef_bio.background: A casual seafood izakaya in central Hiroshima (Fukuro-cho, near Hatchobori) built around the region's most famous ingredient: the local oyster. The kitchen sources Hiroshima-brand oysters (the 'Kaki Komachi' brand) prized for their plump, thick bodies and deep umami.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on fresh seasonal ingredients and approachable Hiroshima specialties in a relaxed, group-friendly setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters prepared many ways - raw, grilled with assorted toppings, as oyster tempura, and in a signature year-round oyster miso hot pot - alongside fresh sashimi platters and an extensive sake selection chosen to pair with the seafood.

anecdotes.text: The house signature is an oyster miso hot pot that can be enjoyed any time of year, making it a reliable way to taste Hiroshima oysters even outside peak season.
Open until 2 a.m., it suits a late dinner after sightseeing or a second stop following another venue, and pairs its seafood with 30-plus drink varieties.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: suocca.net confirms the Fukuro-cho location, Hiroshima 'Kaki Komachi' brand oysters (広島県産 牡蠣小町), the 1,850-yen oyster miso hot pot, grilled oysters and sashimi. hotpepper confirms 17:00-02:00 hours, 30+ drink varieties, and a note that raw-oyster service pauses April-September.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu relies on standard Japanese soy sauce and includes fried/battered items such as oyster tempura, so wheat exposure is pervasive.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is built around oysters, fish, dashi, and meat; no separation from animal ingredients is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local oyster izakaya open late into the night; staff communication is mainly in Japanese, so a translation app or some basic Japanese helps, but the relaxed group-friendly atmosphere is welcoming.

places.name: 魚地心 広島八丁堀店 (Uochishin Hiroshima Hatchobori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. As a seafood izakaya, the menu relies on wheat-based soy sauce, ponzu, batters (fried oysters, gansu fish cakes), and dashi-based dishes; no dedicated GF menu or fryer is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire concept centers on fish, oysters, sea urchin, and other seafood, with wheat-based soy sauce and fish-stock dashi throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Sun, Tue-Thu & holidays 17:00-24:00; Mon 17:00-22:00; Fri-Sat & day before holidays 17:00-25:00 (Tabelog lists Mon-Thu/Sun/holidays 17:00-00:00, Fri-Sat 17:00-01:00). Open year-round (no regular closing day).
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori / Horikawacho, Naka Ward (around the Ebisu-dori arcade)
places.cuisine: Seafood izakaya specializing in Seto Inland
places.notes: A spacious multi-floor seafood izakaya in central Hiroshima near the Ebisu-dori arcade, built around fresh fish from the Seto Inland Sea and Hiroshima's celebrated oysters.

chef_bio.background: A spacious multi-floor seafood izakaya (occupying the 2nd-4th floors, ~106 seats) in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district near the Ebisu-dori arcade, built around fresh fish from the Seto Inland Sea and the region's celebrated oysters.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carefully selected dishes built on premium, ingredient-first sourcing (素材に拘った厳選料理).
chef_bio.specialty: Seto Inland Sea seafood and oysters served many ways - raw, grilled, steamed, and fried - alongside sashimi, seared fish, sea-urchin-and-spinach (ウニほーれん), handmade gansu fish cake, anago rice, and an oyster dashi tea-rice to finish.
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms a 2F-4F seafood izakaya near Hatchobori station serving Seto Inland Sea fish and Hiroshima oysters raw/grilled/steamed/fried, plus gansu, uni-spinach and oyster-dashi chazuke, under the concept 素材に拘った厳選料理. tabelog adds ~106 seats, one-year oysters, a five-way oyster tasting, and anago rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi, simmered, and grilled seafood dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce and ponzu, which contain wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A large, lively local seafood izakaya geared toward Japanese diners and group banquets, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some Japanese (or a translation app) and is best reserving a private room in advance.

places.name: 牡蠣フライ串と麦酒 (Kaki Fry Kushi to Bakushu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is breaded, deep-fried oyster skewers — the coating is wheat flour and panko (breadcrumbs), and items are deep-fried in shared oil. The anago-meshi skewer and other fried offerings also rely on wheat. No gluten-free options are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire concept is built around fried oysters (shellfish) and anago (conger eel), both animal products; batter/breading typically contains egg. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00 - 17:00 (daily). Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (within walking distance
places.cuisine: Fried oyster skewers and local Miyajima beer
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on the Miyajima Omotesando shopping street in March 2022, on the ground floor of the Kinsuikan building just a few minutes' walk from the ferry terminal and within strolling distance of Itsukushima Shrine. It serves Miyajima's famous oysters in the most portable

chef_bio.background: Opened on the Miyajima Omotesando shopping street in March 2022, on the ground floor of the Kinsuikan building (the ryokan behind it) just a few minutes' walk from the ferry terminal and within strolling distance of Itsukushima Shrine. It serves Miyajima's famous oysters in the most portable form possible - hot, freshly fried oysters on a skewer that you can eat as you wander the shopping street.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly deep-fried oyster skewers, served single-skewer style for walking and eating, alongside anago (conger eel) skewers and local beer.

anecdotes.text: A standout item is the limited-quantity anago-meshi skewer (around 500 yen) - Miyajima's classic conger-eel-over-rice reimagined as a single hand-held skewer with sweet sauce and optional sansho.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The kenbaiki-pro case study and Retty confirm the Kinsuikan 1F location on Miyajima Omotesando, a March 2022 opening operated by the ~120-year-old Kinsuikan inn, and 11:00 opening. miyajima-komachi confirms the ~500-yen limited 'あなごめし串' skewer alongside the fried-oyster-skewer specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is breaded, deep-fried oyster skewers; coating is wheat flour and panko and items are fried in shared oil, so cross-contamination is inherent and unavoidable here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All offerings are oyster (shellfish) or anago (eel) based and fried in the same oil; there are no plant-based items.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple grab-and-go skewer stand where you point at what you want and pay — very easy for a foreign student even with no Japanese, though staff likely speak limited English.

places.name: 次郎吉 (Jirokichi)
places.gf_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour and the usual sauce contains wheat, so the regular dishes are not gluten-free. However, the shop publicly states it can accommodate gluten-free requests (alongside vegetarian and vegan). Treat GF as available on request rather than guaranteed: confirm the specific dish, batter, sauce, and griddle handling directly, ideally by phoning ahead.
places.vegan_detail: Most of the menu is meat/seafood-based (pork, squid tempura, egg, plus oyster/offal teppanyaki) and standard okonomiyaki contains egg and meat, so the default dishes are not vegan or vegetarian. The shop explicitly advertises that it can prepare vegetarian and vegan versions of its okonomiyaki, and the official Hiroshima tourism site lists it as veg/vegan-friendly and popular with international diners. Best treated as reliable-on-request: state your needs (no meat, no egg, no fish/bonito) clearly and ideally arrange in advance.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 17:30–22:00; closed Mondays. Lunch menu available from 800 yen. One-drink-per-person request (200 yen water charge if no drink ordered; courses excluded).
places.neighborhood: 中区中町 (Nakamachi) — Namiki-dori / Fukuromachi area, just east
places.notes: Owner-chef Fukui Kyohei, born in 1985, is a Hiroshima native who grew up with okonomiyaki as part of everyday life. He trained for four years at the well-known local okonomiyaki house Goemon before opening his own place in February 2014.

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Fukui Kyohei, born in 1985, is a Hiroshima native who grew up with okonomiyaki as part of everyday life. He trained for four years at the well-known local okonomiyaki house Goemon before opening his own place in February 2014.
chef_bio.philosophy: He works the teppan himself every day and says his motivation comes from customers' smiles, with a guiding belief that bringing joy to guests is his own joy; he emphasizes building real relationships with local producers for his ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, worked daily on the iron griddle.

anecdotes.text: The signature is the shiso-laced 'Jirokichi' okonomiyaki, while the most-repeated order is the 'Jirokichi roll' packing soba noodles, scallion and cheese into a crisp roll. Look for the yellow sign across from the Seven-Eleven, about a 10-minute walk from Fukuromachi tram stop.

Because traditional okonomiyaki uses egg and meat that vegans and vegetarians cannot eat, the shop makes a point of offering vegetarian and vegan versions plus gluten-free accommodation, and is noted as very popular with overseas visitors.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hitosara's chef profile states 福井恭平 (b.1985, Hiroshima), four years at 五右衛門, and independence in Feb 2014, plus his 'customers' smiles' motivation and producer ties. hitosara's shop page confirms the shiso 次郎吉 okonomiyaki, the soba/scallion/cheese 次郎吉ロール, and the yellow sign opposite a Seven-Eleven ~10 min from Fukuromachi. dive-hiroshima confirms vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free options and strong overseas popularity.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop states it can handle food allergies / dietary restrictions, and publicly advertises vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free accommodation.
Listed by the official Hiroshima tourism site as able to accommodate vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free needs, and popular with international guests.

positives.text: Explicitly offers vegan and vegetarian okonomiyaki on request, a rarity for Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki which normally relies on wheat batter, egg and meat.
Gluten-free accommodation is advertised as available, so the kitchen is accustomed to adapting dishes for dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming counter-and-table griddle spot used to international visitors; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, and it helps to mention vegan/vegetarian or gluten-free needs clearly (ideally when you arrive or by phone ahead).

places.name: 桂愛 (Keiai)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free oriented. Documented dishes (soy-sauce ramen, gyoza, soy-meat karaage, mapo tofu over rice) typically involve wheat via noodles, dumpling skins, batter, regular soy sauce, and wheat-gluten soy meat. A GF diner must ask; no GF accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Effectively fully plant-based: multiple reviews state it uses no meat, fish, or eggs, and it operates as shojin (Buddhist temple) style Chinese cuisine. Dishes such as mapo tofu and karaage use soy meat, with plant-based broth. No dairy was noted in described dishes, though dairy was not explicitly ruled out by every source; confirm if strictly vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-22:00 (sources vary slightly; Tabelog lists 11:30-14:00, 18:00-22:00). Hours and closed days may change; confirm with the shop before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Nakamachi, Naka Ward (Hondori, near Fukuromachi tram stop)
places.notes: 桂愛 (Keiai) opened on April 14, 2024 in a previously vacant storefront in Nakamachi, central Hiroshima, and is run by two women, one cooking and one handling the front of house.

roles: owner-operators (two women: one in the kitchen, one front-of-house)
chef_bio.background: 桂愛 (Keiai) opened on April 14, 2024 in a previously vacant storefront in Nakamachi, central Hiroshima (Churis Nakamachi Bldg 1F), and is run by two women, one cooking and one handling the front of house.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking based on Buddhist temple cuisine (精進料理) that uses no meat, fish, or eggs and also omits the five pungent roots (garlic, onions, scallions, Chinese chives), aiming for a gentle, compassionate flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan/vegetarian Chinese cooking in a Buddhist shojin style: soy-meat mapo tofu, soy-sauce ramen, soy-meat karaage, and gyoza, with plant-based stock and a daily lunch set around 990 yen.

anecdotes.text: Local food writers repeatedly call it possibly the only vegetarian Chinese restaurant in the city, and note that vegan/vegetarian Chinese is an unusual, almost unheard-of concept locally.
A reviewer described the soy-protein dishes as having a gently compassionate flavor (じんわりと慈悲深い味), lighter than meat-based versions.
Another diner emphasized it serves completely vegetarian Chinese food with no meat, fish, or eggs, nor the five pungent roots.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: modokimaru confirms the April 14, 2024 opening at Churis Nakamachi Bldg 1F, the two-woman operation, shojin principles (no meat/fish/egg, no garlic/onion/scallion/nira), the ~990-yen lunch set of soy-sauce ramen, mapo tofu and karaage, the 'first vegan/vegetarian Chinese I'd heard of in the city' remark, and the 'じんわりと慈悲深い味' description. ameblo corroborates the fully vegetarian, five-pungent-free cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu includes soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen, indicating use of regular wheat-based soy sauce typical of Chinese cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is described as entirely plant-based with no meat, fish, or eggs used anywhere, which substantially limits animal-product cross-contact risk.

positives.text: Fully plant-based shojin-style Chinese kitchen using no meat, fish, or eggs, making it a reliable choice for vegans and vegetarians.
Also omits the five pungent roots (garlic, onion, scallion, Chinese chives), helpful for those following strict shojin or allium-free diets.

cultural_comfort.note: Small two-person family-style shop with a ticket vending machine and Japanese-language menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, but the all-plant menu makes ordering low-stress for a foreign student.

places.name: 宮島 Cuillere（キュイエール） (Miyajima Cuillere)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature lunch sandwiches are made on house-baked bread (wheat) and are not gluten-free. French bistro dishes may use flour, butter sauces, and standard seasonings. Anyone strictly gluten-free should contact the restaurant in advance to ask what can be prepared.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen offers a dedicated vegetarian and vegan menu alongside its main bistro and sandwich menus, confirmed on the official website. Most of the regular menu is meat/fish-based (oyster dishes, charcuterie, confit chicken, seasonal fish), so vegans should order specifically from the veg/vegan menu and confirm preparation. Exact current vegan dishes are not published online; contact the restaurant to confirm.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00) / 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Open Mon-Sun and holidays. Hours may change without notice.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (a back alley off Machiya-dori beyond the Omotesando shopping street, ~7 min walk from Miyajima Pier)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Run by a French-trained chef who cooks authentic French bistro and Western fare on Miyajima island. The owner draws on training in France and was particularly drawn to French regional and traditional home-style dishes and pastries. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Run by a French-trained chef who cooks authentic French bistro and Western fare on Miyajima island. The owner draws on training in France and was particularly drawn to French regional and traditional home-style dishes and pastries.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to showcasing seasonal Seto Inland Sea and Hiroshima produce, treating humble local ingredients with classic French technique, and pairing house-baked bread with premium fillings.
chef_bio.specialty: Casual French bistro cooking built around carefully sourced Hiroshima and nationwide ingredients: at lunch, a rotating range of sandwiches on house-baked bread; at dinner, proper French dishes such as oyster bourguignon (牡蠣のブルギニョン) and ayu confit (鮎のコンフィ), paired with local sake, craft beer, and sommelier-chosen wine.

anecdotes.text: The name Cuillere is French for spoon, reflecting the owner's French bistro identity.
Tucked in a back alley off the Omotesando shopping street about a 7-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, it is a quiet, 22-seat hideaway away from the tourist crowds.
Lunch centers on sandwiches pairing select ingredients with house-baked bread, while dinner offers full French dishes like oyster bourguignon and ayu confit.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms a French chef-owner carefully cooking select Hiroshima and nationwide ingredients (フレンチシェフのオーナーが... 丁寧に調理), lunch sandwiches on house-baked bread, and a vegan menu. tabiiro confirms the back-alley Omotesando location ~7 min from the ferry, 22 seats, and dinner French dishes including oyster bourguignon and ayu confit with local sake, craft beer and sommelier wine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-baked wheat bread is central to the lunch menu (sandwiches), so a wheat-flour environment is present in the kitchen; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A dedicated vegetarian/vegan menu is offered, but the kitchen also prepares meat, oysters, and seafood; no statement about separate prep or cross-contact controls is published.

positives.text: The restaurant explicitly provides a vegetarian and vegan menu in addition to its standard offerings.

cultural_comfort.note: A small chef-run bistro on Miyajima used to international tourists; lunchtime sandwiches are very approachable, and it is wise to reserve ahead online for dinner and to point to the menu rather than relying on English.

places.name: ろくめいかん (Rokumeikan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The core dish is sukiyaki, which is simmered in a warishita sauce based on wheat-containing soy sauce, so the menu is not gluten-free and there is no indication of GF handling.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around Hiroshima beef sukiyaki, with sets including beef, local egg, and dashi/warishita; there is no documented vegan or vegetarian option, so it is not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima Shrine / Omotesando area, ~3 min walk from the ferry pier)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Rokumeikan is a small, recently opened sukiyaki restaurant on Miyajima, a short three-minute walk from the ferry pier near the Itsukushima Shrine approach. It serves Hiroshima beef sukiyaki and quietly focuses on giving each visiting group an unhurried, attentive meal.

chef_bio.background: Rokumeikan is a small, recently opened sukiyaki restaurant on Miyajima, about a two-minute walk from the ferry pier at the start of the Itsukushima Shrine approach. It serves Hiroshima beef sukiyaki and quietly focuses on giving each visiting group an unhurried, attentive meal, preparing the sukiyaki carefully for one party at a time.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name draws on the spirit of the historic Rokumeikan as a place where people gather, converse, and are welcomed; the kitchen sources beef, vegetables, eggs, and seasonings from Hiroshima as much as possible and aims to preserve the natural flavor of each ingredient while leaving diners with a calm memory.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima beef sukiyaki, with tiered sets (梅/竹/松, roughly 80g-240g of beef) that pair the beef with carefully selected seasonal vegetables, grilled tofu, Hiroshima egg, rice, and red miso soup.

anecdotes.text: The shop welcomes small parties and notes that reservations for even two or three guests are no problem, reflecting its intimate, one-group-at-a-time style.
It opened recently as a new sukiyaki restaurant on Miyajima, about a two-minute walk from the ferry pier.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: TableCheck・ヒトサラ・宮島観光協会ブログ・indeed求人が、宮島桟橋から徒歩約2分の新しいすき焼き店で、広島牛のすき焼きを一組ずつ丁寧に提供し、野菜・卵・調味料を可能な限り広島産でそろえ、少人数（2〜3名）の予約も歓迎、店名は『人が集い・語らい・もてなす』鹿鳴館に由来する、と伝える。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant specializes in sukiyaki, which is cooked in a warishita sauce based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free version is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu sets are centered on Hiroshima beef and include local egg and dashi-based broth, with no documented vegan or vegetarian alternative.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, reservation-focused local sukiyaki shop that serves groups one at a time, so a foreign student will be most comfortable booking ahead and being ready to communicate in Japanese.

places.name: 宮島ゲストハウス三國屋 (Miyajima Guest House Mikuniya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Japanese vegetable curry is typically thickened with a wheat-based roux, and the dinner is served self-service as a single set dish, so it should not be assumed gluten-free. A gluten-sensitive diner would need to ask the family directly about the roux and seasonings before relying on it. Breakfast items (bread, cereal, oatmeal) also contain gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The single dinner dish is a handmade vegetable curry. The official site states plainly that no animal products are used at all ("動物性食材は一切使用しておりません"), so the curry itself is fully vegan. Note: an optional fried-chicken (karaage) topping is offered for those who want it, and the shared kitchen stocks non-vegan convenience items (instant noodles, microwave meals), so a vegan diner should simply order the curry without the karaage topping. The curry is the only cooked meal served and it is plant-based by default.
places.hours_raw: Dinner (curry) 18:30-20:30, breakfast 5:30-9:30 (at the house / self-service). Dinner served daily except holidays.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (about a 5-min walk from Itsukushima Shrine, near Momijidani Park)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Mikuniya was started in 2013 by a father who wished to convey more of Miyajima's charm to people from Japan and abroad. Originally a social-studies teacher, he chose the guesthouse form rather than a hotel, believing it would allow a more homey and flexible hospitality. The building was once used as a recreation facility of the Mint Bureau, and with its large Japanese garden and big living room it still preserves a Japanese ambiance today. In 2020 an annex, a renovated 100-year-old traditional house, was also added. It is now run by the founder's son, Junya Terasawa, together with the resident cat "Miku." (Map pin approximate - Omotesando; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: 三國屋は2013年、宮島の魅力を国内外の人にもっと伝えたいと願った一人の父によって始められました。彼はもともと社会科の教員で、ホテルではなくゲストハウスという形を選ぶことで、よりアットホームで柔軟なもてなしができると考えました。建物はかつて造幣局の保養所として使われていたもので、広い日本庭園と大きなリビングルームを備え、和の趣を今に残しています。2020年には築100年の古民家を改装した別館も加わりました。現在は創業者の息子・寺澤淳也さんが、看板猫の『みく』とともに切り盛りしています。
chef_bio.philosophy: ホテルではなくゲストハウスという形を選び、国内外の旅人と島の暮らしをつなぐ、アットホームで柔軟なもてなしを大切にしています。近年は宿泊客だけでなく、島で働く人や住む人にもカレーを開放し、地域の食卓のような場を目指しています。
chef_bio.specialty: 夕食に毎日手作りされる、動物性食材を一切使わない野菜カレー。野菜の甘みとスパイスのバランスが特徴で、希望者には鶏の唐揚げをトッピングできます。セルフサービスで、旅の仲間と語らいながらゆっくり味わえます。

anecdotes.text: 元社会科教師だった父が、宮島の魅力をもっと伝えたいという想いから2013年に開いた宿。ホテルではなくゲストハウスという形を選んだのは、より親密で柔軟なもてなしをするためでした。
建物はもともと造幣局の保養所として使われていた歴史を持ち、広い日本庭園と大きなリビングを備えています。
もともと宿泊者向けだった手作り野菜カレーを、宿泊しない方や宮島で働く人・住む人にも提供し始めました。基本のカレーはヴィーガン対応で、希望者は鶏の唐揚げをトッピングとして追加できます。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト（miyajimamikuniya.com）とnote記事が、2013年に元社会科教師の父が創業し、造幣局保養所だった建物（日本庭園・大リビング）を使い、2020年に築100年古民家の別館を加え、現在は息子・寺澤淳也さんと看板猫みくが運営、夕食は動物性不使用の手作りヴィーガンカレーで、希望者は鶏唐揚げをトッピング可、近年は非宿泊者や島で働く人にも開放している、と伝える。

vegan_cross_contact.text: The handmade vegetable curry uses no animal products at all, but an optional fried chicken (karaage) topping is offered and prepared in the same home kitchen, and the shared kitchen also stocks non-vegan convenience items; a vegan diner should order the curry without the karaage topping.

positives.text: 夕食の野菜カレーは『動物性食材は一切使用しておりません』と明記された、毎日手作りのプラントベースのカレーです。

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, home-style family-run guesthouse that actively welcomes international visitors, so a foreign student can comfortably arrange a meal here, ideally messaging ahead.

places.name: ちんちくりん 薬研堀本店 (Chinchikurin Yagenbori Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki: the crepe-like batter is wheat flour, the signature dish is layered with wheat noodles (custom-made by Isono Seimen), and it is finished with Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation, so it is not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg, pork, beef chuck, seafood (squid tempura, oysters), cod roe, cheese, and offal cooked on a shared teppan, with wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, so it is not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–00:00 next day (food L.O. 23:30, drink L.O. 23:30; delivery/takeout until 23:00). Closed every Tuesday.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori / Nagarekawa / Ebisucho area (Tanakamachi, Naka Ward)
places.notes: This is the sōhonten (flagship) of Chinchikurin, a Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppan house in the Yagenbori area of central Naka-ku. The shop frames itself as a keeper of Hiroshima's food culture, presenting layered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki as the city's local soul fo

chef_bio.background: This is the sōhonten (flagship head shop) of Chinchikurin, a Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppan house on Yagenbori street in central Naka-ku. The shop frames itself as a keeper of Hiroshima's food culture, presenting layered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki as the city's local soul food and welcoming solo diners, families, and regulars at its counter and tatami seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Inheriting and advancing Hiroshima's okonomiyaki culture, with an emphasis on the shared, convivial experience between the cook at the teppan and the diner.
chef_bio.specialty: The house namesake 'Chinchikurin-yaki' is a classic Hiroshima okonomiyaki built with egg and squid tempura; other signatures include a beef-chuck (中落ち) okonomiyaki and a mentaiko-cheese version, alongside teppan-grilled offal and seafood. The noodles are custom-made by Isono Seimen and are thoroughly blanched to bring out their natural sweetness, then seared in soybean oil for a crisp-outside, chewy-inside texture, and paired with Otafuku sauce.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen sources its okonomiyaki noodles from a dedicated noodle maker, Isono Seimen, and blanches them to draw out sweetness before searing in soybean oil for a crisp-yet-chewy finish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト（chinchikurin.com / okonomiyaki.chinchikurin.com）が、当店を広島市中区薬研堀通りにある『ちんちくりんの総本店』とし、磯野製麺の麺をしっかり湯がいて甘みを引き出し大豆白絞油で焼き上げること、オタフクソース使用、看板のちんちくりん焼（玉子・いか天）、中落ち・明太チーズ等のメニュー、広島の食文化を受け継ぎ発展させる姿勢を明記している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is assembled and cooked on a shared teppan griddle where wheat batter and wheat noodles are cooked alongside all other dishes; no separate gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are cooked on a shared teppan used for pork, beef, seafood, and egg; no vegan preparation area is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, welcoming teppan counter where the cook interacts with diners; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, and solo diners and families are explicitly welcomed.

places.name: お好み・鉄板焼道場 三箆 (Okonomi Teppanyaki Dojo Mihera)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish, Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and wheat noodles, and dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce that contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan/vegetarian-friendly. Okonomiyaki here is layered with pork and finished with egg and bonito-based sauce, and the teppanyaki menu centers on pork, chicken (seseri, bonjiri) and seafood (squid, shrimp). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun & holidays: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:45), 18:00-23:00. Wednesday closed. (Note: Tabelog lists the dinner start as 17:00 while the official site lists 18:00; treated as 18:00 per official site.)
places.neighborhood: Nobori-chō, Naka-ku (Hatchōbori / Ebisu-chō area, walkable f
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-run okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop tucked into the Nobori-chō neighborhood in central Hiroshima, an easy walk from the Hondōri and Hatchōbori shopping streets. The proprietor honed the craft at a well-known Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop before striking out

chef_bio.background: A small, family-run okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop tucked into the Nobori-chō neighborhood in central Hiroshima, an easy walk from the Hondōri and Hatchōbori shopping streets. The proprietor honed the craft at a well-known Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop before striking out to open this independent counter spot.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop leans into the energy of the open counter, where guests sit close to the griddle and watch their food cooked in front of them, paired with table seats that have their own built-in teppan grills.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki where the noodles are spread wide and crisped on the griddle and dusted with garlic powder for a savory, energizing finish; signature plates include the house Mihera-yaki, a spicy tan-tan style okonomiyaki, and a crispy griddle-fried potato salad. There are also colour-coded topping sets (red kimchi/gochujang, black curry/cheese, white cream/cheese) and teppanyaki small plates like lotus-root butter grill and garlic shrimp.

anecdotes.text: The name 三箆 (Mihera) refers to three overlapping griddle spatulas (hera), which form the shop's logo.
The okonomiyaki noodles are spread wide and crisped on the teppan with lard into individual strands and finished with garlic powder for a crunchy, savory bite.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ぐるなび（Gnavi）が、店名『三箆』は三枚のヘラが重なるロゴに由来し、店主は有名広島お好み焼店で修業、麺は鉄板に広げてラードでカリッと焼き上げガーリックパウダーで仕上げること、名物のみへら焼・担々お好み焼・クリスピーポテトサラダ、赤（キムチ）黒（カレー/チーズ）白（クリーム/チーズ）のトッピングセット、蓮根バター・ガーリックシュリンプ等の鉄板料理、鉄板付きカウンター席を紹介している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour okonomiyaki batter and wheat noodles are cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside all other dishes, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared teppan is used for pork, chicken, seafood and egg, so any non-meat item would be cooked on the same surface as animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, lively neighborhood counter shop where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way; English support is not advertised, so come ready to enjoy a casual local atmosphere.

places.name: お好み焼 鉄板焼 やつなみ (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Yatsunami)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house. Okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and layered with wheat-based yakisoba/udon noodles (Isono Seimen), so the core dishes are not gluten-free. The house uses Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based. No gluten-free menu, preparation, or accommodation is documented, and food is cooked on a shared communal teppan griddle. Not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki batter typically contains egg and the dish is built around pork and topped with egg; signature items include pork-cartilage stew, salt-grilled beef shoulder (corne), and seasonal oysters. Sauces and toppings are animal-derived. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays & Saturday: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays. (Note: Tabelog lists slightly broader hours of 11:30-14:30 and 17:00-23:00; the more recent Hitosara listing of 11:30-14:00 / 17:30-22:00 is used here.)
places.neighborhood: Kamihatchobori, Naka-ku (near Hiroshima Castle and Shukkeien
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yatsunami opened in November 2019 in the Kamihatchobori business district, a short walk from Hiroshima Castle and Shukkeien Garden. Owner-chef Yasuo Yoshino (born 1981), who grew up in Hiroshima, trained at well-regarded Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops and at teppanyaki res

chef_bio.background: Yatsunami opened in November 2019 in the Kamihatchobori business district, a short walk from Hiroshima Castle and Shukkeien Garden (about 170m from Shukkeien-mae streetcar stop). Owner-chef Yasuo Yoshino (born 1981), who grew up in Hiroshima and discovered okonomiyaki through student part-time work, trained at well-regarded Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops and at teppanyaki restaurants in Tokyo before opening his own counter. He largely runs the place himself, cooking on the griddle in front of guests at its roughly 21 seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yoshino aims to preserve the 'unchanging, old-fashioned flavors of Hiroshima' (昔ながらの変わらない広島の味) and to keep the shop a relaxed gathering place for the neighborhood. He sources locally, using Isono Seimen noodles and Otafuku sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with cabbage and crisp noodles (the signature 'Yatsunami-yaki'), alongside teppanyaki such as salt-grilled corne (rare marbled beef shoulder). The slow-simmered pork-cartilage stew (豚軟骨のすじ煮込み) in a lightly sweet broth is described as the most repeat-ordered dish.

anecdotes.text: The lightly sweet slow-cooked pork-cartilage stew is reported to be the restaurant's most repeat-ordered item.
Yoshino trained at prominent Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops and Tokyo teppanyaki restaurants before opening Yatsunami in November 2019.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ヒトサラが、店主・吉野恭生（1981年広島生まれ、学生時代のアルバイトでお好み焼に魅了）が広島の名店と東京の鉄板焼で修業し2019年11月に上八丁堀で開業、昔ながらの広島の味を守り磯野製麺・オタフクを使うこと、名物やつなみ焼・塩コーネ・一番リピートされる豚軟骨のすじ煮込みを伝える。食べログは上八丁堀（縮景園前電停174m）、鉄板カウンター・テーブル計21席を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All food is cooked on a single shared teppan griddle alongside wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant uses Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is continuously used for pork, beef, oysters and egg, so any item would share surfaces with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run neighborhood okonomiyaki counter where ordering and chatting will go most smoothly with a bit of basic Japanese, though pointing at the menu and watching the chef cook on the griddle makes it approachable for a friendly visitor.

places.name: 中華そば うぐいす (Chuka Soba Uguisu)
places.gf_detail: Wheat-based ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (tonkotsu-shoyu) broth are the core of every bowl, so the signature chuka soba is not gluten-free. Oden side dishes are simmered in soy-based dashi and many oden items (fish cakes, etc.) contain wheat. This is a tiny one-person counter shop with no allergen accommodations documented. Not suitable for a gluten-free traveler.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy-sauce base and bowls are topped with chashu pork; oden is simmered in fish/meat-based dashi. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options. Not suitable for a vegan or vegetarian diner.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat and days before holidays 20:00–02:00 next day; closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Ebisucho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] One of the original sources of Hiroshima-style chuka soba. The shop traces back to 1948 (Showa 23), when three ramen yatai (food stalls) appeared in postwar Hiroshima and launched the city's ramen culture; Uguisu was one of those three. The founder is said to have learn

chef_bio.origin: 広島市中区流川町（central Hiroshima)
chef_bio.background: One of the original sources of Hiroshima-style chuka soba. The shop traces back to 1948 (Showa 23), when three ramen yatai (food stalls) appeared in postwar Hiroshima and launched the city's ramen culture; Uguisu was one of those three. The founder is said to have learned how to make soup at a small eatery in Manchuria before opening a stall in Hiroshima's Nakashinjidai district, eventually settling into the Nagarekawa shop that has watched over the neighborhood's nightlife for decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-fashioned tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone soy sauce) chuka soba, often ordered with a generous heap of green onions (negi chuka soba), plus simple oden simmering on the counter as a side.

anecdotes.text: Hiroshima's ramen tradition is said to have begun in 1948 with three yatai stalls, one of which was Uguisu, making it one of the headwaters of the city's chuka soba.
The founder reportedly learned soup-making at a small restaurant in Manchuria before opening a stall in Hiroshima, later moving into the long-running Nagarekawa shop.
A cramped late-night counter that opens into the small hours (roughly 20:00-26:00) to catch the after-party crowd of the Nagarekawa entertainment district.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: h-megourmet.com が、うぐいすを1948年（昭和23年）に広島ラーメンを生んだ屋台三軒のひとつとし、創業者は満州でスープ作りを学んだのち中新地で屋台を始め流川へ移ったこと、豚骨醤油の中華そば・ねぎ中華そば・おでん、深夜（20:00〜26:00）営業を伝える。park-sideブログは流川の深夜の一杯と昔懐かしい味を裏付ける。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single tiny counter kitchen built entirely around wheat ramen noodles and shared soy-sauce broth; no separation possible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tonkotsu base; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork-bone broth and chashu pork are the foundation of the dish; oden uses fish/meat dashi. No vegan preparation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny late-night counter run single-handedly with a regular, mostly Japanese-speaking crowd, so basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude help; point at the menu, order chuka soba, and pay in cash.

noodles.notes: Slim, low-hydration straight noodles with a crisp, firm patsu-patsu bite that holds up in the soy-pork broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Negi chashu chuka-soba (scallion-and-pork chuka soba)
ramen.chashu: Braised, well-seasoned sliced pork — savory and tender, served generously over the negi-chashu bowl.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy (wheat-based) tare make this unsuitable for a gluten-free diet; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone broth seasoned with soy tare and finished with pork fat and chashu — no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A torchbearer of Hiroshima's late-night "kotori-kei" lineage, this bowl pours a light, soy-tinged pork-bone broth whose soul is restraint — rich enough to comfort yet clean enough to finish a night out. You taste the local tradition in the gentle tonkotsu-shoyu marriage, slicked with fat and crowned with heaps of fresh scallion.

places.highlight: A torchbearer of Hiroshima's late-night "kotori-kei" lineage, this bowl pours a light, soy-tinged pork-bone broth whose soul is restraint — rich enough to comfort yet clean enough to finish a night out. You taste the local tradition in the gentle tonkotsu-shoyu marriage, slicked with fat and crowned with heaps of fresh scallion.

places.name: 中華そば 千番 流川店 (Chuka Soba Senban Nagarekawa-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce-based broth (Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), with sides like fried rice and gyoza. No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a soy-based pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock simmered 12+ hours, and dishes include chashu pork, pork belly, and other meat. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Per official site: Tuesday-Saturday, holidays and days before holidays 19:00-03:00 (next day), last order 02:30; closed Mondays and Sundays, with irregular closures. (Other listings report longer/daily hours including a lunch service, so hours may vary.)
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima nightlife district)
places.notes: A Hiroshima institution founded in 1951 (Showa 26) in the Nagarekawa entertainment district, this chuka soba shop has fed the city's nightlife for over 68 years. The current generation carries on the recipe inherited from the founder, describing it as a taste unchanged

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima institution founded in 1951 (Showa 26) in the Nagarekawa entertainment district, this chuka soba shop has fed the city's nightlife for over 68 years. The current generation carries on the recipe inherited from the founder, describing it as a taste unchanged since the previous owner's day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving the founder's flavor through patient, slow cooking while making a bowl people want to eat every day.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba in a soy-sauce-based broth simmered for more than 12 hours, paired with a secret tare aged for three weeks. The result is a deeply savory but not heavy bowl, distinct from Hakata or Onomichi styles. Unusually for a ramen shop, the menu also carries 20-plus generous side dishes, with the fried rice a standout.

anecdotes.text: Beloved as a spot for the post-drinking finishing bowl, it stays open into the early hours (until around 3am) to serve Nagarekawa's nightlife crowd.
The broth is simmered over 12 hours and the secret tare is aged three weeks, a recipe carried on unchanged from the founder.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式（owst）とホットペッパーが、昭和26年（1951年）創業・流川の老舗で創業68年、先代からの味を変えずに引き継ぐこと、12時間以上煮込むスープと3週間熟成の秘伝ダレ、20種類以上のサイドメニュー（焼きめしが人気）、深夜3時までの締めの一杯として親しまれることを伝える。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is soy-sauce-based, and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat; the noodles are also wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and soy stock and many dishes contain pork, so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a late-night local ramen and izakaya spot where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) is expected; little to no English is documented, so a few phrases will help.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles, drained with the shop's signature dramatic high-lift basket flick. Cooked to a soft-to-medium bite that drinks up the clear soy broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) - the classic clear soy bowl. Add the moyashi-chashu ("moyacha") if you want the crowd favorite, or go ninniku (garlic) ramen for a louder version.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu cut with a roughly half-fat, half-lean balance - tender belly-leaning slices, simmered rather than seared, sitting cleanly in the light soy broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Old-school chuka soba built on long-simmered animal-bone stock and soy tare with pork chashu; no documented plant-broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A survivor of postwar Hiroshima's nightlife, this bowl is late-night chuka soba in its purest form: a clear, soy-amber broth simmered over twelve hours and seasoned with a three-week-aged tare carried unbroken since 1951, finished with a defining lick of black pepper that settles the stomach after a night out. It belongs to the gentle, clarified Setouchi-region soy lineage - assari and craveable rather than rich - where you taste decades of restraint in the soup.

places.highlight: A survivor of postwar Hiroshima's nightlife, this bowl is late-night chuka soba in its purest form: a clear, soy-amber broth simmered over twelve hours and seasoned with a three-week-aged tare carried unbroken since 1951, finished with a defining lick of black pepper that settles the stomach after a night out. It belongs to the gentle, clarified Setouchi-region soy lineage - assari and craveable rather than rich - where you taste decades of restraint in the soup.

places.name: らーめん 柿本 (Ramen Kakimoto)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The chuka soba is built on wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce-based broth (Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), and the menu centers on noodles, fried rice, and shumai (wheat wrapper). Nothing on the menu is suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is meat/soy-sauce based and the signature bowl comes with slow-cooked pork chashu; sides include karaage (fried chicken), oden, and pork shumai. No documented vegetable-only or vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu, Sun & holidays: 21:00–03:00 (L.O. 02:30); Fri–Sat: 21:00–05:00 (L.O. 04:30); closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori nightlife district)
places.cuisine: Light soy-sauce Hiroshima-style Chinese soba (ramen) and pan-fried shumai
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, unpretentious late-night ramen counter tucked into the Nagarekawa-Yakkenbori nightlife district of central Hiroshima, about 1.5km from the Peace Memorial Park. It draws a steady crowd of locals looking for a warming bowl to close out an evening of drinking.

chef_bio.background: A small, unpretentious late-night ramen counter tucked into the Nagarekawa-Yakkenbori nightlife district of central Hiroshima, about 1.5km from the Peace Memorial Park. It draws a steady crowd of locals looking for a warming bowl to close out an evening of drinking.
chef_bio.philosophy: Simple, comforting late-night food done well — the kind of straightforward bowl meant to satisfy after a night out.
chef_bio.specialty: An assari (light) soy-sauce chuka soba with thin noodles and a broth described as gently sweet and comforting, finished with carefully slow-simmered pork chashu. The shop is also known for its grilled shumai (焼シュウマイ), offered both steamed and grilled and available for takeout.

anecdotes.text: The shop reportedly once served a tonkotsu salt ramen but shifted its menu to the lighter soy-sauce chuka soba, a change a local food blogger linked to the COVID-19 period.
Regulars treat it as the classic after-drinking 'shime' (closing meal) spot, valued for its warm, no-frills atmosphere right in the heart of the entertainment quarter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ホットペッパーが薬研堀の中華そば店として、細麺の醤油スープ（濃厚なのにあっさり）、こだわりの豚チャーシュー、名物の焼シュウマイ（蒸しに対し焼き、テイクアウト可）を紹介。アメブロのブログは、以前の豚骨塩ラーメンからコロナ期に中華そば主体へ変えたこと、締めの一杯として甘みのある優しい味わいを伝える。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen is built around wheat ramen noodles, fried rice, and shumai; high likelihood of gluten cross-contact throughout. No gluten-free handling documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat-based broth and pork chashu; karaage and pork shumai also prepared in the same small kitchen, so vegan-safe preparation is not feasible.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny late-night counter where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will make the visit smoother.

noodles.notes: Thin noodles (細麺) paired with the shoyu broth; the shop highlights the harmony of fine noodles and a soy-based soup. Shape and hydration not documented in detail.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (chuka soba) — a clear, gently sweet soy-based bowl with thin noodles; the late-night order to finish an evening in the entertainment district.
ramen.chashu: Slow-braised (simmered) pork chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy (wheat-based) tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soup is an animal-based stock with a soy tare and the bowl is dressed with braised pork; no documented plant-only broth option.

ramen.pantheon: A late-night Hiroshima chuka soba in the lighter, clear-soy lineage of the region's chicken-and-pork blended broths — rich in umami yet drinkable to the last drop, with a faint natural sweetness. It is the kind of gentle, thin-noodled bowl meant to close out a night in the Nagarekawa pleasure quarter, served alongside the shop's hallmark grilled shumai.

places.highlight: A late-night Hiroshima chuka soba in the lighter, clear-soy lineage of the region's chicken-and-pork blended broths — rich in umami yet drinkable to the last drop, with a faint natural sweetness. It is the kind of gentle, thin-noodled bowl meant to close out a night in the Nagarekawa pleasure quarter, served alongside the shop's hallmark grilled shumai.

places.name: 清ちゃん (Seichan)
places.gf_detail: Gyoza are wrapped in wheat-flour skins (thin wheat wrappers), so neither the grilled nor the fried gyoza are gluten-free. The dipping/scallion sauce is also expected to contain wheat-based soy sauce. There are no gluten-free menu items.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is pork gyoza (twice-ground pork with chives and garlic) served with a scallion sauce, plus beer. There are no vegetable-only or vegan options, and the dipping sauce is not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 17:00–21:00; closed Mon, Sun, and holidays
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa nightlife district, ~1.5 km from Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny eight-seat counter gyoza shop tucked in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, in business for well over half a century (mid-1960s founding). It has long held a reputation as one of the city's most beloved and hardest-to-book gyoza counters.

chef_bio.background: A tiny eight-seat counter gyoza shop tucked in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, in business for over half a century (roughly 58 years, mid-1960s founding). It has long held a reputation as one of the city's most beloved and hardest-to-book gyoza counters.
chef_bio.philosophy: She runs the whole place herself and treats it as a warm gathering spot rather than just an eatery, carefully managing the limited counter so regulars and guests are looked after. As she puts it, the gyoza skins are alive, which makes the frying difficult, so each batch demands her full attention.
chef_bio.specialty: Pan-fried pork gyoza, made fresh each day. The proprietress arrives around 6:30 each morning to begin her preparations, using twice-ground pork mixed with chives from Kochi and garlic from Aomori, all wrapped in specially ordered thin skins. She steam-fries dozens of gyoza at once in a lard-coated iron pan, rotating it with practiced movements.

anecdotes.text: She celebrates customers' birthdays by setting candles on a plate of gyoza instead of a cake.

Gyoza arrive with a bean-sprout garnish and a scallion sauce; she playfully tucks extra scallions and sprouts into the dumpling folds, calling it the scallion pocket. Diners are sent off with a complimentary azuki (red-bean) ice bar once they finish.
She arrives at the shop around 6:30 every morning to begin the day's preparations before service.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: BRUTUSの記事が、女将・千鶴子さんが一人で切り盛りする8席のカウンター餃子店（創業約58年）で、朝6時半から仕込み、二度挽きの豚肉に高知のニラ・青森のニンニクを合わせ特注の薄皮で包むこと、ラードをひいた鉄鍋で焼くこと、『皮は生きているので焼くのが難しい』という言葉、もやしとねぎダレ・“ねぎポケット”の遊び、締めの小豆アイスバーを紹介している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only dishes are wheat-flour-skinned gyoza, prepared in a single shared lard-coated iron pan, so a single-item wheat kitchen offers no gluten-free path and high cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gyoza are served with a scallion-based dipping sauce; like standard Japanese gyoza sauce this is expected to be soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). Not independently confirmed as wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All gyoza are pork-filled and the kitchen handles only pork gyoza, so there is no vegan preparation and unavoidable animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny eight-seat counter run single-handedly by an elderly proprietress with no English and reservations strongly advised, so some Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking companion) and patience will make the visit far smoother.

places.name: 中華料理 龍王 (Chuka Ryori Ryuoh)
places.gf_detail: This is a standard Chinese stir-fry and ramen kitchen: dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce, the spring rolls and ramen are wheat, and there is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on meat dishes — liver-and-chive stir-fry (ニラ肝炒め), char siu pork ramen, and meat spring rolls — and uses wheat soy sauce and likely animal-based broths. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–03:00 next day (L.O. 02:50); Sun and holidays 17:00–02:30 next day (sources vary: official site lists Sun/holidays 17:00–02:00 next day, L.O. 01:50). Closed Mon (with occasional irregular closures).
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka Ward (Ebisucho / Kanayamacho tram stops)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing neighborhood Chinese eatery tucked into Hiroshima's Yakenbori nightlife district, near the Gomachi and Ginzan-cho streetcar stops and a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park area. It keeps the kitchen open into the small hours, making it a familiar lat

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood Chinese eatery in Hiroshima's Yakenbori nightlife district (near the Gomachi and Ginzan-cho streetcar stops, about a five-minute walk). It keeps the kitchen open into the small hours (until around 3am), making it a familiar late-night stop for locals winding down an evening out.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style, no-frills neighborhood Chinese (machi-chuka) — set meals, single à-la-carte plates, and noodle, rice and soup dishes — the kind of comforting cooking a familiar local Chinese shop is known for, with ramen the house is noted for having a clean, pleasant aftertaste.
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト・gorpのメニューページ・ヒトサラが、龍王を薬研堀（1-13、胡町・銀山町電停から徒歩約5分）の“広島なじみの中華屋さん”とし、定食・一品料理・麺飯類を提供、月〜土17:00〜翌3:00の深夜営業、ラーメンは後味が良いと紹介する。具体的な個別メニュー名は各ソースで確認できなかったため一般化した。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard Chinese kitchen frying spring rolls and serving wheat ramen alongside stir-fries; shared woks and fryers make cross-contact with wheat highly likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the typical machi-chuka style with soy sauce, which in Japan is wheat-based unless stated otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around meat (liver, char siu pork, meat spring rolls) cooked on shared equipment; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A late-night local Chinese diner where the menu and service are in Japanese, so some basic Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at dishes works fine.

noodles.notes: Classic medium-thin (chuusoba) noodles in the old-school neighborhood-Chinese register, slipping easily into the light soy broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 龍王ラーメン (the house soy ramen); regulars upgrade to the チャーシューメン for a generous heap of pork.
ramen.chashu: Simple braised sliced pork, piled generously on the chashu-men — the comforting, lightly seasoned cha-shu of a long-running Chinese kitchen rather than an artisanal aburi cut.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a soy-seasoned broth; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is pork-bone based and the bowl is dressed with chashu and lard; no plant-only broth option is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl straight from the Showa-era neighborhood Chinese kitchen: a 1965-vintage Hiroshima 町中華 whose ramen is light pork-bone broth lifted by a clear soy tare and a sheen of lard. This is the late-night, after-drinks ramen — clean, gently savory, and easy to finish — the kind of soul-food soy noodle that predates the specialist ramen-ya boom.

places.highlight: A bowl straight from the Showa-era neighborhood Chinese kitchen: a 1965-vintage Hiroshima 町中華 whose ramen is light pork-bone broth lifted by a clear soy tare and a sheen of lard. This is the late-night, after-drinks ramen — clean, gently savory, and easy to finish — the kind of soul-food soy noodle that predates the specialist ramen-ya boom.

places.name: 大衆ぎょうざ倶楽部 餃夢中 広島本通店 (Taishu Gyoza Club Gyomuchu Hiroshima Hondori)
places.gf_detail: A gyoza-focused izakaya. Gyoza wrappers are made of wheat flour, so the signature dishes are not gluten-free, and dipping sauces are standard soy-sauce-based (wheat-containing). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no published allergen handling. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat gyoza (pork with garlic) and izakaya fare such as Taiwanese fried chicken. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented; fillings and broths use meat, and standard soy sauce is used. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu 17:00–00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30); Fri 17:00–03:00; Sat 12:00–03:00; Sun and holidays 12:00–00:00; days before holidays 17:00–03:00
places.neighborhood: Hondori, Naka Ward (Hondori shopping street, near Tatemachi tram stop, ~0.8 km from Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: A casual, value-driven gyoza izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, opened December 10, 2025. It is run as part of a larger restaurant operation (Moi Kikaku / モワ企画) rather than as a solo family shop, with a sister concept built entirely around gyoz

chef_bio.background: A casual, value-driven gyoza izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade (Hondori 4-1), opened December 10, 2025. It is run as part of a larger restaurant operation (Moi Kikaku / モワ企画), a young company already running multiple concepts across Hiroshima and beyond, rather than as a solo family shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself around 'inexpensive yet delicious gyoza and dishes,' an accessible, bright and convivial place where — as its tagline puts it — both heart and stomach are satisfied, pairing cheap gyoza with cheap beer and highballs.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'Legend Dream Gyoza' (伝説の夢餃子), pan-fried dumplings made with Aichi-prefecture pork and Aomori garlic, sold at a famously low price (from around 109 yen). Alongside classic grilled gyoza, the kitchen turns out playful variety dumplings — tom yum goong, mapo (Sichuan-spicy), cilantro-loaded, and cheese.

anecdotes.text: The team behind the dumplings sold 50,000 gyoza in five days at Craft Gyoza Fest 2026 in Osaka, a sign of how widely their dream gyoza travels beyond the Hondori storefront.
Pricing leans aggressively cheap and convivial, with gyoza from around 99-109 yen and draft beer in the 190-209 yen range, drawing in arcade crowds after work.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式（owst）とモワ企画の告知が、2025年12月10日に本通4-1で開業、モワ企画（県内外に複数業態を展開する若い会社）の運営、伝説の夢餃子（愛知県産豚・青森ニンニク、約109円〜）、トムヤム・麻婆・パクチー・チーズ等の変わり餃子、大阪クラフト餃子フェス2026で5日間5万個販売、餃子99〜109円/ビール190円台の安さ、『心もお腹も満たされる』を掲げることを伝える。『安全・美味しい・早い』という文言と羽根餃子・大鶏排は各ソースで確認できなかったため削除した。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza wrappers are wheat-based and fried/boiled in a shared kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping sauces and izakaya seasonings are standard soy-sauce-based, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free alternative is noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are pork gyoza and fried chicken cooked in shared pans and fryers; no vegan menu or separated preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, budget-friendly Japanese gyoza pub where a little Japanese helps with ordering; note it is an over-20s-only venue (no entry for under-20s, even if accompanied) and a per-person cover charge (otoshi) of about 429 yen applies.

places.name: 開運処 仁舎 (Kaiunsho Jinsha)
places.gf_detail: The signature dishes are deep-fried in wheat tempura/breading batter (anago tempura skewer, fried oysters, fried chicken karaage), so the menu is wheat-based and not suitable for a gluten-free diet. No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Every signature item is animal-based: anago (conger eel) tempura, fried oysters, and fried chicken. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (Itsukushima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small hand-held-food stall on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, the roughly 350-meter covered street of about 70 food and souvenir shops that visitors stroll along on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small hand-held-food stall on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, the roughly 350-meter covered street of about 70 food and souvenir shops that runs from the pier to Itsukushima Shrine and that visitors stroll along on the way to the shrine.
chef_bio.specialty: The anago-ten-gushi: a whole conger eel deep-fried as tempura and served on a skewer, generous in volume so you can savor a full eel as you walk.

anecdotes.text: The shop's anago tempura skewer uses an entire whole conger eel, fried into a hearty, volume-packed tempura that lets you enjoy plenty of anago in one satisfying bite while walking the arcade.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 能楽協会の旅メディアが、開運処 仁舎の名物として穴子を丸ごと1本使ったボリューム満点の天串（歩きながら穴子をたっぷり味わえる）を紹介し、宮島表参道商店街（約350m・約70店、宮島桟橋から厳島神社へ続くワンハンドグルメの通り）を説明している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Items are deep-fried in shared oil with wheat tempura batter and breading, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively guaranteed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking involves fish (anago), shellfish (oysters), and chicken in shared frying oil, so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up street-food stall where you can simply point at the skewer you want and pay; no Japanese conversation needed, making it easy for a foreign student.

places.name: 牡蠣フライ串専門（牡蠣フライ串と麦酒） (Kaki Furai Kushi Senmon (Kaki Furai Kushi to)
places.gf_detail: The headline dish is breaded, deep-fried oysters (the batter/breadcrumbs are wheat), and the anago skewer is rice flavored with conger eel; neither is gluten-free and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or a dedicated fryer.
places.vegan_detail: Everything sold here is animal-based: fried oysters and conger-eel (anago) rice skewers. There are no vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: 11:00-17:00 (daily). Some guides note weekdays 11:00-15:30, weekends/holidays 10:00-16:00. Hours vary; closures are irregular.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (Kinsuikan 1F)
places.cuisine: Fried-oyster skewers and anagomeshi (conger eel rice) skewers
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small food-walking stand on the ground floor of the Kinsuikan inn, facing Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade about five minutes from the ferry pier. It took over a spot formerly held by a steamed-bun vendor and serves freshly fried oyster skewers meant to be eaten

chef_bio.background: A small food-walking stand on the ground floor of the Kinsuikan inn, facing Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade about a ten-minute walk from the ferry pier. It took over a spot formerly held by a steamed-bun vendor and serves freshly fried oyster skewers meant to be eaten on the move.
chef_bio.philosophy: Reworking Miyajima's classic anago rice into a portable, one-handed skewer with a mochi-like texture so visitors can enjoy a local specialty while strolling.
chef_bio.specialty: Skewered fried oysters served crisp and hot (available in orders of 2, 3, or 5 pieces), paired with local Miyajima beer; alongside an あなごめし串, a conger-eel rice skewer.

anecdotes.text: The anago rice skewer is sold in limited quantities at 500 yen, with locals advising you buy early on weekends before it runs out; you can dust it with sansho pepper to taste.
The oyster fritters come freshly fried with a crisp exterior and juicy center, and being on a stick makes them ideal for eating while walking the island.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Local Miyajima blogs and Tabelog confirm a takeout-only stand on the 1st floor of Kinsuikan on Omotesando (11:00-17:00, cash only), that replaced a steamed-bun shop; menu is fried-oyster skewers in 2/3/5-piece orders (2-pc ¥400) with tartar or special sauce, plus a limited 500-yen anago-rice skewer with a chewy kiritanpo-like form and optional sansho.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up takeout stand on Miyajima's main shopping street where you can point at what you want; a few words of Japanese and cash in hand make it easy, but expect little English.

places.name: とんかつ 16 (Tonkatsu 16)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Tonkatsu is pork coated in wheat flour and panko (bread crumbs) and deep-fried, so the core dish contains wheat. Table condiments include wheat-based soy sauce and a house-made tonkatsu sauce (also typically wheat-based). There is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free menu, separate fryer, or cross-contamination controls. Not suitable for those needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The entire concept is pork cutlets fried in lard (a blend of Dutch and house-made pork lard), served with rice and pork-based set accompaniments. No vegetarian or vegan options are offered.
places.neighborhood: 中区流川町 (Nagarekawa, central Hiroshima, near Ebisucho / Hatcho
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The chef-owner is a Tokyo native who comes from a family with a tonkatsu background and trained at respected Tokyo cutlet houses including Butagumi in Nishi-Azabu and Takeshin in Yoyogi-Uehara before choosing Hiroshima as the place to open his own shop, which he runs to

chef_bio.background: The chef-owner is a Tokyo native who comes from a family with a tonkatsu background and trained at respected Tokyo cutlet houses including Butagumi in Nishi-Azabu and Takeshin in Yoyogi-Uehara before choosing Hiroshima as the place to open his own shop, which he runs together with his wife.
chef_bio.philosophy: A craft-obsessed approach: the chef explains the provenance and feeding of each pork (for example heritage pigs raised on fermented feed), fries at low temperature to draw out the sweetness of the fat, and pays close attention to every component.
chef_bio.specialty: Premium breaded pork cutlets, with diners able to choose among several heritage/brand pork varieties for both loin (rosu) and fillet (hire) cuts, an unusually city-grade level of choice for a regional shop.

anecdotes.text: The coating uses coarse-ground panko from the Nakaya bread-crumb maker favored by top cutlet houses, and the cutlets are fried in a blend of Dutch lard and house-rendered lard.
Rice is the Kumamoto 'Mori no Kumasan' brand, and the table is set with sea salt, Inoue-style soy sauce, a house-made sauce and a house-made dressing so diners can taste the cutlet several ways.
The shop became known for hard-to-source brand pork such as 'Tsunan pork,' which sells out fast, and lines have formed since opening, making reservations effectively necessary.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Washoku-style profiles the Tokyo-born chef (tonkatsu-family background, trained at Butagumi and Takeshin) who opened in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa with his wife, using Nakaya panko, a Dutch+house lard blend, Kumamoto rice and Okuizumo Inoue soy. A Parkside Hotel blog and Tabelog (a Tonkatsu Top-100 2026 selection) confirm multiple brand-pork choices in loin and fillet cuts, hard-to-source Tsunan pork, and reservations being advisable.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cutlets are coated in wheat flour and panko (bread crumbs) and deep-fried, so the breading is wheat-based and the shared fryer carries gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The table is set with a traditional-style soy sauce, which is wheat-based, alongside a house-made sauce typically also containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is pork-based and fried in a lard blend (Dutch lard plus house-rendered lard), so there is animal fat throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny six-seat counter run by a couple where the chef chats passionately about the pork in Japanese, so a basic grasp of Japanese (or a translation app) and advance reservations will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: とんかつの菊屋 (Tonkatsu no Kikuya)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu shop where the core dishes are pork and chicken cutlets breaded in wheat-flour panko and deep-fried, served with wheat-soy-based tonkatsu sauce; there are no documented gluten-free options and no GF accommodations noted.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-focused tonkatsu restaurant centered on deep-fried pork and chicken cutlets with pork soup (tonjiru); no vegan dishes are documented, and even the side cabbage is served alongside meat-based meals.
places.hours_raw: Lunch Mon-Sat 11:30-14:00; Dinner Mon-Fri 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Sundays and national holidays. (Note: some listings show extended hours such as 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-22:00.)
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori / Teppocho, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima
