places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run tonkatsu specialist tucked into the Teppocho/Hatchobori district of central Hiroshima, within walking distance of the city's downtown and the streetcar lines. The shop is fitted out with a large solid-wood counter and chunky wooden tables, giving it a

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run tonkatsu specialist tucked into the Teppocho/Hatchobori district of central Hiroshima, within walking distance of the city's downtown and the streetcar lines. The shop is fitted out with a large solid-wood counter and chunky wooden tables, giving it a warm, natural, unpretentious feel that draws a steady weekday crowd of local office workers.
chef_bio.specialty: Crispy-coated tonkatsu with tender, juicy pork, served as set meals with rice, pork soup (tonjiru), and pickles; the oversized 'jumbo' chicken cutlet set is a signature draw, with free refills of rice, pork soup, and cabbage.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the jumbo chicken cutlet as 'cushion-sized,' praising both its surprising size and its value for money, which keeps the lunch counter busy with salarymen.
The dining room is built around a large solid-wood counter and tables, creating a calm, natural space where the food is the focus.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Gnavi, Ekiten and Tabelog place the shop at Teppocho 7-7 in Naka-ku (near Otemachi/Otomachi stop), describe a large solid-wood counter and tables, and confirm the signature 'jumbo' chicken cutlet reviewers call 座布団 (cushion)-sized, with a value-focused salaryman lunch trade and free refills of rice, soup and cabbage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are breaded in wheat-flour panko and deep-fried in shared frying oil, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkatsu sauce and standard soy-based seasonings used here are wheat-containing; no wheat-free sauce alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on deep-fried pork and chicken and pork-based soup, so all preparation involves animal products with no separate vegan handling noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local cutlet shop popular with office workers at lunch; little to no English, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will help you order smoothly.

places.name: つかんと (Tsukanto)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet) specialist; the coating is wheat-flour batter and panko breadcrumbs, and tonkatsu sauce typically contains wheat. Not suitable for gluten-free diners; no gluten-free preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on deep-fried pork cutlets; no vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00; closed Tuesday and Saturday (per Tabelog). Operates lunch only.
places.neighborhood: Fukuromachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, near Hondori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A specialist lunch-only counter in central Hiroshima devoted to a single craft: tonkatsu. The shop is run solo by its proprietor and recently moved into a compact storefront in Fukuromachi, a short walk from the Hondori shopping street, after operating in the nearby Nek

chef_bio.background: A specialist lunch-only counter in central Hiroshima devoted to a single craft: tonkatsu. The owner-run shop recently moved into a compact storefront in Fukuromachi, a short walk from the Hondori shopping street, after operating in the nearby Nekoyacho area.
chef_bio.philosophy: The aim is to capture the best of both worlds in one cutlet: the chef frames the goal as enjoying the crunch of the coating and the umami of the meat together.
chef_bio.specialty: Low-temperature-cooked tonkatsu: the pork is first gently cooked at low temperature, then finished by frying in hot oil, so the interior stays rosy and tender while the panko crust turns crisp.

anecdotes.text: The shop frames its signature as an 'いいとこ取りのカツ' (a cutlet that takes the best of both sides), pairing a rare, tender interior with a crisp crust through its two-stage low-temperature-then-fry method.
The counter serves lunch only and recently relocated to the Fukuromachi Kyoei Building after operating in Nekoyacho, sharing its space with Lemon Stand Hiroshima later in the day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own pages (via Silencer Inc.) describe tonkatsu made by low-temperature-cooking the pork then deep-frying at high heat for a rare, tender interior and crisp crust, marketed as 'いいとこ取りのカツ' where you enjoy both the coating's crackle (衣の音) and the meat's umami; the lunch-only counter (11:30-14:00) relocated on July 1 to the Fukuromachi Kyoei Building from Nekoyacho. The owner is not named.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded and deep-fried in wheat flour and panko; gluten is unavoidable here and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkatsu is typically served with tonkatsu/Worcestershire-style sauce, which generally contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce option is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around frying pork cutlets, so shared oil and surfaces make it unsuitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, solo-run lunch counter where basic Japanese helps for ordering; no English menu is documented, but pointing at the day's tonkatsu set works fine and payment is flexible (cash, cards, IC, QR).

places.name: とんかつ かつまる (Tonkatsu Katsumaru)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu (deep-fried breaded cutlet) specialist; nearly every dish is coated in wheat panko and fried, the curry is thickened with wheat flour, and tonkatsu sauce/soy seasonings contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork, fish, and oyster cutlets and meat-based curry (beef-tendon), with the frying oil custom-blended from pork back fat. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.neighborhood: 中区本通 (Hondori), central Hiroshima, near Tatemachi Station
places.notes: A street-level tonkatsu specialist that opened on Hiroshima's central Hondori shopping arcade in summer 2024, in a spot that previously housed a mackerel restaurant. It is run as a casual, accessible cutlet house aimed at both quick lunches and relaxed evening meals wit

chef_bio.background: A street-level tonkatsu specialist that opened on Hiroshima's central Hondori shopping arcade in summer 2024, in a spot that previously housed a mackerel restaurant. It is run as a casual, accessible cutlet house aimed at both quick lunches and relaxed evening meals with drinks.
chef_bio.philosophy: Careful pork selection and a bespoke frying oil to hit a crisp golden crust over tender meat, served in a wide range of approachable set meals.
chef_bio.specialty: Crispy-yet-juicy pork cutlets fried in a custom oil blended with pork back fat for deep savory flavor, alongside a signature curry simmered over three days until the vegetables dissolve into a mellow, sweet-savory sauce with beef tendon. The kitchen also works with Shimane-raised Iwami brand pork and Seto Inland Sea oyster cutlets.

anecdotes.text: The curry served with the cutlets is cooked over three days so the vegetables melt down into a mellow, sweet and savory sauce.
Opened July 30, 2024 on Hondori near Round One, in a space that had previously been a mackerel restaurant, offering 25-plus set meals plus front-counter takeout.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's official page and a local review confirm a July 30 2024 opening on Hondori (Naka-ku) near Round One in a former mackerel restaurant, 26 seats (6 counter/20 table), 25-plus set meals with storefront takeout, a custom frying oil blended with pork back fat, Shimane Iwami-brand pork and Hiroshima oysters, and a curry simmered three days with beef tendon.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are coated in wheat panko breading and deep-fried, and the house curry is flour-thickened, so wheat is pervasive across the menu and shared fryers are used.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard tonkatsu sauce and Japanese soy-based seasonings used with cutlets contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Frying oil is custom-blended with pork back fat, so all fried items carry animal fat regardless of topping.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in-friendly cutlet spot on the busy Hondori arcade with picture-and-set-meal menus and card/QR payment, so a foreign student can manage comfortably with simple Japanese greetings and pointing.

places.name: 洋食 こけもも (Yoshoku Kokemomo)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic yoshoku (Japanese Western) restaurant with no gluten-free offering. Core dishes are inherently wheat-based or breaded: hamburger steak (typically bound with breadcrumbs), beef cutlet (panko-breaded and fried), demi-glace and brown sauces (roux/flour-thickened), meat spaghetti (wheat pasta), and stews thickened with flour-based roux. No dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredients, or cross-contamination controls are documented. Not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-focused Western diner whose signature dishes are beef hamburger steak, beef cutlet, tongue/oxtail stew and beef stew, with butter, cream, dairy and egg used throughout (e.g., custard pudding dessert). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:30-15:00 (food L.O. 14:00, drink L.O. 14:30), 18:00-21:30 (food L.O. 20:30, drink L.O. 21:00); closed Tue and Wed
places.neighborhood: 中区中町 (Nakamachi, central Hiroshima — near Fukuromachi tram s
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kokemomo is the second-generation sister restaurant of 広亭タナカ (Hirotei Tanaka), a Hiroshima yoshoku institution founded in 1991 in the Dobashi/Tsuchihashi area. Kokemomo opened in 2012 in Nakamachi, central Hiroshima, bringing the family's classic Western cooking to a mo

roles: sister/second shop of 広亭タナカ
chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima — an offshoot of the long-running 広亭タナカ.
chef_bio.background: Kokemomo is the second-generation sister restaurant of 広亭タナカ (Hirotei Tanaka), a Hiroshima yoshoku institution founded in 1991 in the Dobashi/Tsuchihashi area. Kokemomo opened in 2012 in Nakamachi, central Hiroshima, bringing the family's classic Western cooking to a more downtown location a short walk from the Peace Park.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen carries forward the founding chef's motto, 時間を惜しまず手を抜かず — spare no time and cut no corners — and aims for 変わらない美味しさと安心感, unchanging flavor and the reassurance of a dish you can always come back to.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-fashioned, orthodox yoshoku: hamburger steak, breaded beef cutlet (ビフカツ), tongue/oxtail stew (タンシチュー) and beef stew, finished with refined demi-glace-style sauces; a firm, classic custard pudding for dessert.

anecdotes.text: The white-toned, softly lit room keeps a homey, grown-up feel, with vintage records and memorabilia carried over from the parent restaurant 広亭タナカ, signalling the lineage the kitchen is proud of.
It is a genuine local favorite: one diner arrived for a Sunday lunch to find the place full, noting that booking ahead had been the right call.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Setouchi-sanpo and modokimaru describe Kokemomo (Nakamachi 4-19, ~20 seats, 6 counter/14 tables, open since 2012) as the sister shop of Hirotei Tanaka, decorated with vintage records and memorabilia from the parent restaurant, serving hamburger steak, bifukatsu, tan stew and a classic pudding, and popular enough that a Sunday visit found it fully booked.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available and the atmosphere is calm and family-friendly, so a foreign student can order comfortably; reservations are recommended for dinner and busy weekend lunches.

places.name: 洋食 広亭タナカ (Yoshoku Hirotei Tanaka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings or accommodations are documented. The cuisine is built around long-simmered demi-glace sauce (vegetables, chicken bouillon, and standard roux/seasonings) and breaded/grilled meat dishes, all of which typically contain wheat. No allergy or gluten-free information is published on the official site or other sources.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric yoshoku house — signature dishes are beef tongue stew, beef stew, beef cutlet, hamburger steak, and filet steak, all using a meat- and demi-glace-based sauce simmered roughly 60 hours. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 18:00-21:00 (food L.O. 20:00, drink L.O. 20:30). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday.
places.neighborhood: 土橋（中区）Tsuchihashi, Naka-ku — west-central Hiroshima, near Pe
places.notes: A traditional Western-style (yoshoku) restaurant founded in 1991 and now run by its second-generation owner. It sits on a quiet stretch of Tsuchihashi in west-central Hiroshima, a few minutes' walk from the tram line, and has been a neighborhood favorite for more than t

chef_bio.background: A traditional Western-style (yoshoku) restaurant founded in 1991 and now run by its second-generation owner. It sits on a quiet stretch of Tsuchihashi in west-central Hiroshima, a few minutes' walk from the tram line, and has been a neighborhood favorite for more than three decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founding chef's motto — 'spare no time, cut no corners' (時間を惜しまず手を抜かず) — has been handed down to the second generation, who pairs it with the belief that 'to stay the same, you must keep changing,' balancing the original recipes with quiet refinement.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef tongue stew (tan stew) and beef stew napped in a rich, gently sweet demi-glace, alongside hamburger steak, beef cutlet, filet steak, and hayashi rice.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen builds its signature demi-glace entirely from scratch — sautéing vegetables, simmering them in chicken bouillon, and straining the result multiple times — a process that takes roughly 60 hours before the sauce ever reaches a plate.
The demi-glace and soups are all made by hand in-house, a craft the restaurant has kept up since it opened in 1991.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The restaurant's own site and Pecomag confirm a 1991 founding at Tsuchihashi (Naka-ku), a second-generation owner, both handed-down mottos (時間を惜しまず手を抜かず and 'to stay the same you must keep changing'), and a signature tan stew whose demi-glace is built from scratch by sautéing vegetables, simmering in chicken bouillon and straining repeatedly over roughly 60 hours; the 33-seat room is known for classical music and abundant flowers.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a beloved local yoshoku institution where ordering and signage are in Japanese, so a little basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother, though pointing at the menu works fine.

places.name: マツノ屋 (Matsunoya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The noodles are described as a thick wheat-style soba (not pure buckwheat), the broths and tare are soy-sauce based (wheat), and the katsudon is breaded fried pork. No gluten-free preparation is offered.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. The menu centers on pork katsudon (breaded fried pork), oyako-don (chicken and egg), and soba served in soy-sauce-based broths or with a meat-topped chili-oil sauce, all of which use animal ingredients and likely fish dashi.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-19:00 (open daily / no regular closing day per listings)
places.neighborhood: Horikawacho, Naka Ward (Hatchobori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent shop that opened in central Hiroshima in April 2014, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop and about 1.5 km east of Peace Memorial Park. It serves an affordable, everyday lineup of soba, katsudon, and oyako-don in a calm, dark-wood interior.

chef_bio.background: A small independent shop that opened in central Hiroshima in April 2014, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop and about 1.5 km east of Peace Memorial Park. It serves an affordable, everyday lineup of soba, katsudon, and oyako-don in a calm, dark-wood interior.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is a punchy, modern take on soba: thick noodles dressed in a spicy chili-oil (rayu) sauce, topped with sweet-savory pork, green onion, and white sesame, served warm or cold depending on the season.

anecdotes.text: The house specialty is described as a new-style thick-noodle soba with a punch of spicy chili oil, topped with sweetly seasoned pork, scallion, and sesame for a hearty, layered bowl.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Retty and Tabelog place Matsunoya in Naka-ku's Horikawacho (Seikou Building 1F), about 2-3 minutes from Hatchobori, opened April 16 2014, operating via a meal-ticket machine with counter seating; the signature ラー油蕎麦 is thick-cut soba in a chili-oil-based sauce (~¥850-980) topped with sweet pork, green onion, sesame (and nori), offered warm or cold, alongside katsudon and oyakodon.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are a thick wheat-style soba and dishes use soy-sauce-based broths and tare, so wheat is present throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba broths and the rayu/katsudon sauces are soy-sauce based, which in Japan typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around pork, chicken, egg, and likely fish dashi; no plant-only dishes are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, casual local soba-and-rice-bowl counter where ordering is simple and quick, but staff likely speak little English and there is no English menu, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing will help.

places.name: 遊鹿里茶屋 (Yukari Chaya)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on matcha, zenzai (sweet red-bean soup) and fruit-syrup kakigori (shaved ice), some of which may happen to be free of wheat, but accompanying wagashi and any sauces are not verified, so a celiac diner should ask about each item's ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. Plain matcha, shaved ice with homemade fruit syrups, and red-bean zenzai are plant-based in principle, but it is unconfirmed whether sweets, syrups or accompaniments contain dairy, egg or honey, so a vegan diner should confirm ingredients before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun). Closed Wed and Thu, plus other irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Takikoji (Itsukushima Shrine–Daishoin area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 遊鹿里茶屋 sits along Takikoji, a hushed historic lane that branches off Miyajima's busy main approach and runs toward Daishoin Temple, away from the crowds near Itsukushima Shrine. It occupies a roughly 170-year-old wooden townhouse (machiya) that has been converted into a  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: 遊鹿里茶屋 sits along Takikoji, a hushed historic lane that branches off Miyajima's busy main approach and runs toward Daishoin Temple, away from the crowds near Itsukushima Shrine. It occupies a roughly 170-year-old wooden townhouse (machiya) that has been converted into a tea house, retaining the feel of an old island mansion.
chef_bio.philosophy: A quiet retreat from the tourist bustle, with careful, gentle hospitality and a garden meant to be lingered over rather than rushed.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional Japanese sweets and tea: matcha with wagashi, zenzai (warm or chilled sweet red-bean soup), and kakigori shaved ice made with homemade fruit syrups such as plum.

anecdotes.text: Guests describe it as a beautifully kept old mansion turned cafe, with a tasteful garden visible from table seats, tatami rooms and a veranda, calling it a welcome break from the crowded sightseeing areas.
Visitors note thoughtful touches such as hand-written menus and small comforts like a blanket, reinforcing the calm, attentive atmosphere.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 4travel reviewers describe 遊鹿里茶屋 on Takikoji lane toward Daishoin as a ~170-year-old machiya converted to a tea house, with table, tatami and veranda seating around a well-kept garden, hand-written menus and thoughtful touches (blankets), serving matcha with wagashi, zenzai and kakigori made with homemade plum syrup; a welcome quiet break from the crowds (~¥1,000-1,999).

positives.text: Menu is built around matcha, fruit-syrup shaved ice, and red-bean zenzai rather than fried or heavy wheat-based dishes, which can give plant-based or wheat-avoiding diners a starting point to ask about.

cultural_comfort.note: A tucked-away traditional sweets cafe with hand-written Japanese menus and little English, so a foreign student will be most comfortable arriving with a translation app or a guide and pointing to what they'd like.

places.name: 茶和々 宮島店 (Sawawa Miyajima)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented and no allergy accommodation is advertised. The warabi mochi is dusted with kinako (soybean flour) and matcha, and while warabi/lotus-root starch itself is grain-free, there is no confirmation of a gluten-free or cross-contamination-controlled product. This is not a suitable destination for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A matcha sweets shop, not a vegan destination. The matcha soft serve ice cream contains dairy and is not vegan. The matcha warabi mochi (made from lotus-root starch and warabi powder, dusted with matcha and kinako soybean flour) may be plant-based, but there is no documented confirmation of its full ingredients or that it is free of animal products, so vegan diners should ask directly. Shaved ice toppings are not documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (per local coverage); closed days not documented
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street, within walking distance
places.notes: [Miyajima] Sawawa is a matcha-sweets brand run by Terakoya Inc., a tourism-focused company with shops in destinations across Japan from Hokkaido and Tokyo to Kanazawa and Arashiyama. The Miyajima branch sits along the Omotesando shopping street within walking distance of Itsukushi (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

roles: Operated by Terakoya Inc. (株式会社寺子屋)
chef_bio.background: Sawawa is a matcha-sweets brand run by Terakoya Inc. (株式会社寺子屋), a Kyoto-based tourism company founded in 1978 with shops in destinations across Japan from Hokkaido and Tokyo to Kanazawa and Arashiyama. The Miyajima branch sits along the Omotesando shopping street within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand emphasizes quality matcha and blending Japanese cultural heritage with an approachable, modern treat for travelers.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut matcha warabi mochi made fresh on-site, generously dusted with matcha, alongside matcha and koicha (rich matcha) soft serve and, in summer, fluffy shaved ice topped with soft warabi mochi.

anecdotes.text: The storefront is decorated with red traditional Japanese umbrellas that suit Miyajima's atmosphere, with a small tea-house-style counter-and-bench area for a brief rest.
The signature hand-cut matcha warabi mochi is made fresh in the shop and dusted with premium matcha, and is described as a long-loved flagship of the brand.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Terakoya's own site (株式会社寺子屋, HQ in Kyoto's Ukyo-ku, founded 1978, 145+ mostly tourist-area shops) confirms 茶和々 as its matcha-sweets brand whose top seller is hand-cut, freshly made matcha warabi mochi dusted with premium matcha; the tabetainjya review confirms the Miyajima Omotesando branch (553-5 Miyajima-cho) with red umbrellas, a small counter-bench area, matcha and koicha soft serve, and summer fluffy shaved ice.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed takeout matcha-sweets stand on the busy Omotesando shopping street where you can point at what you want, so it is easy for a foreign student even with no Japanese; bring cash, as cards are not accepted.

places.name: もみじ亭 (Momiji-tei)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe and fried noodles, and teppanyaki dishes use wheat-based sauces and soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The standard okonomiyaki contains pork and egg, and the batter broth is made from kombu and bonito (fish) dashi. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu & Sun 12:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Fri-Sat 12:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). After 5pm and all day weekends/holidays, one paid drink per person is required (infants excluded).
places.neighborhood: Kamiyachō / Hondōri, Naka-ku — central Hiroshima, about 1 km
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, third-floor okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop in central Hiroshima run by its owner, Fujiwara, an avowed anime and game fan who has woven those passions through the whole space. The stairwell and walls are lined with anime posters and figurines, and a maid-unifor

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop in central Hiroshima run by Fujiwara, an avowed anime and game fan who has woven those passions through the whole space. The stairwell and walls are lined with anime posters and figurines, and a maid-uniformed staff member greets guests, so the room feels as much like a hobby shop as a griddle counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fujiwara puts personal, quality service and conversation ahead of volume. When tourist crowds once swelled past 100 people a day against a comfortable capacity of around 40, he chose to cap numbers rather than chase profit, so that he could keep cooking carefully and chatting with guests about anime and games.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and a long list of teppanyaki dishes. Beyond the classic pork-and-noodle okonomiyaki, the shop is known for playfully named originals such as the 'Yandere-yaki,' which piles on grated mountain yam, natto, and okra for a creamy, sticky texture. The batter is built on house-made kombu-and-bonito dashi, and the cabbage is cut into large pieces for extra crunch.

anecdotes.text: Featured on a local Hiroshima TV okonomiyaki program, where the owner's anime-and-game theming and the signature 'Yandere-yaki' (mountain yam, natto, okra) were highlighted alongside his house-made kombu-bonito dashi batter.

In March 2023 the owner described being overwhelmed by more than 100 visitors a day, far beyond the shop's usual 40-person comfort, and chose to limit capacity to protect service quality rather than maximize sales.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: An HTV okonomiyaki program page names もみじ亭 and owner 藤原さん, its anime-poster/figurine decor, the 'Yandere-yaki' topped with mountain yam, natto and okra, house-made kombu-bonito dashi batter and large-cut cabbage. The shop's own site confirms an owner-plus-one operation with maid-costumed staff that capped guest numbers after early-2023 tourist surges past 100/day (against ~40 comfort), no reservations except groups, and no takeout on food-safety grounds.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour crepe and wheat noodles, and teppanyaki is finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce and soy sauce, so gluten is present throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The batter broth is made from kombu and bonito (fish) dashi and the standard okonomiyaki contains pork and egg, so dishes are not suitable for vegans.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, anime-themed owner-run shop with a playful, chatty atmosphere; a few words of Japanese and an openness to friendly banter will go a long way, though a foreign student can manage with basics and pointing at the menu.

places.name: 奥田焙煎珈琲 (Okuda Baisen Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Food offerings center on sandwiches and toast (wheat bread), so the morning/food menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are documented; the draw here is the coffee rather than the food.
places.vegan_detail: This is a coffeehouse focused on self-roasted siphon coffee with morning sets of toast and sandwiches; no documented vegan offerings, and standard morning items use bread, butter and egg. Coffee itself is plant-based, but no vegan menu is advertised.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 07:30-19:30; closed Sun. Morning service runs until around 11:30.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka Ward (~3 min walk from Hatchobori Stn)
places.cuisine: Classic coffee house with house-roasted coffee
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing pure-coffeehouse (junkissa) tucked into a quiet lane in Hatchobori, central Hiroshima, founded in September 1984 (Showa 59). It has been welcoming guests under warm lamplight for around three decades, run by a single proprietor.

chef_bio.background: A long-standing pure-coffeehouse (junkissa) tucked into a quiet lane in Hatchobori, central Hiroshima, founded in September 1984 (Showa 59). It has been welcoming guests under warm lamplight for around four decades, run by a single proprietor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner emphasizes how extraction method changes a coffee's flavor and enjoys showing customers the brewing process, treating the siphon extraction as part of the experience.
chef_bio.specialty: All coffee is roasted in-house and brewed by siphon; counter guests can watch the extraction up close at the brass siphon equipment. The lineup is broad, with about 8 single-origin coffees and 5 blends, including a deep-roasted Italian Blend. A well-priced morning service offers a choice of around 6 items such as sandwiches and toast.

anecdotes.text: Behind a casual orange-roofed storefront, the interior opens up surprisingly: mirrored walls amplify the space and a hidden back seating area creates a calm, set-apart, almost non-everyday retreat from the busy street outside.
Counter seats face the golden siphon apparatus where the coffee brews with a gentle bubbling sound, so guests can watch their cup being made.
A retro junkissa that opened in September 1984 and has offered a healing space under warm lamplight ever since, with roasting equipment and interior decor on display.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and the coffeeyashibo review confirm a Hatchobori junkissa opened in 1984, run as a one-person operation (47 seats: 7 counter/40 table), serving house-roasted, siphon-brewed coffee with about 8 single-origins and 5 blends (incl. a deep-roasted Italian), a morning service, an orange-roofed storefront and a mirror-widened interior with a hidden back seating area.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional Showa-era coffeehouse run by a single proprietor; ordering is simple but you should expect a Japanese-only menu and cash-only payment, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.

places.name: 元祖八昌 (Ganso Hassho)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and crepe, includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and is finished with wheat-containing okonomiyaki sauce (Mitsuwa). No gluten-free preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The standard okonomiyaki contains pork, egg, and the wheat-flour batter is seasoned with egg and mirin; the house style centers on meat-and-egg soba okonomiyaki. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-14:30 (some sources list 10:30-13:30); closed Sundays and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Takeyacho, Naka Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 元祖八昌 traces its roots to the red-noren 'Hassho' okonomiyaki shop and is regarded as the original of the Hassho lineage. It was opened in 1965 by the eldest son of Furuta Shozaburo, the inaugural 'village chief' who founded Okonomimura, Hiroshima's famous okonomiyaki com

chef_bio.background: 元祖八昌 traces its roots to the red-noren 'Hassho' okonomiyaki shop and is regarded as the original of the Hassho lineage. It was opened in 1965 by the eldest son of Furuta Shozaburo, the inaugural 'village chief' who founded Okonomimura, Hiroshima's famous okonomiyaki complex. The shop later moved to its current Takeyacho location and took the name 元祖八昌 ('the original Hassho'). Its red storefront earned it the nicknames 'Aka-Hassho' (red Hassho), and a daruma lantern at the shop also brought the name 'Daruma Hassho.'
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner keeps to the orthodox basics of Hiroshima okonomiyaki. The batter binder is wheat flour seasoned with egg and mirin so it deepens and supports the flavor of the ingredients rather than overpowering them, and the shop uses a slightly spicy Mitsuwa sauce as a deliberate flavor choice.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, notably the meat-and-egg soba okonomiyaki.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1965 by the eldest son of Okonomimura's founding 'village chief,' the shop has been run since 2016 by his third daughter, Yuka, as the second-generation owner.
Known affectionately as 'Aka-Hassho' for its red storefront and 'Daruma Hassho' for the daruma lantern, with the owner describing the Mitsuwa sauce as 'a little spicy.'

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Chugoku Shimbun profiles the 1965 founding by the eldest son of Okonomimura founder Furuta Shozaburo and succession to third daughter Yuka (2nd generation) in 2016, the egg/mirin batter and 'a little spicy' Mitsuwa sauce; Tabelog confirms the Takeyacho location and Aka/Daruma Hassho nicknames.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A single shared griddle (teppan) is used for all okonomiyaki, which are wheat-based and include wheat noodles; cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki is finished with Mitsuwa okonomiyaki sauce, a wheat-containing sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg, and the egg/mirin-seasoned wheat batter are cooked on the same shared griddle as all dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, old-school counter shop run by a local family where ordering is simple but little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese words and patience help.

places.name: 鉄ぱん屋 弁兵衛 八丁堀店 (Teppanya Benbee Hatchobori-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, whose batter and the layered yakisoba/udon noodles are wheat-based, and dishes are finished with wheat-based sauces and soy sauce on a shared teppan. No gluten-free menu is offered; cross-contact on the shared iron griddle is unavoidable.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (egg, pork, bonito/seafood dashi), oysters, sea urchin, gyoza, and wagyu beef steak, with fish-based and wheat-soy seasonings throughout. No vegan menu is advertised; a strict vegan would struggle here.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka Ward
places.notes: Benbee is a Hiroshima teppanyaki and okonomiyaki brand with several locations around the city and beyond. This Hatchobori branch sits in the heart of central Hiroshima's shopping and entertainment district, just a short walk from the streetcar stop, and brings together

chef_bio.background: Benbee is a Hiroshima teppanyaki and okonomiyaki brand with several locations around the city and beyond. This Hatchobori branch sits in the heart of central Hiroshima's shopping and entertainment district, just a short walk from the streetcar stop, and brings together a casual griddle-counter atmosphere and private tatami-style rooms with their own built-in teppan.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's guiding idea is 'dekitate' - serving food at its just-cooked best, hot off the iron plate. Diners are meant to take in the sight of the chefs working the griddle, the aroma of cooking, and the sizzle of ingredients as part of the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles, alongside Seto Inland Sea seafood such as local oysters and sea urchin with spinach, the house Benbee gyoza served with a special soup, and wagyu beef steak cooked on the teppan.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant offers both an open counter where you watch the cook work the iron plate and traditional private rooms with their own teppan, so groups can have dishes grilled and served piping hot right at the table.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Balcom brand page and the official Hatchobori reservation page confirm the multi-location Hiroshima teppanyaki/okonomiyaki brand, the 'dekitate' (freshly cooked, piping hot) philosophy, a live counter plus private rooms with built-in griddles, and specialties including okonomiyaki, oysters, uni with spinach, house gyoza with soup, and wagyu steak.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan iron griddle where wheat-based okonomiyaki batter, noodles, and sauces are prepared, making gluten cross-contact effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle is used for pork, seafood, egg, and beef, so plant-only dishes cannot be guaranteed free of animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, tourist-friendly teppanyaki izakaya in central Hiroshima where a little Japanese helps, but staff are used to visitors and you can point at the griddle or menu photos to order.

places.name: 鉄板 村田屋 (Teppan Murataya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The okonomiyaki is made with a wheat-flour batter, sauces are typically wheat-based soy/okonomiyaki sauce, and many griddle and izakaya dishes involve wheat. There is no mention of gluten-free options or allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The menu centers on beef, seafood, sashimi, and egg dishes, and the okonomiyaki and sauces use animal/fish-derived ingredients. A few vegetable-forward items exist (e.g., grilled potato salad, though it includes bacon, butter, and cheese), and the kitchen will accommodate custom requests when ingredients are available, but no documented fully vegan dishes. A vegan diner would need to ask in Japanese and verify each item.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–05:00 next day (food/drink L.O. 04:30). Official site and HotPepper list no regular closing day (open year-round); note Tabelog lists Sunday closed, but the official site is more authoritative and states open daily.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, late-night teppan izakaya tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, where the owner cooks meat, seafood, and vegetables on the iron griddle right in front of guests at the counter. The owner personally shops the market for the day's ingredients, and

chef_bio.background: A small, late-night teppan izakaya tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, where the owner cooks meat, seafood, and vegetables on the iron griddle right in front of guests at the counter. The owner personally shops the market for the day's ingredients, and a blackboard lists the daily recommendations.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking with seasonal, carefully sourced ingredients and serving dishes hot and freshly made, taking time and care; the staff accommodate custom requests when the ingredients are on hand.
chef_bio.specialty: Salt-grilled beef koune (a prized cut), garlic rice finished with pickled plum, torch-finished grilled potato salad with bacon and cheese, dashi-rolled egg made with beef stock, daily fresh sashimi, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

anecdotes.text: The okonomiyaki was added to the menu in response to customer requests, and the kitchen makes it by slowly sauteing the onions first for sweetness.
Open until 5:00 a.m., it draws a late-night and after-work crowd who watch the cooking unfold across the counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Murataya site and Hotpepper listing confirm the Nagarekawa counter teppan izakaya open 18:00-5:00, owner-sourced daily ingredients and blackboard specials, and specialties including rare beef koune, garlic rice with pickled-plum crystal, torched potato salad, a beef-stock dashimaki, fresh sashimi, and okonomiyaki added by customer request.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and typical griddle/izakaya seasonings rely on wheat-flour batter and wheat-based soy/okonomiyaki sauce; no gluten-free sauces are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking is done on a shared iron griddle used for beef, seafood, and egg dishes, so vegetable items are prepared on the same surface as animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen accommodates custom requests when ingredients are available, but no formal allergy or dietary handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small late-night counter griddle spot where the owner cooks in front of you; no English service is documented, so basic Japanese or pointing at the daily blackboard specials helps, and the relaxed counter is welcoming to solo diners.

places.name: 塔之岡茶屋 (Tonooka Chaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The menu centers on wheat-based items: udon (wheat noodles) and zenzai, plus mochi dusted with kinako that is served as a sweet. Nothing on any fetched page mentions gluten-free options or cross-contamination control, so celiac travelers should treat this as not suitable.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan labeling. The signature Taiko power mochi is kinako (roasted soybean flour) and sugar over mochi, which is plausibly plant-based, and the homemade amazake is traditionally rice-koji based; however udon broth and zenzai preparations are not documented as vegan, and no page confirms ingredients or cross-contact. A vegan diner would need to ask in person about specific items.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Omachi), below the Five-Story Pagoda, near Itsukus
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small open-air tea house set on the stone steps of Miyajima between the town below and the Five-Story Pagoda above, just beside the Senjokaku hall. Guests rest on tatami or cushioned low seats with a view down over the rooftops toward the pagoda and the sea. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small open-air tea house set on the stone steps of Miyajima between the town below and the Five-Story Pagoda above, just beside the Senjokaku hall. Guests rest on tatami or cushioned low seats with a view down over the rooftops toward the pagoda and the sea.
chef_bio.specialty: Taiko no Chikara Mochi (power mochi) and homemade amazake, alongside udon and zenzai.

anecdotes.text: The house specialty, Taiko no Chikara Mochi, traces to the construction of the Senjokaku hall ordered by Toyotomi Hideyoshi. When the hall was left unfinished after his death, this sweet mochi was given to the laborers; in an age when sugar was precious, workers ate the power mochi to regain strength and carry on building the hall. Today it is served as dumpling-sized mochi coated in kinako and a dusting of sugar.
In front of the tea house stands the Ryugen no Matsu, a carefully tended pine roughly 200 years old whose form recalls a dragon's whiskers, giving the spot its old, time-worn atmosphere.
The shop pairs its sweets with green tea and offers a homemade koji amazake that visitors enjoy while looking out at the Five-Story Pagoda.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabetainjya and Rurubu confirm the Miyajima hillside tea house beside Senjokaku and the Five-Story Pagoda, the Taiko-riki (power) mochi legend tied to Hideyoshi's Senjokaku construction and its laborers, the kinako-and-sugar coating, the ~200-year-old Ryugen-no-Matsu pine, and the homemade koji amazake.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny traditional open-air tea stop where you sit on tatami or low outdoor seats; pointing at the few signature items works fine, but having a Japanese-speaking friend or guide helps since English is limited.

places.name: 風籟堂 紅葉谷店 (Furaido (Furaidou) Momijidani)
places.gf_detail: Some core items are naturally gluten-free — roasted sweet potatoes (yaki-imo, including Anno-imo) and roasted chestnuts are just the whole vegetable/nut. However the signature roasted-sweet-potato soft serve is a dairy ice cream and the momiji butter sandwich and latte items involve wheat/dairy. No gluten-free program, dedicated equipment, or allergen labeling is documented, so a celiac traveler should stick to the plain roasted sweet potato / roasted chestnut and ask to confirm nothing else is added.
places.vegan_detail: The plain roasted sweet potato (yaki-imo) and roasted chestnuts are plant-based by nature. But the shop's headline products — the roasted-sweet-potato soft serve, the momiji butter sandwich, and lattes — all contain dairy/butter and are not vegan. No vegan-specific menu or labeling is documented; a vegan visitor can reasonably order the plain roasted sweet potato or chestnuts but should confirm no butter or dairy topping is added.
places.hours_raw: Hours not posted on the Tabelog listing. The brand's roasted-sweet-potato-soft-serve page lists 9:00-17:00, irregular holidays for Furaido's Miyajima operation; not branch-confirmed for the Momijidani location.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Momijidani (from Itsukushima Shrine toward Momijidani Park / the ropeway)
places.cuisine: Japanese sweets and roasted-sweet-potato desserts / cafe
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Furaido is a Miyajima-based sweets maker that roasts and sells domestic chestnuts (kuri-ka), Anno-imo roasted sweet potatoes, and momiji butter sandwiches on the island. This Momijidani location is a small takeout counter tucked inside the souvenir shop Sasaki Yaeko no  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Furaido is a Miyajima-based sweets maker that roasts and sells domestic chestnuts (kuri-ka), Anno-imo roasted sweet potatoes, and momiji butter sandwiches on the island. This Momijidani location is a small takeout counter tucked inside the souvenir shop Sasaki Yaeko no Mise, sitting along the quiet path that leads from the shrine area up toward Momiji Valley and the ropeway.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on the natural sweetness of the sweet potato itself: Tanegashima Anno-imo is roasted until a sticky, honey-like sweetness oozes from inside, almost creamy in texture.
chef_bio.specialty: Roasted-sweet-potato soft serve — an everyday soft serve blended with four carefully chosen varieties of roasted sweet potato and topped with warm roasted sweet potato — alongside whole roasted Anno-imo and roasted chestnuts.

anecdotes.text: Tucked off the main souvenir bustle, it can be a calm stop on the climb to the ropeway — one visitor found the shop quiet with nobody else around, eating their roasted sweet potato on a bench set up beside the counter.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The kurika.jp brand pages confirm the domestic roasted chestnuts (kuri-ka), Tanegashima Anno-imo roasted until honey-like and creamy, a soft serve blending four sweet-potato varieties topped with warm roasted sweet potato, and momiji butter sandwiches; Tabelog confirms the Momijidani counter inside the Sasaki Yaeko no Mise souvenir shop.

positives.text: The plain whole roasted sweet potato (yaki-imo / Anno-imo) and roasted chestnuts are single-ingredient items that are naturally gluten-free and plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed point-and-pay takeout sweets counter on the walk up to the ropeway — easy for a foreign student with no Japanese, since you just pick a roasted sweet potato or soft serve and pay cash.

places.name: ベーカリーカフェ SAN-HITORI（サン ヒトリ） (Bakery Cafe SAN-HITORI)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery; bread, cream pan and savory rolls are all made from wheat. There are no gluten-free items, and the small kitchen handles flour throughout, so it is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature products center on a dairy-based fresh cream, and the menu features savory breads, fried chicken and other items that use eggs, dairy and meat. No vegan items are documented, so it is not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 07:00-19:00; Sat 08:00-18:00; Sun & holidays closed. Morning set 07:00-10:00, lunch 11:00-14:00.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka Ward (near Hatchobori Station and Fukuya)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent bakery cafe that opened in February 2019 a minute's walk from Hatchobori Station, in the heart of central Hiroshima's shopping district. The owner grew up with the trade, as their father ran a bakery, and that family heritage inspired them to open t

chef_bio.background: A small, independent bakery cafe that opened in February 2019 a minute's walk from Hatchobori Station, in the heart of central Hiroshima's shopping district. The owner grew up with the trade, as their father ran a bakery, and that family heritage inspired them to open their own shop aiming for a warm, old-fashioned neighborhood-bakery feel. From a 7 AM opening it draws a loyal following of local office workers and shoppers, who come for morning sets, lunch, and a constantly changing lineup of breads.
chef_bio.philosophy: Handcrafted, carefully developed products and attentive, smiling hospitality, recreating the comforting atmosphere of a classic local bakery.
chef_bio.specialty: Their pride and joy is a fresh cream pan, topped with a cookie crust and filled with a smooth, gently sweet house-made cream that was developed over three months in collaboration with a pastry chef. Alongside the cream pan they offer savory breads, deli items, fried chicken, soft serve ice cream, and seasonal limited breads.

anecdotes.text: The owner's father ran a bakery, which inspired them to open SAN-HITORI, and they describe their signature cream as a point of pride co-developed with a patisserie professional.

The fresh cream for the signature cream pan reportedly took three months to develop in collaboration with a pastry chef, and the bakery opens at 7 AM with selectable morning sets popular among nearby office workers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Hiroshima Pan Festa profile confirms the owner opened SAN-HITORI because his father ran a bakery, aiming for an old-fashioned warm atmosphere, and that the house cream was co-developed with a patissier; pecomag confirms the February 2019 opening near Hatchobori, the 7:00 open with morning sets, and the three-month cream development for the signature cream pan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour bakery where flour is handled throughout the small kitchen, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy-based cream, eggs and meat items (such as fried chicken) are prepared in the same kitchen, so cross-contact for vegans is likely.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, casual neighborhood bakery where you point at what you want and order at the counter; a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way, and you can sit in with free Wi-Fi.

places.name: あなご料理廣島 紅緒 (Anago Ryori Hiroshima Benio)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The cooking is traditional washoku built around soy sauce, ponzu, simmering broths, tempura batter, and noodle dishes, all of which typically contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: This is an anago (conger eel) and seafood specialist; signature dishes are eel sashimi, simmered eel, oysters, and chicken-broth noodles. Dishes rely on fish, shellfish, and dashi, and seasonings use wheat-based soy sauce. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–23:00 (Mon–Sat, holidays, and days before holidays). Last order around 22:00. Closed Sun.
places.neighborhood: Yakkenbori, Nagarekawa (central Hiroshima, near Ebisucho Sta
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Junji Nakahara decided to become a chef after watching a skilled fishmonger at work during a part-time job at an izakaya. He went to culinary school after high school and spent more than a decade cooking Japanese cuisine at a Hiroshima hotel before opening his own place

chef_bio.background: Junji Nakahara decided to become a chef after watching a skilled fishmonger at work during a part-time job at an izakaya. He went to culinary school after high school and spent more than a decade cooking Japanese cuisine at a Hiroshima hotel before opening his own place, Benio, in 2003. He runs it essentially on his own, handling everything from sourcing the fish to the cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: His aim is to make Hiroshima's prized anago an approachable, everyday pleasure rather than a special-occasion luxury, served in a relaxed setting with the care of a chef with decades of washoku training.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel), most famously served as 'nama-chiri' sashimi with ponzu, made from live eel dressed fresh that morning; he also serves slow-simmered boiled-eel nigiri, plus seasonal Setouchi seafood like rock oysters.

anecdotes.text: The eel sashimi (nama-chiri with ponzu) is made from live conger gutted fresh each morning, prized for its crisp texture and clean, refined fat; it is ordered by nearly everyone who visits.
The restaurant occupies a roughly 60-year-old traditional townhouse in the lively Yakkenbori district, where the owner welcomes guests who travel from afar for his anago.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Hitosara chef profile confirms Nakahara Junji (b.1968, Hiroshima), inspired to cook after watching a fishmonger at a part-time izakaya job, trained at culinary school and 10-plus years at a Hiroshima hotel, opening Benio in 2003 and running it largely solo with a mission to make anago affordable; Hitosara and Gorp confirm the fresh-gutted live-eel sashimi and the ~60-year-old Yakkenbori townhouse.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes are served with ponzu and soy-sauce-based simmering liquids, and the menu includes tempura and wheat noodles, all standard wheat-containing washoku preparations.

cultural_comfort.note: A small dinner-only local spot run single-handedly by the owner-chef; little to no English is indicated, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, and reserving ahead is wise for the counter or tatami room.

places.name: おばんざい暖 (Obanzai Non)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. As a Japanese obanzai izakaya the cooking relies on standard soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings (wheat-containing soy sauce), and many small plates and simmered dishes are not gluten-free. Nothing indicates dedicated GF preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu centers on Setouchi seasonal fish and seafood and other Japanese home-style dishes, with fish-based dashi and soy-sauce seasonings throughout; no vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 18:00-23:00; Mon irregular days off (reservation required, call by noon the previous day); closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori / Ebisu-cho, central Hi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, intimate Japanese eatery in central Hiroshima that opened in summer 2023, serving handmade obanzai (Japanese home-style small plates) alongside seasonal fish and seafood from the nearby Setouchi (Seto Inland Sea).

chef_bio.background: A small, intimate Japanese eatery in central Hiroshima that opened in summer 2023, serving handmade obanzai (Japanese home-style small plates) alongside seasonal fish and seafood from the nearby Setouchi (Seto Inland Sea). It is run by a husband-and-wife team.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade obanzai small plates and Setouchi seasonal seafood, served as a relaxed multi-dish experience at the counter.

anecdotes.text: The room is designed to feel calm and settled, with soft warbler-green walls and wooden furnishings, and an intentionally small entrance that gives it a tucked-away, hidden-retreat feel.
The kitchen rotates new small plates regularly so the lineup stays varied for returning guests.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog dates the opening to 29 July 2023 and confirms the husband-wife operation, obanzai/Setouchi-seafood focus and rotating menu; Gnavi and a suocca writeup corroborate the calm uguisu (warbler-green) walls, wooden furnishings and small hidden-retreat entrance.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-focused izakaya where phone reservations and ordering are in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable bringing some Japanese or a Japanese-speaking companion.

places.name: パン ドール (Pain D'or)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery; breads and sandwiches are made from wheat, so there are no gluten-free options.
places.vegan_detail: A general bakery whose signature items (such as the salted-butter bread) use dairy butter; no items are documented as vegan, so vegans would need to ask which, if any, breads are made without butter, milk, or egg.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 07:00-19:00; closed Wed, Sun, holidays
places.neighborhood: Funairi-Nakamachi, Naka-ku (near Funairimachi tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent neighborhood bakery in Funairi-Nakamachi that opened in June 2015, a short walk from the Funairimachi tram stop and roughly two kilometers from Peace Memorial Park.

chef_bio.background: A small independent neighborhood bakery in Funairi-Nakamachi that opened in 2015, a short walk from the Funairimachi tram stop and roughly two kilometers from Peace Memorial Park.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked breads and sandwiches, with a daily lineup said to top 100 kinds; the shop's most popular item is a chewy salted-butter roll made with French sea salt and priced around 130 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop opens at 7:00 a.m. to catch early commuters and students, and its small eat-in corner of two-person tables lets customers sit with a free coffee — an amenity that tends to fill up on weekends.
A nostalgic chocolate-coated doughnut ('choco flower') is a quietly beloved item, not overly sweet.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The okappe bakery-hopping writeup (2018, shop then ~3 years old, consistent with a 2015 opening) confirms the Funairi-Nakamachi location by the Funairimachi tram, 100-plus daily breads, the #1 chewy French-sea-salt butter roll at ~130 yen, the 7:00 opening, the choco flower doughnut, and the two-person eat-in tables with free coffee that fill on weekends; Hitosara confirms the address and 7:00 opening.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed self-serve neighborhood bakery where you pick up bread on a tray and pay at the counter, so a friendly greeting and pointing are enough even with little Japanese.

places.name: 出雲そば大黒屋 本店 (Izumo Soba Daikokuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a soba (buckwheat noodle) shop, but Izumo soba is generally not gluten-free: the noodles typically use wheat flour as a binder and the dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen handling is documented. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The dipping broth uses fish/bonito-based dashi and wheat soy sauce, signature dishes include meat soba (肉そば) topped with raw egg, and the shop also operates as an izakaya. No documented vegan options or plant-based dashi.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 18:30-02:00 (L.O. 01:30); closed Sunday, Monday, and holidays
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka Ward (near Hatchobori, right by Kanayamacho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A longstanding Izumo soba specialist in the Ginzan-cho area of central Hiroshima, founded in 1961 (Showa 36) and operating for over sixty years. It is a small, family-style independent shop that has become a local fixture, popular both for lunch and as a late-night spot

chef_bio.background: A longstanding Izumo soba specialist in the Ginzan-cho area of central Hiroshima, founded in 1961 (Showa 36) and operating for over sixty years. It is a small, family-style independent shop that has become a local fixture, popular both for lunch and as a late-night spot to finish off an evening out.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, hospitable neighborhood eatery focused on traditional Izumo soba and a welcoming, comfortable atmosphere for regulars and newcomers alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Izumo soba, one of Japan's three great soba traditions, served in the classic warigo style (stacked lacquered tiers where the dipping sauce cascades from one layer to the next so each bite tastes a little different). A three-color warigo and a warm meat soba topped with raw egg are signature dishes.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe being warmly greeted by the older women who run the shop, who send guests off with a cheerful 'Please come again soon!' and create a relaxed, comfortable mood.
The shop stays open into the early hours, making it a favorite stop for a bowl of soba after a night out.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Park-Side Hotel gourmet blog confirms the 1961 (Showa 36) founding and 60-plus years, the family-run shop with warmly welcoming older women, the warigo tiered soba, the three-color warigo and warm meat soba with raw egg, and the late-night hours; Gnavi confirms the Ginzan-cho location, warigo/three-color warigo, 'three great soba' billing, and dinner service to 2:00.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Izumo soba noodles typically contain wheat flour as a binder and are prepared in shared boiling water; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The traditional soba dipping broth (tsuyu) is wheat-based soy sauce and bonito/fish dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu includes animal products such as meat soba topped with raw egg, and the shop also serves izakaya fare; broth uses fish dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, old-school neighborhood soba shop run by friendly local women; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will go a long way.

places.name: タンツーメン (Tantsumen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The single signature dish is a wheat-flour noodle (ramen-style) bowl, so the noodles themselves contain gluten. The broth is a chicken-based stock and toppings include seasoned ground pork; no gluten-free preparation or menu is documented. There is no indication of dedicated gluten-free handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The one dish served, tantsumen (担仔麺), is built on a chicken-based clear broth and topped with seasoned ground pork. No plant-based version is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:00-15:00; closed Mon and Sun
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka Ward (Hatchobori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A pocket-sized daytime noodle shop tucked on the third floor of a building in the Ginzan-cho block of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Hatchobori shopping district. The room leads a double life: by day it is a quiet lunch counter, and after hours it becomes a sm

chef_bio.origin: The house dish is tantsumen (担仔麺), a noodle style with roots in Taiwan, reinterpreted here in a gentle Hiroshima form.
chef_bio.background: A pocket-sized daytime noodle shop tucked on the third floor of a building in the Ginzan-cho block of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Hatchobori shopping district. The room leads a double life: by day it is a quiet lunch counter, and after hours the same third-floor space becomes a separate night-time establishment, so the noodles are very much a midday-only affair.
chef_bio.philosophy: A do-one-thing-well counter: simple, inexpensive, and built around a single signature bowl rather than a long menu.
chef_bio.specialty: A single focused bowl of tantsumen served at a modest price. The broth is a light, clear chicken-based soup, deliberately not spicy, finished with a topping of seasoned ground pork. Diners are encouraged to change up the flavor partway through with plenty of ground sesame and a splash of vinegar.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe a light, clean chicken broth with a fresh hint of celery rather than the heavy, fiery character of typical Taiwan ramen, and recommend loading on ground sesame and a touch of vinegar to shift the flavor as you eat.
The space runs as a lunch-only noodle counter from late morning to mid-afternoon, while the same third-floor room becomes a separate night-time establishment after closing.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms the 3rd-floor Ginzan-cho location, the Taiwan-origin tantsumen (担仔麺), the single lunch-only bowl (Tue-Sat 11:00-15:00) of light, clear, non-spicy chicken broth topped with ground pork, and the sesame/vinegar and celery notes; the listing shows the same third-floor space operating as a separate night-time venue after hours (so the earlier 'evening bar' wording was corrected).

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature and essentially only dish is a wheat-noodle bowl, so gluten is central to the kitchen; no gluten-free handling or separate preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is chicken-based and the standard topping is seasoned ground pork, so the dish is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians and there is no plant-based alternative noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny lunch-only counter serving one dish at around 700 yen; ordering is simple, but expect Japanese-only menu and service, so knowing a few basic phrases helps.

places.name: 浪漫座 (Romanza)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a Hiroshima seafood izakaya, dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce, tempura/fried items (croquettes), and other wheat-containing preparations, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian offering is documented. The menu centers on oysters, conger eel, sashimi, sea urchin, wagyu beef and other animal products, with dishes seasoned using fish- and wheat-soy-based sauces.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat, days before holidays, and holidays 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Sun (when Sun and Mon are consecutive holidays, open Sun and closed Mon instead)
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka Ward (within the Hondori area)
places.notes: A Hiroshima izakaya tucked into the basement of Hotel Yamashiroya in the Tatemachi district, just within the Hondori arcade area near the city center. The room is dressed in nostalgic Showa- and Taisho-era retro decor, with antique clocks, a phonograph and other vintage

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima izakaya tucked into the basement of Hotel Yamashiroya in the Tatemachi district, just within the Hondori arcade area near the city center. The room is dressed in nostalgic Showa-era retro decor, with antique clocks, a phonograph and other vintage objects giving it the feel of a grown-up hideaway.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let guests enjoy Hiroshima's seasonal local cuisine and fresh seafood at reasonable prices in a calm, retro setting suited to both small gatherings and private parties.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima regional seafood, above all locally beloved oysters served grilled, plus conger eel (anago) sashimi, sea urchin with spinach, daily fresh sashimi assortments, and beef dishes such as seared wagyu.

anecdotes.text: The interior is themed as a Showa-retro adult hideaway, decorated with antique clocks and a phonograph, and offers three private rooms for gatherings.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official owst page confirms the basement location in Hotel Yamashiroya (Tatemachi, a 2-minute walk from the Tatemachi tram stop), the Showa-retro adult-hideaway decor with antique clocks and gramophones, three private rooms, and the Hiroshima seafood focus (grilled oysters, anago, uni with spinach, daily sashimi and wagyu).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi, grilled and simmered seafood dishes at this izakaya are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a relaxed local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way; no English service is documented, so a translation app helps for ordering.

places.name: 寿屋珈琲 幟町店 (Kotobukiya Coffee Noboricho)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The menu centers on coffee plus wheat-based items: panini sandwiches, buttered toast, and a coffee soft-serve. No gluten-free bread or dedicated handling is mentioned anywhere on the official site or other sources.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The signature coffee soft-serve and the milk-based blend drinks contain dairy; panini and buttered toast use wheat and likely dairy. Black hand-drip coffee itself is plant-based, but no labeled vegan food options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:30 (sources vary; one source lists 9:30-18:00 winter hours). Closed Sundays and holidays.
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka Ward (near Kanayamacho / Hatchobori, within the Peace Memorial Park area)
places.cuisine: Old-fashioned coffee house with house-roasted coffee
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running Hiroshima coffee house. The Kotobukiya business began in 1947 in central Hiroshima selling coffee and beverages, formalized as a roasting wholesaler in 1951, and moved to the Noboricho location around 1952 — making this the company's flagship retail shop,

chef_bio.background: A long-running Hiroshima coffee house. The Kotobukiya business began in 1947 in central Hiroshima selling coffee and beverages, formalized as a roasting wholesaler in 1951, and moved to the Noboricho location around 1952 — making this the company's flagship retail shop, in operation for over seventy years. The company sources and roasts green beans from around the world in pursuit of a mild, approachable flavor.
chef_bio.philosophy: A welcoming specialty coffee shop that helps each visitor — even people new to coffee — discover the beans and brewing style they enjoy. Manager Ikeda believes a truly memorable cup leaves a lasting impression: he tells of a customer who carefully recreates the shop's blend at home by mixing specific proportions of beans, because the flavor stayed in their memory.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-dripped coffee from roughly 30 fresh bean varieties, the location-exclusive 'Noboricho Soft Blend,' and the much-loved coffee soft-serve ice cream paired with house-made coffee jelly; also panini, buttered toast, and fruit smoothies.

anecdotes.text: Manager Ikeda first fell for coffee as a university student visiting roasting cafés; though he needed milk to enjoy it back then, years later as a salaryman he tasted a cup so good he could drink it black — it carried the Kotobukiya (Toriyu) label — and a decade later he ended up joining the company. He earned a Coffee Meister certification to build customers' trust while carrying on the shop's decades-long legacy.
The shop began in 1947 in central Hiroshima and settled at the Noboricho address around 1952, becoming the company's flagship store and now operating for over seventy years.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: poppo-cafe.co.jp gives the company history (founded 1947, roasting from 1951, Noboricho from 1952) and the Noboricho menu (~30 beans, exclusive Noboricho Soft Blend, coffee soft-serve); emeraldmountain.jp profiles manager Ikeda and his Coffee Meister certification.

positives.text: Black hand-dripped coffee is available from about 30 bean varieties, which is naturally plant-based for those avoiding dairy in their drink.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed old-school coffee house where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; pointing at the case for the coffee soft-serve works fine, and solo visitors are very welcome.

places.name: つぼやきカレー キートン 幟町店 (Tsuboyaki Curry Keaton (Keyton) Noboricho-te)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Japanese-style curry is thickened with a wheat-flour-based roux, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodation is documented at this shop. Travelers with celiac disease should avoid it.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The curries are meat- and seafood-based (chicken cutlet, corned beef, special beef, seasonal fried oyster) and the wheat-flour roux is not documented as plant-based. No vegan or clearly vegetarian option is listed; an honest assessment is that this is an omnivore curry shop.
places.hours_raw: Listings differ: one source shows 11:00-15:00 and 18:00-20:00 (lunch and dinner split); another shows a continuous 11:00-21:00. A weekday 13:00-18:00 time-service offers a free mini salad or soft drink. Confirm on the day of visit.
places.neighborhood: 中区幟町 (Noboricho, near Hatchobori / within the Peace Memorial
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Keaton (also written 亀屯) began in 1985 as a curry specialty shop. It was started at the height of Japan's family-restaurant boom, founded on the conviction that to truly compete in food service you should specialize in one thing done well rather than offer a sprawling m

chef_bio.origin: Founded 1985 in the Higashihiroshima area before spreading to central Hiroshima and other regions.
chef_bio.background: Keaton (also written 亀屯) began in 1985 as a curry specialty shop. It was started at the height of Japan's family-restaurant boom, founded on the conviction that to truly compete in food service you should specialize in one thing done well rather than offer a sprawling menu. From a single shop the curry grew into a well-loved name across Hiroshima Prefecture and beyond, with the Noboricho location operating as one of its branches in central Hiroshima.
chef_bio.philosophy: A specialist's focus on curry above all else, with a recipe the shop says keeps evolving over the decades rather than standing still.
chef_bio.specialty: Tsuboyaki curry: a deeply spiced curry finished to order and brought to the table bubbling hot in a thick (roughly 4mm) custom-made iron pot, with the rice served separately so the curry stays piping hot throughout the meal. Diners customize toppings and dial in their heat on a playful lettered spice scale (A regular, B spicy, C extreme), with milder options for children.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained as an aspiring painter in his student years, and the calm, wood-toned dining room is dressed with antiques he personally selected, giving the shop a retro, collector's-den atmosphere.
Each order is finished one bowl at a time in its own iron pot and carried out still sizzling, a signature serving style the shop has built its name on since 1985.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: keyton-curry.com and higashihiroshima-digital.com confirm the 1985 Higashi-Hiroshima founding, the specialize-in-curry philosophy, the custom 4mm iron-pot tsuboyaki service, the owner's painter background/antiques, and an A/B/C heat scale.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, counter-style curry shop where you can point at the picture menu and pick a spice level on a simple A-to-Z scale, so a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way; bring cash as cards are not accepted.

places.name: 瑠羽樹 (Ruuju)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The menu centers on fried oysters (wheat breading), oyster udon (wheat noodles), and anago/oyster dishes typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free items or handling are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around oysters and anago (conger eel); dishes use fish/shellfish and wheat-based soy sauce. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog listing: 10:30 until sold out. Annex reference hours: Mon–Sat 11:00–16:00, Sun 9:00–16:00. Varies by source, so confirm in advance.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima-cho (around Itsukushima Shrine / Omotesando)
places.cuisine: Diner focused on oyster dishes and anagomeshi (conger eel rice)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small dining spot tucked away on a quieter back street of Miyajima, a short walk off the main approach to Itsukushima Shrine. It is run as an offshoot of a high-end Miyajima inn, which channels the inn's kitchen toward an approachable, lunch-focused menu of the island (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small dining spot tucked away on a quieter back street of Miyajima, a short walk off the main approach to Itsukushima Shrine. It is run as an offshoot of a high-end Miyajima inn, which channels the inn's kitchen toward an approachable, lunch-focused menu of the island's signature seafood.
chef_bio.specialty: Local Miyajima classics: fried-oyster set meals (カキフライ定食), oyster udon, and anago (conger eel) rice, served in generous, hearty portions.

anecdotes.text: A diner who set out for a specialist conger-eel shop wandered in here by chance on the way toward the mountain shrine and came away satisfied, singling out the unusually large, tasty oysters in the oyster udon and the relaxed terrace seating among other visitors.
Staff explained to a guest that the eatery is operated as an arm of an upscale Miyajima inn.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog's listing and a diner review confirm the Miyajima food-stall serving anago rice, fried-oyster sets and oyster udon, the large tasty oysters, terrace seating, and that staff described it as run by an upscale Miyajima inn.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu centers on anago rice and oyster dishes, which in this region are conventionally prepared with standard wheat-based soy sauce/tare; no gluten-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is oyster- and conger-eel-focused, so shared equipment and stocks make vegan dining impractical here.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual local eatery on a quieter Miyajima back street; pointing at the photo menu and basic Japanese will get you by, and English may be limited.

places.name: 肉のやま金 (Niku no Yamakin)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The lunch tonteki is breaded/fried pork, and ginger pork and yakiniku rely on soy-based marinades and dipping sauces that contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated meat restaurant — pork tonteki and ginger pork at lunch, A5 wagyu yakiniku and beef-bone soup (seolleongtang) at dinner. No vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka Ward
places.cuisine: Yakiniku and pork dishes: lunch is tonteki a
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent meat house in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district that wears two faces through the day. At lunch it is a casual, generous pork spot built around Hiroshima-raised sangenton (three-breed) pork, served as a hearty tonteki or ginger pork. Come evenin

chef_bio.background: A small independent meat house in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district that wears two faces through the day. At lunch it is a casual, generous pork spot built around sangenton (three-breed) pork, served as a hearty tonteki (pork steak). Come evening it shifts into a quieter A5 black wagyu yakiniku restaurant served in private sunken-kotatsu (horigotatsu) rooms.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen selects its beef daily, working directly with suppliers and limiting it to top-grade A5 marbling (BMS10 and above) for the dinner yakiniku.
chef_bio.specialty: The lunch tonteki (pork steak) made with sangenton, offered in escalating portion sizes; at dinner, the signature wagyu kiri-otoshi (assorted premium offcuts) of A5 black wagyu.

anecdotes.text: The lunch pork is offered in five sizes — small (100g), regular (150g), large (200g), extra (250g) and jumbo (450g) — with the same price from small through large, and free cabbage refills.
The shop sits near a long-standing bowling alley in Hatchobori, just a few minutes' walk from the tram stop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official gorp.jp and gnavi list the dual lunch (sangenton tonteki, five sizes, free cabbage refills) / dinner A5 black-wagyu yakiniku (BMS10+, wagyu kiri-otoshi, horigotatsu rooms) and the Hatchobori location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ginger pork and yakiniku use soy-based marinades and dipping sauces, which in Japan are normally made with wheat.
