cultural_comfort.note: A friendly local meat spot where a few words of Japanese help, especially for the dinner yakiniku; the lunch pork sets are easy to point and order.

places.name: 日本料理 まめ福 (Nihon Ryori Mamefuku)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese restaurant whose cooking is built on dashi, soy sauce and seasoned broths, which typically contain wheat. Sushi, tempura, and simmered dishes commonly involve wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation is documented. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe here.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on natural Setouchi fish and seafood, with meat available on request, and nearly everything is built on fish/bonito dashi and seasoned with soy-based sauces. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 13:45); Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (closed Monday if Monday is a public holiday).
places.neighborhood: Teppocho, Naka Ward (Hatchobori / Jogakuin-mae area, ~1.2 km east of the Genbaku Dome)
places.cuisine: Family-run Japanese cuisine / kappo using wild Setouchi fish and local vegetables
places.notes: An established Hiroshima Japanese restaurant in the Teppocho district, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Jogakuin-mae areas. The founding chef trained for ten years at a renowned kaiseki restaurant in Osaka before opening here, and he still visits the local market al

chef_bio.background: An established Hiroshima Japanese restaurant in the Teppocho district, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Jogakuin-mae areas. The founding chef trained for ten years at a renowned kaiseki restaurant in Osaka before opening here, and he still visits the local market almost daily to hand-pick premium natural fish from the Setouchi Inland Sea.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef pursues deliciousness above rigid convention, saying that with good seasonal ingredients in hand he will freely move between Japanese, Western and Chinese touches, and pairs the food with domestic wines as well as sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Natural Setouchi seafood prepared by the method best suited to each fish (aged, grilled, or served raw), alongside seasonal vegetables from contracted local farms. Seasonal highlights include bamboo shoots and wild vegetables in spring, conger eel and sweetfish in summer, matsutake in autumn, and fugu and snow crab in winter.

anecdotes.text: The founding chef apprenticed for ten years at a well-known Osaka kaiseki house and visits the market nearly every day to choose the day's fish himself.
Vegetables come from contracted local farms, and dishes are tailored to each ingredient through aging, grilling or serving raw.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms the chef's 10-year Osaka kaiseki training, near-daily market visits for natural Setouchi fish, contracted-farm vegetables, cross-genre (Japanese/Western/Chinese) cooking, domestic wine and sake, and the seasonal ingredients.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese restaurant relying on dashi, soy sauce and seasoned broths, dishes here almost certainly use wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergy or gluten information is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is fish- and seafood-centered with dashi-based seasoning throughout, so vegan diners would face fish stock and animal ingredients in most dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a calm, traditional Japanese restaurant with private rooms where staff likely speak limited English, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student feel comfortable, especially at the more formal dinner courses.

places.name: シマラボ (Shimalabo)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. As a classic French kitchen, dishes routinely involve flour, bread, and butter-based sauces, so gluten exposure is likely. Because it is a small, fully reservation-only, course-based restaurant, you would need to discuss gluten restrictions directly when booking; do not assume any dish is gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant. While it is praised for organic and natural-farming vegetables (notably from Watanabe Farm in Higashi-Hiroshima), the menu is built around meat, wagyu beef, foie gras, and classic French butter/dairy and seafood preparations, so it is not vegan by default. Any plant-based meal would need to be arranged in advance with the chef, and is not documented as available.
places.hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday and holidays: Lunch 12:00–14:30 (L.O. 13:00), Dinner 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 19:00). Closed Mondays (open if Monday is a public holiday, with a substitute closure on Tuesday or Wednesday). Closed Dec 31–Jan 3.
places.neighborhood: Kamihatchobori, Naka Ward (in front of Shukkeien Garden)
places.notes: Owner-chef Mitsunori Shimamura was born in Hiroshima in 1972 and discovered cooking through a high-school part-time job. He trained at the French campus of the Tsuji Culinary Institute, worked at leading Japanese hotels, and apprenticed in Lyon and Cannes before returni

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Mitsunori Shimamura was born in Hiroshima in 1972 and discovered cooking through a high-school part-time job. He trained at the French campus of the Tsuji Culinary Institute, worked at leading Japanese hotels, and apprenticed in Lyon and Cannes before returning home. He was involved from the opening of Mikuni Hiroshima, then carried forward the spirit of master chef Kiyoshi Mikuni to open his own restaurant, which he renamed Shimalabo in 2017; he later opened a sister restaurant, Shimalabo Bis, in 2020.
chef_bio.philosophy: Inheriting Mikuni's belief that 'the ingredients decide the cooking,' the kitchen builds its menus around seasonal, locally-sourced produce — especially organic, natural-farming vegetables from Watanabe Farm in Higashi-Hiroshima — and pursues sustainable, near-zero food-waste cooking. The dining room is run with a warm, home-like sense of hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Course-based 'cuisine du terroir' (la cuisine du terroir) — generous, classically-rooted French cooking that puts local Hiroshima produce and proteins at the center, with theatrical tableside service.

anecdotes.text: After cooking in Lyon and Cannes and helping open Mikuni Hiroshima, Shimamura set out on his own to carry forward Kiyoshi Mikuni's culinary spirit in his home city, renaming the restaurant Shimalabo in 2017.
The kitchen sources organic, natural-farming vegetables from Watanabe Farm in Higashi-Hiroshima and aims for zero food waste, reflecting the chef's terroir-driven philosophy.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The hitosara chef profile and a Hiroshima-hirobiro interview confirm chef Shimamura (b.1972 Hiroshima), Tsuji French-campus training, Lyon/Cannes, the Mikuni Hiroshima opening, the 2017 rename to Shimalabo, the 2020 Shimalabo Bis, and the waste-free terroir philosophy.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant is fully reservation-only and course-based, and asks diners to communicate special needs (for example, smaller portions) at the time of booking, which suggests requests can be discussed in advance, though no specific allergen protocol is documented.

positives.text: Emphasis on organic, natural-farming vegetables sourced from a named local farm, which may help diners discuss ingredient sourcing with the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a refined, fully reservation-only fine-dining French restaurant where booking ahead (by phone or online) and basic Japanese for arranging your meal will smooth things along; it is a special-occasion splurge rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: 宮島咖喱麵麭研究所 宮島本店 (Miyajima Curry Pan Kenkyujo Miyajima Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a curry-bread (カレーパン) specialty shop. Every signature item is deep-fried bread made from wheat flour, so it is not suitable for gluten-free diets. There is no documented gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's identity is the oyster curry bread, which contains whole Hiroshima oysters (shellfish); other varieties feature Hiroshima black wagyu beef and the curry roux. The breads are not vegan, and no documented plant-based option exists.
places.hours_raw: 08:00-18:00 (ends when sold out)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (along the approach toward Itsukushima Shrine)
places.cuisine: Takeaway specialist for oyster curry bread, each stuffed with a whole Hiroshima oyster
places.notes: [Miyajima] On the bustling approach to Itsukushima Shrine, this stand is the Miyajima flagship of a Hiroshima curry-bread project run by a bakery with more than 40 years of history. The Miyajima oyster curry-bread shop opened in 2014 and was rebranded as the Miyajima Curry Pan Ken (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: On the bustling approach to Itsukushima Shrine, this stand is the Miyajima flagship of a Hiroshima curry-bread project run by a bakery with more than 40 years of history. The Miyajima oyster curry-bread shop opened in 2014 and was rebranded as the Miyajima Curry Pan Kenkyujo (Miyajima Curry Bread Laboratory) in April 2021, becoming what is billed as Hiroshima's first curry bread specialty shop.
chef_bio.specialty: Oyster curry bread (kaki curry pan) stuffed with whole, plump Hiroshima-grown oysters, fried fresh and crisp to order. The mild, lightly spiced curry roux is gentle enough for children and older guests, and the shop offers more than 15 original curry-bread varieties, including ones made with Hiroshima black wagyu beef.

anecdotes.text: The oyster essence is said to melt into the curry roux so the umami spreads richly through each bite, with two plump Hiroshima oysters tucked inside the bread.
Demand has been enormous: the shop has had days when as many as 2,500 curry breads sold in a single day.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: bigset.jp, umahiro.jp and a Yahoo expert article confirm the 40+ year bakery, the 2014 opening, the April 2021 rebrand to Miyajima Curry Pan Kenkyujo, 15+ varieties (incl. black-wagyu), two Hiroshima oysters per bun, and up to 2,500 sold per day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All products are wheat-flour curry breads fried in a shared kitchen, so there is no gluten-free safe option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items contain oysters (shellfish) and other varieties contain wagyu beef; breads are not plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual takeaway counter on the busy shrine approach where you can simply point at the oyster curry bread and pay cash, so very little Japanese is needed, though there is no English menu.

places.name: 藤い屋 宮島本店 (Fujiiya Miyajima Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Momiji manju is a castella-style cake made from wheat flour, so the core product contains gluten. Their alternative "momiji mochi" is described as a blend of wheat flour with rice and mochi powder, so it also contains wheat. No gluten-free items are documented and there is no indication of allergen-controlled preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Momiji manju is a wheat-and-egg castella batter wrapped around fillings (the classic smooth/chunky red bean, plus matcha, custard cream, and chocolate). The castella batter and custard/chocolate fillings are not vegan, and no plant-based versions are documented. A vegan visitor would not have a confirmed option here.
places.hours_raw: 09:30-17:00 daily (some sources list 08:30-18:00; hours may change seasonally — confirm directly)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (along the approach to Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Miyajima] Fujiiya has been making momiji manju on Miyajima since 1925 (Taisho 14), and is counted among the island's oldest and most respected makers of the maple-leaf-shaped bean cakes that are Hiroshima's signature souvenir. The main store sits midway along the Omotesando shopp

chef_bio.background: Fujiiya has been making momiji manju on Miyajima since 1925 (Taisho 14), and is counted among the island's oldest and most respected makers of the maple-leaf-shaped bean cakes that are Hiroshima's signature souvenir. The main store sits midway along the Omotesando shopping street that leads from the Miyajima ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine, and visitors can watch the momiji manju being made on the in-store production line and eat them freshly baked.
chef_bio.philosophy: Their origin and pride is the elegantly refined smooth bean paste — a 'fuji-iro' (wisteria-colored) an made by peeling the adzuki skins before cooking only the beans — made without preservatives, using carefully selected ingredients and craftsmen's technique for a gentle, wholesome flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked momiji manju, especially their signature smooth bean paste, alongside matcha, custard cream, chocolate, and chunky red-bean varieties; the retro-styled café lets you eat them warm with coffee or matcha.

anecdotes.text: Fujiiya has continued making momiji manju on Miyajima for more than 90 years since its founding in Taisho 14 (1925), and its origin product is the elegant peeled-adzuki 'fuji-iro' smooth bean paste made without any preservatives.
Inside the retro-atmosphere shop you can enjoy freshly baked momiji manju along with coffee or matcha while watching them being made.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: momiji-manju.jp and fujiiya.co.jp confirm the 1925 (Taisho 14) founding, Big-Three status, the peeled-adzuki 'fuji-iro' koshian made without preservatives or synthetic colors, and the Omotesando shop's freshly-baked café.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire product line is wheat-flour-based (castella-batter momiji manju and a wheat/rice/mochi 'momiji mochi'), so the shop is not a gluten-free environment and no allergen-segregated preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Momiji manju batter is a wheat-and-egg castella; custard cream and chocolate fillings are also not plant-based, and no vegan version is documented.

positives.text: Momiji manju are made fresh on-site and the shop emphasizes no preservatives and carefully selected ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, tourist-friendly confectionery on the main Miyajima approach where you can simply point and buy a freshly baked sweet, though deeper café conversation will be in Japanese.

places.name: かんな (Kanna (Shizenshoku Restaurant Kanna))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The kitchen is a Japanese natural-food kitchen using soy sauce and miso (both typically wheat-containing) and serving fried dishes with batter, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. A celiac or strict gluten-free diner must ask in advance and cannot assume safe dishes.
places.vegan_detail: The focus is organic, pesticide-free vegetables and an organic miso soup, which is very vegetable-forward, but the daily lunch is not strictly plant-based — documented dishes include a swordfish/mahi-mahi and onion fry, so fish appears and dashi/seasonings may contain fish. Vegans should call ahead to confirm a plant-based version of the day's set is available.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); closed Tuesday and Sunday. Lunch only.
places.neighborhood: Sumiyoshicho, Naka-ku (near Funairi-Honmachi, southwest of P
places.notes: Kanna is a natural-food lunch restaurant on the top floor of the Hiroshima Ryoin, an integrative-wellness facility in central Hiroshima a short walk southwest of Peace Memorial Park. Though it sits inside a clinic-and-wellness building, the dining room is open to the pu

chef_bio.origin: It is part of a wellness/integrative-care facility rooted in MOA International's Okada method of natural living and nature farming.
chef_bio.background: Kanna is a natural-food lunch restaurant on an upper (5th) floor of the Hiroshima Ryoin, an integrative-wellness facility in central Hiroshima a short walk southwest of Peace Memorial Park. Though it sits inside a clinic-and-wellness building, the dining room is open to the public, not just patients.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds a daily-changing lunch around pesticide-free and organic vegetables delivered straight from nature-farming growers, with the stated aim of cooking 'Japanese food that is gentle on heart and body.' The miso soup uses an organic rice miso for a naturally deep flavor, and even the desserts, like a cake made with pesticide-free lemons, follow the same natural-ingredient ethos.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily organic-vegetable set lunch and an organic-lemon cake.

anecdotes.text: Regulars praise the miso soup, made with an organic rice miso for a naturally deep flavor.
The facility describes its cooking as the head chef's pride: 'Japanese food that is gentle on heart and body,' made with fresh ingredients shipped directly from organic nature-farming producers.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and moa-natural.jp confirm the MOA/Okada nature-farming Hiroshima Ryoin dining room (5th floor, open to the public), the organic daily set lunch, the organic-lemon cake, and the 'food gentle on heart and body' concept.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried dishes (e.g. a swordfish and onion fry) appear on the daily menu, indicating wheat batter and shared frying; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese natural-food kitchen serving set meals and miso soup, standard wheat-containing soy sauce and miso are used; no wheat-free seasoning is noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The daily set has included fish (a swordfish/mahi-mahi fry), and dashi or seasonings may contain fish, so a plant-based meal is not guaranteed without asking.

positives.text: Cooking centers on pesticide-free, organic vegetables sourced directly from nature-farming growers, which is a strong base for vegetable-forward diners.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet local natural-food lunch spot inside a wellness facility with little English, so a few Japanese phrases and a reservation call will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.

places.name: お食事てんしん (Oshokuji Tenshin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a Japanese washoku/teishoku eatery serving oyster and anago dishes, expect standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat), and fried/breaded preparations (e.g. fried oysters) and rice-bowl tare sauces that use wheat. Treat as not gluten-safe unless you can confirm specific dishes in person.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on oysters, anago (conger eel) and other fish/seafood teishoku, and Japanese seasonings here typically include dashi, soy sauce and other animal/fish-derived ingredients. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00; irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Omotesando / around Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-style eatery tucked into the streets of Miyajima near Itsukushima Shrine, serving honest, affordable home-style Japanese set meals to islanders and visitors alike. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small, family-style eatery tucked into the streets of Miyajima near Itsukushima Shrine, serving honest, affordable home-style Japanese set meals to islanders and visitors alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Miyajima set meals built around local seafood, with fresh oyster dishes featured in the winter season.
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Miyajima Tourism Association listing confirms お食事てんしん as a reasonably-priced Miyajima eatery featuring fresh oyster dishes in the winter season.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual, cash-only local spot a short walk from the shrine; point at the dish you want and a few words of Japanese or a translation app will carry you through with ease.

places.name: うぐいす（中華そば） (Uguisu (Chuka Soba))
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Hiroshima chuka soba shop: the broth is a soy-sauce-forward pork-bone soup and the wheat noodles come from a local noodle maker (丸島製麺所). The soy sauce contains wheat and the noodles are wheat, so the ramen is not gluten-free; no gluten-free preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dish is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with char siu pork and toppings; oden is also served. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 20:00–02:00 next day (Mon–Sat). Closed Sun and holidays.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward (by Shintenchi Park / Nagarekawa–Yagenbori entertainment district, ~3 min walk from Ebisucho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A late-night chuka soba counter in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagawa entertainment district, beside Shintenchi Park. It traces its roots to Hiroshima's postwar ramen culture born around 1948, when food carts began serving Chinese noodles in the city, and it carries the so

chef_bio.background: A late-night chuka soba counter in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, beside Shintenchi Park. It carries the so-called 'kotori-kei' (small-bird lineage) of Hiroshima's postwar street-cart chuka soba tradition.
chef_bio.specialty: Chuka soba in the kotori-kei Hiroshima style: a light pork-bone broth with a forward soy-sauce note, firm low-hydration noodles, and generous green onion; the popular order is the negi (green onion) char siu bowl. Oden is also offered.

anecdotes.text: It is a classic late-night 'shime' (after-drinking) ramen stop, opening only from 8pm to 2am for the entertainment-district crowd.
The kotori-kei style is described as a lighter pork-bone soup, so visitors note you can enjoy a late-night bowl 'guilt-free.'
The noodles are made by a local noodle maker (Marushima Seimenjo), which historically supplied Hiroshima's street-vendor ramen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mopeco.net and a hatenablog review confirm the Nagarekawa late-night (8pm–2am) kotori-kei chuka soba, the light soy pork-bone broth, the negi char-siu bowl, oden, and the Marushima Seimenjo noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single tiny counter kitchen serving only wheat-noodle ramen and oden; no separate gluten-free preparation is documented and cross-contact would be unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce-forward pork-bone soup; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with char siu pork and oden; no vegan/vegetarian options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, well-worn late-night counter where ordering in simple Japanese (or pointing at the short menu) goes smoothest, and a relaxed post-drink crowd makes a solo foreign visitor feel welcome.

noodles.notes: Thin, relatively low-hydration noodles with a snappy, firm bite that holds up well against the soy-forward pork broth — firmer than typical for the city's late-night bowls.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka Soba (中華そば) — or upgrade to the Negi Chashu-men with extra scallions and pork
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu, served in generous quantity on the topped versions

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is pork-bone based and the bowl carries pork chashu and a soy tare; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A classic Hiroshima late-night "kotori-kei" bowl: a light, skimmed pork-bone broth driven by a punchy soy tare — clean and refreshing rather than heavy, the soul of a city style built for the after-drinks finish. The slight body and well-judged soy tang carry the lineage of Hiroshima's shoyu-tonkotsu tradition in every sip.

places.highlight: A classic Hiroshima late-night "kotori-kei" bowl: a light, skimmed pork-bone broth driven by a punchy soy tare — clean and refreshing rather than heavy, the soul of a city style built for the after-drinks finish. The slight body and well-judged soy tang carry the lineage of Hiroshima's shoyu-tonkotsu tradition in every sip.

places.name: 広島風お好み焼き くらわんか (Hiroshima-fu Okonomiyaki Kurawanka)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour, the dish is built on soba (wheat) noodles, and it is finished with okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce that contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The okonomiyaki is made with egg and pork, the signature dish is topped with oysters, and the okonomiyaki sauce typically contains fish-derived ingredients. No vegan or vegetarian option is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (some sources list 11:00-17:00); closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Miyajima / Omotesando Shopping Arcade (宮島・表参道商店街)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop that opened in 1991, set just inside the Omotesando shopping arcade a short walk from the Miyajima ferry pier and on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. You can spot it by the giant rice-paddle (shamoji) shaped sign out front. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A family-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop that opened in 1991, set just inside the Omotesando shopping arcade a short walk from the Miyajima ferry pier and on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. You can spot it by the giant rice-paddle (shamoji) shaped sign out front.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the oyster kurawanka-yaki: a classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with cabbage, soba noodles, pork and egg, then topped with plump local Miyajima oysters so you taste the griddled oyster in nearly every bite.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits along a long griddle counter so guests can watch the okonomiyaki being cooked in front of them, and you can choose to eat straight off the hot iron plate with a spatula or ask for a plate and chopsticks.

On a famously seafood-rich island, the oyster version arrives with generous, plump oysters layered with egg and okonomiyaki sauce, treated almost as a topping so you can enjoy the griddled oysters distinctly from the okonomiyaki itself.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: okeikojapan and Tabelog confirm the 1991 founding, the shamoji-shaped sign on Omotesando, the Hiroshima-style oyster okonomiyaki with Miyajima oysters, and the griddle-counter dining.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is unavoidable here: okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour, the dish is built on wheat soba noodles, and it is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside other wheat-based okonomiyaki.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce, which conventionally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared griddle with egg, pork and oysters, so even non-oyster items contact animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming family-run griddle shop in a tourist arcade where you can point at the menu and watch your okonomiyaki cooked in front of you; basic Japanese greetings help but are not essential.

places.name: 穴子天丼・天丼 天ぷら みしなや (Anago Tendon・Tendon Tempura Mishinaya)
places.gf_detail: No primary source confirms gluten-free service. One third-party directory (Atly) labels it a "gluten-free tempura specialty," but this is unverified and a gluten-free review site reportedly contradicts it. Standard tempura batter is wheat flour, and the signature anago/sweet sauce typically uses wheat-based soy sauce, so a celiac visitor must ask directly and cannot assume any dish is safe.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on anago (conger eel) tempura and anago-meshi, served with miso soup and dashi-based dipping sauces, so dishes are built around fish and animal-derived stock. No vegan option is documented; the vegetable tempura is fried in shared oil with seafood and uses standard batter.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30). Tourist guides also say roughly 11:00 to around 16:00. No evening service.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, behind Daiganji Temple
places.cuisine: Tempura, conger-eel tendon, and anagomeshi (conger eel rice)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small tempura specialist that opened in mid-April 2024 on Miyajima, tucked behind Daigan-ji temple about a two-minute walk from the Itsukushima Shrine exit. It occupies the spot of a former tempura shop once known on the island, carrying on a local connection to fried

chef_bio.background: A small tempura specialist that opened in mid-April 2024 on Miyajima, tucked behind Daigan-ji temple about a two-minute walk from the Itsukushima Shrine exit. It occupies the spot of a former tempura shop once known on the island, carrying on a local connection to fried fare, and is regarded as the island's only dedicated tempura house. The back tatami room has a coveted window seat with a view of the great torii gate.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly fried tempura and conger eel — the signature anago-meshi (soft-simmered, lightly charred conger over rice with a sweet glaze, miso soup and a small side), plus a tempura set of shrimp, seasonal vegetables and a large piece of anago tempura served with two dipping sauces. Meals finish with a fried momiji-manju sweet.

anecdotes.text: The shop is greeted by resident dogs, including one named Lucky, who welcome guests at the entrance.
It took over the site of a former Miyajima tempura shop, continuing the area's long association with tempura.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: japan-stroll.com confirms the mid-April 2024 opening behind Daigan-ji, the former tempura shop (津久根島) on the site, status as Miyajima's only tempura specialty shop, torii-view tatami window seat, and resident dogs including ラッキー君.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago dishes use a sweet glaze/sauce typical of Japanese anago-meshi, which is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce; not confirmed gluten-free by any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura is fried in oil shared with shrimp and conger eel, so it is not free of animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A small island tempura shop best approached with a translation app and a friendly attitude, as English support and posted allergen information are not documented.

places.name: 青ひげ 本店 (Aohige Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a teppanyaki/steak house, dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce and prepared sauces that typically contain wheat, and no dedicated gluten-free handling is mentioned. Travelers requiring strict gluten avoidance should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant centers on A4 Hiroshima beef steak and Setouchi seafood (including live lobster and abalone), with no vegan or vegetarian dishes documented. Sauces and seasonings are wheat-soy-based. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Sun & holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Fri-Sat & day before holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Thursday closed.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka Ward, Hiroshima
places.notes: Aohige Honten is a Hiroshima steakhouse built on its founder's wish to energize people through food, a calling he first felt as a student and later turned into a teppanyaki restaurant. The owner personally honed his teppanyaki technique to serve refined cuisine in a cal

chef_bio.background: Aohige Honten is a Hiroshima steakhouse built on its founder's wish to energize people through food, a calling he first felt as a student and later turned into a teppanyaki restaurant. The owner personally honed his teppanyaki technique to serve refined cuisine in a calm, modern-Japanese setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to local sourcing, aiming for 100 percent local procurement of vegetables, seasonings, and ingredients from Hiroshima, alongside regional drinks like Kamotsuru sake from Saijo and wines from Miyoshi.
chef_bio.specialty: A4-grade Hiroshima beef steak, where diners choose sirloin, tenderloin, or lean cuts; the kitchen offers A4 and A5 at the same price and recommends A4 for its balance of marbling and lean meat. Setouchi seafood such as live lobster and abalone is prepared teppanyaki-style or as sashimi.

anecdotes.text: The founder set out to energize people through food, a desire that began during his student years and led him to open this steakhouse and refine his own teppanyaki craft.
The restaurant offers A4 and A5 Hiroshima beef at the same price and recommends the A4 grade for its balance of fat and lean meat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: aohige.jp/honten confirms the founder's motivation (discovered as a student) to energize people through food, the 100% local-sourcing goal, Kamotsuru sake and Miyoshi wine, the A4/A5-same-price policy recommending A4, and Setouchi live lobster and abalone.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a teppanyaki and steak house, dishes are seasoned with Japanese soy sauce and prepared sauces that typically contain wheat; no gluten-free sauces are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on beef and seafood cooked on a shared teppan griddle, so heavy cross-contact with animal products is expected.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished, upscale teppanyaki steakhouse where menus and service run mainly in Japanese, so a translation app or a few key phrases will help a foreign student order comfortably.

places.name: 鮨 稲穂 (Sushi Inaho)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Edomae-style sushi counter. Sushi is served with soy sauce (which contains wheat), and many preparations involve nikiri soy glaze, simmered eel, and other elements made with wheat-based soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated gluten-free preparation, and no indication staff accommodate celiac-level needs. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A sushi specialist whose entire focus is fresh local fish and seafood from the Seto Inland Sea, served as an omakase course. Dishes rely on fish, shellfish, eel, and fish/dashi-based seasonings, plus wheat-soy sauce. There is no vegan offering documented and the concept is fundamentally fish-based. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat and Sun 12:00–14:00 (L.O. 13:00) and 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); closed Mon (lunch on weekends only)
places.neighborhood: Ebisucho, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (Hatchobori / Ebisucho area, Windsor Building 2F)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef trained in Japanese cuisine and sushi in Hiroshima and Osaka before opening his own counter. He does not come from a Tokyo edo-mae lineage, and has elevated his craft through deliberate study, mentorship, and steady refinement over the years.

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef trained in Japanese cuisine and sushi in Hiroshima and Osaka before opening his own counter. He does not come from a Tokyo edo-mae lineage, and has elevated his craft through deliberate study, mentorship, and steady refinement over the years.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than copying Tokyo conventions, he pursues a sushi that reflects Hiroshima: the shari (sushi rice) uses locally grown Koshihikari blended with aged rice and two regional red vinegars (one from Onomichi, one from Tojo) for a mellow rather than sharp flavor, and he maintains direct relationships with fishermen from his home islands to secure distinctive regional catches.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style edo-mae sushi built around local Seto Inland Sea fish, with white fish featured prominently as a quiet statement of terroir.

anecdotes.text: He sources fish directly from fishermen on his hometown islands, securing regional catches that rarely reach ordinary markets, and leans into austere white-fish selections that show commitment to local terroir over crowd-pleasing.

His shari is built on Hiroshima Koshihikari blended with aged rice and seasoned with two local red vinegars from Onomichi and Tojo, aiming for a mellow, rounded flavor instead of a sharp Tokyo-style edge.
He has created regionally inspired dishes such as a reinterpreted Azuma-style sushi using local konoshiro, and serves rare seasonal items like nine-eye grouper liver when available.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: sushi-blog.com identifies owner-chef 三原美穂, his Hiroshima/Osaka training (est. March 2014), lack of a Tokyo edo-mae apprenticeship, the Koshihikari-plus-aged-rice shari with red vinegars from Onomichi and Tojo, sourcing from hometown-island fishermen, and dishes like konoshiro Azuma-zushi and nine-eye grouper (九絵) liver.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional edo-mae sushi counter, dishes are served with and prepared using soy sauce and nikiri glaze, which contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce or accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely fish- and seafood-focused with dashi and fish-based seasonings throughout the omakase course; there is no documented vegan preparation or separation.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, reservation-only counter sushi restaurant where the chef serves an omakase course; basic Japanese helps a great deal and a foreign student should book ahead and be ready for an upscale, etiquette-conscious setting.

places.name: ステーキ青ひげ (Steak Aohige)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a teppanyaki steakhouse, dishes are typically finished with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and other seasonings, and there is no stated dedicated handling for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a beef and seafood steakhouse centered on Hiroshima wagyu, oysters, conger eel, abalone, and spiny lobster grilled on the teppan. There is no documented vegan menu or vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00-14:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00-21:30). Closed Wednesdays (except December).
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (near Hondori Stn, within the Peace Memorial Park area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner founded the restaurant out of a wish to energize people through food, and has devoted himself to mastering teppanyaki, personally selecting every ingredient and attending closely to both the cooking and the room's design.

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima City, central Naka-ku in the Otemachi district, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park area.
chef_bio.background: The owner founded the restaurant out of a wish to energize people through food, and has devoted himself to mastering teppanyaki, personally selecting every ingredient and attending closely to both the cooking and the room's design.
chef_bio.philosophy: Local production for local consumption (地産地消): showcasing Hiroshima Prefecture beef and Seto Inland Sea seafood prepared simply on the iron griddle so the ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: A4-grade Hiroshima beef grilled on a teppan, offered as tenderloin, sirloin, and lean (akami) cuts that guests can taste side by side, alongside Seto Inland Sea seafood such as oysters, conger eel, abalone, and spiny lobster.

anecdotes.text: The room is built around a seamless large L-shaped teppan griddle, described as a first of its kind, with the chef grilling Hiroshima wagyu and local shellfish directly in front of seated guests.
The owner's guiding wish is 「食べ物を通して人を元気にしたい」(to make people energetic through food).

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: aohige.jp/steak confirms the Naka-ku Otemachi (LaForet Bldg 2F) location, the 食べ物を通して人を元気にしたい wish, A4 Hiroshima beef in tenderloin/sirloin/lean cuts, Setouchi oysters/conger eel/abalone/spiny lobster, the 'Japan's first' seamless large L-shaped teppan, and a 地産地消 sourcing philosophy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a teppanyaki steakhouse, dishes are conventionally seasoned with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and tare; no gluten-free soy sauce or allergen handling is documented on the official site.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Beef and seafood are grilled together on a single shared L-shaped teppan, so any plant items would share the same cooking surface as meat and fish.

cultural_comfort.note: An intimate counter-and-table teppanyaki room where the chef cooks in front of you; a foreign student will be welcomed but should expect Japanese-language service and is best off reserving online in advance.

places.name: 菓子処 きむら (Kashidokoro Kimura)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature momiji manju are built on a chiffon/sponge cake batter, and the shop is fundamentally a Western pastry (洋菓子) bakery, so wheat flour is central to essentially everything made here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The chiffon/sponge-based momiji manju and the shop's Western pastries rely on eggs and dairy; the cheese and cream varieties are dairy-based, and items like the omelet plate use eggs. No vegan offerings are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (closed Thu). Before its renovation there was also an evening service listed for Fri and Sat, 19:00-22:00.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (toward Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A confectioner trained at a pastry school in Tokyo brought Western patisserie technique back to Miyajima and turned it on the island's most famous sweet, the momiji manju (maple-leaf cake). (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: The owner was bothered by the dry, pasapasa texture of conventional momiji manju and, after much trial and error, developed a version built on a moist chiffon/sponge cake batter that stays soft and fluffy even days later.
chef_bio.background: A confectioner trained at a pastry school in Tokyo brought Western patisserie technique back to Miyajima and turned it on the island's most famous sweet, the momiji manju (maple-leaf cake).
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality over variety: rather than chasing a long menu, the shop concentrates on a handful of carefully crafted sweets that bridge traditional Japanese confectionery and Western patisserie.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade momiji manju in five no-compromise varieties: red bean (tsubu-an), original cheese (a blend of Danish and processed cheeses), chocolate, cream, and apple filled with fruit sauteed and simmered in wine. Alongside these the shop works as a full Western cake bakery, and since a May 2025 renewal it also runs a cafe pairing pastries with Japanese (wakocha) black tea, including a signature Miyajima omelet plate.

anecdotes.text: The owner, trained at a Tokyo pastry school, questioned the dry texture of ordinary momiji manju and reworked the dough into a chiffon-cake base that keeps its moisture over several days.

In May 2025 the shop reopened as a Western-pastry-and-Japanese-tea cafe, keeping its signature chiffon-based momiji manju while adding sit-down pastries, a homemade-jam drink, and a Miyajima omelet, described as a modernization that still preserved the shop's original character.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: momiji-manju.jp confirms the Tokyo-pastry-school-trained owner, the chiffon-batter momiji manju that stays moist for days, the five varieties and Western-cake business. modokimaru.com confirms the May 21, 2025 renewal into a 洋菓子・和紅茶 cafe (13 seats) that retained the chiffon momiji manju while adding a house-jam drink and a Miyajima omelet, keeping the shop's original character.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run sweets shop and cafe on Miyajima's main approach street; little to no English is expected, so pointing at the colorful momiji manju varieties or a translation app will carry you through.

places.name: 味邑 (Misato)
places.gf_detail: This is a conventional home-style set-meal eatery with no gluten-free accommodation documented. The menu centers on breaded cutlets (katsu), fried chicken karaage, and chanpon noodles — all wheat-based — and dishes are seasoned with regular Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free options or awareness of celiac needs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The set meals revolve around meat and fish (pork cutlets, fried chicken, fish set meals), and even a vegetable stir-fry would typically be cooked with animal-based seasonings and soy sauce on shared equipment. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
places.neighborhood: Central Hiroshima, Ebisucho / Teppocho (near Hatchobori)
places.notes: A long-standing, no-frills home-style food hall tucked into central Hiroshima near Hatchobori. The dining room is plain and comfortable — white walls, reed-textured paneling, wooden floors — the kind of unpretentious neighborhood place where local office workers fill th

chef_bio.background: A long-standing, no-frills home-style food hall tucked into central Hiroshima near Hatchobori. The dining room is plain and comfortable — white walls, reed-textured paneling, wooden floors — the kind of unpretentious neighborhood place where local office workers fill the seats at midday.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, filling home cooking at fair prices for the working neighborhood.
chef_bio.specialty: Generous, well-balanced daily set meals: rotating daily specials (日替り定食), hearty home-style 'おふくろ' sets, fish set meals, vegetable stir-fry, breaded cutlets, fried chicken karaage with lemon, and chanpon noodles — most lunch sets priced affordably under 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen and floor are run by the master together with several staff, keeping the busy lunch rush moving while still feeling like home cooking; reviewers single out the generous portions, such as a fried-chicken special served with five large pieces.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog places 味邑 in Tetsuppocho, Naka-ku (~230m from Hatchobori/Ebisucho), describing a plain 食堂 with white walls, waist-high reed paneling and wooden floors, run by a master and roughly five staff, serving daily/おふくろ/fish sets, a ¥950 veg stir-fry set, karaage with lemon and chanpon at sub-¥1,000 lunch prices.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A home-style eatery serving breaded katsu, fried karaage, and chanpon noodles from a shared kitchen; heavy wheat use and shared frying/cooking surfaces make cross-contamination very likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese home-style manner, which uses regular soy sauce containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat and fish are central to the menu and the kitchen is shared, so even vegetable dishes are prepared alongside animal products and animal-based seasonings.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a busy local lunch spot frequented by office workers; menus and service are in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at the daily set-meal board works fine.

places.name: 牡蠣と肉の店 ‐IPPO‐ 薬研堀店 (Kaki to Niku no Mise -IPPO- Yagenbori-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu centers on charcoal-grilled yakiton and oyster dishes (including fried oysters / 牡蠣の唐揚げ) seasoned with regular wheat-based soy sauce and tare; fried items use wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The concept is built entirely around oysters and pork; even non-meat sides rely on fish/wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:30). Note: other sources list Tue–Sun 17:00-23:00.
places.neighborhood: Central Hiroshima, Yagenbori / Nagarekawa (near Ebisucho tram stop)
places.cuisine: Izakaya serving oyster dishes and charcoal-grilled yakiton of Hiroshima's 'Seto Momiji' brand pork
places.notes: This Yagenbori basement izakaya is run by the Hiroshima company Dot One, whose stated mission is to create smiles between producers and customers through original cooking using heritage local ingredients. It is one of a small family of related -IPPO- shops in the city,

roles: operator: 株式会社ドット・ワン (Dot One)
chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (central Hiroshima City, Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district, in the basement next to the Seven-Eleven, about a 5-minute walk from the Gumachi tram stop).
chef_bio.background: This Yagenbori basement izakaya is run by the Hiroshima company Dot One, whose stated mission is to create smiles between producers and customers through original cooking using heritage local ingredients. It is one of a small family of related -IPPO- shops in the city, alongside a bone-in-pork branch and a factory shop for house-made products.
chef_bio.philosophy: A celebration of two of Hiroshima's signature products on one plate - the sea (oysters) and the land (locally raised pork) - emphasizing freshness, local sourcing and a relaxed izakaya atmosphere with private rooms.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh Hiroshima oysters sourced from Maruken Suisan (including kaki-komachi-style branded oysters) served raw, fried and in hot pots, paired with charcoal-grilled yakiton made from 'Seto Momiji', a Hiroshima brand pork raised on oyster-shell-based feed and butchered fresh each morning.

anecdotes.text: The shop's pork, 'Seto Momiji', is a Hiroshima brand fed on feed containing oyster shells, tying the meat back to the same waters that produce the oysters served alongside it.
Oysters are supplied by Maruken Suisan and described as ocean-scented and plump, served raw, fried and in a signature oyster hot pot.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ippo-hiroshima.com confirms operator 株式会社ドット・ワン, the producer/customer 'smiles' mission with heritage ingredients, related IPPO branches (bone-in pork in Nishihara, an IPPO Factory shop), Maruken Suisan oysters (kaki-komachi), and 'Seto Momiji' oyster-shell-fed Hiroshima brand pork butchered fresh each morning.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oyster and yakiton dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and tare, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around oysters and pork, with shared grills and fryers, so vegan separation is not provided.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local oyster-and-grilled-pork izakaya where some Japanese will help a lot, though a translation app and pointing at the menu will get you through; it is welcoming but not specifically set up for English speakers.

places.name: やけっぱち (Yakeppachi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and yakisoba/udon noodles, and it is cooked on a shared griddle. The okonomi sauce and the izakaya's soy-based dishes use wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a meat-and-griddle izakaya whose okonomiyaki contains egg, pork and bonito/seafood-based dashi and toppings, and the signature dish is a beef-tendon curry stew (すじカレー煮込み). Sauces are fish/wheat based and dishes are cooked on a shared teppan. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 16:00–22:00; closed Tue
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori (Naka Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] やけっぱち began life as a long-running local izakaya in Hatchobori. After the original izakaya closed, the son of the family behind the Hakushima (白島) shop took over and renovated the original building, reopening it as a teppan-and-okonomiyaki spot that keeps Hiroshima soul

chef_bio.background: やけっぱち began life as a long-running local izakaya in Hatchobori. After the original izakaya closed, the son of the family behind the Hakushima (白島) shop took over and renovated the original building, reopening it as a teppan-and-okonomiyaki spot that keeps Hiroshima soul food affordable and casual.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious neighborhood place to enjoy Hiroshima's own comfort food at reasonable prices in a relaxed izakaya setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked right in front of you on the griddle, plus local Hiroshima dishes; the much-loved house specialty is すじカレー煮込み (slow-simmered beef-tendon curry stew).

anecdotes.text: The current owner is the son of the family who ran the Hakushima location; when the original Hatchobori izakaya closed he renovated the same building to reopen it as a teppan okonomiyaki shop.
Regulars single out the beef-tendon curry stew (すじカレー煮込み) as a standout, memorable dish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog (Hatchobori 11-4, Shimamura Bldg 2F) confirms the original やけっぱち izakaya closed and was reopened by the son of the Hakushima shop's family after renovating the building as an okonomiyaki/teppanyaki spot, with reviewers praising the house すじカレー煮込み (beef-tendon curry stew).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is wheat-batter based and cooked on a shared griddle alongside noodles and other floured items, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent to the cooking style.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomi/teppan sauces and the izakaya's soy-seasoned dishes use standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes share a single teppan griddle used for meat, egg and seafood, and okonomiyaki batter/dashi contain egg and bonito, so vegan separation is not feasible here.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny local evening teppan izakaya where ordering is in Japanese and the vibe is neighborhood-regular; a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese or pointing at the griddle menu will get by, but little to no English is expected.

places.name: 辛めん屋 茂すけ (Karamenya Mosuke)
places.gf_detail: This is a noodle shop built around wheat. The tsukemen and ramen noodles are wheat, and the spicy dipping broths are not gluten-free. There are no gluten-free options and no indication the kitchen accommodates celiac needs.
places.vegan_detail: A spicy tsukemen and dan dan noodle counter. The broths and tare are meat- and fish-based (typical of Hiroshima tsukemen), the noodles are wheat, and toppings are not plant-based. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-15:00; closed Sundays and national holidays.
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku
places.cuisine: Hiroshima-style tsukemen, soupless dan dan n
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact, stylish noodle bar that opened in February 2023 in the Hatchobori district of central Hiroshima. The owner reportedly spent around four years developing the shop's signature spicy sauce before opening, part of a wave of newer Hiroshima tsukemen makers inspire

chef_bio.background: A compact, stylish noodle bar that opened in February 2023 in the Hatchobori district of central Hiroshima. The owner reportedly spent around four years developing the shop's signature spicy sauce before opening, part of a wave of newer Hiroshima tsukemen makers inspired by the city's long-standing dipping-noodle tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: Pairs a deliberately modern, cafe-like room — wood and black tones, careful lighting, a clean counter — with a from-scratch focus on its spicy sauce, and offers a small touch of hospitality with complimentary lemon water.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style spicy tsukemen built on a soy-based dipping sauce made with Seto Inland Sea fish stock, plus a spicy ramen using the same fish-and-soy broth, with heat adjustable from mild to very hot around a 'secret' spicy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop's spicy sauce was reportedly refined over roughly four years before opening, and customers order via a ticket machine — unusual for a tsukemen shop — while being served free lemon water.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: h-megourmet.com and TJ Hiroshima describe a Feb-2023 Hatchobori spicy-noodle bar serving Hiroshima tsukemen (soy/Seto-Inland-Sea-fish dipping sauce) and ramen on the same broth, with an original spice blend, ticket-machine ordering, free lemon water and a wood-and-black cafe interior; the shop is presented as a new-wave Hiroshima tsukemen maker. No source lists soupless dan dan noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the core of the menu, so flour and gluten are present throughout the kitchen; this is not a safe environment for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Spicy tare and dipping broths are meat/fish based and prepared on shared equipment with the noodles, so no dish can be assumed plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A small lunchtime noodle counter that orders via a ticket vending machine and runs in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at the spice-level numbers works fine.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin, firm straight noodles, served chilled (ice-water rinsed) in the Hiroshima tsukemen manner; noodle reviewers note a pleasant firm bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (Hiroshima tsukemen): chilled firm noodles plated with boiled cabbage and cucumber, dipped in a thin, fiery soy-based tsukedare laced with chili and sesame, with the heat dialable from 0 up past level 4.
ramen.chashu: Thin slices of braised pork with a loose, tender texture, served as a cold-noodle topping rather than a thick centerpiece cut.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the spicy dipping sauce is built on a soy (wheat) shoyu base, so the core dishes are not celiac-safe; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The tsukedare and dishes rely on animal/seafood-derived dashi, soy tare and pork chashu; no documented plant-only broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: This is Hiroshima's own tsukemen creed in a bowl: ice-chilled, firm noodles dragged through a thin, soy-bright dipping sauce that burns with chili and rings with sesame, the heat climbing level by level on your command. Drawing on the lineage of the city's pioneering spicy-cold-noodle houses, it trades the heavy emulsified broths of other regions for a lean, electric tare where the soul is chili and umami rather than fat.

places.highlight: This is Hiroshima's own tsukemen creed in a bowl: ice-chilled, firm noodles dragged through a thin, soy-bright dipping sauce that burns with chili and rings with sesame, the heat climbing level by level on your command. Drawing on the lineage of the city's pioneering spicy-cold-noodle houses, it trades the heavy emulsified broths of other regions for a lean, electric tare where the soul is chili and umami rather than fat.

places.name: プチ平家 (Puchi Heike)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature anago (conger eel) rice is glazed with a wheat-based soy sauce tare, udon and tempura are wheat, curry uses a wheat roux, and fried items are breaded — there are no documented gluten-free dishes or precautions.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on conger eel, oysters, white fish, and other seafood, plus pork cutlet and fried chicken; noodle and rice dishes rely on fish/wheat-soy-based broths and sauces. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Business hours irregular; regular closing day not publicly published
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Minami-onishimachi — near Its
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Puchi Heike is a long-standing, casual food stop at the foot of the Jukeiso (聚景荘) ryokan on Miyajima, set just beside the stone stairway up to the inn and diagonally across from the Miyajima History and Folk Museum. It is run by the same family operation behind the ryok (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Puchi Heike is a long-standing, casual food stop at the foot of the Jukeiso (聚景荘) ryokan on Miyajima, set just beside the stone stairway up to the inn and diagonally across from the Miyajima History and Folk Museum. It is run by the same family operation behind the ryokan, giving it the feel of an old-fashioned island rest house rather than a polished tourist restaurant.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago-meshi (conger eel rice) set meals and grilled local oysters, alongside homey island fare like udon, soba, curry, and assorted fried dishes.

anecdotes.text: It is described as a 昔ながらの御食事処 (an old-fashioned, traditional eatery) sitting at the base of the Jukeiso ryokan, serving the anago-meshi set meal as its signature dish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: miyajima-jukeiso.com describes プチ平家 as a 昔ながらの御食事処 operated by the Jukeiso (聚景荘) ryokan, at the base of the inn beside its stone stairway near the Miyajima History and Folk Museum, with an anago-meshi set as its signature dish plus grilled oysters, curry, udon, soba and fried items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago rice is finished with a soy-sauce-based tare, which in standard Japanese preparation contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, old-fashioned local eatery where some Japanese will help; menus are mostly in Japanese, so a translation app or pointing at dishes is useful for a foreign student.

places.name: GEBURA（ゲブラ）本店 (GEBURA (Honten))
places.gf_detail: This is a drinks-only bar built around fresh Hiroshima lemon syrup, sodas, lemonades, cocktails and highballs, so the core menu has no flour-based or obviously gluten-containing food. However, beer is on the menu (Hiroshima Lemon Beer) and contains gluten, and the homemade syrups/sodas have not been documented as tested or labeled gluten-free. No published gluten-free claim exists, so a celiac or sensitive traveler should ask staff which specific drinks (e.g. lemon soda made only from lemon, sugar and water) are safe and avoid the beer.
places.vegan_detail: Several drinks appear plant-based: the signature Hiroshima Lemon Frozen, Hiroshima Lemon Soda, homemade ginger ale, and the lemon syrup are made with lemon (peel and juice) and sugar via an anhydrous method, with no documented animal ingredients. Many cocktails (lemon highballs, gin/whiskey lemon drinks, mojito) are also plausibly vegan, but the menu is not labeled vegan and some recipes could include honey, dairy or animal-derived additives. There is no dedicated vegan declaration, so confirm individual drinks with staff. Note the Haribo gummy soda likely contains gelatin (not vegan).
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (per Tabelog listing for the head store)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), center of the Omotesando shop
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] GEBURA opened on February 17, 2020 in the center of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, in a historic wooden townhouse (古民家) renovated by a local Miyajima-based architect. The design blends contemporary minimalist concrete with the traditional building, with a long c (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Hiroshima Prefecture; it is the original head store of a small family of Miyajima drink stands that has since grown to several locations on the island.
chef_bio.background: GEBURA opened on February 17, 2020 in the center of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, in a historic wooden townhouse (古民家) renovated by a local Miyajima-based architect. The design blends contemporary minimalist concrete with the traditional building, with a long counter inside and additional counter and table seating on a terrace looking out over the shopping street.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is 'Hiroshima Lemon x Miyajima.' Lemons are sourced from contract farms in Setoda in the Seto Inland Sea and the syrup is made by an anhydrous (無水) method using the whole fruit, peel and juice together, to capture full lemon flavor while minimizing bitterness.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima lemon craft drinks: a signature frozen Hiroshima lemonade, lemon sodas, homemade ginger ale, and lemon-forward cocktails and highballs, all built on a homemade lemon syrup.

anecdotes.text: A reusable GEBURA cup comes with your first drink, and every refill afterward is just 200 yen at any GEBURA location on the island, encouraging visitors to keep sipping as they explore Miyajima.
The shop occupies a historic wooden house renovated by a resident architect of Miyajima, with a simple concrete-based interior and terrace counter facing the Omotesando shopping street.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: gebura.jp confirms the 'Hiroshima Lemon × Miyajima' concept, Setoda lemons, the anhydrous whole-fruit syrup method, frozen lemonade/sodas/cocktails, the reusable-cup ¥200-refill program across island locations, and GEBURA as head store of several Miyajima shops. rurubu.jp confirms the Feb-17-2020 opening in a 古民家 renovated by a local architect on the Omotesando street.

positives.text: Core drinks are made from a homemade syrup of just Hiroshima lemons (peel and juice) and sugar via an anhydrous method, so the signature lemonades and sodas are simple and plant-based by ingredient.
Lemons are sourced from pesticide-free / contract farms in Setoda, Hiroshima, with the whole fruit used in the syrup.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up drink counter on the main shopping street where you order and prepay at the front, so a foreign student can manage easily with a few Japanese words and pointing, though menus and staff are primarily Japanese.

places.name: ニニン (ninin)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→high. Real open plant-based + GF cafe, self-branded プラントベース・グルテンフリー (own claim OK). But 'dedicated' needs celiac-grade corroboration and NONE exists: absent from endlessdistances & FMGF; all sources are JP lifestyle/vegan bloggers, none celiac-grade, none addressing CC. Kept at high: multiple sources agree whole menu is GF/plant-based (coffee + vegan bakes, no wheat flour), no glutening reports. — Prior note: A dedicated gluten-free kitchen: all of the housemade sweets and food are made entirely without wheat flour. The owner explicitly checks that plant milks and other ingredients contain no wheat, and a documented anecdote describes a parent telling their allergic child that they could choose anything in the shop. No formal cross-contamination protocol is published, so travelers with celiac disease should confirm preparation details on arrival, but the entire menu is built around being wheat-free.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. All sweets and food are made with no eggs, milk, butter, or other animal products — plant milks (oat, soy) and nuts replace dairy, and items like the cheese-free cheesecake, carrot cake, ice cream, and cashew-onion quiche are all vegan. They also avoid white sugar, artificial coloring, and preservatives.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists 08:00-18:00; some write-ups cite 08:00-17:00 or 10:00-18:00 and closed days have been described variously (Tue/Wed, or Thursday with irregular closures). Hours and days vary — confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Hakushima-kitamachi, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (right by JR Shin-Hakushima Stn)
places.cuisine: Plant-based / gluten-free coffee stand and cafe
places.notes: ninin (the name plays on "two people") is a coffee stand opened in March 2023 by a husband-and-wife pair who relocated to Hiroshima. During the pandemic they reconsidered their work and lifestyle, reviewed their own diet for health reasons, and decided to build a busine

roles: husband roasts and brews the coffee
wife develops and bakes all the sweets and food
chef_bio.background: ninin (the name plays on "two people") is a coffee stand opened in March 2023 by a husband-and-wife pair who relocated to Hiroshima. During the pandemic they reconsidered their work and lifestyle, reviewed their own diet for health reasons, and decided to build a business together — the wife's love of coffee first drew the husband in, and he became just as devoted. Before settling into their permanent shop near Shin-Hakushima, they ran pop-ups and temporary spaces in the Kanto region.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand with no eggs, dairy, butter, wheat, white sugar, artificial coloring, or preservatives. Their thinking is rooted in health, animal welfare, and the environment — the husband has spoken about how livestock emissions threaten the future of Ethiopian coffee growing, tying his bean sourcing to sustainability.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-net roasted (手網焙煎) Ethiopian coffee, roasted in tiny batches over direct flame so the profile can be adjusted in real time — sometimes in front of guests — paired with the wife's housemade plant-based, gluten-free sweets.

anecdotes.text: The husband has said his happiest moment was overhearing a mother tell her allergic child, "ここにある物は全部選んでいいんだよ" — you can choose anything here.
The couple build their own furniture and tables, giving the small shop a handmade, personal feel, and welcome pets on the terrace.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: modokimaru.com confirms the March-2023 husband-and-wife plant-based/gluten-free coffee stand near Shin-Hakushima, hand-net-roasted Ethiopian coffee, the no-eggs/dairy/wheat/white-sugar/additives approach, the pandemic-era lifestyle and diet rethink, the husband's concern that livestock emissions threaten Ethiopian coffee growing, and the anecdote of a mother telling her allergic child everything there was safe to choose. Allergen safety is not asserted.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is wheat-free, so there are no wheat-flour products in the kitchen, but no formal celiac cross-contamination protocol has been published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All sweets and food are fully plant-based with no animal products on the menu, eliminating dairy/egg cross-contact at the recipe level.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner explicitly verifies that plant milks and ingredients contain no wheat, choosing only wheat-free products; a documented anecdote describes a parent being told their allergic child could safely choose anything in the shop.

positives.text: A dedicated plant-based and gluten-free cafe — every item is made without eggs, dairy, or wheat, so vegan and gluten-free guests can order anything on the menu.
Sweets are also made without white sugar, artificial coloring, or preservatives.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, husband-and-wife coffee stand where a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; the all-plant-based, wheat-free menu makes ordering easy for vegan and gluten-free students.

gf_review.why: Real open plant-based + GF cafe, self-branded プラントベース・グルテンフリー (own claim OK). But 'dedicated' needs celiac-grade corroboration and NONE exists: absent from endlessdistances & FMGF; all sources are JP lifestyle/vegan bloggers, none celiac-grade, none addressing CC. Kept at high: multiple sources agree whole menu is GF/plant-based (coffee + vegan bakes, no wheat flour), no glutening reports.
red_flags: No celiac-grade source; not on FMGF/endlessdistances
Oat milk on offer = gluten CC risk unless certified GF
gf_review.plain_rice: N/A — coffee + vegan sweets, no rice/meal. Safest = naturally-GF vegan bakes; confirm oat-milk sourcing in person.

places.name: 尾道ラーメン 暁 (Onomichi Ramen Akatsuki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish centers on flat wheat noodles (sourced from an Onomichi noodle maker) and a soy-sauce-based broth, both of which contain wheat. No gluten-free options are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth is built on pork and chicken stock with rendered pork back-fat and a soy-sauce base; noodles are wheat. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closes earlier when sold out
places.neighborhood: Teppocho, Naka-ku — a 3-minute walk from Hatchobori, central
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An Onomichi-style ramen shop that opened in February 2015 in Teppocho, central Hiroshima, letting locals enjoy authentic Onomichi ramen without the long trip to its coastal hometown. It later grew popular enough that a second location opened in Naka-ku's Komachi distric

chef_bio.background: An Onomichi-style ramen shop that opened on 12 February 2015 in Teppocho, central Hiroshima (a 2-3 minute walk from Hatchobori streetcar stop), letting locals enjoy authentic Onomichi ramen without the trip to its coastal hometown. It later grew popular enough that a second location opened in Naka-ku's Komachi district in April 2018.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honoring traditional, old-fashioned Onomichi ramen while drawing out a refined depth of flavor — nostalgic in appearance, sophisticated on the palate.
chef_bio.specialty: Onomichi ramen: a deeply dark-looking but surprisingly light and complex shoyu broth of pork and chicken stock, finished with sweet rendered pork back-fat (seabura) and a scorched-soy-sauce aroma, served over medium-thin flat wheat noodles from Onomichi's Inoue noodle factory.

anecdotes.text: Despite its almost intimidatingly dark broth, the soup tastes light yet intricate — a reviewer urged diners to see past the humble, old-school appearance to find a profound flavor.
The shop is run essentially as a one-person operation, with the owner handling both the cooking and the front of house during the dinner rush.
Lunchtime prices are gentle (standard ramen around 750 yen), and even on weekdays the small shop can draw a queue.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Onetabi review confirms the Teppocho/Hatchobori location, solo operation, ~750 yen pricing, Inoue-seimen flat noodles, and scorched-soy pork+chicken broth with sweet back-fat. Tabelog gives the exact opening date (2015-02-12) and address; the Komachi 2nd branch (Tabelog/ramendb) opened April 2018.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen works with wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy sauce broth throughout, so any non-wheat order would face heavy cross-contamination; no allergen separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base with a scorched-soy-sauce aroma; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from pork and chicken stock with rendered pork back-fat, so all dishes share animal-based stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, no-frills local ramen counter where a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will get you by; little to no English, and cash is essential.

noodles.notes: Thin, flat-cut straight noodles sourced from an Onomichi noodle maker (Inoue Seimen), the classic Onomichi shape. Despite being slender they grip and lift the soy-rich broth well, giving strong soup-and-noodle cohesion with a firm bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (Onomichi ramen) - the dark roasted-soy chicken-and-pork broth crowned with chunks of seabura back-fat, thin flat noodles, chashu, menma and green onion.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu (loin-style), simmered tender; not aburi/char-grilled.
notable_toppings: seabura (chunks of pork back-fat floating on the surface)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners. No rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a seafood (gyokai) accent, finished with rendered pork back-fat and pork chashu. No plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful keeper of the Onomichi flame in central Hiroshima: its soul is the dark, roasted-soy chicken-and-pork soup studded with sweet chunks of melting back-fat that seal in heat and richness, a signature of the Seto Inland Sea fishing-town style. You taste that lineage the moment the slender, flat Onomichi noodles carry the deep shoyu broth to your lips.

places.highlight: A faithful keeper of the Onomichi flame in central Hiroshima: its soul is the dark, roasted-soy chicken-and-pork soup studded with sweet chunks of melting back-fat that seal in heat and richness, a signature of the Seto Inland Sea fishing-town style. You taste that lineage the moment the slender, flat Onomichi noodles carry the deep shoyu broth to your lips.

places.name: 広島風お好み焼 大福 (Hiroshima-fu Okonomiyaki Daifuku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udan), and it is finished with Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce, which contains wheat. Gyoza wrappers are also wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free options.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The okonomiyaki standardly includes egg, pork, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, the shop is known for hormone-yaki (offal), and the sauce contains animal-derived ingredients. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00 - 22:00; closed Sun (Tabelog). Other local sources list lunch 11:00–14:30 and dinner 17:30–23:00, closed Sun and holidays; hours may vary.
places.neighborhood: Osugacho, Minami-ku (Ekinishi / west side of Hiroshima Stati
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, traditional okonomiyaki shop tucked into the Ekinishi district just west of Hiroshima Station, run as a family operation. Regulars describe a cheerful grandfather and a kind grandchild working the teppan together, drawing a steady crowd of locals to the counter

chef_bio.background: A small, traditional okonomiyaki shop in the Osugacho area on the west side of Hiroshima Station (the after-work Ekinishi quarter), run as a family operation. Regulars describe a cheerful grandfather and a kind grandchild working the teppan together, drawing a steady crowd of locals to the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-school Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked press-style: an iron weight is set on the layered pancake and pressed down to flatten and crisp it before the egg is added and the whole thing is assembled. Hormone-yaki (grilled offal) is a standout. A basic soba-meat-egg okonomiyaki runs about 500 yen.

anecdotes.text: The okonomiyaki is finished using a heavy iron weight pressed down by hand to flatten and crisp the pancake before the egg is layered in, giving the old-school crispy texture; a basic soba-meat-egg version is about 500 yen, described as clearly cheap, with a thin batter, plenty of garlic, and well-seasoned sauce on the noodles.

Reviewers describe a cheerful grandfather and a kind grandchild running the shop together, with hormone-yaki (offal) singled out as superb, and a warm, homey atmosphere where local regulars gather and you sit right by the hot teppan.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Ameblo review confirms the Minami-ku Osugacho address, press/iron-weight cooking, generous garlic, well-seasoned sauce, and a 500-yen soba-meat-egg. Retty review confirms the grandfather-and-grandchild family operation, praised hormone-yaki, and warm counter atmosphere near Hiroshima Station.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki is finished with Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; combined with the wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, the dishes are not suitable for gluten-free diners.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, old-school family-run counter shop where local regulars gather, so little to no English is likely; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the okonomiyaki will go a long way, and the warm grandfather-and-grandchild team is welcoming.

places.name: カフェ クラシック (Cafe Classic)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings or accommodations are documented. The signature hotcakes and sandwiches are wheat-flour based, so this is not a gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. The cafe's signature items center on eggs (locally sourced free-range eggs in hotcakes, egg-rich homemade pudding, egg sandwiches), so it is not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 10:00-18:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00-18:00; closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A mother-and-daughter team, Midori and her daughter Yu, opened Cafe Classic in December 2017 along the Hondori shopping arcade in central Hiroshima, fulfilling their shared dream of running their own shop.
