chef_bio.background: A mother-and-daughter team, Midori and her daughter Yu, opened Cafe Classic in December 2017 along the Hondori shopping arcade in central Hiroshima (Seikado Building 1F, ~4 min from the Hondori tram stop), fulfilling their shared dream of running their own shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: They chose the simple, straightforward name 'Classic' to reflect an unpretentious, timeless ideal, and designed a calm space (counter of Tochigi wood, chestnut tables and chairs, a zelkova door, seasonal wildflowers from Midori's own garden) where solo guests and friends alike can spend unhurried time feeling at ease.
chef_bio.specialty: French-press coffee from a small specialty roaster in Futaba (Higashi-ku), homemade old-fashioned pudding with a firm texture and slightly bitter caramel, water-drip iced coffee served in a wine glass, and fluffy hotcakes made with local free-range eggs.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's interior features a Tochigi-wood counter, chestnut-wood tables and chairs and a zelkova door, accented with seasonal wildflowers cut from Midori's own garden.
Iced coffee is made by the cold water-drip method and served in a wine glass so it is not diluted by ice, keeping a balanced bitterness and clean finish.
The hotcakes (750 yen) are made with free-range eggs from Hiroshima-area farms, and the coffee beans come from a specialty roaster in Futaba, Higashi-ku.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Pecomag confirms mother Midori and daughter Yu, the 2017 opening on Hondori (Seikado Building), the natural-wood interior (tochigi counter, chestnut tables, zelkova door) with wildflowers from Midori's garden, the Futaba specialty roaster ('Smile'), and 750-yen hotcakes with Hiroshima free-range eggs. Tabelog confirms the water-drip iced coffee served in a wine glass.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 17-seat family-run coffee shop where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, unhurried attitude will make a foreign student feel welcome, though English support is not advertised.

places.name: アルヒ モリノナカ (aruhi_morinonaka)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a general cafe and dining bar serving courses, lunch sets, and sweets developed in collaboration with local Hiroshima restaurants; menus include wheat-based dishes and there is no stated gluten-free preparation or allergy accommodation. Gluten-free travelers should not rely on this venue.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The cafe is known for fruit-topped Greek yogurt and a range of cafe and dining-bar dishes; some fruit/yogurt or salad items may suit vegetarians, but most savory dishes use animal products and no plant-based menu is advertised. A vegan diner would need to ask in advance about specific items.
places.hours_raw: Sun–Thu 11:00–22:00; Fri and Sat 11:00–24:00 (lunch 11:00-14:00, cafe 11:00-22:00, dinner 17:30-22:00; Fri–Sat until 24:00). Irregular days off (some sources say closed Thu).
places.neighborhood: 中区本通（八丁堀・立町エリア / central Hiroshima, Hondori arcade)
places.notes: Aruhi Morinonaka opened in April 2024 on the 4th floor along Hiroshima's central Hondori arcade, a short walk from the PARCO department store via the Kinza shopping street. It bills itself as the first cafe and dining bar in Hiroshima to use projection mapping, projecti

chef_bio.background: Aruhi Morinonaka opened in 2024 on the 4th floor along Hiroshima's central Hondori arcade (1-5 Hondori), a short walk from the PARCO department store via the Kinza shopping street. Its official site bills it as Hiroshima's first cafe and dining bar to use projection mapping, projecting forest-themed imagery across the interior to create an immersive, storybook-like 'forest in the middle' atmosphere for everything from lunch to late-night drinks.
chef_bio.philosophy: To offer an extraordinary, non-everyday space where food, drink, and immersive forest visuals come together for relaxation and celebration.
chef_bio.specialty: Fruit-topped Greek yogurt and photogenic cafe and dining-bar fare, served in a projection-mapped forest setting; lunch sets, cafe sweets, and dinner courses.

anecdotes.text: It is promoted as Hiroshima's first cafe and dining bar to stage its space with projection mapping, turning the dining room into an ever-changing forest scene.

The interior floral decoration is supervised by a florist who is the younger brother of TV personality Hiroyuki Ariyoshi, and dishes are developed in collaboration with established local Hiroshima eateries such as the kappo restaurant Nagayama.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site states it is 'Hiroshima's first cafe & dining bar using projection mapping,' with a forest concept, 4F Hondori location ~90m from PARCO, and popular fruit Greek yogurt; pecomag frames the projection mapping as a first for the Chugoku-Shikoku region. Gorp confirms the florist is Hiroyuki Ariyoshi's younger brother and the Kappo Nagayama menu collaboration. Exact month of the 2024 opening not confirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: A modern, spacious cafe and dining bar that welcomes casual visitors and groups, but staff communication is in Japanese and no English menu is documented, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 47（キャラントセット） (47 (Quarante Sept))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The signature items are chiffon cake, pasta, gratin, and bread, all wheat-based, so a celiac visitor would find very little safe here. Ask staff directly if you need to avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offerings are documented. The menu centers on cakes (eggs/dairy), pasta, gratin, and meat/fish plates, so vegan options are not assured; salads exist but dressings and sides are not confirmed vegan. Ask staff for plant-based choices.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu & Sun 11:30-22:00; Fri, Sat & day before holidays 11:30-00:00
places.neighborhood: 中区本通 / 八丁堀・本通 (Hondori, central Hiroshima, opposite Hiroshim
places.cuisine: Cafe / dining bar with cake & dessert specia
places.notes: 47 is a cafe and dining bar in the heart of central Hiroshima, set on the second floor of a building along the Hondori arcade directly opposite Hiroshima PARCO. The space is themed as a romantic hideaway, designed around the image of a secret refuge belonging to Marie A

chef_bio.origin: It operates under the Diamond Dining group (DD Holdings), a company known for distinctively themed cafes and dining venues, and this branch brings that concept-cafe styling to Hiroshima's main shopping street.
chef_bio.background: 47 is a cafe and dining bar in the heart of central Hiroshima, set on the second floor of a building along the Hondori arcade opposite Hiroshima PARCO. The space is themed as a romantic hideaway, designed around the image of a secret refuge belonging to Marie Antoinette, with a calm, softly decorated interior meant to feel like a sanctuary in the middle of the busy shopping district.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe describes its food as gentle, simple, body-kind cooking, aiming to give guests a relaxing pause from the bustle of the city center.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is a chiffon cake called BeBe Chiffon (べべシフォン), made by the in-house pastry chef, served alongside parfaits and other desserts. Lunch is offered buffet-style: guests pick a main such as the weekly pasta, a gratin, or a special plate, then help themselves to bread, salad with a champagne-vinegar and yuzu dressing, soup, and drinks.

anecdotes.text: The interior concept is built around the idea of Marie Antoinette's secret hideaway, framing the cafe as a quiet escape tucked above the busy Hondori arcade.
Its location across from Hiroshima PARCO makes it a popular choice for relaxed lunch gatherings in the city center.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The DD Holdings shop page confirms the Diamond Dining operation, the Marie Antoinette secret-hideaway concept, the in-house pastry chef's BeBe Chiffon, the champagne-vinegar/yuzu-dressing lunch buffet, and 'body-kind, comforting simple cuisine.' Hot Pepper places it on 2F of a Hondori building near PARCO.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, central arcade cafe used to casual walk-in customers; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu or display will get you through comfortably as a foreign student.

places.name: 焼念場 (Shonenba)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu centers on Hiroshima okonomiyaki (wheat-flour batter and noodles), teppanyaki and yakitori glazed/seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and miso, and a signature offal-and-short-rib dish in a special miso sauce — all contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around meat and seafood — butter oysters, wagyu koune (cow neck), beef offal/hormone, yakitori chicken, and okonomiyaki made with egg, pork and seafood. No documented vegan dishes; even side items would share the teppan with animal products.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-00:00 (Tabelog); official site lists 18:00-00:00. Open daily, irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: 広島駅西（エキニシ）/ Ekinishi, west side of Hiroshima Station, Minami
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shonenba is a small, independently run teppanyaki izakaya tucked into the Ekinishi alleys on the west side of Hiroshima Station. The owner spent years honing teppanyaki cooking and runs the place hands-on, grilling each order on the iron plate right in front of guests a

chef_bio.origin: Ekinishi (駅西), the dense after-work drinking quarter immediately west of Hiroshima Station in Minami-ku.
chef_bio.background: Shonenba is a small, independently run teppanyaki izakaya tucked into the Ekinishi alleys about four minutes' walk west of Hiroshima Station's south exit. The owner honed teppanyaki cooking over the years and runs the place hands-on, grilling each order on the iron plate right in front of guests at the counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Dishes are prepared one by one with care on the teppan in front of the customer.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is 'Shonenba-yaki' — domestic beef offal and short rib grilled in a secret spicy miso sauce. Other counter favorites are butter-grilled oysters, salt-grilled wagyu koune (cow neck, a Hiroshima specialty), Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, tonpei-yaki, and yakitori made with carefully chosen domestic chicken.

anecdotes.text: The two-story shop keeps a casual counter downstairs for solo diners, dates and a quiet drink, while the upstairs tables can be rearranged and reserved privately for groups of about 12 to 18.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official Shonenba site confirms the Ekinishi location (~4 min from Hiroshima Station south exit), the signature Shonenba-yaki (domestic beef offal and short rib in a secret spicy miso sauce), butter-grilled oysters, salt-grilled wagyu koune, okonomiyaki, tonpei-yaki, and piece-by-piece domestic-chicken yakitori, plus a downstairs counter and reservable upstairs tables for groups of 12-18.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan (iron griddle) used for wheat-batter okonomiyaki, soy/miso-sauced meats and seafood, so high cross-contact risk for gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki, yakitori and the signature miso-sauce dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking is done on a shared griddle used for oysters, beef offal, wagyu and chicken; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local counter izakaya where the owner grills in front of you; a little Japanese or a translation app helps, and the ground-floor counter is the easiest seat for a solo foreign student.

places.name: カフェ ハヤシヤ (CAFE HAYASHIYA)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings are documented. The café's signature items — granola-based parfaits, roll cake, and the like — contain wheat, and no gluten-free menu or accommodation is mentioned on any source. There is no information indicating dedicated gluten-free preparation. Travelers needing strict gluten-free should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Many items are dairy-based (milk-syrup kakigōri, soft serve, gelato, milk-based shaved ice) and lunch features oysters, so most of the menu is not vegan. Some fruit-syrup shaved ice could potentially be plant-based, but this is not confirmed; a vegan diner would need to ask in person about specific items and toppings.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Machiya-dōri — one street bac
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] CAFE HAYASHIYA sits on the quiet Machiya-dōri, one street back from Miyajima's bustling Omotesandō, reached through a narrow alley beside its sister shop. It is the café arm of a grilled-oyster house with more than sixty years of history on the island, extending that fa (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: CAFE HAYASHIYA sits on the quiet Machiya-dōri, one street back from Miyajima's bustling Omotesandō (about 8 minutes' walk from Miyajima Port). It is the café arm of Yakigaki no Hayashi, a grilled-oyster house with more than sixty years of history on the island, extending that family name into homemade sweets and drinks within a townhouse space that blends traditional and modern design.
chef_bio.philosophy: A devotion to local Hiroshima and Setouchi ingredients: strawberries bought directly from growers such as a farm in Shobara, whole Setoda lemon peels in the syrups, sake-lees syrups from nearby breweries, and an optional pure-ice upgrade made from Miyajima's natural water. The aim is to let visitors fully taste the charm of Hiroshima and Miyajima in a calm space that conveys island culture.
chef_bio.specialty: Homemade granola parfaits and seasonal kakigōri (shaved ice) — including Setouchi lemon-milk and local strawberry-milk versions — using about a dozen house-made syrups.

anecdotes.text: The strawberries are sourced directly from farmers, with seasonal varieties from Hibagon Farm in Shobara City, and the lemon syrup is made using whole Setoda lemon peels.
An optional upgrade (about 200 yen) uses 'Miyajima pure ice' made from 100% natural Miyajima water, said to contain beauty-friendly silica.
The café carries on the flavors of its sister grilled-oyster restaurant, which has over 60 years of history on the island.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site confirms the Machiya-dori location one street behind Omotesando, the sister grilled-oyster restaurant Yakigaki no Hayashi (60+ years), granola parfaits as the top item, and ~a dozen house-made kakigori syrups using Setouchi lemon and regional sake lees. Home-tv confirms strawberries direct from Hibagon Farm in Shobara, whole-Setoda-lemon-peel syrup, and the optional 100%-Miyajima-water 'pure ice' with silica.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed tourist-area café where a friendly greeting and pointing at the picture menu will get you far, though staff may not speak much English so a few basic Japanese phrases help.

places.name: 麺屋台 我馬 紙屋町店 (Menyatai Gaba Kamiyacho)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop with pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth; the noodles are wheat and there is no gluten-free menu or documented gluten-free preparation, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The core product is tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen with pork-based toppings and broth; no vegan or vegetarian options are documented, so this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-00:00 (L.O. 23:30), closed Tuesdays; open on holidays
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho, Naka-ku
places.notes: Ramen Gaba was born in March 2000 with its first shop in the Misasa district of Hiroshima, and has since grown into a fixture of the city's ramen scene with locations across Hiroshima and beyond. The Kamiyacho branch sits in the heart of central Hiroshima, a short walk

chef_bio.background: Ramen Gaba was born in March 2000 with its first shop in the Misasa district of Hiroshima, and has since grown into a fixture of the city's ramen scene with locations across Hiroshima and beyond. The Kamiyacho branch sits in the heart of central Hiroshima, a short walk east of the Peace Memorial Park, and serves the same tonkotsu that built the brand's name.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder, originally from Fukuoka, trained at an Italian restaurant and at a renowned Fukuoka ramen shop before opening Gaba. The shop's stated ideal is that a great bowl is about more than flavor and aroma alone — the space, the timing, and the service all come together to complete the experience, with the goal of making people smile through ramen.
chef_bio.specialty: Rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) ramen, with the two signature bowls being 'Shiro-uma' (white, mild) and 'Aka-uma' (red, spicy); diners can also choose noodle shape and broth combinations, and gyoza sets are popular.

anecdotes.text: The original Misasa shop was an immediate hit, reportedly taking in roughly 10 million yen in sales within its first 20 days of opening.
The founder, from Fukuoka, trained in both Italian cooking and at a famous Fukuoka ramen shop before launching Gaba in March 2000.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Earth-food's corporate history confirms the Misasa main shop opened March 2000. The Go!Hiroshima company database and Jounetsu-K broadcast archive corroborate that the Fukuoka-born founder trained at an Italian restaurant and a famous Fukuoka ramen shop, and that the first Misasa shop earned ~10 million yen in its first 20 days. The official site confirms the Shiro-uma / Aka-uma tonkotsu bowls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen is served with wheat noodles in a shared kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and the shop centers on meat ramen; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, casual chain ramen counter open until midnight; you order at the counter and a few basic Japanese phrases plus pointing at the picture menu will get you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: Ultra-thin straight low-hydration Hakata-style noodles built for a quick slurp and the kaedama (noodle-refill) ritual; order them barikata (extra firm) if you like snap.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiro-uma (white) tonkotsu ramen — the refined house original; adventurous eaters jump to the Aka-uma (red), spiked with spicy miso and burnt black oil. Add a kaedama noodle refill to finish.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, sliced and laid over the bowl in the Hakata manner.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui Hakata noodles plus a shoyu/miso-based tonkotsu seasoning mean gluten is unavoidable; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is long-simmered pork-bone broth finished with pork lard and pork chashu; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: Born in Hiroshima in 2000, this house pours a Hakata-lineage tonkotsu — a long-boiled, milky pork-bone soup drawn over hair-thin straight noodles, complete with the Kyushu kaedama refill ritual. The signature Shiro-uma keeps the broth clean and refined, while the fiery Aka-uma layers in spicy miso and scorched black oil for a darker, deeper roar.

places.highlight: Born in Hiroshima in 2000, this house pours a Hakata-lineage tonkotsu — a long-boiled, milky pork-bone soup drawn over hair-thin straight noodles, complete with the Kyushu kaedama refill ritual. The signature Shiro-uma keeps the broth clean and refined, while the fiery Aka-uma layers in spicy miso and scorched black oil for a darker, deeper roar.

places.name: 宮島かき氷処 幸氷 (Miyajima Kakigori-dokoro Sachigori)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Kakigori syrups, condensed-milk and flavored toppings, and any wafer/biscuit garnishes are not certified gluten-free, and there is no indication of allergen control or dedicated equipment. No GF information is published.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The documented specialties are milk-based, including Setouchi lemon milk and chocolate kakigori topped with condensed milk and cream, so dairy is central. No vegan or plant-based options are advertised.
places.hours_raw: Business hours not clearly documented on the official Instagram or aggregator listings. One unverified search snippet stated 18:00-22:00 daily; treat as unconfirmed and check on arrival.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping arcade, near Itsukushima Shrine
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent shaved-ice shop that opened on the Miyajima Omotesando shopping arcade in April 2024, within easy walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small independent shaved-ice shop that opened on 23 April 2024 on the Miyajima Omotesando shopping arcade, within easy walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.specialty: Kakigori built around Setouchi (Seto Inland Sea) flavors, with a signature Setouchi lemon-milk kakigori and a chocolate version, often finished with a momiji (maple-leaf) motif that nods to Miyajima.

anecdotes.text: Customers buy a ticket from a vending machine set up outside before stepping into the small, nostalgically styled shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog gives the opening date (2024-04-23), the Miyajima Omotesando location near Itsukushima Shrine, the Setouchi lemon-milk (¥1,200) and chocolate kakigori with maple-leaf designs, the nostalgic interior, and the outdoor vending-machine ticketing system.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple ticket-vending-machine kakigori counter on the main shrine approach where you can point at a flavor and pay cash, so almost no Japanese is needed, but bring cash and don't expect English menus.

places.name: Jcafe 紙屋町店 (Jcafe Kamiyachō)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The core menu — hot sandwiches, pasta, breaded fried chicken, and baked curry — is built on wheat (bread, pasta, batter, roux), so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu documented. Dishes lean heavily on cheese, eggs, ham, and meat (e.g. ham-cheese-tomato hot sandwich, creamy carbonara, soft-boiled-egg-and-cheese baked curry). Salads or coffee may be the only plant-based options; a vegan should ask directly about ingredients.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–23:00, irregular closures
places.neighborhood: Kamiyachō / Hondori (Naka-ku)
places.notes: J CAFE is a revival of a beloved Hiroshima café from the 2000s, an era when stylish cafés were proliferating across the city. After years away, it reopened on January 6, 2026, in a spacious, light-filled spot just off Hondori shopping street in Kamiyachō. The room pairs

chef_bio.background: J CAFE is a revival of a beloved Hiroshima café from the 2000s, an era when stylish cafés were proliferating across the city. After years away, it reopened on 6 January 2026 in a spacious, light-filled spot just off Hondori shopping street in Kamiyachō (2-3-20 Kamiyacho, across from the Hondori Sun Mall). The room pairs a greenery-accented ceiling with an open, calming feel — counter seats for solo guests, sofa corners, big tables, and cushioned wall seating, with a rotating mirror ball as a playful nostalgic touch. It is stroller-friendly and welcomes everyone from lone visitors to families and groups, serving from lunch straight through to late evening.
chef_bio.philosophy: An all-purpose neighborhood café meant to be used freely throughout the day — for a relaxed lunch, an afternoon break, or an evening visit.
chef_bio.specialty: A 'hidden specialty' baked curry topped with a soft-boiled egg and cheese; also known for hearty hot sandwiches.

anecdotes.text: Locals describe the baked curry with soft-boiled egg and cheese as a 'hidden specialty' of the café — deep and spicy, enriched by the cheese and creamy egg.
The reopened space deliberately blends 2000s nostalgia with contemporary design, even down to a rotating mirror ball over the spacious interior.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The mrs.living newopen report confirms J CAFE as the revival of a 2000s-era Hiroshima café, reopened 6 January 2026 at 2-3-20 Kamiyacho across from the Hondori Sun Mall, with a greenery-accented ceiling, rotating mirror ball, counter/sofa/large-table/wall seating, stroller access, and the 'hidden specialty' baked curry with soft-boiled egg and cheese alongside hot sandwiches.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, spacious modern café where a foreign student can comfortably point at the menu or use a few Japanese phrases; an English menu is not confirmed, so basic greetings help.

places.name: おにぎり屋 三兎 (Onigiriya Santo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The onigiri are seasoned/filled with items such as braised anago (conger eel), tuna mayo, salmon, and koji- or soy-flavored fillings, which typically involve wheat-containing soy sauce; no dedicated GF preparation or allergen handling is published. Treat as not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-and-meat-forward onigiri shop (anago, oyster in olive oil, salmon/ikura oyako, tuna mayo, salmon tartare). Plain rice-based fillings like umeboshi (pickled plum) may be incidentally plant-based, but no vegan options are advertised and seasonings likely include non-vegan dashi/soy; a vegan diner would need to ask what is currently available.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (listings vary; one source notes Wednesday as the usual closed day, but hours and closed days may change — confirm before visiting).
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), foot of the five-story pagoda / Machiya-dori
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Santo began as a hand-made onigiri food truck based in the Higashi-Hiroshima (Saijo) area, where the owner sold carefully crafted rice balls from a mobile kitchen. In autumn 2024 the shop opened a permanent location on Miyajima island, at the foot of Itsukushima's five-

chef_bio.origin: Higashi-Hiroshima (mobile kitchen), later established on Miyajima (Itsukushima).
chef_bio.background: Santo began as a hand-made onigiri food truck based in the Higashi-Hiroshima (Saijo) area, where the owner sold carefully crafted rice balls from a mobile kitchen. In autumn 2024 (October) the shop opened a permanent location on Miyajima island, at the foot of Itsukushima's five-story pagoda along the old town-house street near the shrine office, taking over a historic building once known as the Yamamoto Torakichi Store.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on local Hiroshima rice and regional seafood, with nori served separately to preserve texture — an approachable, ingredient-forward take on the humble rice ball.
chef_bio.specialty: Generously filled hand-shaped onigiri made with Hiroshima-grown rice, with the nori handed over separately so it stays crisp until you wrap it yourself. Signature fillings showcase local ingredients: braised anago (conger eel), oyster marinated in olive oil, salmon-and-ikura oyako, smoked-takuan salmon tartare, plus classics like umeboshi and house tuna mayo.

anecdotes.text: The shop moved from a traveling food truck in the Saijo/Higashi-Hiroshima area to a fixed storefront on Miyajima, opening in autumn 2024 in the former Yamamoto Torakichi Store building at the base of the five-story pagoda.
Nori is given to customers separately so it can be wrapped just before eating, keeping it crisp; the shop also sells miso soup and even beer and sake to pair with the hearty rice balls.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabetainjya confirms Santo's food-truck origin in the Saijo/Higashi-Hiroshima area, its October 2024 permanent opening on Miyajima at the base of the five-story pagoda in the former Yamamoto Torakichi Store, use of Hiroshima rice, nori served separately, the premium fillings (braised anago, olive-oil oyster, salmon-ikura, smoked-takuan salmon tartare, etc., ~300 yen each), and the sale of miso soup, beer and sake.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Fillings include koji-seasoned and braised items (e.g., braised anago) and savory varieties that typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around fish and seafood (anago, oyster, salmon, ikura, tuna), so plant-based items would be prepared in the same fish-handling space.

positives.text: Onigiri are hand-made to order with the nori provided separately, so customers can see the simple components and add the seaweed themselves.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny independent grab-and-go onigiri counter where pointing at the display and a few words of Japanese go a long way; little to no English is documented, but ordering is simple and low-pressure.

places.name: ふじの茶屋 里の店 (Fuji no Chaya Sato no Mise)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes are anago (eel) over rice with a wheat-based sweet soy tare and udon (wheat noodles), and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or awareness.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on grilled conger eel (anago) with a fish/soy-based sauce, and udon sets typically use dashi; no documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Hours not formally published; the shop is reported to open irregularly, mainly during busy seasons (New Year, Golden Week, Obon, and the autumn foliage season). Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine / Omotesando
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, old-fashioned tea house tucked into Miyajima's shrine district, about two minutes on foot from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine and right beside the local Folk History Museum. Guests eat inside a traditional wooden house with tatami rooms and a little terrace, a  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small, old-fashioned tea house tucked into Miyajima's shrine district, about two minutes on foot from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine and right beside the local Folk History Museum. Guests eat inside a traditional wooden house with tatami rooms and a small terrace, a quiet spot away from the busier main street.
chef_bio.specialty: A mini anago (conger eel) rice bowl brushed generously with a sweet tare, served as a set with kitsune udon (about 1,500 yen).

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe a calm, nostalgic meal in an old-style house, with a generously sweet sauce over the anago pairing well with the rice.
Reviewers note the set of udon and a mini anago bowl, eaten in a traditional old-house setting that felt settled and relaxed.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Miyajima-komachi confirms the location (2 min from Itsukushima Shrine, next to the Folk History Museum), the tatami/terrace old-house seating, and the mini-anago-bowl + kitsune-udon set (¥1,500) with a quite-sweet sauce (that reviewer found the eel slightly firm). The 4travel review confirms the same udon+mini-anago set in a calm old-house setting (and found the eel softly grilled); sources disagree on eel texture.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago bowl is served with a sweet soy-based tare, which in this style of dish is wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen information is published.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional family-run tea house with no English menu and irregular opening hours, so it is best approached with a Japanese-speaking friend, a translation app, and flexibility about whether it is open.

places.name: 喫茶えびすや (Kissa Ebisuya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings. The menu centers on wheat-based items — hot cakes, toast, and shrimp pilaf — and there is no documentation of gluten-free preparation or awareness. Not suitable for someone needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. The food menu (shrimp pilaf, hot cakes with egg and dairy, toast, zenzai red-bean soup) and standard kissaten drinks rely on egg, dairy, and seafood. No plant-based menu or accommodation is noted.
places.hours_raw: Business hours irregular; closed Wed
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Omotesando / area in front of Miyajima Pier
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A retro Showa-era coffee shop sitting just in front of the Miyajima ferry terminal, at the gateway to the Omotesando shopping street. The shop opened in 1975, growing out of the family's former inn, 'Ebisuya Ryokan,' which was converted into a cafe — so the building car (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A retro Showa-era coffee shop sitting just in front of the Miyajima ferry terminal, at the gateway to the Omotesando shopping street. The shop opened in 1975, growing out of the family's former inn, 'Ebisuya Ryokan,' which was converted into a cafe — so the building carries the history of welcoming travelers across two eras.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic kissaten fare: drip coffee, hot cakes, toast, shrimp pilaf, and zenzai (sweet red-bean soup), plus a momiji-manju set served with coffee (around 650 yen).

anecdotes.text: The cafe began life as Ebisuya Ryokan, a family inn, before being remade into a coffee shop in 1975 — a small piece of Miyajima's hospitality history just steps from the pier.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog (JP/EN) lists a 1975 establishment, the former 'Ebisuya Ryokan' inn origin, a location right in front of the Miyajima ferry terminal, shrimp pilaf and a maple-leaf-manju set (about 650 yen); owner listed as Yuko Uchiyama.

positives.text: Foreign-language menus are available, which can make it easier to ask about ingredients despite limited English.

cultural_comfort.note: A small old-school family-run coffee shop right by the Miyajima ferry pier; a friendly greeting and pointing at the picture menu (foreign-language menus are available) will carry you through, but bring cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: 昭和カフェ 松大 (Showa Cafe Matsudai)
places.gf_detail: A traditional kissaten serving coffee with toast and light dishes such as oyster curry bread; bread is wheat-based and there is no gluten-free menu, no documented GF accommodation, and no dedicated fryer. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee and tea can be ordered, but the food (toast, café au lait, oyster curry bread) involves dairy and seafood, and there is no documented vegan menu or plant-milk option. Not a vegan destination; at most a black-coffee stop.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–17:00; irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Omotesando / Hamanocho, ~2 min walk from Miyajima Pier
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A quiet, old-fashioned coffee house tucked along the path near the Miyajima ferry pier, about a two-minute walk from the terminal. It carries the atmosphere of a Showa-era kissaten and was originally tied to the island's ferry history — long ago the spot served as a wai (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A quiet, old-fashioned coffee house tucked along the path near the Miyajima ferry pier, about a two-minute walk from the terminal. It carries the atmosphere of a Showa-era kissaten and was originally tied to the island's ferry history — long ago the spot served as a waiting area in front of the ferry pier before becoming the small coffee shop it is today. It later took on the name COFFEE MATSUDAI / 昭和カフェ 松大 while keeping the same interior and staff.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm refuge from the crowded approach to Itsukushima Shrine — a place to rest tired feet over an unhurried cup of coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully prepared drip-style coffee and café au lait, with toast and light bites; a notable item is the hot, crispy oyster curry bread.

anecdotes.text: Despite sitting on the busy main street to the shrine, regulars describe it as surprisingly quiet and a healing escape for footsore visitors.
Long ago this tea house served as the waiting area in front of the ferry pier, and it later changed its name to 'COFFEE MATSUDAI 昭和カフェ' while the interior and staff stayed the same.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 4travel and modokimaru reviews describe a former ferry-pier waiting area, a recent rename to COFFEE MATSUDAI / 昭和カフェ with the same interior and staff, an oyster curry bread (¥400), and a quiet healing spot for footsore visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, quiet old-school Japanese coffee house with no indication of English support, so a foreign student should be ready to order simply (point at the menu) and embrace the local, no-frills atmosphere.

places.name: お好み焼き 桜まる (Okonomiyaki Sakuramaru)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, whose batter and the layered noodles (yakisoba) are made from wheat flour, and okonomiyaki sauce is typically wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature dishes feature Miyajima oysters, pork, egg, and seafood, and Hiroshima okonomiyaki standardly includes egg, meat, and bonito/fish-based sauce and dashi. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (last order around 15:00-15:30). Wednesday closed; occasionally also closed on some Fridays / irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Omotesando shopping street area near
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop sitting along the Omotesando approach on Miyajima, a short walk from the ferry terminal toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is an intimate counter-and-tatami spot built around the everyday foods Hiroshima locals grew up on. (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop sitting along the Omotesando approach on Miyajima, a short walk from the ferry terminal toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is an intimate counter-and-tatami spot (about 20 seats) built around the everyday foods Hiroshima locals grew up on.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames a trip as the pairing of beautiful scenery and good food, inviting visitors to enjoy Hiroshima's down-to-earth comfort dishes - okonomiyaki and oysters - side by side.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki topped with oysters harvested in the waters off Miyajima, layered with the region's characteristic thin noodles, plus foil-grilled oyster dishes.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen grills oysters caught off Miyajima right on the teppan and sets them atop the okonomiyaki, billing it as a way to taste Hiroshima's and Miyajima's local specialties in one dish.
The Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is built with notably thin noodles, layering batter, vegetables, meat, noodles and egg before being pressed and steamed on the griddle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site and gorp.jp confirm Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with slightly thinner noodles, Miyajima-harvested oysters grilled on the teppan and placed atop the okonomiyaki, foil-grilled oysters, about 20 counter/tatami seats, and the 'beautiful scenery and delicious food' message.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based okonomiyaki batter and wheat noodles are cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside all dishes, so a gluten-free meal is not feasible here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen labeling is published; okonomiyaki sauce and seasonings are standardly wheat-containing and no gluten-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oysters, pork, egg and other seafood are central to the menu and share the same griddle; no vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help a traveler ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the shop is used to tourists, so a foreign student can order comfortably without much Japanese.

places.name: ひろしま きんちゃい屋 (Hiroshima Kinchaiya)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe, wheat noodles, and wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauce, and the teppanyaki dishes also rely on these. No gluten-free options are documented; not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki uses egg, pork, squid tempura, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, and teppanyaki centers on meat and seafood. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu–Sun 18:00-02:00; closed Wed (some sources say opens 17:00)
places.neighborhood: Kanayamacho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact, owner-run okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter that opened on August 23, 2023, tucked back from the street along the Butsudan-dori area near Ginzan-cho. The shop seats just six at a single counter facing the griddle, where the friendly owner cooks and keeps up

chef_bio.background: A compact, owner-run okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter that opened on August 23, 2023, tucked back from the street in the Ginzan-cho area of central Hiroshima. The shop seats just six at a single counter facing the griddle, where the friendly owner cooks and keeps up easy conversation with guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with fine noodles, plus a range of teppanyaki. A signature plate is the 'Kinchai no Meigetsu Soba,' topped with squid tempura, shredded nori, and a sunny-side egg.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the owner as a relaxed, likable guy who deftly works the small griddle and chats with everyone seated at the counter.
Because the storefront sits set back from the street, visitors are advised to watch for the shop's sign so they don't walk past it.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The modokimaru review and Tabelog confirm an August 23, 2023 opening in Naka-ku's Ginzan-cho, six counter seats set back from the street, a friendly owner, and the 'Kinchai no Meigetsu Soba' (¥1,580) with squid tempura, seasoned nori and a firmed egg.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter, noodles, and sauces are wheat-based and cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside all other dishes, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki uses wheat-containing okonomiyaki/soy-based sauces; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a single shared griddle with pork, seafood, and egg, so vegan separation is not feasible.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny six-seat counter run solo by a friendly owner who chats with guests while he cooks; no English documented, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ (Hiroshima Kaki Miyamae)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on oysters (raw, grilled, and breaded-and-fried kaki fry), anago, teppanyaki and izakaya dishes; fried items use wheat batter/breading and dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. Not a gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oyster and seafood izakaya whose core dishes are oysters, conger eel (anago), small sardines, and A5 wagyu, with fish/shellfish and wheat-soy seasoning throughout. No vegan menu or vegan accommodation is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu-Sun 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Tue & Wed (official site notes the holiday schedule can be irregular)
places.neighborhood: Naka-machi, Naka-ku (Heiwa-koen / Hondori area, ~5 min from
places.cuisine: Seto Inland Sea cuisine / oyster izakaya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An at-home, owner-run Seto Inland Sea cuisine spot in central Hiroshima built around the region's signature ingredient: oysters from the bay that lands the most oysters in Japan, brought in daily from the local market.

chef_bio.background: An at-home, owner-run Seto Inland Sea cuisine spot in central Hiroshima (Nakamachi, near Fukuromachi) built around the region's signature ingredient: oysters from the area that lands the most oysters in Japan, brought in daily from the local market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let guests taste the best of Hiroshima in one homey room, sourcing seafood directly and stocking around 30 local sakes including brewery-direct and seasonal limited bottles.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima brand 'Kaki Komachi' oysters (large, over 150g) served raw, grilled, or fried; white-grilled local anago; small sardines; teppanyaki; and A5 wagyu, paired with a deep local-sake list.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen prides itself on a lineup of roughly 30 local sakes, including brewery-sourced and seasonal limited bottles, to pair with the oysters.
Signature 'Kaki Komachi' oysters can be ordered raw, grilled, or as kaki fry, with multi-course menus showcasing Hiroshima specialties.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site, HotPepper and Tabelog confirm 'Kaki Komachi' oysters exceeding 150g served raw/grilled/fried, white-grilled anago, teppanyaki, A5 wagyu, roughly 30 local sakes, and oysters sourced daily from Hiroshima's market, Japan's largest for oyster landings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya style; soy-sauce-based seasonings contain wheat, and fried oysters (kaki fry) use a wheat batter/breading.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on oysters, anago, sardines and other seafood plus wagyu, so animal products are pervasive across the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A homey, small owner-run izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will go a long way; no indication of English support.

places.name: 鉄太 (Tetta)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house. The okonomiyaki batter and the noodles layered into it are wheat-based, and dishes are finished with wheat-containing okonomiyaki/soy sauces, so it is not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood teppanyaki/okonomiyaki counter: signature dishes include uni-horen (sea urchin and spinach), egg-rich okonomiyaki, and black Wagyu offal, and sauces contain fish/wheat-based ingredients. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-03:00 (L.O. 02:30) daily, per the operator's HotPepper listing (no regular holiday). Tabelog lists 18:00-02:00 with Sundays/day-after-holidays closed; the operator page shows open year-round.
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka Ward (Hatchobori / Nagarekawa area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter in the Yakenbori nightlife quarter, where everything is cooked on the iron griddle right in front of you at a small counter. The kitchen leans on carefully chosen Hiroshima ingredients, and the okonomiyak

chef_bio.background: A long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter in the Yakenbori nightlife quarter, where everything is cooked on the iron griddle right in front of you at a small counter. The kitchen leans on carefully chosen Hiroshima ingredients, and the okonomiyaki noodles are coated in a house-made dashi and griddled slowly for over ten minutes so the outside turns crisp while the inside stays moist.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make the most of select local ingredients and cook each okonomiyaki slowly and properly rather than fast.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, plus the local favorite uni-horen (sea urchin and spinach) on the teppan and black Wagyu offal.

anecdotes.text: The signature uni-horen (sea urchin with spinach) drew wider attention after being featured on television.
Regulars keep coming back in part for the easygoing owner, who chats with guests across the counter as he cooks.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: HotPepper confirms a Yakenbori counter shop cooking on the teppan, house-made dashi noodles griddled over ten minutes for a crisp outside and moist inside, uni-horen (¥1,700) featured on TV, black wagyu offal, exclusively Hiroshima-sourced ingredients, and an engaging owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A single shared teppan griddle is used for all dishes, including wheat-batter okonomiyaki and noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki/soy-style sauces that are typically wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is used for meat, seafood (sea urchin), egg and Wagyu offal, so any item would share surfaces with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only local spot where the owner chats with guests in Japanese and there is no English menu or website, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) makes the visit much smoother.

places.name: 鉄板焼・お好み焼 まいる～夢 (Teppanyaki Okonomiyaki Mile-mu)
places.gf_detail: Okonomiyaki batter is wheat-based and not gluten-free, and teppanyaki dishes are typically finished with wheat-containing soy sauce or sauces. There is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, so this is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg, bacon, butter, and other meat/seafood teppanyaki and okonomiyaki dishes; the signature omurice uses egg and bacon, and sauces rely on fish/soy bases. No documented vegan options.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward (Hatchobori / Ebisucho area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run teppanyaki and okonomiyaki counter that built its reputation over 18 years on Ebisu-dori (胡子通り) before relocating about eight years ago to its current spot in central Hiroshima. Through the move it has kept the same intimate, family feel and a loyal followi

chef_bio.background: A family-run teppanyaki and okonomiyaki counter that built its reputation over 18 years on Ebisu-dori (胡子通り) before relocating about eight years ago to its current spot in the Nagarekawa area of central Hiroshima. Through the move it has kept the same intimate, family feel and a loyal following.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, with a much-loved fluffy omurice (omelette rice with bacon, onion, ketchup rice, and a finish of melted butter) as the standout signature dish. The owner is especially known for deft work with butter on the griddle.

anecdotes.text: Regulars rave about a simple scallion dish, with one diner saying they had never eaten such delicious scallions before.
Visitors are often surprised to find such consistently delicious cooking tucked away here, with a common refrain that everything on the menu is good.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms 18 years on Ebisu-dori before relocating about eight years ago to the Nagarekawa area, the family-run feel, a signature omurice with ketchup rice, bacon and onion finished with melted butter, the owner's butter skill, and a much-praised scallion dish.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, late-night family-run teppanyaki counter where ordering is in Japanese; a few basic phrases or a translation app will help, and the warm owner makes it welcoming once you're seated.

places.name: 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ 離れ (Hiroshima Kaki Miyamae Hanare)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings are documented. The menu centers on oysters, white-grilled conger eel (anago), small sardines, A5 wagyu steak and teppanyaki — Japanese-style dishes that typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or allergy handling is mentioned.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The restaurant is an oyster and seafood izakaya — signature dishes are raw and grilled oysters, anago, small sardines and wagyu beef. No vegan or plant-based menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays 17:00-23:00 (last order 22:00). Closed Wednesdays. Weekends/holidays may use 2-hour time-limited staggered seatings.
places.neighborhood: Komachi / Jizo-dori, Naka Ward (near Chuden-mae Stn, within the Peace Park area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A sister branch (離れ) of the original 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ, this small Seto Inland Sea izakaya sits on Jizo-dori in Komachi, a short walk from Chuden-mae and within the Peace Park area of central Hiroshima.

chef_bio.background: A sister branch (離れ) of the original 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ, this small Seto Inland Sea izakaya sits on Jizo-dori in Komachi, a short walk from Chuden-mae in central Hiroshima.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner sources oysters daily directly from Hiroshima's market — Japan's largest for oyster landings — and procures high-quality meat through his own channels to offer it at reasonable prices, while personally curating the sake selection.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima brand oysters served raw and grilled, alongside white-grilled anago (conger eel), small sardines, A5 wagyu steak and teppanyaki, paired with a curated lineup of around 30 local sake.

anecdotes.text: Oysters are brought in fresh each day from Hiroshima's market, which has the highest oyster landing volume in Japan.
The same owner, Asayama Ikuya, runs both the original 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ and this 離れ.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official sites, HotPepper and Tabelog (plus search) confirm owner Asayama Ikuya operates both branches; the 離れ sits in Komachi on Jizo-dori near Chuden-mae, serving Kaki Komachi oysters (raw/grilled), white-grilled anago, A5 wagyu sourced through the owner's channels, and about 30 curated local sakes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu is built on oysters, white-grilled anago, small sardines and teppanyaki, which in Japanese cooking typically use wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a seafood and oyster izakaya with no documented vegan or plant-based dishes; cross-contact with fish and shellfish should be expected throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, dinner-only local oyster izakaya where some Japanese helps; reservations are made through Japanese booking sites, so a foreign student may want a Japanese-speaking friend or translation app to book and order comfortably.

places.name: 中華そば まる 舟入店 (Chuka Soba Maru Funairi-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The chuka soba and chashu-men use wheat noodles in a soy-seasoned broth (soy sauce contains wheat), so there are no gluten-free options. Not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu/soy) base and dishes are topped with chashu pork; this is a meat-based ramen specialist with no vegan menu. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: 中区舟入中町 (Funairi-nakamachi, central Hiroshima, west of the Pe
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running Hiroshima chuka soba shop. The current proprietor inherited the recipe and approach from the previous owner of the Funairi location and reopened it after renovation in February 2020, continuing a flavor that locals have known since childhood.

chef_bio.background: A long-running Hiroshima chuka soba shop. The current proprietor inherited the recipe and approach from the previous owner of the Funairi location and reopened it after renovation in February 2020, continuing a flavor that locals have known since childhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserve the nostalgic, time-honored taste and pass it to future generations while welcoming families warmly with a smile.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional Hiroshima-style chuka soba: thin noodles in a soy-seasoned pork-bone broth, served simply (chuka soba 750 yen, chashu-men 850 yen).

anecdotes.text: The owner describes wanting to keep sharing 'that flavor familiar since childhood' with as many customers as possible for years to come, welcoming everyone with a smile.
Reopened in February 2020 after renovation, with the current owner inheriting the previous Funairi proprietor's recipe.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mypl.net and Tabelog confirm the current owner took over from the previous Funairi proprietor and relaunched in February 2020 after renovation, serving a nostalgic soy pork-bone chuka soba with thin noodles and bean sprouts (chuka soba ¥750, chashu-men ¥850).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth and seasoning are soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the noodles are wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork-bone broth and pork chashu are central to the menu; all dishes share this meat-based broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 12-seat counter ramen shop with no English support and cash only, so come with the dish name written down and exact change ready.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles (中細ストレート) that grip the soy-pork broth; described as noticeably firm with an almost dried-noodle character compared with typical Hiroshima shops.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka Soba (中華そば) — the classic soy-pork-bone bowl; upgrade to Chashu-men for a generous fan of pork slices.
ramen.chashu: Soft, melting braised pork slices (とろりとした柔らかいチャーシュー); the Chashu-men piles on a generous stack.
notable_toppings: crisp thin bean sprouts (細もやし)

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a soy (wheat) tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone and soy broth with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook bowl of "genso" Hiroshima ramen: a brown soy-and-pork-bone soup, mellow and savory rather than heavy, where the lineage shows in the gentle tonkotsu sweetness married to soy depth. The signature crisp thin bean sprouts and a tangle of medium-thin straight noodles complete a bowl that tastes of postwar Hiroshima yatai culture.

places.highlight: A textbook bowl of "genso" Hiroshima ramen: a brown soy-and-pork-bone soup, mellow and savory rather than heavy, where the lineage shows in the gentle tonkotsu sweetness married to soy depth. The signature crisp thin bean sprouts and a tangle of medium-thin straight noodles complete a bowl that tastes of postwar Hiroshima yatai culture.

places.name: 肉のますゐ (Niku no Masui)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty is breaded, deep-fried tonkatsu (wheat flour, egg, panko) cooked in shared oil, and Western dishes, curry, and sukiyaki rely on wheat-based soy sauce and sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a butcher-run meat restaurant whose entire identity is beef sukiyaki, pork cutlets, beef cutlets, and other meat dishes; no vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Diner 11:00–20:45 (2nd floor until around 21:00, last order 20:20–20:30). Butcher shop 10:00–19:00. Closed every Wed and the 2nd Tue (some sources say the 2nd and 4th Tue).
places.neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, Naka Ward) — about a 2-minute walk from t
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A butcher's shop that opened a dining room in 1956, during the years Hiroshima was rebuilding after the war. Decades on it is still run as a family meat business with a restaurant attached, beloved by locals as one of the city's classic yoshoku (Japanese-style Western f

chef_bio.background: A meat shop that opened a dining room in 1956, during the years Hiroshima was rebuilding after the war. Decades on it is still run as a family meat business (精肉店直営) with a restaurant attached, beloved by locals as one of the city's classic yoshoku (Japanese-style Western food) institutions, spread across two floors that fill with a lunchtime crowd.
chef_bio.philosophy: A reporter described the kindness and warmth of the place: the most popular dish is prepared ahead so customers barely have to wait, a small everyday courtesy that keeps regulars coming back.
chef_bio.specialty: Service tonkatsu — a thin, crisp pork cutlet served almost the moment you order it, generously coated in the house sauce (around 380 yen with rice) — alongside carefully prepared beef sukiyaki, where staff grill premium marbled loin at just the right temperature. As a butcher-run kitchen, the restaurant trades on the quality and value of its meat.

anecdotes.text: The signature service tonkatsu has been famous for being remarkably cheap and quick — a thin, crispy cutlet with rice (about 380 yen) that arrives with almost no wait because it is prepared in advance.

Opened in 1956 ('Showa 31'), the same year as the famous economic line 'the post-war era is over,' the meat-shop restaurant became a local favorite known above all for its sukiyaki, with waitresses grilling beautifully marbled beef tableside.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hread.home-tv, gotrip and mrs.living confirm a 1956 (Showa 31) founding as a meat-shop-run (精肉店直営) yoshoku restaurant in Hatchobori, a ¥380 service tonkatsu (originally devised for cash-strapped students) prepared in advance, tableside sukiyaki with marbled beef, and a two-floor layout busy at lunch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tonkatsu and other fried cutlets are breaded in wheat flour and panko and deep-fried; frying oil is shared across breaded items, so heavy cross-contact with gluten is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as sukiyaki and Western-style preparations use standard Japanese soy sauce and sauces, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a meat-focused, butcher-run restaurant where nearly every dish centers on beef or pork; no vegan or plant-based items are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, casual local institution where ordering is easiest with a few Japanese words or by pointing at the menu; staff are warm but English is limited, so a translation app helps.

places.name: とんかつ とんき (Tonkatsu Tonki)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu specialist: the pork cutlets are breaded in wheat flour and panko (breadcrumbs), and the house tonkatsu sauce is not gluten-free. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, and the menu is built around fried, breaded items, so it is not suitable for someone requiring gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: A pork-cutlet specialty restaurant. Core dishes are deep-fried pork; the frying oil is 100% lard, and the standard set includes pork soup (tonjiru). There are no documented vegan options, and the shop is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:00–14:00 (L.O. 14:00) and 17:30–20:00 (L.O. 19:30); closed Sundays, holidays, and days adjacent to holidays. (A separate local listing notes 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–21:00; lunch and dinner service confirmed.)
places.neighborhood: Kanayacho, Minami Ward (2–3 min walk from Inarimachi tram stop, near Hiroshima Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running tonkatsu shop in Hiroshima's Minami Ward that opened in 1981 and has become so well known locally that, as one feature put it, in Hiroshima 'tonkatsu means Tonki.' It is a small, independently run shop on a counter-centered floor that nonetheless opens in

chef_bio.background: A long-running tonkatsu shop in Hiroshima's Minami Ward that opened around 1981 (roughly 45 years in business) and has become so well known locally that, as one feature put it, in Hiroshima 'tonkatsu means Tonki.' It is a small, independently run shop on a counter-centered floor that nonetheless opens into a surprisingly deep interior with tables and a tatami room. A related shop in the Nishi Ward area exists as a branch-off (noren-wake).
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner describes aiming for 'food I myself find delicious — something anyone who eats it will think is delicious' (自分が食べておいしいもの。誰が食べてもおいしいと思ってもらえるようなものを目指しています).
chef_bio.specialty: Loin tonkatsu set (roasu katsu teishoku), with thick, tender cutlets, a homemade sauce, shredded cabbage with a choice of two dressings, hearty pork soup, pickles and orange. First refills of rice and pork soup are free.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen rotates four separate oil pots so the oil can rest between uses for a crisper fry, and avoids crowding cutlets to keep the oil temperature steady; the frying oil is 100% lard to concentrate umami.
Sauce and breadcrumbs are made in-house, and the shop is calm and tidy with a counter that makes it comfortable to visit alone.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: hread.home-tv and mypl.net confirm a Minami-ku shop about 45 years old (opened ~1981), the 'tonkatsu means Tonki' reputation, four rotated oil pots, 100% lard frying oil, house-made sauce and breadcrumbs, a loin katsu set with free first refills, and a Nishi-ku noren-wake branch. The owner's personal name is not published in the sources.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are coated in wheat flour and panko and deep-fried, so the shared fryers carry gluten; no gluten-free preparation is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House tonkatsu sauce is used on the cutlets; tonkatsu sauce is wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Frying oil is 100% lard (pork fat), so all fried items are cooked in animal fat; the set also includes pork soup.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional local tonkatsu shop with counter seating where you can comfortably eat alone, but staff likely speak mostly Japanese and there may be no English menu, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 一楽章　未完成 (Ichigakusho Mikansei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are documented anywhere. The curry roux is made without oil but there is no information confirming it is wheat-free, and Japanese curry roux commonly contains wheat flour as a thickener. A gluten-free traveler would need to ask directly about each dish; treat as not confirmed GF.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen's hook is oil-free curry built on vegetables, fruits, and spices, which gives it a health-conscious, veg-friendly reputation. However, the signature and most popular dish is a tandoori chicken curry, and the evening menu includes general izakaya items and alcohol, so this is not a vegetarian/vegan establishment. A vegetable-based curry appears to be available, but ingredients (dairy, stock, honey/fruit) are not documented as vegan. A vegan diner should ask whether a specific curry can be made without animal products.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 11:30-00:00; Sat, Sun, and holidays 12:00-00:00. Closed Wed, plus other irregular closures (only closed for Obon and New Year).
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho, Naka Ward (~2 min walk from Kamiyacho-higashi Stn, very close to Hondori / Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: The cafe grew out of a classical-music kissaten the owner first opened about 30 years ago. After running that earlier room for many years she reinvented the concept, drawing on her food-industry experience to make curry the centerpiece, and in 2013 moved to a larger spa

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (central Naka-ku, Kamiyacho, near the Hondori shopping street and Peace Memorial Park area)
chef_bio.background: The cafe grew out of a classical-music kissaten the owner first opened about 30 years ago. After running that earlier room for some 22 years she reinvented the concept, drawing on her food-industry experience to make curry the centerpiece, and in 2013 moved to a larger space near Hondori (Kamiyacho) so she could finally combine the two things she loves most.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's playful motto is that the most delicious curry is the one made at home, so she sets out to make 'the second most delicious' - cooked daily with care and kept fresh rather than aged. The bigger dream realized in the current space was to let people enjoy real curry and live classical music together, casually, without needing a concert hall.
chef_bio.specialty: Oil-free homemade curry simmered from vegetables, fruits, and spices; a much-loved version is a generous chicken curry.

anecdotes.text: Lunchtimes sometimes feature live piano and violin, and the evening occasionally turns into a mini-concert with performers, spotlights and all - the room is built around a grand piano, cello, and contrabass.
A signature quirk is the no-oil cooking; the curry is prepared without any added fat, which is why regulars describe it as an unusually 'healthy' curry.
Beyond the standards there are playful options such as a banana curry, reflecting the fruit-and-vegetable base of the recipes.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hiroshima Keizai Shimbun and the official site confirm owner Nakano Akemi, a classical-music kissaten of about 30 years that operated 22 years before relocating near Hondori/Kamiyacho into a larger 23-tsubo space in 2013, oil-free curry from vegetables/fruits/spices, live piano and violin, a grand piano/cello/contrabass performance area, and the 'second most delicious curry in Hiroshima' motto; a Yahoo News feature covers the novelty banana curry.

positives.text: The curry is cooked without any oil/fat, using a base of vegetables, fruits, and spices, which can suit health-conscious and lighter-eating diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed adult salon-style cafe where classical music and curry mix; staff are described as friendly and chatty, but expect a mostly Japanese-speaking setting, so a few basic phrases will help.

places.name: リゾート (Resort)
places.gf_detail: This is a rice gratin specialist whose dishes are built on wheat-based béchamel/cream sauces and breaded items (cream croquettes, lasagna), so they are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Signature gratins center on dairy (béchamel, cheese, butter rice) plus chicken, shrimp, clams, squid, white fish, ham and egg; no vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka Ward (Tatemachi tram stop / Nakanotana shopping street, very close to Hondori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Resort opened in 1975 in Hiroshima's Fukuro-machi district, founded by Numaguchi Tadashi together with three friends. It became a fixture of local life and at one point ran three locations. After closing, it was revived in 2014 at its current Tatemachi address, where it

chef_bio.chef_name: Numaguchi Tadashi (founder); Yoshikawa Hirohito (son-in-law, current co-manager)
chef_bio.background: Resort opened in 1975 in Hiroshima's Fukuro-machi district, founded by Numaguchi Tadashi together with three friends. It became a fixture of local life and at one point ran multiple locations around the city (including branches such as Sanmall). After a period of closure, it was revived in 2014 at its current Tatemachi address, where it sits a short walk from the Hondori shopping arcade and the Nakanotana market street.
chef_bio.philosophy: The family wants the gratin to taste creamy and gentle so it is loved by children and adults alike, and to carry the historic shop forward to the next generation while adapting to the times.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice gratin (raisu guratan) baked piping hot in custom lidless ovens, topped with creamy bechamel and cheese grated fresh from whole blocks. Beloved varieties include Monaco (tomato/ketchup rice with chicken), Oscar (butter rice with shrimp) and Andre (curry-flavored rice with clams), plus shrimp cream croquettes and a homemade onion salad.

anecdotes.text: The founder and his daughter's husband, Yoshikawa Hirohito, led the revival; Yoshikawa said he did not want to let such a historic shop come to an end, and aims to grow Resort so it can be passed to the next generation.
The dishes are called 'rice gratin' rather than 'doria' because doria was not yet a familiar term in Japan when the shop opened in 1975.
Cited as a Hiroshima lunch staple loved for around half a century since its 1975 founding.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, longtime local Western-style eatery where ordering is straightforward by pointing at the picture menu, though staff likely speak mostly Japanese.

places.name: インド料理ラソイ 本通り店 (Indo Ryori Rasoi Hondori-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Plain rice and most curry sauces are naturally wheat-free, but naan is wheat and some curries may be thickened or finished with wheat-containing ingredients; there is no stated dedicated GF handling, so a celiac diner must ask directly. Treat as unverified.
places.vegan_detail: The listings state the kitchen "flexibly accommodates vegetarian and vegan menus" (ベジタリアン・ビーガンメニューにも柔軟に対応) and vegetable curries are available. However, Indian curries here commonly use ghee, butter, cream and dairy (e.g. butter chicken is a signature), so a strict vegan should specify no dairy/ghee when ordering. Accommodation is on-request rather than a fixed vegan menu.
places.neighborhood: Hondori, Naka Ward (3 min walk from Hondori Stn, around the Hondori shopping street)
places.notes: A casual Indian curry restaurant that opened in September 2025 on the fourth floor of a building just off Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from Hondori Station. It serves authentic Indian curries in a relaxed, exotic-feeling space, with signature dishes

chef_bio.background: A casual Indian curry restaurant that opened in September 2025 on the fourth floor of a building just off Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from Hondori Station. It serves authentic Indian curries in a relaxed, exotic-feeling space, with signature dishes such as butter chicken and seafood curry, lunch buffets, and biryani and curry set menus.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic Indian curries including butter chicken and seafood curry; lunch buffet and biryani/curry sets

anecdotes.text: Opened on September 10, 2025 as a fourth-floor curry house just off the Hondori arcade, offering an all-you-can-eat Indian lunch buffet.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen states it flexibly accommodates vegetarian and vegan menu requests, indicating staff will adjust dishes on request.

positives.text: Vegetable curries and flexible vegetarian/vegan accommodation are available, so plant-based diners have on-request options.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, accessible Indian curry spot with multilingual delivery-app menus, picture menus, and cashless payment, so a foreign student can order comfortably with little Japanese.

places.name: 蔦 (Tsuta)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature dishes are wheat-based pasta (napolitan, curry spaghetti) and breaded/fried cutlets (panko = wheat), with ketchup/curry sauces. Nothing on any fetched page indicates gluten-free options or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on meat and animal products — napolitan with bacon/sausage and toppings such as egg, tonkatsu (pork cutlet), hamburger and minced cutlet; curry-cutlet spaghetti; and fried-fish rice sets. No documented plant-based dishes.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00-16:00, Sat 11:00-14:00; closed Sun and holidays (Tabelog). Note: HotPepper lists 7:00-19:00 (Fri until 17:00), so sources differ.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka Ward (~4–5 min walk from Shiyakusho-mae tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsuta is a long-running, independent old-style café (junkissa) in central Hiroshima's Otemachi district, a short walk from the Shiyakusho-mae tram stop and near the Peace Memorial Park area. Its facade is famously wrapped in ivy (the name 蔦 means 'ivy'), and inside it k

chef_bio.background: Tsuta is a long-running, independent old-style café (junkissa) in central Hiroshima's Otemachi district, a short walk from the Shiyakusho-mae tram stop and near the Peace Memorial Park area. Its facade is famously wrapped in ivy (the name 蔦 means 'ivy'), and inside it keeps a warm, nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialties are a classic ketchup-flavored napolitan spaghetti and a hearty katsu-curry spaghetti, with other curry-spaghetti variations (chicken, hamburger) and rice-and-fried-fish plates on the menu. Portions are generous and the cooking is comforting, old-school yoshoku café fare.

anecdotes.text: At lunchtime the little café regularly draws a queue of around ten people waiting to get in.
The blog author recalls coming here as a 'reward' during university days, calling the food that good.

cultural_comfort.note: A nostalgic, locals-heavy Showa-era café where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English, so a few Japanese words or pointing at the menu helps; expect a lunchtime queue and cash payment.

places.name: いし井 (Ishii)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki/kappo restaurant with no gluten-free accommodation. The menu is built around wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based seasoning, and documented dishes include soba (buckwheat noodles often cut with wheat flour), tempura/fried courses, and duck soba — all gluten risks. There is no indication of GF menus, separate preparation, or staff allergen handling. Not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Courses center on seafood and fish-based Japanese cooking: documented dishes include five-variety sashimi (yellowtail, sea bream, raw oyster from Jigozen, bluefin tuna, scallop), an anago (eel) fried course, chawanmushi (egg), and duck soba. Dashi (fish/bonito stock) and wheat soy sauce run through the cuisine. While there are some plant-forward items (the signature bean aspic appetizer, soy-milk tofu, soy-milk hot pot, taro croquettes), the kitchen is not set up for vegan dining and a fully plant-based meal is not offered.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–24:00; closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area, near Kanayamacho tram stop)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter-style kappo run single-handedly by its chef-owner, who does all the cooking and management himself. The restaurant grew out of the local 笹組 group and was previously known as 豆と根菜 いし井 ("Beans and Root Vegetables Ishii") before becoming independent at its

chef_bio.background: A small counter-style kappo run single-handedly by its chef-owner, who does all the cooking and management himself. The restaurant grew out of the local 笹組 group and was previously known as 豆と根菜 いし井 ("Beans and Root Vegetables Ishii") before becoming independent at its current Nagarekawa address.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the virtues of Japanese washoku and fermentation culture (日本の和食、発酵文化の良さ), turning everyday ingredients into refined seasonal dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Japanese kaiseki built around fish and shellfish and a signature emphasis on bean dishes — the bean-aspic appetizer is repeatedly singled out as the highlight — alongside soy-milk tofu and other vegetable preparations. A deep all-you-can-drink sake list featuring premium nationwide labels such as Kokuryu and Tate-no-kawa alongside regional Hiroshima sake accompanies the food.

anecdotes.text: The signature opening dish, a bean aspic (豆のジュレ寄せ), is repeatedly called the best bite of the meal, and the menu leans into beans and soy-milk tofu throughout.
Because one chef cooks and serves everyone alone, the house offers a generous three-hour all-you-can-drink format so guests are never rushed despite the solo pace.
The place evolved from the 笹組 group and was formerly run as 豆と根菜 いし井 before its current incarnation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Documented courses include soba noodles, tempura/fried items and anago (eel), all typical wheat and gluten sources in a shared kappo kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine is traditional Japanese washoku seasoned with standard soy sauce and dashi, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Plant items such as the bean aspic and soy-milk tofu are served on the same fish-and-shellfish-focused course menu (sashimi, oysters, eel, duck), so cross-contact with animal ingredients is likely.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, solo-run counter restaurant where Japanese is the working language and reservations are essentially expected, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a Japanese-speaking companion.

places.name: 酒処 大甚 (Sakedokoro Daijin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented.
