This is a sake-focused izakaya whose menu centers on teppanyaki, simmered dishes, and sashimi; Japanese soy sauce and izakaya sauces typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Celiac travelers should not rely on this venue.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The kitchen revolves around grilled beef, sashimi, and other fish/meat dishes, and standard izakaya seasonings (dashi, soy sauce) use animal/wheat ingredients. A vegan diner would need to ask directly and would likely be limited to a few plain vegetable sides at best.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 17:30-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Sunday closed. If a Monday is a public holiday, the shop opens Sunday and closes that Monday instead.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Ebisu-dori, near Hatchobori / Ebisucho tram stop)
places.notes: A sake-centered izakaya that opened in early March 2026 in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Ebisucho tram stops. It spreads over two floors, with a casual counter-and-table ground floor and a more relaxed upstai

chef_bio.background: A sake-centered izakaya that opened in early March 2026 in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Ebisucho tram stops. It spreads over two floors, with a casual counter-and-table ground floor and a more relaxed upstairs of sunken horigotatsu seating and a private room for gatherings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house pitch is generous, good-value drinking and eating: an all-you-can-drink sake format meant to let guests work their way through a wide range of bottles without watching the tab.
chef_bio.specialty: Japanese sake, with a deep lineup of over twenty pours including Hiroshima local labels, paired with hearty teppanyaki-style grilled wagyu beef, small plates, and sashimi.

anecdotes.text: Early guests describe a bright, clean, brand-new space with plenty of seating styles and praise the breadth of the sake list and the generous portions of the food.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a lively local sake izakaya with no indication of English support, so a foreign student comfortable with some basic Japanese (or a translation app) and Japanese izakaya etiquette will get the most out of it.

places.name: 瀬戸の肴と牡蠣 AVLI (Seto no Sakana to Kaki AVLI)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on seafood served with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and seared/grilled preparations, with no indication of gluten-free accommodations.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood and oyster izakaya; the core offering is raw and seared fish, oysters, and shirasu, plus grilled meat. No vegan menu or vegan accommodations are documented, and standard dishes rely on fish and wheat-based soy sauce.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun, holidays, and days before holidays: 17:00–00:00 next day (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:00/23:30). Closed Mon.
places.neighborhood: Ebisucho, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area)
places.cuisine: Seafood izakaya / oyster bar serving flame-seared Setouchi fish and oysters
places.notes: AVLI is a seafood izakaya tucked on the 10th floor of a building in central Hiroshima, offering private rooms with views over the city's evening skyline. It is built around the bounty of the Seto Inland Sea, a body of water known for its warm climate and calm currents t

chef_bio.background: AVLI is a seafood izakaya tucked on the 10th floor of a building in central Hiroshima, offering private rooms with views over the city's evening skyline. It is built around the bounty of the Seto Inland Sea, a body of water known for its warm climate and calm currents that make it a treasure trove of seafood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on the aburi technique, lightly searing ingredients so the surface aroma and the inner tenderness both come forward, drawing out the original depth of flavor in each piece of seafood.
chef_bio.specialty: Seared (aburi) preparations of fresh local seafood: Hiroshima oysters, raw and seared fish, and fresh raw shirasu (whitebait), often finished with freshly pot-cooked (kamado-style) takikomi rice.

anecdotes.text: Seasonal Setouchi seafood is chosen for the menu through the year, with seared dishes and pot-cooked rice forming the backbone of the experience.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are Japanese seafood preparations (sashimi, seared fish, oysters) typically served with wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on fish, oysters, and grilled meat, so vegan diners face pervasive cross-contact with animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an evening seafood izakaya in Hiroshima's nightlife district where ordering is mostly in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: 大衆肉割烹 肉じるしるし (Taishu Niku Kappo Niku Jirushirushi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a meat izakaya whose dishes rely on wheat-containing staples: the signature nikuzushi/meat stew uses a house-made demi-glace (a wheat-flour roux base), grilled and simmered dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce (brewed with wheat), and fried items use wheat flour batter. Nothing on the documented menus or listings indicates gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire concept is meat-centric (niku kappo) — beef skirt (harami) steak, meat sushi, yakitori, and demi-glace meat stew. Sauces and broths use meat, fish-based dashi, and soy sauce. No documented plant-based or vegetable-only set menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00-00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30); closed Sun (open when Mon is a holiday)
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka Ward (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori area)
places.notes: A casual meat-focused kappo izakaya in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, marked by a retro lantern at the entrance just off Chuo-dori. It sits a short walk from central Hiroshima and is built for everything from after-work drinks to group banquets, with counter

chef_bio.background: A casual meat-focused kappo izakaya in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, marked by a retro lantern at the entrance just off Chuo-dori. It sits a short walk from central Hiroshima and is built for everything from after-work drinks to group banquets, with counter seats and private tatami rooms for parties.
chef_bio.philosophy: A stated focus on meat quality and freshness, with multiple cooking styles offered so guests can choose lighter cuts or hearty portions to pair with either beer or sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef skirt (harami) steak and a signature meat stew (nikuni) simmered in a house-made demi-glace sauce, alongside chicken, pork and beef prepared by grilling, simmering and frying.

anecdotes.text: The entrance is identifiable by a retro lantern in the Nagarekawa area just off Chuo-dori.
Known for gyu-harami steak and a meat stew (nikuni) made with house-made demi-glace sauce, with chicken, pork and beef offered grilled, simmered or fried.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Grilled and simmered meat dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat, and the signature demi-glace stew is built on a wheat-flour roux.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local meat izakaya where the menu and service run mostly in Japanese; a translation app and a willingness to point at dishes will help, and reservations through a Japanese-language site or by phone smooth the way for groups.

places.name: 赤のれん (Aka-noren)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The core menu (udon, simmered/oden dishes, rice porridge) relies on wheat noodles and wheat-based soy sauce and dashi seasoning, with no documented GF options or allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on fish-based dashi, sashimi, meat udon, egg dishes and oden simmered in soy/fish stock; no vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Specific opening/closing hours are not published on any fetched source; only the closed day (Sundays) is listed. Call ahead to confirm hours.
places.neighborhood: Yayoicho, Naka Ward (near Kanayamacho tram stop, Hatchobori / Yagenbori area)
places.cuisine: Old-style local Japanese diner / casual drin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, unpretentious neighborhood eatery tucked into central Hiroshima a few minutes from the Ginzan-machi tram stop. It keeps a frankly retro, Showa-era feel that regulars describe as stepping back in time.

chef_bio.background: A long-running, unpretentious neighborhood eatery tucked into central Hiroshima a few minutes from the Ginzan-machi tram stop. It keeps a frankly retro, Showa-era feel that regulars describe as stepping back in time.
chef_bio.specialty: Homestyle comfort food: meat udon and nabeyaki (hot-pot) udon with a light, gentle dashi, rice porridge (zosui), hand-formed onigiri (musubi), simmered dishes such as squid and vegetables, oden, and tempura.

anecdotes.text: Regulars praise the light, easygoing broth and warming udon as the perfect thing after a few drinks in the neighborhood.
The room is described as an old-fashioned, Showa-era retro space that feels like a step back in time.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu of udon, oden and simmered dishes indicates standard wheat-based soy sauce and dashi seasoning; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on fish dashi, with sashimi and meat udon on the menu, so animal products are used throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, old-fashioned local spot where almost everything happens in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable arriving with a few basic Japanese phrases and a relaxed, friendly attitude.

places.name: まいちえキッチン（Mai+Chie kitchen） (Mai+Chie Kitchen (Maichie Kitchen))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free dedication or specific GF labeling found on any fetched page. The core offering is bread and baked goods (wheat-based), so most items are not gluten-free. A traveler needing GF should ask directly about individual items; nothing confirms dedicated GF or cross-contamination controls.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan establishment — described across listings as an "all vegan organic takeout cafe" using organic ingredients, with no animal-based products. Menu includes vegan tantanmen, vegan fried "chicken", organic Buddha bowls, breads, and desserts. Opened June 2025.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (per Vegewel). Open weekends; closed Mon-Thu and on days when participating in markets.
places.neighborhood: 西区三滝本町（三滝駅周辺）/ Mitakihonmachi, Nishi Ward (near Mitaki Stati
places.notes: A small, independently run vegan organic cafe that opened in June 2025, operating out of a shared kitchen space (Mitaki Share Kitchen) in a quiet residential pocket of western Hiroshima, a short walk from Mitaki Station. Everything is plant-based and built around organi

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run vegan organic cafe that opened in June 2025, operating out of a shared kitchen space (Mitaki Share Kitchen) in a quiet residential pocket of western Hiroshima (Nishi-ku), a short walk from Mitaki Station. Everything is plant-based and built around organic ingredients, with the focus on takeout.
chef_bio.philosophy: An all-vegan, organic approach to everyday comfort food, offering familiar dishes reimagined entirely without animal products.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made vegan baked goods and breads alongside seasonal hot dishes such as a hearty vegetable-laden dry (soup-less) tantanmen; weekend lunch menus round things out.

anecdotes.text: Signature vegetable-laden dry tantanmen is praised as spicy with real umami depth and pleasantly chewy noodles.

positives.text: Entirely vegan kitchen, so there is no animal-product cross-contact to worry about for plant-based diners.
Uses organic ingredients across the menu.
English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, making it easier to ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: English staff and an English menu are available, so a foreign student can order comfortably; bring cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: お好み焼 もり (Okonomiyaki Mori)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), plus a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce (here a "大福ソース"-style sweet sauce). There is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergen accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature dishes are 肉玉そば/肉玉うどん (pork-and-egg okonomiyaki with noodles), with egg, pork, and squid (イカ天) standard. The batter and sauce are not vegan and there are no documented plant-based versions.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00), 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Monday and Tuesday. Note: some sources describe the closing days as Monday plus the 2nd Tuesday, and lunch-only/irregular daytime hours in summer; hours are best treated as variable.
places.neighborhood: Fujimicho, Naka Ward / Jizo-dori shopping street
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop founded in 1962 (Showa 37) on Jizo-dori, a downtown shopping street in Naka-ku that locals describe as a 'shitamachi within the city.' It has operated in the same spot for over half a century and is now run by Miho Matsuoka, the third-gen

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop founded in 1962 (Showa 37) on Jizo-dori, a downtown shopping street in Naka-ku that locals describe as a 'shitamachi within the city.' It has operated in the same spot for over half a century and is now run by Miho Matsuoka, the third-generation owner, who says she has been cooking her own okonomiyaki here since she was in elementary school. The shop got a light refresh in 2024 but keeps its Showa-era feel, with vintage photographs of its history on the walls.
chef_bio.philosophy: Matsuoka calls her approach 'subtraction okonomiyaki' (引き算のお好み焼き): she goes easy on fish powder, salt, and tenkasu so the dish comes together as one soft, unified bite rather than a pile of toppings. When she asked her founder grandmother for the batter recipe, the answer was 'there's no such thing -- you just feel it,' which pushed her to mix the batter by sense, adjusting for the day's temperature and water.
chef_bio.specialty: Rustic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, especially 肉玉そば (pork-egg with soba noodles) and 肉玉うどん (with udon), with toppings kept deliberately minimal -- essentially just squid tempura (イカ天) and green onion.

anecdotes.text: Matsuoka personally walks to the greengrocer to buy cabbage and bean sprouts, touching each one to check quality, then carefully draws out the bitterness (aku) before cooking.
In 2020 she launched a mail-order frozen okonomiyaki line, cooking each piece herself at the factory, saying she wants people to remember 'not our name, but our taste.'
The shop has been recommended on television by comedian and self-styled gourmet Watabe Ken as a Hiroshima okonomiyaki spot worth seeking out, with the niku-tama udon singled out for its soft texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooked on a shared teppan grill where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared; no gluten-free handling is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Uses a sweet okonomiyaki-style sauce (described as a 大福ソース-type sauce), which is wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Single shared teppan used for pork, egg, squid and noodles; no separate vegan preparation indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, old-school counter-only neighborhood shop run by the family with no English menu expected, so basic Japanese or a translation app and patience will help a foreign student feel welcome.

places.name: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き 蔵屋 本店 (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Kuraya Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house. The okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat-based crepe layer, the okonomi sauce is wheat-based, and teppan dishes are seasoned with regular wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or celiac accommodation is documented, and there is no indication of a dedicated cooking surface. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around meat, oysters and other Seto Inland Sea seafood; signature plates include sea urchin with spinach, A5 sirloin steak, oysters, and Hiroshima beef. Okonomiyaki standardly contains pork, egg and a bonito/fish-based sauce, and the teppan is shared with animal products. No vegan menu or vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays/days before holidays: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 22:30). Closed Sundays. 2-hour seating limit. Customers under 20 not admitted.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (near Hiroshima City Hall / about 700m fro
places.notes: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the city's central avenues and the Peace Memorial Park area. The interior is designed to evoke a traditional sake brewery (sakagura), a calm wood-toned Japanese space

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of central Hiroshima (Otemachi), a short walk from the city's central avenues and the Peace Memorial Park area. The interior is designed to evoke a traditional sake brewery (sakagura), a calm wood-toned Japanese space with counter seats and private sunken-floor rooms, each fitted with its own teppan griddle so dishes arrive sizzling.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with crisp, browned edges, alongside teppanyaki of local beef and Seto Inland Sea seafood. Signature plates include sea urchin sauteed with buttered spinach (uni-horen), crispy teppan potato salad, oysters, A5 sirloin steak, and a griddled hotcake with ice cream to finish, paired with a well-chosen lineup of local Hiroshima sake.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is styled after a sake brewery, with private horigotatsu rooms that each have their own built-in griddle so food is cooked and served hot at the table.
The kitchen leans on Hiroshima and Seto Inland Sea ingredients, from local beef and oysters to a signature sea-urchin-and-spinach plate, matched with regional sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made on a shared teppan griddle with a wheat-flour batter and wheat-based crepe layer; the same surface cooks noodles, meat and seafood, so gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki here use standard wheat-based okonomi sauce and regular soy sauce; no wheat-free or tamari soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The teppan is shared with meat, oysters and other seafood, and okonomiyaki normally contains egg, pork and a fish-based sauce, so vegan separation is not available.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome, though detailed dietary requests may be hard to communicate; note you must be 20 or older to enter.

places.name: 天麩羅 すえ廣 (Tempura Suehiro)
places.gf_detail: This is a tempura house: the core of the menu is items deep-fried in wheat-flour batter, so the signature dishes are not gluten-free. Tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu) and the soy sauce used also typically contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free batter, a dedicated fryer, or GF accommodation. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: The set meals and most plates include shrimp, squid, fish (mackerel), and chicken, and even vegetable tempura is fried in shared oil alongside seafood/meat and served with fish-based broth and sauces. There are vegetable tempura items on the menu (eggplant, pumpkin, mushroom), but they are not prepared vegan and there is no dedicated vegan offering. Not a vegan destination.
places.neighborhood: Nakamachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, near Hondori and Peac
places.notes: Tempura Suehiro opened in December 2020 in central Hiroshima's Nakamachi district, a short walk from Hondori and the Peace Park area. It pitches a modern, casual take on tempura — affordable enough for an everyday lunch yet served in a clean, refined wooden space.

chef_bio.background: Tempura Suehiro opened in December 2020 in central Hiroshima's Nakamachi district, a short walk from Hondori and the Peace Park area. It pitches a modern, casual take on tempura — affordable enough for an everyday lunch yet served in a clean, refined wooden space.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house proposes 'a new style of enjoying tempura' — pairing just-fried tempura with sake and wine in an izakaya-like, approachable setting rather than as formal haute cuisine. Each item is fried to order in front of the guest and brought to the counter on individual mesh trays as soon as it is done, so it arrives at peak crispness.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly fried tempura served one piece at a time. The signature Suehiro Teishoku is a generous set of seven tempura items (shrimp, squid, mackerel, chicken, and three vegetables — eggplant, pumpkin, mushroom) with rice, miso soup, and pickles, plus free rice refills.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen serves tempura piece by piece on individual mesh draining trays so each item reaches the diner at maximum crispness, and the affordable teishoku comes with free rice refills (up to three bowls).
Individual tempura pieces start from about 150 yen, and at dinner the house leans into pairing tempura with sparkling wine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tempura is fried in wheat-flour batter in shared oil; vegetable pieces share the same fryer as seafood and chicken, so cross-contamination with gluten (and seafood) is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tempura is accompanied by dipping sauce/soy-based seasonings that conventionally contain wheat; no wheat-free alternatives are indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura is fried in the same oil as shrimp, squid, fish, and chicken, and sets are served with fish-based broth and pickles, so plant items are not free of animal cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, modern tempura spot where solo diners are welcome at the partitioned counter; basic Japanese greetings will go a long way, though the simple point-and-order teishoku format makes it manageable even with limited language.

places.name: 出汁とおでん わいく (Dashi to Oden Waiku)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Oden simmers in a shared dashi seasoned with soy-sauce-based broth (Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the menu leans on sashimi with soy sauce, grilled anago, and fried (battered) local chicken. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house dashi is built on bonito (fish) and chicken bone alongside kelp and shiitake, and the core offerings are oden simmered in that stock, fresh seafood sashimi, grilled conger eel, and chicken. No documented vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: Official site: 17:30-23:30, closed Mondays. (Tabelog lists 17:00-24:00.)
places.neighborhood: 中区袋町 (Fukuro-cho, central Hiroshima, off Namiki-dori near Ho
places.cuisine: Owner-run oden izakaya: multi-dashi oden, Se
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Waiku is a small owner-run oden izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in Fukuro-cho, just off Namiki-dori in central Hiroshima. It opened in 2016 and has built its identity around carefully made oden served alongside local seafood and sake.

chef_bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Naka-ku), Seto Inland Sea region
chef_bio.background: Waiku is a small owner-run oden izakaya tucked on the second floor of the Takada Building in Fukuro-cho, just off Namiki-dori in central Hiroshima. It opened in 2016 and has built its identity around carefully made oden served alongside local seafood and sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name and spirit center on 'wa' (和) — harmony and human connection — with the owner stating a goal of nurturing a warm space where people bond over a shared meal. The kitchen refuses to cut corners on time or ingredients, building the signature broth from multiple stocks rather than one.
chef_bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a layered house dashi, plus fresh Seto Inland Sea sashimi, grilled conger eel (anago), and karaage of local Hiroshima red chicken, paired with curated Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: Rather than a single base, the broth is built by combining several dashi sources — bonito, kelp, chicken bone, and shiitake — a layered approach the owner treats as foundational to the oden.
Beyond oden, the kitchen leans on regional character: sashimi from the Seto Inland Sea, grilled conger eel, and karaage made from local Hiroshima red chicken, plus house-made yuzu kosho and miso.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site states the shop cultivates '人と人との繋がり、すなわち和' (human connection, i.e. 'wa') and builds its dashi by layering 鰹・昆布・鶏ガラ・シイタケ; it lists Seto Inland Sea sashimi, grilled anago, Hiroshima red-chicken karaage and house yuzu kosho/miso. A Tabelog reviewer notes 2016年開業 (opened 2016).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is simmered in a single shared dashi pot seasoned with soy-sauce-based broth, so items share the same wheat-containing liquid.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cuisine relies on dashi and soy-sauce seasoning typical of Japanese oden and sashimi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house dashi is made with bonito (fish) and chicken bone in addition to kelp and shiitake, so even vegetable oden items are cooked in an animal-based stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy local oden counter where a little Japanese helps; staff are welcoming but the experience is geared to Japanese-speaking diners, so a translation app and pointing at the oden pot go a long way.

places.name: 麺場神徳 (Menba Jintoku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Tsukemen uses wheat noodles and a fish/pork-bone dipping broth with wheat-based soy sauce. A low-carb/konjac noodle option exists for dieters, but the broth and tare are not gluten-free, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a double soup of fish stock and pork bone (tonkotsu), and dishes rely on animal stock and fish powder. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 7:00 PM–4:00 AM (last order 3:30 AM). Closed Sun.
places.neighborhood: 中区流川／胡町 (Nagarekawa / Hatchobori, central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A specialty shop for Kanto-style seafood dipping noodles tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, open from evening into the small hours for a late-night bowl.

chef_bio.background: A specialty shop for Kanto-style seafood dipping noodles tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, open from evening into the small hours (roughly 7pm to 4am) for a late-night bowl.
chef_bio.philosophy: Guests can dial the spice of the dipping sauce across five levels, and low-carb or konjac noodles are offered for diet-conscious diners.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made medium-thick noodles served with a double broth of fish stock and pork bone (gyokai-tonkotsu), eaten dip-style; the kitchen also makes a spicy mazesoba dusted with fish powder.

anecdotes.text: Diners can choose the spiciness of the dipping sauce from five levels, and lighter low-carb or konjac noodles are available as an alternative.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site: 神徳特製の中太麺を魚介と豚骨のＷスープで提供 (house medium-thick noodles in a fish + pork-bone double broth), five heat levels, 低糖麺・こんにゃく麺 options, and a 辛まぜそば with fish powder; hours PM7:00-AM4:00 in Nagarekawa, 14 seats, closed Sundays.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and wheat-based soy sauce are central to the menu, so gluten is present throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth and tare for tsukemen rely on standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from fish stock and pork bone, and fish powder is used in dishes, so animal products are pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: A small late-night counter tsukemen shop in the nightlife district where you can order by pointing at the menu, but staff likely speak limited English and it is cash-only, so a little Japanese helps.

noodles.notes: House special chu-futo (medium-thick) noodles with a firm, springy chew built to grip the thick, viscous dipping broth. A konjac (konnyaku) noodle swap is offered as an ultra-low-calorie alternative.

ramen.signature_bowl: Gyokai Tonkotsu Tsukemen — medium-thick noodles served alongside a small bowl of intensely rich, magma-hot seafood-and-pork-bone double broth, dusted with fragrant fish powder; finish by diluting the leftover dip with soup-wari.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork belly, soft and unctuous, offered as a separate topping (with a large-portion upgrade available).

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles with a soy-based dipping broth; even the konjac-noodle swap sits in the same wheat-and-fish-and-pork dip, so it is not celiac-safe. The dip also contains pork bone and seafood.

vegan.note: The core dipping broth is built on pork bone and fish (gyokai), with pork-belly chashu and fish powder throughout. No documented plant-based broth, so it is not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A Kanto-lineage gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen transplanted to central Hiroshima's nightlife district: thick straight noodles meet a small bowl of near-boiling double broth where deep pork-bone body and a heavy dusting of fish powder fuse into one assertive, umami-saturated dip. You can dial the heat across five levels and slide toward a spicy-miso version, but the soul stays the same — a bold, post-drinking bowl finished by stretching the leftover dip with hot soup-wari.

places.highlight: A Kanto-lineage gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen transplanted to central Hiroshima's nightlife district: thick straight noodles meet a small bowl of near-boiling double broth where deep pork-bone body and a heavy dusting of fish powder fuse into one assertive, umami-saturated dip. You can dial the heat across five levels and slide toward a spicy-miso version, but the soul stays the same — a bold, post-drinking bowl finished by stretching the leftover dip with hot soup-wari.

places.name: 焔屋 (Homuraya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is tsukemen made with wheat noodles, and the dipping broth uses soy sauce (wheat-based); there is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The tsukemen broth is meat/fish-based and the shop's dishes feature pork (chashu/charred pork) and likely fish stock; no vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-14:00, 18:00-22:00; Sunday closed.
places.neighborhood: Higashi-Hakushima, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent noodle shop in the Higashi-Hakushima area of central Hiroshima, set in a cream-colored multi-story building. It was previously known under the name Mendo Hakushima-ten before becoming Homuraya.

chef_bio.background: A small independent noodle shop on the ground floor of a pale cream-colored three-story building in the Higashi-Hakushima area of central Hiroshima. It was previously known under the name Mendo Hakushima-ten (めん道白島店) before becoming Homuraya.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (spicy dipping noodles), neatly plated and traditionally garnished with lemon; lunch sets pair the noodles with charred-pork rice, and in the evening the shop shifts into an izakaya serving a wider range of dishes.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the tsukemen as having a clean, crisp, refreshing quality with carefully arranged plating, served Hiroshima-style with a wedge of lemon.
The shop is run as a husband-and-wife operation, known locally for generous portions and good value.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog lists it as 焔屋（旧店名 めん道白島店）, 店主さんご夫妻の2名体制 (husband-and-wife 2-person team) in a 淡いクリーム色の3階建て building, praised for ボリュームとコスパ. Syokuki review highlights 整然とした丁寧な盛り付け, Hiroshima-style tsukemen with lemon, charshu-rice lunch sets, and an evening izakaya menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat noodles, so flour and gluten are present throughout; no gluten-free handling is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsukemen dipping broth uses soy sauce, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes feature pork (charred pork / chashu) and the broth is meat/fish based, so vegan dishes are not separated or available.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, no-frills local noodle shop where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) will go smoothly, but little to no English is likely, so a foreign student should be comfortable with a casual Japanese-only setting.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles sourced from a local noodle maker (Harada Seimen), chilled in ice water before dipping for a cool, springy bite typical of the Hiroshima tsukemen style.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima tsukemen: chilled medium-thin noodles served with boiled cabbage and a cool, soy-based chili dipping sauce, with adjustable heat (regular up through super-spicy levels). Add a chashu-rice or tororo-rice set at lunch.
ramen.chashu: Grilled pork belly slices served alongside the noodles rather than braised in broth.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the dipping tare is soy-sauce (wheat) based; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The cold dipping sauce draws umami from Seto Inland Sea seafood broth and is topped with grilled pork; no documented plant-only broth option.

ramen.pantheon: A clean, crisp take on Hiroshima tsukemen, the city's own cold dipping-noodle tradition: ice-chilled noodles plunged into a soy-based sauce sharpened with chili, vinegar, and sesame and rounded by Seto Inland Sea seafood umami. The soul lives in that bracing sweet-tart-fiery dip rather than a hot broth, eaten with cool boiled cabbage and a squeeze of lemon, with the heat dialed up to taste.

places.highlight: A clean, crisp take on Hiroshima tsukemen, the city's own cold dipping-noodle tradition: ice-chilled noodles plunged into a soy-based sauce sharpened with chili, vinegar, and sesame and rounded by Seto Inland Sea seafood umami. The soul lives in that bracing sweet-tart-fiery dip rather than a hot broth, eaten with cool boiled cabbage and a squeeze of lemon, with the heat dialed up to taste.

places.name: 冠生園 (Kanseien)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu—ramen (wheat noodles), gyoza (wheat wrappers), and Chinese dishes seasoned with soy sauce (typically wheat-based)—is built on wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on meat and seafood dishes (hoikoro/twice-cooked pork, sweet-and-sour pork, ebi chili, char siu, mapo tofu typically made with pork) and uses wheat-based soy sauce. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–14:00, 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sun & holidays closed; occasional irregular closures
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (very close to Hatchobori / Ebisucho / Nagarekawa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, long-running Chinese restaurant in central Hiroshima's Nobori-cho, a short walk from Hatchobori and the Nagarekawa nightlife district. Founded in 1984, it was renovated and reopened in April 2018, but keeps the feel of a personal, neighborhood eatery with just

chef_bio.background: A small, long-running Chinese restaurant in central Hiroshima's Nobori-cho, a short walk from Hatchobori and the Nagarekawa nightlife district. Founded in 1984, it was renovated and reopened in April 2018, but keeps the feel of a personal, neighborhood eatery with about two dozen seats (24) across a counter and a handful of tables.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on fresh, handmade preparation—favoring domestic ingredients where possible, avoiding frozen products, and cooking with canola oil for a lighter, health-minded finish.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style Beijing- and Sichuan-leaning Chinese cooking: mapo tofu, twice-cooked pork (hoikoro), sweet-and-sour pork, ebi chili, eight-treasure stir-fry, gyoza, and Chinese-style noodles. A fast, well-priced weekday lunch set is a draw for nearby workers.

anecdotes.text: A regular diner described the lunch mapo tofu as a gentle, mild version with a striking aroma of green onion, served quickly enough to suit the weekday office crowd, with half-portion options that make it easy for solo diners.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site states 素材はできるだけ国産…冷凍のものは使っておりません and use of canola oil, offering both Beijing and Sichuan cuisine; est. 1984, renovated/reopened April 2018, 24 seats in Nobori-cho near Hatchobori. A blogger describes a mild, green-onion-fragrant lunch mapo tofu served fast with half-portions.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese dishes here are seasoned in the standard way with soy sauce, which is typically wheat-based, and the menu prominently features ramen and gyoza made with wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood Chinese spot used to quick lunch-rush regulars; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a friendly, affordable meal, but expect little to no English.

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles in the Hiroshima chuka-soba mold, served on the softer, lower-hydration side to match a mellow soy-pork broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka-soba (中華そば) — the classic Hiroshima soy-pork-bone bowl, often taken as the lunch set paired with a mini mapo-tofu rice bowl.
ramen.chashu: Thin, modest slices of braised pork laid a few to a bowl in the spare, old-school Hiroshima style — supporting cast rather than centerpiece.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork bone, chicken and seafood stock with pork chashu and fat; no plant-based broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A living torch for Hiroshima's beloved "kotori-kei" (little-bird) chuka-soba lineage: this is the soul of the legendary Suzume shop, carried on here by its own chef after the original closed. You taste that heritage in the gently cloudy soy-and-pork-bone soup, rounded with chicken, vegetables and a whisper of seafood — light, nostalgic, and unmistakably Hiroshima.

places.highlight: A living torch for Hiroshima's beloved "kotori-kei" (little-bird) chuka-soba lineage: this is the soul of the legendary Suzume shop, carried on here by its own chef after the original closed. You taste that heritage in the gently cloudy soy-and-pork-bone soup, rounded with chicken, vegetables and a whisper of seafood — light, nostalgic, and unmistakably Hiroshima.

places.name: めん 呼白 (Men Yohaku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Ramen noodles are wheat-based, and the soy sauce used in the tare contains wheat. The signature soy and iriko (dried anchovy) broths also rely on these wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broths are built on animal stock (pork, chicken) and iriko dried anchovies, and bowls are topped with pork chashu, chicken, and duck. The side "meat rice" is pork. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (often closes earlier when the day's noodles sell out)
places.neighborhood: Nishi-Tokaichimachi, Naka-ku — a short walk from Tokaichimac
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny nine-seat counter ramen shop tucked into a residential back alley in central Hiroshima, opened in late 2021. Despite its hidden location, it has become one of the most talked-about noodle shops in the city, regularly drawing queues at lunchtime.

chef_bio.background: A tiny nine-seat counter ramen shop tucked into a residential back alley in central Hiroshima (Nishi-Tokaichi-cho), opened on 30 November 2021. Despite its hidden location, it has become one of the most talked-about noodle shops in the city, regularly drawing queues at lunchtime.
chef_bio.specialty: Refined, clear-tasting bowls: a chicken-based soy (shoyu) ramen as the main and an iriko (dried anchovy) ramen as the specialty sub, both with thin, smooth, springy noodles. Bowls can be topped with an assortment of meats — rare and roasted pork chashu, chicken, and duck loin — and a popular side rice bowl topped with meat ('meat rice').

anecdotes.text: Despite being hidden in a quiet alley with no parking (only bike parking), the shop draws lines long enough that it sets out waiting chairs and lets customers preview the posted menu outside while they wait.
It was voted number one in a Hiroshima ramen poll of 176 local fans run by a food blogger, with its premium shoyu bowl (特上醤油) singled out for a restrained, layered, umami-rich broth.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Opening-date site (kaiten-heiten) records a 2021-11-30 open; the owner (trained at Tokyo's らーめんMAIKAGURA and Osaka's 麺一盃) built the shop around a 鶏清湯 shoyu main using 長州黒かしわ鶏 with a sub 瀬戸内いりこ ramen. Modokimaru notes a 9-seat counter, outdoor waiting chairs/menu, no car parking, four meats (rare/hung-roast pork, chicken, duck), and a 176-fan poll that ranked it #1 with the 特上醤油 praised for restrained, layered umami.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature bowl is a shoyu (soy sauce) ramen; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, and no wheat-free tare is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and toppings are animal-based (pork, chicken, duck) and use iriko dried anchovies, so there is no separation from animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and dishes are also displayed outside for ordering, so a foreign student can manage easily — just expect to queue and bring cash.

noodles.notes: Slender, smooth and supple noodles built on a multi-flour blend centered on Harukaze/Harugataka wheat, made in-house; texture described as tsuru-tsuru/mochi-mochi and tuned soft-to-springy to carry the delicate clear broth and its aroma.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (shoyu) — chicken-clear chintan bowl with a six-soy blended tare; the iriko soba and the loaded Tokujo Iriko (special iriko, ~¥1,650) are the other must-tries.
ramen.chashu: A quartet of meats over one bowl: a soft rosy rare loin slice, a tsurushi-yaki hung-and-roasted (char-grilled) chashu, a chicken chashu, and a slice of seared duck loin (kamo rosu) — pork plus poultry, each prepared differently so no two bites taste alike.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based fermented-soy (shoyu) tare make every bowl off-limits for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Both broths are animal/fish based — whole-chicken clear soup and a Seto-Inland dried-sardine (iriko) soup — with chicken fat and pork/poultry chashu throughout. No plant-only bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A refined, almost kappo-style take on clear chuka soba: the soul is a whisper-clean chintan drawn from Yamaguchi's Choshu black chicken (and Nagoya Cochin) or, in its sister bowl, pure Seto Inland dried sardine and nothing else, lifted by a tare blending fermented soy sauces from Hiroshima, Shodoshima, Hyogo and Wakayama. It reads as a modern Seto-Inland evolution of light Onomichi/Tokyo shoyu lineage — gentle and translucent on the surface, but with a deep regional-poultry and iriko umami that lingers long after the last sip.

places.highlight: A refined, almost kappo-style take on clear chuka soba: the soul is a whisper-clean chintan drawn from Yamaguchi's Choshu black chicken (and Nagoya Cochin) or, in its sister bowl, pure Seto Inland dried sardine and nothing else, lifted by a tare blending fermented soy sauces from Hiroshima, Shodoshima, Hyogo and Wakayama. It reads as a modern Seto-Inland evolution of light Onomichi/Tokyo shoyu lineage — gentle and translucent on the surface, but with a deep regional-poultry and iriko umami that lingers long after the last sip.

places.name: 桂林 (Keirin)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Chinese restaurant; dishes rely heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, and items such as fried chicken (唐揚げ), yakisoba, and various stir-fries involve wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and seafood (Peking duck, shark-fin soup, seafood yakisoba, fried chicken, ham-and-vegetable sets) and uses wheat soy sauce; while a vegetable stir-fry might be requested, no vegan options are documented and broths/sauces likely contain animal products. Vegans would need to ask carefully in Japanese.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (lunch and dinner service)
places.neighborhood: 広島市中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori)
places.notes: A neighborhood Chinese restaurant founded roughly four decades ago, Keirin has become a fixture in central Hiroshima for affordable, generous home-style Chinese cooking. It occupies a multi-floor building with a nostalgic, old-school feel, including plastic food samples

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood Chinese restaurant founded roughly four decades ago, Keirin has become a fixture in central Hiroshima for affordable, generous home-style Chinese cooking. It occupies a multi-floor building with a nostalgic, old-school feel, including plastic food samples lined up in the storefront window and casual triangular tables near the entrance.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps prices reasonable so anyone can enjoy a hearty plate of Chinese home cooking.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the Chinese lunch set (中華ランチ): a single plate piling stir-fried vegetables and protein around a rounded mound of rice, an affectionately described 'grown-up's children's lunch' that keeps regulars coming back. The broader menu ranges from everyday staples like happosai and fried chicken to fuller dishes such as Peking duck, shark-fin soup, and seafood yakisoba.

anecdotes.text: A local food blogger noted that many old-style neighborhood Chinese restaurants have closed over the years, which makes Keirin's enduring, nostalgic 'Chinese lunch' experience all the more cherished by post-war generations.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Syokuki blogger calls the 中華ランチ a 大人のお子様ランチ (happosai, fried chicken, ham, egg over a rice mound on one plate) and laments that 昔ながらの中華屋 are disappearing; the shop is 42年前に創業した. Ekiten's menu lists フカヒレスープ, 海鮮焼きそば and a full 北京ダック; Tabelog shows a multi-floor building with banquet rooms.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a Chinese kitchen serving fried chicken, noodles, and wheat-soy-sauce dishes, shared fryers and surfaces make cross-contamination very likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Chinese dishes here are seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is wheat-based.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a longstanding local Chinese restaurant where ordering is in Japanese and English menus are unlikely, but the lunch sets are easy to point to and the casual ground floor is comfortable for solo diners.

noodles.notes: Standard chuka-style noodles typical of an old-school Chinese restaurant; exact thickness/shape not documented but presented in the classic medium, lightly wavy chuka soba form.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the house's plain soy noodle bowl in a clear, light broth; a chashu-men (チャーシュー麺) upgrade is offered for more pork topping.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu (standard simmered loin/belly style of a classic Chinese diner); details not individually documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy-based seasoning make this bowl unsuitable for celiacs; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Built on an animal (chicken/pork) broth with pork chashu; no documented plant-based broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: This is the nostalgic Showa-era chuka soba of a long-running neighborhood Chinese kitchen — a clear, gentle soy broth that tastes of simple poultry-and-bone stock rather than any modern specialist's intensity. It belongs to the old-school machi-chuka lineage, where the noodle bowl is one comforting line on a sprawling Cantonese-leaning menu rather than the headline act.

places.highlight: This is the nostalgic Showa-era chuka soba of a long-running neighborhood Chinese kitchen — a clear, gentle soy broth that tastes of simple poultry-and-bone stock rather than any modern specialist's intensity. It belongs to the old-school machi-chuka lineage, where the noodle bowl is one comforting line on a sprawling Cantonese-leaning menu rather than the headline act.

places.name: 流川 餃子センター (Nagarekawa Gyoza Center)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty is gyoza wrapped in thick wheat-flour skins (the operator is even a gyoza-wrapper maker), and the menu centers on ramen, fried rice and Chinese dishes that rely on wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature gyoza are filled with pork, and the menu is built around meat dishes and a green-onion/miso-soy sauce; no vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawacho, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (Ebisucho Stn / Nagarekawa entertainment district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A downtown gyoza-and-Chinese counter shop that has long been a neighborhood fixture in the Nagarekawa district. When the original elderly owner planned to retire and close, the operators behind the well-known Hiroshima gyoza brand 餃子家 龍 stepped in to keep it alive, reno

chef_bio.origin: Nagarekawa, the nightlife heart of central Hiroshima
chef_bio.background: A downtown gyoza-and-Chinese counter shop that has long been a neighborhood fixture in the Nagarekawa district. When the original elderly owner planned to retire and close, the operators behind the well-known Hiroshima gyoza brand 餃子家 龍 stepped in to keep it alive, renovating the small shop and relaunching it on 20 January 2026 so the local institution would not disappear.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserve a beloved neighborhood gyoza shop and its thick-skinned, sauce-forward style rather than let it close with its founder.
chef_bio.specialty: Yaki-gyoza with a thick, hand-quality wrapper griddled to a crisp, chewy finish, served with a distinctive green-onion sauce; gyoza also come boiled and in broth, alongside fried rice and other home-style Chinese plates.

anecdotes.text: The shop was set to close when its aging owner prepared to retire, but the company running the 餃子家 龍 gyoza brand took it over, renovated it, and reopened it on 20 January 2026 to carry on the local institution.
The gyoza are described as having a crisp griddled face yet a chewy wrapper, with a naturally sweet filling and a punchy green-onion-based sauce; they are offered fried, boiled, or in broth.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Modokimaru: 店主の高齢により閉店予定だったが、餃子家龍などを展開する会社が事業を引き継ぎ, renovated and reopened 2026-01-20 in Nagarekawa (中区流川町5-27). Menu: 焼き餃子/水餃子/スープ餃子, 炒飯; thick chewy wrappers, naturally sweet filling, chopped green onion + sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat-skinned gyoza, ramen and fried rice, so flour and wheat-based seasonings are pervasive; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on standard Japanese/Chinese soy-based seasonings, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The gyoza are pork-filled and the menu is meat-centered, making cross-contact with animal products unavoidable; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny downtown counter-and-table gyoza joint in a nightlife district where ordering in basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) is expected, so it suits a foreign student comfortable with a little Japanese rather than a full-English experience.

places.name: 日本料理 児玉 (Nihon Ryori Kodama)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Japanese kaiseki built on dashi and soy sauce (typically wheat-based) plus seasonal seafood; no gluten-free program is documented. A gluten-free diner would need to inquire directly in advance, and accommodation is not guaranteed.
places.vegan_detail: Omakase courses are centered on Seto Inland Sea seafood (abalone, scallop, mackerel sushi) and fish-based dashi, so the menu is not vegan and there is no documented plant-based option.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 20:00); Sat–Sun 11:30–14:00 and 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 20:00); closed Monday, year-end/new year, and mid-August.
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka Ward, Hiroshima (Kanayamacho Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in the early 1950s in Yamaguchi Prefecture (sources give 1951 or 1952), Kodama was inspired by his father, a former naval cook. From age 18 he trained across traditional ryotei, wedding venues, hotels and kappo restaurants, earning the rank of master instructor fro

chef_bio.background: Born in the early 1950s in Yamaguchi Prefecture (sources give 1951 or 1952), Kodama was inspired by his father, a former naval cook. From age 18 he trained across traditional ryotei, wedding venues, hotels and kappo restaurants, earning the rank of master instructor from the Japan Cooking Association, before opening his own house in 2007.
chef_bio.philosophy: "I want to pay attention to every detail within my reach." Kodama emphasizes drawing out each ingredient's full potential through patient, hand-crafted technique.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic seasonal Japanese cuisine drawing on Hiroshima's bounty: Seto Inland Sea seafood such as abalone (served with a miso glaze) and scallop, scallop shinjo mousse, saba (mackerel) sushi, and mountain delicacies of the Chugoku region. Served as omakase courses only.

anecdotes.text: His devotion to slow craft is literal: ko-mochi ayu no kanroni (sweetfish simmered with roe) takes two full days of simmering, and kuri no shibukawa-ni (chestnuts cooked in their inner skin) takes three days to complete.
Run warmly by Kodama and his family, the room keeps a homey, family-like atmosphere while holding a crisp, dignified air.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara profiles Kodama Kazuyuki, b.1951 Yamaguchi, inspired by his ex-naval-cook father; trained from age 18 in ryotei, wedding halls, hotels and kappo, holding Japan Cooking Association master-instructor rank; opened his own restaurant in 2007. His 子持ち鮎の甘露煮 simmers a full two days (bones edible) and 栗の渋皮煮 takes three days; omakase includes abalone with miso, scallop shinjo and saba sushi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese kaiseki of this style relies on dashi and soy sauce, which is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built on seafood and fish-based dashi (abalone, scallop, mackerel), so animal products are integral throughout the omakase.

cultural_comfort.note: A refined family-run kaiseki house serving omakase courses only; reservations are essential and you should expect a Japanese-language experience, so a foreign student would do best with some Japanese ability or a Japanese-speaking companion.

places.name: 平野屋 (Hiranoya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature item is an ice cream monaka, whose wafer shell is made from wheat (and starch), and other listed snacks include a Hiroshima okonomiyaki roll, which is wheat-based. No gluten-free options or allergy accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The headline product is dairy ice cream (vanilla, matcha, chocolate cake, cherry blossom) sandwiched in a monaka wafer, and other items include a meat-wrapped rice ball stick and an okonomiyaki roll. No documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-18:00 (Tabelog); official site lists 10:00-17:00. Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, just past the Karamon gate of Itsukushima Shrine o
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hiranoya sits right where the crowds spill out of Itsukushima Shrine, on the west bank of the little stream that runs through Miyajima's approach. It is a family-run souvenir shop that doubles as a tiny rest stop: alongside shelves of straps, magnets, kokeshi dolls, dar (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Hiranoya sits right where the crowds spill out of Itsukushima Shrine, about a minute from the shrine exit on Miyajima's approach. It is a family-run souvenir shop that doubles as a tiny rest stop: alongside shelves of straps, magnets, kokeshi dolls, daruma and other Miyajima keepsakes, a small counter with around ten seats serves a few sweet treats to give footsore visitors a place to pause.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature is the maple-leaf-shaped ice cream monaka (momiji-man ice monaka): a crisp wafer pressed in the shape of Miyajima's famous momiji-manju and filled to order with ice cream, offered in vanilla, matcha, chocolate cake (gato-chocolat) and cherry blossom. They also sell light snacks like a Hiroshima okonomiyaki roll and a meat-wrapped rice ball stick (each around 400 yen).

anecdotes.text: The shop's calling card is a freshly filled ice cream monaka shaped like a momiji (maple leaf), echoing Miyajima's iconic momiji-manju, served as a warm-crisp wafer around cold ice cream.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site: souvenir shop 厳島神社出口より徒歩1分 with もみじまん・アイスモナカ in バニラ/抹茶/ガトーショコラ/桜 and abundant straps, magnets, kokeshi and daruma. Tabelog confirms ~10 seats inside the souvenir shop and lists 広島お好み焼きロール (¥400) and 肉巻きおにぎり棒 (¥400).

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up souvenir shop and snack counter on the main shrine approach; you can point at the ice cream monaka and a few words of Japanese plus gestures are plenty, though detailed English is not assured.

places.name: とんかつ料理 さち (Tonkatsu Ryori Sachi)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu specialist: cutlets are coated in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the core dishes contain gluten. The signature dipping sauce (grated daikon mixed with a secret tare) is also soy-sauce/Worcestershire-style and not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around deep-fried pork cutlets (including domestic three-breed pork loin sets). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian main dishes; even sides and the tare sauce are not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Sakaimachi (near the Peace Memorial Park, west side
places.notes: Founded in 1975 (Showa 50), Sachi has been frying tonkatsu for local Hiroshima diners for roughly half a century, just west of the Peace Memorial Park in the Sakaimachi neighborhood.

chef_bio.origin: A long-standing independent tonkatsu specialist often cited as part of Hiroshima's tradition of dipping-sauce (tsuke-dare) tonkatsu.
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1975 (Showa 50), Sachi has been frying tonkatsu for local Hiroshima diners for roughly half a century, just west of the Peace Memorial Park in the Sakaimachi neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generous, value-focused home-style frying: refillable rice and cabbage, complimentary self-serve coffee at lunch, and a sauce designed to be lighter than typical heavy Worcestershire styles.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a lighter, refreshing house dipping sauce made by mixing grated daikon radish with a secret tare. The 'Sachi style' is to soak the cutlet in the sauce until the crisp coating just softens before eating. The flagship dish is the Special Loin Cutlet set made with domestic three-breed pork, and rice is the prized Nita-mai from Okuizumo, with free refills of rice and cabbage.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the 'Sachi style' of dipping the cutlet into the daikon-and-secret-tare sauce until the breading softens, a lighter alternative to heavy sauces.
Rice uses Okuizumo's Nita-mai, called 'the grand champion rice of the west,' and rice and cabbage refills are free.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: mypl (Machipla) article: 昭和50年創業, ~half century of tonkatsu; 秘伝のタレに大根おろし (plus a touch of quail-egg yolk), the 'Sachi style' of soaking the cutlet ヒタヒタ until the breading softens; flagship 国産三元豚 Special Loin set (150g ¥1,600); rice is 西の横綱米 奥出雲の仁多米; free rice/cabbage refills and self-serve lunch coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are breaded in wheat flour and panko and deep-fried, so the fryer and core dishes contain gluten; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dipping sauce is a secret soy-sauce/Worcestershire-style tare mixed with grated daikon, which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely pork-cutlet based; no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local tonkatsu shop with no indication of English menus or staff, so a few words of Japanese (and pointing at set-meal photos) will make ordering easiest for a foreign student.

places.name: 和風喫茶 ローン (Wafu Kissa Lawn (Tonkatsu Lawn))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is tonkatsu — pork cutlets breaded in wheat-flour batter and panko, then deep-fried. There is no gluten-free menu, and no documented dedicated fryer or allergen accommodation. Side dishes and sauces (tonkatsu sauce) typically contain wheat. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The menu centers on deep-fried pork cutlets (tonkatsu), and sets come with miso soup (commonly fish/dashi based). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00, 17:30–20:00 (last order applies); closed Sun. Weekday lunch 990 yen. Some sources list evening service until 20:30.
places.neighborhood: Naka Ward, Takanobashi (Kokutaijimachi)
places.notes: The shop opened in 1954 as a Japanese-style café (wafu kissa) and over the decades became beloved by the neighborhood for its tonkatsu, gradually shifting its identity to a tonkatsu specialist while keeping the warm, easygoing café atmosphere.

roles: family-run neighborhood café and tonkatsu house
chef_bio.background: The shop opened in 1954 as a Japanese-style café (wafu kissa) and over the decades became beloved by the neighborhood for its tonkatsu, gradually shifting its identity to a tonkatsu specialist while keeping the warm, easygoing café atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on quality pork and a lighter fry: cutlets are cooked in rice oil for a crisp, less heavy finish, and lunch sets are generous and affordable with free refills of rice and cabbage plus complimentary coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut pork loin tonkatsu (cutlets over 2cm thick), including premium pork such as Kinka pork (kinkaton) and Ishimi pork loin cutlet sets; weekday lunch specials around 990 yen.

anecdotes.text: Began life in 1954 as a coffee shop before becoming known for its tonkatsu, making it a genuine long-running local institution.
Cutlets are fried in rice oil for a crisp texture with lighter oil; lunch comes with free refills of rice and cabbage and a complimentary coffee.
Known locally for very thick cutlets (over 2cm) and an affordable katsu-sando set, with free parking.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Modokimaru: 1954年に喫茶として開店 then a tonkatsu specialist in Kokutaiji-cho; fried in rice oil, 平日ランチ990円, ご飯とキャベツのおかわり1回無料 plus lunch coffee, using Ishimi pork loin among premium pork. Syokuki: 特厚とんかつ2cm以上, a ~500-yen katsu-sando set, 金華豚, free downtown parking, generous refills.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core offering is breaded, deep-fried pork (tonkatsu) using wheat batter and panko, so the kitchen and fryer routinely handle gluten; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat-and-fry focused kitchen with no documented vegan menu; sets include miso soup typically made with fish dashi.

positives.text: Cutlets are fried in rice oil rather than heavier oils, giving a lighter, crisper result (relevant for those avoiding certain oils, though not for gluten or animal-product concerns).

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, longtime local spot where ordering is done by smartphone QR code, so a foreign student with little Japanese can manage, though menus and staff communication are mostly in Japanese.

places.name: ナカノターナーズ 本通り本店 (Nakano Turner's Hondori Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a steak and Western/izakaya restaurant; steaks, salt beef and yakiniku are typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and marinades, and the menu also includes okonomiyaki-style Hiroshima dishes whose batter is wheat. No dedicated GF preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen handling is mentioned on any source. A celiac or strict gluten-free diner should not assume safe options here without direct confirmation in person.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The restaurant is a meat-focused steakhouse — its signature dishes are steak, house-made salt beef, and yakiniku. No vegan or vegetarian menu or accommodations are documented on any source.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 12:00–14:00 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:00–00:00 (L.O. 23:30). Open year-round except Dec 31 and Jan 1. (Tabelog listing notes a Wednesday closure and 11:30 lunch start; Hitosara, the more current source, lists open daily with 12:00 lunch start.)
places.neighborhood: Naka Ward, Tatemachi / Hondori (across from the Hiroshima Kokusai Hotel, ~800 m east of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: Born in 1989 in Hiroshima Prefecture, the head chef and branch manager Sugishima Yasuhiro found his way into cooking through an unexpected door — a part-time job at an okonomiyaki shop. From there he trained formally across several styles, including teppanyaki, traditio

chef_bio.background: Born in 1989 in Hiroshima Prefecture, the head chef and branch manager Sugishima Yasuhiro found his way into cooking through an unexpected door — a part-time job at an okonomiyaki shop. From there he trained formally across several styles, including teppanyaki, traditional Japanese cuisine, and Western cooking, before joining the company behind this restaurant in 2012 and taking over as manager of the Hondori main branch in 2014.
chef_bio.philosophy: He brings together skills cultivated through a deliberately varied culinary background to create the restaurant's hearty, meat-forward dining experience.
chef_bio.specialty: American- and Canadian-style meat cookery — steaks, house-made salt beef, and teppanyaki — drawing on nearly two decades across multiple culinary disciplines.

anecdotes.text: His culinary career began almost by accident, working part-time at an okonomiyaki restaurant before he trained across teppanyaki, Japanese, and Western kitchens.
The house signature is the generous 'Turner's Steak,' served alongside a house-made salt beef, in a multi-level space ranging from an intimate loft to a pet-friendly terrace.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara profile: 杉島保広, b.1989 Hiroshima; got into cooking via an okonomiyaki part-time job, trained in teppanyaki, Japanese and Western cuisine, joined the company in 2012 and became Hondori branch manager in 2014; ~19 years' experience specializing in American/Canadian meat cookery. Restaurant page notes the signature Turner's Steak and house salt beef across a multi-level space.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, popular central-Hiroshima steakhouse and izakaya where a few words of Japanese will help; staff are used to a casual crowd but English support is not documented, so come ready to point at the menu.

places.name: 蕎麦屋香月 (Sobaya Katsuki)
places.gf_detail: The noodles themselves are jūwari (100% buckwheat, no wheat binder), but this is a traditional soba-ya, not a gluten-free kitchen. The dipping tsuyu is made with standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat), and the kitchen and water bath handle nothing as gluten-free. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate utensils, or wheat-free sauce is documented. Not safe for those requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional soba shop whose tsuyu is built on katsuo/fish-based dashi and soy sauce, and the menu features items such as maki-sushi. No vegan-specific menu or preparation is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Dinner service (17:00-21:00) is offered on Monday and Thursday only (excluding holidays). Closed Wednesday. Often sells out and closes early when the day's soba runs out.
places.neighborhood: 中区十日市町 (Tōkaichimachi, Naka-ku), central Hiroshima, near the
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent soba-ya in the Tōkaichimachi neighborhood of central Hiroshima, west of the Peace Memorial Park. The shop has built a devoted local following and is regularly named among the city's best soba destinations.

chef_bio.background: A small, independent soba-ya in the Tōkaichimachi neighborhood of central Hiroshima, west of the Peace Memorial Park. The shop has built a devoted local following and is regularly named among the city's best soba destinations.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner keeps the dining room deliberately uncrowded, spacing the tables to give each guest a calm, unhurried meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Jūwari soba — noodles made from 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder. Diners choose between 'white' soba (marunuki, from hulled buckwheat for a smoother, more delicate noodle) and 'black' soba (hikigurumi, whole-grain, with a pronounced buckwheat aroma and a firmer, chewier bite). Hand-rolled maki-sushi is a noted side.

anecdotes.text: The noodles are so sought-after that one writer reported being turned away on multiple visits because the shop had already sold out and closed for the day — they pour the day's batch of fresh juwari and stop when it is gone.
Regulars debate white vs black: the whole-grain black soba delivers the fullest buckwheat fragrance and a firm chew, while the white is prized for a smoother finish.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Japanese food blogs (nisinihonwalker, modokimaru) describe a Tokaichimachi jūwari soba shop with ~16-18 generously spaced table seats, black (whole-grain) and white soba, a sell-out/close-when-gone model, and a 3-piece maki-sushi side; owner name not published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a traditional soba shop, not a gluten-free kitchen; even though the noodles are 100% buckwheat, the dipping sauce uses wheat-containing soy sauce and there is no documented separation for gluten-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu (dipping sauce) is standard Japanese soy-sauce-based, which contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tsuyu and dashi are fish/katsuo-based and the menu includes maki-sushi, so dishes are not vegan and no plant-based alternatives are documented.

positives.text: Noodles are jūwari (100% buckwheat, no wheat flour binder), so the soba itself contains no wheat — though it is prepared in a kitchen that handles wheat-based sauces.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, popular local soba shop with no English menu noted — a little basic Japanese (or pointing at the white vs black soba) helps, and arrive early since they close once the day's noodles sell out.

places.name: DASHIとSOBA 水車 (DASHI to SOBA Suisha)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The noodles are described as smooth Sarashina-style soba, which mixes buckwheat with wheat flour, and tempura uses a wheat batter. The standard dashi is also made with bonito and finished with soy sauce (which contains wheat). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated noodle pot, and no allergen protocol published. Celiac travelers should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Has a genuine, dedicated plant-based menu line ("プラントベース"/vegan zaru soba) using an animal-free dashi made from kombu, shiitake mushrooms and vegetables instead of the regular bonito-based broth — staff-developed specifically so guests who do not eat animal products (and Muslim/halal-conscious diners) can eat here. Note the standard menu (oyster soba, tempura soba, most warm-broth dishes) uses bonito/fish dashi and is not vegan, so order specifically from the plant-based items. The vegan offering is broth-and-noodles; the noodles themselves still contain wheat, so it is vegan-friendly but not gluten-free.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun, holidays, days before holidays, and days after holidays 11:00 - 21:30 (closures follow Ekie's schedule)
places.neighborhood: Matsubara-cho, Minami-ku — inside the ekie NORTH retail area
places.notes: Suisha is run by Mikoshiya Foods Co., Ltd. (三吉屋食品株式会社), a local Hiroshima soba operator. The original 'Suisha' soba house opened in the Aqua Hiroshima Center-gai arcade on October 18, 1995, and this Hiroshima Station branch inside ekie opened on March 29, 2018, later re

chef_bio.background: Suisha is run by the Hiroshima food company 三吉屋食品株式会社. The original 'Suisha' soba house opened in the Aqua Hiroshima Center-gai arcade on October 18, 1995, and this Hiroshima Station branch inside ekie opened on March 29, 2018, later refreshed into its current 'DASHI to SOBA' concept with new lattice woodwork by a local carpenter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated motto is 気軽に気楽に気兼ねなく — to be enjoyed 'casually, comfortably, without holding back' — positioning it as an approachable, family-friendly soba stop at the station.
chef_bio.specialty: Dashi-forward soba: the kitchen grinds and brews its broth fresh each morning, working with eight distinct stocks (kombu dashi, white dashi, soba dashi, joujiru, soba-gaeshi, don-gaeshi, don dashi and tempura-don dashi). The base first-press dashi blends three kinds of bonito shavings from waters off Kumamoto and Nagasaki with Rishiri kombu from Hokkaido, finished in a slightly sweet Kansai style the shop notes is unusual for Hiroshima. The smooth Sarashina-style noodles are paired with tempura, and signature plates include ten-zaru soba, a refreshing Setouchi lemon soba, and an evening sake-and-soba 'banshaku' set.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen developed an animal-free plant-based dashi (kombu, shiitake and vegetables) so that guests who avoid animal products and Muslim/halal-conscious travelers can enjoy the same soba; the plant-based zaru soba is offered at both the ekie and the Aqua Center-gai branches.
The shop's plant-based soba has been featured in an English-language vegetarian guidebook published by the Chugoku Economic Federation.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own site (fusha-suisha.net) and its ekie page confirm operation by 三吉屋食品, the 1995 Aqua Center-gai and 2018 ekie openings, the 気軽に気楽に気兼ねなく motto, eight house stocks, a slightly sweet Kansai-style first dashi (Kumamoto/Nagasaki katsuo + Rishiri kombu), Sarashina noodles, and a kombu-shiitake-vegetable plant-based dashi for vegan/halal guests featured in a Chugoku Economic Federation vegetarian guidebook.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sarashina-style soba noodles are made from buckwheat blended with wheat flour and are boiled in shared water; no gluten-free noodle or dedicated pot is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The regular dashi/tare is finished with soy sauce and the kitchen runs multiple soy-based stocks (soba-gaeshi, don-gaeshi, etc.); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The plant-based dashi is animal-free, but it is prepared in the same kitchen that runs bonito-based broths and shared noodle-boiling water; no separate-equipment or cross-contact protocol is published.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Only general guidance is given: bringing homemade food for infants/preschoolers with allergies is allowed with prior consultation; no allergen chart or formal allergy-handling protocol is published.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated, staff-developed plant-based (vegan) soba using a kombu-shiitake-vegetable dashi with no animal ingredients, explicitly aimed at guests who cannot eat animal products and at international visitors.
The plant-based menu is described as halal-conscious ('ハラール対応'), though no formal halal certification is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in noodle shop right inside Hiroshima Station with picture-friendly menus and a relaxed "come as you are" attitude; a foreign student can comfortably point-and-order, though staff communication will mostly be in Japanese.

places.name: 朝日珈琲サロン 胡町店 (Asahi Coffee Salon Ebisucho)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a traditional coffeehouse whose food centers on toast, sandwiches, cakes and omelet-curry — all wheat-based. No dedicated GF menu or cross-contamination handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. Morning sets and dishes feature eggs, dairy, natto with dried shrimp, and cakes; no documented vegan options. Black coffee is the only reliably plant-based item.
places.hours_raw: 08:00-19:00 daily; morning service 08:00-11:30
places.neighborhood: 八丁堀・胡町 (Hatchobori / Ebisucho), Naka-ku, Hiroshima
places.notes: Asahi Coffee Salon was founded in 1950 by the company that still runs it (朝日コーヒーショップ), and this Ebisucho branch opened in 1968 in the basement of a building beneath the bustling Ebisu shopping street in central Hiroshima. Stepping down from the busy street, guests find

chef_bio.background: Asahi Coffee Salon was founded in 1950 by the company that still runs it (朝日コーヒーショップ), and this Ebisucho branch opened in 1968 in the basement of a building beneath the bustling Ebisu shopping street in central Hiroshima. Stepping down from the busy street, guests find a hushed, Showa-era interior that has kept its vintage character for decades, complete with antique touches.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop operates under the spirit of "心贈ります" (offering heart/sincerity), aiming to give each guest thoughtful service and a sense of calm, and on serving nutritionally balanced morning meals to energize locals.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-drip original-blend coffee with a deep, easy-drinking flavor, served alongside generous morning sets, cakes, and retro café fare such as omelet curry.

anecdotes.text: All the tables and chairs are handcrafted in a traditional style meant to help guests relax, and the calm Showa atmosphere stands in deliberate contrast to the busy street above.
A signature "Health Set" morning (750 yen) tops toast with shiso leaf and natto mixed with dried shrimp and green onion, reflecting the shop's focus on nourishing breakfasts.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The official site and Tabelog confirm a 1950 company founding, the 1968 basement branch in Horikawa-cho/Ebisu near the Hatchobori stop, the 心贈ります motto, custom-made tables and chairs, hand-drip original blend, omelet curry, and a 750-yen shiso-and-natto Health Set morning.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed Showa-era basement coffeehouse where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you through; little English is expected, but the calm atmosphere and simple coffee-and-toast offerings make it easy for a foreign student.

places.name: 喫茶めくる (Kissa Mekuru)
