places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a toast-and-baked-goods kissaten — morning sets center on wheat toast and French toast, and the signature item is a flour-adjacent pudding; bread is the core of the menu, so it is not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan place. The menu is built around toast with butter, boiled eggs, yogurt, bacon/scrambled eggs, and pudding (egg/dairy), so most morning and cafe items contain dairy and egg. A plain black coffee or tea works, and one might ask about toast without butter, but there is no documented vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: Sources disagree. Tabelog lists Mon–Sat 08:30–14:30, closed Sunday. One review blog lists morning 08:30–10:30 then 11:00–16:00, closed Sundays/holidays plus irregular closures. An older cafe blog lists 08:00–18:00 closed Wednesday. The Sunday-closed, early-afternoon-close pattern appears in the more recent sources.
places.neighborhood: 十日市町 (Tokaichimachi), Naka-ku — about a 5-minute walk west o
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, much-loved neighborhood coffee shop in Tokaichimachi, a short walk west of the Peace Memorial Park, opened in February 2012 and run by its owner with a small staff. The interior was renovated with bare concrete floors and plaster walls, then warmed with dried f

chef_bio.background: A small, much-loved neighborhood coffee shop in Tokaichimachi, a short walk west of the Peace Memorial Park, opened in February 2012 and run by its owner with a small staff. The interior was renovated with bare concrete floors and plaster walls, then warmed with dried flowers hung from the ceiling, wicker baskets, postcards, and vintage school-style chairs, giving it a nostalgic, cozy feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, healing space meant to let visitors slow down and relax over a simple, carefully made breakfast.
chef_bio.specialty: Morning sets served until early afternoon: thick buttered toast (including a butter-cinnamon-sugar version) or French toast with a boiled egg, thick homemade-style yogurt, and a small vegetable soup, presented on aluminum trays that evoke a school lunch. The firm-set house pudding (めくるプリン) with bitter caramel is a signature.

anecdotes.text: The name めくる means 'to turn pages,' echoed by a small library of books in the shop for customers to leaf through.
Simple-looking plates show real care: the yogurt is thick and creamy and the vegetable soup is described as having flavor seeped all the way through.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Kotogurashi, modokimaru and Tabelog confirm a Tokaichimachi kissaten opened February 10, 2012, run by the owner with one staff member (~20 seats), a renovated bare-concrete/plaster interior with school chairs and dried flowers, aluminum-tray morning sets with cinnamon-sugar toast, the 'to turn pages' name with in-shop books, and the firm めくるプリン with bitter caramel.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, homey neighborhood coffee shop run by the owner and one or two staff; little to no English is documented, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite approach will make ordering the morning set easy.

places.name: ゲート・イン (T's Cafe Gate In)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings documented. As a traditional kissaten serving parfaits, cakes, toast, sandwiches, pasta, and rice-plate lunches, wheat is present throughout the menu (bread, pastries, parfait components, batters). No dedicated gluten-free preparation or labeling is mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The signature draw is fresh-milk soft-serve parfaits, and the lunch bento centers on meat dishes; coffee-shop fare relies on dairy and eggs. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); closed Wednesdays, also 12/31 and 1/1
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gate In is a retro kissaten (classic Japanese coffee house) that opened in 1976 in the Otemachi district of central Hiroshima, just steps from the Kamiyacho-nishi tram stop and within easy walking distance of Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. For roughly half a c

chef_bio.background: Gate In is a retro kissaten (classic Japanese coffee house) that opened in 1976 in the Otemachi district of central Hiroshima, just steps from the Kamiyacho-nishi tram stop and within easy walking distance of Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. For roughly half a century it has held its prime corner near the Edion twin towers, keeping the calm, settled atmosphere of an old-school cafe.
chef_bio.philosophy: A long-running local gathering spot that has quietly watched over the neighborhood and its children for decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fresh-fruit parfaits built on house fresh-milk soft-serve ice cream, with rotating offerings such as fig and Earl Grey, Shine Muscat, and Hiroshima-lemon parfaits; also known for daily made-to-order lunch bento.

anecdotes.text: A decorative white horse holding a cup stands at the entrance as the shop's landmark. Inside it hides an encouraging message that children can only read once they have grown tall enough to peer in - 'You've grown up' - a small token of the cafe watching over neighborhood kids as they grow.
The cafe is famous locally for its photogenic seasonal parfaits made with premium fruit like Shine Muscat and Hiroshima lemon over smooth fresh-milk soft serve, drawing a steady following.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Pecomag and Tabelog confirm a 1976 Otemachi kissaten near the Kamiyacho-nishi tram, the white-horse landmark with a hidden 'you've grown up' message readable only once children grow tall enough, seasonal fresh-milk soft-serve fruit parfaits (fig/Earl Grey, Shine Muscat, Hiroshima lemon), and made-to-order lunch bento.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, classic neighborhood coffee house where a foreign student can comfortably point at the parfait menu and get by with a few Japanese greetings; English support is not advertised.

places.name: フルフル (Furufuru)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffeehouse whose signature items are toast, sandwiches, and grilled sandwiches, all made with wheat bread; the house-made pudding also is not gluten-free. There is no gluten-free menu or documented gluten-free accommodation. The only naturally gluten-free items are the fresh-pressed seasonal fruit juices and coffee/tea.
places.vegan_detail: A retro coffeehouse centered on sandwiches, ham toast, buttered toast, and a dairy-and-egg-based house-made pudding topped with whipped cream, so it is not vegan-oriented and no vegan menu is documented. Fresh-pressed single-fruit juices (strawberry, golden kiwi, plum, grape, peach, pear, etc.) are the only items likely to be plant-based, but vegan handling is not confirmed and should be asked about on site.
places.hours_raw: 09:00 - 19:30 (Tue-Sun). Closed Mondays. Morning set served until 11:00.
places.neighborhood: Mikawacho, just off Namiki-dori in central Naka-ku, a short
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Furufuru is a long-running retro coffeehouse tucked just off Namiki-dori in central Hiroshima, on the ground floor of a distinctive red-brick building marked by a blue-and-white striped awning at the door. It has been pouring coffee here for around half a century, openi

chef_bio.origin: Central Hiroshima, Naka-ku (Mikawacho, near the Hondori/Namiki-dori shopping streets)
chef_bio.background: Furufuru is a long-running retro coffeehouse tucked just off Namiki-dori in central Hiroshima's Mikawacho, on the ground floor of a distinctive red-brick building marked by a blue-and-white striped awning at the door. It opened in 1973 (Shōwa 48) and has been pouring coffee here for around half a century.
chef_bio.specialty: Morning sets and freshly made sandwiches, plus seasonal fresh-pressed single-fruit juices that rotate through strawberry, golden kiwi, plum, grape, peach, and pear, and a beloved house-made pudding crowned with whipped cream.

anecdotes.text: The small room is wrapped in a vintage European mood: every window is set with stained glass, the walls wear fabric-like patterned wallpaper, and soft jazz keeps the air calm and unhurried.
Doors open at 9 a.m. with an affordable morning set served until 11, pairing a little fruit with toast, sandwiches, or a grilled sandwich and coffee or tea.
The house-made pudding is a signature: smooth and gently sweet under a generous cap of whipped cream.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog gives a 1973 (Shōwa 48) opening for an 18-seat Mikawacho kissaten; tabetainjya describes the red-brick building with a blue-and-white striped awning, all-stained-glass windows, jazz, a 9-11am morning set, rotating single-fruit fresh juices (strawberry, golden kiwi, plum, grape, peach, La France pear), and a house pudding under whipped cream. Owner name not published.

positives.text: Seasonal fresh-pressed single-fruit juices are available, which are naturally plant-based and gluten-free, though preparation handling is not documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, old-school neighborhood coffeehouse where no English is expected and the menu is in Japanese, so a foreign student should come prepared to point at items or use a translation app and follow the quiet, relaxed pace of the room.

places.name: ボラボラ 立町店 (Bora Bora Tatemachi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era coffee house whose food menu is built on toast, sandwiches, hotcakes and similar light fare — all wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free preparation, no dedicated equipment, and no indication of GF awareness. Not a viable option for those who must strictly avoid gluten; at most one could order a black coffee or cream soda.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg, cheese and ham toast, hotcakes and house-made dressing — egg- and dairy-heavy with no documented vegan dishes. Black coffee and cream soda (syrup + soda) may be incidentally plant-based, but there is no stated vegan offering or accommodation. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 07:00–19:30; Sat 07:00–17:30; Sun & holidays 08:00–17:30. Morning service 07:00–11:00 (Sun/holidays 08:00–11:00). (Some sources list closing as 19:00/17:00; Tabelog lists 19:30/17:30.)
places.neighborhood: 広島市中区立町 (Tatemachi), central Hiroshima — a 3-minute walk fro
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Bora Bora opened in 1976 and has remained a fixture of central Hiroshima's coffee-house culture for nearly half a century. The 'Tatemachi branch' name dates from a time when the shop had more than one location. Tucked along a lane off Hondori near the Tatemachi tram sto

chef_bio.background: Bora Bora opened in 1976 and has remained a fixture of central Hiroshima's coffee-house culture for nearly half a century. The 'Tatemachi branch' name dates from a time when the shop had more than one location. Tucked along a lane off Hondori near the Tatemachi tram stop, it keeps the unhurried Showa-era spirit alive, opening early at 7 a.m. for the morning crowd.
chef_bio.philosophy: A classic 'junkissa' approach: quality coffee, simple light fare, and a calm, nostalgic room where regulars can linger over a newspaper.
chef_bio.specialty: Morning toast sets (plain, cheese, or ham toast with salad and coffee), plus longtime favorites like hotcakes and a vivid cream soda. The house-made salad dressing became so popular with regulars that the shop began bottling and selling it separately.

anecdotes.text: Step inside and you find a brick-walled interior you would never guess from the street, with a striking blue-tiled roof feature carried indoors, green-tiled table seating and matching lamps, and a small kamidana (household shrine) high on the wall.
The house dressing proved so popular that regulars' demand led the shop to sell it in bottles to take home.
The morning fare is light and unfussy — toast sets, hotcakes and a bright cream soda — true to a traditional coffee house, served from 7 a.m. in a coffee-scented, relaxing room.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Junkissa.blog, morning-hiroshima and modokimaru confirm a 1976 founding, the 立町店 name as a remnant of former multiple locations, a brick interior with blue-tile roof feature, green-tile seating, matching lamps and a kamidana, a 7am opening, plain/cheese/ham toast sets, hotcakes and cream soda, and a house salad dressing sold in bottles by customer demand.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming neighborhood retro café where a foreign student can comfortably order a simple morning set or coffee, but the staff are an older couple and menus/conversation are in Japanese, so a little preparation or a translation app helps.

places.name: 小料理屋 永山 (Koryoriya Nagayama)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen runs on Japanese soy sauce (wheat-based) and the miso soups and seasoned side dishes use soy-sauce/miso preparations; some onigiri fillings (shrimp tempura, corn tempura) are battered in wheat, and the menu rotates without published allergen handling. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented. Plain salt onigiri and umeboshi (pickled plum) onigiri with nori are inherently low-gluten items, but cross-contamination from a shared kitchen cannot be ruled out — a celiac traveler should not rely on this place.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The house dashi is made from bonito (katsuobushi), tuna, and kombu, so even vegetable-forward miso soups and side dishes contain fish stock. Miso soup varieties include pork (tonjiru-style) broths, and popular onigiri fillings are grilled mackerel, mentaiko (pollock roe), and seasoned ground meat. While the menu emphasizes fresh vegetables, there is no documented animal-free preparation, so it does not suit vegan or strict vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Sat morning 7:30-10:00; Lunch Mon-Sat 10:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner Wed-Thu 18:00-21:00, Fri-Sat 18:00-22:00. Closed Sundays (and Mondays that fall on national holidays). Note: hours vary somewhat across listings; the official site shows no Mon/Tue dinner service.
places.neighborhood: 中区胡町 (Kuchimachi/Kocho), central Hiroshima near the Ginzan-c
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An intimate roughly-15-seat washoku spot run by owner-chef Ryunosuke Nagayama. It began life as an evening izakaya, but during the COVID-19 pandemic the owner started serving morning and lunch sets, and the daytime onigiri-and-miso-soup teishoku grew so popular that lin

chef_bio.background: An intimate roughly-15-seat washoku spot run by owner-chef Ryunosuke Nagayama. It began life as an evening izakaya, but during the COVID-19 pandemic the owner started serving morning and lunch sets, and the daytime onigiri-and-miso-soup teishoku grew so popular that lines now form regularly.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef cooks around peak-season produce, noting that an ingredient's true peak is fleeting, and keeps seasoning simple and refined to draw out vegetable umami. The house dashi is built carefully from bonito, tuna (maguro-bushi), and kombu, and the kitchen leans into the benefits of fermented foods like miso.
chef_bio.specialty: An onigiri-and-miso-soup teishoku lunch: diners choose an onigiri (salt, umeboshi, mentaiko, grilled mackerel, shrimp or corn tempura, seasonal mushroom, or seasoned meat) and a miso soup (pork tonjiru-style, seasonal vegetable, and rotating creations), served with about five small vegetable-forward side dishes; additional onigiri can be added.

anecdotes.text: The lunch service was born out of the pandemic pivot: the formerly evening-only izakaya added morning and lunch sets and became a queue-forming local favorite.
The owner sources produce that does not appear in supermarkets, such as premium Hokkaido tomatoes, and builds custom broth ratios for each day's miso soup.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's site plus pecomag and tabiiro confirm owner-chef 永山龍之助, ~14-15 seats, an evening izakaya that added COVID-era morning/lunch service now drawing queues, an onigiri (salt, umeboshi, mentaiko, grilled mackerel, shrimp/corn tempura, mushroom, seasoned meat) and miso-soup teishoku with five side dishes and a bonito/tuna/kombu dashi, plus produce such as premium Hokkaido tomatoes not sold in supermarkets. Extra onigiri are addable (for an extra charge).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared home-style kitchen with wheat-based soy sauce, miso preparations, and battered tempura onigiri fillings; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Side dishes and seasonings use Japanese soy sauce (standard shoyu is wheat-based), e.g. blanched bitter melon dressed in ponzu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: House dashi is made from bonito, tuna, and kombu, so vegetable miso soups and side dishes still contain fish stock; some miso soups also include pork.

positives.text: Menu emphasizes fresh seasonal vegetables and several side dishes, and plain salt or umeboshi onigiri with nori are simple options, though none are prepared as certified allergen-free.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, locals-favorite counter spot where staff likely speak mostly Japanese and lunch queues form, so a few basic Japanese phrases and patience go a long way for a foreign student.

places.name: ピース ブルバード (Peace Blvd)
places.gf_detail: The core appeal is rice (onigiri), which is naturally gluten-free, and the prized Okuizumo Nita-mai (仁多米) is the centerpiece. However, sets are built around Japanese sides and dishes that commonly use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso, and listed mains such as fried oysters (kaki-fry) and grilled anago use wheat batter/sauce. No gluten-free protocol, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented, so a strictly gluten-free diner would need to ask in detail and verify each component.
places.vegan_detail: Set meals are centered on fish and meat (e.g., simmered mackerel/saba, fried oysters, grilled anago) and use dashi/miso/soy-based seasonings, so the standard offerings are not vegan. Plain salted onigiri rice and some vegetable sides (e.g., spinach with white-miso dressing) may be plant-forward, but no vegan menu, vegan dashi, or animal-free preparation is documented; a vegan diner would need to ask whether any plant-only combination can be assembled.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Sat/Sun/holidays: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Note: at the time of one report the cafe was operating shortened hours; normal hours were expected to run later.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, near Peace Memorial Pa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Peace Blvd opened on June 1, 2020 as an onigiri cafe attached to the flagship shop of Nitaya, a Hiroshima onigiri specialist. The building's ground floor pairs two storefronts: the takeout-only Onigiri Nitaya Otemachi main shop on one side and the eat-in Peace Blvd on t

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima (Otemachi, Naka-ku); the rice comes from Okuizumo in Shimane.
chef_bio.background: Peace Blvd opened on June 1, 2020 as an onigiri cafe attached to the flagship shop of Nitaya, a Hiroshima onigiri specialist. The building's ground floor pairs two storefronts: the takeout-only Onigiri Nitaya Otemachi main shop on one side and the eat-in Peace Blvd on the other, where the same rice balls are served as proper sit-down set meals. The name nods to the shop's location near Peace Memorial Park and Peace Boulevard.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let the rice be the star: the onigiri are salted to highlight the rice's natural sweetness, with the quality of the Nita-mai itself treated as the main attraction rather than elaborate fillings.
chef_bio.specialty: Onigiri set meals built on prized Okuizumo Nita-mai rice, served as a daily-changing lunch with a main, sides and miso soup; the shop also offers a fuller Nitaya onigiri zen, plus sweets such as parfaits and fondant chocolat.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers single out the rice itself as the real draw, describing the salting as balanced to bring out the rice's sweetness and the rice as genuinely delicious.
The eat-in cafe sits beside the takeout-only Nitaya Otemachi main shop, which supplies its onigiri and bento, letting visitors near Peace Park enjoy the same rice balls as a relaxed set meal.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabetainjya and modokimaru confirm Peace Blvd opened June 1, 2020 as the eat-in counterpart to the takeout-only Onigiri Nitaya Otemachi main shop, serving set meals built on Okuizumo Nita-mai rice salted to highlight its sweetness, plus a Nitaya onigiri zen and sweets like parfaits and fondant chocolat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals feature Japanese dishes such as simmered mackerel and white-miso-dressed vegetables, which in standard Japanese cooking use soy sauce and miso that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

positives.text: The cafe centers on rice and onigiri made from high-grade Okuizumo Nita-mai, and plain salted rice balls are naturally gluten-free even though the surrounding set components are not.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming onigiri set-meal spot a short walk from the Peace Park where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the daily lunch will get you a friendly, easy meal; solo diners are comfortable at the counter.

places.name: もん 八丁堀店 (MON Hatchobori-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-bread bakery; the signature gratin rings, curry rings, sandwiches, croissants and loaves are all made from wheat flour, so there are no gluten-free options. No gluten-free products or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on bread and cafe dishes using dairy, eggs and meat — the house specialties are a gratin ring (cream/cheese roux) and curry ring, plus hot sandwiches, omelette rice, doria and parfaits. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 10:00-19:30 (L.O. 19:00); Sat 10:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:00); closed Sundays and holidays. (Official site lists a daily 9:00-18:00 cafe service window with a Thursday 14:00-15:00 break; lunch 11:00-14:30, afternoon menu 14:30-17:00, takeout 10:00-17:00.)
places.neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] MON is a long-established Hiroshima bakery founded in 1953 (described as a roughly 65-year-old shop). From its earliest days it has baked bread in a French stone-bed (stone-floor) oven, aiming for a crisp crust and a moist, tender crumb.

chef_bio.background: MON is a long-established Hiroshima bakery founded in 1953 (described as a roughly 65-year-old shop). From its earliest days it has baked bread in a French stone-bed (stone-floor) oven, aiming for a crisp crust and a moist, tender crumb.
chef_bio.philosophy: The bakery wants its bread to be eaten as a staple in place of rice, so it puts particular care into its loaf breads, choosing select ingredients and baking slowly over time.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature item is the gratin ring (a ring-shaped bread filled with house-made roux); the curry ring and curry doughnut are also popular, and all the roux fillings are made in-house. At the Hatchobori branch, bread is baked at the main Minamitakeya location and delivered within about 15 minutes, offering 30-40 varieties daily.

anecdotes.text: The Hatchobori branch was designed with a softer white-and-pink interior and offers pasta lunch sets and omelette rice not available at the main shop, aimed at welcoming female customers.
The shop has used a French stone-bed oven since its founding to give the bread a crisp exterior and moist interior.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: MON's official site and Tabelog confirm a 1953 (Shōwa 28) founding, a French stone-bed oven used since founding for a crisp-crust/moist-crumb loaf, a bread-as-staple philosophy, the signature gratin ring with house-made roux (plus curry ring and curry doughnut), and a Hatchobori branch with a white-and-pink interior, pasta/omelette-rice lunches, and 30-40 daily varieties baked at the Minamitakeya main shop and delivered within ~15 minutes.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only neighborhood bakery-cafe with no indication of English support, so a foreign student would do best with a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend to navigate the menu.

places.name: 吉鮨 (Yoshizushi)
places.gf_detail: Edomae sushi here is finished with wheat-based soy sauce (nikiri) and uses cooked/cured fish; no gluten-free program is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: An omakase sushi counter built around fish and seafood, with wheat-soy seasonings throughout. No vegan offering is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00–22:00; closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A Hiroshima native who fell for cooking as a child and discovered sushi during his university years, Yoshimura trained in Tokyo to learn the techniques and discipline of Edomae sushi before returning home. He opened his own counter at age 35 and has run it for nearly th

chef_bio.background: A Hiroshima native who fell for cooking as a child and discovered sushi during his university years, Yoshimura trained in Tokyo to learn the techniques and discipline of Edomae sushi before returning home. He opened his own counter at age 35 and has run it for decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: He works by the motto 'opening for the first time today' (本日開店) — approaching each day's service with fresh sincerity, aiming for craftsmanship and hospitality that leave a lasting impression on guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae sushi using seasonal fish from the Seto Inland Sea and the Sea of Japan, prepared with classic techniques of curing (shime), marinating (zuke), and aging (nekasu). The rice is seasoned with red vinegar and salt rather than sugar, to draw out its natural sweetness and umami.

anecdotes.text: Ingredients are chosen by the chef himself, with the day's fish selected fresh before service.
Yoshimura opened the restaurant at age 35 after his Tokyo apprenticeship and has built it into one of the region's most respected sushi counters.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara and the shop's gorp site confirm chef 吉村憲治, a Hiroshima native who discovered sushi at university, trained in Tokyo, opened his own counter at 35, the 本日開店 motto, a 9-10-seat counter, shari seasoned with only salt and red vinegar (no sugar), seasonal Seto Inland Sea/Sea of Japan fish, and 〆る・漬ける・ねかせる (curing/marinating/aging) techniques.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Edomae nigiri is conventionally brushed with nikiri (a wheat-based soy sauce reduction); no gluten-free soy sauce is documented for this restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, higher-end omakase sushi counter where the chef sets the pace; some Japanese-language ability and reservation etiquette help, and it suits a special occasion rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: キュイエール (Cuillere)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The kitchen centers on house-made bread sandwiches at lunch and French dishes at dinner, so wheat is pervasive; a celiac/GF traveler must ask directly and should not assume safe options exist.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant's own site states a vegan/vegetarian menu is available (ビーガンメニュー取り揃えております), but no dish-level detail or ingredient/cross-contact information is published. Confirm specifics when booking.
places.neighborhood: 宮島 (Miyajima)
places.notes: [Miyajima] A French bistro tucked away in a quiet back alley off Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the island's pier. The owner-chef trained in France and was inspired by the regional dishes and traditional confections encountered during that apprenticeship.

chef_bio.background: A French bistro tucked away in a quiet back alley off Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, about a seven-minute walk from the island's ferry pier. The owner-chef trained in France and was inspired by the regional dishes and traditional confections encountered during that apprenticeship.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcase local and seasonal ingredients through honest, unhurried French cooking, with everything from the bread up made with care.
chef_bio.specialty: By day, sandwiches built on house-made bread with carefully chosen fillings; by night, leisurely, authentic French cooking. Dishes draw on Hiroshima and Japanese seasonal ingredients and have included oysters with herb butter, seasonal fish papillote, Setouchi mussels marinière, beignets, and seasonal fruit tarts. A notable lunch item is an an-butter sandwich pairing house-made red-bean bread with red bean paste from Iwamura Momiji-ya, a long-established Miyajima momiji-manju shop.

anecdotes.text: The name Cuillere is French for spoon.
An an-butter sandwich pairs house-made red-bean bread with red bean paste from Iwamura Momiji-ya, a long-established Miyajima momiji-manju maker, tying the bistro to the island's local food culture.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's official site and tabiiro describe an owner-chef who trained in France (inspired by regional dishes and confections), a bistro in an alley off Omotesando ~7 minutes from the Miyajima pier, sandwiches on house-made bread by day and French cooking by night (oyster with herb butter, seasonal fish papillote, Setouchi mussels marinière, beignets, seasonal fruit tarts), and an an-butter sandwich using red bean paste from the long-established Miyajima momiji-manju shop Iwamura Momiji-ya. Owner name not published.

positives.text: A vegan/vegetarian menu is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, France-trained-chef bistro tucked in a Miyajima back alley; a foreign student can manage with basic greetings, though a reservation via the website (which supports some English) makes a visit smoother.

places.name: 銀山ベース (Kanayama Base)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The signature dishes are breaded-and-fried katsu sandwiches (wheat bread and wheat-flour coating) and sandwiches generally, so the core menu is wheat-based. Sauces likely use standard wheat-containing soy sauce. Treat as not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. The menu centers on meat (steak plates, pork-cutlet katsu sandwiches) and a coffee/bar lineup; nothing on the page indicates plant-based dishes. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun 08:00-22:00; Thursday closed. (One local write-up notes hours can be irregular.)
places.neighborhood: Ginzan-cho, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Peace Memorial Park area,
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, casual American-style cafe and bar tucked into the Ginzan-cho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, an easy walk from the Hatchobori area and within the Peace Memorial Park orbit. It sits right by the Ginzan-cho tram stop near the Kyobashi River, in a low-key loca

chef_bio.background: A small, casual American-style cafe and bar in the Ginzan-cho (銀山町) neighborhood of central Hiroshima's Naka-ku, an easy walk from the Hatchobori area. It sits right by the Ginzancho tram stop, with the Kyobashi River just to the east, in a low-key local pocket rather than a tourist strip.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty, value-minded plates with an American-casual feel: katsu sandwiches and other sandwiches, and a popular one-plate steak-and-rice lunch for around 1,000 yen, plus coffee and a bar menu. The shop opens early (from 8am), so morning service is offered too.

anecdotes.text: Locals point out the shop name 銀山 is read 'Kanayama' (not 'Kinzan' or 'Kanakin'), a quirk noted for visitors from outside the prefecture.
Known for offering a satisfying 'stamina' steak lunch for around 1,000 yen, served as a steak-and-rice plate.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Syokuki confirms the name is read 'Kanayama' (not Kinzan/Kanakin), the Ginzancho/Naka-ku (中区銀山町1-2) location, the American-casual cafe-and-bar concept, 8:00–21:00 hours and the sub-¥1,000 steak lunch. Owner name is not published.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood cafe-bar where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; some basic Japanese helps as it is a small local spot rather than a tourist-facing place.

places.name: 自然食レストラン かんな (Shizenshoku Restaurant Kanna)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a Japanese natural-food kitchen, seasonings such as miso and soy sauce typically contain wheat, and items like the lemon cake are wheat-based. There is no stated dedicated fryer or allergen protocol, so a celiac diner must call ahead and explain wheat strictly.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetable-forward natural-food kitchen using pesticide-free and organic produce, organic-rice miso, and traditional wood-barrel-fermented seasonings; the menu centers on plant-based dishes and desserts like a pesticide-free lemon cake. It is not certified fully vegan, and the daily-changing set may include fish, egg, or dairy, so confirm by phone (082-248-4221) and request a plant-only plate when you reserve.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30). Closed Tue and Sun.
places.neighborhood: Sumiyoshicho, Naka Ward (Funairi-honmachi, southwest of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: Kanna is a natural-food lunch restaurant on the fifth floor of the Hiroshima Ryoin wellness building, run in the spirit of the MOA natural-food movement that promotes eating in harmony with nature. It is the kind of quiet, word-of-mouth place locals describe as 'known t

chef_bio.background: Kanna is a natural-food lunch restaurant on the fifth floor of the Hiroshima Ryoin (広島療院) wellness facility in Naka-ku, run in the spirit of the MOA natural-food movement that promotes eating in harmony with nature. It is the kind of quiet, word-of-mouth place locals describe as 'known to those in the know.'
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking that lets the ingredients speak: organic produce, organic-rice miso from Aomori made by traditional wood-barrel fermentation, and seasonings chosen without unnecessary additives.
chef_bio.specialty: A daily-changing organic lunch built around pesticide-free and organic vegetables grown by Hiroshima-area farms, finished with a homemade pesticide-free lemon cake for dessert.

anecdotes.text: The miso soup is made with organic-rice miso from Aomori, slowly aged and fermented in traditional wooden barrels, with no unnecessary additives.
Dessert features a lemon cake made with pesticide-free lemons.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and MOA's own natural-health site confirm Kanna occupies the 5th floor of Hiroshima Ryoin (広島療院), a facility operated under MOA, which promotes natural/organic food. bm-peekaboo confirms the daily organic lunch, the Aomori organic-rice miso (wood-barrel fermented) and the pesticide-free lemon cake. Owner name not published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese natural-food kitchen, miso and soy-sauce-based seasonings used here typically contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

positives.text: Cooking emphasizes pesticide-free and organic vegetables and additive-free, traditionally fermented seasonings, which appeals to clean-eating diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet local lunch spot tucked inside a wellness building; little to no English is likely, so a translation app and a phoned-ahead plant-based request will make your visit smooth.

places.name: お食事処　きらく (Oshokuji-dokoro Kiraku)
places.gf_detail: The house specialty is charcoal-grilled local anago and anagomeshi, which are glazed with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare; no gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented, so this is not a gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on conger eel (anago), oysters and other fish-based set meals seasoned with wheat-soy sauce; no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (around the Omotesando shopping street, near Itsukushima Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independently run eatery on Miyajima, just off the path between the Omotesando shopping arcade and Itsukushima Shrine.

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run eatery on Miyajima (Hatsukaichi City), just off the path between the Omotesando shopping arcade and Itsukushima Shrine.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop invites visitors to try its charcoal-grilled local anago at least once.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled local anago (conger eel), a Miyajima specialty; anagomeshi (anago over rice) is the island's signature dish and its natural context.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes itself simply by its specialty: charcoal-grilled delicious local anago that it hopes guests will try at least once.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Miyajima tourism association listing confirms Kiraku specializes in charcoal-grilled local anago on the shrine approach (宮島町504-2). Hitosara is only a bare listing with no menu, seat or owner detail. Owner name not published.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run Japanese eatery on Miyajima where some basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) and cash will make ordering smoother, as English support is not documented.

places.name: 御食事処 山一本店 (Oshokuji Dokoro Yamaichi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature anago-meshi is glazed with a soy-sauce-based tare that contains wheat, and dishes such as tempura and grilled-oyster sauces also typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Celiac travelers should treat this place as unsafe without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around seafood — Miyajima anago (conger eel) and oysters — and dishes use fish-based dashi and wheat-soy-based tare. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (official site). Some sources list 10:30-17:00 with irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (in front of the pier, around the Omotesando shopping street)
places.cuisine: Diner serving conger eel dishes and seafood
places.notes: [Miyajima] 山一本店 began not as a restaurant but as a fish market run by local Miyajima fishermen. To share the oysters and conger eel pulled from the waters around the island with the steady stream of visitors arriving by ferry, the family opened an eatery directly in front of the M (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: 山一本店 began not as a restaurant but as a fish market run by local Miyajima fishermen. To share the oysters and conger eel pulled from the waters around the island with the steady stream of visitors arriving by ferry, the family opened an eatery directly in front of the Miyajima pier, just a minute's walk from where the boats land.
chef_bio.philosophy: 宮島の海で獲れた新鮮な穴子と牡蠣を、訪れる人にその場で味わってもらうこと。
chef_bio.specialty: 宮島産の穴子と牡蠣を使った郷土料理。看板はあなごめしや焼き牡蠣などの穴子・牡蠣料理。

anecdotes.text: The shop traces its roots to a fish market operated by fishermen, and opened a dining room right in front of the pier so ferry arrivals could eat the day's Miyajima oysters and conger eel.

Set across multiple floors with table seating downstairs and large tatami banquet rooms above (roughly 190 seats in total), it has long served as a roomy, accessible stop for both individual travelers and tour groups near Itsukushima Shrine's approach.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Umahiro confirms Yamaichi began as a fishermen's fish market and opened a dining hall directly in front of the Miyajima pier (~1 min from the ferry) to serve local anago and oysters, with table seating downstairs and large tatami banquet rooms upstairs (2F ~80, 3F ~70; ~190 total). Owner name not published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-meshi is finished with a soy-sauce-based tare, which in Japan virtually always contains wheat; tempura and oyster dishes likewise involve wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on fish and shellfish (conger eel, oysters) and fish-based dashi, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive throughout the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, tourist-friendly dining hall right by the Miyajima ferry pier where pointing at the anago-meshi or oyster set on the menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" is enough; a few words of basic Japanese help but are not required.

places.name: and kitchen（アンドキッチン） (and kitchen)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→ask. IDENTITY + EVIDENCE FAILURE. Two conflated entities: (1) real Hiroshima Naka-ku lunch cafe with NO GF/celiac claim; (2) Tokyo/Shibuya '&Kitchen' delivery brand under beauty salon &BEAUTÉ marketing グルテンフリー diet food. GF marketing attaches to the TOKYO brand, not the Hiroshima cafe. ZERO celiac-grade sources list Hiroshima venue as GF; absent from FMGF Hiroshima & JP GF listicles. Presume unsafe. — Prior note: Positions itself as a gluten-free and additive-free kitchen (the delivery listing is explicitly tagged 「グルテンフリー 無添加」), centered on enzyme brown rice (酵素玄米) and koji fermentation rather than wheat. Confirm the specific day's plate when you visit, as full per-dish gluten-free verification was not available from published pages.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is plant-forward and built around enzyme brown rice and koji, but published pages did not confirm that every component is fully vegan (dashi, eggs or dairy in sides were not documented). Ask staff which items on the day's plate are fully plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:30–14:30 (lunch); closed Mon and Sun
places.neighborhood: Dobashicho, Naka Ward (around Dobashi tram stop, west of Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: Positions itself as a gluten-free and additive-free kitchen (the delivery listing is explicitly tagged 「グルテンフリー 無添加」), centered on enzyme brown rice (酵素玄米) and koji fermentation rather than wheat. Confirm the specific day's plate when you visit, as full per-dish gluten-

chef_bio.background: and kitchen (アンドキッチン, also styled &kitchen) is a small lunch cafe in Dobashi-cho, Naka-ku, Hiroshima, about a two-minute walk from the Dobashi tram stop west of Peace Memorial Park. It is categorized as a cafe/sweets spot with 18 seats and styles itself as a gluten-free, additive-free kitchen built around domestically sourced ingredients and seasonings, with a curry among its signature plates. Its public presence is mainly Instagram (@and___kitchen) and delivery listings; no source names the owner or gives a founding date.
chef_bio.philosophy: Home cooking the shop labels gluten-free and additive-free, using carefully selected domestic ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily plate lunches the shop labels gluten-free and additive-free; a gluten-free curry is a signature.

anecdotes.text: The shop's delivery and takeout menu is branded 'gluten-free, additive-free' (グルテンフリー 無添加), underscoring its niche as an allergy-conscious, additive-free kitchen.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hot Pepper confirms the Dobashi-cho location (中区土橋町4-28, ~2 min from the Dobashi tram, 18 seats). The Uber Eats delivery listing brands the shop 'gluten-free / additive-free (グルテンフリー 無添加)' and lists a curry among its dishes. This is the shop's self-description, not a celiac-safety guarantee. No source names the owner or a founding date.

positives.text: Describes itself as a gluten-free and additive-free kitchen, with meals based on enzyme brown rice and koji fermentation instead of wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small Japanese-speaking lunch spot; a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the day's plate) will go a long way, and the gluten-free/additive-free focus makes it welcoming for cautious eaters.

gf_review.why: IDENTITY + EVIDENCE FAILURE. Two conflated entities: (1) real Hiroshima Naka-ku lunch cafe with NO GF/celiac claim; (2) Tokyo/Shibuya '&Kitchen' delivery brand under beauty salon &BEAUTÉ marketing グルテンフリー diet food. GF marketing attaches to the TOKYO brand, not the Hiroshima cafe. ZERO celiac-grade sources list Hiroshima venue as GF; absent from FMGF Hiroshima & JP GF listicles. Presume unsafe.
red_flags: Name conflation: Hiroshima cafe vs Tokyo &BEAUTÉ delivery brand — GF claim belongs to Tokyo entity
Hiroshima local listing makes NO GF/allergen claim
Absent from FMGF Hiroshima and all GF listicles
GF is diet/beauty marketing, not celiac safety
Rice dishes risk wheat via soy sauce, no wheat-free sourcing stated
gf_review.plain_rice: Unverified, not assumable. No documentation of plain rice or wheat-free seasoning at the Hiroshima cafe.

places.name: 松大珈琲 (Shodai Coffee)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house, not a gluten-free destination. Plain brewed coffee (nel drip / siphon) and tea are naturally gluten-free, but the café also serves light snacks and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or cross-contamination control. A celiac traveler should stick to black coffee/tea and ask directly about any food item.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee and tea are vegan, but milk-based drinks and most kissaten snacks/sweets typically contain dairy or egg, and no vegan menu is documented. A vegan visitor can order black coffee safely but should ask about milk, butter, and egg in any food before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 8:00–16:00 (closed Wed and Thu)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Machiya-dori / Omotesando area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small vintage coffee house on Miyajima that keeps the spirit of a Showa-era junkissa alive, just off the temple-town lanes near Itsukushima Shrine. It serves authentic coffee brewed slowly by nel (flannel) drip and siphon, with takeout also available for visitors wand

chef_bio.background: A small vintage coffee house on Miyajima that keeps the spirit of a Showa-era junkissa alive, just off the temple-town lanes near Itsukushima Shrine. It serves authentic coffee brewed slowly by nel (flannel) drip and siphon, with takeout also available for visitors wandering the island.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carefully brewed, traditional coffee served in an unhurried, retro setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-brewed coffee — nel drip and siphon — in a nostalgic Showa atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The café is described as a junkissa that retains a Showa-era feel, where you can enjoy authentic coffee brewed by nel drip or siphon, with takeout available.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The Miyajima tourism association member listing confirms Shodai Coffee is a Showa-era junkissa serving nel-drip and siphon coffee with takeout, 16 non-smoking seats. Owner name and founding date not published.

positives.text: A foreign-language menu is available and the café has barrier-free access.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, welcoming vintage coffee house that offers a foreign-language menu and has barrier-free access, so a foreign student can order comfortably even with little Japanese.

places.name: 広島つけ麺 かず 光町本店 (Hiroshima Tsukemen Kazu (Hikarimachi Honten))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is tsukemen built on wheat noodles, and the dipping dashi is made with chashu (pork) and typically contains soy sauce, so wheat is present in both the noodles and the sauce.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature dipping broth is made from pork chashu (and Rishiri kombu), and the noodles are served with chashu, so the core dish contains animal products. No documented vegan option.
places.neighborhood: Hikarimachi, Higashi-ku — within walking distance of Hiroshi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 広島つけ麺 かず is a long-established Hiroshima tsukemen specialist that began in 1997 and has become one of the names credited with sparking Hiroshima's tsukemen culture. The original Hikarimachi shop sits just a short walk from Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit, a tight co

chef_bio.background: 広島つけ麺 かず is a long-established Hiroshima tsukemen specialist that began in 1997 and is credited as a 'fire-starter' (火付け役) of the Hiroshima tsukemen boom — the style itself traces back to 1954's Shinkaen. The original Hikarimachi shop (Mitsumachi 2-8-1) sits about an 8-minute walk from Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit, a tight counter-and-table room run as a single independent location.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style cold tsukemen: house noodles topped with boiled cabbage, green onion, cucumber and chashu, dipped into a light (あっさり) dashi drawn from natural Rishiri kombu and pork chashu. The dipping sauce is balanced between tartness and chili heat, and diners choose from 16 levels of spiciness.

anecdotes.text: The dashi is described as drawn from natural Rishiri kombu and chashu, giving a light flavor that is rounded out by the sweetness of the boiled vegetables, with 16 selectable spice levels.
Regulars note the dipping sauce strikes a good balance of sourness and chili heat, with complexity beyond simple spiciness.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Gnavi and modokimaru confirm the Hikarimachi honten (Higashi-ku, Mitsumachi 2-8-1, ~8 min from Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit), the light Rishiri-kombu + chashu dashi, 16 spice levels and the sour-spicy balance. City of Hiroshima and area listings confirm the 1997 founding and Kazu's role as a popularizer ('fire-starter') of Hiroshima tsukemen (the style originated in 1954).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping dashi for Hiroshima tsukemen is built on chashu and a savory soy-based sauce, which conventionally contains wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature broth is made with pork chashu, so the kitchen handles animal products throughout the core dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local noodle counter where ordering is simple (pick your spice level 1–16); little to no English is likely, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the menu and pay in cash.

noodles.notes: Original house noodles served cold, chilled in ice water, with a smooth, firm, springy bite. Standard, large (1.5 balls) and double portions are offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hiroshima tsukemen (tsukemen) — cold noodles dipped in a soy-based, sesame-and-chili dipping sauce, with the spice level chosen from a 16-step scale. Topped with boiled cabbage, green onion, cucumber and char siu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, sliced and served alongside the cold noodles; it soaks up the spicy sesame-soy dipping sauce.
notable_toppings: table-side sesame, chili oil, chili flakes and vinegar for adjusting heat

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) dipping base make this unsafe for celiac diners; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The dipping sauce is built on a Seto Inland Sea small-fish and char siu dashi, and the bowl is topped with pork belly char siu; no plant-only broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is the original spark of the Hiroshima tsukemen boom, open since 1997 and credited with defining the city's signature cold-noodle, spicy-dip style. Its soul is a deceptively light dashi of natural Rishiri kombu and char siu finished into a soy-sesame-chili dip — savory, vinegar-bright and as fiery as you dare across sixteen levels of heat.

places.highlight: This is the original spark of the Hiroshima tsukemen boom, open since 1997 and credited with defining the city's signature cold-noodle, spicy-dip style. Its soul is a deceptively light dashi of natural Rishiri kombu and char siu finished into a soy-sesame-chili dip — savory, vinegar-bright and as fiery as you dare across sixteen levels of heat.

places.name: たこつぼ (Takotsubo)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional eel and kappo restaurant. The signature anago/unagi dishes are charcoal-grilled and basted in a wheat-based soy sauce tare (the proprietary sauce has been continuously replenished since founding). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and the core dishes rely on wheat-containing soy sauce. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A fish-and-seafood-centered restaurant built around eel, conger eel, sea urchin, and Seto Inland Sea catch. Dishes use fish, wheat-soy-based tare, and dashi. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 12:00–14:00, 18:00–22:00; closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 広島市中区堀川町 (near Ebisucho Station / Hatchobori / Nagarekawa)
places.cuisine: Japanese kappo cuisine specializing in anago
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established kappo (traditional Japanese) restaurant in central Hiroshima that has been operating for over 135 years, with roots reaching back before the war. It sits along the Ebisu-dori shopping arcade in the Horikawa-cho district, run as a small husband-and-wif

chef_bio.background: A long-established kappo (割烹) restaurant in central Hiroshima that has been operating for over 135 years, with roots reaching back before the war. It sits along the Ebisu-dori shopping arcade in the Horikawa-cho district, run as a small, long-established family kappo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the seasonal bounty of the Seto Inland Sea, adapting the menu to what is freshest each day, and grilling eel over charcoal so the skin turns crisp while the flesh stays soft.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and conger eel (anago). The house signature is 'hitsumushi', a steamed eel-over-rice dish served in a wooden box, available with either anago or unagi. The proprietary basting tare has been continuously replenished since the restaurant's founding.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen's tare (basting sauce) has been continuously topped up and carried forward for well over a century, and the eel is charcoal-grilled until the skin is crisp and the flesh stays fluffy.
Counter seating lets diners watch the chefs work and ask questions about the cooking.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Takotsubo's own site and hread confirm 135+ years in business (pre-war roots), the 'hitsumushi' signature (anago or unagi), charcoal-grilling, Seto Inland Sea seafood, a tare continuously replenished since founding, and counter seating; hread also notes a Michelin star (2018). The Ebisu-dori arcade association lists it in Horikawa-cho (4-18). No source confirms a husband-and-wife operation, so that specific was removed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The eel dishes are glazed with a proprietary soy-based tare that has been replenished since founding; Japanese soy sauce tare is wheat-based, so the signature dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is built around eel, conger eel, sea urchin and Seto Inland Sea seafood with fish-based dashi; there is no documented vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, long-established family-run kappo restaurant where Japanese-language ability helps a lot; counter seating is welcoming but the atmosphere is traditional, so go with a few polite phrases and be prepared that English may be limited.

places.name: とんかつ ローン (Tonkatsu Lawn (Lown))
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet) specialist. The breading is wheat flour and panko, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around pork cutlets and other meat-based fried dishes; tonkatsu sauce, miso soup, and sides typically contain animal products. No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: Takanobashi, Naka-ku (Kokutaiji-cho), central Hiroshima
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This Takanobashi-area institution opened in 1954 as a coffee shop and, over the decades, transformed into a beloved local tonkatsu destination, now in its seventh decade of business. It remains a small, independently run neighborhood shop just south of the city center.

chef_bio.origin: Founded in 1954 (Showa 29) as a kissaten (coffee shop) in Kokutaiji-cho before evolving into a tonkatsu specialist.
chef_bio.background: This Takanobashi-area institution opened in 1954 as a coffee shop and, over the decades, transformed into a beloved local tonkatsu destination, now in its seventh decade of business. It remains a small, independently run neighborhood shop just south of the city center.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on carefully sourced heritage pork and generous, comforting sets (free refills of rice, miso soup, and cabbage), keeping quality high while staying affordable for regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: Pork cutlets cut from premium heritage breeds, especially three-breed (sangen) pork and Kinka 'golden' pork from Hirata Farm in Yamagata, plus Shimane (Iwami) pork. The Yamagata pork is prized for its fine fiber, tender bite, and delicately melting fat. Weekday lunch sets are an exceptional value (around ¥990), and lunch comes with complimentary coffee, a nod to the shop's coffee-house roots.

anecdotes.text: Though it now serves almost exclusively tonkatsu, the shop began life as a coffee shop in 1954, and a free hot coffee still comes with the lunch sets as a quiet echo of that origin.
The signature pork comes from Hirata Farm in Yamagata Prefecture, with the top Kinka 'golden' grade described as having a silken, fine grain and marbling that can rival fine beef.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Modokimaru confirms the 1954 kissaten origin in Kokutaiji-cho, the switch to tonkatsu, ~¥990 weekday lunch, free lunch coffee and free rice/cabbage refills. The shop's gorp page confirms Hirata-Farm (Yamagata) sangen and Kinka pork plus Shimane/Iwami pork, with rice/miso/cabbage refills. Owner name not published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded with wheat flour and panko and deep-fried; no gluten-free preparation or separate fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkatsu sauce and accompanying condiments customarily contain wheat-based soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-centric (pork cutlets); shared frying oil and meat-based sides make it unsuitable for vegans, and no vegan items are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood tonkatsu shop where ordering is via a QR-code menu and weekday lunch sets are budget-friendly; a little Japanese or pointing at photos goes a long way, as English support is not advertised.

places.name: ブランジェリー 101ブリオ (Boulangerie 101 Brio)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour boulangerie — baguettes, croissants, danishes, cream pan and other breads are all made with wheat. No gluten-free products or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offerings are documented. Most items use dairy and eggs (e.g. the signature vanilla-bean cream pan, danishes, meat-and-cheese breads). A plain baguette or some lean European-style loaves may happen to be egg/dairy-free, but this is not confirmed, so a vegan diner would need to ask about specific loaves in person.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 09:00–16:00; closed Sun (relocated Teppocho location, opened Aug 2025). Note: the former Hashimoto-cho shop was listed as 7:00–20:00; current listing shows 09:00–16:00.
places.neighborhood: 中区鉄砲町 (Teppocho, near Hatchobori / Jogakuin-mae, central Hir
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running independent boulangerie founded around 2004 and operated by the small local company Brio, this is a neighborhood institution that relocated within central Hiroshima and reopened in its current spot in 2025. For roughly two decades it has been the kind of

chef_bio.background: A long-running independent boulangerie founded around 2004 and operated by the small local company Brio (有限会社ブリオ), this is a neighborhood institution that relocated within central Hiroshima and reopened in its current Teppo-cho spot in 2025. For roughly two decades it has been the kind of everyday bakery locals rely on, turning out a wide range of European-style breads and pastries.
chef_bio.philosophy: Traditional European baking methods with house-made fillings and sauces, aiming for an everyday bakery that offers a full, varied selection rather than a niche.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature item is its cream pan — a mushroom-shaped bun filled with vanilla-bean-flecked custard cream — alongside baguettes, croissants, danishes and savory meat-and-cheese breads.

anecdotes.text: At its previous location the shop was beloved for staying open late (until 8pm), so commuters could still find a wide selection of fresh bread in the evening when most bakeries had sold out or closed.
The cream pan is treated as the shop's emblem, with in-store signage marking it as the signature offering and its cream made with real vanilla beans.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms 有限会社ブリオ (est. 2004) runs the bakery as a husband-and-wife team and that it relocated/reopened at Teppo-cho, Naka-ku on 2025-08-26. Syokuki confirms the mushroom-shaped vanilla-bean cream-pan signature and the previous shop's late (until 8pm) hours. Owner name not published.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a working wheat-flour bakery; flour dust and shared surfaces make heavy gluten cross-contact unavoidable. Not appropriate for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and pastries are produced together using shared dough, ovens and equipment; dairy- and egg-containing items (cream pan, danishes) share the same production line as any plainer loaves.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood bakery where you simply pick up bread and pay in cash — pointing at what you want works fine, though menus and staff are likely Japanese-only.

places.name: 広島赤焼 えん 駅西本店 (Hiroshima Akayaki En Ekinishi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauce, and shared dishes like gansu (breaded fried paste) and various teppan items also use wheat. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen centers on oysters, pork, koune (beef neck), gansu (fish-paste fritter), and seafood, with dashi, egg, and wheat-soy sauces throughout. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sat 17:30–22:00 (food L.O. around 20:30); closed Tue and Sun. Sources differ slightly: official site lists close at 22:00 / last entry 20:30; aggregator lists 17:30-20:30.
places.neighborhood: 南区・エキニシ (Ekinishi, just west of Hiroshima Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A teppan izakaya in the Ekinishi dining alleys just west of Hiroshima Station, set across two floors: a small teppan counter on the ground floor and table seating with private rooms upstairs. The concept is to let visitors enjoy a casual, all-in-one taste of Hiroshima.

chef_bio.background: A teppan izakaya in the Ekinishi dining alleys just west of Hiroshima Station, set across two floors: a small teppan counter on the ground floor and table seating with private rooms upstairs. The concept is to let visitors enjoy a casual, all-in-one taste of Hiroshima.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is 'kigaru ni oishiku Hiroshima-zukushi' — to casually and deliciously serve a full spread of Hiroshima, focusing on the dishes locals love, with carefully sourced ingredients ('tenshu kodawari no shiire') and local sake to match.
chef_bio.specialty: Oyster-topped Hiroshima okonomiyaki is the signature, most famously the 'Mashimashi Kaki' okonomiyaki piled with about 15 oysters. The kitchen also leans hard into other local favorites: gansu (fried fish-paste fritter), koune (grilled beef neck), and a range of oyster preparations from classic kaki-butter to Hiroshima-lemon steaming.

anecdotes.text: The standout dish is the 'Mashimashi Kaki' okonomiyaki, a hot teppan okonomiyaki crowned with a mound of about 15 oysters.
The restaurant frames itself around 'kaki-oko' (oyster okonomiyaki) and other Hiroshima ground-foods, served teppanyaki style in the Ekinishi alley scene.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The restaurant's own site confirms the concept 'kigaru ni oishiku Hiroshima-zukushi,' the 'Mashimashi Kaki' okonomiyaki piled with ~15 oysters (¥2,647), gansu, koune, oyster dishes and local sake, in the Ekinishi alley west of Hiroshima Station. Owner name not published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki and teppan izakaya dishes here are finished with standard wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauces; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local teppan izakaya where a few basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will carry you comfortably; English support is limited.

places.name: たけはらの味 ぴぃす (Takehara no Aji Piisu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen builds around a soy-sauce-based bonito dashi (the signature negi-shabu broth), and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat. The uni-horen is served on a toasted baguette (wheat), and izakaya dishes generally use wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on oysters, sea urchin, sashimi and other seafood, pork (negi-shabu), and Takehara beef, with a fish-based (bonito) dashi running through the cooking. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drinks L.O. 23:30). Tabelog lists 17:00–23:00. Closed Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: 南区松原町・エキニシ（駅西）, about a 5-minute walk from the south exit of
places.cuisine: Owner-run izakaya specializing in Takehara a
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner grew up in Takehara, a historic port town on the Seto Inland Sea, and built this small izakaya in the lively Ekinishi district just west of Hiroshima Station as a way of bringing his hometown's flavors to the city.

chef_bio.background: The owner grew up in Takehara, a historic port town on the Seto Inland Sea, and built this small izakaya in the lively Ekinishi district just west of Hiroshima Station as a way of bringing his hometown's flavors to the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than serving generic 'Hiroshima products,' the owner chooses the finest ingredient from the right corner of the prefecture for each dish, because flavor varies by where things are grown and raised. The sake list reflects the same homecoming spirit: only sake from three breweries in his native Takehara, including seasonal limited releases.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is negi-shabu: East Hiroshima white scallions paired with Seto Momiji pork, swished through a house-made, ginger-accented bonito dashi with a slightly sweet soy base. Etajima oysters arrive large, intensely flavored and milky; other draws include uni-horen (sea urchin and Hiroshima spinach on toasted baguette) and koune, a rare cut of Takehara beef regarded locally as a hidden signature.

anecdotes.text: The negi-shabu broth is a house-made, ginger-spiked bonito dashi with a soy base built to coax sweetness out of East Hiroshima white scallions and Seto Momiji pork.
The sake program stocks only the three breweries of the owner's hometown, Takehara, including seasonal limited bottles for comparison tasting.
Koune, a rare Takehara beef cut, is described as a 'hidden signature dish' of the house.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Foodconnection confirms the Takehara-native owner's per-region sourcing philosophy, the negi-shabu (East Hiroshima white scallions + Seto Momiji pork in ginger bonito dashi), Etajima oysters, uni-horen, koune as a 'hidden signature', and a sake list limited to three Takehara breweries with seasonal releases, near Hiroshima Station. Owner name not published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature negi-shabu uses a soy-sauce-based bonito dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the broth and similarly seasoned dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cooking is built on a fish (bonito) dashi and the menu is dominated by oysters, sea urchin, pork and beef, so vegan dishes are not available and shared broths/equipment make plant-only meals impractical.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny 14-seat owner-run izakaya where ordering and conversation will largely be in Japanese, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help; reservations by phone are advisable since seating is very limited.

places.name: メロンパン 広島福屋八丁堀店 (Melon Pan Hiroshima Fukuya Hatchobori-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery — every product (melon pan, sweet rolls, sandwiches) is made from wheat dough. There are no gluten-free options.
places.vegan_detail: The signature rugby-ball melon pan is filled with homemade custard-style cream and the breads are enriched dough, so they are not vegan; the lineup also includes meat and fish sandwiches (croquette, chicken nanban, ebi katsu, gyu katsu). No vegan items are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sun 10:00-19:30; Fri-Sat 10:00-20:00. Closed days follow the Fukuya Hatchobori department store. Note: the signature melon pan is not stocked on Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Ebisucho / Hatchobori, Naka Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This counter on the basement food floor of the Fukuya Hatchobori department store sells the breads of a Kure institution founded in 1936 (Showa 11). The shop is approaching its 90th anniversary and is run by the founding family, now in its third generation under Ryuma N

chef_bio.background: This counter on the basement food floor of the Fukuya Hatchobori department store sells the breads of a Kure institution founded in 1936 (Showa 11). The shop is approaching its 90th anniversary and is run by the founding Nakashio family, now in its third generation under Ryuma Nakashio (中塩隆馬), who upholds the recipe handed down from founder Haruma Nakashio (中塩春馬), with bread still baked at the Kure main shop and delivered fresh to outlets across Hiroshima Prefecture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeping the founder's original method unchanged and aiming for simple, natural, nostalgic flavors.
chef_bio.specialty: The famous rugby-ball-shaped melon pan: a long oval loaf with a crisp biscuit crust outside and soft sweet bread filled with homemade cream inside. The shop also makes a wide variety of other sweet rolls and sandwiches.

anecdotes.text: The founder created the distinctive rugby-ball melon pan because melons were an expensive luxury at the time, and he wanted ordinary people to be able to enjoy the idea of melon; the shape was inspired by makuwa-uri (Oriental melon), which was then commonly called 'melon'.
The melon pan and related products are registered trademarks, and the bakery is approaching its 90th year in business.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 呉のメロンパン公式サイト（kuremelon.com）と広島県ブランドショップTAUの記事が、昭和11年創業・三代目中塩隆馬・まくわうり由来のラグビーボール型・登録商標を裏付ける。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All products are wheat-flour breads made and handled together; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sweet breads use enriched dough and cream fillings, and the counter shares space and handling with meat and fish sandwiches, so no vegan-safe product can be assured.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple department-store bread counter where you point at what you want and pay — no Japanese conversation needed, though staff likely speak little English.

places.name: 天邪鬼お好み焼き (Amanjaku Okonomiyaki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and yakisoba/udon noodles, the okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat, and the signature karaage is wheat/starch-battered fried chicken. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Okonomiyaki here uses egg, pork, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings; the menu centers on meat, fried chicken (karaage), shrimp tartar, and beef tendon. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Closed Sundays. Open for both lunch and dinner; dinner offers all-you-can-drink courses. Exact daily open/close times are not published on Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, or Hitosara; the listings advise confirming hours with the shop directly (082-222-2139).
places.neighborhood: 中区幟町 (Noboricho), near Jogakuin-mae / Hatchobori
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent okonomiyaki spot tucked into the Noboricho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, a short walk from Jogakuin-mae and the Hatchobori area and within reach of the Peace Memorial Park district. The staff cook authentic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki right in

chef_bio.background: A small, independent okonomiyaki spot tucked into the Noboricho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, a short walk from Jogakuin-mae and the Hatchobori area and within reach of the Peace Memorial Park district. The staff cook authentic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki right in front of you on the teppan.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (layered with noodles, cabbage, and egg) is the centerpiece, but the shop is quietly known for its karaage (fried chicken), treated by regulars as a hidden specialty alongside teppan dishes like shrimp tartar and beef-tendon ponzu.

anecdotes.text: Although it is first and foremost a Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop, its karaage (fried chicken) is repeatedly singled out as a surprising hidden specialty.
Beyond okonomiyaki, the shop serves a range of teppan dishes and offers evening all-you-can-drink courses, making it a relaxed spot for groups of family or friends.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The teppan is used for wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles, so any non-batter item cooked on the same surface would share heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard okonomiyaki sauce and seasonings used at Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops are wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared teppan alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood okonomiyaki shop where staff cook on the teppan in front of you; little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 隅吉 (Sumiyoshi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles (the shop runs its own noodle-making operation, 隅吉製麺), and the broth and accompaniments rely on standard Japanese seasonings including soy sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Oden is typically simmered in a soy-sauce/dashi broth and is not gluten-free either.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature broth is built on chicken and pork bones, and other menu items (gyoza, oden) contain animal products. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat, holidays 19:00–06:00 next day (L.O. 05:45 next day) / closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa / Yagenbori entertainment district, central Hiros
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sumiyoshi opened in 2018 in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter with the stated goal of creating a new style of Hiroshima ramen. It is an independent shop that even runs its own noodle-making operation (Sumiyoshi Seimen) at a separate location in the city's Minami

chef_bio.background: Sumiyoshi opened in 2018 in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter with the stated goal of creating a new style of Hiroshima ramen. It is an independent shop that even runs its own noodle-making operation (Sumiyoshi Seimen) at a separate location in the city's Minami Ward, so the noodles served are made in-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: To create a new kind of Hiroshima ramen rather than copy existing styles, pairing an original double-stock broth with noodles made by the shop itself.
chef_bio.specialty: A double broth simmered from both chicken and pork bones, described as rich but with a clean, moreish finish; alongside the ramen, the shop is known for its carefully developed house-made oden.

anecdotes.text: As a late-night spot in the Nagarekawa entertainment district, it serves from evening through until early morning, making it a go-to bowl after a night out.
The shop produces its own noodles under the Sumiyoshi Seimen name at a separate facility in Minami Ward.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is centered on wheat ramen noodles boiled in shared water, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ramen and oden in this style are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is made with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature broth is made from chicken and pork bones, so it is not vegan and meat stock is present throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, late-night local ramen counter in a nightlife district where staff likely speak mainly Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, but pointing at the menu works fine.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles chosen to carry the light white-soy double broth without overwhelming it.

ramen.signature_bowl: 隅吉ラーメン (Sumiyoshi Ramen) — the chicken-and-pork double broth seasoned with a white soy sauce (shiro-shoyu) tare lifted by fish and vegetable umami; order the 黒吉 (Kurokichi) version if you want it deepened with house black-garlic mayu oil.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu cut with a higher fat ratio, yet trimmed of heaviness so it reads tender and savory rather than overly rich.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce-based tare; no rice or konjac noodle or gluten-free seasoning is offered, so it is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a chicken-and-pork bone broth with fish dashi and pork chashu; no plant-only broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Hiroshima bowl that breaks from the city's classic soy-tonkotsu mold: its soul is a chicken-and-pork double soup, simmered rich but finished clean, married to a delicate white soy sauce tare and a whisper of fish-and-vegetable umami. The result is a refined, late-night chuka soba whose lineage you taste in the soup's balance of body and clarity rather than in heaviness.

places.highlight: A modern-original Hiroshima bowl that breaks from the city's classic soy-tonkotsu mold: its soul is a chicken-and-pork double soup, simmered rich but finished clean, married to a delicate white soy sauce tare and a whisper of fish-and-vegetable umami. The result is a refined, late-night chuka soba whose lineage you taste in the soup's balance of body and clarity rather than in heaviness.

places.name: 壽ゑ廣餃子 立町店 (Suehiro Gyoza Tachimachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is gyoza, whose wrappers are made from wheat flour, and the brothless tantanmen is a wheat-noodle dish. Standard izakaya seasonings (soy sauce, miso) here also typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The house specialty gyoza are filled with pork mince infused with a medicinal-herb broth made with Chinese miso, and the menu centers on meat and izakaya fare. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:45–13:30), Dinner 17:00–23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30). Sources differ on whether lunch is served daily or weekdays only; one source indicates weekend lunch is not offered and Sunday may be temporarily closed.
places.neighborhood: Tachimachi, Naka-ku (near Hondori and Hatchobori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual neighborhood izakaya in central Hiroshima, a minute's walk from the Tachimachi tram stop, built around one obsession: house-made fresh gyoza. The dumplings are wrapped by hand each day for a chewy, springy skin.

chef_bio.background: A casual neighborhood izakaya in central Hiroshima, a minute's walk from the Tachimachi tram stop, built around one obsession: house-made fresh gyoza. The dumplings are wrapped by hand each day for a chewy, springy skin.
