chef_bio.philosophy: A play on 'health and beauty through food' — the herbs and spices folded into both the dumplings and the drinks are meant to make a hearty izakaya meal feel a little restorative.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is 薬膳生餃子 (yakuzen fresh gyoza): a pork-heavy filling worked together with an original medicinal-herb broth that is slow-simmered from roughly eight spices — star anise, dried citrus peel (陳皮), cinnamon bark (桂皮), Sichuan/flower pepper (花山椒) and more — blended with Chinese miso. Two styles are made for side-by-side tasting: 黒餃子 (with garlic and Chinese chives) and 白餃子 (without). The kitchen also turns out Hiroshima-style brothless tantanmen in three spice levels and a lineup of medicinal-herb sours.

anecdotes.text: The medicinal broth at the heart of the gyoza is cooked from about eight spices including star anise, dried citrus peel, cinnamon bark and flower pepper, then blended with Chinese miso before being worked into the pork filling, while the wrappers are folded by hand for a chewy bite.
Guests are encouraged to order both the black (garlic-and-chive) and white (no garlic or chive) gyoza to taste them against each other, alongside four signature medicinal-herb sours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat-based gyoza and wheat-noodle tantanmen, so a shared kitchen would involve heavy flour handling; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gyoza dipping sauce and izakaya seasonings at a shop like this customarily use standard soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature gyoza are filled with pork and Chinese miso, and the menu is meat-centered, so no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming gyoza izakaya about a minute from the Tachimachi tram stop where solo diners are common; a little spoken Japanese helps, but pointing at the gyoza menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will get you by.

places.name: 焼 むらかみ (Yaki Murakami)
places.gf_detail: This is a yakitori specialist: skewers are seasoned with tare (a soy-sauce-and-mirin glaze that contains wheat) or salt, and the meal often finishes with a wheat-noodle chicken-salt ramen. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, so this is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around charcoal-grilled chicken (thighs, offal, skin, tsukune meatballs, hand wings) and a chicken-based broth ramen, all animal-based. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sundays plus occasional irregular closures
places.neighborhood: 中区立町 (Tatemachi, central Hiroshima, near Hondori/Hatchobori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter-only yakitori shop that opened on April 3, 2023 in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district, a few minutes' walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. The opening was actually pushed back about a week from its planned late-March date because of smoke-management is

chef_bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Tatemachi, Naka-ku), next to a ramen shop in the busy Hondori/Hatchobori entertainment area.
chef_bio.background: A small counter-only yakitori shop that opened on April 3, 2023 in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district, a few minutes' walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. The opening was actually pushed back about a week from its planned late-March date because of smoke-management issues, before the doors finally opened.
chef_bio.philosophy: The room is built as a single counter so guests sit close to the fire and take in the cooking with all five senses, the smoke, the sizzle and the aroma. The chef's attentive technique is part of the experience: misting each skewer at the start and snipping away any char with scissors mid-grill.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken cooked over high-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal from Kochi, prized for its stronger aroma. The chef sources different rare and regional birds by cut: prized Amakusa Daio (a long-reared Kumamoto heritage breed) for thigh and breast, offal from a Hiroshima brand chicken, and Ehime's Himekko jidori for lighter dishes like toriwasa and tataki. Signatures include sumi-grilled offal, crispy seseri (neck), creamy chi-gimo (liver), shiso-scented tsukune meatballs, hand wings cooked on iron skewers, and a closing bowl of chicken-salt ramen.

anecdotes.text: The owner previously cooked at the yakitori restaurant 'Yakitori Taniji' before opening his own counter.
Regulars note that the seats nearest the entrance are the best value because you can watch the chef's handwork up close.
The opening was delayed by about a week, to April 3, 2023, due to smoke-management issues at the new location.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・ホットペッパーの店舗情報とレビューが、土佐備長炭、天草大王のもも・むね、広島産ブランド鶏の内臓、愛媛・媛っこ地鶏、2023年4月3日開店を確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare glaze is soy-sauce and mirin based, which contains wheat; the meal is also often finished with a wheat-noodle chicken ramen.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter where the chef works right in front of you, so a little Japanese and an advance reservation go a long way; it is an intimate, food-focused spot rather than a casual walk-in.

places.name: 酒蔵魚好人 クダコ (Sakagura Uokojin Kudako)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a Japanese izakaya, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi-style seasonings, and there are tempura/fried items, so it is not a safe choice for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around fish and shellfish — oysters, live octopus and other sashimi, conger eel, small sardines, and seafood clay-pot rice — and uses fish-based dashi and soy sauce. There is no documented vegan offering; not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-23:00; Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-00:00; Sat 17:00-00:00; Sun closed. (Some listings note dinner from 17:00, L.O. 23:00, with an irregular schedule.)
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori and Kanayamacho)
places.notes: The owner-chef trained extensively in izakayas and Japanese restaurants before opening this place in 2003, and built it around a deep commitment to local Hiroshima ingredients from both the sea and the mountains.

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef trained extensively in izakayas and Japanese restaurants before opening this place in 2003, and built it around a deep commitment to local Hiroshima ingredients from both the sea and the mountains.
chef_bio.philosophy: To bring out the essence of each ingredient through meticulous, unhurried preparation, serving only dishes the kitchen is fully satisfied with.
chef_bio.specialty: Seto Inland Sea seafood — live octopus sashimi, oysters paired with spinach using sun-dried oysters for concentrated umami, seasonal conger eel and small sardines, and made-to-order seasonal clay-pot rice.

anecdotes.text: The name refers to Kudako, a renowned fishing ground in the Seto Inland Sea, reflecting the focus on local sea ingredients.
Even a simple potato salad is mixed by hand for about an hour, illustrating the kitchen's painstaking approach.
Oysters are selected at peak season by local distributors and sun-dried to concentrate their umami before being cooked with spinach.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese izakaya, dishes are seasoned with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on fish and shellfish, so vegan dishes would share preparation surfaces and fish-based dashi/soy seasoning.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a local Japanese seafood izakaya where the daily fish and oyster offerings change with the season, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps when ordering and asking what is fresh that day.

places.name: 和食屋 きくお (Washokuya Kikuo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes built around regular (wheat-based) soy sauce — the signature boar/deer/chicken stew is simmered in a soy-based broth — and fried items such as the yuba-wrapped fried shrimp use wheat batter, so this is not a suitable choice for gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around fish from Kusatsu fishing port, game meat (wild boar, deer) and red chicken, smoked eggs/mentaiko/cheese, and fish-and-soy-based seasonings. No plant-based menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat, holidays 18:00–23:00 (drinks L.O. 22:00 / food L.O. 23:00). Closed Sun. Some sources list opening at 17:00 or 17:30.
places.neighborhood: 中区幟町 (Noborimachi), central Hiroshima, near Hatchobori / Gin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hironaka says his interest in cooking grew under the influence of his mother, a skilled home cook. After graduating from Hiroshima Suishin culinary college, he trained for five years at a Japanese restaurant, five years at a hotel, and served as head chef within the Yam

chef_bio.background: Hironaka says his interest in cooking grew under the influence of his mother, a skilled home cook. After graduating from Hiroshima Suishin culinary college, he trained for five years at a Japanese restaurant, five years at a hotel, and served as head chef within the Yamashiroya group, also earning a Hiroshima fugu (pufferfish) preparation license, before opening his own place in December 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carefully made dishes paired with regional sake that impresses connoisseurs — each dish is matched with a complementary local sake, in a relaxed space meant for hosting people who matter, from solo diners to banquets.
chef_bio.specialty: A creative-washoku izakaya in a converted old-house (kominka) style space. Known for an assortment of house-smoked items (boiled eggs, cheese, mentaiko) smoked with different wood chips like oak, cherry and white oak; a signature inoshika-tori stew of wild boar, deer and red chicken simmered a full day in a soy-based broth; and sashimi platters from fish delivered live from Kusatsu fishing port.

anecdotes.text: Hironaka traces his love of cooking to his mother, a talented home cook, before going on to train in restaurants, a hotel, and as a group head chef, then striking out on his own in December 2017.
The smoked platter is made by deliberately switching between oak, cherry and white-oak wood chips for different ingredients, and the namesake boar-deer-chicken stew is simmered for a full day.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature simmered dishes are built on a soy-based broth, indicating standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is central to the cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an owner-run Japanese izakaya where ordering and conversation will be in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 呉麺屋 胡通り店 (Gomenya Ebisu-dori (Goji-dori) Branch)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The shop is built around wheat noodles (flat chewy Kure reimen noodles, ramen, tsukemen), and the sauce/broth is described as seafood-and-soy based, which typically contains wheat. Chashu pork, fried rice and other sides further rule out a gluten-free meal. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature Kure reimen is topped with shrimp, chashu pork and boiled egg, and the sauce carries fish/seafood umami. Ramen and the meat/seafood-heavy side menu (steak, senjigara pork) likewise contain animal products. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu 19:00–03:00, Fri–Sat 19:00–04:00 (per Tabelog); some listings show closing as late as 03:30/05:30. Closed Sundays. Late-night noodle shop.
places.neighborhood: Horikawacho, Naka-ku (Ebisucho / Ebisu-dori; steps from Hatchobori / Ebisucho tram stop, busy downtown area very close to Hondori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gomenya is a small Hiroshima noodle operation specializing in Kure reimen, a regional cold-noodle dish born in the city of Kure. This branch sits in the heart of Hiroshima's Hatchobori / Ebisu entertainment quarter and runs deep into the night, drawing both locals after

chef_bio.background: Gomenya is a small Hiroshima noodle operation specializing in Kure reimen, a regional cold-noodle dish born in the city of Kure. This branch sits in the heart of Hiroshima's Hatchobori / Ebisu entertainment quarter and runs deep into the night, drawing both locals after work and curious first-timers wanting to try the local specialty.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hospitality-first, greeting every bowl with the cheerful call 'はい、お待ちど〜様ですっ！' ('Here you go, thanks for waiting!'), welcoming regulars and newcomers alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Kure reimen — flat, chewy cold noodles dressed in a distinctive sweet-and-sour sauce with a spicy kick and seafood umami, topped with cucumber, shrimp, chashu pork and boiled egg. Selectable spice level. Also serves ramen, tsukemen and hearty sides.

anecdotes.text: Kure reimen is presented as an independently developed regional cuisine, distinct from typical summer cold ramen or Korean naengmyeon, defined by its sweet-sour-yet-spicy sauce and springy flat noodles.
A diner described the medium-spicy Kure reimen as an 'endless loop' of chili heat giving way to fish-based umami and sweet-sour notes, paired with fluffy, well-separated fried rice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on wheat noodles (Kure reimen, ramen, tsukemen) and a soy/seafood-based sauce; a shared noodle kitchen means heavy gluten presence and high cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sauce/broth is described as soy- and seafood-based, which in Japanese noodle shops typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dish and sides include shrimp, pork chashu, egg and fish-derived umami; no vegan preparation documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, late-night counter noodle spot where you order verbally rather than by ticket machine, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the Kure reimen) will go a long way for a foreign student.

places.name: 湯葉と豆腐の店 梅の花 広島店 (Yuba to Tofu no Mise Ume no Hana Hiroshima-t)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. The menu is built around tofu and yuba (soy products), but kaiseki courses rely heavily on Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which typically contain wheat, and fried yuba/tempura-style items use wheat batter. The restaurant only handles a fixed list of allergens (eggs, dairy, buckwheat, peanuts, crab, shrimp, walnuts) by advance reservation and explicitly states it cannot accommodate allergens beyond that list — wheat/gluten is not among them. A celiac or strict gluten-free diner should not rely on this restaurant; ask in detail when reserving.
places.vegan_detail: The food centers on house-made tofu (from Hokkaido Toyomusume soybeans), freshly pulled yuba, yudofu hot pot, and tofu shumai — all soy-based and plant-forward — but the standard kaiseki courses also include seafood, egg, and dairy items, and use dashi/soy sauce that may contain fish and wheat. There is no published vegan or vegetarian set menu. Vegan/vegetarian accommodation may be possible only by arranging it in advance when booking; confirm directly by phone or online reservation before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Hiroshima Station area (Matsubaracho, Minami-ku; Fukuya Hiroshima-Ekimae store, 11F)
places.cuisine: Tofu and yuba (tofu skin) Japanese kaiseki
places.notes: The restaurant turns two humble soy staples — tofu and yuba (the delicate skin lifted from simmering soy milk) — into a full seasonal kaiseki experience. It makes its own tofu in-house from Hokkaido Toyomusume soybeans and pulls fresh yuba to order.

chef_bio.origin: A specialist tofu-and-yuba restaurant occupying the 11th floor of the Fukuya department store directly beside JR Hiroshima Station's south side.
chef_bio.background: The restaurant turns two humble soy staples — tofu and yuba (the delicate skin lifted from simmering soy milk) — into a full seasonal kaiseki experience. It makes its own tofu in-house from Hokkaido Toyomusume soybeans and pulls fresh yuba to order.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the craft of fresh, handmade tofu and yuba as the heart of a refined Japanese meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature dishes include tofu shumai, yudofu (simmered tofu hot pot), deep-fried yuba, and yuba gratin, served as multi-course kaiseki sets.

anecdotes.text: Diners eat against a wide view over the Hiroshima cityscape, and the floor includes fully enclosed private rooms used for celebrations, memorials, and formal gatherings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soy-sauce-based seasonings and wheat-battered fried yuba/tempura items are used throughout the kitchen, so gluten cross-contact is likely; the restaurant does not list wheat among the allergens it can accommodate.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaiseki dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tofu and yuba dishes share a kitchen with seafood, egg, and dairy preparations; standard dashi may contain fish, so plant-only diners should request accommodation in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodation is offered only on a limited basis to guests who reserve in advance, and only for eggs, dairy, buckwheat, peanuts, crab, shrimp, and walnuts; allergens outside that list cannot be handled.

positives.text: The core ingredients are house-made tofu and freshly pulled yuba, so plant-forward diners have genuinely soy-centered options to build a meal around when they arrange it ahead.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished department-store kaiseki restaurant where staff are used to guests but English is limited; a few Japanese phrases and an advance reservation noting any dietary needs will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.

places.name: おやつカフェ ユルム。 (Oyatsu Cafe Yurumu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The baked goods are made with domestic wheat flour (国産小麦粉), so muffins and scones contain gluten. The shop focuses on egg/dairy/white-sugar avoidance, not gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around plant-based baking: no eggs, no dairy, no white sugar. Sweetness comes from beet sugar and brown rice malt syrup (genmai mizuame), and soy milk replaces dairy. Muffins, scones and similar baked goods are the core offering, alongside fruit smoothies (e.g. organic strawberry). Suitable for vegans, though confirm any specific item as recipes rotate daily.
places.hours_raw: Tuesday–Saturday 11:00–18:00; closed Sunday and Monday
places.neighborhood: Ushida-honmachi, Higashi Ward (near Hakushima Station on the
places.notes: The owner, Harada Naomi, was once an ordinary company worker until she developed severe atopic dermatitis. Searching for foods that were gentler on her body, she began baking her own sweets without eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar.

chef_bio.background: The owner, Harada Naomi, was once an ordinary company worker until she developed severe atopic dermatitis. Searching for foods that were gentler on her body, she began baking her own sweets without eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar.
chef_bio.philosophy: She wants people to care for themselves and let both body and mind 'loosen and unwind' (yurumu) through gently sweet treats made from ingredients that go easy on the body.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based muffins and scones made with domestic wheat flour, beet sugar, brown rice malt syrup and soy milk, plus seasonal fruit smoothies such as one made from pesticide-free organic strawberries from Hatsukaichi.

anecdotes.text: After sharing her homemade allergy-friendly sweets on social media, she received so many requests from other people with allergies that she eventually opened the café.
The shop's name 'yurumu' (to loosen / relax) reflects her wish for guests to relax body and mind over gentle, body-friendly sweets.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen bakes with domestic wheat flour, so the environment is not gluten-free and cross-contact with wheat is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The whole product line is made without eggs, dairy, or white sugar, so the kitchen does not handle those animal ingredients alongside the baked goods.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner started baking precisely because of her own allergy (severe atopic dermatitis) and built the shop around requests from other allergy-sufferers, so she is highly attuned to egg/dairy/white-sugar avoidance.

positives.text: Fully egg-free, dairy-free and white-sugar-free baking using beet sugar, brown rice malt syrup and soy milk — a reliable vegan-friendly sweets stop.
Prioritizes domestic, organic and pesticide-free ingredients where possible, such as organic strawberries for smoothies.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run bake café with only a few counter seats, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps and a friendly konnichiwa goes a long way; staff are used to allergy-conscious customers but English may be limited.

places.name: お好み村 あとむ (Okonomimura Atomu)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki batter and the soba/udon noodles layered into it are wheat-based, and the standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free preparation, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for those requiring gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional okonomiyaki house whose signature dishes contain pork, egg, and wheat noodles cooked on a shared teppan with fish/animal-based sauces. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–02:00 next day (food & drink L.O. 01:30 next day). Another source lists 11:00–24:00. Open daily.
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi / Nagarekawa area (~4–5 min walk from Hatchobori Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Atomu is one of the long-running okonomiyaki stalls inside Hiroshima's famous Okonomimura, a building stacked with small okonomiyaki shops in the Shintenchi entertainment district. It has been serving since the 1990s and is a cozy, counter-only spot where you watch the

chef_bio.origin: Hiroshima, within the Okonomimura okonomiyaki building
chef_bio.background: Atomu is one of the long-running okonomiyaki stalls inside Hiroshima's famous Okonomimura, a building stacked with small okonomiyaki shops in the Shintenchi entertainment district. It has been serving since the 1990s and is a cozy, counter-only spot where you watch the cook work the iron griddle right in front of you.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on the teppan, including the namesake house okonomiyaki 'Atomu' made with kimchi and garlic for a hearty, stamina-packed version, plus the classic nikutama soba (pork, egg, and noodle) okonomiyaki.

anecdotes.text: The house's signature 'Atomu' okonomiyaki is loaded with kimchi and garlic for a stamina-boosting, full-flavored take on the Hiroshima classic.
Diners sit at the counter and watch the cook finish each okonomiyaki with fluid, practiced hand movements right on the griddle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle where wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-containing sauce are all used; heavy cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are cooked on the same teppan used for pork, egg, and seafood, so vegan separation is not possible.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-only okonomiyaki stall inside the Okonomimura food building where you sit right at the griddle; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect little to no English and cash only.

places.name: プリムべェール (PriMevErE)
places.gf_detail: Pure fresh fruit juices and lemon soda/lemonade should be naturally gluten-free, but no allergen information is published. The savory items (rice burgers, mofles/waffle pastries) and possible seasonings cannot be assumed gluten-free. There is no dedicated gluten-free handling; a gluten-sensitive diner should stick to plain fruit juices and confirm at the counter.
places.vegan_detail: The fresh fruit juices (e.g., Hiroshima lemon lemonade/lemon soda, the seasonal Hiroshima Mix, pineapple frozen drinks) are fruit-based and suitable for plant-based diners. The food side (rice burgers / rice-sandwich onigiri) features conger eel (anago) and oysters, which are not vegan; the cheese-filled mofles contain dairy. Stick to the fruit juices and lemonade for a vegan-friendly choice.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-17:30 (official site lists 10:30-17:00; hours vary with ferry operations)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street
places.notes: [Miyajima] A small fresh-juice bar tucked along the Omotesando shopping street on Miyajima, billed as the island's only dedicated juice bar. It serves cups of juice pressed from seasonal fruit, with a signature Hiroshima Mix that changes with what is in season and Hiroshima-grown  (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: A small fresh-juice bar tucked along the Omotesando shopping street on Miyajima, billed as the island's only dedicated juice bar. It serves cups of juice pressed from seasonal fruit, with a signature Hiroshima Mix that changes with what is in season and Hiroshima-grown lemon turning up in the lemonade and lemon soda. Alongside the drinks it offers Miyajima-style rice burgers and rice-wrapped onigiri.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fresh fruit juice refreshes the throat with the sweetness and acidity unique to each fruit — a vitamin pick-me-up for visitors strolling the island.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh seasonal-fruit juices and Hiroshima-lemon drinks

anecdotes.text: Positioned as a 'vitamin charge' rest stop for Miyajima sightseers, with the tart Hiroshima-lemon soda recommended as an energizing pick for hot summer visits.
Described as Itsukushima's only juice bar, with the seasonal Hiroshima Mix as its most popular cup.

positives.text: Fresh fruit juices and Hiroshima-lemon lemonade/soda are fruit-based, giving plant-based and gluten-avoiding visitors a simple, naturally suitable option.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up juice stand on the main shopping street where pointing at the colorful fruit displays and a few words of Japanese easily get you served.

places.name: 厳島BARL 厳齋 (Itsukushima BARL Issai)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on oysters and anago dishes such as oyster carbonara, pizza, and fried oysters, which involve wheat (pasta, dough, frying batter) and typically wheat-based soy/seasonings. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would need to ask carefully; nothing on the available pages confirms gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Listings note a vegetarian menu is available ("ベジタリアンメニューあり"), but no detail on what those dishes are and no confirmation they are vegan. The restaurant's signature dishes are oysters, anago, and other seafood/Western fare that use fish and animal ingredients, so a strict vegan should ask in advance which specific items can be made without animal products.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Mondays (if Monday is a holiday, closed the following Tuesday instead)
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), about a 1-minute walk from the Miyaj
places.notes: [Miyajima] 厳島BARL 厳齋 (Issai) is a casual Western-style bar and cafe that sits just about a minute's walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, making it a natural first or last stop for travelers heading to Itsukushima Shrine. It opened relatively recently as a BARL-style spot offering cr

chef_bio.background: 厳島BARL 厳齋 (Issai) is a casual Western-style bar and cafe that sits just about a minute's walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, making it a natural first or last stop for travelers heading to Itsukushima Shrine. It opened relatively recently as a BARL-style spot offering Spanish- and Italian-based Miyajima-style European cooking built on Hiroshima's signature local ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: A relaxed place to ease into or wind down a Miyajima visit, serving the area's prized oysters and conger eel in approachable, Western-influenced small-plate and cafe style rather than as a formal seafood restaurant.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters and anago (conger eel) worked into Spanish- and Italian-based Miyajima-style European dishes - raw and grilled oysters, a popular oyster-and-mushroom ajillo, carbonara, an oyster Japanese-style pizza, and a two-kind seiro (steamed-rice) set of oyster and anago - paired with local Hiroshima sake and wine.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen reworks Hiroshima's oysters and conger eel into Spanish- and Italian-influenced bar dishes such as carbonara, an oyster Japanese-style pizza, and a signature oyster-and-mushroom ajillo, alongside raw and grilled oysters.
Positioned a one-minute walk from the Miyajima pier, it is pitched as a spot to drop into right after arriving by ferry and to relax in before heading home.

positives.text: A vegetarian menu is listed as available, so there are at least some meat-free options on offer.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, tourist-friendly spot right by the Miyajima pier where pointing at the menu and a few words of Japanese will get you by; staff are used to visitors but full English service is not confirmed.

places.name: 聚景荘 (Jukeiso)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The kitchen centers on seafood kaiseki and casual set meals (fried oysters/oyster cutlet, tempura/fried items, udon, soba, curry) that use wheat batter, wheat-based soy sauce, and shared frying. Anago-meshi and other dishes rely on soy-based seasonings. Nothing on any fetched page indicates gluten-free preparation, so GF travelers cannot rely on this kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The inn states on its official cuisine page that vegetarian (菜食主義者) meals can be prepared with advance reservation (※事前予約要). However, this is described as vegetarian, not vegan, and no plant-only/dairy-and-egg-free detail is published. The default cuisine is heavily seafood-based (oysters, conger eel, sea bream, octopus, squid) with fish dashi and wheat-soy seasonings. A vegan would need to confirm specifics (dashi, egg, dairy) directly and book ahead.
places.hours_raw: On-site restaurant Heike lunch: 11:30–14:00, advance reservation required. Dinner kaiseki for staying guests served at 18:00 / 18:30 / 19:00 (17:30 on special-event days). Casual eatery Petit Heike: irregular hours. Inn check-in 15:00–18:00, check-out by 10:00.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), on the hillside near Itsukushima Shr
places.cuisine: Kyodo ryori / Japanese kaiseki cuisine ryoka
places.notes: [Miyajima] Jukeiso is a traditional cuisine-ryokan founded in 1926 (Showa 1), set on a hillside overlooking Itsukushima Shrine, its great torii gate, the five-story pagoda and the treasure pagoda. Beyond lodging, it serves food: the on-site restaurant Heike offers reserved lunch c (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: Jukeiso is a traditional cuisine-ryokan founded in 1926 (Showa 1), set on a hillside overlooking Itsukushima Shrine, its great torii gate, the five-story pagoda and the treasure pagoda. Beyond lodging, it serves food: the on-site restaurant Heike offers reserved lunch courses scaled to different budgets, and a casual eatery called Petit Heike at the foot of the inn — diagonally across from the Miyajima History and Folklore Museum, beside the stairway up to Jukeiso — serves everyday Miyajima fare such as anago-meshi (conger-eel rice) and grilled oysters.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house presents its cooking around the four seasons of Miyajima — spring cherry blossoms, summer breezes, autumn foliage, winter snow — aiming for encounters and emotions guests will keep in their hearts, with every dish prepared by hand for both flavor and visual beauty.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Seto Inland Sea kaiseki built on Hiroshima oysters and anago (conger eel), alongside sea bream, yellowtail, flounder, octopus, squid and turban shells.

anecdotes.text: The inn frames Hiroshima's oysters as a point of pride, noting the region accounts for more than half of Japan's oyster production.
At the base of the inn, the old-fashioned spot Petit Heike serves anago-meshi set meals and grilled oysters, sitting just beside the staircase that climbs to Jukeiso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes battered/fried items (fried oysters, oyster cutlet, tempura-style fried fish and shrimp, tonkatsu, fried chicken) and noodles (udon, soba), indicating shared wheat handling and frying.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as anago-meshi rely on standard Japanese soy-based seasonings, which are typically wheat-containing; no gluten-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core kitchen is seafood-based kaiseki (oysters, conger eel, sea bream, octopus, squid) with fish-derived dashi, so shared equipment and stock are likely; a separate vegetarian menu exists only by advance request.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The inn states vegetarian meals can be prepared with prior reservation (ベジタリアン（菜食主義者）の料理もご用意可能です。※事前予約要), showing willingness to accommodate special diets when notified in advance.

positives.text: Vegetarian cuisine is offered on request with advance booking, and lunch courses are tailored to the guest's budget, suggesting flexibility for diners who communicate needs ahead of time.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional family-run inn with a reserved-course dining room, so booking ahead in Japanese (by phone or through the official site) is expected and a few words of Japanese will smooth your visit.

places.name: いちなり (Ichinari)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour, the layered noodles (yakisoba) are wheat, and the okonomi sauce contains wheat-based soy sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg-centric okonomiyaki/teppanyaki shop; the signature dish is 肉玉そば (pork, egg and noodles). Batter typically contains egg and dashi, and okonomi sauce is not vegan. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 18:00–04:00 / Sun 18:00–00:00 / closed Mon
places.neighborhood: 流川・薬研堀（中区）/ near Ebisu-chō Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] いちなり is a small okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop run by a husband-and-wife team inside the お好み共和国ひろしま村 okonomiyaki hall, on the third floor in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Hiroshima. The couple relocated to this spot a few years ago and keep the place

roles: husband-and-wife owners running the shop together
chef_bio.background: いちなり is a small okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop run by a husband-and-wife team inside the お好み共和国ひろしま村 okonomiyaki hall, on the third floor in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Hiroshima. The couple relocated to this spot a few years ago and keep the place open late into the night, drawing both locals and visitors after dinner.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style 肉玉そば (pork-and-egg okonomiyaki layered with noodles), cooked to order on the teppan.

anecdotes.text: Visitors single out the layered yakisoba noodles as thinner and springier than ordinary supermarket noodles, giving the okonomiyaki extra texture.
The owners are remembered as cheerful and easy to talk to; one guest recounted lingering at the counter for about three hours because the atmosphere was so welcoming.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based okonomiyaki batter and yakisoba noodles are cooked on a shared teppan griddle, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomi sauce is brushed on the finished okonomiyaki and is made with wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg and dashi-based ingredients are cooked together on the same griddle as all dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, casual couple-run counter shop where the owners are described as warm and chatty, but expect mostly Japanese-only conversation, so come ready to point and smile or use a few Japanese phrases.

places.name: おばんざい りなさん家 (Obanzai Rina-san-chi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The core menu of obanzai, oden, and nikusui (beef broth) is built on Japanese dashi and soy sauce, which contain wheat, and there is no documentation of gluten-free preparation or accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Dishes rely on fish/bonito dashi and Japanese soy sauce, and the signature nikusui is a beef-broth dish; no plant-based menu is documented and there is no indication that vegan requests can be accommodated.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–03:00 (closed Sun, also closed 1st and 3rd Mon)
places.neighborhood: Yagenbori / Ebisucho, Naka-ku (~5 min walk from Kanayamacho Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, home-style obanzai izakaya in Hiroshima's Yakkenbori nightlife quarter, opened on 22 January 2026 by owner Rina Nakagawa, who previously worked as a nurse.

chef_bio.background: A small, home-style obanzai izakaya in Hiroshima's Yakkenbori nightlife quarter, opened on 22 January 2026 by its owner, a former nurse known as Rina — the shop's name, りなさん家, means 'Rina's house.'
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's idea is a place to unwind at the end of the day, somewhere you naturally drop by after work and feel welcomed home with a sense of tadaima.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai (a weekly-rotating selection of seasonal side dishes), oden, and nikusui, prepared with a deeply infused dashi that one reviewer described as gently soaking into the body.

anecdotes.text: Run by a former nurse, the place is described by a guest as a shop wrapped in kindness, relaxed and unpretentious, where both mind and body unwind after a tiring day.
The weekly-rotating obanzai means there is something new to enjoy each visit, suiting both a casual drink and a proper meal.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The obanzai, oden, and nikusui menu is dashi- and soy-sauce-based; Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and there is no gluten-free preparation documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes use fish/bonito dashi and the signature nikusui is beef broth; no vegan options or separate preparation are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter izakaya in a nightlife district where the warm owner makes you feel at home, but it is a late-night, Japanese-language local spot, so some basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help a foreign student feel comfortable.

places.name: おばんざいBar うさぎ (Obanzai Bar Usagi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The obanzai dishes are seasoned with dashi and soy sauce (which contains wheat), and the menu includes items like macaroni salad and fresh bread, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The rotating obanzai spread includes meat and fish-based dishes (chicken dishes, mapo tofu, butter chicken curry) and the home-style seasoning typically relies on dashi and wheat-based soy sauce. There are several vegetable small plates (vinegared vegetables, blanched greens, braised eggplant, simmered pumpkin), but no confirmed fully plant-based preparation; a vegan diner would need to ask the owner directly.
places.hours_raw: 19:00–00:00 (Tue–Sat, days before holidays, etc.). Closed Sun and Mon.
places.neighborhood: Ebisucho, Naka-ku
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter bar tucked into the Mushika Building on Hiroshima's lively Mushika dori, run single-handedly by its owner, known to regulars simply as the mama. She starts cooking in the morning and prepares every dish herself, turning out a daily-changing spread of hom

chef_bio.background: A small counter bar tucked into the Mushika Building on Hiroshima's lively Mushika dori, run single-handedly by its owner, known to regulars simply as the mama. She starts cooking in the morning and prepares every dish herself, turning out a daily-changing spread of home-style obanzai.
chef_bio.philosophy: Offer warm, lovingly prepared home cooking in an intimate, hideaway setting where solo diners feel at ease.
chef_bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat handmade obanzai (Japanese home cooking) served at the counter, alongside a deep drinks list of sake, shochu and whiskey.

anecdotes.text: The owner prepares each obanzai dish one by one from the morning, and the daily lineup of eight or so small plates rotates constantly, with weekly specials such as butter chicken curry.
The counter has a hidden-gem, Kyoto-alleyway feel and a warm, home-like atmosphere that draws a loyal local crowd, including notable Hiroshima regulars.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter bar run solo by the owner; you'll be most comfortable with some basic Japanese, and as a cash-only late-night spot it suits an evening sit-down rather than a quick meal.

places.name: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き 蔵屋 紙屋町店 (Okonomiyaki Teppanyaki Kuraya Kamiyacho-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and noodles, and the savory dishes use standard wheat-based soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on oysters, sea urchin (ウニホーレン), beef (コウネ, A5 wagyu), pork bacon, eggs, and seafood; okonomiyaki uses egg and pork and is cooked on shared griddles with meat and fish. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) / 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 22:30), daily including holidays. Sundays may close earlier depending on customer volume. Closures are irregular.
places.neighborhood: Otemachi / Kamiyacho, Naka-ku
places.notes: A teppan izakaya in central Hiroshima a short walk from Hondori and the A-Bomb Dome, part of the local Kuraya group. The dining room blends a calm, traditional Japanese feel with private rooms, and every seat - including the counter and the tatami rooms - has its own bu

chef_bio.background: A teppan izakaya in central Hiroshima a short walk from Hondori and the A-Bomb Dome, part of the local Kuraya group. The dining room blends a calm, traditional Japanese feel with private rooms, and every seat - including the counter and the tatami rooms - has its own built-in iron griddle so food is served hot and freshly cooked right in front of you.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the idea that food tastes best straight off the iron plate, the restaurant fits its private griddle into every room so groups, dates, and solo diners alike can eat freshly cooked dishes; counter guests are welcomed by an easygoing owner-chef.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on the teppan alongside Seto Inland Sea seafood and griddle dishes: local oysters, the Hiroshima specialty uni horen (sea urchin on sautéed spinach), salt-grilled koune (a rare, collagen-rich wagyu beef cut), and premium A5 wagyu, all paired with local Hiroshima sake.

anecdotes.text: The menu leans into Hiroshima specialties such as uni horen (sea urchin on sautéed spinach) and salt-grilled koune, a rare collagen-rich wagyu beef cut popular with diners.
Counter seats let solo diners chat with the approachable owner-chef while their food is grilled.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki, noodles, and griddle dishes share a common teppan; wheat batter and wheat-based sauces are central to the menu, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce (both typically wheat-containing) are used; no gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All seats use a shared iron griddle that cooks oysters, beef, pork, and seafood, so any vegetable item would contact animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, mainstream teppan restaurant near the A-Bomb Dome where a foreign student can point at the picture menu and watch dishes cooked on the griddle, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese.

places.name: 穴子飯 木村屋本店 (Anago-meshi Kimuraya Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The anago is served with sweet tare/soy-based sauce (the "black" and "red" styles) and the white style is served with a special soy sauce — Japanese soy sauce and sweet tare both contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no allergen documentation, and the kitchen does not advertise any gluten-free handling. A celiac traveler could not eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Every main is conger eel (fish), served over rice with miso soup and pickles, and even the white-grilled style uses soy sauce. There are no plant-based mains or documented meat/fish-free options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu-Sat: 10:30-15:00 and 17:00-22:00; Wed & Sun: 10:30-15:00; Tue: closed. (Official site notes lunch-focused hours 10:30-16:00 with irregular holidays; evening izakaya service on some days.)
places.neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (steps from the Atomic Bomb Dome and Kamiyacho-nishi, very close to Peace Memorial Park)
places.notes: A specialist devoted to anago-meshi, Hiroshima's century-old conger-eel-over-rice dish, set in Otemachi within sight of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park. The shop is run by owner Souji Kimura, who grew up around the trade — his father was a sushi chef — and the k

chef_bio.background: A specialist devoted to anago-meshi, Hiroshima's century-old conger-eel-over-rice dish, set in Otemachi within sight of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park. The shop is run by owner Souji Kimura, who grew up around the trade — his father was a sushi chef — and the kitchen is led by a head chef with more than twenty years of sushi experience.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner inherited from his sushi-chef father the idea of preparing every dish 'with love, just as you would cook for your own children and parents,' and the shop commits to serving food as delicious as possible using premium ingredients — pairing Hiroshima's traditional anago-meshi with refined sushi technique to create a 'new Hiroshima taste.'
chef_bio.specialty: Three contrasting styles of anago-meshi served side by side: 'black,' raw conger grilled over charcoal for a crisp, fragrant, chewy bite; 'red,' simmered then finished with a special sweet sauce until meltingly tender; and 'white,' shirayaki grilled plain to taste the eel itself, served with real wasabi and a special soy sauce. Each set comes with soup and pickles, and the chef oversees every preparation.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant frames itself as carrying forward more than 100 years of Hiroshima anago-meshi history while taking on new challenges in a place where World Heritage sites stand.
The owner says the lesson to cook 'with love just like preparing meals for our children and parents' came from his father, who was a sushi chef.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The white-style anago is served with a special soy sauce, and the black/red styles use a sweet tare; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen information is published; the site provides no gluten, wheat, or cross-contamination details, so allergy diners would need to phone the shop directly.

cultural_comfort.note: An English website and menu make ordering easy for a foreign student, and the simple three-style anago format means you can just point and choose.

places.name: 汁なし担々麺 一楽 幟町店 (Shirunashi Tantanmen Ichiraku Noboricho-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle dandan-men shop; the noodles, the meat sauce, and the seasoning base are not gluten-free, and no gluten-free option is documented. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
places.vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on a pork/meat sauce with sesame paste, and sets include a soft-boiled egg, so the dishes are not vegan. No vegan option is documented.
places.neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Noboricho area, north side of Peace Boulevard)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is the second shop in the Ichiraku family, the central-Hiroshima branch of a brothless dandan-noodle (汁なし担々麺) house that started in Miyoshi in northern Hiroshima. It opened in the Noboricho neighborhood on July 7, 2025.

chef_bio.background: This is the second shop in the Ichiraku family, the central-Hiroshima branch of a brothless dandan-noodle (汁なし担々麺) house that started in Miyoshi in northern Hiroshima. It opened in the Noboricho neighborhood on July 7, 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking aims for a notably light, clean flavor rather than heavy richness; the meat sauce leans gently sweet and the sesame is present but restrained, with adjustable spice levels that stay mild.
chef_bio.specialty: Brothless dandan noodles (汁なし担々麺) served with two kinds of green onion, plus a soup version. The brothless set comes with rice and a soft-boiled egg.

anecdotes.text: Both the brothless and soup versions are described as having a characteristic lightness of flavor, with the meat sauce emphasizing sweetness and the sesame kept gentle; even at spice level 2 it reads as mild.
Staff indicated the menu is planned to expand, possibly adding mapo tofu as offered at the original Miyoshi location.

cultural_comfort.note: You order at a touch-panel ticket machine at the entrance, so a few words of Japanese and pointing will get you through comfortably even with no fluent staff English confirmed.

noodles.notes: Straight medium-gauge noodles suited to being tossed and mixed (and good for dipping into the soup version); paired with the sansho-and-chili tare rather than served in broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dan dan noodles) — order it with a soft-boiled egg and rice, mix everything vigorously at the table, choose your sansho (fresh or standard) and chili level, then finish by combining the leftover egg and tare with the rice.
notable_toppings: choice of fresh or standard sansho

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy-based meat-miso tare make this dish unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The tare is built on a sweet-savory pork meat-miso, so the dish is meat-based; no documented plant-only version.

ramen.pantheon: A second-generation outpost of a Miyoshi original, this is Hiroshima's hometown soupless dan dan noodle in its lighter, more refined dialect — no soup, just straight noodles tossed in a sweet-savory pork meat-miso laced with chili and tingling sansho. Where the city's classic version leans fiery, here the heat stays gentle and the sesame and Sichuan-pepper aromatics do the talking, ending in the ritual rice-and-egg finish.

places.highlight: A second-generation outpost of a Miyoshi original, this is Hiroshima's hometown soupless dan dan noodle in its lighter, more refined dialect — no soup, just straight noodles tossed in a sweet-savory pork meat-miso laced with chili and tingling sansho. Where the city's classic version leans fiery, here the heat stays gentle and the sesame and Sichuan-pepper aromatics do the talking, ending in the ritual rice-and-egg finish.

places.name: きよちゃん (Kiyochan)
places.gf_detail: The ramen and tsukemen use wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned pork-bone broth, so dishes are not gluten-free and there is no gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is pork-bone and soy-sauce based and the bowls are topped with char siu (roast pork), so the food is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sat 11:30–14:00 and 18:30–21:00; Sun 11:30–14:00; closed Mon & Tue. Closes early when the soup and noodles run out.
places.neighborhood: 立町 (Tatemachi), Naka-ku — between Hondori and Hatchobori, ne
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kiyochan first opened in 1973 and has served Hiroshima-style chuka soba (ramen) for around half a century. After a continuous family lineage, it reopened in 2018 under its third-generation owner, who carries on the shop's classic recipe.

chef_bio.background: Kiyochan first opened in 1973 and has served Hiroshima-style chuka soba (ramen) for around half a century. Passed down through the family, it was revived in 2018 under its third-generation owner, who carries on the shop's classic recipe.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately intimate counter-only shop that has kept its traditional, minimalist approach to classic Hiroshima ramen across three generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style ramen and tsukemen built on an amber, gently pork-bone-scented soy-sauce broth, with medium-thin firm wheat noodles topped simply with bean sprouts, scallions, and char siu. The char siu is briefly torched before serving for a lean, savory roasted note. Wonton dishes are also offered.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so small — a counter of only about five seats — that it fills up before noon, with regulars lining up by midday.
Opened in 1973 and revived in 2018 under third-generation ownership, keeping the long-running family recipe alive.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy sauce broth are central to every bowl, so a shared cooking environment makes the food unsuitable for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a soy-sauce (shoyu) style broth, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is pork-bone based and bowls are topped with char siu pork, so animal products are integral to the cooking, not just toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny five-seat local counter where ordering is simple but communication is in Japanese, so a few basic words or pointing at the menu will help a foreign student feel at ease.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin (chu-hoso), straight, cooked firm with a noticeable wheat character. Classic Hiroshima-style noodle that drinks up the amber tonkotsu-shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (the classic Hiroshima-style tonkotsu-shoyu bowl)
ramen.chashu: Lean pork chashu lightly aburi (torched/char-finished) just before serving, sliced thin; low in fat, prized for its fragrant, smoky-savory edge rather than melting richness.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare put gluten throughout the bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone broth with soy tare and pork chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A half-century-old standard-bearer of Hiroshima chuka soba, now run by the third generation, ladling out the city's signature amber-hued tonkotsu-shoyu soup: gentle pork-bone body laced with a sweet-savory soy backbone, light enough to drink to the bottom. You taste old-school Hiroshima in every sip, finished with thin firm noodles and a fragrant aburi-torched lean chashu.

places.highlight: A half-century-old standard-bearer of Hiroshima chuka soba, now run by the third generation, ladling out the city's signature amber-hued tonkotsu-shoyu soup: gentle pork-bone body laced with a sweet-savory soy backbone, light enough to drink to the bottom. You taste old-school Hiroshima in every sip, finished with thin firm noodles and a fragrant aburi-torched lean chashu.

places.name: ねぎ庵 流川店 (Negian Nagarekawa-ten)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and wheat noodles, and teppanyaki dishes are seasoned with standard soy-sauce-based sauces, so this is not a gluten-free destination. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not rely on this restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on Hiroshima oysters, koune (beef neck), pork, egg, and seafood-and-wheat-based okonomiyaki, with sauces containing wheat soy sauce and likely bonito/fish stock. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Vegan diners should look elsewhere.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu: 17:00–02:00 next day (food L.O. 01:00 next day, drinks L.O. 01:30 next day) / Fri, Sat, days before holidays: 17:00–03:00 next day (food L.O. 02:00 next day, drinks L.O. 02:30 next day) / Sun, holidays: 17:00–23:30 (food L.O. 22:30, drinks L.O. 23:00)
places.neighborhood: Nagarekawa / Yagenbori entertainment district, Naka-ku (near
places.notes: A lively late-night okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, part of the local Negian group of okonomiyaki restaurants.

chef_bio.background: A lively late-night okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, part of the local Negian group of okonomiyaki restaurants.
chef_bio.philosophy: A stated devotion to quality in three things: the ingredients, the noodles, and the scallions.
chef_bio.specialty: The house identity is built around scallions: Hiroshima-grown negi piled generously over Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, alongside regional specialties like Hiroshima oysters and koune (a wagyu beef neck/brisket cut). The kitchen describes its focus as a commitment to ingredients, noodles, and scallions.

anecdotes.text: The signature is okonomiyaki and teppanyaki heaped with locally grown Hiroshima scallions, served alongside regional teppan dishes such as oysters and koune (a collagen-rich wagyu beef cut).
A known item is a salt-and-mayo tonpei (egg-and-pork) dish the group promotes as a contest winner — taking first place nationally in the 2010 Otafuku Idea Menu Contest.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles cooked on a shared teppan griddle, making cross-contact with gluten unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki dishes are seasoned with standard sauces and soy sauce, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared on a shared griddle with pork, egg, oysters, and beef, so vegan separation is not provided.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which helps travelers identify ingredients before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and solo diners are welcome, so a foreign student can comfortably order Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki here even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 古今果anco (Coconca anco (Kokonka anco))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature ankoronne is built on a fermented-butter puff pastry, which is wheat-based; other items (sablé, financiers) are also wheat flour confections. No gluten-free options or allergen handling were documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. While the red bean paste (anko) itself is plant-based, the flagship ankoronne uses fermented butter pastry and a custard cream made from Brown Swiss cow's milk, so it contains dairy. Financiers and other baked goods typically contain butter and eggs. No vegan items were documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:30 daily (one source lists 10:00–18:00); irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (toward Itsukushima Shrine / ferry terminal)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 古今果anco is the casual Miyajima outpost of Fujii-ya (藤い屋), a momiji-manju and mochi maker with roughly 90 years of red-bean-paste tradition. It opened on February 25, 2017 on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, directly across from Fujii-ya's main shop, as an offshoot (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: 古今果anco is the casual Miyajima outpost of Fujii-ya (藤い屋), a momiji-manju and mochi maker with roughly 90 years of red-bean-paste tradition. It opened on February 25, 2017 on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, directly across from Fujii-ya's main shop, as an offshoot of the 'Coconca/古今果' brand the company first launched in 2015 at Miyajima-guchi as a confectionery workshop and café.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'Japanese-Western fusion' approach to sweets, building modern pastries on the foundation of Fujii-ya's nine-decade-old traditional red bean paste so that the heritage anko is the heart of each creation.
chef_bio.specialty: Ankoronne — a coronet-shaped pastry of fermented-butter puff pastry filled with the shop's artisan anko and a custard cream made from Brown Swiss cow's milk from the Mirasaka Fromage Workshop, finished with Breton sea salt from western France.

anecdotes.text: The storefront's design echoes the Seto Inland Sea, mirroring the aesthetic of the original 2015 Coconca venue at Miyajima-guchi.
The signature ankoronne layers fermented-butter puff pastry with house anko and Brown Swiss milk custard cream, finished with Breton salt as an accent.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed counter-style sweets shop where you can point at the pastry you want and pay easily, so a few words of Japanese and gestures are plenty for a foreign student.

places.name: 電光石火 駅前ひろば店 (Denko-Sekka Ekimae Hiroba)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for gluten-free diners. Okonomiyaki is made from a wheat-flour batter and is layered with wheat noodles (soba or udon). The okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or accommodation; everything is cooked together on a shared teppan griddle.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built on Hiroshima okonomiyaki, which centers on egg, pork, and noodles, with squid tempura, oysters, wagyu offal and other animal toppings. The signature dish layers a double egg with meat and seafood, and the sauce is a seafood-flavored Worcester-style sauce. No vegan or vegetarian dishes or modifications are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), open daily / year-round
places.neighborhood: Hiroshima Station area (Ekimae Hiroba / Full Focus Building, 6F)
places.notes: Denko-Sekka is a Hiroshima okonomiyaki brand whose Ekimae Hiroba branch sits on the 6th-floor okonomiyaki theme floor (Hiroshima Okonomi Monogatari Ekimae Hiroba) of the Full Focus Building, about a three-minute walk from Hiroshima Station. The shop has grown from Hiros

chef_bio.background: Denko-Sekka is a Hiroshima okonomiyaki brand whose Ekimae Hiroba branch sits on the 6th-floor okonomiyaki theme floor (Hiroshima Okonomi Monogatari Ekimae Hiroba) of the Full Focus Building, about a three-minute walk from Hiroshima Station. The shop has grown from Hiroshima into a brand with locations across Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: Their stated ethos is to deliver a piping-hot dish overflowing with passion and happiness ('情熱と幸せ溢れるアツアツの一枚を'), prizing the sensory experience of the teppan's heat, the aroma of the sauce, and bright, energetic service alongside quality cabbage, batter, and house sauce.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked fresh on a hot teppan in front of guests. Their signature 'Denko-Sekka' okonomiyaki is known for an unusually fluffy, tender texture, layering a double egg with meat, noodles, squid tempura, shiso, and green onion.

anecdotes.text: The team describes its approach as cooking 'at full power from 10 in the morning,' with all-counter seating so guests can watch every okonomiyaki being grilled in front of them.
Beyond the classic, the kitchen offers playful original toppings, including an 'ebi-mayo' shrimp-mayonnaise version finished with a house signature sauce they say is found nowhere else.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made from a wheat-flour batter and topped with wheat noodles, all cooked on a single shared teppan griddle alongside every other order, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, a wheat-based Worcester-style sauce; no wheat-free or gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle is used continuously for pork, egg, squid, oysters, and other animal ingredients, so any item would have animal-product cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, tourist-friendly okonomiyaki shop right by Hiroshima Station where you watch the cooking at a counter; a few words of Japanese help but the picture-friendly griddle experience makes ordering easy for a foreign student.

places.name: 本家 村長の店 (Honke Soncho no Mise)
places.gf_detail: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and wheat noodles (soba/udon) and is finished with wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Signature dishes contain pork, egg, squid, shrimp, oysters and cheese, and use wheat-and-soy-based sauce; the batter typically includes egg and dashi. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-21:00 (food L.O. 20:30); closed Thursdays (open if Thursday is a holiday)
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (next to the Okonomi-mura building, near
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running okonomiyaki shop in Hiroshima's Shintenchi district, standing next to the famous Okonomi-mura building near Alice Garden. The shop carries on the flavor of one of the founders behind Okonomi-mura and has been beloved by locals for about 50 years.

chef_bio.background: A long-running okonomiyaki shop in Hiroshima's Shintenchi district, standing next to the famous Okonomi-mura building near Alice Garden. The shop carries on the flavor of one of the founders behind Okonomi-mura and has been beloved by locals for about 50 years.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hiroshima-specialty okonomiyaki refined over 50 years, cooked on the teppan in front of guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with soba noodles, plus seafood and teppanyaki; the house Soncho Deluxe (soba, squid, shrimp, cheese, green onion, pork and egg) and the soba-mochi-cheese okonomiyaki are signatures, alongside butter-grilled oysters and other Seto Inland Sea dishes.

anecdotes.text: The shop inherits the flavor of a founder of Okonomi-mura and has been refining its Hiroshima okonomiyaki for about 50 years.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式(owst)がお好み村創業者の味を受け継ぎ約50年と記載。村長デラックス(そば・いか・えび・チーズ・ねぎ・豚・玉子)、そばもちチーズ、牡蠣バター焼等を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared teppan (griddle) where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and noodles are prepared, making cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce, which is wheat- and soy-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared teppan is used for pork, egg, seafood and cheese dishes, so any item would share the same cooking surface as animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming local okonomiyaki shop where you can sit at the counter and watch your dish cooked; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a long way, though detailed allergy questions may be hard to convey.

places.name: 八戒 (Hakkai)
places.gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki crepe batter is wheat flour, the dishes include wheat noodles (soba/yakisoba), and okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki: dishes are built on pork, egg, and seafood (e.g., uni, squid), with wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce and a layered egg/meat preparation. No vegan menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–03:00 next day / closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Shintenchi), Okonomimura 4F
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 八戒 is one of the individually run okonomiyaki stalls on the 4th floor of Okonomimura, the famous Hiroshima building where dozens of small okonomiyaki shops sit side by side on stacked floors. It is a compact, counter-style place open late into the night.

chef_bio.background: 八戒 is one of the individually run okonomiyaki stalls on the 4th floor of Okonomimura, the famous Hiroshima building where dozens of small okonomiyaki shops sit side by side on stacked floors. It is a compact, counter-style place open late into the night.
chef_bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with crispy noodles, alongside a wide range of teppanyaki small plates; the suggested way to eat is to start with grilled appetizers and finish with the okonomiyaki. Popular orders include soba-niku-tama with cheese and seafood items.

anecdotes.text: Diners are encouraged to graze through teppanyaki small plates first and end the meal with an okonomiyaki featuring crispy pan-fried noodles, rather than ordering just a single pancake.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ぐるなびがお好み村4F、パリパリそば入り広島風お好み焼き、そば肉玉チーズ、おつまみから締めのお好み焼きまでを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no allergen-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared teppan is used for pork, egg, and seafood dishes; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, late-night counter okonomiyaki spot inside the multi-shop Okonomimura building; staff are used to tourists and you can order by pointing at the menu, but expect a casual local atmosphere with little dedicated English support.

places.name: ガーデンハウス ヒロシマ (GARDEN HOUSE HIROSHIMA)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or claims found. Core offerings are wheat-based (wood-fired pizza on hand-stretched dough, pasta, ricotta pancakes), and there is no mention of gluten-free preparation, separate equipment, or a dedicated fryer. A celiac traveler should not assume any dish is safe without contacting the restaurant directly (050-1721-6566).
places.vegan_detail: A vegan/vegetarian menu is offered. Documented plant-based items include a falafel and vegetable plate at lunch, and the listing notes vegan and vegetarian menu options are available. Specific ingredients and cross-contact are not documented, so vegans should confirm details (e.g., dairy, egg, fish-derived stocks) with staff when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:30). Closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: Motomachi, Naka-ku (inside Hiroshima Central Park / Hiropa, near the Atomic Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park, adjacent to Edion Peace Wing)
places.notes: GARDEN HOUSE began in Kamakura and grew into a small group of garden-side, all-day dining cafes. This Hiroshima outpost is the brand's seventh location and its first in the Chugoku region, opening inside the HiroPa complex in Hiroshima Central Park within easy walking d

chef_bio.background: GARDEN HOUSE began in Kamakura and grew into a small group of garden-side, all-day dining cafes. This Hiroshima outpost is the brand's seventh location and its first in the Chugoku-Shikoku region, opening inside the HiroPa complex in Hiroshima Central Park within easy walking distance of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around 'local sourcing (chisan-chisho)' and 'handmade,' the cafe treats the surrounding park as one large garden, aiming for a seamless connection between the dining space and nature so guests can eat amid greenery.
chef_bio.specialty: North-California-style all-day fare: hand-stretched pizza fired in a wood oven at around 450C, pasta, ricotta-cheese pancakes, and seasonal plates, paired with Japanese natural wine and Seto Inland Sea ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The design intentionally blurs the line between the restaurant and the park, framing the green space as 'a large garden' so the shop and garden feel like one continuous, nature-filled place.
The signature Bismarck pizza is built on hand-stretched dough wood-fired at about 450C for roughly a minute, topped with cream-cheese sauce, egg, cheese and prosciutto.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ペコマグが7店舗目・中四国初出店、平和公園近くのHiroPa、薪窯450℃約1〜1分半、ビスマルク(クリームチーズ/卵/チーズ/生ハム)、リコッタパンケーキ、地産地消・手作りを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on a wood-fired oven for pizza and serves pasta and pancakes, so flour is used heavily throughout; no gluten-free handling is documented.

positives.text: A vegan/vegetarian menu is offered, including a falafel and vegetable plate, giving plant-based diners a clear option.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a relaxed, modern park-side cafe used to tourists and casual walk-ins, with online reservations and card/QR payment, so a foreign student can comfortably drop in and point at the menu.

places.name: BioGaia TALKS (BioGaia TALKS (Baiogaia Tokusu))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labeling is documented on the official site, menu page, or listing. The cafe centers on fermented foods and yogurt, with curry and rice-bowl dishes; whether any item is gluten-free and how cross-contamination is handled is not stated, so a celiac traveler would need to ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The signature products are dairy yogurt and smoothies made with milk (recreating a 1917 Hiroshima yogurt recipe), and lunch items include amazake butter chicken curry, so meat and dairy are present. There may be some plant-forward fermented or vegetable dishes, but no items are documented as vegan and none are labeled as such; confirm in person.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri 11:00-18:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00-17:00; Closed Mondays. (Listing also breaks weekday service into 11:00-14:00 and 14:00-18:00 blocks.) Open 11:00-21:00 on event days per some sources.
places.neighborhood: Kamiyacho, Naka-ku (Kamiyacho-higashi / Ebisucho-higashi, near the Atomic Bomb Dome)
places.notes: BioGaia TALKS is a fermented-food cafe and dialogue space in central Hiroshima's Kamiyacho district, near the A-Bomb Dome. It is run by the Swedish probiotics maker BioGaia, built around the idea that good gut health and good conversation nourish people together. The ca

chef_bio.background: BioGaia TALKS is a fermented-food cafe and dialogue space in central Hiroshima's Kamiyacho district, near the A-Bomb Dome. It is run by the Swedish probiotics maker BioGaia, built around the idea that good gut health and good conversation nourish people together. The cafe's signature is yogurt made with L. reuteri lactic-acid bacteria, including a plain yogurt that faithfully recreates what is described as Japan's oldest yogurt recipe, born in the Nomura family of Hiroshima in 1917 and revived by the fifth generation.
chef_bio.philosophy: The space runs on the motto that 'knowledge is the finest entertainment,' pairing a healthy, fermentation-forward menu (kin-katsu, or 'bacteria wellness') with talk events where experts and guests share ideas face to face across medicine, science, food, and art. Greenery, a nursing room, and diaper-changing facilities make it a calm, family-friendly place to eat, read, work, or attend an event.
chef_bio.specialty: L. reuteri yogurt and smoothies, plus body-friendly fermented lunch dishes such as amazake (sweet-sake) butter chicken curry and a neba (sticky-fermented) rice bowl, available for dine-in or takeout.

anecdotes.text: The plain yogurt recreates a recipe said to be Japan's oldest, born in Hiroshima's Nomura family in 1917 and faithfully revived by the fifth generation.
A new lunch lineup featuring fermentation-based dishes like amazake butter chicken curry and a neba rice bowl launched in September 2025, available both for dine-in and takeout.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kafe.biogaia.jpがL.ロイテリ菌ヨーグルトと1917年広島・野村家発祥『日本最古』レシピを5代目が再現と明記。goguynetが2025年9月の発酵ランチ(甘酒バターチキンカレー・ねば丼)を報道。

positives.text: The kitchen is built around fermented foods and a body-friendly, health-conscious concept, so wholesome ingredients are a stated focus.

cultural_comfort.note: A bright, modern, family-friendly cafe in central Hiroshima where a few words of Japanese help with ordering, though the casual counter-and-table setup is easy to navigate for a foreign student.

places.name: もみじ饅頭 木村屋 (Momiji Manju Kimuraya)
places.gf_detail: Momiji manju are made from a wheat-flour, egg-rich castella-style batter, so they contain gluten and are not gluten-free. No gluten-free options or claims were found.
places.vegan_detail: The cakes use an egg-rich wheat batter, and the signature fillings include dairy (Danish cream cheese), custard cream, and chocolate. Even the bean-paste versions are baked in the egg-and-flour shell, so nothing here is vegan. No vegan options were found.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:00 (irregular days off). Some sources list 9:00–17:00.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street
places.cuisine: Small hand-baked family shop for momiji manju
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] もみじ饅頭 木村屋は、宮島桟橋から表参道商店街に入ってすぐの角にある、もみじ饅頭の手焼き専門の小さな個人店です。店頭で一つひとつ焼き上げており、焼き立てのほかほかをその場で味わえます。 (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.background: もみじ饅頭 木村屋は、宮島桟橋から表参道商店街に入ってすぐの角にある、もみじ饅頭の手焼き専門の小さな個人店です。店頭で一つひとつ焼き上げており、焼き立てのほかほかをその場で味わえます。
chef_bio.philosophy: 厳選した材料と卵をたっぷり使った生地で焼き上げることにこだわっています。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板はデンマーク産ナチュラルクリームチーズを使ったクリームチーズもみじ饅頭で、なめらかで濃厚な味わいが人気を集め、売り切れることも多い一品です。ほかにこしあん・つぶあん・カスタードクリーム・チョコ・ラムに漬けたレーズンなど多彩なバリエーションを揃え、宮島名物のお好み焼きともみじ饅頭を掛け合わせた『おこもみじ』(店の登録商標)も名物です。

anecdotes.text: デンマーク産ナチュラルクリームチーズを使ったクリームチーズもみじ饅頭が看板商品で、なめらかで濃厚な味わいから売り切れ必至の人気ぶり。
宮島名物のお好み焼きともみじ饅頭を組み合わせた独自のおやつ『おこもみじ』を考案し、店の登録商標としている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトが『おこもみじ』(お好み焼き×もみじ饅頭)を木村屋の登録商標と明記。デンマーク産クリームチーズの看板商品や各種フレーバーをoyakodeブログで確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All momiji manju are baked from a shared wheat-flour batter on the same griddle, so the entire product line contains gluten with no gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The batter is egg- and flour-based and fillings include dairy cream cheese, custard, and chocolate; bean-paste versions share the same egg-flour shell, so there is no vegan-safe item.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter-service sweets stall on the main shopping street where you point and pay; no Japanese needed beyond a smile, and the freshly grilled cakes are easy to order on the go.

places.name: 吉川七浦堂 (Kikkawa Shichiura-do)
places.gf_detail: Momiji manju are made from a wheat-flour castella-style batter, so they contain gluten. This is not a gluten-free option.
places.vegan_detail: Momiji manju batter is a castella-style sponge that typically contains egg (and often dairy/honey), so these are not vegan. The filling is koshi-an (smooth red bean paste), which is plant-based, but the cake itself is not. No vegan-specific information was found.
places.neighborhood: Miyajima, Omotesando shopping street (near Itsukushima Shrine, across from Miyajima Post Office)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running family momiji manju shop on Miyajima's main shopping street, across from the island post office and a short walk from the ferry terminal toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is believed to have been founded around 1923 (late Taisho era), giving it roughly a cent (Map pin approximate — Omotesandō; use the Maps link for exact spot.)

chef_bio.origin: 宮島（広島県廿日市市宮島町）, on Itsukushima island
chef_bio.background: A long-running family momiji manju shop on Miyajima's main shopping street, across from the island post office and a short walk from the ferry terminal toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is believed to have been founded around 1923 (late Taisho era), giving it roughly a century of history, and is often described as one of the island's original momiji manju makers.
